Double whammy
It's a double whammy that has hit not just Chilwesa, but millions of
other small farmers like him in Zambia and elsewhere in Africa. As poor
countries have lowered their tariffs on imports - often on the advice of
international financial institutions - their markets have been flooded by
cheap produce from abroad. Zambia recently lowered its import duty on mealie
maize - the staple byproduct of maize - by five per cent.
Imported products can be cheaper because they are produced more
efficiently on large commercial farms. But sometimes they are simply
'dumped' - or sold below the cost of production - by producers in wealthy
countries where the agricultural sector benefits from massive government
subsidies.
Either way, the worst hit is the small farmer.
An important aspect of the problem is food security. Because maize is
the staple in Zambia there is always the looming threat of a food crisis. It
is a familiar scenario: cheap or dumped imports trigger a fall in prices,
leading small farmers to consume their maize rather than sell it cheaply to
mills.
Mill-owners in turn tend to hoard their stocks hoping for a price
rise. In a country with pockets of drought and ridden with poor
transportation, sourcing maize from remote areas is a further problem.
Special treatment
As a result, the protection of vital food crops is high on the agenda of
trade ministers from many developing countries gathered at Hong Kong for the
6th ministerial meeting of the World Trade Organisation (WTO).
They want to protect their markets - and their poorest populations -
from the worst effects of agricultural dumping by persuading the WTO to
allow countries to select their own individual lists of 'Special Products',
while introducing what they call Special Safeguard Mechanisms (SSM).
According to the proposal, special agricultural products, seen as part
of a longer-term strategy in the face of dumping, would comprise those
commodities that are vital for national food security, for safeguarding the
livelihood of rural populations, and for national development. These
commodities would be exempt from commitments to lower duties on
imports.
The SSM proposal, if agreed would allow developing countries to protect
themselves and their farmers by imposing higher than normally-allowed duties
on any product when there are sudden import surges or price fluctuations -
twin scourges that have ruined small farmers.
At Hong Kong, the G20 group of large developing nations appeared on
Wednesday (14 December) to lend its weight to these long-standing demands of
the G33, which includes the poorer countries of the world.
The G20, which is led by India, South Africa, Brazil and Argentina, said
that a "gateway for special products and special safeguard measures" in
agriculture was crucial for moving the talks ahead.
"This is a key issue for the G20. There is a general view that without
agreement on special products and Special Safeguard Mechanisms, other things
can't go forward," Celso Amorim, Brazil's foreign affairs minister told
reporters.
National action
Back on his farm, Chilwesa thinks national governments can also chip
in.
"It would be wrong to blame anyone, but the government should be held
responsible. Perhaps if we small-scale farmers organised ourselves and
formed cooperatives it might work and we could sell. It is worth exploiting
but how much muscle do we have?" Chilwesa says.
The irony is that Zambia's national food security is based largely on
maize grown by small farmers, who account for 80 per cent of the total maize
production.
Dipak Patel, the Commerce, Trade and Industry Minister and Coordinator
for the Least Developed Countries at Hong Kong, says although Zambia is not
expected to participate in the negotiations on tariff reduction formula,
their outcome will have implications for the competitiveness of Zambian
agricultural produce.
"Zambia stresses the need to take into account the impact of tariff
reductions on both importing and exporting countries," Patel says.
Patel says it is imperative that countries, while lowering their
tariffs, are able to protect their Special Products. On Zambia's list are:
maize, cassava, millet, groundnut, sunflower seed, rice, sugarcane, cotton
seed, wheat, soya beans, tobacco leaves, sorghum; vegetables such as
tomatoes and onions; and dairy and poultry products.
The G33 has finalised a proposal that would give developing countries
the right to designate up to 20 per cent of their agricultural tariff lines
as Special Products.
Food crisis looms
The negotiations in Hong Kong have become particularly important for
ensuring the food security of ordinary Zambians because of the looming food
crisis, which has prompted the government to issue urgent appeals for
international food aid.
The crisis, which is expected to affect about two million of Zambia's
11.6 million-population, is blamed by the government on poor yields as a
result of crop failures due to low technology and poor use of inputs.
But farmers attribute the problem to poor agricultural policies, which
they say have led to understaffing of agriculture extension officers,
limited irrigation facilities and high costs of agricultural inputs.
The rise in the death toll from the HIV/AIDS pandemic is also rapidly
incapacitating farmers, their families and workers.
A vulnerability assessment report for 2005 prepared by the permanent
secretary for agriculture, Richard Chizyuka, acknowledges that the
government is faced with insufficient financial resources and that poor
roads present a further problem in distributing food aid.
To this list, farmer Chilwesa adds poor marketing and
transportation.
"Small farmers cannot carry their produce to the market on a bicycle.
They will need big transport. The government has the resources to reach
these people, to buy and sell their crop," he says.
What are Chilwesa's expectations of the Hong Kong WTO meeting for
farmers like him?
"The goalposts change every time Africa wants to make inroads into
Europe.
Either demand for standards of products are raised which defeats the
farmers, or tariffs are raised which makes trying to sell products in
western markets prohibitive."
Chilwesa believes that rather than only looking towards western markets
to sell locally-grown agricultural commodities, the Zambian government
should acquire new technologies to transform these commodities into quality
products for export.
"The government should also build marketing institutions which can buy
and sell our produce on a cash basis," he advises.
Most of all, the government should enter into contracts with small
farmers, help them organise, and encourage them to produce more,
high-quality produce.
Mildred Mpundu is a freelance journalist in Zambia.

Original_Search_Title: Saraki Presents Budget of Human Development
Headline: Nigeria: Saraki Presents Budget of Human Development
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara State, has promised to sustain his
administration's vigorous drive in development of Agriculture, as the basis
of economic growth, employment generation and poverty reduction.
while, presenting the 2006 budget to the state Assembly, Saraki said it
is quite important that the administration focus on human development
component of its development agenda.
The budget is totalled N35.657 billion.
Titled Budget of human Development, Saraki said development of human
persons is the end goal of all other governance and development projects,
and the surest way to achieve sustainability in all other sectors.
He said the broad framework of his administration, agricultural in the
coming year, is captured under the state food security programme which is
inspired by the federal policy.
He assured that the coming year shall also give increased attention to
government's cassava initiatives, in collaboration with Arteminisin and
UBA.
Saraki said a fertilizer blending and allied products complex would be
established in the new year.
The complex, he affirmed, would located in Share in Ifelodun local
government and is to be established through private investment with an
initial estimate production of 13,000 metric tones of fertilizer per
annum.
In the social sector, Saraki assured that the renovation project of
schools which started in the outgoing year would be carried on to the new
year with additional 16 schools across the state.
An additional 360 classrooms under renovation will also be brought in
and construct 20 new ones.
"At the end of this exercise, we shall be nearing our target of 45
students per classroom by 2007 with an estimate of 70 students per classroom
in the congested schools".
He noted that, one of the most critical inputs that we have decided upon
is the provision of text books to students in our secondary
schools. Informing that, "having completed all the modalities in 2005, the
Book Revolving Initiatives for Schools (BREINS) will fully take off in the
coming year with supply of text books in core subjects: English, Mathematics
and the Sciences, to every secondary students in Kwara state".

Original_Search_Title: Another Year, Another Food Crisis
Headline: Southern Africa: Challenges 2005-2006: Another Year, Another Food Crisis
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — For several years now, Southern Africa has faced acute food shortages
that look set to continue in 2006. According to the United Nations, several
countries in the 14-member Southern African Development Community (SADC)
will still be relying on food handouts next year to prevent millions of
their citizens from starving to death.
Between 2002 and 2004 some 14 million people received emergency food
from aid agencies, according to the United Nations World Food Programme
(WFP).
Of that total, some five million remained extremely vulnerable. "The dry
spell, which decimated harvests in early 2005, has increased this number to
over 12 million in seven countries - out of a total population of 70
million," according to a 2005 WFP brief.
The question that begs asking is whether anything can be done to prevent
this situation recurring year after year.
"There are things we can do in the short term. We can grow more
drought-resistant crops like sorghum, millet and cassava," WFP spokesman
Mike Sackett told IPS. "Farmers should be less reliant on maize."
Maize, the staple food in Southern Africa, is ill-adapted to cope with
drought - something that has spelt disaster for parts of the region that
have experienced erratic rainfall in recent years.
"In the long-term there should be less reliance on rainfall and more
reliance on irrigation," said Sackett, who is based in South Africa's
commercial hub of Johannesburg. Irrigation requires a considerable
investment, however - making it unaffordable for several states. With the
exception of South Africa, Botswana and Namibia, SADC nations would probably
need donor funding to set up viable irrigation systems.
Sackett noted that the distribution of seed and fertiliser also needed
to be better managed: "For example, last year farmers received fertilisers
late."
To end the ongoing cycle of food shortages and its attendant problems,
agricultural officials in the SADC region have embarked on a comprehensive
programme to assist farmers.
"One of our roles will be to vet contracts for our illiterate
farmers. We will support them so that their contracts (such as for supplying
food to U.N. agencies) are respected. We will also help them to understand
the necessity of paying debts to avoid being blacklisted," Nelson Chisenga,
an agricultural specialist at the Pretoria-based Southern African Federation
of Agricultural Unions (SACAU), told IPS.
Earlier this month, SACAU invited about 20 delegates from six of its
member countries - Zambia, Zimbabwe, South Africa, Namibia, Malawi and
Madagascar - to South Africa's capital, Pretoria, to inform them about key
issues affecting agriculture, such as trade and marketing.
"Awareness levels among farmers are abysmally very poor. For example,
they believe that trade doesn't concern them," Ajay Vashee of the
Lusaka-based Zambia National Farmers Union told IPS. Zambia is said to have
800,000 small-scale farmers out of a population of about eight million.
"We want our farmers to look beyond our borders or even
internationally," he added. "The major problem is instilling marketing
skills We would like to get the farmers to understand the dynamics of
trade."
With this in mind, the union intends training farmers in trade
negotiations, and familiarising them with the World Trade Organisation
(WTO). The WTO is currently meeting in Hong Kong, where the agricultural
tariffs and subsidies provided by wealthy countries are coming under
discussion. These farm supports give farmers in the developed world a
considerable advantage over their counterparts from poorer nations.
Zambia is already reaping the rewards of having adopted a number of
sensible policies on farming. "Zambia did quite well in 2003 to 2004,"
Sackett said. "The country had a (food) surplus even though it had been hit
by drought."
In October this year, however, the government of President Levy
Mwanawasa appealed for emergency food aid for 1.7 million people. The WFP
says it is responding to the food needs of 1.1 million people, while the
United Nations Children's Fund is planning to address problems with water
provision and sanitation, child health, nutrition and education.
Further south, in Zimbabwe, the situation is considerably worse - with
aid agencies accusing the government of President Robert Mugabe of fuelling
famine through bad policies.
"The humanitarian situation in Zimbabwe is extremely serious and it's
deteriorating," Jan Egeland, U.N. under secretary for humanitarian affairs
and emergency relief co-ordinator, told journalists in Johannesburg Dec. 7,
after returning from Zimbabwe. "WFP is feeding two million people in
Zimbabwe. It could go to four million."
The agency has appealed for 276 million dollars to help relieve food
shortages in Zimbabwe, where AIDS is exacerbating the humanitarian
crisis.
"Some 3,000 people die of AIDS every week; there are one million AIDS
orphans. It's a serious situation," said Egeland.
Despite the drought, there is enough food in Southern Africa to meet
demand - it's just that supplies are being sent elsewhere. "Within the
region we have the market, but farmers look internationally. I think the
small-scale farmers should satisfy the regional market before looking
outside," Chisenga said, taking a different position to that adopted by
Vashee.
However, farmers claim that trade barriers prevent them from doing just
this. "For example, Zimbabwe is able to export poultry product to Malawi,
(but) Malawi is not allowed to export poultry products into Zimbabwe,"
Chisenga noted.
Visa requirements also present a headache, as do border delays. "Trucks
take, for example, long at Chirundu: it can take an average of five days. At
Beitbridge it can take a maximum of one day if your documents are in order,"
Chisenga said. Chirundu is the main border post between Zambia and Zimbabwe,
and Beitbridge the main post between Zimbabwe and South Africa.
"Beitbridge is open 24 hours a day. You drive through and reach
Churundu, and find that the border is closed. So you count that day lost -
and you queue behind the vehicles that arrived before you," he added. "The
delays at borders affect farmers a lot."

Original_Search_Title: Soludo Explains High Cost of Garri
Headline: Nigeria: Soludo Explains High Cost of Garri
Body_Text:
Central Bank Governor, Prof. Charles Soludo has said that the price of
one of Nigeria's staple foods, garri is on the increase because of export of
cassava and draught in neighbouring countries.
"Inflation has become more constant on food prices, food prices
constitute 63 percent of the consumer price index.
"Despite bumper harvest, the price of garri is higher, because we have
started export of cassava to China. This will improve the lot of Nigerian
farmers" Soludo told a gathering of journalists at a seminar in Calabar on
Friday.
He added however, that the upward trend in prices would be checked next
year because of availability of analytical data.
He said the daily analytical balance sheet being produced by the CBN
would help to closely monitor inflationary trends.
"It is not yet over, because the Nigerian economy is not yet there,
until we have an economy that can guarantee our future and the future of our
children, we cannot relent, we also need to do more farming to lesson the
pressure on garri. If we produce more of other foods, the export of garri
will not so much push the price upwards," he said.
Meanwhile, the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) will Thursday introduce a
new micro-finance policy to back-up the current banking consolidation in the
country.
CBN governor, Prof Charles Soludo dropped this hint while addressing
participants of the seventh annual seminar for finance correspondents and
business editors in Calabar on Friday.
Soludo said the new micro-finance policy was designed to give support to
efforts aimed at stabilising prices.
According to the CBN governor, stability of prices would help to reduce
volatility in the nation's economy. He said following concerted efforts to
ensure stronger banking system in the country; efforts were also in top gear
to ensure micro-economic stability to give support to the consolidation
process.
Nigeria, he said, was ranked among the ten most volatile economies among
210 countries in the world in year 2000, but that a more stable
micro-finance policy would reduce volatility in the economy.
"Inflation, government revenue and expenditure were also volatile,
investment was done on speculations, for post consolidation to be
sustainable, we need micro-economic stability," he said.
Soludo pointed out that the CBN would in the coming year, focus more on
monitoring the country's monetary policy to ensure stability.
"Next year will be an acid test for us in this direction as we are
determined to perform our key function of monitoring the monetary
policy.
"Part of this is to get handle of an analytical balance sheet, we now
get the analytical balance sheet on daily basis, it is a revolution," he
said.

Original_Search_Title: Weah's Monrovia Strongholds Condemn CDC Violence
Headline: Liberia: Weah's Monrovia Strongholds Condemn CDC Violence
Body_Text:
Monrovia — George Weah of the Congress for Democratic Change (CDC) has reportedly
denied making statements widely reported in local and international media
that he declared himself winner of the November 8 presidential run-off
Sunday night at a rally at his party's headquarters.
Dr. C. William Allen, Minister of Information in Liberia's transitional
government, told reporters Tuesday at the Capitol Building that the CDC
standard-bearer made the denial at a two-hour long meeting with Chairman
Gyude Bryant. The meeting was reportedly held at the Executive Mansion.
Yesterday's meeting followed a declaration by Weah that his CDC was
cheated in the run-off. Prior to the run-off, the CDC also claimed it was
denied victory in the general and presidential elections of October 11.
Despite his denial of his claims to be the 'legitimate' president of
Liberia, credible international news media such as the BBC and Reuter News
Agency, with the corroboration of various local Liberian media, especially
Star Radio, quoted the apparently disgruntled loser of the recent run-off
election as saying that he won the election but, because of widespread
vote-rigging, he was denied the Liberian presidency.
"I am President of this country, whether you like it or not; it will not
change. I told President Mbeki this. I repeat that I was cheated in the
elections," Weah was quoted as saying upon his return home on Sunday from
his visits with the Ghanaian President, John Kufour, and South African
President Thabo Mbeki.
Recordings of Weah's self-declaration as president of Liberia have since
been played on the airwaves of local and international stations. Observers
are eager to know if Chairman Bryant took the denial seriously.
Although the CDC currently protests the election results with ongoing
hearings at the National Elections Commission (NEC), Weah returned
surprisingly from visits with a flat dismissal of the results, regardless of
the ongoing hearings.
Not only has the pronouncement stirred up the country, but it has also
incited CDC supporters to perpetrate violent acts after the Sunday
rally.
Marauding party youths, fresh from the rally, clashed with National and
UNMIL Police, pelting them with stones and bottles. This violent
confrontation resulted in two deaths as of yesterday, serious injuries and
extensive damages to UNMIL and other vehicles. At least fifty arrests have
been made.
On Monday, a cabinet meeting was moved out of Monrovia to Kakata, 36
kilometers northeast of the capital, following intelligence from Government
security agencies that there were plans to stabilize the state.
Throughout Monday, peacekeepers remained heavily deployed around the CDC
headquarters, while helicopters hovered overhead, apparently to detect any
mass gathering of persons not easily detectible by personnel on the
ground.
Weah's denial came about the same time a key member of his party, Milton
Teahjay, appeared on a local radio station denouncing the UP's victory and
threatening to stall the inauguration of President-elect Ellen
Johnson-Sirleaf on January 16.
A CDC official, appearing on a local radio station Monday, argued that
Weah's pronouncement that he won the election was in keeping with the
party's protest that it was cheated on November 8. He also claimed Weah's
Sunday declaration was nothing new.
Minutes before Weah made his Sunday declaration, Milton Teahjay
presented him to the wildly cheering crowd as "the legitimate 23rd
president-elect." Observers say that statement, more than anything else,
incited the CDC partisans to take to the streets on Sunday night.
Many Partisans Disappointed
Since the defeated presidential candidate made the declaration, public
concern over the statement has been mounting. The former United Nations
Goodwill Ambassador has won long-standing respect among locals as a peaceful
man. But many people in Monrovia were shocked that would make such a
pronouncement, which tends to threaten their newfound democracy and the
peace and stability that the international community had done so much and
spent so much money to bring to war-torn Liberia.
For many residents of West Point, a densely populated slum community in
Monrovia, which Weah carried handily during both the October 11 and the
November 8 runoff elections, their standard bearer's pronouncement, which
triggered violence, was counterproductive to lasting peace and
security.
"I urge Mr. George M. Weah and his CDC supporters to gracefully accept
the election results and help move the country forward with its
reconstruction," says one Mr. Blamoh. The 24-year-old Liberian also
emphasized, "We have a huge task of rebuilding our country that has been
extensively destroyed by political fortune hunters over the years."
Amos B. Wesseh, 45 year-old woodcutter, said he was disturbed by the CDC
supporters' violent protests and demonstrations which, he said, tended to
mar the restoration of peace and stability in the country. "I want to
admonish Mr. Weah and his supporters to still seek the legal solutions to
their problems."
Princess Sonpon of West Point had a different view. She believed Weah
did win both the October/November elections. "Look my brother, thousands of
Liberians were at CDC headquarters everyday in Congo Town, yet the National
Election Commission reported that the Unity Party Standard Bearer,
Mrs. Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf, won the election. I'm not satisfied with the NEC
report' Ms. Sonpon pointed out.
A 77-year-old cassava seller, Madam Elizabeth Sonpon Weah, said, "Let my
son, George Weah leave this president business because, the president
business is not for small children. I want my great grand children to have
better education and jobs to take care of me in my last days" Madam Weah
appealed in anger and desperation.
A similar survey conducted at Buzzy Quarter in Monrovia yesterday
discovered that residents of that community are unhappy about CDC's
pronouncement.
An elderly man, Joseph Kolubah, a shopkeeper, noted, "Let Oppong adhere
to the election results and allow peaceful atmosphere to prevail because we
are tired of war and ardently need to rebuild our lives."
Moses Pewu, a schoolteacher, pointed out that Weah's remark was not
healthy for peace and urged him to give up his claims to the presidency. "We
are war-wearied and must do everything to abolish war and strive to move the
country forward," he said.
For his part, Tarnue Gayflor, a student, said, "If Oppong has any
problems with the conduct of the elections, let him seek redress through the
court system instead of instructing his supporters to take to the streets
and cause pandemonium while the matter is still before the court."
Also commenting on the Sunday incident, a Buzzy Quarter resident said
the arrest of over 50 people in the aftermath of the Sunday riot was
necessary to serve as a deterrent for other would-be troublemakers.
Other residents condemned what they called the "unruly behavior of the
CDC supporters. Many young people who claimed to be CDC members out-rightly
condemned the action of their colleagues and stressed the need for justice
to prevail in the case.
In New Kru Town, the opinion is divided among many residents. Nathaniel
B. Nyenkan, a teacher at the New Era School, said "Weah's statement
declaring himself president on Sunday was too naïve, because his case is
before the National Elections Commission (NEC) for investigation. So let him
wait for the ruling from NEC. This is my advice."
Thomas Nimely, a 48-year old instructor in mathematics, said "Weah's
statement was derived from the principle that 'justice delayed is justice
denied.' So, I hold the NEC responsible for Weah's statement to declare
himself president. He's right to speak out, but I am not in favor of the
chaos that came out as a result of the statement. I feel that Weah was
cheated, but I am not a troublemaker to support trouble shooting
statements."
A high school pupil, Musa Lumeh, is the CDC's junior mobilization
chairman for the Doe Community. "I don't support Weah Sunday's statement
because I am not in favor of the argument that he is the elected 23rd
president. We want the country to move forward with development, but not
with unnecessary statements that have no basis of truth," he said.
Lawrence Kofa, a 32-year old physics teacher, expected Weah to act more
responsibly since he is often referred to as a peace ambassador. "So, let
him behave in that manner rather than proclaiming himself the winner of
elections that he clearly lost. I therefore want him to remain calm to
maintain his status as a goodwill ambassador. "
For Tony R. Bartu, a businessman, Weah is entitled to his personal
opinion. "I have no misgivings with his statement, but let others not use
that to cause chaos. The police also contributed to the chaos, so they paid
the price. Notwithstanding, let the law take its course," he said.
Jerome Jackson, 37, thinks different. "The statement of Mr. Weah was
reckless, because the elections were free, fair and transparent."
An elderly man, Ansuman Sonny of the Point Four Community on the Bushrod
Island, thinks much the same way. "I did not agree with Weah because the
statement did not go down well with us in times like these when we are
yearning for peace. Let him accept it as it is since he claimed to be a
unifier"
An elderly woman who referred to herself as Ma Nancy said she is related
to Mr. Weah. She described Weah's statement as something made out of
frustration, but called on him to realize that he lost the elections.
Labor Union Frowns Upon Statement
Meanwhile, the Forestry, Logging & Allied Workers' Union of Liberia
(FLAWUL) has condemned Weah for making the statement.
FLAWUL maintained that by conducting free, fair and transparent
elections on October 11 and November 8, respectively, and results widely
accepted by Liberians and the international community, Liberia has won
victory of good over evil of war, political instability and regular killing
and displacement of the populace by day.
"It is therefore prudent for every peace-loving Liberian to accept the
results and begin to work harder for the country and put hands around the
elected government, in good faith, to develop the country and bring
improvement in the lives of Liberians," a release said.
"The awkward position taken by Mr. Weah and his advisors to gang up
hooligans, failed educated people and quark politicians to fight against the
widely accepted results of the election and impose him on the Liberian
people at all cost, has the propensity to undermine the peace and stability
of the state, erode the confidence of the international community, drive
investors away and continue to keep Liberians in the state of unemployment,"
said the release.
The release said the CDC was "gradually tugging the country into another
round of crisis." It said the Union condemned this act in the strongest
possible terms "and declared it unacceptable to workers in their totality."
The Union therefore called on the Liberian people and the international
community, including UNMIL, to take the move by Mr. Weah seriously and
condemn the act.

Original_Search_Title: Waiting for the Oil Boom
Headline: São Tomé and Príncipe: Sao Tome And Principe: Waiting for the Oil Boom
Body_Text:
Sao Tome — Beatriz Azevedo points to a woman carrying a plastic bowl of fish on her head as she wades chest deep through a river where it flows into the sea.
This river separates the coastal village of Sao Joao dos Angolares from a nearby beach where fishermen beach their canoes.
"Two men were drowned in recent months while trying to carry their outboard motors across this river, says Azevedo, the head of the local women's association.
"When the oil money comes in we are going to build a bridge here."
Everyone in Sao Tome and Principe is convinced that this small island state tucked away in the Gulf of Guinea is on the verge of an oil boom.
The US oil major Chevron has announced that it will start drilling its first offshore exploration well in block one of a Joint Development Zone (JDZ) shared with Nigeria during the first half of January.
The seismic data already gathered there is very encouraging and Total, the French oil company, has discovered a major oil and gas field just 15 km to the north inside Nigerian territorial waters.
Total's Akpo field will produce 225,000 barrels per day of oil equivalent when it comes on stream in 2008.
There are high hopes that Chevron will find a gusher of equally impressive proportions nearby.
Billions of dollars in the pipeline
Afonso Varela, the Legal Director of Sao Tome and Principe's National Petroleum Agency (ANP), can scarcely contain his enthusiasm.
"If we are lucky enough to find an oilfield with 1.5 billion barrels of recoverable reserves (similar in size to Akpo), even after sharing its revenue with Nigeria, we stand to receive about US $9 billion over a period of 25 to 30 years," he said.
That is a mind-boggling sum for this twin-island state of 140,000 people.
Sao Tome presently scrapes by on $5 million a year from cocoa exports and around $25 million a year of foreign aid.
Most of the inhabitants of this former Portuguese colony are fishermen, who brave the Atlantic waters in dugout canoes, and subsistence farmers, who slash out plots in the jungle to grow plantains, cassava and a few vegetables.
But young people are drifting away from the villages to Sao Tome city where youth unemployment is estimated to be around 50 percent and expectations that oil will provide an instant solution to poverty are running high.
Dozens of new four-wheel drive cars in the sleepy capital and a crop of luxury houses mushrooming in the posh new suburb of Campo de Milho, have convinced ordinary people that money from oil-related activities is already flowing into the pockets of the ruling elite.
Corruption could soak up the money
"I don't see how buying flash new cars can do much for the good of the people," said Olavo Vingar, who exchanges wads of grubby Sao Tome dobras for dollars and euros in Sao Tome's central market.
Carrying a calculator in one hand and a bag of local currency in the other, Vingar says he would prefer to get a proper job.
But with the minimum wage set at $40 a month, this 34-year-old man can't find anything else to do that would allow him to feed his seven children.
Lucretio Goncalves, who wanders the streets with his camera, offering to take photos of people for a dollar a picture, is equally sceptical that the politicians will spend Sao Tome's oil revenues on improving the lives of ordinary people.
"Some oil money is already coming in, but it is just benefiting half a dozen people who run the country. It doesn't reach ordinary people, just those who govern us," Goncalves said.
"I am absolutely certain that in this country, the oil money is not going to be well used," said Goncalves, who walks the streets with his camera because he is unable to find work as a stone mason.
With the World Bank's encouragement, parliament last year voted through a new law drafted by US lawyers that is supposed to ring-fence Sao Tome's oil revenues and prevent greedy politicians from diverting the money into their own pockets.
This oil revenue law is designed to ensure that all the money is spent on priority development projects such as improving the country's roads, schools and hospitals and its erratic electricity and water supply, while making sure some of it is set aside for the future in a Permanent Fund.
But legal experts say the new law will only work if the government and the courts are willing to enforce it. And some respected voices are already predicting that many of its provisions will be quietly ignored.
"Sao Tome is a state that simply doesn't function," said Pascoal Daio, an independent lawyer who is one of the pessimists. "This law is very pretty, but it is not being applied."
Daio pointed out that Patrice Trovoada, the son of former President Miguel Trovoada who has frequently served as a cabinet minister and government adviser, has accumulated huge personal wealth without having to explain the source of his riches.
And he noted that several top government officials owned shares in ERHC, a company controlled by Nigerian millionaire Sir Emeka Offor, which has been granted generous pre-emption rights in several offshore blocks in the JDZ.
"I don't understand how any government could give away such privileged rights to a company," Daio said.
Praia Banana - the government hopes to develop tourism in the twin-island state
Signature bonuses
As the public debate smoulders on about oil-fuelled corruption, some legitimate oil money is already entering government coffers in the form of "signature bonuses."
These front-end payments are trickling in as Sao Tome signs a series of production sharing agreements with companies keen to drill for oil in deep-sea waters that Sao Tome agreed in 2001 to share with Nigeria.
Earlier this year, Sao Tome received US $49.2 million as its share of the $123 million signature bonus paid by Chevron and its partners for the right to explore block one of the JDZ.
Nigeria receives 60 percent of all oil-related revenues from the formerly disputed JDZ, while Sao Tome receives 40 percent.
In the coming weeks, the two governments expect to sign production-sharing agreements with other oil companies covering a further four blocks in this broad swathe of ocean 200 km south of the Niger delta.
These contracts should trigger the payment of a further $55 million of signature bonus payments to Sao Tome in 2006.
However, government officials warn that even if oil is discovered in commercial quantities, there will be a 10-year gap before Sao Tome starts to receive large-scale oil production revenues.
Rafael Branco, the Economic Director of the National Petroleum Agency, reckons production will only start in 2012 - although Chevron says that the fast track development of block one could produce first oil as early as 2010.
Branco also warns that even when the oil does start flowing, Sao Tome will have to wait a further three to five years before it starts to receive large-scale revenues.
That is because the oil companies involved will first have to recover the capital they have invested to bring the oilfield on steam. And that sum is likely to be $2 billion or more.
Once the big inflow of oil revenues to government coffers does start - probably sometime between 2015 and 2017 - Sao Tome may well receive several hundred million dollars per year.
That would turn it overnight into one of the richest countries in Africa, at least on the basis of gross domestic product (GDP) per capita.
Santo Antonio, capital of Principe island
Foreign aid still needed
However, until then, this tiny state- the second smallest in Africa after the Seychelles - will continue to need a large injection of foreign aid.
President Fradique de Meneses, with the backing of the World Bank, is therefore asking international donors to help Sao Tome prepare for a new era of oil wealth by financing an ambitious public investment plan.
This Poverty Reduction Strategy aims to reduce poverty by two thirds by 2015.
It also aims to modernise and reform the way in which government operates, improve the country's decaying infrastructure, create a solid platform for private sector investment and stimulate economic growth of at least five percent a year.
A government study carried out in 2003 calculated that 54 percent of all Sao Tomeans live in poverty.
If implemented in full, the Poverty Reduction Strategy would lift over 50,000 people out of the poverty trap and reduce the social and political pressures that are currently building up as a result of high youth unemployment.
The government of Sao Tome asked donors at a round-table conference in Brussels on 6 December for US $169 million to finance the first three years of this ambitious plan and Prime Minister Maria do Carmo Silveira came away with immediate pledges of $60 million.
The economy has gone steadily downhill since independence in 1975 as the old cocoa estates have been broken up and abandoned and the country has run up a stifling external debt of $320 million.
That is equivalent to over $20,000 per capita, making Sao Tome one of the most heavily indebted countries in the world.
Living standards have fallen - a study by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) estimated in 1994 that only 40 percent of the population lived below the poverty line - and the country's infrastructure has started to fall apart.
Narrow roads, once tarred and cobbled, are now full of potholes, and a third of the airport runway on the small island of Principe is unusable since the government has no money to resurface it, meaning only small planes can land there.
This year, the increasingly dilapidated state of the country's water supply system and public latrines has led to a resurgence of cholera.
More than 700 people have fallen ill with this highly infectious disease since the latest outbreak began in October and over 20 have died.
The small airport on Principe needs resurfacing
Democracy provides hope
But with a new International Monetary Fund (IMF) agreement in place since August, Sao Tome is set to benefit from debt relief through the Highly Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC) initiative in 2006, and western donors appear well disposed to maintain and increase the current inflow of bilateral aid.
Diplomats say that a key factor in Sao Tome's favour is that despite rampant corruption at all levels of government, the country is at least a functioning democracy.
Elections are held regularly and no one party dominates parliament, so the government depends on a series of shifting coalitions.
There have also been three changes of president through the ballot box since multiparty democracy was introduced in 1991.
There is a strong awareness amongst senior government officials that Sao Tome cannot afford to make the same mistakes with its oil as two of its close neighbours - Angola and Equatorial Guinea.
These two states have frittered away their oil revenues on self-enrichment by the ruling elite, while little has been done to use the money for the benefit of ordinary people.
"Oil is not a solution for us," says Adelino Castelo David, the government economist charged with raising donor money for the Poverty Reduction Strategy. "It is simply a tool which can help us if we learn to use it well."
He speaks of using it to develop agriculture, where pepper and vanilla are now being promoted as an alternative to cocoa, tourism, which is still in its infancy, and the development of Sao Tome as a regional trade centre with a deep water port serving as an entrepot for other states in the region.
Acacio Bonfim, a former finance minister who now heads Sao Tome's largest commercial bank, Banco Internacional de Sao Tome e Principe, agrees.
"Oil revenues must be invested in priority areas in the fight against poverty," he said.
"Oil, in my view, is not an alternative to agriculture. Neither is it an alternative to tourism or anything else," he added.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Govt Acts to Speed Up Maize Importation
Headline: Zambia: Govt Acts to Speed Up Maize Importation
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Zambia's agriculture minister, Mundia Sikatana, says the government has decided to waive a requirement that scientists check whether duty-free maize imported from South Africa has been genetically modified in order to speed up shipments.
This follows complaints by the Millers Association of Zambia (MAZ) and the Zambia National Farmers Union (ZNFU) that the requirement to test for GM organisms had delayed maize imports meant to avert hunger.
The maize crop failed in the most recent drought and up to 1.7 million Zambians are now threatened by hunger, forcing President Levy Mwanawasa to declare a national disaster and appeal for international relief.
"There is no problem now - everything is smooth and stocks will start rolling in as soon as possible," Sikatana told IRIN, "the scientific check the millers complained about has been removed."
ZNFU complained that the "red tape" had delayed the arrival of maize stocks in the country, on top of delays at the borders, and appealed for the waiver of a 15 percent duty on imported maize to be extended beyond 31 December.
"So far, only 7,000 mt of maize has arrived in the country, out of the required - or authorised - 200,000 mt, and this is a matter of concern to us. Also, other countries, such as Zimbabwe and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), ordered maize from South Africa before Zambia [did]," Songowayo Zyambo, executive director of the ZNFU, told IRIN.
Zyambo also expressed concern that if maize imports from South Africa were delayed any further it would disturb next year's maize marketing period and distort the markets. "We don't want a situation where duty-free or subsidised maize starts competing with local maize," he explained.
The British government responded on 9 December to Mwanawasa's declaration of a national food disaster by giving Zambia a US $6.9 million grant to buy relief food for vulnerable groups.
Initially the government was reluctant to admit that Zambia was facing a critical food shortage and said the country had stocks of cassava and potatoes that could replace maize, the staple food.
Zambia last experienced a critical food shortage in 2002, when drought and floods affected different parts of the country, leaving up to three million Zambians in need of relief food.

Original_Search_Title: Smuggled Rice Floods Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Smuggled Rice Floods Nigeria
Body_Text:
It is an old maxim that doing the same thing all over again and
expecting a different result is, at best, self-delusion. The prohibition of
imported parboiled rice scheduled to take effect in 2006 and the reduced
authorised importation of the commodity this year have produced the same
results whenever government bans importation of a commodity in high demand
before adequate local production can be achieved. Invariably, smuggling
thrives. The nation loses a great deal of revenue, which would have accrued
through import duties. Simultaneously, the corruption of the staff of the
Nigerian Customs Service, NCS, escalates.
The advertorial by the Rice Millers Importers and Distributors of
Nigeria, RMIDN, in the newspapers, points to the dangers ahead. According to
RMIDN, 449,070 tonnes of parboiled rice were shipped to Benin Republic with
Nigeria as destination. Given the increased consumption of rice in December
and January, it is safe to assume that the total for the year will rise far
above that. From Benin Republic, the imported rice finds its way into
Nigeria through many routes, but the main entry points are Idiroko, Seme and
Shaki in the South West.
A nation blessed with all the resources -- land, water and people --
required for sustainable food surplus, has no business importing items it
can produce locally. Rice, with gari and bread, have become indispensable
for Nigerians. The new cassava export policy has placed gari beyond the
reach of most people. With domestic rice being more expensive than the
imported one, demand for imported rice has risen significantly. Smuggling
will ultimately strangle the fledgling local producers leaving us worse than
when we started. Nothing suggests that history will not repeat itself the
way we are implementing this policy.
Government should face reality and allow more rice to be imported
legally, while it works with domestic producers to increase output. Without
this, government will find itself losing the battle to the real
beneficiaries of its rice import ban because a lot of the rice smuggled into
the country is sub-standard and constitute a health hazard. Most
importantly, government will continue to lose revenue, the Benin Republic
and corrupt officers of the NCS will continue to profit so will the
smugglers selling inferior rice at premium price.
We call on government to review the rice import ban. Government should
also grant more import licences to professional rice dealers and consider
agricultural subsidies for rice farmers to encourage them to grow more rice
for the nation to become self-sufficient in the commodity.

Original_Search_Title: Shekarau's Drive in Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Shekarau's Drive in Agriculture
Body_Text:
One of the much-admired qualities of Malam Ibrahim Shekarau, the
Executive Governor of Kano State, is his propensity for teaching his
students, biological children and socio-political associates the lessons of
life by personal example.
He brought this characteristic into governance, and it is working fine
for the people of Kano State, and by extension, Nigeria, as the end result
is of benefits to the whole nation.
The most recent illustration of his tendency to teach by example was his
decision to join rice farming in the outgoing cropping season.
Alhaji Ado Chiranchi and Alhaji Ladan Kwantagora, the duo who present
the influential agricultural extension Hausa programme on the network of
FRCN Kaduna reported in the November 24, 2005 edition that the rice produced
by Governor Ibrahim Shekarau on his private medium-sized farm was so good
that experts from the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development
graded it and recommended that it should be packaged and released as seeds
so that other farmers could benefit from its high quality.
His interest in agriculture is fitting for a leader in a state where
over 80 per cent of the population engages in one form of agriculture or the
other.
In recognition of this fact, the Kano State government under his
leadership accorded the sector the high priority it deserved as demonstrated
in the vigorous efforts made by the government to ensure the timely supply
of critical agricultural inputs since its coming into office in May
2003.
The bold decision by the state government in 2003 to place an order for
100,000 tonnes of fertilisers worth N4 billion, which was sold to farmers at
the subsidised price of N1, 000 per bag in order to encourage them was as
revolutionary and impact-making as the decision by the same government to
introduce a formal free-feeding programme for the needy during the
month-long annual Ramadan fasting for Muslims.
So also was the purchase of 50 brand new tractors and implements for
N150, 000,000 and the refurbishing of dozens of others to assist farmers in
the state. The tractors and implements were stationed in local government
headquarters, near the farmers, for easy accessibility.
Malam Ibrahim Shekarau said in his 2004 budget speech that his
government negotiated and obtained "a soft loan of over N180 million from
the Nigerian Agricultural and Cooperative Bank (NACB) for animal traction
under the work-bull scheme."
Around 340 farmer families benefited from the scheme, he explained.
The supply of subsidised pesticides, seeds and seedlings and massive
extension services by specialists and experts from the Kano State Ministry
of Agriculture constitutes another set of incentives to farmers, all with
the sole objective of boosting food production in the state.
Just like many appreciative citizens of Kano State, this writer is aware
of and actually visited the locations of at least three irrigation sites out
of the many that were expanded in 2003/2004 to put thousands of additional
hectares of land under irrigation in different parts of the state.
Governor Ibrahim Shekarau said in his 2004 budget speech that the
government intended to open 25 new fadama areas "through the provision of
2,500 tube-wells and wash-bores, 18.5 km access roads, two nos. drilling
rigs, as well as the procurement and distribution of 1,000 units of
irrigation water pumps and accessories."
Facts on the ground confirm that the Kano State government has delivered
on the contents of the preceding paragraph ahead of schedule. This
fulfilment of promises by the government reaffirms the commitment of its top
leadership to leading by example.
The mopping-up of excess grains from farmers worth N120 million at the
end of the 2003 harvest season, and the commitment of N250 million to
purchase over 75,000 bags of assorted grains at the end of the 2004 harvest
season by the Kano State government under the watch of Malam Ibrahim
Shekarau constitute real encouragement for farmers, who perennially suffer
from high post-harvest loss, demoralising low prices and grossly inadequate
on-farm storage facilities.
With the engagement of over 10,000 farmers' associations and groups, or
at least 229 groups in each of the 44 local government areas in the state,
the mass food production programme initiative of the government, on which
some N54 million was spent, easily contributed in achieving more than the
target of producing 70,000 tonnes of grains and vegetables at the end of the
2004 cropping season. It should be expanded, incorporating additional crops,
especially sweet potatoes, cocoyam, cassava and guinea nuts.
Information garnered from various sources while researching for this
article indicates that over N20 million was invested in extending irrigation
channels around Thomas Dam near Dambatta alone. As a result, more land was
brought under irrigation, thereby boosting food production, reducing rural
underemployment and poverty, generating more income for the farmers,
enhancing their nutritional regime and generally improving the well-being of
the hundreds of farmers, grocers, transporters, marketers and consumers
involved in the chain of activities in situations of this nature.
Many watchers of the Shekarau administration marvel at how the
government so quickly from inception came into grips with the situation on
the ground and deftly went ahead to implement well-thought out programmes
not only in agriculture, but also in all other facets of human
endeavours.
They are also impressed by the fact that the government has not faltered
in any way, but has remained increasingly successful in delivering the
required support services and financing for boosting agriculture, as it
promised it would do in Kano State.
The secret lies in the volumes of reports carefully articulated by its
transition committee ahead of Malam Ibrahim Shekarau's inauguration as an
elected executive governor of Kano State.
In the lengthy section of the report that dwelt on agricultural
development in the state, suggestions were offered on step-by-step action
and policies in the immediate, short, medium and long-term periods for
agricultural development. The constraints and remedial measures, the
potentials and the work that must be done to accomplish them were all
identified - together with time frame for achieving each specific
target.
The government is relentlessly implementing those laudable ideas,
turning them into tangible benefits that improve the well-being of the
citizens in a verifiable manner, and at the same time furthering the
pre-eminent position of Kano State as a net contributor to the national
economy; in this case, through its massive agricultural production.
Rabo Makeri is a Kano-based analyst.

Original_Search_Title: We'll Consider Northern Agitation for Power - Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: We'll Consider Northern Agitation for Power - Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Being text of an address by President Olusegun Obasanjo at the 2005
national convention of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), Abuja on December
10, 2005
Protocol. I feel very delighted to welcome you all, great men and women
of our great party: the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP). As we gather here
today to reaffirm our support for and belief in the party and its declared
goals, we also show to the world that we are one happy family united in
motivation, vision, commitment, loyalty and objectives. Since its formation,
we have seen our party become stronger, bolder, larger and steadily
institutionalized.
When we say that we are the largest political party in Africa, it is not
a joke. It is the truth and we must be proud of that fact. In addition, we
have succeeded in learning from our experiences, strengthened our
institutions and structures, built discipline and mapped out an agenda for
progress, prosperity and development.
Let me use this opportunity to thank the founding fathers of our great
party for their vision, patriotism and sense of mission. I commend them for
giving to Africa such a strong and focused political party that is setting
the pace on many fronts as we collectively march towards democratic
consolidation.
Let me very briefly give you a report of our stewardship in
government. You are a"1I very familiar with what we met in 1999: a totally
dislocated, fragmented, decaying, badly governed, highly demoralised and
corruption-stricken nation and people. Not only was civil society
suffocated, but political spaces were closed and there was not much economy
to speak about. The vast majority of Nigerians relied on dubious and
extra-legal coping methods of survival to the detriment of the soul of the
nation.
Our first term in office required courage, boldness and alternative
thinking. In spite of the numerous distractions, largely the products of
demilitarisation and readjustment to democratic values and practices, we
succeeded in laying the foundation for peace, stability, security, growth,
development and democracy.
Great members of the PDP: our party never claimed that reforms would be
easy. We never said we were going to bring about change through magic. But
we did claim and we still stand by that claim that it would no longer be
"business as usual." Today, the entire country, Africa and the world know
that it is no longer business as usual in Nigeria.
I want to thank you all, including other patriotic Nigerians for their
support, suggestions, contributions, sacrifices and commitment to progress
and prosperity for all. We must continue to work harder till we get to what
I call the cruising level of growth and development.
Since our second term in office, we have transformed Nigeria. Let me
repeat that statement: the PDP has transformed Nigeria. This is certainly
not the Nigeria of yesterday and we are beginning to reap the rewards of our
sacrifices and commitment to a better Nigeria. In agriculture, we have
achieved unprecedented growth rates. We are even exporting cassava to China
and that is first in our history. We are, today, the number one cassava
producer in the world. Technology to enhance output of cassava and cassava
products has been developed and is being accessed by farmers.
Our economy is turning around with inflation gradually coming down,
employment expanding, productivity growing and poverty declining. The
manufacturing sector is experiencing a boom and investors are coming into
Nigeria in an unprecedented rate. In oil and gas, especially gas, we have
made unprecedented breakthrough.
In telecommunications we are among the top three fasted growing markets
in the world and within Nigeria, there is a telecommunications revolution
going on. The area of ICT development has not been left out and we are
working on outsourcing and software development. In the area of
infrastructure, we are working to fix failed roads, construct new ones and
work with the private sector. We have a great plan to rehabilitate,
reconstruct and expand our railway system. Our effort at building a strong
economic foundation is also informed by our support for public-private
partnership and in Transcorp Nigeria to undertake large investments within
and beyond the shores of Nigeria.
Our space programme is making progress, teaching hospitals are being
rehabilitated and reequipped with state-of-the-art facilities and we to
continue to invest heavily in security to protect lives and property. In the
area of power, we are far ahead of where we were in 1999 and we have
initiated plans that will no doubt culminate in steady and unbroken supply
of power to all Nigerians. In the area of water, we have invested more in
irrigation, dams, boreholes and water systems than at any time in the
history of Nigeria and our farmers and rural dwellers are happier for
this. We have managed the economy very well to build up robust foreign
reserve, tame inflation and interest rate and stabilize the rate of
exchange. Against all odds, we secured debt relief for Nigeria.
Distinguished ladies and gentlemen, I could go on reeling out the
achievements of the PDP government till tomorrow. There is no aspect of the
economy that we have not impacted positively. There is no Nigerian that we
have not touched through proactive and positive initiatives. There is no
community that has been left out. Even our detractors have become humbled
and silenced by our single-minded commitment to make Nigeria a better place
and by our specific achievements on several fronts. We shall not waver from
our desire to make life better for all Nigerians. This is our promise, our
commitment and our goal.
When we came into office in 1999, the challenge was how to hold Nigeria
together. Dozens of iconoclastic, extra-legal and violent organizations
dotted the political landscape. At that time, the Nigerian state was
unsteady, uncertain, weak and badly delegitimated. Today, the PDP has united
Nigeria. We have replaced fear with hope. We have replaced uncertainty with
confidence; and replaced despondency with dedication and purpose. We have
created new paths and opportunities for dialogue, tolerance, negotiation and
peaceful engagement. We have rekindled faith in Nigeria, our communities and
families. We have engendered a new morality and spirituality anchored on a
new faith in the new Nigeria that we are all striving to build and we have
shown that any day, anytime and anywhere, peace and understanding are
preferable to violence and discord. Let me thank all of you, great members
of the PDP and indeed all Nigerians, for their respective roles in this
process.
The PDP government realized that economic reforms without political
reforms cannot be sustained. We in the party recognize that democracy admits
of opposition and pressure groups. But such groups must remain constructive
and not constitute obstacles to peace and progress. They cannot hide under
the guise of being a pressure group or opposition to engage in political
rascality, lies, indiscipline, disloyalty, destabilization and
destruction. If we are to move forward as a people and as a nation, we must
emphasise discipline, patriotism, unity, nationalism, loyalty, peace,
security, stability, love and integrity as part of our core values. This is
why we complemented the economic reforms with political reforms in order to
lay the required holistic foundations for growth, development and
democracy.
Speaking on discipline and loyalty, the PDP decided to begin the reforms
with itself. We realized that every human organization requires periodic
review, self-assessment and redesign of policies and priorities. This, we
have successfully carried out and I thank the National Working Committee of
the Party, the NEC, the Board of Trustees and all members of the party for
recognizing the essence of reform and embracing the innovations with
enthusiasm. Of course, the reform of the party is only the precursor of the
much-needed political reform and sanitization in the country. It is
important that we clean up our politics in order to consolidate our
democracy and reap the full rewards of democratic politics. You will all
agree with me that only a political party that is dynamic, democratic and
disciplined can be relied upon by all Nigerians to achieve results, change
the image of Nigeria and inspire the generality of the people to greater
heights. It is only such a party that can embrace all interest groups under
a huge reliable umbrella. It is only such a party that can ensure party
discipline and loyalty while identifying persons of positive track record
for political office. We in the PDP have already embraced this perspective
and we must not get distracted from our commitment to party and
country.
Let me make a few comments on a couple of national issues that shows the
resolve of our party and government for discipline, integrity, and
sensitivity.
Our fight against corruption in all its manifestations remains
unrelenting. Let me state categorically that no one or group can distract,
discourage or divert us from our war on corruption. We have no reasons to
take too seriously the pseudo-complaints of those selfish few who do not
wish Nigeria well and whose track records are so cloudy and shady. It is
amazing that such persons are now the ones pontificating about
corruption. Let us remain focused on the war against corruption because the
benefits will be good for the entire nation.
In this regard, I want to commend the members of the Bayelsa State House
of Assembly who have lived up to their constitutional responsibilities, the
dignity of their office and the sacredness of their duties. I am very proud
of them and the PDP as a party is proud of them.
I want to commend the elders and the youths of Bayelsa state who
acknowledged the impeached governor as their son but at the same time,
condemned the shame and disgrace he has brought on all Bayelsans by his
gross misconduct. It is my sincere hope that other states that have fallen
into or may fall into the same situation will take a wise cue from
Bayelsa. I want to also thank the leadership and rank and file of the PDP
that stood firm to support the government to take all necessary action to
show that we are a party of honour and decency, good governance, integrity,
constitutionality, discipline law and order.
Finally on Bayelsa, I thank the Nigerian media for their courage and
steadfastness in upholding the honour and dignity of Nigeria and the
obligation and responsibility of the media to stand for what is noble,
dignifying, patriotic, just and soothing to the psyche and soul of a people
and country.
By the action of all these people, professions and institutions, again,
Nigeria has regained her place of honour which would have appeared tarnished
by the action of the former governor of Bayelsa State. As a member of
INTERPOL and in reaction or in answer to the mutual assistance agreement
between Nigeria and Britain, Nigeria will take the appropriate action as
required by the British authorities on this matter.
As a party, going back to 1998/99, we addressed the principle of power
shift and rotation. It was discussed extensively, debated rigorously and
agreed to even without it being included in the party's constitution or the
nation's constitution.
That spirit of discussion and dialogue which stood us in good stead then
must not now be appropriated, manipulated, compromised, contaminated or
corrupted by any individual or group no matter how highly placed. As a
party, we must resist efforts by some individuals or groups to use their
pedestrian understanding of power shift and power rotation to hold the
country to ransom. In any case, in engaging in any political discourse or
positioning, we must not use it as a cover to distort, mangle, deny, rewrite
or redirect history in very dangerous directions.
As a party, we will not substitute violence, threats, intimidation,
arrogance and lack of consultation's for dialogue, debate, accommodation,
negotiation, inclusion, tolerance and peace. I believe that credentials
which were found to be good enough and acceptable for holding Nigeria
together when the preoccupation of most Nigerians was how to prevent the
country from disintegrating, should not now be subject to question when
together, people have worked to stabilize, regenerate, and reposition the
country for unity, peace, security, stability and democracy. Let us remember
that the fabric of our unity and integration is still comparatively
fragile.
I have read with some understanding the document issued by the
South-South Assembly. What I will say to PDP members of that Assembly is
that while nobody will turn deaf ears to their well-made case and
well-marshaled points, they should allow the spirit of dialogue, debate,
tolerance, negotiation and give-and-take to prevail. When the time comes,
they should accord me, as the leader of the party, the opportunity to
interpret our policy and principles of power shift to suit the occasion
which will definitely consider the seeming agitation from the North-of-the
country.
The new PDP has a big and large enough umbrella to accommodate all
Nigerians that are willing and ready to be members of our reformed,
democratic, disciplined and people-oriented party. Let us join hands, eschew
bitterness and opportunism, avoid treachery and disloyalty, imbibe
democratic values and stand for fairness, truth, equity and justice.
From complaints in the field, we do know that the recent registration
exercise has not covered all those who wish to belong to our great party. I
saw this myself in my own ward. Therefore, immediately after this
convention, the chairman of the PDP and the National Working Committee have
a plan for massive campaign and massive registration of members. It is in
this way that we can continue to justify our claim to being the largest
political party in Africa.
Let me also use this forum to suggest that just as we have a Discipline
Committee in the PDP, we should also have a Reconciliation Committee to
mediate and sooth nerves when and where necessary.
In conclusion, let me reiterate that the PDP and the Government that
emanated from it will not be diverted from the path of rectitude and the
path of making Nigeria great no matter what the cost may be to us as leaders
of the party and government.
Today, we have cause to be proud, to hold our heads high and be happy
that as a party, we are fulfilling our promises to Nigeria and Nigerians. We
shall not compromise in our undiluted commitment to make life better for all
and make Nigeria a great nation anchored on morality, spirituality, equity,
integrity, love and fear of God. This is a promise.
I congratulate the newly elected leaders of the party from the ward to
the national level. I particularly congratulate and commend the outstanding
management and leadership of the party at the national level under the
chairmanship of Senator Ahmadu Ali. The task ahead will not be easy but with
God on our side, we will continue to make progress.
Long live the PDP
Long Live Nigeria
May God continue to bless us all.

Original_Search_Title: The Rich Also Cry: Adedoyin At 70 Regrets Going Into Banking
Headline: Nigeria: The Rich Also Cry: Adedoyin At 70 Regrets Going Into Banking
Body_Text:
Prince Samuel Adedoyin hails from Agbamu in Kwara State. Born in Lagos on December 4, 1935, he had his early life in the rich and socio-cultural setting of Agbamu. The man, a philanthropist, simple, diligent, intelligent, dynamic, shrewd and innate business-man combines these rare qualities that differentiate him from his peers in industry and commerce where he has excelled.
Prince Adedoyin, an apostle of industrialisation, is the Executive Chairman of Doyin Group of Companies, a conglomerate and foremost indigenous group of companies. He started his business venture as a petty trader, and with God's grace, prudent management of resources, commitment to excellence and resilience, is today an industrial giant.
His business interests spans manufacturing, real estate, banking, hospitality industry, the energy sector etc.
In the Adedoyin industrial empire are Stafford Chemical & Industries Ltd which produces sulphuric acid, aluminum sulphate and sodium silicate; Consolidated Foods & Beverages Ltd produces food seasoning cubes, gifts for beer, animal feeds, tea, milk, palm oil, groundnut oil, hardened palm oil, margarine, beverage.
Another of his company is Global Soap & Detergent Industries Ltd which main products include soap, detergents, glycerine & sulphonic acid, There is yet Doyin Pharmaceuticals Nigeria Ltd producing tablets, capsules, injectable, fruit juices, bottled table water, and flavoured Drinks, etc. Doyin Industries Ltd that produces Cosmetics, medicaments, toothpaste; Doyin Investments Ltd, which churns out cassava starch, corn starch, automobile/house paints; real estate developers, etc.
There is Peniel Hotel & Suites which provides accommodation and hospitality industry and City Express Bank Plc involved in commercial and international banking services then Merritts Oil Lifting and Distribution of lubricants, e.g. petrol, etc.
Prince Adedoyin virtually has his hands in almost every industrial pie and has been successful in all of these except City Express Bank.
Significantly, the Prince in his backward integration efforts, and to satisfy his raw material need as well as service other industries, has a cassava, maize and palm plantation in Kwara State, for the production of cassava starch, corn starch and palm oil. The farm provides job opportunities, and its by-products enable setting up of cottage industries in the surrounding communities. These companies, apart from effectively playing their role as good and responsible corporate citizens, also earned many awards and honours, from several organisations, bodies and government agencies for their quality products.
It also fulfils Prince Adedoyin's resolve to exhibit his group prowess in the areas of science, engineering, accounting, banking and finance, pharmacy, building, architecture, surveying, nursing, etc. This is better demonstrated in that as at today, Doyin Group is a wholly owned Nigerian company with no single expatriate staff, inclusive of its management and directors.
As a philanthropist and industrialist, Prince Adedoyin's contributions to mankind is numerous, and this is recognised and appreciated globally with several awards and honours bestowed on him by various associations, professional bodies, and government agencies. These amongst others include, Freedom of the City of London award by the Lord Mayor of London; Pan African Broadcasting Heritage and Achievement Award (Entrepreneural), National Productivity Merit Award, ECOWAS Gold award as a Renowned Industrialist, Champion of Industrial Diversification award- Standards Organisation of Nigeria, Honorary Doctorate Degree (Honoris Causa) University of Illorin , Honorary Doctorate Degree (Honoris Causa) - University of Kensington, USA, Fellow of the Nigerian Society of Engineers (FNSE), Officer of the Federal Republic (OFR), Dr. Kwame Nkrumah International award for Excellence in Enterprise.
Prince Adedoyin, strongly believe in the industrialisation of Nigeria which, if well pursued, will lead to Nigeria's economic emancipation. His contributions to the development and growth of the nation's development are borne out of a passion for industry and community services. This zeal has resulted in community development projects which includes provision of water, tarred roads, electricity, hospital, post office etc., Educational Endowment Fund for needy students, the industry, generation and provision of direct employment for over 5,500 Nigerians, excluding suppliers and distributors; economic empowerment of the teeming masses.
Undoubtedly, Prince Adedoyin's industrial activities, which are constantly expanding promote national growth and development. A devoted Christian, he is happily married with children.
At 70, Prince Adedoyin has one regret: that of venturing into banking. He was the promoter of City Express Bank which started experiencing liquidity problems mid last year and was eventually taken over by the CBN. The former Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of the bank, Mrs Sola Adeoti (Adedoyin's daughter), is said to be critically ill.
Adedoyin's pain is not just that the bank went burst but that his trusted friends were using the opportunity to demand a pound of flesh from him. According to Adedoyin, a top Presidency official who invested in City Express Bank, despite being a very close friend of Adedoyin, demanded and collected his N400 million investment in the bank, when it was obvious that things were very bad for the ailing financial institution.
After the collection of the N400 million, which was paid by Adedoyin, he went ahead to demand for interest on his money. Another friend and confidant of Adedoyin also demanded and collected his N500 million investment in the bank, this was also paid personally by Adedoyin. His travails arising from the ailing City Express Bank are many as the old man is paying through his nose. Apart from the fact that, he his now using money from his industries as well as the sale of his choice properties to offset the debts incurred by the bank, one of the former ED in bank (Mr. Shehu Jafiya) has just been appointed by the CBN to supervise the apex bank's takeover. Femi Adeoti, husband of Sola Adeoti, the former managing director and daughter of Adedoyin, was alleged to have obtained some facilities from the bank to execute some networking for MTS First Wireless. He was arrested by the EFCC and questioned over repayment terms, he was released on bail and had since left the Nigeria shores for London, the wife is also said to be critically ill but still in the custody of the EFCC. These are part of Adedoyin's headache.
While reconciliation of the debt profile of the bank's customers is currently being undertaken by the new management put in place by the apex bank, it was learnt that the total deposit base of the bank as at the time it went under was about N13.6 billion. It was also learnt that one of Adedoyin's sons, and owner of Daayad Ventures Limited, is currently helping the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) in their investigations into the N132.84 million facility obtained by his firm about three years ago to finance the importation of vehicles for an oil company, but which was never redeemed.
Adedoyin said about N700 million was passed into the account of Doyin Investment Limited through several separate accounts the group had with City Express bank. This resulted in a cumulative debt of N1.257 billion in the name of Doyin Investments which the company said it was unaware of. The group is contesting the amount it is said to be owing the bank, saying its indebtedness is not more than N485 million for which there was adequate security through the mortgage Prince Samuel Adedoyin has with Union Homes.
The company's account is alleged to have been fraudulently loaded with loan facilities to the tune of N700 million between 2002 and 2004. The company's loan exposure to the bank, it was learnt, is about N485 million. Investigation showed that the loans were contracted through six separate accounts. In the deal, N140 million was credited as loans to the company through account no 6258.
At another time, N50 million was advanced to the company through account no 4859 and another N30 million through account no 4837. Another account no 0314 carried a loan facility of N60 million which the management of the bank advanced to the company. Yet another was account no 0446 bearing a loan facility of N35 million which was opened as a loan account in favour of Doyin Investment.
Account no 6266 bearing a loan facility of N170 million was equally opened as a loan account for the company. All the six accounts add up to N485 million which the company admitted was its legal obligation to the company. But the Doyin Investment loan facility with the bank was allegedly doctored with additional facility of N700 million. This when put together add up to N1.257 billion that the bank carried in its books as loan outstanding from Doyin Investment.
Prince Adedoyin said he had protested the loans to the CBN and the Presidency, stating: "I did not take or give loans to the tune of N12 billion as reported by a section of the Nigerian media."
He said the query he raised over the spurious loans on his accounts had not elicited any reply from the management of the bank. Prince Adedoyin said his company stopped banking transactions with the bank in 2003, stating that his bank of first choice was First Bank with which he had transacted business for 39 years.
He said the facilities he took from the bank had the mandatory collateral in the form of mortgages he had with Union Homes which he pledged to the bank to help it out of its dire situation. He said it was not true that he gave out loans to his cronies and surrogates. He, however, admitted that his son was given a facility to the tune of N132.8 million to finance an LPO from Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) with a 365-day tenor. According to him, the facility given to DAAYAD Ventures Limited had five pre conditions. Prince Adedoyin said the bank did not play its role of taking direct delivery of the cars.
He said Mr. Ayorinde Adedoyin said he had paid N70 million of the N132 million loan facility. Chairman of Doyin Investment said he was happy when the CBN governor told him that CBN was taking over the bank. He said the apex bank by that action had taken a load off his neck. He lamented getting involved in banking, saying: "I did not know this is how difficult it is to manage a bank. If any one steals a trailer load of products from my factory I will know, but there in the bank if they steal one billion naira I will not know. I did not manage the bank, I was only the chairman. The only thing I ever benefitted from the bank is the car they attached to the chairman's office and the sitting allowance they paid to me. There was no dividend paid. The only time there would have been dividend, the bank said it was going for private placement which did not succeed. I am happy it did not succeed."
