Original_Search_Title: John B. Ndeh : "Regional Dev't Authorities Quicken Growth"
Headline: Cameroon: John B. Ndeh : "Regional Dev't Authorities Quicken Growth"
Body_Text:
John B. Ndeh, MIDENO Director General says the organisations also reinforce development of other ministries on the field.
How can you assess the work done by regional development agencies like MIDENO, which you manage in the country?
If government created agencies to act on its behalf to implement development programmes, it is because from experience it has been realized that the bureaucratic procedures of government departments slow down developmental processes. For instance, in the North West Province, since independence the pace of development was very slow, in spite of plausible government programmes. It is for this reason that, the government in August 1981 created MIDENO. The objective was to quicken the pace of development in the Province through helping the rural population to increase their agricultural production and productivity, agriculture being the life-wire of the economy of the area. Of course, through the implementation of MIDENO Phase I and Phase II Reformulated there has been considerable development in the province. Food crop and livestock production has increased because of the introduction of improved crop varieties and the extension of improved production technologies; portable water supplies have been realized in a number of villages; health centres, classrooms and community halls have been constructed, a number of bridges and culverts have been built, farm-to-market roads rehabilitated, etc. The two projects brought in a total of approximately FCFA 14 billion to the North West Province within 23 years. If MIDENO had not been created, it is obvious that this amount of money would not have been brought into the Province. The projects I have cited, would not have been there. The living condition of the rural population would remain stagnant.
Don't you think these agencies are duplicating the job of some ministries such as Agriculture and Rural development within the areas in which they carry out their projects?
Former MINAGRI was covering these rural areas before the rural development agencies were created. Rural Development was added to the name of the ministry to lay emphasis on development for the benefit of the rural masses. They are development organisations that cut-across, not only for food production but livestock, rural infrastructure, health, education, research etc. Further more, the decree that creates most of them indicate that, they are not only to coordinate and supervise, but reinforce, where necessary, the development efforts of various ministries in the Province.
How do you collaborate with the other development structures?
As I have just mentioned, other development structures in the Province are our partners. We reinforce their capacities to bring about the realisation of more projects and
quicker. But, we also oversee what they do with our support.
MIDENO, SOWEDA and the Mission de development de l'Océan, etc have been running projects for several years but there seem to be no major changes in these areas. Do you think the State should continue to organize development programmes through these structures?
No changes? That would be exaggerated negativism! What about the pipe borne water in villages where they would be absent without these structures? Classrooms, bridges and culverts, improved seeds and livestock breeds? What about the 1,080 income-generating activities financed with some CFA 1.4 billion micro-credit money through MIDENO? Those income-generating activities have certainly resulted in an improvement in the standard of living of beneficiaries. Many people erroneously believe that when we talk of a development project worth FCFA 10 billion, it means that it will help to pave all roads and put up sky-scrapers for people to see.
The organizations created by the State for developmental purposes have, on the most part, helped to speed up improvement in the lives of the population. They are therefore still very necessary. You see. Take this analogy. It takes two persons to bear a child, that is, a man and a woman. The State is there (the man) and there must be a woman to receive the State before a "child" is born. What I mean here is, there must be a formal structure in the region or area to tell the State what is good for the people, their developmental needs, and how to provide these needs. There must be a formal structure in the area to coordinate the activities of all the development stakeholders and many other issues.
What are the major difficulties regional development agencies, face in undertaking the tasks assigned to them?
There are many. To begin with, to change the mentalities of beneficiaries to embrace developmental activities even when planned with them is usually very slow in rural settings. The unnecessary interference of some elites and politicians with counter development activities at times confuses the rural masses. Difficulties in obtaining sufficient funds in good time for time-bound activities, salaries and logistics. Difficulties in inculcating sustainability of activities in the minds of beneficiaries. Problems of inadequate access to up-to-date communication technology (e.g. internet, power projectors, cameras, etc) to facilitate the quick dissemination of information to beneficiaries and other stakeholders in the development process.
What solutions are you seeking to such difficulties?
I have said that the funds allocated are usually insufficient and there are delays in the disbursement of funds. We, for example, are dealing with the Cameroon Government and the African Development Bank (ADB). With the Cameroon Government, the funds are sometime not available when needed, while with the ADB, the disbursement procedures are quite cumbersome and there is little one can do. However, an important solution lies with the Government in recognising that its contribution to the projects is of vital importance for the success and should therefore do everything possible to give priority to it.
As the supervisory authority of GP-DERUDEP, what is new since the 1st ADB disbursements for the project?
Many things have been done; and even before that disbursement that came in June, a lot of things were already being done. For instance, the project sought and obtained the Bank's approval to pre-finance certain time-bound activities like seed multiplication, and therefore before the first disbursement, Irish potato seeds (Pre-Basic) were already being produced by IRAD. The same holds true for UNVDA Ndop for basic seeds in rice. The project also jointly organized a national Technical Launching workshop with Resource Persons from the ADB. This workshop brought together all the partners, both Provincial and National. Draft Protocol agreements were equally prepared and sent to the Bank for examination and approval, and many other things. All these were done before the first disbursement by ADB.
Start-up workshops were also organized throughout the province. More seeds were added to rice and potatoes. They include maize, beans, cassava cuttings. Community Development Assistants were trained and are about being posted to their various areas of service. The Irish Potato Farmer Leaders in the Province and other stakeholders in the crop have also had some training workshop to identify the problems and come out with lasting solutions. A good number of tender documents have already been prepared and sent to the Bank for approval. An ADB Supervision Mission has already been received in the project.
Some people say that MIDENO has outlived its usefulness with the birth of the Grass field project?
Those who think that MIDENO has outlived its usefulness do not know the original objective of the Authority. We should therefore remind them that MIDENO was created and charged with the overseeing and supervising of the implementation of development projects approved for the North West Province. They should also seek to know the difference between a project with a defined lifespan and an organization or authority like MIDENO with defined objectives for the region and which does not have a stated lifespan.
Others talk about your overbearing influence in the management of GP-DERUDEP.
That is definitely not true. I have nothing to do with the day-to-day management of GP-DERUDEP. Rather, like a good father who is interested in the welfare of the child, I effect regular supervision missions to the project to make sure that they are on the right track and that is the role defined by the project document for MIDENO. People, should not be confused with my personal relationship with the Project Coordinator and MIDENO and the Project. They are two different issues.
What makes GP-DERUDEP the project of hope for the rural masses?
It has a special design, methodology of implementation; its estimated cost is FCFA 15.7 billion, its life-span shall be longer, six years; 51% of the total cost will be spent to provide infrastructure, such as water supplies, community halls, classrooms, farm-to-market roads, rural market stores, etc. All these, added to the ambitious activities envisaged under the Agricultural Production component of the project, make it really a "Project of Hope". But I will be quick to say here that it will succeed to the satisfaction of all the rural masses only if funds flow uninterrupted, all the stakeholders play their respective roles and the management of the project is allowed to implement the project the way it was designed.

Original_Search_Title: FG Generates N13bn From Onne FTZ, Escapes WTO Sanctions
Headline: Nigeria: FG Generates N13bn From Onne FTZ, Escapes WTO Sanctions
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Government realised the sum of N12.84 billion from the Onne
Free Trade Zone, Onne Port, Rivers States, in the first six months of this
year.
It also generated the sum of N5.41billion through the operations of the
Nigerian Custom Service at the port while another N752.14million was
generated through the Nigerian Ports Authority at the same port within the
same period of time.
Similarly, the country has cleared its arrears of duties to the World
Trade Organisation. The arrears, arose as a result of the failure of the
country to pay its duties to the world body since 2001, but wih the latest
development,Nigeria has eventually saved itself from facing stiff sanctions
from the World Trade Orgaization.
Minister of Commerce, Ambassador Idris Waziri, disclosed these in Abuja
on Friday at the Annual Ministerial Press Briefing organised by the ministry
of commerce in conjunction with the ministry of Information.
Revenue profiles of government agencies located at the Onne Port have
increased within the last one year due to increased activities of the
enterprises operating in the zone.
Waziri who acknowledged that the Federal Government campaign for the
export of cassava derivatives said commodity export have created a shortage
in both local and foreign market needs thereby leading to sharp increase in
prices.
He charged private investors to explore avenues to invest in the
production of cassava in the country, noting that current demands especially
from China could not be met this year and had been shifted forward to
2006.
My recent visit to China also proved to be worthwhile as our cassava
chips would now attract $170 as against $110. Also, the Chinese Cassava
Starch Processing association has decided to visit Nigeria on a cassava
investment and trade mission in January 2006.
These give a clear indication that a cassava based industrial revolution
has been set in motion by the Federal Government through the presidential
initiative on cassava.
Waziri said the Federal Government approved the payment to the WTO to
save Nigeria the shame of losing certain benefits from the international
body.
Waziri had a week ago raised alarm that Nigeria may be de-listed as
executive member of World Trade Organisation (WTO) if it refused to pay up
her membership due. The MINISTER had warned that Nigeria cannot afford to
lose the Vice Chairmanship position as it lost the slot for the post of
Deputy Director General of WTO urged the Minister of Finance to release
money for the payment of the due.

Original_Search_Title: How to Break the Deadly Cycle of Hunger, Aids And Poverty
Headline: Africa: How to Break the Deadly Cycle of Hunger, Aids And Poverty
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The HIV-Aids pandemic has virtually devastated agricultural activity
in sub-Saharan Africa, observes Curtis Abraham who argues that good
nutrition is the very foundation upon which progress is achieved
This year's World Aids Day was marked yesterday, and as in the past, staggering statistics were highlighted.
What's clear today is that HIV/Aids in Africa is increasingly being
viewed alongside the twin crises of hunger and poverty. But although
the eradication of hunger and poverty are the number one priority
issues on the list of the United Nations Millennium Development Goals,
the topic of malnutrition is only talked about, it seems, when
associated with headline-grabbing famines or some major refugee crisis.
But when these humanitarian crises fade from the media spotlight,
malnutrition - chronic malnutrition - often remains.
Good nutrition is the cornerstone, the very foundation upon which sound socioeconomic development is achieved.
Therefore, it is the best investment African governments and others
in the developing world can make. They must realise that the vitamins,
minerals, fats, proteins and carbohydrates their populations have
access to is inextricably linked to their country's Gross Domestic
Product (GDP).
The statistics on hunger and malnutrition in sub-Saharan Africa is frightening.
An estimated 40 per cent of Africa's poor are undernourished and
about 42 per cent of African children are stunted, while 30 per cent
are underweight.
Sadly, this situation looks set to worsen. A recent report by the I
n t e r n a t i o n a l Food and Policy Research Institute (IFPRI) says
that if current trends continue, child malnutrition in Africa is
expected to grow from 38.6 million to 41.9 million by end of 2005.
The statistics on diseases and death are equally alarming. Severely
malnourished children are eight times more likely to die than their
well nourished counterparts.
Malnutrition-associated deaths account for 55 per cent of children
below five years of age in the developing world. A lack of a proper
diet not only increases the likelihood of contracting a debilitating or
fatal childhood disease such as measles, acute respiratory infections
and diarrhoea, but the severity and duration of these ailments are
further exacerbated by poor nutrition.
Why is there a perennial need for food aid in sub-Saharan Africa?
The HIV-Aids pandemic is a major factor. An estimated 80 per cent of
Africa's population depends on agriculture for their livelihood.
However, agriculture on the continent has fallen on hard times.
The HIV-Aids pandemic, for example, has virtually devastated
agricultural activity in sub-Saharan Africa. According to a recent
report by the System-wide Initiative for HIV/Aids and Agriculture based
at the Africa Rice Centre, folks were switching from cash crops such as
coffee to less labour intensive ones such as cassava and maize.
Moreover, these crops are not only less profitable but are also less nutritious than those cultivated in normal times.
The reasons for this is that the most productive generation is dying
off, leaving the elderly and orphans who are untrained in farming
skills, let alone in life-skills.
In Rwanda, for instance, there has been a 60 per cent to 80 per cent
drop in farm labour. In the west African country of Burkina Faso, 20
per cent of rural families reduced their agricultural work or had even
abandoned their farms altogether because people had died or were dying
from HIV-Aids.
In Kenya, agricultural activity on cultivated land in some areas are said to have experienced a 68 per cent decline.
Cruel and vicious
It's a cruel and vicious circle since, on the one hand, HIV-Aids
sufferers need good nutrition to maintain good health and the prospects
of a longer life. But for this to happen they need a variety and
abundance of nutritious foods, which they can only get through
productive agricultural activity. Frail bodies weakened by
opportunistic infections are unable to do any strenuous digging.
Furthermore, African women play a major role in the continent's agriculture.
In most parts of the continent it is they who are the chief source of agricultural labour.
But it is the African women who are bearing the brunt of the Aids
crisis and because of this food production has decreased. Furthermore,
women are also the caretakers of those infected with the deadly virus.
African populations facing chronic hunger and under-nourishment also put themselves at risk of contracting HIV.
During times of hunger, due to drought or famines or simply chronic
food shortages, young African women, mainly teenagers, will prostitute
themselves simply to earn enough money to feed their families.
This practice is called seasonal prostitution and it is what has been taking place in Niger recently.
Another major development in recent years has been the
under-investment in agricultural research and development. Starting
from the 1950s up to recent
times, it was World Bank lending that helped agricultural development in sub-Saharan Africa and elsewhere.
However, the rise of Non-Governmental Organisations over the last
generation has exerted pressure on donors to broaden the development
aid agenda to include such "special interest" issues as gender rights,
the environment, pastoralist issues and so forth. This has effectively
toppled agriculture off its anointed pedestal.
Africa does indeed grow enough food to feed itself. In fact, the
UN's World Food Programme procures a considerable percentage of food
from Africa.An estimated 28 per cent of WFP's commodity procurement
comes from low-income countries mainly in sub-Saharan Africa and the
Asian subcontinent, ironically two areas of the world where hunger and
malnutrition are rife.
Very often where people are hungry, it is not so much because of a
lack of available food as lack of purchasing power or lack of access to
food such as poor infrastructure and poor food distribution process.
Unfortunately, African countries can't afford to import food, which would make up for any shortfall in domestic production.
The rich industrialised countries are destroying Africa's climate
through the emission of green house gases, which causes global warming,
and which is further weakening food security on the continent.
In fact, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, a consortium
of several thousand independent scientists, says that nutritional
status is a direct result of climate change on the African continent.
Subsistence farmers are increasingly vulnerable to the vagaries of
Mother Nature particularly in the forms of floods and droughts, and
because of global warming such natural hazards are not only becoming
more frequent but also more severe in their ferocity over the last
generation.
Bittersweet offering
The final Gleneagles communiqué at this year's eagerly awaited G8
Summit of the world's richest countries was a bittersweet offering for
Africa. Yes, there was an increased pledge of aid to the African
continent to the tune of $25 billion, more than a doubling of 2004
levels. But because of US obstinacy there was little to no movement on
tackling both the speed and consequences of global warming.
This additional cash is at risk of being entirely consumed by the
hunger which will be created by the change in Africa's climate.
That under-nutrition in Africa and elsewhere is still not taken as
seriously as other aspects of development presents a serious problem.
Years of nutrition advocacy appear to have had only limited impact.
Apart from feeding the hungry being the right humane thing to do,
African governments and international development agencies still do not
recognise the importance of nutrition in their policies and strategies.
The question begs to be answered: why is it so difficult for them to
see that better nutrition brings about socio-economic development and a
way out of poverty?
This neglect has largely to do with the section of the population
whom it affects the most, children. Furthermore, in the still largely
male dominated African societies children are seen as the
responsibility and domain of women.
Add to this the fierce competition among various interest groups for
limited financial resources and the fact that there is a woeful lack of
co-ordination between the different government ministries whose
mandates include nutrition issues.
Increased military expenditure, especially as a result of military
adventurism, is also to blame for a rise of malnutrition on the
continent. Military adventurism by African governments drains resources
from education, particularly when it relates to adult female literacy,
which is about 58 per cent.Why is this significant?
Well, according to experts, food availability and female education
are the two most important factors in reducing child malnutrition.
Prison of poverty
The lack of proper nutrition is a vicious circle; without the
essential macronutrients (carbohydrates, proteins and fats) and equally
important micronutrients (iron, iodine, vitamin A, etc.) the human
body, particularly the brain, cannot grow and develop normally.
Young children with abnormal brains due to malnutrition tend not to
do well in school. With a lack of formal education, children in
developing countries will find it difficult to escape the prison of
poverty. It is common
knowledge today that a child's ability to concentrate in the classroom is directly linked to proper feeding in the home.
The most basic kind of malnutrition is called protein energy
deficiency. This is a diet lacking in energy because of a lack of all
the major macronutrients such as carbohydrates, fats and proteins.
Equally dangerous to the human body is micro-nutrient malnutrition, which is a shortage of one or more vitamins or minerals.
Micronutrient deficiencies are ranked eighth among the top 10 risks
to health worldwide according to the World Health Organisation.
Vitamin A is contained in foods such as carrots and is essential for good eyesight.
But at the moment an estimated 21 million people are at risk of
vitamin A deficiency. At its worse, a lack of Vitamin A causes some
500,000 children globally to lose their eyesight annually.
More alarming is that 70 per cent of these perish within one year of losing their vision.
To its credit, Vitamin A capsules are now on Uganda's list of
essential drugs, and guidelines for vitamin A supplementation are
included in the National Standard Treatment Guidelines.
Iron deficiency plagues as many as 4-5 billion souls or 66-80 per
cent of the world's population, and an estimated 2 billion people, or
more than 30 per cent of the world's population, are anemic.
However, some African countries have rolled up their sleeves and
have got down to the task of keeping hunger and malnutrition at bay.
Many of their initiatives have had remarkable success and provide
models for other countries to emulate.
It's been universally recognised that good nutrition starts while
the baby is still in its mother's womb. Malnutrition suffered in the
womb can lead to a predisposition to non-communicable diseases such as
hypertension, coronary heart disease and diabetes in later life.
Some African countries have realised the health benefits to both
mother and child of breastfeeding and are in the process of
establishing programmes to promote it.
School feeding programmes, which aim to encourage increased rates of
enrolment and attendance, reduce dropout rates and alleviate short-term
hunger, have been remarkably successful in getting poorly fed children
to show up for classes.
In Ghana's school feeding programme has steadily increased the ratio
of girls per boys enrolled in schools from 0.65 in 1998 to 1.00 in
2002, totally closing the gender gap. Why is this significant?'
Well, as more females attend school the less likelihood their
children will end up malnourished. Better nourished girls are more
likely to remain in school and thus learn more.
They will miss fewer days to sickness and pay better attention in
the classroom. This in turn will improve their employment prospects as
adults as well as give them greater control over their sexuality, which
relates to family planning and preventing the spread of HIV-Aids.
Even in countries emerging from years of armed conflict such as
Angola, the issue of hunger and malnutrition has become a major
priority for the government.
In Angola, war, population displacements and landmines have affected
people's access to cultivable land and thus have resulted in very
limited diets resulting in pellagra, a particularly nasty ailment whose
symptoms includes dermatitis, diarrhoea and dementia and which can lead
to death.
However, WFP in recent discussion with the Dos Santos administration
has agreed to make nutrition for school children a priority issue and
has in principal agreed to increase financial and operational
responsibility for school feeding over the next two years.
Furthermore, in a new initiative, a local miller working in
co-operation with WFP is set to produce fortified maize meal (the
staple of many sub-Saharan African countries) which will support local
enterprise and promote sustainability of fortification in the country.
Food fortification campaign
Recently, Nigeria embarked on a nationwide Vitamin A food
fortification campaign in conjunction with Unicef which aims to have
iodine included in domestically manufactured consumables such as salt
so as to effectively combat diseases such as goiter that are caused by
iodine deficiency.
The ingredients for success in the fight against hunger and
malnutrition in sub-Saharan Africa and elsewhere are not unknown:
stability, democracy and freedom, political will by African
governments, dedication and commitment on the part of donors (partners
in development is the euphemism used these days) makes for a good start.
Add to this the development of sustainable rural and urban food
systems, nutrition education, school feeding programmes, and food
fortification campaigns can make considerable inroads in the fight
against malnutrition.
Curtis Abraham is a writer on development issues in Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Rice Farm Project of SLPP in Partnership With LCIP
Headline: Liberia: Rice Farm Project of SLPP in Partnership With LCIP
Body_Text:
People need to work for a living. People need paid work. Basic needs of
this are obvious, and we largely take it for granted that the socio-economic
development of the agriculture sector, that we enjoy will provide the many
work opportunities required.
Work brings social order and wealth. Most of us have had a selection of
different jobs over the years, and we regularly raise families with the
ethic that they will eventually enter the work force. But who ought to
provide the work? Ah, there you are with a major issue for the future of our
existing economic models. Ultimately, of course you, yourself ought to
provide the work.
Some of us are really increasingly and persistently refuse to undertake
the lower paid or more manually demanding work that is essential for the
well being of our society.
Well, look at the case with a farming group that has exemplified the
impact of the agriculture sector, by working along side a non-governmental
agriculture organization, the Sustainable Livelihood Promoters Programme
(SLPP) based in Vonzula, Garwula District, Grand Cape Mount County,
northwestern Liberia.
Mr. Daniel Waritay, executive director of SLPP, explained to me that his
NGO is a recipient of a sponsorship from Liberia Community for
Infrastructure Programme (LCIP), for the rehabilitation of agricultural
activities in Porkpa District, Grand Cape Mount County. The direct
beneficiaries involved are 105 ex-combatants and 45 war affected
persons.
He said the beneficiaries were residents of four communities including
Damballa, Boesan, Guassey and Bendeja; together they developed 30 acres and
planted lowland rice, tuber crops and vegetables. Current sales from the
vegetable component of the project, Mr. Warity revealed have netted over
twenty thousand Liberian Dollars. "The other crops, which the communities
cultivated such as rice and cassava will soon be ready for harvest," he
boasted.
Gbessay Mabandi, a beneficiary farmer in the group, disclosed that the
rice and cassava harvest would be a profitable one and that is the reason
why she is, on behalf of the group requesting rice mill and cassava
processing machine to boost food production so that their income would be
increased.
These farmers in order to manage their financial resources so well, set
up a committee to make sure that funds accrued from the harvests are used
for development purposes that will benefit the four communities.
Cecelia Dagoseh, another beneficiary is confident that their project
greatly enhanced the agricultural potential of the people of the four
communities. They have learnt skills through practical application at the
project site; and she is certain that those skills would be applied on her
farm and in her backyard garden activities.
Certainly the project has not only increased the farmers' desire to go
into lowland rice production but it has also greatly contributed to the
peace and reconstruction process in Porkpa District as has been observed by
an elderly man.
Mr. Sumo Z. Lapelleh, who is serving as the FAO National Consultant in
Grand Cape Mount County, told me his organization was teaming up with SLPP
with intentions to develop more swamplands to attract farmers. Sumo himself,
through his efforts, has organized women groups in Garwula-Tombe, Porkpa and
Gola Konneh Districts to be trained in vegetable production.
In Tienii, which is the district headquarters of Tewor, Sumo put his
extension machinery into motion with another women group about 5,000 who are
now engaged in cassava and peanut production.
The worth noted efforts of the women do not only exist in Tienii alone,
where sixty acres of land, fifty in cassava and ten in peanuts production,
but also in other 13 communities within the district, where fifteen acres of
land, ten for cassava production and five for peanuts cultivation. Rapidly
there are similar anxieties expanding in some communities outside the
district.
Satta Fahnbulleh Balo is the leader of the group in Tienii and she said
the idea of going into mass agriculture production was firstly perceived at
one occasion during a citizen development meeting held in Tienii. She said
the aim is to use the proceeds generated from sales, for some development
projects such as the construction of guesthouses, clinics and other
infrastructure that will benefit the entire Tewor District.
In an effort to generate the needed funds, membership due ranges from
ten to twenty-five Liberian Dollars. The due is paid on a monthly
basis.
Another community of Mr. Lapelleh's concentration is Perry farm, where
Amos Kahkia, who is working as chairman of a group of 60 workforce
comprising of 40 men and 20 women. They are undertaking self-help vegetable
project with the aim of constructing an assembly hall in their
community.
Mr. Kahkia's group that is mainly war affected persons and returnees are
divided into ten smaller groups. Each group works two days per week on the
vegetable project. They use the rest of the days in the week to do
individual farm work.

Original_Search_Title: Unido Formulates Master-Plan for Cassava Sector
Headline: Nigeria: Unido Formulates Master-Plan for Cassava Sector
Body_Text:
Abuja — The United Nations Industrial Development Organisation (UNIDO) plans to
hold a stakeholders forum to work out a master-plan for the cassava sector
in Nigeria. The forum is holding at the UN Building at the Central Area
between December 7 and 9.
According to a statement from UNIDO representative in Nigeria, Mr. David
Tommy, the programme is building on the presidential initiative, which was
established to exploit Nigeria's comparative advantage to expand cassava
production.
"The initiative seek to develop and satisfy the huge untapped domestic
market potential for cassava utilisation in the food, feed and
agro-industrial sector as well as build an export industry that can earn $5
billion a year in value-added cassava-derived exports within the next five
years," the statement said.
Experts have been recruited to carry out an assessment study on cassava
processing that will identify the comparative advantage of the zones and
regions of the country.
The programme is a part of UNIDO's country service framework for the
country. The workshop is attracting policy makers, beneficiaries, the
private sector and donor agencies.

Original_Search_Title: The Gun, the Grasshopper And the Terrorism of Editors...
Headline: Uganda: The Gun, the Grasshopper And the Terrorism of Editors...
Body_Text:
Nairobi — On the morning of Saturday November 19, I nearly committed an act of
terrorism. But for fortune, I suppose I would by now have been facing the
military court martial, with very little sympathy from the outside world,
since my prosecutors would be counting on the support of the all-powerful
Bush administration, which in its war on terrorism welcomes the prosecution
of any terror suspect around the world.
It started as a normal morning journey to the office, from the Ntinda
suburb of Kampala through Kamwokya and Kololo to the Monitor offices in the
Industrial Area -- driving fairly fast due to the light Saturday traffic. It
was in the Kamwokya sector of my journey that I nearly killed an armed
policeman.
Kampala is the sort of city where even upmarket residential areas have
their share of bush and cassava plots. It was in one such overgrown plot
that the policeman had joined some people to hunt the seasonal edible
grasshoppers called nsenene.
He had probably been posted to cover a section of the road, part of the
heavy police presence all over Kampala these days, ever since one retired
colonel returned from 'temporary residence' in South Africa and got himself
arrested over charges the government calls criminal and the opposition calls
political.
Anyway, the good policeman was doing what he is trained to do - chasing
a fleeing suspect. This time the suspect was green in colour, weighed about
10 grammes and was flying away through the low scrub, ignoring the
policeman's order to stop and be eaten.
The suspect crossed the road first, which did not bother me because even
if I hit him or her ( it is difficult to tell the gender of the little
beasts unless you are an entomologist) my car would not be dented. Then the
policeman came rushing after him/her. I slammed the brakes on and was thrown
forward against my seatbelt.
The policeman also stopped himself immediately. Smartly, he refused to
look in my direction and turned back to the overgrown plot, his hand on the
strap of the submachine gun slung over his shoulder. My eyes connected to
the strap, through my brain and to my aching shoulder where another strap
was cutting through my shirt and flesh. At least I now knew those seatbelts
really work. I stepped on the accelerator and proceeded with my journey, the
sweat breaking out on my forehead at the thought of having nearly killed a
man. The dashboard clock said 9.00 am.
During the rest of the journey to the office, I tried to imagine what
would have happened if I had hit the man with the gun. The newspaper
headlines that morning were about a group of people who had been charged
before the military court, though they were not soldiers, over an alleged
offence related to guns. So suppose I had hit the policeman and injured him,
then done the natural thing and put the injured policeman and his gun in my
car and taken him to hospital? Suppose, in a bid to save his job, the
policeman had denied he was chasing nsenene and insisted that I had hit him
while he was on duty at the roadside? Whom would the security people
believe, between a newspaperman and a policeman?
The night before, the security forces had searched the Monitor offices
for posters for a fundraising drive for legal expenses of the jailed,
retired colonel whom the state does not love. In a way, the security arm has
this tendency to believe that the Monitor as an institution supports the
colonel's bid for the country's presidency. So after killing/injuring a
policeman and putting his gun in my car, what would be the chances of not
being linked to the other guys who are charged with terrorism and being
linked to the jailed colonel?
When people begin an insurgency, they often attack lone policemen and
take their guns. The prosecution could easily conjure up a conspiracy to
have a man remanded for a year before trial on an offence that attracts the
death penalty.
See how delicately poised your personal freedom really? It takes on daft
cop chasing one nsenene when the insects are in season and a cup containing
a few hundred costs just over one dollar. By chasing this one measly insect
instead of parting with a dollar for a nutritious, filling snack, the
miserly fellow almost had me charged with a capital crime. Not nice-
Joachim Buwembo is managing editor of The Monitor of Kampala.

Original_Search_Title: Seed Bank to Protect Plants From Extinction
Headline: Uganda: Seed Bank to Protect Plants From Extinction
Body_Text:
Kampala — SCIENTISTS at the National Agriculture Research Organisation (NARO) have
developed a gene bank to avoid the erosion and extinction of Uganda's
indigenous plants and genetic resources.
The gene bank, stationed at Entebbe Botanical Gardens (EBG), is the
country's first research centre and will store seeds of a variety of
indigenous plants and crops.
"In case such plants disappeared from Uganda, farmers would be able to
get the original seeds from the gene bank and plant them," John Mulumba, the
Eculator of the Botanical Gardens, said recently.
He said that before being taken to the bank, the seeds would be first
tested to ensure that they are pest- and disease-free such that they are
able to stay for 400 years.
Mulumba said they are to concentrate on storing seeds of indigenous
plants that face a high risk of vanishing from the country. He said some of
the genetic resources, like small indigenous tomatoes, egg plants, beans,
cassava, mangoes and bananas are being replaced by new species, mainly due
to the effects of pests and diseases.
"Activities like clearing forests, reclaiming wetlands, poor
agricultural practices and the use of toxic fertilisers have contributed to
the loss of soil fertility and the disappearance of Uganda's indigenous
crops and plants. When land degrades, it can't be able to hold the
vegetation and the nutrients needed for the growth of a plant," said
Dr. Dennis Kyetere, the Director General of NARO, while launching the gene
bank recently.
"The preservation of indigenous crops and plants will ensure food
security in the country and also help Ugandans to retain the medicinal
plants that have played a vital role in the treatment of various diseases,"
he added.
Mulumba said the establishment of the Botanical Gardens has enabled
indigenous crops like the Robusta coffee originally grown for ritual
purposes to rage on until now.
"Arabica coffee was first introduced in the Botanical Gardens in the
1900s and it gained commercial importance in the cooler areas of Bugisu," he
said.
He said the EBG was established in 1898 with the main objective of
studying plants of potential value to Uganda.
Several species from tropical Africa that still rage on include coffee
canephora, coffee congensis, coffee liberica and coffee stenophyll.
"All these can be seen depicting interesting characteristics in the
gardens," said Mulumba.

Original_Search_Title: South West : Farmers Instructed to Produce, Sell Seeds
Headline: Cameroon: South West : Farmers Instructed to Produce, Sell Seeds
Body_Text:
The Agriculture Research Institute, IRA, has opened a new chapter in Agro Research - that of encouraging farmers to consider seed production for marketing.
A cross- section of farmers from the six Divisions of the South West were at IRA Ekona near Buea recently. The research Institute opened its doors to explain to farmers the potential benefits that seed production reserves for those who venture into it. "Even more than food-crop, selling seeds can be profitable because the producer can market seeds twice in the season", a research expert explained in IRA.
IRA's open day took visitors to the seed multiplication laboratory, the grafting unit and the trial and demonstration farms in Ekona.
While at the farms visitors were taught that one-hectare of land could contain 50,000 plants of maize at 8 centimetres demarcated rolls. Most importantly, researchers explained that maize does not grow well in water-lock sites.
They insisted that in order to reap full benefit from their labour the farmers must consider the type of seeds they sow in the farms. IRA has developed improved yam, cassava, cocoyam, and maize seeds. The Institutes' problem remains the vulgarisation of its results. "That is why the HIPC project extended its assistance to IRA some three months ago so that farmers could acquire good seeds and seedlings developed through research". Mrs. Poubon Christine, HIPC support project head explained. On hand to grace the occasion were IRA's Director, Jacob Mbua Ngeve, Dr. The Charles of IRAD Nkolbissong Yaounde and the South West Provincial Delegate of Rural Development, Mr. Leku.

Original_Search_Title: Micro-Credit to Be Channeled Through Community Banks - Kpakol
Headline: Nigeria: Micro-Credit to Be Channeled Through Community Banks - Kpakol
Body_Text:
THE National Coordinator (NA) of the National Poverty Eradication
Programme (NAPEP), Dr. Magnus Kpakol has said that existing Community Banks
across the nation would be designated Micro Finance Banks and would become
outlets for channeling micro-credit from the federal government and
commercial banks to the poor at the grassroots. He said in Abuja, at the
weekend that the launching of the new micro-credit scheme by the Central
Bank of Nigeria (CBN) at the middle of next month would commence the
implementation of the Multi-Partner Matching Fund which would attract
resources from NAPEP, Commercial Banks, states and local government, as well
as, major private sector operators.
His words, "the CBN will launch the micro-finance scheme mid next month
and we will be their grassroots partners. We also will help them to generate
funds to put into the micro finance banks through our market partners.
"We have told the governors, and said governors, you bring a certain
amount and we bring matching fund and then get commercial banks to also
raise funds for credit to people in those states as part of our on-going
partnership with the private sector operators.
"We have got United Bank for Africa (UBA), Zenith, Oceanic, Trade Bank
(which is now part of Afribank), Global Bank (now part of Intercontinental),
and Equatorial Trust Fund. These are our commercial partners in the
Multi-Partner Matching Fund Scheme for Micro Credit.
"And then we will bring in Local Governments. This can generate N2
million and upward per state to put into these Micro Finance Banks that we
will creating, along with others. This is new. The money will be tapped
through cooperatives. From commercial banks will give funds to the micro
finance banks which will pass the credit to the poor through cooperative
organizations.
"We will be working with the cooperatives on how to qualify for the
credit. We have to be sure that adequate arrangement is put in place, the
kind of activities in which to put te money, for instance the, the
Presidential Cassava Initiative. There must an effective arrangement for
repayment by those who receive credit facilities, otherwise, the whole thing
would collapse.
"NAPEP will work with those at the grassroots to build capacity among
those who receive credit, by helping them to identify organizations to sell
their produce to, as well as, the kind of high yielding species to grow in
other to ensure maximum output. The poor person in the village does not know
this kind of thing. That is the job of NAPEP.
"We want to build NAPEP into a repository of knowledge. A true knowledge
of how to fight poverty, so that NGOs, local governments and other
grassroots organizations could get that kind of value from us".
He stressed the need for micro-credit for poor members of the nation if
the people were ever to move up on the economic ladder, as no amount of
provision of infrastructure facilities could really remove poverty without
an avenue for individual income generation. His word, "if you put an
infrastructure for the people at the grassroots without a visible means of
income, that infrastructure will definite collapse. If the people don"t have
an income to fix the pump, when the pump breakdowns, for instance, it
becomes what in Economics, we call "the tragedy of the commons".

Original_Search_Title: Repatriation of Nigerians Not Connected With Bakassi
Headline: Cameroon: Repatriation of Nigerians Not Connected With Bakassi
Body_Text:
The Nigerian Consul General to Northwest and Southwest Provinces,
H.E. John Abhulimen, has debunked rumours that the repatriation of his
compatriots from Cameroon is connected with the Bakassi crisis.Abhulimen was
speaking on November 19, on his maiden official visit to his kith and kin in
Limbe.
Nigerians ready to leave
"It has nothing to do with the Bakassi crisis. This has to do with their
welfare, to cater for their needsâ-oe"He said Cameroon and Nigeria have long
been friendly nations and there was the need to maintain that unity and
cordiality.
"We are so united that even when we go to the World Cup together we fail
together," Abhulimen stated.The Consul General said there are already
indications that more than 15,000 Nigerians will be leaving Cameroon in the
days ahead.
From the outset of the repatriation on November 12, The Post was told
that some 3800 had made known their intentions to be repatriated. But as at
November 15, the Consul disclosed that the number had multiplied five
fold. There are indications it may scale up, even higher.
While Abhulimen was making this disclosure to hundreds Nigerians who
gathered at the CDC Labour Club at Bota Middle Farms at his reception, some
1,500 others were at the Bota wharf waiting to sail home.
"Why stay here doing nothing? I want to assure you that there are
opportunities at home. If you are a fisherman, you can go back home and
fish. If you are a farmer, you can go back home and farm," he said.
Abhulimen said the Chinese and some other Asian countries have
discovered that cassava has a certain chemical, though not yet disclosed,
that is of a very high scientific use. He said this was a big opportunity
for those who could go back home and engage in cassava cultivation.
"The current demand for cassava for export is such that the present
population of Nigerian farmers, alone, cannot sustain," he said.
Ordeals
When The Post visited the Bota Wharf, there was every sign of a people
who have been battered by years of hardship. Stress edged on almost
everyone's face.Of the lot, suckling babies, toddlers and many other kids of
ages ranging from two to five, were in the majority.
To compound the numbers were the women: nursing mothers and some
youthful ones, too. There were scores of old people, sapped by years of toil
and moil. These were the lot who have been putting up with years of
incessant visits by police and gendarmes, at times, harassments, who came
for residence permit checks.
Jude Ihediegwu, one of the homebound Nigerians and a driver by
profession, told The Post that he came to Cameroon in 1962, as a young
man. He said while they lived under the West Cameroon government, they never
paid for any residence permit.
"It was until 1980 that we started paying the levy of FCFA 10,000 to the
treasury as residence permit for a two-year period," he said. This amount
was later raised to FCFA 20,000, then to FCFA 60,000 and to FCFA 80,000 and
then to FCFA 120,000, last January, Ihediegwu explained.
For 42 long years in Cameroon, he said he had been paying his levies but
under unbearable conditions."We have been paying with beatings. Sometimes
the police bust into our houses in the early morning and sprayed us with
teargas," Ihediegwu recounted.
Godlove Nefoteso, 29, a fisherman, said he was born here in Cameroon. He
said he was going back because he had seen that there was no solution,
staying on here.Nefoteso said he was forced to bribe the police FCFA 20,000
in June this year when they visited him. He said his permit, which he paid
in 2003, had three more days before expiring. But the police insisted on
harassing him. He bribed them.
Fako SDO
The Second Assistant SDO of Fako, Vincent Nafongo, acknowledged that
Nigerians have contributed enormously to the development of Cameroon in the
areas of trade, culture, social and economic. He said Cameroon and Nigeria
have gone a long way and there was need to conserve the relationship.
The Nigerian Union President, Chief Andrew Essien, said they were not
against the payment of residence permit. "Our worry is that the government
of Cameroon keeps increasing it."

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Gets First Commodity Exchange
Headline: Nigeria Gets First Commodity Exchange
Body_Text:
Lagos — A commodity exchange, Abuja Securities and Commodity Exchange Plc, has
hinted that it would commence business next year and has invited brokers and
warehouses assaying companies to partner with it in that direction.
Business Champion gathered last week in Abuja that the ASCE which would
trade in spot transaction in high quality grains such as white and yellow
maize, soyabeans, cowpea, benniseed and millet, as well as cassava and
sorghum.
In the light of the development the exchange has invited commodity
brokers and warehouse and assaying firms to partner with it in the areas of
dealership and warehousing.
The brokerage firms, would have the minimum share capital requirement as
specified by SEC, which also should have such firms registered with it.
Apart from registering with the Corporate Affairs Commission, (CAC), the
potential broker must possess, at least a three-year audited account or
statement of account for new firms.
Our correspondent also learnt that the broker would have at least three
years of experience or somebody with such experience in the firm and should
be willing to abide by the rules of the exchange.
Specifically, the warehousing and assaying company should have their
facilities located in accessible places by road or rail and must have
storage capacity of at least 500 metric tons.
Such warehouses must be operated by experienced personnel vast in
warehouse warrant issuance, grading and classing instruments and weighbridge
and/or other weighing instruments. The warehouse must also show existence of
bailment by being public in its operations.
All such expressions of interest, it was gathered, would be accompanied
by a non-refundable fee of N.01 million in the case of the warehousing and
assaying firms, while the commodity brokers would have to apply before the
middle of next month.
Apart from agricultural produce, the ASCE would also trade on solid
minerals and energy goods.

Original_Search_Title: FG Moves to Address Women Marginalisation in Politics
Headline: Nigeria: FG Moves to Address Women Marginalisation in Politics
Body_Text:
Oshogbo — Federal Government said it has concluded arrangements to establish six
political training centers in the six geo-political zones of the country to
address the alleged marginalisation of women in the nation's politics.
Government said also it was determined to provide loans in collaboration
with the Bank of Industry (BOI) and the Nigerian Agricultural and
Cooperative Bank (NACB) to provide loans for women desirous of going into
business.
Speaking during a courtesy call on the Governor of Osun State, Prince
Olagunsonye Oyinlola in Osogbo as part of her campaign to better the lot of
women and children in the country, the minister of Women Affairs, Mrs Mariam
Ciroma said in view of the precarious position of women in the nation's
political process, government deemed it necessary to do so.
"Government has concluded plans to establish training institutes for
women where women are expected to acquire skills on political
participation.
The training centers which are part of our efforts to liberate
politically the Nigerian women would be established in the six geo-political
zones", she said.
On loans, she affirmed that the Bank of Industry (BOI) and the Nigerian
Agricultural and cooperative Bank (NACB) in collaboration with the
government planned to give loans to rural farmers to improve their
entrepreneurial drive while urging the women to improve on their cassava
processing drive since there is high demand of the product in Southeast Asia
which
Nigeria is yet to meet.
In his remarks, Governor Onyinlola pledged the support of his
administration in realising the objectives of the ministry as they relate to
the child's rights campaign noting that he had sent the Child's Rights Act
to the state legislature.
The Speaker of the state legislature, Honourable Adejare Bello commended
the efforts of the minister while noting that the House would expedite
action on the bill before it.
The chairman of the Osun State Council of Chiefs and the Oni of Ife who
was represented by the Owa-Obokun of Ijesaland, Chief Omolaran Adekunle said
the royal fathers were in support of the minister's campaign.

Original_Search_Title: President Declares Food Shortage A National Disaster
Headline: Zambia: Opinion
Body_Text:
PRESIDENT Mwanawasa yesterday officially declared the current food
shortage a national disaster and appealed for assistance from donors to
help tackle the situation.
Hopefully, the declaration will open doors for co-operating partners
to help Government minimise the negative effects of the drought and
prepare for the coming season.
In the past decade or so, Zambia has repeatedly faced droughts and
billions of Kwacha have been spent on efforts to cure the effects of
these natural calamities. Unfortunately, it appears that measures taken
to manage such situations in the long term are inadequate.
There is need to work out well grounded, multi-faceted and
sustainable long-term measures on how to handle and manage drought
situations.
We must begin to examine the environmental and climatic conditions,
especially in the southern part of the country, which is always
severely affected by the drought.
Is it not true that this region of the country has been hit by
serious deforestation? How do our current farming practices affect the
environment and the yield? Do they harm the climatic conditions or not?
What needs to be done to stop the over dependency on maize as a
staple food given its vulnerability to both pest and insufficient
water? Why are people reluctant to start growing drought resistant
crops such as cassava and millet?
Since most of staple food is grown by small scale farmers who depend
on cattle for draught power, what measures are being taken to control
livestock diseases such corridor which has almost decimated cattle
populations in Southern Province and East Coast Fever in Eastern
Province?
What happened to the irrigation fund which Government announced to
help reduce dependency on rain-fed crops? What other affordable and
sustainable technology can Zambia adopt to suit our local situation
here?
Is the fisheries department addressing issues of fish depletion in
areas such Luapula Province where greater parts of the population are
fishermen and traders?
Is the Food Reserve Agency (FRA) playing its correct role and is it adequately funded and staffed?
We hope that in the National Development Plan that is being mooted
now, the agriculture policy will address the questions raised above and
many more.

Original_Search_Title: NDDC Chairman Accused of Shunning Due Process
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Chairman Accused of Shunning Due Process
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Chairman of the Niger Delta Development Com-mission (NDDC),
Amba-ssador Sam Edem has been accused of running the agency like a sole
administrator.
In a letter dated November 7, 2005 and signed by eight states
representatives, the chairman was accused of running NDDC without due
process or regard to the provisions of the Commission's Act and
Standing Orders of the Board.
The representatives who who are members of the agency board said,
"we find it quite strange, to say the least, that the Chairman of the
NDDC has chosen a contra-direction to the level functioning of the
Board of the Commission", part of the letter read.
The was particularly accused of awarding contracts without due
process, adding that this was in utter disregard for the Commission's
Authorisation Manual.
Among the allegations raised by the representatives include:
unnecessary and unconscionable continued stay at Hotel Le Meridien
amounting to wastefulness and unbudgeted expenditure on the part of the
chairman;
Trivialisation of and disregard of board meetings by the chairman,
board meetings have been reduced to mere presentation by potential
contractors and consultants;
General lack of work focus and direction in the affairs of the commission;
shoddy implementation of the cassava project which is lacking in
focus and direction. board members have found themselves at cross-road
on this project and;
Stiffling of the functions of the board and its committees.
The states' representatives also expressed dismay over review of
sharing indices as it affect states in their individual budgets.
Another issue raised against the chairman was over-centralisation of
functions, expenditure, activities and programmes of the commission at
the headquarters to the total exclusion of the states' offices and
board members.
They also pointed the Non-commissioning of completed projects which
according to the letter, was causing deterioration to a state where
further funds may be required.
In the petition signed by Mr. Godwin Eyo, representating Akwa Ibom
State; Dr. Mrs Georgina Nwagha - Bayelsa State; Mr. Ekpo Okon - Cross
River; Mr. Val Arenyeka - Delta State; Mr Mathew Iduos - Edo; Prince
Emmanuel Assor - Imo; Dr. O Omotehinse - Ondo State and Mr. Dimabo
Oruama - Rivers State, the representatives resolved to cease from
attending board meetings or unilateral briefings or any other fora
forthwith.
They however gave the chairman seven days ultimatum to address all
the issues raised or they will be forced to take further action deemed
necessary.
Obasanjo had in April enjoined the board to work for the
entrenchment of "prosperity, progress and development of the NDDC in
line with the Niger Delta Regional Master Plan.
The President had made the appeal at the Presidential Villa as part
of the fence mending meeting occasioned by the earlier crisis in the
Commission in which the Managing Director was sent on compulsory leave.

Original_Search_Title: Government Seizes Cargo Barge Carrying Agricultural Humanitarian Supplies
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Congolese Government Seizes Cargo Barge Carrying Agricultural Humanitarian Supplies
Body_Text:
Kinshasa — A barge that is integral part of the African Wildlife Foundation's (AWF)
wildlife conservation and development program in the Maringa Lopori Wamba
Landscape was confiscated on November 12, by the Congolese military. The
barge carrying agricultural products and humanitarian supplies to provide
economic benefits for the people of the region, was confiscated for the
purpose of transporting soldiers from Basankusu to Mbandaka.
This barge was one part of a cargo trip launched by AWF in mid
September. The Ferbo I, a private boat, had been traveling up and down the
Congo and Maringa Rivers to collect agricultural products from local farmers
who had not seen a boat go up those rivers in over 7 years.
The crew, including an AWF employee, was asked to vacate the barge. The
agricultural products collected from people throughout the region were taken
off the boat and put into storage in Basankusu.
AWF is operating in the Democratic Republic of Congo under formal
agreement with the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and in close collaboration
with key Ministries including the Ministries of Rural Development, of Plan,
and the Environment.  This confiscation came as a total surprise to AWF
staff and representatives on the ground and will have an impact on AWFs
development conservation objectives in the region.
AWF has petitioned the Ministry of Defense to return the boat as soon as
possible and has asked them to refrain from future requisition of AWF
property without prior discussion.
The cargo trip - the first in seven years - promised relief to local
communities who have been unable to bring their corn, rice, peanuts, cassava
and other crops to market due to the total collapse of any remaining
regional infrastructure caused by the region's civil war. Local communities
were expected to benefit immediately from this transport, giving them access
to much needed supplies. Over the longer-term, regular transport on the
rivers would allow farmers re-establish their livelihoods, while reducing
the destruction to local forests and wildlife.
"We understand and support the government's efforts to maintain peace
and security in the region." says AWF's Landscape Coordinator in the Congo,
Jef Dupain. "But in light of the significant benefits the opening of the
rivers will have on local populations, we have petitioned the Ministry of
Defense to return the boat as soon as possible and refrain from future
requisition of AWF property without prior discussion."
"We are deeply concerned about the government's disruption of the Ferbo
I's travel. We believe that regular transport along these rivers will enable
farmers to re-establish their livelihoods by cultivating already-cleared
land, while reducing the destruction to local forests and wildlife," says
Justin Belani, AWF's Agro-Economist Specialist.  Belani was among the crew
who were asked to vacate the barge.
During the area's civil war, an estimated three million people
died. Desperate residents who once sustained healthy crops of coffee, cocoa,
rubber, and palm oil were forced to flee deeper into the forests in search
of food, causing great destruction to the forests. In addition, locals began
illegally hunting bonobos in search of meet, taking a huge toll on the
population of these threatened species. One of the most threatened primate
species in Africa, the bonobo, or pygmy chimpanzee, is genetically humans'
closest relative. The bonobos are currently being hunted and slaughtered for
food in post-war Congo.

Original_Search_Title: We Are Not Making Zimbabweans Homeless -Ambassador
Headline: Zimbabwe: We Are Not Making Zimbabweans Homeless -Ambassador
Body_Text:
John Mvundura is Zimbabwe's ambassador to Nigeria. A seasoned
diplomat with twenty-five years' experience in the circle, he was the dean
of Zimbabwean Diplomatic Corps. He was also the first ambassador to be
appointed in Zimbabwe. He bares his mind on the Zimbabwean political
landscape and the controversial land and demolition issues. In this
exclusive interview, he accused the Western media of the negative news about
his country. Excerpts:
Tell us about yourself?
My name is John Shumba Mvundura. I am a Zimbabwean diplomat. I have been
ambassador of Zimbabwe since 1980. I have been doing the same job in various
places in Africa. In 1963, I became a member of Zanu PF youth. Later, I went
into higher politics. In 1980, President Mugabe became the prime minister
and head of government. That was when I was appointed an ambassador and the
first ambassador to Mozambique where we launched our struggle from. I was in
Mozambique for eight years as an ambassador. In 1988, the president recalled
me and decided to re-post me to Malawi. I facilitated a cease fire agreement
between rebels and the government. After that, the president recalled me
again to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. I worked there as a head of legal
and protocol department. Later, I decided to take a voluntary
retirement. But the president recalled me to open the embassy in Libya. I
stayed there from 2000 to 2004. My main assignment was to get oil to
Zimbabwe because we are a land-locked country. The Libyans under the
leadership of Muammer Ghaddafi understood us and the oil deal went
well. From there, the president transferred me to Nigeria at the end of
2004. I presented my credential in February, 2005. On my education, I
studied political science and completed my PhD degree in 2001.
The western media has portrayed Zimbabwe in bad light like the political
turmoil and food shortage. Give us a glimpse into the political atmosphere
in Zimbabwe?
Let me take you back to history, but I want to thank Nigeria for its
contributions in 1976 and 1979. That was the peak of our struggle. When we
were in Lusaka where they held the Commonwealth Summit, we agreed that we
should go to Lancaster in London. The liberation leaders were given an
aircraft by Nigeria to go to London. From Lancaster, the constitution was to
be decided on and we came back, holding that constitution. But during the
Lancaster conference, we had bitter arguments on the land questions because
the historical and racial imbalances were there. It was our concern. Even
now, a country can't be called its name without land. We couldn't call
Zimbabwe- Zimbabwe for what? We are on land, whatever flies will land, so
land was important. Britain agreed during Lancaster conference because we
said that we can't go and buy our land which was invaded by these occupants
and that we can't give them money for that. The British and the Americans
agreed to give the white farmers money to buy or sell, willingly, the
farms. But for the equipment, infrastructure and the houses, it was the
responsibility of the government and we agreed. Zimbabwe and the leadership
of Mugabe agreed. The house and the infrastructure were all improvements and
we agreed to compensate them. They agreed and signed. It went on. That was
under the leadership of Margaret Thatcher and it was a conservative
government; it was signed by the Queen. Later, we asked to go into
implementation. We started that but it was a long space. Margaret Thatcher
had just put in about 20 per cent of the contribution that was really
needed. But she didn't appear to be doing something about it and later John
Major came in and continued with the same pace. In 1997, when the Labour
Party took over in Britain under the leadership of Tony Blair, President
Mugabe congratulated him on his new leadership and even sent in a
high-powered delegation to London to welcome him into government and inform
him about the programme that we have been pursuing. We told him that we
wanted to pursue these issues. During that discussion- I remember an Irish
lady categorically refused and said that they were a new government and they
were not obliged to pay. They said they weren't going to pay or do anything
about the land issue in Zimbabwe. Those ministers came empty handed and
reported back and flashed it in the media every where. The people on the
streets could easily read and understand what transpired in London. But from
there, our president didn't stop. He said that I should come down and
organise a donor conference in order for other people to understand our
plight that Britain is not willing. There was a donor conference and people
came from many countries and they pledged a lot to help the land reforms. In
our understanding, the West went round to discourage those who pledged and
they were not forth-coming. President Mugabe was left without any choice. He
once warned that there could be a volcano that will erupt soon because he
could see the vibration from the populace. Later, the local chiefs became
agitated about vast land that was unused and they told Mugabe that they were
going to demonstrate their right to take their land back and they did.
About the last elections, reports had it that the elections were flawed
and that the oppositions were suppressed. How would you react to this?
Let me say that Zimbabwe does not want to be re-colonised
again. Secondly, we don't want Zimbabwe to be driven by a remote control;
never. We don't want somebody to come and dominate in the ruling of Zimbabwe
once more. Zimbabwe is for Zimbabweans. And at the same time, we can't go
with somebody who is driven by remote control to have a regime change over a
legitimate government for that matter. We have been going to elections from
1980 up till now and we never skipped any elections. But now, because the
western world is waiting with the opposition for a regime change, what else
could they have said? Even journalists are hypnotised by the Western
world. In Africa, we had a team which came to observe the elections. Other
countries, including Nigeria were there and they said that the elections
were free and fair. But only the Commonwealth, which is a racist club,
reported that it wasn't free and fair and they spread this in the western
world. That was falsehood. It was free and fair and the AU under the
leadership of President Obasanjo sent in a team which said it was free and
fair. It is not fair to want to hear from America and else- where and take
that as a positive remark.
How would you describe the political system? Is it a multi-party system
or a one party system?
It is very much a multi-party system. If it is a one party state, why
are we talking about the opposition? The opposition has been there all
along. In 1980, there were 30 parties which contested. The number has been
reducing because they were merged. In 1999, we went for constitution
reforms. President Mugabe sent in 400 people, including opposition
people. But the opposition together with their masters outside decided to
reject this constitution in the referendum. Mugabe later announced that it
was the will of the people. They want to put aside the Lancaster
constitution, but they say that they don't want this constitution. So, we
continued with the Lancaster constitution.
Does the Lancaster constitution allow for a president to be elected more
than twice?
There is no limit. Why are people talking about the limits to restrict
the wishes of the people? In a democratic society, it is the majority that
decides. So why is the majority saying we want Mugabe to be our leader and
you say he has stayed for so long? What about other countries where the head
of states are there for more than 30 years? It is because they fear Mugabe
and his leadership qualities. We don't want an opposition that is driven
from outside: they are puppets and they cannot do anything on their own. We
are talking about the MDC under the leadership of Morgan Tsvangirai. He
spends his time outside Zimbabwe. He campaigns outside and he is an agent of
regime change. There are many parties, it is not only MDC.
How many parties do you have presently?
We have about eight or seven at the moment.
Give us an insight into the demolitions in Zimbabwe which left about
700, 000 people homeless, according to UN estimations?
This was a plan long time ago in our programme. Some corner shops and
some houses were built on sewage lines among other things. We warned
them. It is the same thing your country is doing here (Abuja) as a master
plan. It is even worse than Zimbabwe. There has been never a building of two
or three-storey that was destroyed in Zimbabwe, never. But because of the
western media and the publicity which was so strong, you put in your mind
that Zimbabwe is not doing well. It was a programme which was there. Morgan
Tsvangirai was trying to say that we were doing this because of their people
in the area. They say that it was a strategy and questioned why Mugabe
didn't do it before the elections. But the warning was already there. The
government even built some houses to put people there. Some people resisted
during the demolitions because they were hiding some things for black
market. We are giving the people a programme. We are not just throwing them
out. You are talking about a figure of 700, 000. It is unbelievable. Now we
have a housing programme which we call Operation Garikai, which means 'you
must stay well'. All the negativity is the publicity of the western media
because their aims are not been met. They want a regime change.
Can we relate the food shortage to the land problems in Zimbabwe?
Not at all. The food shortage was a result of drought, floods and the
HIV/AIDS pandemic. But it is not only in Zimbabwe too. In Malawi, we have
the same situation. In Mozambique and South Africa, we have the same
situation too. They only talk of Zimbabwe even though when the white farmers
were going, they sabotaged the economy. But it wasn't the commercial farmers
who can bring agriculture to its highest level; it was the communal
farmers. In Zimbabwe, with the little land that we had, we were doing
something. The commercial farmers were cultivating tobacco and
cotton. Seventy per cent of our food were from the communal farmers. If it
weren't for the droughts, Zimbabwe would have been one of the most important
countries in Africa. Also, in addition to the droughts and floods, there are
sanctions on Zimbabwe as we are talking now. The Western world encouraged
the World Bank and the IMF not to give loans to Zimbabwe, how do you think
one can survive?
There are some of these Zimbabwean white farmers in Nigeria, does your
government have any reservations against that?
No. Zimbabwe doesn't interfere in a country's internal affairs. We have
nothing against Nigeria. It wasn't a negotiation between governments. We are
not bordered at all.
Describe Nigeria-Zimbabwe relationship?
It is very good. We can't even write a good history in Zimbabwe without
putting Nigeria and its contribution. Not only its contribution during the
independence period but even now. You know reciprocally, President Mugabe
and President Museveni came to Nigeria to talk to Gen. Abacha about
President Obasanjo. They persuaded Abacha until he revoked the execution. In
Nigeria-Zimbabwe relations, I see no problem at all. Everything is in
constant motion and developmental. We are pushing for a joint commission. We
are discussing it. When we finish, the committee will go to Zimbabwe to
discuss.
Are there economic relations?
The economic relations were discussed in the Commonwealth and the
African Union. But bilaterally, we are trying to establish one. As we talk,
a high-powered delegation is about to come to Nigeria for cassava. We think
that Nigeria is advanced in cassava production and we need to learn and
transfer knowledge and technology. We also want to negotiate oil.
What is your take on Africa and Nigeria's bid in the UN Security
Council?
Our position is that everybody should go for veto power. Africa's voice
should be heard. But if we go there to fold our arms when decisions are
taken, it is a shame to Africa. We are not worried about who is going. What
we are worried about is the principles at which Africans are joining. If you
go and do nothing, are we representing Africa?
How would you describe your stay in Nigeria?
Home away from home.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agencies Warn of Looming Food Crisis
Headline: Burundi: UN Agencies Warn of Looming Food Crisis
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — A serious food crisis is looming in the northern and eastern provinces of Burundi, a consultant to the UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) said on Thursday.
"The lean season will be longer than normal," Méthode Niyongendako, the consultant, said in Bujumbura, the nation's capital.
The lean season usually runs from September to December but this year it started in August and is not expected to end until late January or in February, according to the November issue of the FAO bulletin Système d'Alerte Précoce, Surveillance de la Securite Alimentaire.
Rural populations are already facing food shortages because the previous harvest in June was also poor and food started running out in August, a month earlier than usual.
Low rainfall is a leading cause of the problem, Niyongendako said. Rains that were expected in September only started to fall recently and villagers in some provinces are only beginning to sow now.
Burundi's eastern provinces have had poor rain three years in a row.
Another cause of the food crisis is the mosaic virus that has decimated cassava. The FAO's deputy emergency coordinator in Burundi, Alexander Huynth, said the food crisis was likely to get worst in the densely populated north provinces because that area had been worst hit by the virus.
Also a 78-percent drop in coffee production has reduced farmers' incomes.
A programme officer for the UN World Food Programme (WFP), Guillaume Foliot, said on Thursday Burundi had a food deficit of 334,000 tonnes in 2005 compared to 254,000 tonnes in 2004.
The agency had been distributing up to 7,000 tonnes of food a month but is now preparing to increase it to 8,000 tonnes.
The governor of Ngozi Province, Niragira Felix, told a local radio station on Tuesday that one person had died of hunger in Gashikanwa Commune and that 20 families had fled their homes in the area in search of food.
He and other government officials in the north called the looming crisis a "famine", but Folit said, "Even if people die in nutritional centres we cannot talk of famine."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Challenges of Free Secondary Education
Headline: Uganda: Challenges of Free Secondary Education
Body_Text:
Kampala — LIKE at the introduction of Universal Primary Education (UPE) in
1997, the Monday pronouncement by President Yoweri Museveni that the
country was now embarking on Universal Secondary Education (USE) has
raised many questions.
The biggest concern is quality, the enormous resources required for
the programme and whether the Government is ready for it at all.
According to the Education Sector Annual Performance Report 2005, an
estimated 700,000 students are currently enrolled in the secondary
sub-sector, with 370,807 in the 780 public schools and another 317,114
in private schools.
This means that about half of the students shall miss out if private schools are excluded.
A senior ministry official said there are many more children out
there who passed PLE, but failed to make it to secondary school due to
lack of fees.
"With USE, these will certainly come and the same problems that
characterized UPE like overcrowded classes, shortage of textbooks and
classrooms will suffice."
Analysts believe the Government is biting too much. "We failed to
recruit more secondary school teachers due to budgetary constraints
this year.
We are still grappling with UPE. The science policy has not yet
taken root and now we are talking of USE. Where shall the resources
come from?" asked the ministry official.
Education Minister, Geraldine Bitamazire, is however not scared. She
says detailed planning and the necessary budgetary provision shall have
been made by the time the programme is implemented in 2007.
"But let us start like we did with UPE and address the challenges as they come," Bitamazire said.
The Minister dismissed as a fallacy, the lack of space in secondary schools.
"If you challenge those who talk of over-congestion in secondary
schools, they can only mention about 10 schools - the Budo's the
Namagunga's and the like. But we have 780 government schools with some
running half full, especially up country. These can still take a lot
