8. Global Call to Action Against Poverty
Africa: Women and Trade
Leading up to the December 2005 World Trade Organization's (WTO) Ministerial Conference in Hong Kong, Pambazuka News will examine some of the issues regarding the WTO as it affects Africa. This week we look at women and trade.
African women are widely recognized as active contributers to economic systems and trade. Women work to generate over half of most household's incomes, and work extremely long hours. They are also the main caretakers of children and other extended relatives, while at the same time accounting for more than half of the workforce that grows, processes and markets food and other goods. At the same time, women are often left out of formal economic planning and development schemes. Rarely do they have access to business loans, and in most cases the land they work does not belong to them. Women also make up the poorest of any social grouping in Africa, and this increasing "feminization" of poverty should be of upmost importance to all.
The WTO is directly involved in shaping the lives of millions of people across the globel, and Africa's women are not exempt. In fact, the policies that the WTO dictates often have devestating effects on individuals, families and communities.
One of the other most specific threats to women in Africa relates to the ending of the quota system in the textile and garment industry. This changed on January 1st, 2005. Up until then, a quota sysem had been in place, making it mandatory for large, wealthy countriies (mainly the US and EU) to spread their orders for textiles and garments over factories in several dozen countries, mostly in developing nations. With the end of this quota system, these obligations have been lifted, meaning that orders can now simply go to the lowest bidder. This coumtry is China, and as a result, garment factories around the world are shutting down because they cannot compete in this new market. Women make up the majority of workers in this industry, and Africa is no different. Women are now starting to lose their jobs to Chinese workers in large numbers. One of the places in Africa where the highest concentration of garment factories are found is Mauritius. Almost a fifth of the working population is involved in the industry in one way or another. Over two thirds of those employed are women. As a result of the end of the quota system, factories are now closing, and the unemployment rates are soaring. At least 10 000 jobs will be lost, and the women that once held them, often the main breadwinners in their families, are left with small severance packages and nowhere to look for new jobs.
Another threat to African women in terms of trade involves trade liberalization. One of the most serious ways that this plays out is in relation to food security, which itself is important on individual, household, national, regional and global levels. Indeed, food security in many ways determines poverty levels in any given country. Agriculture is one of Africa's largest industries, and women are its largerst contributors. Trade liberalization in relation to agriculture has meant a number of things. Chemical pest control and fertilizers have made their way into the African agriclutrural sector, leaving small scale farmers unable to afford them, and thus keep up with the market. Women also make up many heads of households, and are left, therefore, with the double burden of carrying for their families and homes, as well as their agricutlrural workload. The opening up of the export market means that many of these women no longer have time to care for crops that would go towards feeding their families, they must work solely for commercial purposes. The wages earned from these crops are limited, as many African countries rely too heavily on one single crop, and many more cannot compete in the global market.
These comprise simply two specific examples of the way in which African women are affected by trade. Numerous others exist, and the realities faced by African women are difficult to summarize because of their enormous variations and complexities. But one thing is possible to conclude: governments and decision makers must take into account the specific circumstance of women in relation to trade, and put into place mechanisms that protect their abilities to meet those basic human rights which are being violated as a result of policies that rarely benefit Africa and its people.

Farewell Chinwuba, Justice and John - "Blow in the winds, glow in the darkest room and in all our hearts"
The Global Call to Action Against Poverty (GCAP) community in Africa has been devastated by the deaths of ActionAid International Nigeria activists Chinwuba Egbe and John Moru, and GCAP Nigeria National Coordinator Justice Egware in the Bellview flight that crashed after take off from Lagos Airport in Nigeria on Saturday, 22 October. Tributes have been pouring in all week via the GCAP email listserve. Below are details about the three activists from the www.whiteband.org website and summaries of the tributes that have been sent. The full tributes can be read by following the link provided.
CHINWUBA EGBE
Chinwuba Egbe (34) was an Education Team Leader and Co-ordinator of Stepping Stones and REFLECT (STAR) with ActionAid International Nigeria (AAIN). In their media release, AAIN called him a "grassroots man to the core who was 'at home' amongst the poor and excluded, discussing and helping to shape issues of common interest and empowering them through participatory approaches." Egbe had contributed to many poor peoples' livelihood through the Reflect Project in more than 30 communities in Nigeria.
JOHN MORU
At 34, John Moru had already made his mark as an activist who was dedicated to the fight against pervading poverty in the midst of plenty in Nigeria, denial of political and human rights to the people, and the threats of international capitalism. Moru contributed to the emergence of a strong governance team in ActionAid International Nigeria (AAIN) and the initiation of the Nigeria Social Forum (NSF).Together with Egware, he was in the forefront of the Global Call to Action against Poverty (GCAP) campaign in Nigeria. He is survived by a wife and a daughter.
JUSTICE EGWARE
Justice Egware (36) not only sustained the growth of the National Coalition on Education (CSACEFA) but also contributed to the building of the organisation as a strong force in educational issues in Nigeria with a view to bringing the 'voice' of civil society into national discourse. In addition, Justice was in the forefront of the struggle for the achievement of the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) in Nigeria through the MDG Campaigns and as GCAP Nigeria Coordinator. Activists across Africa will remember him as a powerful voice during the Africa Standing Tall against Poverty concert in Accra in the days before the World Summit. They will recall how motivational he was at the World Summit and in the days thereafter at the International Facilitation Group. He is survived by a wife and son.
Any person who watched the Standing Tall against Poverty concert in Accra on television or who attended the concert will remember the powerful and immortal words of Justice Egware on September 3 when he said, "Today, we are standing tall together to say No to Poverty. Africa is not a poor continent and we all agree that we are a generation that believes that poverty can be eradicated."
Tributes:
- Dear fellow campaigners and friends,
It is with great sadness and deep shock that we bring you this news. Justice Egware, co-ordinator of GCAP Nigeria and John Moru of Action Aid Nigeria are believed to have been on the fateful Bellview flight 110 that crashed after take off from Lagos airport on Saturday night. 117 people were on board. 13. Women and Gender
Africa: The Protocol on the Rights of Women in Africa and Female Genital Mutilation
As a part of the coalition supporting the ratification of the Protocol on the Rights of Women in Africa, Pambazuka News will profile various aspects of the protocol over the next six weeks. This week we will take a look at issues surrounding harmful practices against women, specifically, female genital mutilation, as related to the African continent. This is what the protocol states:
Article 5 - Elimination of Harmful Practices
"States Parties shall prohibit and condemn all forms of harmful practices which negatively affect the human rights of women and which are contrary to recognised international standards. States Parties shall take all necessary legislative and other measures to eliminate such practices, including:
b) prohibition, through legislative measures backed by sanctions, of all forms of female genital mutilation, scarification, medicalisation and para-medicalisation of female genital mutilation and all other practices in order to eradicate them "
Female genital mutilation occurs around the world, most widely in Africa - where as many as 29 countries are known to be practicing it. Estimates range as to how many women have experienced the procedure, but figures are said to be around 78 million women who are affected.
With a practice as diverse as the cultures in which it occurs, female genital mutilation is a highly contested custom. The discourse surrounding female genital mutilation is polarized, with some arguing that the procedure is a cultural tradition and must be upheld in order for honor to be bestowed upon a woman and her family. Others, on the opposite side of the argument, contend that the procedure is in blatant disregard of basic human rights. The Protocol clearly takes the side of the latter argument.
It is extremely difficult to make generalizations about female genital mutilation, as the practices vary in different parts of Africa. The age at which it occurs is one such factor, as babies as young as a week old are sometimes subject to FGM, and sometimes grown women. Most girls undergo the procedure anytime between the ages of eight and fourteen.
Many different types of FGM exist, with the least common one being circumcision: cutting the hood of the clitoris. This is the mildest form of female genital mutilation. Excision involves the cutting of the clitoris and all or part of the labia minora, while infibulations means that the clitoris is cut along with the labia minor and most of the anterior part of the labia majora. Both sides of the vulva are then pinned together (with silk, catgut or thorns), leaving only a tiny opening preserved with the insertion of a small piece of wood or reed for the passage of urine and menstrual blood. The girl's legs are then bound together to permit the formation of scar tissue. An intermediate form of this removes the clitoris and either part of or the entire labia majora. Generally, a female elder of the village, or a birth attendant performs FGM. Anesthetics are rarely, if ever used, and girls are held down by their mother or other female relatives. Traditional herb mixtures, earth and ashes are usually rubbed on the wound to stop bleeding. Conditions are generally very unsanitary, and the instruments are generally not sterilized. The consequences of this include chronic infection, pain during intercourse, menstruation and childbirth, as well as psychological trauma.
The Protocol on the Rights of Women in Africa is making steps in the right direction with regards to FGM. It goes beyond simply calling for an end to the practice, but demands that countries criminalize the practice and prosecute those who perform it. It also puts in place the framework for providing counseling support and treatment to survivors of FGM. Furthermore, the Protocol calls for awareness raising campaigns, and also includes a mechanism to allow intervention into the prevention of FGM cases.
Beyond the Protocol, changes are being made on the African continent. In some Kenyan communities, for example, many women are practicing an alternative to female genital mutilation. Girls spend a week in enclosure, just as girls about to be circumcised do, but instead are taught about the harmful effects of FGM and are given information to combat local beliefs and superstitions surrounding the practice. These girls are taught about their bodies and about relationships with men, and are encouraged to go to school and make decisions pertinent to their lives. They are also provided with a mentor - an older woman from their community that they respect and can listen to their concerns. At the end of the week, the girls undergo a graduation ceremony and are reintroduced into their communities as women.
*Researched and written by Karoline Kemp, a Commonwealth of Learning Young Professional Intern

Original_Search_Title: 'It Was Stressful Living in Exile'
Headline: Uganda: 'It Was Stressful Living in Exile'
Body_Text:
Upon arrival at Nairobi's Jomo Kenyatta International airport from
Johannesburg, Dr Kizza Besigye had a chat with Daily Monitor's Simon
Kasyate. They talked about life in exile, what he had missed most Uganda,
and the political game plan. Here are the excerpts.
How have your four years out of Uganda been like?
Ah! Quite busy actually and stressful in other respects, but generally
quiet in that I did not have any disturbances to my person from any
quarter. There were threats that came from time to time. All those threats
came from Uganda, but I must say that I am really grateful to the South
African security system, which made my stay quite secure, and I think
comfortable.
You said stressful? Let's look at some detail here; how stressful
were these days?
Well, stressful in that anybody who has not been forced to live outside
his/her country will not understand especially the emotional stress that is
found in living without a base. You know, when you are in your country, you
travel and you know you are coming back home. If you don't have that
understanding within you that you have somewhere you call home, that by
itself is extremely stressful.
The fact that you have no family, no friends to whom you have been used
to interact with, you are removed from your environment drastically and
indefinitely, you don't know when you can reconnect with your
environment--that imposes, I think, a unique stress which nobody would
relate to unless someone has experienced that kind of situation.
But it was also stressful in terms of the kind of activities that I have
had to engage in mainly travelling up and down with very little means. You
live on a very, very tight budget, you don't know how you'll meet all the
requirements that are imposed on you and all these are quite stressful.
Your escape from Uganda in 2001 was based on certain conditions; have
these now been addressed to such a point that they will not adversely affect
your stay?
These conditions, I think, have actually gotten worse. Since I left,
many of my colleagues, many of our supporters have been arrested, very many
are still languishing in prisons in Uganda on tramped up charges of treason
and of other criminal charges. Some have been in prison for up to three
years without trial. There is a lot of intimidation of those who are not in
prison; some have been sacked from their jobs because of their political
association with us.
Those in businessâ-oenobody would want to openly associate with members
of the opposition because once they do then they are harassed by the tax
men, by the licensing authorities; they cannot get tenders. So the situation
continues to be hostile.
However, one significant change has taken place, which is that as a
result of the G6 action, the constitutional court ruled and declared the
movement system as a one party state. That was a landmark ruling because it
opened the way for other political parties to be registered and to
function.
And as a result of that, our political pressure groups, which they were
until then, the reform Agenda and others, were able to transform themselves
into legal political parties, to open political activities throughout the
country.
We have offices of FDC in every district of Uganda, we have committees,
we have structures up to every village in our country and therefore we now
have an institutional mechanism within which we can continue to struggle for
our rights.
When I left Uganda in 2001, I was struggling as Kiiza Besigye. I did not
have any organisational backup. Today we have an organisation and it is that
organisation, together with me, that have realised that in order to advance
the struggle to another level this is an appropriate time that I should come
back, all the risks and the hostile environment notwithstanding.
We know that the situation is still as hostile or more hostile than it
was but we think that an opportunity exists to confront the hostility and to
fold it back. Some of us may become causalities in the process but the
process will be served, the process will advance through our engagement and
through my coming back and participation in the struggle for further freedom
and for further democratisation of our country.
So, what is your political game plan?
This is a matter that will have to be discussed within the FDC, within
the G6, the group of six political parties that have been co-operating in
struggling against the dictatorship, as a whole. We must together structure
an action plan that we can then follow. It is not a matter for me
alone.
But immediately, what is going to concern me is the delegates'
conference of the FDC, which is taking place from October 28. That will give
us a way to now have a new leadership that is fully empowered to undertake
whatever processes are envisaged subsequently.
If this delegates' conference anoints you to take the FDC mantle, are
you ready for the task?
Entirely! I am available to FDC, I am available to Uganda to undertake
whatever responsibility is necessary for the purpose of making a transition
to a democratic dispensation. And I have made this very clear right from
2001, that my political engagement is only limited to the requirements of
transiting to a democratic dispensation. Once that happens then I am afraid
I wont be available anymore.
And if the conference does not place you at the helm, what role would
you then play and how?
Well, we are in a political engagement as I have said to ensure that we
achieve democratic governance, that we achieve good governance. It is not
that good governance will come only when I am leading. Therefore whoever is
felt to be an appropriate person to lead the process, we must all rally
around that person and offer whatever we are able to offer to make sure that
the process moves forward.
And even if the FDC chose me as their flag bearer, I would still
recommend that the FDC engages in dialogue with the other parties and that
together with the G6 people, we can come out with one person to lead us into
this democratic struggle. And that person may not be the FDC candidate. Even
if it is any other person outside the FDC who is felt to be the best chance
for leading forward, we would all rally around that person.
Are you thus calling for a coalition of all political forces against
the NRM?
Yes! We in the FDC have always advocated for an alliance of all
pro-democracy political parties and this is why we have been very anxious to
promote the G6, to work through the G6 mainly for the purpose of achieving
the democratic transition.
Once a democratic transition has been achieved, once there are equitable
rules, regulations, laws and institutions, once there is equal facilitation
for all the political players, once a political playing field is leveled and
we can have a free and fair electoral process, then we do not have to work
through an alliance.
Every party can now espouse its development programme and the people of
Uganda can choose from them. But the immediate challenge now is not so much
the alternative development strategies; it is having a dispensation that can
allow any strategy whatever it is to function freely and beneficially for
the whole country.
There is rumour that upon arrival, you will be arrested and charged
with criminal offences. What is your take on that?
That will not be surprising at all. I have heard very recently that the
government has been rather uneasy about bringing charges of treason because
they don't have any kind of evidence obviously; but secondly then they would
be under pressure as the offended party to drop those charges. So I hear
that they maybe working on getting someone they can front to raise
complaints as a private citizen, on the basis of which then, they would say
they are obligated to enforce the law and to act in a manner that would then
require me to be arrested.
And I hear that one of the things that they are indeed considering is to
get somebody who could say that I raped her at some stage, and that she
lodges a complaint and on the basis of that they would then arrest me.
On a lighter note, is there anything particularly in terms of food
that you have so much missed that you can't wait to have?
Well, I would like to have my porridge from millet; I would like to have
my cassava for breakfast, all of which are not available where I was. So I
am looking forward to enjoying these and the wonderful fruits that can only
be found in Uganda. You can never find a pineapple like that which you find
in Uganda, or the small yellow bananas, the bwenvus or Kabalagala as we call
them. All these are things one looks up to as they are coming back
home.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria's Food Crisis: Something's Wrong With US
Headline: Nigeria's Food Crisis: Something's Wrong With US
Body_Text:
Government officials talk of food security, investment in agriculture
and there are annual budgets that seem to underline commitment to these. The
reality, however, is that the prices of foodstuffs have gone beyond the
reach of most Nigerians. The ferocious campaign for export of cassava
products has worsened the situation with the acute rise in price of garri,
Nigeria's main staple. Are there solutions to the food crisis? Our panelists
take a profound look at the issue ...
I would want you to make your opening remarks on the food situation
in general, before we can go to the specifics.
Animasaun: Words used in the invitation letter were apt. One was
affordable, two, sky-rocketing, three, crisis. Anybody will tell you that
x-amount of Naira that used to buy x-volume of these food items will now
barely buy a tenth of what it used to buy. As for cassava, of course, the
derivations are many, at least from the angle of the Yoruba. I think it is
about the same thing for the peoples of this country. You can drink garri,
you can put it in your beans. For the Egba man, lafun. I know there are
derivations in the East where I also had the opportunity to work and I don't
know how many people can afford any of these things today. I used to eat six
wraps of fufu but now the one I bought for N10 would barely go for two
morsels and the trouble is that salaries and wages are not keeping pace with
the prices. Any nation that cannot feed its population is definitely not a
secured nation. It can be attacked not by guns. As the English will say, the
way to a man's heart is through his stomach.
Malomo: It is quite disheartening and when one looks at the situation of
food in Nigeria, the question will be, why food crisis? Nigeria has abundant
land, very fertile land, she has huge population, so why should there be
crisis in the food industry? There is something wrong with us. Either we are
not producing enough or we are producing and we don't have money to buy,
because, we are not working or the government is not spending. So, the
challenge ahead is for us to now look seriously and find out what is wrong
and make the necessary changes. The government may be doing something, but
we are not aware of what the government is doing. We are in democracy and
democracy is supposed to be participatory. Participatory in the sense that
we have information and we share information, the continued dialogue between
the ruled and the rulers. Our democracy should be participatory so that we
can make contributions, we would learn from ourselves, and we would exchange
ideas and from there, we would be able to contribute ideas to help the
country.
Adeoye: There is a common knowledge that things are not as they used to
be, since the basic need of every man is food, and we know that feeding is
the smallest thing that you can do for anybody. Then if people came to your
house you gave them food, but now, you are afraid if they come because you
cannot afford it. These are basic problems, essentially, and when these
problems persist, there is going to be crisis. People don't normally steal
food, but people are stealing food now, which is alien to our community. I
know that over 80 per cent of our food is being produced by the peasants,
but the problem has not even arisen now because they are in the age bracket
of 60. Most of these people are dying off. Remedies would have been for the
younger people to be taking up this work, but people are not going to the
farms. You can't even have labour. I come from a place where you use labour
everyday and everybody wants N1,000 per day now for labour. As a result,
unless we do something very quickly to replace these peasants, (who are only
producing to satisfy themselves and just putting a little in the market) and
get medium scale producers and large scale producers in a pragmatic way, the
situation will become very terrible. Go out now, you see everybody on okada,
and our students whom we are raising to go into agriculture, are afraid of
going into agriculture because they now find out that agriculture does not
pay. The inputs are not there. The situation is virtually precarious, and we
must arrest the situation now.
Nwabua: In a country where we have all manner of problems - oil crisis,
youth crisis, political crisis, economic crisis, women crisis, road crisis,
I don't think it is normal for us to single out food crisis, because it is
part of the crisis that is enveloping the whole country. The essential
question must be asked: how did we come to this stage? Many analysts have
said that when oil was discovered, that Nigerians abandoned the land. From
that time, we have been going deeper into food problems, in terms of
abandoning the land. I was a farmer. I will return from school, my mother
would say 'young man, your food is in the farm'. I will rush to the farm to
have my afternoon meal. Sometimes, I will get to the farm, my brother will
say 'okay, you have come, take this home first with a bicycle before you
come and take yam'. It was part of a culture. What we are seeing nowadays is
that the agriculture we were proud to tell the whole world that is the
mainstay of Nigeria's economy, at the same time, we have driven agriculture
to the backstage. I think that instead of treating it as a crisis, we should
treat it as a national shame. In India where I studied, you can't believe
what is happening there. Anything that tampers on agriculture, is treated as
a treasonable felony, because that is the source of survival. I would want
us to take the issue very seriously. If we have survived other crises, we
may not and we are not likely to survive food crisis in Nigeria.
Dr. Adeoye, talked about the human resource. There is no agriculture
without the human resource, and he mentioned the fact that we have an aging
farm population, you said over 60. But you forgot about the gender factor,
that over 80 per cent are also women. What is your own solution for
restoring some kind of balance to the situation before it becomes a bigger
shame than what Dr. Nwabua said?
Adeoye: It is the peasant and the small holders who have been feeding us
and that is what you see in the market. These people have been sustaining
food production over the years, and we have been enjoying it. They want to
carry all the food to the major markets. Lagosians are enjoying, people in
Ibadan are enjoying. If you go to my village, it is more expensive to buy
yam, than to go to Bodija market, because they would just think "I'll take
it to a major market". But the pattern is changing now, particularly when
this cassava issue came. Now, you can't get to my village, because the garri
community is Oyo North. People have bought up all the cassava on the field
up-front for the next three years. So, actually, there is no food
there. What we are selling as commercial crops now for export is naturally
what we are supposed to be eating. My suggestion is that we have to go back
to the farm settlement scheme. There is no option. If all the factor inputs
for agriculture are there and I bet it, government has to provide them and
the farm settlement scheme. Let people come together as cooperatives, let
there be fund. Don't give them money, get them the land, get them the input,
let them produce. The intellectual right is what they are going to sustain,
it means they have the expertise to farm. That is enough sacrifice for the
nation, because if people go into the oil industry, and they go into
business and they make their own money, these farmers are producing for the
system. This is because, what we are doing now, we are just marketers of
secondary products. People sit by a box of money and they say it is money,
and they get something from somewhere and sell it.
These have to be planned from local government to the state government
and then to the national level. Once you can acquire land for them, and then
there are funds for it, and there are equipment and other infrastructure,
people will be attracted. When these crops or the products are ready, the
government should also make available, markets, then up-front deductions of
the money that has been ear-marked. Once this has been put in the wheel, our
agriculture students will like to go back to the farms. In the University of
Ibadan, we have a practical training - four-year degree programme. We are
taking them to rural communities. In Ile-Ogbo community, we have integrated
them now. Only this morning, we were going to integrate Alafia and all those
areas. These students are enjoying the service now, but the question they
keep on asking is, after the youth service, they give us a taste of earning
money and you dump us, we are looking for job for three or four years. I
have a long list of agriculture graduates with B.Sc. M.Sc. and Ph.D. who are
looking for jobs and I say it is a shame. You tell them to go to the farm,
they don't have a land, they don't have money, they don't have inputs. This
is where the nation has the responsibility. We must make agriculture
attractive now, and use our oil money, to lay a good foundation. I believe
we will be exporters of food. I had a dream of an agrarian nation, and I
believe that dream will still come true. I have had several dreams before,
they have been fulfilled. This will not be an exception. I want to beg the
government to help me fulfil my dream for these youths.
Why fish farming and what more can the government do to help fish
farmers?
Animasaun: First, about the gender percentage, I do not think that it
will matter, whether we have more men or more women, what will matter will
be the amount of production. He suggested the farm settlement scheme, I
would say that would just be another production factor and those who
espoused it, Chief Awolowo Farm Settlement Scheme, has not told us really
that it was a fantastic contribution to agriculture, it might have
contributed, maybe because we did not allow it to really work itself out
logically. My own experience has been that conscious nations do not rely
much on the number of people on the farm for the quality or the volume of
production. I worked in Scandinavia for the government for sometime, and at
that time, only 7 per cent of the population produced the agricultural
output that fed the nation. What they do at the university is transferred to
farmers and of course, with some subsidy from the government. If we
subsidise fuel consumption, why can't we subsidise agricultural production?
I don't think I am qualified really to talk about fish farming. I am new,
but I have discovered that you need big money to start and to be able to
pattern your production so that you can have a consistent output every month
from which you can now base your income.
I would have had to run to you for funds if I didn't have a little from
my work place. I didn't have to borrow, the interest could create
problems. I think we need government intervention for some of us who are not
knowledgeable in those things before we started, not to be left to the
capitalists who say they run courses for the beginners and most of them are
really charlatans who know next to nothing. By the time you know it, your
fish would have died off, and you are left holding the baby. At least, I can
boast of employing a few people now, but I know the headache of thinking of
how the salaries would come. It is a good thing that some people are taking
responsibilities. If I push a little fish into the market, then we are
lessening the pressure, afterall, we are talking about protein for the
population. We need government's helping hand for anybody who is to go into
farming. I guess even for the fish farmer, that will be like having a kind
of integrated farming. There is a lot of waste that you can feed to the
fish, that is food to the fish. I guess everybody needs some kind of
farming, backyard farming for vegetables. I grow okro in my little
plantation. The pleasure of looking at them in the morning, the flowers
especially, that by the afternoon, the flowers are up. You can see the okro
bud, the next day it gets bigger, it is a therapeutic thing to the
soul.
Alhaji Animasaun had touched upon mechanised farming, because when
you have a small portion of the population feeding a whole population, it
means mechanised farming. How practical is mechanisation in Nigeria and
would our terrain support mechanised farming especially in places with a
great deal of soil erosion even without mechanised farming?
Nwabua: Alhaji Animasaun talked about government coming in. There must
be money, but above money, we need the necessary political will. Political
will is lacking in Nigeria's developmental efforts. We come out with
beautiful concepts about how Nigeria should develop, how we should move
forward but when you look at the actors, they are lacking the required
political will to translate those beautiful concepts into reality. This is
more of the bane of the Nigerian society. These days, we talk about
government disengaging from investing. They want what we call private-driven
economy, or I may say private-driven agricultural development.
Here, the principal actors should be the banks, but how do you now bring
in the banks? Before then, the question arises, how patriotic are the
Nigerian banks? In 1985, there was the Agricultural Trading Guarantee Scheme
by the Federal Government in which case you just walk into any bank, ask the
bank to come and take a look at the size of your farm to enable it make
necessary recommendations to the authorities about how much assistance you
can get. Even then as a youth corper, I had a big farm. I planted maize, but
then, we had the Grains Board in Minna. I was calculating that the moment I
could farm on say five hectares of land, I should be able to supply at least
three tons of maize to the Grains Board, thereby making my money and in turn
employing more hands. With that in my mind, I walked into a bank in Minna, I
filled the necessary forms. The bank's officials came to the site. They
wanted to know how much I would give them? As a youth corper? Thank God for
the on-going war against corruption. At the end of the day, they succeeded
in giving me N2,500, which was not even anything for the trouble I had going
to their offices. In less than two years, I was unable to continue with the
project, and I backed out. I started to work at the Army Cantonment as a
teacher. The problem is that in Nigeria, we have what it takes to turn this
economy from an oil-propelled economy to agrarian one in a matter of three
years, given the necessary incentives.
I have mentioned political will and sagacity, I just have to mention
that governments, not just this government, governments, have not been
sincere, rather, they now want to pay lip service to agricultural
development. Our salvation does not lie in oil. Government can ask banks to
fund agriculture by paying part of the annual profit into an Agricultural
Fund. This fund will be available to willing farmers. They will register,
field workers will go to the field and ascertain they are the owners of the
particular land they want to use for agriculture, they get funds
directly. Nothing stops the Central Bank from having field workers all over
Nigeria for on the spot assessment of the implementation of the
programme. The moment we come and we are in air-conditioned rooms, thinking
these things will work, they will not work. Let me reinforce that more than
anything else, the Nigerian state owes the citizenry more in terms of
food. In terms of cassava, when Dr. Adeoye talked about people buying up
farms, I want to tell you that even in my area, Aniocha South in Delta
State, I know of a contractor who has already bought what will be produced
on a farmland for five years, with the contract written and signed. What is
he going to do? He is going to export the cassava, raw. And it brings us to
the point that for every policy in Nigeria, there are people out there to
reap billions of Naira from it. If someone has now contracted a farming
group, like a cooperative society, to have their harvest for the next five
years, then what do you think? That is why I maintain that we need the
necessary political will. We need the media.
The media should now stop its celebrative aspect and go into real
investigative journalism. All around the world, we have agricultural
journalists, but it is not a popular concept around here. There should be
agricultural units in the media to investigate some of these lapses in
agricultural development efforts. Finally, when Alhaji Animasaun talked
about seven per cent of the population farming to feed the Scandinavians, I
would want to draw our attention to the fact that in the United States, only
two per cent feed the population of 250 million people, and they even have
surplus to feed the whole of Africa, and at the end of every year, they have
close to 2,000 tonnes of fish which they drop into the ocean to feed the
fishes. But in Africa, we are dying of lack of protein. Let me use this
medium to tell the government that they are not doing enough, that boardroom
meetings are not enough. The National Assembly's interest on the subject is
not enough. How many of them at the National Assembly have any experience in
farming?
We have left out one important factor, that is population. At
independence, it was under 50 million, that population is now close to 130
million. And we are having approximately 250,000 new mouths to feed
monthly. Given the fact that the population you want to feed is increasing,
you mentioned land, the nation has abundant land, but the land you can
cultivate is actually reducing because we are building roads, we are
building schools, most of what is now Express Road from Ibadan to Ile Ife
used to be farmland. How do we balance between increasing population and
shrinking land? What role will mechanised farming play in all these?
Malomo: I mentioned participatory governance, and fortunately, we are in
a democracy now. I read somewhere where a study had been done, and it has
been confirmed that as a democratic society, famine does not occur. Famine
occurs in a situation where people are under servitude.
Like famine occurred in India when the colonial government was there,
and since the colonial government left, there has not been any
famine. Famine occurred in China when there was depression, there was
authoritarian rule, so there was no freedom. In essence, here, we have
freedom but we are doing nothing with our freedom. We are becoming two
dependent on government and others. I do not think the government should
play so much role as being said here. The role that government should play
is that of policy formulation and involving the people. Production should be
left to the people. Government can only give incentives. Part of the
incentives should not even be cash being given to farmers, but inputs,
infrastructure. Even with infrastructure, people can provide some for
themselves, because like they say in Yoruba, something you have not worked
for, does not last long with you. If somebody gives you something, you
fritter it away because you do not know the value. I want to quote something
I read from a book: British Economic History, first published in 1934. It
says: "In some tropical regions, indeed where food is abundant and is
obtainable with little effort, and where the need for clothing and shelter
is light, and is easily satisfied, men have not been forced into that
measure which has taken place elsewhere. They have remained in a barbaric
state and they have little or no economic history." This was written in 1934
and I think it still applies to us in tropical society.
We have mentioned here about somebody going to buy five years supply of
cassava. The cassava that is being exported, what is it being exported for?
China is consuming now, because China has been linked in this expansion. It
has in a wholesale manner, taken the capitalist hold. But it has its own
culture to determine what it is going to do. What is good in the market
today, they are making use of it, in a market system that determines its
supply and demand. Government, in our recent past, under the military was so
profligate, although our money is still being stolen. It is unfortunate that
even in a democracy where money is being stolen, people make untenable
excuses. I am looking at a challenge, a challenge that will bring results. I
am a dental surgeon, I am not practising. I have access to people in
government. A classmate of mine is a governor, I have access to the
president. If there is anything that I would want, it is some form of loan,
like the Nigerian Agricultural Development Bank is there, it is supposed to
finance agriculture, I can sit down and map out a project that will employ
this number of people. We cultivate this number of hectares of land at a
particular point, 2,000 or 3,000 or 1,200 hectares of cassava and we are
going to produce so, so and so.
On our own, we have been able to raise this amount of money, may be a
significant portion and say 'can we get a loan for the balance at a very
good interest rate?' As regards government policy on agriculture, I do not
know much about it, we do not have much information. Unfortunately, I do not
have access to the internet. I thought of going to look at the government
website and see if I can get any information. I travelled to Abeokuta and I
got this information. Let us take cassava for instance, each time I am in
Abeokuta, I always see vans with the inscription: TUKPA Expansion Programme
or something like that. I don't know where they were going to. But there is
somewhere in Ogun State where NALDA acquired one thousand hectares of land
and they were able to open up 500 which is now having cashew nuts that have
been producing cashew flesh. And I thought probably that's where they go to,
and I went to that place again. The other 500 hectares have been lying
waste. The farmers are having problems with the 500 hectares that have been
cultivated. They only use the nuts, the cashew flesh is left to rot, and
they keep the nuts at home, until people come from France, and they sell it
to them. And I said, no, you are going to stop that. I asked someone with 10
acres, what his yield was? He said three tonnes per acre. He sells a tonne
for N50,000. We are thinking of giving them some money for the flesh. In the
farmers' institute, NEPAP taught them to process the flesh into marmalade,
jam and mixed fruit juice but they do not have the money. About three years
ago, NEPAP said the machine would cost between N500,000 and a million. I do
not have N1 million. One is thinking about writing progress reports to the
government to seek its contribution.
I call it a challenge. Oil was never there in 1960 -1970, it was managed
by private industries. Even when you have multi-nationals in food industry,
government is never involved. Where government is involved, there is
corruption, inefficiency, and the programme fails at the end of the day. On
cassava, the professor said there was no problem about cassava export
affecting cassava producers. Thailand is the sixth largest producer of
cassava. A year ago, we were having this discussion about this group we are
trying to form, and the fellow now brought an e-mail from China. He wanted
to export cassava to China. He offered a price, and the man at the other
end, said: 'I can get it for one third the price in China. He said this
Obasanjo cassava export programme will fail. Why do you have to export
cassava? Why not use the cassava locally and when you have surplus, you can
export? At the same time, we should be careful. Government should not
control people in a market economy. Once there are controls here and there,
the whole situation fails. When there are incentives, the people would be
able to meet the challenge of producing more. I was reliably informed that
the yield for the past 10 years in Nigeria is about 10 metric tonnes per
hectare. What are the constraints in increasing yield? They include inputs,
the cost of labour, farm land, and transportation. Those are the challenges
that we have to tackle and we could tackle them in conjunction with
government. We still import starch and other products. Talking about oil,
crude oil prices are rising and the government has started a policy of
ethanol production. The challenge for us is to meet the level of production
that can sustain export and the food industry.

Original_Search_Title: The Bellview Plane Crash: How the Plane Was Buried in Crater
Headline: Nigeria: The Bellview Plane Crash: How the Plane Was Buried in Crater
Body_Text:
Lagos — Bellview Flight 210 which had 117 passengers on board, including its
crew, according to eye witness accounts, fell from the skies of Lisa village
like a huge ball of fire at about 8p.m Saturday.
Eye witnesses said the Boeing 737 was already engulfed by fire before it
touched the ground, resonating a huge sound. There were no survivors.
The crash made a very deep crater of 50 metres in radius and 40 feet in
depth.
The aircraft was buried in a huge crater without any part of it showing
making rescue efforts by the Ogun State Government, National Emergency
Relief Agency (NEMA) and the Red Cross Society of Nigeria meaningless.
The farm in which the accident occurred looked bulldozed as trees were
uprooted. Smoke kept oozing out of the deep crater in which plane was
buried, an indication that the remains of the aircraft buried in the earth
was still smoldering
As at 8 p.m when this report was being filed, all that was left of the
plane was still smouldering.
All that could be seen on the plantation were scattered human parts,
pieces of papers, international passports, documents of varying importance,
luggage of various sizes and cloths.
The gory crash site did not bear any huge part of the crashed Boeing 737
jetliner.
The biggest parts of the Boeing 737 that could be found at the scene of
the crash were not bigger than the size of a car door.
Vanguard was told by the Baale of Lisa Village, Chief Sadiku Odugbemi,
that the site of the crash used to be a plantation.
Crops on the plantation included Cocoa, Cassava and Kolanuts.
Chief Odugbemi said because of the fear of fire and darkness, residents
could not go near the crash site until yesterday morning.
The Baale said when the villagers got to the site of the crash this
morning, all that could be seen were body parts which were neither
recognisable nor could the parts be assembled to make a whole.
It was after this that the villagers, led by the Baale went to Sango
Police station to report the incident.
If anybody survived, according to the Baale, the person would have
walked out into the village since that was the nearest human settlement
there.
As at the time of filing this report, naval officers and policemen were
at the site.
They were there to keep the site from being tampered with pending full
official investigation.
However, with the continued influx of the villagers to the crash site
and the uncontrollable nature of the crowd, with wailing and crying, this
could not be effectively done.
Ambulances from the Lagos State Government and the Ogun State Government
were seen at the crash site.
An eye witness told Vanguard that earlier in the evening, a medical team
had visited the site pack some human parts into a sack and took the parts
away.
The Baale said whereas it took him 10 years to tend the plantation and
whereas he would not be asking for compensation, he pleaded that government
should come and do some environmental impact assessment so that the grounds
can still be usefull.
Some of the eye witnesses also said they sited Dr. Iyabo Obasanjo, the
Ogun State Commissioner for health who was said to be too grieve-stricken to
talk.
A resident of the village, which is at least 12 kilometers from Sango
Chief Ojo Akinlabi, told newsmen that the villagers heard a very loud noise
which sounded like a bomb blast but that none of the villagers could do
anything not knowing where it came from.
Sympathisers, relatives of people in the plane and top government
functionaries besieged the site of the crash obviously hoping that there was
something that they could be able to with most of them however going back
dejected.
Mr. Emeka Oputa who had his friend, Holland based business man
Mr. Charles Ofokwu 27 on the aircraft was at the site saying that he had
been praying since he heard about the accident that Charles should
survive.
With tears streaming down his face, Mr. Oputa said that Charles was on
his way to Abuja for a visit and that he had in fact called that they should
come for him at the airport when the plane was delayed before he called
again that they should not bother as they were already being asked to
board. He said Charles whom he dropped at the airport at about 5.30pm was to
come back on Tuesday and make plans for his return to Holland.
Chairman of the Red Cross Society of Nigeria in Ogun, Prof. Charles
Ohiku said that they did not arrive at the scene until about 12.30 because
the initial report was confusing. Deputy Governor of Ogun Alhaja Salmot
Badru, the secretary to the State Government, Alhaji Sarafa Ishola, several
commissioners including Mr. Niran Malaolu [Information] Kola Onadipe
[Special Duties] Iyabo Obasanjo Bello [Health] Folake Marcus Bello [Women
Affairs] among others were at the scene of the crash.
Later, Dr. Mrs. Iyabo Obasanjo Bello told newsmen that because of the
extent, it was certain that nobody in the aircraft would have survived
adding that the police have being asked to secure the place.

Original_Search_Title: Rebranding Nigeria's Image
Headline: Nigeria: Rebranding Nigeria's Image
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigeria, by virtue of its size, population and enormous natural
endowment such as crude oil, tin, gold, limestone, etc, has always been
expected to play a leading role in African politics.
These potentials were already recognized at independence. Her large
size, huge population and abundant natural resources endowed her with a
promising destiny and natural mandate to be a leader as well as a major
player in international politics.
However, over the decades, events in the domestic scene conspired to
ensure that these potentials remained not more than a dream. The
intervention of the military in Nigerian politics for about 29 years out of
45 years as an independent state had greatly marred the image of Nigeria
especially in the international community.
The arrogant and overbearing posture of the military dictators notably
the Abacha regime and the increasingly gross abuse of human rights, lack of
accountability and widespread corruption further aggravated the negative
image of the country.
These development weakened Nigeria's moral authority in Africa's affairs
ultimately leading it to becoming a pariah nation. Globally, events in
Nigeria were considered bad examples for an increasingly democratic
Africa. In fact, the seizures of power in some West African countries like
Gambia, Niger and Sierra Leone were attributed to the bad influence of
Nigeria's military; more so, a Nigerian was the Army Chief of Gambia's Armed
Forces.
Unfortunately, Nigeria suffered its most humiliating period in foreign
relations after the annulment of the presidential election result in 1993
and the subsequent sanctions imposed on her by the international
community. Again, the height of the Military's intransigence under Sanni
Abacha coincided with the period of global democratic renaissance. The rule
of law, good governance, accountability, transparency and protection of
human rights had become the rallying cry of the international
community.
Indeed, certain developments on the domestic scene combined to
successfully and cumulatively propel the country on the wrong side of
civilized behaviour and acceptability by the international community.
Infact, at no time in Nigeria's history, as an independent nation, had
there been as much international political and economical pressures as on
the heels of the annulment of the June 12 presidential elections. The
annulment was seen as an impudent subversion of the democratic process by
the Nigerian military. Nigeria's predicaments at this stage in her political
life forced it to seek friendship with countries of similar mould.
The last straw that broke the camel's back was the execution of famous
writer and minority rights activist, Ken Saro-Wiwa, in November
1995. Nigeria was expelled from the Commonwealth of Nations, an organization
composed of former British colonies.
In swift successions, the European Union (EU), the USA, and several
other nations imposed sanctions on Nigeria. Even African leaders expressed
great disappointment over developments in Nigeria. This incident made it
impossible for Nigeria to defend the African Nations cup, which she won in
Tunisia in 1994.
However, by some divine providence, in June 1998, General Abdulsalami
Abubaker, who succeeded the late dictator addressed many of the injustice
perpetuated by the former regime, a development that brought Nigeria into
the mainstream of international relations and friendship. The international
community appears to have so far responded favourably to the enthronement of
democracy in Nigeria as well as the policy thrust of the Obasanjo's
Administration, which is private sector driven. To be candid, this
Government has shown the determination and zeal to consolidate its gains in
various aspects of our national life so that Nigeria can emerge as an
economically viable and stable nation.
This determination has manifested in the establishment of anti-graft
agencies such as Independent Corrupt Practices Commission (ICPC) headed by
eminent Jurist and retired Supreme Court Judge, Mustapha Akanbi, Economic
and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), piloted by committed and dedicated
police officer, Nuhu Ribadu among others. This administration is also
seriously committed to improving the quality of lives of Nigerians by
establishing poverty eradication agencies such as National Poverty
Eradication Programme (NAPEP), National Economic Empowerment Development
Strategy (NEEDS), and New partnership for Africa's Development
(NEPAD). These and the activities of other agencies and commissions
especially the National Drug Law Enforcement Agencies (NDLEA) and National
Agency for Food Administration and Control (NAFDAC) headed by transparent
and hard working Professor of pharmacology, Prof. Dora Akunyili, has placed
Nigeria on the right side of international community.
The recent debt relief granted the country by the Paris Club is an
indication that the world is appreciating the effort of the present
administration towards economic recovery. The prosecution of former Senate
President, Adolfus Wabara, over bribery and corruption with former Minister
of Education, Professor Fabian Osuji, has shown the world that the country
is determined to wipe out corruption at all levels. The on going prosecution
of former police chief Tafa Balogun; the recent indictments of Mrs Amaka
Anajemba amongst others are clear indications of this administration's
commitment to transparency and accountability. This will in turn attract
investors to the country. Unemployment will be reduced minimally, while
armed robbery, political thugery and the godfather syndrome will be a thing
of the past.
As Nigeria is in the fourth decade of her independence, and under
democratic rule, these positive moves by the government will help Nigeria to
build a strong and reputable image by adopting and implementing popular
policies that will transform the lives of Nigerians positively while still
maintaining her role as a leading player in African affairs.
Western or European foreign media organisations especially British
Broadcasting Corporation (BBC), Voice of America (VOA), Radio France
International and popular news agencies such as Associated press (AP),
Agence France Presse (AFP) and Reuters etc which are responsible for the
gathering and disseminating of information globally often focused on
Africa. Many foreign reporters have refused to change with continuously
changing developments in Nigeria and African nations. They continue with
their old and traditional approach to reporting the continent. Rather than
reporting only the negative side of the Continent, which most of the time is
unbalanced with emphasis on conflict and crisis situation, foreign reporters
should seek out causes of conflict and analyse important political and
economic factors, prepare the audience for possible future development. If
this is done, Nigeria would change the bad press the country receives. The
New World Information and Communication Order (NWICO) should be respected
and adhered to by information managers.
The Federal Ministry of Information and National Orientation in
conjunction with communication practitioners and educators have reiterated
their commitment to organising regular seminars to formulate a veritable
communications policy for the country.
The government of virtually every country has one or more departments or
agencies involved in communication with other nations. Seasoned public
relations professionals without any political or ethnic sentiment are given
the opportunity to make positive contributions to the Heat of Africa
project.. Even larger sum should be devoted to lobbying efforts to obtain
favourable legislation for the country's product. For example, Costa Rica
has urged the US Congress to import sugar at favourable rate. The recent
exportation of cassava to China is a step in the right direction. . Much
effort and millions of dollars should be spent on tourism industry,
attracting visitors whose expenditure would aid the country's economy.
Voice of Nigeria (VON), Nigeria Television Authority (NTA) and other
media organisations are meeting up with the challenges of sending broadcast
signals worldwide to foster national interest abroad, disseminate news and
influence the internal affairs of other nations like it did on the
restoration of democracy in Sao Tome Precipe, settling political crisis in
Liberia, Sierra Leone and currently in Sudan. Where she is the chief
mediator and also hosts the warring factions.
Other actions programmes that might be necessarlly include the
establishment of a national information bureau to provide facts and
published statements of favourable opinions about Nigeria in some of the
influential countries in the word like United State of America (USA) Britain
etc. Appointment should be made with key media people and other influential
citizens, including educators, business leaders' government officials and
core Public Relations professionals
For the government to succeed in the implementation of its policies and
programmes and consequently enjoy the respect and continued favour of the
international public, it requires the understanding, the co-operation and
indeed the involvement, as appropriate, of the international public. For the
country to take back its place as the giant of Africa, all hands must be on
deck.

Original_Search_Title: Food Insecurity Getting Worse
Headline: Mozambique: Food Insecurity Getting Worse
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambique's Minister of Agriculture, Tomas Mandlate, on Monday
expressed concern at the deteriorating state of food insecurity in the
country, which meant that Mozambique was still a long way from achieving the
first of the Millennium Development Goals - which is to eradicate extreme
poverty and hunger.
Speaking at a session of the African Commission on Agricultural
Statistics, Mandlate warned that continuing drought has placed more than
560,000 people in 22 districts in southern and central Mozambique in need of
food aid.
Currently 68 per cent of the Mozambican population live in the
countryside, and work overwhelmingly in agriculture. About 75 per cent of
rural income comes from agriculture. Despite this, agriculture only accounts
for 25 per cent of the Gross Domestic Product.
Mandlate said that, thanks to poor levels of technology, minimal use of
modern inputs, and dependence on rainfall, the productivity of agriculture
in Mozambique is very low, as is the level of income it provides.
Average grain yields, the Minister said, do not exceed a thousand kilos
per hectare.
The main food crop in the country remains maize, which Mandlate said is
cultivated by over 80 per cent of all small and medium sized farms. Of
non-grain crops, cassava is the most significant, grown by 54 per cent of
family farms.
As for livestock, Mandlate said the figures point to a herd of over 1.2
million cattle in the country, about five million goats, and 1.7 million
pigs.
Speaking to reporters, Mandlate admitted that it is difficult to give an
exact figure for the number of people affected by drought, but it was
certainly rising.
In the third quarter of the year there were somewhat more than 400,000
people whose food security as affected by drought.
But this quarter the number has risen to about 560,000.
There have been sporadic reports of hunger-related deaths - the latest
in a television report last week from Panda district in the southern
province of Inhambane.
Mandlate could throw no light on these reports, and referred reporters
to the Health Ministry, and the country's relief agency, the National
Disasters Management Institute.
"When there is hunger, the Agriculture sector assesses the situation for
the provision of agricultural inputs", he said.
"But when it is a question of rescue and ascertaining the reasons for
any deaths, that is the task of other institutions".

Original_Search_Title: Rivers Set to Curb Building Collapses
Headline: Nigeria: Rivers Set to Curb Building Collapses
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — Rivers State government has moved against future collapse of buildings
under constructions as it has acquired new technologies that detect
buildings with faulty foundations.
Also, it has ordered the demolition of a hotel and a house for failing
the test of a fortified building.
This was disclosed to newsmen by the state Commissioner for Budget and
Economic Planning, Mr. Ejims Enwuliwe.
The commissioner said to forestall the ugly incident of collapsed
structures in the state, an instrument to be used to test the strength of
walls and foundation of buildings has been acquired by the state
government.
He said with the acquisition of the instrument known as Smeck Hammers,
any structure including walls, foundation and blocks, which do not meet the
required standard for modern construction, would be destroyed to avert
future occurrences.
The commissioner said to keep the state clean and ensure the safety of
lives and property, one building on King Jaja Street, Port Harcourt and a
hotel in GRA Phase II have been earmarked for demolition, saying the
exercise is a continuous one.
According to him, Rivers State government has concluded plans to
establish an ethanol factory in partnership with Nigerian National Petroleum
Corporation (NNPC) in the state.
Enwuliwe, who said the offer of providing 10 hectares of land to host
the company, is open to communities in the state, noted that the land when
provided would be used for the cultivation of cassava and sugar cane which
are major components of ethanol, a major component for the production of
petrol.
On the recently launched Home Grown School Feeding and Health Programme,
the commissioner said an Executive Council Committee is already working out
details of its implementation and used the opportunity to call on interested
food vendors in the various localities to apply to his office.
He asserted that the launch of the massive food production programme in
the state was aimed at empowering the people through agriculture stressing
that money for the programme would soon be released to participants. Mr
Ejims Enwukwe also said that the State Executive Council received the report
on the celebration of the just concluded World Food Day which was a huge
success, the qualification of Dolphins Football Club for the CAF Cup
Championship as well as a number of awards conferred on the State Governor,
Dr Peter Odili for his leadership qualities in the state by notable
institutions, and congratulated him on the achievements.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture: Lagos Set to Launch Food for Life Programme, As State Marks WFD
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture: Lagos Set to Launch Food for Life Programme, As State Marks WFD
Body_Text:
The Lagos State Government used the celebration of this year's World
Food Day held recently to repackage all its agricultural programmes for the
rest of its tenure under the "Food for Life" project. The State Commissioner
for Agriculture & Cooperatives, Asipa Kaoli Olusanya, made this known in
a chat with Vanguard in Ikeja during the celebration of the World Food
Day.
In his words: "Today, Lagos State will join 35 other States of the
federation and indeed more than 150 other member countries of the United
Nations to celebrate the World Food Day. It is in recognition of the need to
make its contribution towards reducing hunger and improving access to food
by all citizens that Lagos State Government incorporated food security as
one of its 10-point development agenda.
"In furtherance to this agenda, this administration, through the
Ministry of Agriculture and Cooperatives, has repackaged all its programmes
for the rest of its tenure under the "Food for Life" programme. This
programme is aimed at bringing about the structural transformation necessary
for the socio-economic and integrated development of the rural areas and
making food available at affordable prices to the citizens of the State."
Olusanya disclosed that among the priority projects under the "Food for
Life" programme is the establishment of ten modern community-based cassava
processing centres, three of which, he noted, have already been completed in
Erimope in Eredo, Igbalu/Oju-Agerno in Imota and Pota in Badagry West local
government area.
"These would be commissioned by His Excellency, the Governor, during the
first ever Lagos State Agricultural Week to be celebrated this year between
8th and 11th November, 2005," he said.
The Commissioner added that a modern Animal Hospital and Veterinary
Laboratory Diagnostic Centre with state-of-the-art equipment has been built
within the Lagos State Veterinary Complex, Oko-Oba, Agege to replace the one
burnt down during political violence in 1992.
"In order to attend to livestock farmers in the state, a livestock
support service would be put in place statewide. To this effect, the state
has been divided into six zones and each zone would have a team of
veterinary personnel headed by a veterinary doctor equipped with dedicated
GSM lines to respond to distressed livestock farmers within 24 hours. This
service is another innovation by the Lagos State Government and it is aimed
at enhancing the productivity of livestock farmers in the state," he
added.
To boost fish production in the State, Olusanya said that the State
government established the 34-Hectare Ikorodu Fish Farm Estate to produce
5,000 metric tonnes of cultured fish annually and the project is expected to
provide employment for over 5000 people directly and indirectly. "The first
phase of the estate is being prepared for commissioning. These are some of
our priority projects under the "Food for Life" programme," he stated.

Original_Search_Title: Why Ex-Soldiers Can't Ignore Politics - Ewang
Headline: Nigeria: Why Ex-Soldiers Can't Ignore Politics - Ewang
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Group Captain Sam Ewang (rtd), former Military Administrator of Ogun and
River states during the regime of late General Sani Abacha, is interested in
becoming the next governor of Akwa Ibom state. In this interview with Mary
Ekah, he explains why ex-military officers cannot stay away from politics
As a retired military officer, are you not concerned about negative
public perception of ex-military men in politics?
I think Nigerians should be grateful to God that military men are
interested in providing leadership because they are the only people that are
trained as leaders. Today you have engineers, architects, administrators and
others, but management is different from leadership. We are talking about
leadership, which is aptitude to use people to effect a change or cause
things to happen and when you are using people, you don't use them
forcefully, the people should accept to do what you want them to do on their
own volition. What is important is that people must see you as a trustee and
you should be trusted with whatever task is given to you because you are
going to account for them. That is the difference between leadership and
management; that is the difference between ambition and responsibility. A
man that takes responsibility also accepts accountability but a man that is
ambitious will never accept to be accountable. So a military man has been
trained as a leader, to accept responsibility and be accountable and knows
what it means to lead. This is the practice all over the world, including
countries like America, Britain, Germany and Russia. So we should be
thankful to God that Nigerian military men are coming out to provide
leadership to the people.
But there have been several cases of financial misappropriation leveled
against military leaders who have held public offices, so how do you
reconcile this with the view that military men are by their training
supposed to be steadfast and accountable?
Speculation is one major bane of our politics. This is one thing I don't
do and like most leaders, I have been a victim. But it is unfortunate that
in Nigeria today even those we consider educated have imbibed speculation as
a source of livelihood. Nobody is interested in verifying facts. That was my
case in Ogun State; my name was rubbished but I was unperturbed because of
my clean records. That was why when they called me for accountability I told
the lawmakers then that I was not going to the floor of the assembly but the
market place to account for my stewardship. In the glare of everyone and I
want every military leader to be ready for this. So if you have any case of
misappropriation of fund against any military leader get your facts and
figures correct and call the person to account. In most cases the
allegations are unfounded and are targeted at destroying the military. Today
Obasanjo is the greatest advocate of war against corruption, was he not a
military man? Ironically, by our training, military men are very
accountable.
What is your take on the issue of resource control as canvassed by the
South-south geo-political zone in the last national conference?
They are two sides to this. One side is that South-south people have had
enough bitter pills and I think the whole nation must be ready to give them
their due otherwise they should tell God and the people why. On the other
hand, my God says, when you are faithful with the little, more will be
given. I am not in support of someone collecting fund and misappropriating
it. I believe that we need to do well with the little we are given and then
it would be unreasonable for anybody to deny us what is ours. For now I call
on the Federal Government to evolve a better process for serving the people
of South-South. But that should not be an excuse for non-performing
governors. When I was in Ogun State I was receiving N159 million monthly
from the federation account, it was properly managed and there was a process
of accountability of the funds generated from it.
Would you subscribe to the argument that the 25 per cent canvassed by
the South-South is on the high side?
I don't think the present allocation is enough because there is so much
to be done. But my only problem on the South-south issue is that the nation
is playing politics with the people; the people have needs and if I were to
choose, I will go back to the Willink Special Committee (of the colonial
era) recommendations and insist on its immediate implementation. Apart from
that, constitutionally, we have been cheated because originally the
South-south allocation should be 50 per cent. So if the people now accept
less, they should be commended because there is much to be done in the
region. I have toured the South-south states and I know what I am talking
about.
Six years into the administration of President Olusegun Obasanjo how
would you assess it in terms of delivering democratic dividends to the
people?
You will discover that political success is a function of the
environment; Nigerians have been ignorant of this. But at the same time I
want to tell you that in the present circumstance the people will not see
his effort until much later. Take banking reforms as an example- it is when
the dividends will begin to come that people will appreciate it. I think
what Obasanjo is doing is to a lay strong foundation for a strong economic
and political future of our nation. Reforms do often go with pains, this is
why we must give the administration a chance.
You have been quiet for some time, why the sudden public visibility?
Like you rightly noted in 2003, I did not come out because I had no
calling at that time. It was not the right time. Mine is not a mere
ambition, but a responsibility, which is an assignment given to me by God
and as such, I cannot say no. When such calls come I obey.
What do you have to offer in politics?
I have God's purpose for my people and like I said, it is not an
ambition, I am taking up a responsibility to serve, it is God's purpose and
I know what it entails and I hope our people are willing and available
because God is waiting for us.
Let's be specific, what would you say is still lacking in terms of
democratic dividends in Akwa Ibom State that will attract your attention
should you become governor?
I believe that from 2007 we will take the state to where it must
be. Some of our youths left universities many years ago and are still
unemployed. Our graduates are riding motorbikes all over the state and
beyond. There are so many things to worry about. So we must provide jobs, we
have the potentials. Let me tell you that this is one of the most blessed
states in the country. This state has seven natural seaports, it has the
longest coastline, it has a power source that nobody has ever considered,
and we have gas, aluminium smelter, all this resources are available and
ready to be tapped. Some of the industries that were established by late
U.J. Esuene and Dr. Clement Isong, even some that were maintained by
Idongesit Nkanga have all gone. Our youths are walking all over the place
having nothing to do and I believe that in one year we can get all the
industries back, expand them and establish more within one year. This is a
state with hordes of talents but not represented in the national (football)
team- if you have one name find out, it means he or she was not brought up
here. Because a stadium that can be built in less than six (6) months is
not. I am concerned about this state and our people because I have the love
of God for them. Mind you, I have done this job before and not only that, I
am a variously trained leader. And leadership entails training.
You have served as a military administrator in both Ogun and River
states, can you pin-point some of the things you did in these states that
would make people say if he could this in some other states why not his own?
In Ogun State, I stayed for two years- from August 1996 to August 1998-
precisely two years. And in the two years, it is on record that I built an
International Trade Fair Complex, completed a stadium, which was just
started and left by a previous government, built a housing estate, generated
fund from African Development Bank (ADB), re-established 12 General
Hospitals and even though I did not complete them before I left, I put a
system in place where nobody could stop it. And today Ogun State happens to
be one of the few- if there is any other state- that has an international
project completed. I formulated an Industrial Park and generated fund and
left it in an account for Industrial Park, built a special school for the
handicapped. I re-established six farm settlements. I also ensured that I
distinguished one area for crops from the other and established four farm
settlement for cassava and today Ogun State is the only state that is
exporting cassava in Nigeria. I built 1,000 housing units up to 90 per cent
completion. I built a special food market. Particularly in Rivers State, I
want it to be noted that even though it was not state built, the road
between Ikot Abasi and Port Harcourt, that was a gift from the Head of State
to me because that was the only request I made. I could have asked for
money, but I said no, give me this road to be able to work from there and
reduce the burden of going through Aba to Uyo. I could have asked for money,
as many would have. But I asked for that road to take me to and from
Uyo.
There are agitations for power shift in the state; currently the Annang
are saying that it should be their turn to produce the governor in 2007. You
are an Ibibio so how do you reconcile this with your current vision to
provide leadership in 2007?
Well, the unfortunate thing is that we may have lost our sense of
history or maybe we are pretending to have forgotten about history, that we
are one and the same person. The Ibibio, Annang and Oron are one. The
situation is that there is absolutely nothing wrong with power rotation
among various senatorial districts but we must put the structure in place
and produce a leader, because this is the need of our time. I am coming to
show our people that we have leaders and I am proud to say that I am
one.

Original_Search_Title: Creek Heaven, Creek Hell: the Bayelsa Story
Headline: Nigeria: Creek Heaven, Creek Hell: the Bayelsa Story
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Ijaw are desperately in search of an honest, trustworthy,
charismatic leader in Yenagoa. For decades, majority of Ijaw politicians
have been playing on the emotions of the primitive, ignorant, tired and
poverty weak minds of the uneducated and illiterate masses in Niger Delta
region.
I may not agree with the policies of Obasanjo's selective crusade
against corruption but I see Governor-General's (Bayelsa) case as a blessing
in disguise for the emancipation of Ijaws and other minorities in Niger
