counterproductive, in that they generate massive retrenchment in a milieu of
excessive population of idle youths roaming the streets for lack of useful
employment. Nothing can be as repressive as reducing the federal public
service by over 75,000 personnel at a time when the embattled country is
crying for enlarged job creation measures. NEEDS states: "Unemployment is
still high at 10.8 in 2003 meant that 6.4 million people were actively
looking for jobs without getting any. A coordinated implementation of
programmes is expected to create at least seven million new jobs over the
period, reduce poverty and lay the foundation for sustained development."
The tragedy staring us in the face today is that instead of creating new
jobs, the ongoing economic reforms are aggravating unemployment all over the
country.
The obvious consequences of the ongoing reforms are predictable-hardship
and abject poverty. Like all imported reforms by the World Bank and the
International Monetary Reform, they are anti-people. Molesti Mbeki Head of
South African Institute of International Affairs said recently: "While China
has lifted some 400,000 people out of poverty in the past twenty years,
Nigeria has pushed 71 million people further down the poverty lines."
According to the recent statistics released by the United Nations
Development Programme (UNDP), 70.2per cent of Nigerians live below $1 per
day benchmark while 90.8 per cent live below $2 per day.
Since 1999, the price of petroleum products have been increased seven
times in the wake of the economic reforms despite the soaring increase in
crude oil revenue. Ironically, the ongoing reforms have not positively
affected the petroleum refineries which are largely dormant in spite of the
billions expended on their reactivation. The latest declaration by the
National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP), that poverty in Nigeria has
been reduced by 54 per cent is laughable because it is not true. The
unprecedented destitution in the city slums and rural areas are clear
testimonies on the rising wave of poverty in the country.
Nigeria today suffers explosive crime rates in view of the increasing
youth unemployment and massive retrenchments. Both the public and private
sectors are competing on retrenchment levels and each sector seems to
surpass the other in the number of staff removed from the payroll. Disguised
unemployment in visible everywhere especially in the commercial sector of
the economy. One can easily appreciate the prevailing incidence of youth
restiveness, violent demonstrations and industrial actions by trade
unions. In a situation in which pensioners of the Nigerian Railway
Corporations have not been paid for thirty months, about 6000 personnel of
the same parastatal were retrenched in September 2005. Privatisation has
been consistently erasing jobs everywhere. Where do we really go from here?
We should redirect the reforms, to concentrate attention on the real
sectors of the economy and other primary areas. Recent endeavour to boost
cassava production is a step in the right direction. But, we should revisit
the recent national development and rolling plans in order to accelerate
food production generally by mobilising all available manpower and resources
in the country. The rate of mechanisation of agriculture must be advanced to
eradicate the conservative hoe and knife culture which still dominates our
farming practice. Since Nigerians are usually adept petty traders, we need
millions of market stalls all over the country to engage unemployed people
especially women. The state and local government must give this aspect of
reform priority attention.
Meaningful industrial revolution cannot be achieved unless the power
sector is revamped as a matter of urgency. Nigeria deserves more practical
education at all levels-primary, secondary and tertiary. Universities and
polytechnics should be provided with well equipped industrial centres as in
Europe and North America. Massive training of health personnel should be a
critical policy in strategic reforms particularly in the countryside. We
should take a cue from Kenya, where rural health training is given prime
consideration. The Land Use Act must be reviewed without further
delay. Millions of houses should be constructed through the adoption of
portable houses and housing cooperatives.

Original_Search_Title: Country Adds Famine to Its List of Woes
Headline: Cameroon Adds Famine to Its List of Woes
Body_Text:
Yaounde — As a result of two decades of decreasing economic activities, Cameroon
is now in a serious financial crisis that is threatening the United Nations
Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).
Cameroon has committed itself to the MDGs, adopted in September 2000 by
189 world leaders, to halve poverty by 2015.
The economic crisis has forced Cameroon to reduce public investment and
increase borrowing from international financial institutions.
Cameroon is relatively rich. It has considerable reserves of petroleum,
wood, cocoa, coffee, aluminum, sugar cane, bauxite, rubber, natural gas,
iron and hydroelectric potential.
Under the staggering weight of its debt, Cameroon's economy is
stagnating. In 20 years, the country's debt has risen from 2.9 billion
dollars in 1983 to 8.5 billion in 2004, according to official figures.
Now Cameroon can add famine to its list of woes, especially in its
southern provinces where nearly two-fifths of the country's 16.5 million
people live.
"About 250,000 people are affected by the famine in the extreme north,
one of the country's poorest regions, and more than a million need emergency
aid," Justin Bagirishya, the country's regional director of the World Food
Programme (WFP), told IPS. "In October, the WFP will provide emergency
rations to the needy."
"The recurrence, since nearly ten years ago, of food insecurity in this
part of the world makes the realisation of MDGs a hypothetical enterprise,"
said Germaine Bitanga, an official at the Ministry of Social Affairs.
Cameroon's Extreme North province is situated in the Sahel, some 1,000
kilometres from the capital, Yaounde. The province is vulnerable to natural
disasters such as floods in the rainy season and long droughts, which
sometimes last nine months of the year.
In 2004, the province experienced little rain which led to a
200,000-tonne drop in production, compared with the previous year's 7.45
million tonnes, according to the Ministry of Agriculture.
The province produces corn, sorghum, rice, cassava (manioc), sweet
potatoes, yams, plantains, beans, vegetables and tomatoes. But a large chunk
of the production is exported to neighbouring countries such as Gabon and
Equatorial Guinea.
The WFP, with an 880,000-dollar grant from France, began delivering
relief aid to the province's nine districts on Sep. 24.
However, the aid will not cover the entire relief operation, which will
last a month and cost two million dollars, the WFP said.
The UN's eight steps of eradicating poverty, which are to be realised by
2015, includes the reduction of famine. In 2005, according to the WFP, more
than 1.2 billion people in the world live on less than a dollar a day and
800 million still suffer from hunger.
Economists fear that foreign debt will further complicate Cameroon's
efforts to attain MDGs. "In two decades, the foreign debt of our country has
gone from 2.9 billion dollars in 1983 to just under 10 billion in 2005,"
Francois Colin Nkoa, a lecturer at the University of Yaounde II's School of
Economics and Management, told IPS.
"Unfortunately", he said, "development did not keep pace and the country
became mired even more in the twists and turns of poverty without anyone
really knowing what all the borrowed money was spent on."
"Cameroon has always received large amounts of aid," Jacques Hiol, a
Yaounde-based attorney, told IPS. "But it seems that the aid has contributed
more to slowing down the country's growth and demoting it from an
intermediate-income country to a Heavily Indebted and Poor Country (HIPC)."
Contacted by telephone, an official at the Ministry of Finance, who wished
to remain anonymous, denied Hiol's claims. According to him, the money
borrowed was used to fund development projects such as the Yaounde-Douala
highway in the 1980s, an international airport, schools, and several
hospitals in Yaounde and Douala, the country's financial capital.
According to a 2005 United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) report,
about 50 percent of Cameroonians live below the poverty line on less than a
dollar a day. Analyses of Cameroon's economy contained in the document
"National Accounts 1992-2003", presented by the National Institute of
Statistics in Yaounde in August, paint a bleak picture of the country's
economic outlook. It shows that due to some regions' vulnerability to
natural catastrophe, Cameroon will neither be able to halve the number of
its citizens living below the poverty line nor provide health care to women,
girls, or children.
"The untenable burden of debt servicing and the inability of the HIPC
initiative to resolve this problem only add to the impoverishment of
Cameroon's people," said Jeanne d'Arc Teumo, the president of the Integrated
Programme Against Poverty, a non-governmental organisation based in
Yaounde. "If nothing is done, we will not be able to eliminate poverty until
2150."
Cameroon is supposed to benefit from the HIPC programme, launched by the
World Bank and the International Monetary Fund (IMF), in 1996. The programme
consists of total or partial debt cancellation from the multilateral
financial institutions.
Unfortunately, critics say the initiative has not produced the desired
results, due to the stringent conditions attached to it.
As a result, civil societies have called on the government to
demonstrate greater incisiveness in fighting corruption and making the
justice system more independent so that it can track and prosecute senior
officials accused of embezzlement.
"Our strategy is to improve governance, fight corruption, strengthen
democracy and pursue appropriate development policies and programmes,"
Frederic Nyambi, a Ministry of Planning official, told IPS.
A new hope, however, is on the horizon. Cameroon is one of 20 countries
in Africa and Asia cited as soon-to-be potential debt relief beneficiaries
at the Group of Eight (G-8) Summit in Gleneagles, Scotland in June, and
confirmed in September at the World Bank and IMF general assemblies.

Original_Search_Title: Only for Bad Leaders Freetown Would Be Amman
Headline: Sierra Leone: Only for Bad Leaders Freetown Would Be Amman
Body_Text:
Freetown — You would sometimes not appreciate how blessed Sierra Leone is, but how
politicians over the years have abused, raped and destroyed her, until you
compare this fine country with other unfortunate but better organized states
in the developing world.
I am writing from the Hematite city of Amman, in the Royal Kingdom of
Jordan. The Iraqi boarder is just an hour's flight or a ten hours journey by
car, from my hotel room at the great Le Meridien. A five star hotel to which
the Bintumani arrangement at Aberdeen is a dwarf, while Cape Sierra hotel in
Freetown will be a non-starter. When people become pleased and satisfied
with mediocrity engendered with paralytic idiosyncrasy, then civilized
pedigree must give way to ambivalent naivety. I must of course not tell you
about the excellent road net work and electricity supply supported by
excellent national media in Amman and its surrounding cities, because you
will feel that traveling between Matotoka and Koidu in the north east or
Kailahun down to Kenema, or the mere fact that you really live in Sierra
Leone, makes you look like a primitive chimpanzee cohabiting in the world of
abandoned lower primates of the 600 BC world in the twenty first
century.
Our leaders, I mean political leaders in Sierra Leone, would often like
to excuse their lack of performance and explain away any comparison with
successful states in the developing countries of the world, as detraction
and unfair criticism. To add injury to death, they try to convince the
uneducated and hungry Sierra Leoneans that after all, it is only the Sierra
Leonean journalists who paint things badly but that every other better
organized developing country; often based on the principles of sound
economic and leadership success, is hinged upon the almighty crude oil such
countries have. In an adverse oil oriented economic world, the unprotected
politically unconscious proverbial man buys this easily.
Oil, a commodity, that even primitive Vamboi in southern Pujehun town
knows, Sierra Leone does not have. Despite President Kabbah's oil commission
packed with old friends. Infact some diplomats have reduced our present
government to being a club of old inefficient friends.
The painful, but unrealistic fact though, is that our leaders, I mean
again that specie of Sierra Leoneans that have been responsible for that
countries woes, including the war, have been traveling to other developing
countries such as Jordan, The Gambia and even next door Abuja city in
Nigeria, on weekly basis.
Doesn't the great Kabbah visit Libya every few months?
Fellow Sierra Leoneans, some president of a man, or his spokesman with
the most expensive eye in a human skull in the poorest country (sorry, Niger
Republic takes that accolade now), would look you in the face and attempt to
decry critics like yours truly by saying that Jordan has oil and so many
more minerals.
Hence, its economic and development story. It will be a big wretched
lie. Jordan has nothing. No oil, no mineral resources and no fertile land to
do profitable agriculture. Not even date palms and your favorite potato and
cassava leaves that grow in Sierra Leone without any presidential permission
can successfully survive here. Jordan, with a population of five million;
just a hundred thousand people less than Tejan Kabbah's own census figures,
in Sierra Leone (not the UN figures mind you), has two things: Tourism and,
a serious and conscientious leadership! That's all to Jordan's success
story.  Nature, compared to Tejan Kabbah's Sierra Leone, has been so unfair
to the kingdom of Jordan, that to see a natural green tree or a black soil
between a thousand square mile journey will be a miracle. You compare that
with Kabbah's (and his previous political successors) Kailahun, Kenema and
Kabala topography in Sierra Leone. And when you add that to the diamond
deposits, you will agree with British DFID country director Richard Hogg,
when he said during the UNDP Human Development Index launch in Freetown "you
will have to take control of your resources, that is diamonds". A statement
anchored on the concerns of Unamsil's HIV/Aids officer, Heruit Beficado when
she asked during the same launch, "what is it that Sierra Leone has?
Diamonds', she answered herself. "But who controls them?". I don't think
that UN official will get that answer even before the UN team leaves Sierra
Leone by December.
But who really is in control of Sierra Leone's diamonds? Let Kabbah
answer this question: who controls our diamonds and he must tell you too why
Sierra Leone is so poor. It will be good for Kabbah to answer this rhetoric
question for Sierra Leoneans, but asked by a UN official who has been
watching Sierra Leone for the past few years, before he, the president
leaves for his new found UN job in December, as bush radio now has it in
Freetown. The ten years of civil strife does not in anyway explain the
wanton political and abyssymal performance of our leaders over the past
twenty years. And the world must know that the fearful reality in Sierra
Leone today, is that the very causes of the ten year civil strife are still
prevalent in the country; four years after the government declared the war
over. In present day Sierra Leone, where a government minister is not sure
whether he actually should be defending a government he does not trust
anymore, and in a situation wherein state sponsored corruption has made
mockery of the international community's good will to resuscitate a failed
state, it will be difficult to pretend that all is well; even on the eve of
the departure of a UN peace keeping mission. But go the president must; come
end of his term in March of 2007, even though his best friends say he wants
to leave by December of this year.
Whichever way, those of us who are very close to His Excellency must
tell him that running away before the race ends, is never a brave solution
to a self created problem of bad leadership. As the yeast of political greed
helps ferment the bread of financial self aggrandizement (from donor funds)
by some leading politicians within the present ruling Sierra Leone People's
Party; may we suggest that the assumption that there has never been a happy
ex-president from Sierra Leone, might not be true of my president friend
Tejan Kabbah.
Without a green scenery, without a good soil to farm and without a
better weather to market to visitors, and without diamonds, gold or oil, the
Jordanian government leads its people to make do with nature's
neglect. Hence through focused leadership, the Kings- past and present, lead
their people to economic and infrastructure exemplary, and enviable
heights. In Sierra Leone, with diamonds, the leaders led the people to war,
and with good and agricultural land for farming, they led them to hunger and
injustice. And with tractors from China, they divide and use for
transportation.
You talk or write about such, or the giving of contracts to Egyptians to
supply electric meters in a country that has no conventional electricity
supply, state house spokes man takes you to the cleaner at the Independent
Media Commission, IMC on behalf of His Excellency.  For debasing the
presidency. The British have stressed observance of nine more conditions
before the November Loan agreement and debt cancellations talks. In addition
to respecting human rights, the government must divest its heavy interest in
the ailing state media and scrap some obnoxious press laws such as libel
against the president. In the event that the IMC cannot serve the
executive's directives properly, this is now being used for political weapon
against critics. Remember journalist Paul Kamara in jail now?  Another, Olu
Gordon, is on his way to jail via the IMC, while state intrigues through a
hit-man lawyer, loose-cannon a member of parliament on murder allegations,
while at the same time using the coroner magistrate as pin pong
scapegoat. An American citizen once said that the intention of the British
government to spend some twenty five million pounds sterling to revamp the
justice system is undermined by deliberate effort to which the system has
been using some corrupt lawyers to thwart the efforts of young and
conscientious magistrates who do not want to play ball with insidiously
fraudulent legal minds at the highest top of the judiciary. According to her
with the judiciary, "Sierra Leone is a lost case".
To save face, what some journalists refer to as the officially
unconstituted and headless IMC has been given the uncomfortable task to
begin discussions with a view to removing the seditious libel and find a
suitable replacement appreciable to the authorities.
In a country that has a Law Reforms Commission, whose functions are to
look at the bad laws and advice government. Recently, even without the
perusal of the British donors, the Law Reforms Commission, which many see as
a one-man board room show for Kabbah's in-law, Peter Tucker forwarded to
cabinet a mineral law draft bill that will, like many draft legislations, be
smuggled into parliament for it to become law. In April, the information
minister smuggled into parliament an ill prepared bill that would have made
the appointment to the IMC chairmanship a part term affairs instead of it
being permanent as inscribed in the Media Practitioners Act 2000. For the
first time in many moths, parliamentarians saw the folly and foolishness of
such a bill and threw it out against the rancour and dissatisfaction of the
powers at state house.
In Sierra Leone, like HIV/AIDS, sedition is a presidential problem.
So that it would be seen that the peer group review demand is in
process; not that anybody is serious actually in addressing the concerns-
the less technical IMC too now without any expert and national consultation
with other stake holders, will also be talking about insurance scheme for
media houses. A suggestion that made the military government of 1992 very
unpopular internationally. The president of the Sierra Leone Association of
Journalists, SLAJ has said that any talk of insurance for media houses will
be much unacceptable. He has warned that it would be a sad reality if so
called independent national institutions, such as the IMC, are to be used by
unscrupulous government functionaries to victimize the press.
But as they say, when so called knowledgeable and technocrats ground a
country, it is the role of mere farmers to plough it out. This now appears
to be the situation in Sierra Leone where leadership is only leadership when
it is seen in gray hairs, and the president's old cronies in every sphere of
national life/institutions. For this, Freetown cannot be Amman.
Development strives and leadership focus are far apart.
I love Jordan but I admire its leaders more. They do not take contracts
from Sierra Leoneans and give them to Egyptians. Neither do they jail
journalists for writing the truth.
From Freetown to Amman through Lagos is a ten hours tiresome flight by
every standard. The last time I did such was from New York to Sydney in
Australia. Some fifteen hours direct flight. I told the Jordanian taxi cab
driver, as he took me to see the King's palace: Your King is so young and
handsome, and he replied me; smiling and full of love and trust in his King,
saying "he is married to only one wife, no girl friend, incorruptible, no
allegiance to any particular group or friends, and above all, he is such a
good leader".
I felt so happy for him, and yet sad for my own situation.
My president is handsome too, but in the words of the cab driver, can I
say of him too "he is such a "! (or like my own president would say, 'dis na
bad hart' of this Jordanian driver).
Can Freetown be another Amman? So beautiful, so clean and yet its
people, so stern, dignify and happy they can put their fate in their
leaders. Only posterity will tell.
Next stop, EGYPT. I might meet the El-sewedy company people who got the
contract to supply electricity meters to my country even though they know we
do not have electricity supply in Sierra Leone. May be after Chejan Kaba (as
my temne tribe friends in Freetown would say) leaves power, somebody will
really set up that commission of inquiry to unravel the mystery behind that
contract. God Bless Her Majesty's Government for continuous support despite
.

Original_Search_Title: Togbe Afede X1v Calls for Positive Attitudes Towards National Dev't
Headline: Ghana: Togbe Afede X1v Calls for Positive Attitudes Towards National Dev't
Body_Text:
Accra — The Agbogbomefia of the Asogli state, Togbe Afede XIV, has called on Ghanaians to stop complaining, but show positive attitude towards work in order to achieve maximum production for national development.
He said there is the need to inculcate in the citizenry the virtues of hard work, positive thinking and perseverance and the need to understand that success at work requires hard work and the willingness to do more than one is asked to.
The Agbogbomefia said these at this year's Asogli Yam Festival, which also commemorated his second anniversary as the paramount chief of the Asogli, state in Ho.
According to him, praying for divine and ancestral guidance does not necessary assure success, adding "we need to change our attitude to education and understand that its purpose is to provide us with knowledge and to develop our intellect, not merely to provide us with certificates, only then can we be prepared enough to succeed in life and be able to contribute to the nations development."
Speaking on the theme " Attitudinal Change- An Important Prerequisite for Prosperity", Togbe Afede noted that sustainable development cannot take place in the absence of peace and unity. He added that there is the need for greater commitment to ensuring that governance is more participatory, accountable, transparent, and responsive.
He noted that important developmental challenges are not external, neither is debt cancellation the solution to our problems. "Development aspirations will prove illusive unless we change our attitudes", he advised.
He expressed disappointment at the way ideas of good governance in the country were being undermined by selfishness and greed.
"This is why a lot of money is being sunk into projects that are never completed, local examples include the Ho Sports Stadium and the Ho-Kpedze road, into which billions of cedis have been sunk over the past thirty years", he added.
He entreated all leaders to fight against the pervasive culture of dependency through their works, actions and pronouncements and also fight superstition, which is becoming a major factor in our inability to accept the need for change and to accept the responsibility of our failures.
Togbe Afede encouraged his fellow traditional rulers to bear in mind their responsibility to bring development to their people and to lead the fight to change the attitudes of their subjects in order to make their task easier.
He however called on all chiefs to come together to provide the inspiration, strength and the leadership that their people have been yearning for. He also entreated all rulers to unite to confront the urgent developmental needs of their people.
On his part the deputy Minister for Trade and Industry, Honourable Kofi Osei-Ameyaw said his ministry has put in place a new industrial reform and accelerated growth program to ensure the development of an export-led industrialization strategy and a domestic market led industrialization strategy based on import substitution.
He stated that the main objective of the program is to accelerate economic growth and development in the rural communities. "The program seeks to facilitate the development of commercially viable, export oriented and domestic oriented competitive business activities at the district level," he added.
Hon. Osei-Ameyaw who was also the Guest of Honour noted that the implementation of the program calls for the commitment of both residents and those outside the district. He however disclosed that through the ministries sensitisation workshops to usher in the concept, the Asogli state has been selected among others for the processing of cassava into chips and pellets for local consumption and exports.
He appealed to the citizens to take keen interest in the project especially, traditional authorities who own tracts of lands for the cultivation of the raw material, which is critical for the sustainability of the industries.
The Auafiaga of Ho Asogli, Togbe Lakle Adzie Howusu XII, in his welcome address called on all Ghanaians to be disciplined and to change their attitude of selfishness in order to work hard to be able to achieve our goal of development for better future.
"The Chinese have developed very fast over the past ten to fifteen years, from discipline and hard work", he pointed out.

Original_Search_Title: Are School Children Now Feeding?
Headline: Nigeria: Are School Children Now Feeding?
Body_Text:
Little Daniel, a primary four pupil at the Laminga Model Primary School
in Nassarawa State had gone to school on Wednesday September 28, 2005 hoping
to be served a plate of food at any point during the school hours. He
probably may have paid little attention to the normal one he gets at home
{on good days} before going to school. He had been hearing that government
has agreed to feed them in school, probably from that day.
His expectation for free food every school day was heightened on the
previous day- Tuesday September 27 when the president of the country
himself, Olusegun Obasanjo, came to Laminga to launch what is called the
School Feeding Programme.
The President had told the audience during the launch that government
decided to embark on the feeding programme to enhance the nutritional status
of school children, because of the widespread incidences of malnutrition
noticed in many developing countries. He observed that malnutrition
compromises the physical and mental development of people, especially
children, and puts them at risk of morbidity and mortality.
The president said he was disturbed by the nutritional indicators for
Nigeria which he described as unacceptably low. His information was derived
from studies which have shown that many primary school children in Nigeria
do not eat enough food to enable them attend school properly, pay attention
to the teachers and perform well in school, even as almost half of school
children aged between seven and 13 years are underweight.
It was that reality that gingered the government to set up several
committees to address the issue of food security. In April 2005, Obasanjo
inaugurated the presidential committee on dairy and fruit juice development
in Nigeria with Tetra Pak {West Africa} providing technical support. The
committee which was chaired by Nasarawa State Governor, Dr Abdullahi Adamu
submitted its preliminary report to the president two months later on June
23, 2005. On that day, the president urged the committee to work faster on
the school feeding programme and challenged Nasarawa State to begin a pilot
scheme of the programme.
After the launch by Obasanjo on September 27 therefore, little Daniel
expected to have his school meals ready from that moment on. He was not
alone in that expectation-many of his friends and school mates as well as
adult Nigerians thought so too.
The reality of the school feeding situation dawned on Daniel when he was
served only Nutri Sip, a juice produced by Tetra Pak. It is a 250ml of ultra
high temperature treated juice containing 30 trace elements that government
says, meet the daily requirements of growing children.
That was all on that day, and every other day since then. But Daniel had
thought that as the name implied, it was going to involve normal solid food
and not only juice. At the moment, the pupils in the about eight designated
primary schools in Nasarawa are being fed with Nutri-Sip at break
time. Feelers from some of the schools indicate that the children are simply
disappointed by just being served juice. Pupils who spoke to Daily Trust in
Nasarawa on Wednesday said they thought they were going to have normal
meals. They said they were already getting fed-up with the juice.
The headmaster of LGEA Model Primary School in Laminga where the pilot
scheme was launched, Mr Hasimu Idris however told Daily Trust that each
primary school pupil is served one pack in a day. He said his school had
enough stock of the juice to last two weeks after which stock would be
replenished from the central store in Keffi. He said his pupils were pleased
with the initiative and it was making the school more attractive than ever
before. Mr Idris said the programme involves the de-worming of children and
is a welcome development while thanking the federal and state governments
for selecting his school for the pilot scheme.
The Nasarawa State Governor, Dr Abdullahi Adamu, clarified the issue
when he noted that the school feeding programme does not envisage the
serving of normal meals such as eba, tuwo, pounded yam or rice to the
children, saying it is mind-boggling to even contemplate that option.
According to him, government's purpose is a feeding programme designed
to specifically prevent and correct nutrient deficiencies among pre-school
and school-going children. To remedy these deficiencies and free the
children from the grips of wasting diseases such as Kwashiokor, he said
experts recommended a nutritional liquid feed that would effectively balance
the consumption of staple foods like maize, millet, rice, cassava and other
starchy foods with high carbohydrate content with Nutri-Sip.
"This is a very simple nutritional approach to the crippling problems of
under-nourishment but it is consistent with similar programmes in at least
61 countries, including Singapore, Indonesia, the United States of America
and South Africa", he said.
More than 450,000 children in the government schools the governor said,
are to benefit from the scheme as the liquid feed has tremendous advantages
over other forms of feeding and does not require refrigeration and is
therefore suitable for storage and administration in rural areas.
"Most importantly, it is healthy because it is a full meal with all the
known nutrients added to it", he added.
The governor said he had so far ordered and paid for 10 million units
the juice which is produced in South Africa and out of this, has taken
delivery of 8 million units currently in the stores in Keffi, Lafia and
Akwanga from where they are taken by special delivery trucks for
distribution to schools in the pilot scheme.
The governor noted that the school feeding programme is a capital
intensive one and would certainly face avoidable problems if it is
import-dependent. He reiterated his belief in local production of the feed
supplement as the only guarantee for the survival of the programme. One of
the partners of Tetra Pak, Solea of USA, conducted laboratory tests of
powder samples of millet, maize, sorghum, cocoa and cassava and local
production is expected to begin between now and the first quarter of
2006.
Daily Trust learnt during the visit to Laminga that contrary to the
widespread belief that the programme was going to involve students in junior
secondary schools, only primary school pupils are yet benefiting from the
programme. The headmaster of the school told our reporter that the scheme
had not yet been extended to the secondary school.
Our enquiries at the Science Primary School in Kuje, in the FCT where
the programme was also launched, indicates that unlike in Nasarawa, pupils
are served at least one meal a day. The school which is financed by the
British Airways, UNESCO and the FCT with the support of parents, engages the
services of 18 cooks to feed its 1,641 boys and 1,808 girls with one meal a
day. The headmistress, Mrs Pauline Goro-Daniel told Daily Trust that the
meals are provided at the morning, afternoon and evening sessions and by
4:00pm, every pupil must have been fed.
She said the pupils are fed with beans and yam porridge. The
headmastress said on Saturday, October 8, parents would meet to launch a
feeding programme where they are expected to donate food items and cooking
materials to support the programme, adding that the programme in the FCT was
community -driven with a large contribution from the federal government. Mrs
Goro-Daniel expressed happiness with the scheme, saying it has attracted
more pupils to the school even as there are no more vacancies. According to
her, the feeding programme has so far, enhanced prompt attendance in
school. A pupil, Patience Danladi told Daily Trust that she was happy that
the programme had saved her from running back home at break time for
food. Also, Daniel Marcus, another pupil, thanked the government for
providing the meals, saying he wished to stay longer in school to have
additional meals like Oliver Twist.
The national school feeding programme was conceived by the federal
government as part of its reform agenda for the education sector as
contained in the Millenium Development Goals{MDGs}which are to achieve
universal basic education and to promote gender equality and empower
women. The concept relates to the provision of meals that are nutritionally
adequate, able to meet the health needs of the child as well as reduce
hunger during school hours. The specific objectives of the programme
include; eradication of hunger among children, improvement of nutritional
status of school children in primary and junior secondary schools, increase
in enrolment and retention of school children, enhance performance of
children in sustainable educational development, reduce morbidity and
mortality rates among school children, and stimulate local food production
and partnerships with stakeholders, etc. To ensure sustainability of the
programme, President Obasanjo has directed the National Planning Commission
to come up with a proposal to to that effect.
The experience derived from the Nasarawa experiment according to
Obasanjo, would give the federal government the basis for its transformation
nationwide. It is envisaged that the programme when fully implemented
nationwide, would be feeding 27 miiloion children annually.

Original_Search_Title: Non-Oil Export: Revitalising a Goldmine
Headline: Nigeria: Non-Oil Export: Revitalising a Goldmine
Body_Text:
The inability of a nation to play and compete in the global economy has
been a major factor responsible for the weak growth and development
indicators in indolent nations. Nations all over the globe now struggle to
dominate external markets as the most effective method of creating wealth
and attaining economic power.
The degree at which a nation floods the global market with its products
as well as their quantity, quality and acceptance in the market determines
the size and strength of its total volume of trade at the global level. This
volume of trade determines the degree of the nation's control of global
economic activities and, by extension, the influence it exerts on the lives
of other nations and the entire global politics.
Some nations produce goods and services in excess. Other nations are in
high demand of that excess. The ability of a nation to produce more than its
domestic requirements offers it an advantage to export the excess to earn
more foreign exchange.
The concept of export is an unending one, no matter the economic
policies enforced and barriers placed either by the developed nations or
their agents like the World Trade Organisation and the World Bank, among
others.
At various occasions, poor nations in the world are the feeding tube to
the developed nations in terms of raw materials and cheap
labour. Apparently, most of the richer countries have less comparative
advantage in terms of raw materials and needed resources for use in their
industries. Nevertheless, that has never been a challenge to them. These
developed nations have placed priority on the high-technology and
skill-intensive production which is being used to produce the finished goods
mostly dumped in the poor nations.
Before the oil boom of the 1960s, the economy of Nigeria was relatively
empowered by the non-oil sector, particularly the agriculture and
manufacturing sectors, which contributed the highest proportion of the
GDP.
The dominant role of agriculture in Nigeria's economy was such that with
very little support from government, agriculture was able to grow at a
sufficient rate to provide adequate food for an increasing population, raw
materials for the emerging industrial sector, increase public revenue for
government, expand employment opportunities and boost foreign exchange
earning.
The little support given by the government, especially during the
colonial rule, to agricultural development was concentrated on export crops
like cocoa, groundnut, palm produce, rubber and cotton, since food
self-sufficiency did not seem to pose any problem worthy of public
attention.
Besides agricultural commodities, Nigeria has vast deposits of solid
minerals such as Iron ore, coal, lead, zinc, tin, columbite, kaolin, gypsum,
barite, phosphate, bitumen and gold, among others.
The neglect of agriculture and solid minerals development began in the
first decade of the country's independence, rapidly worsened in the second
decade and finally climaxed in the 90s as a result of the oil boom which
created serious distortions in the economy and exacerbated mass labour
migration from the agricultural sector to the cities. Since then,
agricultural activity declined, resulting in the sector reducing its status
as the mainstay of the Nigerian economy.
Nigeria, as a nation has obtained the needed human and material
resources for both domestic and export market, but that has not been the
challenge. The country has obtained most factors of production, but it is
more difficult to obtain an institution that combine and transform these
factors into production and wealth.
Recent survey shows that even though Ghana and Nigeria are the leading
cocoa producing countries, an average cocoa farmer in these countries has no
knowledge of what will be the finished products of the raw cocoa. All what
they are after is to produce and sell at the price fixed by the
International Cocoa Organisation in London.
In spite of Nigeria's large population and high demand for consumer
goods, the country has hardly any indigenous firms capable of operating as a
large food processing industry or conglomerate. Most of the roughly 180
companies listed in the Nigeria Stock Exchange are multinationals or
organisations that were initially established and managed by foreign
companies.
Many believe that the obstacle to attaining a reliable non-oil export in
Nigeria has to do with the neglect of the sector by both government and the
public. Government, on its part, has failed to provide the necessary
environment for the private sector to grow and meet the export requirement,
specifically to meet the demand of the competitive foreign markets.
Nigerian governments, for the past decades, invested tens of billions of
dollars in building infrastructure, but contributed little to the growth of
the private sector due to the poor maintenance culture of resources and high
level corruption. Government's inability to provide the required incentives
coupled with the problems of bureaucratic bottlenecks by government agencies
such as Ports Authority, Customs and the Police also immensely contributed
to the collapse of the private sector.
On the public side, non- export attitude and poor perception of the
sector has contributed much to its neglect. Prior to oil discovery,
Nigerians achieved some level of progress in capacity building, especially
in agriculture, but later misplaced the priority and relied on revenue from
the oil sector.
As in the industrialised nations, where firms struggle to learn best
practices from the successful ones, in Nigeria firms' ability to learn and
introduce new technology to add value to the raw products is very
little.
Nigerians have not yet appreciated the productive sector irrespective of
the abundant opportunities it generates through wealth creation, employment
and revenue generation. The rich in the society have considered
manufacturing as a long term investment and not lucrative enough compared to
equity market, liquid investments, banking and insurance as well as oil
sector.
The intermediate and lower class individuals in the country are not
involved in the small scale industrial business but in petty businesses,
hiding under the endemic constraints attached to manufacturing
business.
Other obstacles impeding the growth and expansion of the non oil sector
is the high costs of local production and of doing business in general which
have the tendency of rendering locally produced goods less competitive at
home and abroad. Contributing significantly to this phenomenon are the high
cost of corporate borrowing and difficult access to credit and other
infrastructure deficiencies.
Economists believed that wealth can only be created through adding value
to non-value particles. This is the belief of modern and the developed
economies. The rich nations are becoming richer while the poor nations are
becoming poorer just because their ability to add value and transform,
through using technology, the non-value particles has differed.
Recognising the new challenge in the world export market of the need to
export value added products as against the raw products, the federal
government recently approved the National Export Strategy framework towards
the development and promotion of the non oil export and made it a priority
sector in the national economic policy thrust of the administration.
President Olusegun Obasanjo said recently at the final stakeholders'
consultative seminar on the new National Export Strategy in Abuja that his
administration believed that issues of eradication of poverty and hunger,
provision of gainful employment, improving material health and the reduction
of child mortality, among others, would only be fairly settled when non oil
export performance is at its best.
The goal expected to be achieved in the new export strategy framework,
according to Obasanjo, are enormous. He said: "Our efforts require the
making of highly critical policy choices that would clearly define the path
and pace of our future economic and social development". The policy choices
of the new document, according to him, are tailored towards the protection
of strategic local and infant industries in spite of globalization.
He urged Nigerians to cultivate an export culture, re-orientate
themselves and take visionary decisions that will take off from education
curricula which, subsequently, will enable Nigeria experience the remarkable
economic transformation that some fast growing developing countries in the
world today are enjoying.
The Special Adviser and Chief Executive Officer of the Nigerian Export
Promotion Council (NEPC), Mrs. Gladys Modupe Sasore, said the new document
circulated widely for comments and inputs, stressing that it is the product
of over twelve months of technical collaboration between the Commonwealth
Secretariat (COMSEC) in London and the NEPC.
She said the strategy document attempts to proffer permanent solutions
to the major impediments, adding that "it is a structured compilation of
private sector submissions on ways to harness the various efforts aimed at
promoting non-oil export in the spirit of public-private sector
partnership."
Stakeholders at the recent seminar on the new strategy, which held in
Kano, Lagos and Port Harcourt, recommended that:
· Government should involve the private sector at every step of policy
formulation for non oil exports.
· NEPC should be granted autonomy to enhance professionalism, including
prompt decision making and use of available fund.
· Media and all publicity programmes should be tailored to suit
grassroots understanding to enable proper awareness, training and
participation in export business.
· Creation of Export Production Village (EPVs) in local government
areas based on products sector advantages to be co- financed by LGAs, states
and federal government.
· Export Production Village (EPVs) on agricultural produce to be given
greater attention and encouraged through provision of incentives, valuable
addition, preservation and storage facilities.
· Garment factories should be established to utilize Nigeria's share
from Agoa and generate jobs for women.
· Shippers and exporters should be protected through the enactment of
an Act for Cargo Defence Council's Law.
The potentials of the non oil export has attracted the attention of the
Obasanjo-led administration which has set up presidential committees to
identify, promote and seek export markets for some agricultural produce such
as Cassava, Cocoa, Groundnuts and Cotton.
The Commerce Minister, Ambassador A.D Idris Waziri, who is the Chairman
of the Presidential Committee on Cassava Initiative and a member of several
other committees, said recently that from Cassava alone Nigeria can generate
about 12 billion Dollars in foreign exchange. He said discovery has also
been made on the cassava leaf which has huge amount of protein and is good
as animal feed, adding that the sale of cassava leaf alone can earn the
country 7 billion Dollars.
With this potential alone, the minister said, the revenue generated from
the commodity can overtake the revenue generated from the Nigeria oil
export. The commodity has a multiplier effect for the economy by generating
employment apart from ensuring food security. Studies have shown that
Nigeria is producing 35,000 metric tones of cassava and has a domestic
market capacity of about 80,000 metric tones.
China has already made a request of about 150,000 metric tones of
Cassava chip from Nigeria even though the country can for now not even meet
its internal demand for the commodity.
Recently, Waziri also told journalists that the country has realised the
sum of 150 million Dollars from the export of sesame seed during the 2004-
2005 cropping season. This is another crop which also suffered neglect from
both the farmers and the authorities, especially in adding value through the
use of modern technology. Waziri said that the country's sesame seed export
market was increasing with the entrance of China and South Korea, in
addition to Japan and Europe.
"Some 150 tons have already been exported to China with another
consignment of 500 tons ready for shipment," he told journalist. The
minister said that sesame seed export now ranked second after cocoa in terms
of value and volume of crop export, adding that with the steady increase in
production over the next three years, it would overtake cocoa. According to
a recent survey, Nigeria is the second largest producer of the crop in
Africa after Sudan and ranks seventh in the world.
The president of the National Sesame Association of Nigeria, Sheriff
Balogun, estimated that about 750 million dollars would be realized from
sesame seed export within the next three to four years. He said the
association was intensifying efforts to raise the volume of export to about
1 million tonnes.
Nigeria has increasingly been loosing faith on Cocoa production even as
the commodity has continued to gain acceptance in the international
market. Production has also fallen from more than 200,000 tonnes annually to
less than 130,000 tonnes. A concerted effort needs to be made on training
the farmers to use more efficient technologies in producing cocoa and
retraining them on how to produce quality commodity through appropriate
drying methods.
The commodity has been their major sources of foreign exchange for Ivory
Coast and Ghana. These two countries rank first and second largest producers
of the crop respectively. Experts have criticized the traditional attitude
of selling the raw crop to the multinational companies like Cadbury and
Nestle which are reaping billions of Dollars through the crop. They argued
that if the cocoa producing nations will do more in adding value to the crop
the benefits will be higher than expected.
Cotton plays a significant role in the livelihoods of poor communities
in Northern Nigeria, and it is believed to have the potentials of boosting
the incomes of poorer farmers and textile workers. With the recent ban on
textile products, some of the textile manufacturing outfits are most likely
to be back on track. The sector, if really exploited, would be a goldmine
and may even restore its pre- independence period glory.
These are a few of the non-oil commodities the country is blessed with
in abundance which have the prodigious potentials of propelling the its
economy to advancement with highly quantitative and qualitative production
and export.
To achieve this, the government of Nigeria must create the enabling
environment for such production and facilitate such export with relevant
policies. It should also facilitate the establishment of export oriented
industries. Nigerians, on the other hand, must divert their attention from
mere marketers of foreign firms to producers of goods and services by
tapping and harnessing the non-oil potentials of their country.

Original_Search_Title: Apostolic Church to Give Out Maize
Headline: Zambia: Apostolic Church to Give Out Maize
Body_Text:
THE New Apostolic Church will distribute 100 tonnes of relief maize to
over 2,073 households in Zambia to mitigate the current food shortage caused
by a partial drought especially in rural areas.
New Apostolic Church Zambia-Malawi president, Charles Ndandula said
apart from the 100 tonnes relief maize for the affected families, the Church
would also distribute 25 tonnes of maize seed to vulnerable but viable
farmers.
The New Apostolic Church would also encourage farmers to plant drought
resistant crops such as cassava if the effects of inadequate rains were to
be minimised.
The Church has trained nine farmers from drought affected areas that
would in turn train others on the management of drought resistant
crops.
It would also provide cassava stems for farmers intending to diversify
from maize.
The whole process of seed pack support, drought resistant crop
management and the distribution of relief maize would cost over K305
million.
In cooperation with the Henwood Foundation, the Church was running
programmes to support the vulnerable in society.
About 100 farmers were also trained during the 2003-2004 farming period
in seed manipulation.
Meanwhile, the ministry of Agriculture and Cooperatives has urged relief
food providers in Zambia to ensure that every vulnerable family benefited
from the aid and not a few selected elite groups.
The ministry has also said guidelines from the Office of the
Vice-President should be followed by everyone.
Agriculture Minister Mundia Sikatana said through his data manager for
policy and planning Michael Isimwaa during the launch of the draught relief
programme for the New Apostolic Church yesterday that Government alone could
not afford to give relief to all affected people.
Mr Isimwaa said the gesture by the New Apostolic Church to concentrate
on the rural community was most commendable because it would prevent people
from selling their assets to get food.

Original_Search_Title: School-Feeding Programme Helps the Whole Family
Headline: Malawi: School-Feeding Programme Helps the Whole Family
Body_Text:
Tekerani — Mpudu Mulende scrapes every last bit of the nutritious soya porridge
from his plate. There is no food at home, and this one meal a day he gets at
school is all the six-year old can rely on.
His parents were lucky: his school is one of 249 in drought-hit southern
Malawi that are part of a UN World Food Programme (WFP) initiative that
keeps 200,000 children in class by feeding them.
There is a further incentive for parents to keep their children in
school, despite the pressure to pull them out to help a struggling
household: 18 consecutive days of attendance each month earns a home ration
of 12.5 kg of maize-meal, the staple food.
"While our enrolment figures have gone up, the enrolment figures in the
neighbouring schools not under the programme have dropped," noted Mulende's
headmaster, Lloyd Mashoni.
Malawi's subsistence farmers, who struggle to make ends meet at the best
of times, have suffered the worst drought in a decade, compounded by the
late delivery of subsidised seeds and fertiliser. Close to five million
people will be in need of food aid between now and March next year.
"We can see the impact of the failure of the maize crops around us -
parents are asking us for more food. They have resorted to eating cassava -
even the banana crop has not done well," said Mashoni.
His school near Tekerani village, about 98 km from Malawi's commercial
capital, Blantyre, lies in the hilly Thyolo district, which is among the
seven worst-affected districts in the country. There is no maize available
in the local markets - only tomatoes, green bananas and mangoes.
Mashoni's school has started a kitchen garden with the help of the UN
Food and Agricultural Organisation to support the upkeep of the school and
its poorest pupils.
"We sold some of our produce - tomatoes, cabbage, maize and mustard seed
- which has helped us buy sports uniforms for our students; the rest we gave
to needy students," said deputy head Patricia Dzumbira.
Ruth Master, another six-year-old, skips along to join the growing queue
of children waiting for their daily bowl of soya porridge. "Many ask for
more, but we cannot give them more; we have to feed them tomorrow as well -
we have limited resources," said Mashoni.
With the help of volunteering parents, the school prepares six 25 kg
bags of soya blend every day. Last year it had an almost 100 percent pass
rate for its Grade 8 exams. As Mashoni observed, "With some food in this
situation of drought, at least these children have a chance."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Republic
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Republic
Body_Text:
I AM a great fan of the Adventures of Tintin created by an author simply
identified as Herge. It is a series that mirrors the sagas of a young
reporter who ventured into the world of espionage, politics, crime and
adventure to solve mysteries and bring bad eggs to justice. One of the
episodes is titled: Tintin and the Picaros. It is set in an imaginary Latin
American Republic of San Theodoros, which routinely went through a series of
military revolutions. It features two arch-rivals, General Alcazar and
General Tapioca.
Alcazar was the leader of the first revolution who was later ousted by
his Chief of Staff, General Tapioca and fled into exile in Europe, where he
later met Tintin and his friends. Alcazar did menial jobs but at the same
time shopped for arms and foreign sponsors to flush Tapioca from power. He
later returned to San Theodoros, formed a band of guerrillas known as the
Picaros and waged a war from the bush. Meanwhile, his rival, Tapioca had
renamed the capital city of his country "Tapiocapolis" and promoted all his
supporters to the rank of Colonel.
Eventually Tintin helped Alcazar to regain power, and the new despot
proceeded to rename the capital of the country as "Alcazaropolis". This was
the general outline of the character of countries referred to as "Banana
Republics". Herge illustrated this (without using the term directly) by
depicting a scene where Tintin was hiding in a banana plantation from
Tapioca loyalists. A knife thrown into the plantation hacked off a bunch of
bananas, which then fell on Tintin's head.
"Banana Republic" was a term coined to reflect the backwardness of the
political culture of emergent nations, which had either just gained their
independence or had just overthrown their monarchies. Always under the heavy
thumb of despots who did not understand the finer values of governance and
public service, these "republics" were personal estates of the
rulers. Therefore, you could always depend on the rulers to cling to power
for life and transfer it to their sons or wives if possible when there were
dead. But ironically, they were almost always under the direct influence of
foreign powers who manipulated factions of the ruling class against one
another, thus creating coups and counter-coups.
A "Banana Republic" is laughably imperfect, lacking in civilised values,
extremely violent and thoroughly Hobbesian. The citizenry have been cowed by
brute force, live in fear of the authorities and have no claims to civic
liberty or rights. They have been conditioned, like the San Theodorans, to
hail every new arrival in the seat of power: "viva el heroe!". These are
some of the undignifying attributes of a "Banana Republic" which make
Nigerians to cry: "is this a Banana Republic?" whenever a leader (from
Babangida to Abacha and now Obasanjo) moves to succeed himself in power and
thus overthrow all our pretensions to a budding "democratic", "federal"
"republic".
Now, if we do not want to be a "Banana Republic", how about a "Cassava"
one? The two "political crops" are related in at least one way. They were
introduced into our environment from the Latin America climes, where "Banana
Republics" once flowered profusely, thus leading Herge to use the Latin
American idiom to portray his satire on the subject.
After 45 years of our independence, Nigeria, some say, is gradually
being built up as a "Cassava Republic". About the only good thing the
international community, perhaps in its crass ignorance, readily knows about
Nigeria is that we are the sixth largest supplier of crude oil to the
world. President Obasanjo's regime rightly claims the credit for Nigeria's
emergence as the world's largest producer of cassava, a farm product that is
assuming greater market value in the world by the day, just like crude oil,
and for the same reason that China and India are rising in industrial
development.
CASSAVA, for goodness sake!" someone once exclaimed. And I said, "Why
not?". It is better to be the "Queen of Junk" than no queen at all, as Moji
Damisa once observed when a reporter asked how she felt being called a name
some thought was infra dig. Cassava is all right. It is our number one
staple in Nigeria, and it deserves to be respected. But what about "Cassava
Republic"?
Some fear that the rise of cassava's influence in Nigeria might be
reflected in the subtle but discernible manipulations of our political
culture, where power, which ought to belong to the people, has now been
hijacked by the Executive. The hand that is promoting cassava is also
promoting a political status for the crop! The voting, where it takes place,
is just a charade to conceal the fact that the Executive had already decided
what it wanted the elections to produce. We may find out that a Cassava
Republic is worked by a horde of mercenary politicians labouring for the
Number One citizen's political interests with hope to be carried along one
way or the other.
This culture, introduced in the early 1990's by Chief Arthur Nzeribe,
failed to produce the final objective of getting Babangida and Abacha to
succeed themselves in office. But it neither stopped the promoters launching
it for Obasanjo nor did Obasanjo desist from patronising them. If this
scheme finally succeeds, a full blown Cassava Republic would be born. It
will possess most of the low-ball attributes of a Banana Republic.
The only difference will be in the fact that where a Banana Republic
makes no pretence of its undemocratic and unstable nature, that of Cassava
Republic is packaged by professional deceivers for the benefit of
professional deceivers. It pretends to be a democracy, just as it pretends
everything!

Original_Search_Title: NDDC Unveils Vision for Youth Restiveness
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Unveils Vision for Youth Restiveness
Body_Text:
Asaba — BOARD and managment of the Niger Delta Devlopment commission (NDDC) have
unveiled the commission's vision to curb the growing youths restiveness and
unemployment rate in the region through sustainable economic programme
driven by agriculture.
The programme would dwell specifically on massive agricultural projects
in the production of cassava, rice and aquaculture, chairman of the NDDC,
Amb Sam Edem, disclosed the commission's plan for the people of the Niger
Delta region Tuesday when he led a delegation of board and management of the
commission on a courtesy visit to Governor James Ibori at the Government
House Asaba, the state capital.

Original_Search_Title: New Viral Disease Attacks Cassava
Headline: Uganda: New Viral Disease Attacks Cassava
Body_Text:
Kampala — CASSAVA has been hit by the Cassava Brown Streak virus Disease
(CBSD) threatening food security in the country. CBSD is one of the
most feared viral diseases that attack all parts of the cassava plant
resulting in total crop failure and it has no known cure.
Emergency crop disease surveillance teams report Wakiso, Mukono,
Luweero and Mayuge districts as the most affected. Other affected
districts include Kaberamaido, Mpigi, Nakasongola, Masindi, Lira, Apac,
Soroti, Pallisa, Kumi and Mubende.
The disease is also spread by the white flies, which transfer liquid
materials from infected plants. The whiteflies are also responsible for
spreading the cassava mosaic disease.
CBSD is now endemic in all East African coastal cassava growing
areas. It is restricted to low and medium altitudes below 1000m. Dr.
Christopher Omwony, a scientist at Namulonge Animal and Research
Institute, said when the disease attacks the cassava plant, leaves
start yellowing and the stem rots. The roots become yellow or brown and
dry up.
"Investigation in Tanzania showed an average yield loss of 34% due
to CBSD," said a paper presented in one of the proceedings of CBSD
workshops in Mombasa in 2002.
Omwony urged farmers to avoid planting any cassava tubers got from unknown gardens.

Original_Search_Title: Rural Development: Urban Drift Can Play a Pivotal Role
Headline: Zambia: Rural Development: Urban Drift Can Play a Pivotal Role
Body_Text:
THE massive rural drift of the last 40 years or so has engendered a plethora of unprecedented socio-economic hardships other than benefits for the majority of Zambians.
It's been an endemic problem, glossed over by economic policy planners without tangible solutions over the years.
Over-population of most industrialised urban centres has not been adequately addressed. It does not match with the provision of an efficient social and public service delivery system.
In the last 13 years, the discourse on unemployment has sucked in a new dimension of mass job losses through retrenchment of workers, as a result of wholesale privatisation of the parastatal and mining sectors.
The effects are being felt far and wide, cutting across a wide spectrum of people. The wounds will take long to heal.
Undoubtedly, unemployment has been a major factor in triggering social ills like crime, prostitution, the problem of street kids and poverty in all its dimensions. This includes lack of income, deprivation in health, education, water and sanitation.
Other off-shoots of poverty are, vulnerability of families in regard to access to physical assets, the state of human capital, levels of income, diversification, participation in the formal safety net and access to credit markets.
These have become conundrums for successive governments and are compounded by corruption and the incidence of HIV/AIDS which has left a trail of orphans, with some joining the ever-increasing roll of street children, sex workers and criminals of all sorts.
The debilitating occurrences have stung and stunned the Zambian people with devastation and languid hopelessness, almost coming to the limit of endurance.
But that's not all!
The rural drift has had an equally bad effect on the countryside itself which has been depopulated and left as a desolate and an undeveloped backwater.
This would be in sharp contrast with the nascent calls by American environmentalists in Oregon and California who want to break the tempo of development and lifestyles in their states by embarking on "rural cleansing."
Since the turn of this century, environmental groups, in their quest to protect the "endangered" bottomfeeder sucker fish in irrigation dams and canals, have strategised to sue or lobby government using America's Endangered species Act and preservation laws, into declaring rural areas off-limits to people who live and work there.
These environmental groups have spoken openly of their desire to concentrate people into cities, turning everything outside city limits into a giant park: marvellous benefits of development?
Yet in the Zambian context life is so simplistic, but hard in rural areas. Even the idea of turning the countryside into a "giant park" would be self-blasphemy and an act of soul-searching.
It is inconceivable to think at the fast rate endangered wildlife species are being wiped out by notorious poachers.
And the one-way human traffic from rural to urban areas is immense. All the young and old educated people have migrated into towns and cities where there is a better promise for a job.
The migrations are despite the visible woes of unemployment being experienced, unendingly, in the urban centres.
Rural areas have been starved of both financial and infrastructural investment at macro level, in real terms, in the pre and post independence areas, making them unattractive places for skilled labour.
Apart from agriculture which pre-dominates as the main economic activity, there is little other investment in the countryside to lure the army of unemployed youth languishing in urban areas.
Since there is no vibrant economic to create jobs, students who graduate from various institutions of learning do not go back to work among their rural people, but seek employment in towns and cities.
The urban and rural divide is ever widening and no measures are seemingly being put in place to close the gap. The ultimate effect of the imbalance in development is the unequitable distribution of economic benefits to the rural poor. And the poverty situation is desperate.
However, a question that has been lingering on the mind of this writer is whether in Zambia, there will one day be an economically beneficial urban drift into the rural areas.
At what stage or level will this backhand drift occur and what will be the factors to influence it?
Per se, it would be a delusion to think that rural areas will be developed by an uneducated cadre of villagers who are currently eking out their existence through subsistence farming. Far from it!
From the outset it would be extremely difficult and long process to change and re-model their mindset which seems to be eternally steeped in stereotype.
Therefore, the concept of full-scale development in rural areas and the subsequent de-population of urban areas may in part be influenced by some of the following factors in this paradigm
. Political government: devolution of power from central to federal government, through creation of states,
. Progressive agrarian reforms
. Participatory progressive municipal governance
. Increase of investment and trade in rural areas.
The devolution of power in the federal government that will culminate into the creation of states that will be the ultimate engine to steamroll the development process and bridge the economic gap between the rural and urban areas.
As provinces will become states, economic and political planning will embrace every sector of society including the grassroots. Economic development will come out of optimal and sustainable exploration of natural and mineral resources as well as other sectors of the economy.
The multi-sectoral industrialisation that will take place in these states will attract skilled labour to work in rural provinces which will become states. The federal government will take full responsibility for the well being of the citizenry through good political and economic governance.
Progressive agrarian reforms will in the long term create agro-based industries in rural areas such as farm equipment, manufacturing, food processing and others.
It is also important for local authorities in rural areas to work in liaison with traditional leaders and play a pivotal role in ensuring that administrative centres and the hinterlands are conducive and attractive to investment and trade to benefit the local communities.
Innovative ideas should be hatched and embarked upon by technocrats in rural councils, ostensibly to create trade and investment opportunities.
As such, businessmen seeking opportunities must be encouraged to invest in appropriate technologies and ventures. Their documents should be processed in the most transparent manner with full regard for the need for sustainable development.
In reality, good trade and investment policy and practice are key determinants of poverty. They create opportunities for employment which is one of the most important mechanisms for income distribution.
However, all this would be utopia without putting in place pragmatic and radical approaches and mechanisms for implementing policies.
It is vital to address these challenges by bringing into focus land drawing lessons from experienced countries where the underpinning factors have closed the gap between urban and rural areas to bring prosperity to the latter.
This means that a critical mass of economic policy thinkers and advocates will have to take keen interest in rural development research to chart the way forward.
Presently, only a minute percentage of urban dwellers are streaming back into rural areas and this is mainly along the line of rail.
These are mostly successful workers in urban areas who are allocated farmland by traditional leaders. Some do not live on their farms, but go there to cultivate and plant each farming season.
Such "migrant" farmers as well as established commercial ones form up a configuration of the farming community while the other component consists of peasant farmers. It is the former group that has little regard for workers' welfare who are paid slave wages, giving raise to poverty.
Retrenchees or pensioners are rarely settling in rural areas because they are scared of ending up as rural destitutes owing to the fact that their terminal benefits, in most cases, are being paid in piecemeal.
They therefore, prefer to remain in urban areas swelling the population of the unemployed while doing all sorts of odd jobs and engaging in micro-entrepreneurship in the informal sector.
But what kind of agricultural and other economic policies can turn the rural areas into safe havens of development in terms of food security, industrialisation as well as general trade and investment?
From the start, it should be stated that there is no shortage of land in Zambia. The State owns the land throughout the country some of it directly and the bigger part indirectly through traditional leaders.
Villagers have been empowered to hold their customary land in "perpetuity" under the traditional set up and chiefs have sweeping powers to allocate or re-allocate such land.
But there are also large tracts of land in traditional and State areas which is uninhabited. This is the land that is often appropriated to commercial farmers under the long term leasehold tenure. Some land is designated as farm blocks by the State and the other as forest reserves.
It is important to note that in most of Zambia's nine provinces, expansive land with rain forests exists and is always beckoning at the citizenry to make good out of it to create food security and employment.
However, there are fewer Zambians with financial clout willing to venture into large-scale mechanised farming while others who have the interest and want to borrow capital loans from banks have been kept away because of high interest rates.
Be this as it may, government should put in place deliberately attractive agriculture focussed monetary policies to enable both emergent and established farmers borrow loans at lower interest rates. This will contribute to increase investment in agriculture and create employment.
Among other things, foreign investment in agriculture should be encouraged, especially in provinces with high rainfall patters like Copperbelt, Central, North Western, Luapula, Northern, Eastern and Lusaka while intensifying on irrigation projects in all regions.
Doubtlessly, if each of these provinces had investment on the magnitude of Mpongwe Development Company (MDC) on the Copperbelt and the former Gwembe Valley Development Company (GVDC) in Southern Province, Zambia would hold a lot of hope for creation of new agro-based industries for rural economic growth.
Another arm that needs strengthening is the Zambia National Service (ZNS) whose capacity should be increased in agriculture to cover the whole country. The institution will take in a lot of unemployed youth from the urban areas to engage in farming and gain an income.
Additionally, greater efforts should be made by government in collaboration with the donors to carry out and support research in sustainable agriculture in the long term.
There is also the need to increase staple food varieties to be grown on commercial basis to include cassava, sorghum, sweet potatoes yams and other smaller grains to lessen dependence on maize which fails in drought situations.
According to food security researchers in Southern Africa, there were previously 3,000 species of plants that were used as staple food crops in Africa, now the figure is down to only 15.
Despite having these indigenous crops, 90 per cent of Africa's major food crops today are foreign. Maize and groundnuts came from Brazil, sweet potatoes from the Caribbean, wheat and rice from the Middle East etc.
Researchers believe that Africa can have more food security if it revived a wide variety of indigenous food crops.
In large and small-scale farming, crop varieties should be increased with a critical need for creation of self-sustaining outgrower schemes in villages to support farmers in chemical inputs and seeds where necessary.
Ideally, such outgrower schemes can emerge out of the current government fertiliser and seed support programme where small-scale farmers are met half-way in inputs costs.
But the structures of such schemes need to be buttressed by a legislative framework necessary to push agriculture forward and sustain the rural population on a viable and long term basis.
For instance in Masaiti District on the Copperbelt Province, a South African Missionary group called Foundation for Cross-Culture Education (FCE) has put together a splendid outgrower scheme for local villagers called the Maize Bank Jubilee Agricultural project.
Under the scheme, which can be used as a model in most rural areas of Zambia, FCE loans farmers fertiliser and seed. They later pay back an agreed quantity of grain into the maize bank.
The maize is then sold to millers to raise funds to purchase more fertiliser and seed which is again loaned to the farmers and the cycle goes on.
The farmers also sell surplus maize for cash and keep the rest for their food needs. The project has worked well since 2000.
FEC Africa Director Jannie Compion believes each individual should take responsibility for change and the moment that is done, transformation will come at an accelerated pace and Africa will be able to adapt to the new trends in the global village.
Indeed, for villagers, land and agriculture are crucial to rural subsistence. Ability to cultivate land on a sustained basis affects economic and social survival.
Good agrarian reforms to include trade for produce will in a large measure contribute to the movement of people from urban centres to work and live in rural areas and earn income.
In this regard, government's rural electrification programme is no doubt on the right track as power will encourage investors to set up high and low profile multi-sectoral industries which will attract artisans and technocrats to work in them.
A new showcase of rural industrialisation is beginning in North Western Province districts of Solwezi, Mufumbwe and Kasempa where large deposits of minerals and precious stones have been discovered.
With the development of these mining areas, a large number of skilled manpower is expected to migrate from urban areas, especially the Copperbelt, to work in the new rural mines.
In due course, these places will be urbanised as more industries will be built and improved social services given to the communities.
But it will be interesting to see how authorities will handle the effects of urbanisation on the mass of people on the periphery and into the rural areas.
Will there be an over-spill of social and economic benefits to the villagers other than the employment created by the mines and support industries?
These are questions that need consistent and pragmatic answers to prevent what happened on the Copperbelt where rural areas have been marginalised with no economic life.
The end game has been stagnation and impoverishment of rural population as a result of lack of economic benefits of these areas.
However, the future will hold brighter hope for rural areas if investment and trade is created there through the setting up of multi-sectoral industries, including agriculture-based ones.
