A simple inventory of the primary produce we have in Edo State presents a range of investment opportunities: rice, rubber, palm oil, cassava, among others. We are very also rich in solid minerals.
Edo State Commissioner for Information and Orientation, Kingsley Osadolor, delivered this address on behalf of Governor Igbinedion at the annual convention of Edo National Association in the United States and Canada

Original_Search_Title: Atiku Urges Leaders to Listen to Masses
Headline: Nigeria: Atiku Urges Leaders to Listen to Masses
Body_Text:
ECHOES of the first relationship between President Olusegun Obasanjo and Vice President Atiku Abubakar resonated in Lagos yesterday at the week leadership awards where the vice president was Chief Guest of Honour.
Alhaji Atiku Abubakar who was careful not to mention the president by name alluded to the crisis of confidence rocking the federal administration when he identified willingness to listen as a major quality of a good leader.
According to the Vice President: "A leader must listen, he must be honest and even handed in his dealing with the people before they could believe in him. He does not have to say the wrong things several times." On the protest against the hike in prices of petroleum products, the vice president explained that the people had a right to protest and a good leader should listen to them.
Apparently criticizing the federal leaders, Vice President Atiku Abubakar drew attention to inconsistency in policy formulation and execution. Citing the government promotion of cassava production as an example, the vice president said government had encouraged farmers to increase their cultivation capacity before giving thought to the machinery required to process the crops.
The Vice President's teeming supporters who thronged the Nigerian Institute of International Affairs, venue of the event responded with a wild ovation.
Speaking earlier, Professor Pat Utomi painted a gloomy picture of life and leadership in the country. Utomi who was Guest Lecturer compared Nigeria to Argentina. He said: "In the 1970s, Argentina was the richest country in the world, but by the 80s, it was drown to the West Africa level of Gross Domestic Product."
Further comparing Nigeria to Malaysia and Dubai. He described "Malaysia as benchmark, Dubai as control. Nigeria is one of the poorest countries in the world today. It is the worst founder of education in the world."
Professor Utomi pointed out the need for Nigeria to have 'her own Sheikh Mohammed (of Dubai), Mahathir Mohammed (of Malaysia), Lee Kuan Yew (of Singapore).
He berated the current set of leaders for lacking vision. His words: "The state of Nigeria is a testament of their lack of vision. I am amazed that we have not had a revolution in Nigeria yet."

Original_Search_Title: USAID Chief Aims to Cut Hunger Through Economic Growth
Headline: Africa: USAID Chief Aims to Cut Hunger Through Economic Growth
Publisher: United States Department of State (Washington, DC)
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — The United States will spend more money to spur economic growth and reduce hunger in Africa in 2006, the head of the U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID) says, urging developed and developing countries to mobilize all sectors of their societies to address the issue of world hunger.
At a September 15 panel discussion on ending hunger in Africa, USAID Administrator Andrew Natsios said the United States seeks to help sub-Saharan Africa achieve its food security goals by helping the region's countries achieve a "sustainable" annual growth rate of 6 percent.
To do that, the administration has requested an increase in its budget for the fiscal year beginning October 1 (FY06) to support agricultural development in Africa, Natsios said.
Achieving sustainable growth is what has helped China reduce its poverty and hunger rates, he said.
His agency strongly supports the Comprehensive Africa Development Program, a joint initiative of the African Union (AU) and the New Partnership on Africa's Development (NEPAD), he said.
The panel at the Waldorf-Astoria Hotel in New York was held in conjunction with the 2005 United Nations World Summit September 14-16. (See The United Nations at 60.)
The majority of sub-Saharan Africans live in rural areas where hunger is most acute, Natsios said, adding that the region's population is expected to grow by more than that of any other region of the world by 2015, thus increasing its need for food.
Approximately 200 million people in Africa are chronically hungry, according to NEPAD. By 2011 Africa will account for half of the undernourished people in the world, according to USAID projections.
USAID's programs will focus on countries that "value" the involvement of their citizens and all donors in developing solutions to end hunger, Natsios said.
USAID's strategy will be to support collective action where private lenders and farmers themselves develop local solutions to persistent hunger and provide mutual accountability for those programs, he said.
In particular, USAID is expanding local alliances devoted to expanding production of grains, cassava, cotton and dairy, he said.
"Broad-based economic growth is critical if Africans are to participate in the new global economy and enjoy higher standards of living," according to USAID.
The agency said its agricultural development assistance is especially directed to countries where food insecurity is greatest and where governments are committed to improving the conditions for increasing growth and reducing poverty.
According to USAID its programs will emphasize these goals:
to improve the use of modern technologies.
to expand credit to farmers.
to strengthen producer associations.
to improve the functioning of markets
to enhance economic incentives for farmers and small-scale entrepreneurs.
to promote diversification of agriculture by the private sector.
to increase exports.
For additional information on development assistance, see U.S. Aid to Africa.
(The Washington File is a product of the Bureau of International Information Programs, U.S. Department of State. Web site: http://usinfo.state.gov)

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Should Listen to the People - Atiku
Headline: Nigeria: Leaders Should Listen to the People - Atiku
Body_Text:
Lagos — Vice President Abubakar Atiku has said the country's leaders ought to listen to those opposed to the recent increase in the pump price of petroleum products, saying listening to the led is one of the hallmarks of good leadership.
The Nigerian Labour Congress (NLC) Wednesday in Lagos and in Benin yesterday, led a coalition of civil society groups in a mass rally to protest the increases in an approach different from the usual recourse to strike action.
But speaking in Lagos yesterday at the Theweek leadership award, Vice President Atiku said it behoves a good leader to listen to the protests of the people against the fuel price hike. Referring to the Lagos rally, he said: "Two days ago we had a rally on fuel hike. We need a good leadership to listen to those people who trekked for several hours."
Saying that those being led "need someone to listen to them, to love and care for them," Atiku regretted that despite Nigeria's abundant hu-man and natural reso-urces, the country "still wobbles," adding that "we have had political leaders all these years but for most of our history we have not had good leadership."
The Vice President said the current administration has tried to provide good leadership by embarking on economic and social reforms as well as fighting corruption, a development which he noted makes it different from past administrations in the country.
He said: "We have tried to provide good leadership in the past six years. We said that we would reform our economy to halt our country's economic and social decline and we have been doing just that. We said that we would liberalize our economy and privatize state enterprises and we have been doing those; we said that we would fight corruption and we have been doing just that; we said we would improve security and we have been doing that.
"Not many people know for example, that we have increased the number of police officers by more than 100. But have we been perfect? of course not; could we have done a few things differently? Of course yes. Have we moved fast enough on some policies? Perhaps no."
"But we have tried to do what we said we would do. And despite our limitations we have done things where others were content with just talking about them and deceiving people. We have provided leadership. We might not have provided what you are expecting or what you expected but I want to assure you that this is only the beginning; our future will be brighter. The lessons of the past six years have prepared us for the challenges ahead.
"Some of our policies have been unpopular. But we have not shied away from taking tough decisions just because we want to be popular. Across this country, we can find leaders who have been providing dedicated service to their people.
"I presided over the last Federal Executive Council meeting, very interesting debate and I was just listening. Everybody tend to talk. And they said we have not thought through some of our policies. Some of them said if we had wanted to export cassava we should have thought through the policy of exporting cassava. Not only just planting cassava, after the planting what is the next thing to do. After the next thing what is the next thing.
"After, the next thing what is the next thing. And suddenly we realise we have too much cassava and there are no machines to peel them. When we realise no machine to peel them we realise we have no driers, when we realise we have no driers we discover that we didn't have millers. And then we are left with rotten cassava. So many people spoke about thinking through policies, the Vice President said."
Harping on the ingredients of good leadership, Atiku said a good leader must lead by example, be honest and committed because "if the leader is honest and even-handed in his dealings with the people, the people believe in him or her. The leader does not have to say one thing several times before he is believed by the led. But when the head is rotten, the rest of the body tends to rot as well."
He continued: "Leadership involves role modeling, that is, leading by example. Leaders must in all their actions demonstrate to their followers that they care for the overall health of the country. Good leaders must bring hope, but they must fulfill the hope otherwise they become frauds."
He also spoke on the need to have a reform of politics in the country in order to compliment current economic reforms and consolidate on democracy, adding that citizens must remain vigilant.
He said: "We must remain vigilant and work diligently and carefully to protect our democratic space and indeed, deepen it. I say this with a deep sense of responsibility. Our efforts at reforming our economy must be matched by similar efforts at reforming our politics. We are getting our economy right but we must also get our politics right because neither can endure without the other.
"We must deepen and secure our democracy. we must move away from arbitrariness and impunity that are the hallmarks of military regimes. Our commitment to democracy must not be merely rhetorical, we must practice it and entrench it in all our institutions, especially those institutions that form the bedrock of democratic systems.
"These include the various arms of government as well as the political parties. The freedom of individual citizens and groups, including the mass media, to freely express their views must be upheld and respected. We must be a country that upholds the rule of law at all times, that is the only way to avoid widespread lawlessness and anarchy."
While saying criticisms is good for leadership, he warned against 'personal and often unconstructive criticisms' which the Vice President noted, could make leaders tune off completely and deprive themselves of good advice.
Earlier in a lecture, Professor Pat Utomi explored the theme of leadership and called on the political class to rise to the challenge of providing good leadership so as to bring the country out of its current underdevelopment. His position was corroborated by Mr. Peter Okocha, chairman of Sub-saharan press, publishers of the
Theweek magazine, who said leaders should be conscious of how history would document them after they have left office.
Four governors, including Obong Victor Attah of Akwa-Ibom, Alhaji Adamu Muazu of Bauchi, Alhaji Adamu Aliero of Kebbi and Alhaji Ibrahim Idris of kogi states respectively, received received awards at the event.

Original_Search_Title: Duke Lauds NDDC On Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Duke Lauds NDDC On Agriculture
Body_Text:
Calabar — The agricultural initiative introduced by the current Chairman of Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC), Ambassador Sam Edem has received a big commendation from Cross River State Governor, Mr. Donald Duke, describing it as a boost to create employment.
Duke, who made the commendation when Edem paid a courtesy visit to his office in Calabar said the introduction of Agriculture and Tourism into the Commission's programme for the sustainable development and empowerment of youths in the region was in consonance with the State Government's development policy.
However, Duke used the occasion to solicit for greater developmental presence in the state as well as the harmonization of projects between the Commission and the State in order to avoid duplication and waste of resources.
He stressed the need for mutual partnership between them, calling the Commission to take tourism drive more seriously.
On the Agricultural potential of the State, he disclosed that according to statistics, Cross River has been identified as the highest producer of cassava in the country as it produces between two to three million tonnes annually.
According to him, NDDC's focus on agriculture with emphasis on cassava production will complement the state government's effort in that direction as well as develop the agricultural potentials of the country.
He lauded the Chairman for charting a new course in economic and youth empowerment drive and his support for the state to ensure that it gets a fair share in the allocation of projects.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture: Panacea to Poverty
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture: Panacea to Poverty
Body_Text:
Abuja — Poverty manifests in several ways ranging from malnutrition, diseases, low rate of life expectancy, child labour, dehumanized working conditions, illiteracy, inadequate and inhabitable shelter, low intelligent quotient and high crime rate. Hence an economy plagued with the above negative indices as obtained in Nigeria can hardly make an appreciable growth not to talk of development over time.
Poverty is indeed Nigeria's major challenge, which, according to the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS) document 'remains daunting.' The National Poverty Eradication Programme, (NAPEP) confirmed that poverty is making more people to experience pronounced deprivation. And that, if not quickly addressed, it can "create a divide that can undermine our confidence."
Nigeria is blessed with enormous human and natural resources, yet well over two-third of her 130 million inhabitants are wallowing in abject poverty. And if the present trends continue, the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) target of 2015 will be a mirage.
We really need to ponder why the poverty level has been on the increase over the years despite the enormous resources and revenue earned by the country all this while. Recall that in 1980, an estimated 27 per cent of the citizenry lived in poverty.
And about two decades later, at least 70 per cent of the ever increasing population wallows in abject poverty in various dimensions. By 1999 more than two-third of Nigerians had income of less than US$1.00 per day and the indices are rising by the day.
Life expectancy mortality rose to 77 out of every one thousand and 700 per hundred thousand respectively due, to inadequate or non functional basic health and social infrastructures.
The country's growth has been stunted with ever growing arm of poor and unemployed people, largely to due dismally low capacity utilization in the industrial sectors of the economy.
However, past governments in Nigeria cannot be said to be oblivious of the depth of poverty in our midst as some programmes were initiated to better the lots of the citizenry. Such palliative programmes or initiatives include the moribund Operation Feed the Nation (OFN), Green Revolution, River Basin Development Authority(RBDA), Director of Foods, Roads and Rural Infrastructure(DFRRI); NALDA, 1991 Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme and Peoples Bank Nigeria(PBN) Agricultural Insurance Company, Family Economic Advancement Programme (FEAP), Nigeria Agricultural and Corporative Bank(NACB) now Nigeria Agricultural Cooperative and Rural Development Bank being an off shoot of PBN, FEAP and NACB all designed to reposition agriculture to its primus place by opening up the rural areas through the provision of infrastructures and credit or loanable funds.
Unfortunately, none of these "lofty" programmes could survive the intricacies of corruption and bureaucracy red-tapism inherent in both the public and private lives and as such couldn't make any meaningful impact on the lives of the rural dwellers.
Well, it is often posited that we have had well conceived ideas and plans in the past but the boat is always rocked at the point of implementation. All the National Development plans were never spared from these vagaries.
Conscious and pragmatic initiatives should be adopted now, to address the problems of poverty and related dehumanizing conditions pervading our land.
The super powers and great economies of this world are loosing sleep knowing well that they can no longer prosper amidst deprivation and hunger ravaged citizens of this world.
The "super rich" among the developed countries have initiated several programmes either in the name of donor agencies, bilateral and multilateral cooperatoration or in recent years, debt or forgiveness which is now being flaunted as political or diplomatic tool.
This act of "benevolence" is to assist poor countries that lack the ability or capacity to pay such debts get off the shackles of debt burden and by implication to enable such poor countries to concentrate on tackling poverty.
The recent report of the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) on Human Development Index (HDI), indicates that average life expectancy in Nigeria has dropped from 51.6 to 43.4 years just as the Country was ranked 158th out of 177th in terms of poverty. Again, this is not the time to start questioning the genuiness or otherwise of the debt which has become so burdensome to Nigeria over the years .
It is quite obvious that the immediate and sustainable panacea to poverty can be found in agriculture. It is widely believed that more than 60 per cent of the population of Nigeria live on agriculture in the rural areas.
No doubt, that it is in recognition of pivotal role of agriculture in the quest for good life and wealth creation that Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO) stipulated that countries, especially the developing ones should allocate 25 percent of their annual budget to agriculture. Unfortunately, what the Nigeria government has been allocating to agriculture is dismal.
Available statistics indicate that the percentage of allocation for agriculture in terms of capital expenditure has been a far cry from the FAO recommended 25 per cent.
The highest allocation so far was 12 per cent recorded in 2004 while the lowest allocation recorded between year 2000 and now were 3.6 per cent and 3.5 per cent for 2000 and 2003 respectively.
Other supportive sectors like education, health and even water resources were equally not spared of problem of under-funding. And the earlier we review our priorities the better for the nation.
For the fact that over 60 per cent of Nigeria's population reside in the rural areas practising agriculture predominantly, if this large segment is reasonably catered for, by way of creating enabling environment for farming and related activities to thrive, the poverty question and its attendant malaise would have been tackled frontally.
Going by the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP), efforts are now being redirected towards partnerships and collaborations with other agencies and groups to source and allocate resources for productive engagements in the economy. To this end, it is being strongly suggested that considerable resources should be channeled to rural based activities because a robust rural economy is capable of kick starting and driving the economy to prosperity.It is regrettable that agriculture is presently plagued by several problems which includes but not limited to inadequate inputs supply, inadequate working capital, crude or rudimentary tools due to low rata of adoption of technology and poor post-harvest technology.Also,the problem of pests like locusts and queala birds, environmental hazards like drought ,erosion and pollution and land acquisition are causing set back to agricultural practice as such government would be doing a good job if functional logistics to curtail these retrogressive impediments.
NEEDS document, being the adopted working guide of the present administration, already enunciated the primus role of agriculture as "main stay of Nigeria's economy" being the highest contributor to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP), largest non-oil export earner, largest employer of labour and a key contributor to wealth creation and potential poverty alleviator. As a large percentage of the population derives its income from agriculture and related activities all hands must be on the deck to revive the sector.
Further to that, the NEEDS programme is aiming at developing agriculture to the level of achieving food security provision of raw-materials and reduction of poverty by enhancing agri-business.
Already there is "presidential initiative" in the development and production of certain cash crops like cassava, rice and even livestock. Yet, as lofty as those initiatives may be, without adequate and functional infrastructure, the initiatives may wither away like previous initiatives and programmes.
Governments at various levels should collaborate to "open up" the rural areas through the provision of feeder roads, portable water, electricity and recreational facilities. In short, a modern farm settlement is desirable more than ever before. Further to that, our research institutes and extension services should be rehabilitated to provide necessary support and encourage innovations among practicing farmers.
Fundamentally, access to arable land and micro credits are vital for proper repositioning of wretched peasants on a good stead to cultivate and rear livestock to the level of sustainability and profitability.
The government, especially at state levels should engage in land preparation and allocating same to farmers in addition to the provision of sub-sidized inputs like fertilizer, agro chemicals and modern implements while local governments should be able to take care of extension services and coordination of cooperative groups.
On the part of the federal government, capital intensive projects like irrigation dams and functional micro credit policy to address problems associated drought and lack of funds to procure essential inputs should be decisively dealth with.
Institutions like NACRB, Nigeria Agricultural Insurance Company and Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme should be recapitalized and made to function effectively.
Profitable agri-business could create avenue for both backward and forward integration whereby employment opportunities and wealth are created.
On the social side, the problems of rural-urban immigration are going to be reversed, as people may now stay back in the rural areas to partake in the agricultural revolution. Even those already stranded in the over populated urban centre would find "village" life more attractive and rewarding. And it will be farewell to poverty.
If we achieve this then we would be on the verge of bidding poverty farewell in Nigeria.
I say amen to that.

Original_Search_Title: Country  Faces Food Insecurity -WFP
Headline: Uganda: Sorghum Has Done Well in Some Districts And Harvesting is Going On At the Moment
Body_Text:
Kampala — YET again, Uganda is faced with food insecurity. According to the
World Food Programme (WFP) Report, 2005 and a Ministry of Agriculture
Situational Report, there is likely to be chronic food insecurity in at
least two regions of the country and low food security in other regions.
The
WFP report indicates that over 3,100,000 people have received food aid
of some kind in 2005. This number includes 1.4 million Internally
Displaced Persons and 0.5 million persons in Karamoja region.
The
food insecurity in IDPs is understandable. These people have not had
organised agriculture for so many years. Areas that are rated as very
insecure include Acholi and Karamoja. Areas that are rated as suffering
from low food insecurity include central and western Uganda, while
areas that have lots of refugees are also predicted to suffer food
insecurity.
The first season of 2005 has performed fairly well
though. The harvesting exercise, which started during the month of June
with mainly beans, is on-going with cereals, especially maize, millet
and sorghum in various regions and this is expected to end this month.
The
projection is that, overall, the harvests from crops grown this season,
especially from beans, maize and sorghum, is above average and should
sustain households in the country up to next season, with some surplus
for export. This is with exception of people in IDP camps and those
affected by drought in localised areas such as Masaka, Rakai, Sembabule
and Kasese.
Over 104,000 households in Masaka, Ssembabule and Rakai districts are facing hunger and death as a result of the drought.
Although
this season was not major for maize growing, being known for having
short rains that cannot support their proper growth, increased acreage
was grown in the districts of Mbale, Kapchorwa, Masindi, Kiboga and
Kamwenge and good harvests are expected.
The prevailing good
markets with favourable prices triggered this. Maize has done very well
in all regions, where it was grown, except in the areas affected by
drought, especially in Masaka, sembabule, Rakai and Kasese.
"In
Busoga, where the country has been getting a lot of maize, the crop was
affected by the dry spell that followed after planting in April and an
average harvest is expected, but not as good as in the above," the
ministry report indicates.
When maize production fails, it has
bigger implications than when root crops fail because maize is a
storable food that can be consumed later. According to Vincent
Galisasanya, chairman Kamuli District Farmers Association, the problem
is made worse by farmers, who do not want to store maize for future
use. "They don't think about the next season. They either eat up or
sell everything," he laments.
Sorghum has done very well in the
districts of Kisoro, Kabale, Soroti, Kaberamaido and Katakwi where the
harvesting exercise is on-going and is expected to end this month.
Sorghum
in these districts is the traditional food crop, but has, of recent,
also gained market because of alternative usage. Epuripuri sorghum
variety is being promoted in the eastern districts, and has a ready
market with Nile Breweries for brewing beer.
This season was not
for growing upland rice and millet in the districts that receive
bi-modal rainfall. In the northern and north- eastern districts, a good
harvest of finger millet is expected because there was a long rainy
reason. A limited supply of both millet and rice is, therefore,
expected from farmers.
Growing of upland rice has become very
popular in the country, and this is appreciated because rice is a good
source of household income and it has a ready market both internally
and across the neighbouring countries.
A kilogramme of rice has
dropped to sh200 for unmilled (kibooko) and sh600 for milled rice in
rural areas. "It is a blessing to us. There is stored food all over the
villages," says Dan Kintu of Kapeeka, Nakaseke district.
In fact,
had there been no rice, central Uganda would have turned into a very
food-insecure area, largely because of the continued failure of
bananas, which are under attack from the wilt.
Besides being a
food security crop, bananas are also good sources of income to farmers,
as well as foreign exchange earner for the country. This is because the
crop is among the horticultural crops in the country, which have access
to foreign markets.
However, there are signs that bananas are not
doing so well. "Preliminary reports from households show that farmers
who used to harvest 16 bunches per week are now harvesting one,"
laments the Agriculture ministry permanent secretary, David Obong.
One
of the constraints to banana production in the country has been effect
of inadequate rains in the last two seasons experienced in the banana
growing regions. The resulting water stress compounded with the various
banana pests and diseases such as weevil, Nematodes, Black sigatoka,
Bacterial Wilt and Fusalium wilt has reduced the quality and size of
bananas being produced.
The spread of Black Sigatoka disease in
some districts is being accelerated by water stress compounded by
nematodes, banana weevils and poor management practices.
This can
be reduced by observing proper husbandry practices, cultural methods to
control nematodes and weevils, water harvesting within the banana
plantations during the rainy season and mulching the plantation at the
end of the rainy season are some of the recommended approaches.
Manure
application should be part and parcel of banana management practices.
This attack by fusarium wilt has been found to be specifically on the
sweet varieties. These are the apple bananas (Ndizi), grosmiche
(Bogoya) and Musa.
NARO has come up with the PHIA varieties
specifically as sweet varieties that are resistant to Fusarium wilt.
These are available at KARI and Banana multiplication centres within
the districts. Farmers where this disease is rampant are advised to
plant PHIA varieties, which are already doing well where they have been
planted.
Root crops are good sources of food security for the
country. Unfortunately, the drought conditions the country experienced
in 2004, followed by that in January to March this year, affected the
production of both cassava and sweet potatoes in all districts where
these crops are grown.
As a result, there has been shortage of
cassava and sweet potatoes for food in the market as well as planting
materials (cassava cuttings and sweet potatoes vines). Because of this,
small acreage has been planted this season, and this implies a
continued shortage of cassava and sweet potatoes for food up to next
season.

Original_Search_Title: Police Arrest Two Activists As NLC Protests Begin
Headline: Nigeria: Police Arrest Two Activists As NLC Protests Begin
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE police have arrested two activists of the Labour and Civil Society Coalition (LASCO) ahead of the commencement, today, of protests/rallies organised by the group over the recent like in fuel prices.
Latif Aremu and one Adedayo, both of whom are members of the United Action for Democracy (UAD), were picked up, yesterday, at Isolo, Lagos for having in their possession bundles of handbills on the protest.
Acting Inspector-General of Police, Mr. Sunday Ehindero, at a meeting with the leadership of the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) on Monday promised that the police would not hinder the protest.
Confirming the arrests, Chairman of LASCO, Dr. Beko Ransome-Kuti, told Vanguard that all efforts to effect the release or the reasons for their arrest and detention were abortive.
He said he made efforts to get clarification from the State Commissioner of Police to no avail as the Commissioner only promised to call him back.
He described the action of the police as overzealousness and at variance with the assurance the AIGP gave the nation on Monday.
He, however, said LASCO was not intimidated by police unsavory interest, affirming that the rally would go ahead as planned.
The Police team led by Deputy Commissioner of Police (DCP) in-charge of operations, Mr. John Haruna, arrived the NLC Lagos sub-secretariat about midday to meet with the planning committee of the rally headed by Comrade Did Adodo.
Sources at the meeting told Vanguard that the labour and police agreed that the rally should be devoid of any intimidation and harassment of innocent citizens not involved in the protest march.
Particularly, Haruna who was accompanied by an Area Commander and a Divisional Police Officer pleaded with the organisers to educate their followers on the need to conduct their activities in a manner that would not provoke crisis. He assured that police were ready to deal with anybody who would want to use the rally to cause mayhem in the town.
"I am pleased with your conduct so far. We are not opposed to the rally, but we expect it to be peaceful as experienced in civilised society. We are ready to protect you and join in ensuring that it goes on well," the DCP said.
LASCO asked Nigerians to resist bad governance and anti people's policies. Labour informed the police boss that it was not at war against with Nigerians, but only interested in registering their opposition to the policies of the present government.
President-General of the Trade Union Congress of Nigeria (TUC), Comrade Peace Obiajulu told Vanguard on telephone that every mobilisation had been made for today's mass protest and rally.
She enjoined Nigerian workers to troop out en masse to express their feelings about the socio-economic and political situations in the country, stressing that "if you do not protest and show your feelings, it means you are not feeling the pains and hardships."
Meanwhile, Citizens Rights Watch (CRW) said it had constituted a team of lawyers to defend anybody arrested in during the protest
Addressing a press conference in Lagos, President of CRW Mr Gabriel Ojumah said: "This government is the most incompetent, the most reckless, most dubious Nigeria has ever had. And as such the masses have passed a vote of no confidence on it. This government must go."
"Citizen rights watch has constituted a team of lawyers to defend participants in the proposed rallies in the event of arrest they can be reached in the following Numbers 08023092351, 014350961, 014361806, 08028339999, 08033028133, 08033461217."
Nweke defends deregulation
Meanwhile, Information Minister, Mr. Frank Nweke, says Nigerians should see the current policy of the Obasanjo administration to deregulate the economy as one sure way of instilling confidence in the country's fiscal position.
Nweke told the BBC, yesterday, that it was unfortunate that some Nigerians - a majority of them well informed and who ought to know better - had come to see deregulation in the context of price increases.
Nweke said the very essence of deregulation in the energy and oil sector would be beneficial to the extent of enhancing "competition . . . guarantee total stability and bring about stability of prices as in the telecommunications sector."
He discounted the idea that President Olusegun Obasanjo was against the subsidy regime not only from his proclamation, but also from the fact that in spite of the huge revenue from high cost of fuel in the international market, there had been little satisfactory response to public agitation as to the end to which the revenue was being put, especially when Nigerians were having to pay more for food and fuel.
Nweke said that it was in recognition of the difficulties associated with deregulation that the Obasanjo administration established the Petroleum Products Pricing Regulatory Agency (PPPRA) with an eye on stabilising prices such that they would not spiral out of the reach of Nigerians, especially those in the low income bracket.
On account of the massive oil revenue in the past six years, Nweke noted that the various tiers of government had been the richer for it, and that, within the distance, "Nigeria has become the world's largest producer of cassava, which has reduced the importation of rice by 40 percent and the importation of vegetable oil."
He justified the regime of oil importation to meet local needs by pointing out that additional refineries were not built beyond 1979, which had made the capacity of existing ones inadequate to satisfy the country's domestic requirement.
Benue Govt asks Police to stop rallies
Benue State Security Council has directed the police and other security agencies in the state to ensure that the planned NLC protest rally against the recent increase in the prices of petroleum products does not take place.
Addressing journalists on the outcome of the council's emergency and expanded meeting on Tuesday, the State's Commissioner for Information, Mr Cletus Akwaya, said the council did not see any direct link between the state and the NLC on the issue of increase in the prices of petroleum products since it was the Federal Government that effected the increase.
He explained that the council stressed the need for the state to remain peaceful and directed further that security agencies should ensure that the citizens go about their lawful duties without molestation from any quarter.
The Commissioner stated further that council chairmen and security agencies particularly the police have been told to make sure that nobody blocks any of the federal roads in the state, adding that the chairmen have been directed to stay at their duty posts throughout the period of the protest rally.

Original_Search_Title: UBA Doles Out N210m to Farmers in Kwara
Headline: Nigeria: UBA Doles Out N210m to Farmers in Kwara
Body_Text:
The United Bank for Africa Plc, one of Nigeria's new mega banks has risen to one of the key challenges to banks in the country, with the approval of a term loan of N210 million to the Kwara state government for on lending to rural farmers in the state.
A statement from the bank said, more than 1000 farmers associations and individuals including youths and retirees involved in the cultivation of rice and cassava are expected to benefit from the facility.
"The loan scheme is an initiative of the state government to improve on cassava and rice production among rural farmers and boost the agricultural revolution taking place in the state," the statement said.
It disclosed further that the facility is guaranteed by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) under the Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme Fund (ACGF) Act of 1977.
The Act gives support to rural farmers who otherwise cannot access bank loans because of their inability to provide adequate collaterals.
"The facility also has the active support of the Kwara Agricultural Development Agency which supplied all inputs including land clearing, ploughing and ridging, cassava stems, fertilizers and herbicides to avoid the possibility of diversion by the farmers," the statement added.
It will be recalled that the consolidated UBA emerged as one of the first mega banks from the on-going consolidation exercise with the merger between old UBA and the dissolved STB.

Original_Search_Title: FG Fine-Tunes Law to Allow 10% Ethanol in Petrol
Headline: Nigeria: FG Fine-Tunes Law to Allow 10% Ethanol in Petrol
Body_Text:
THERE are indications that the Federal Government has commenced working on a law to allow 10 per cent ethanol in petrol, in a revolutionary attempt to mitigate the impact of rising prices of petroleum products in the international market on local consumers.
It was also gathered that the Brazilian state-run oil giant, Petroleo Brasileiro SA (PBR), or Petrobras, expects to start exporting ethanol to Nigeria soon, and the market could reach 3 million liters per day. Samir Passos Awad, Petrobras' Managing Director for Nigerian operations made the disclosure while speaking in Sao Paulo, Brasil recently.
He explained that the Nigerian government is drawing up a law to govern ethanol usage, which may include a measure allowing up to 10 per cent ethanol in gasoline.
Awad based his calculations for ethanol consumption on Nigeria's gasoline consumption of around 30 million liters per day.
The executive couldn't provide a timetable for Brazil to start ethanol exports. "It is too difficult to specify a timetable for the approval of this law, but I don't discard that it could happen before the end of 2005," Awad said during a Brazil-Nigeria trade conference in Sao Paulo Thursday.
Nigeria's President Olusegun Obasanjo is on a state visit to Brazil, which ends later Thursday. Brazil is the world's biggest producer of ethanol.
The country blends 25 per cent of ethanol - won from cheap sugar cane - into its gasoline, while an increasing number of cars run on any mixture of gasoline or ethanol, which is available at all gas stations in the country.
Brazil is already in talks to export ethanol to a series of other countries, including the U.S., China, Japan and South Korea. The Nigerian government is "very keen" to promote the use of ethanol, and wants to encourage foreign private sector participation through joint ventures, said Chief Emeya, chairman of Nigeria's Ethanol Manufacturing Group.
"The price of oil is going up and ethanol is a way to bring down the price for individuals in Nigeria," Emeya said.
Awad said the firm signed a memorandum of understanding in August with the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation. (NNPC) to provide information about technologies used in Brazil to manufacture ethanol.
"We have some options to help Nigeria to produce ethanol, for example, if in Brazil we use sugar cane to produce ethanol, in Nigeria it is possible to develop technology to produce ethanol from cassava," Awad said.
Ethanol exports could help counterbalance Brazil's imports of oil and oil products from Nigeria. Brazil is keen to reduce its trade deficit with the African nation, which in 2004 was $2.99 billion, according to Brazilian government trade officials.
Nigeria's top oil official said ethanol could play a crucial role in a more balanced energy matrix, and that ethanol does not compete with other energy sources.
"I don't really see major competition at this point" between different energy sources, said Dr. Edmund Daukoru, Nigeria's Minister of Petroleum Resources. "Each element in that energy balance is appropriate for particular applications, and we have to exploit those specialties in the energy business," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Democracy And Citizens' Participation
Headline: Nigeria: Democracy And Citizens' Participation
Body_Text:
Owerri — Popular participation of the people in governance is one of the
ingredients that mark democracy out of other forms of government. This level
of participation is against the backdrop that no man (be he an elected
representative or not), has the right to run the affairs of the other, to
the latter's total exclusion and marginalization.
This accords with the understanding that all times sovereignty resides
in the people, and as such, there are moments that require that certain
specific questions or legislative proposals be referred to them for a
popular decision. What clearly emerges here is that the mandate the people's
representatives hold is a limited and not an absolute one.
It is this right of participation in governance, reserved for the people
under a democracy, that informs the postulation of social contract theory
that, government being a social contract between the rulers and the ruled,
the latter have the right of dissent against and, withdrawal of their
mandate from any elected leader, that goes contrary to the terms of the
contract -ensuring "the greatest happiness for the greatest
number". Following from this, Utilitarian philosophy posits that since
"pleasure" and "pain" are the two sovereign masters of mankind, it is the
duty of government, to first, weigh every action or law, in terms of how
much happiness or pain, it would produce for the people. Accordingly (the
Utilitarians contend), any law that cannot ensure happiness for the people
is bad, and should be discarded.
In short, it is a universal principle that governance, should be
predicated on the right of the people to have a say in the running and
managment of their own affairs. And it was the unavailability of this right
to the colonial peoples, that sparked off the nationalist struggle and
agitation for independence.
Under the contemporary democratic practice therefore, certain methods
through which the people (though they have given their mandate to the
elected representatives) are still afforded the opportunity to express their
wishes and preferences in certain critical issues of public interest, have
been adopted and are widely applied. These methods, range from referendum,
popular consultation (as between lawmakers and their constituents) to a
properly articulated and distilled public opinion.
Of all these methods, the most popular, that is usually resorted to,
when significant questions that would have far reaching implications on the
present and future existence of the people, crop up, is referendum. This is
usually the case, when fundamental constitutional matters, are
concerned. For instance, some European countries had to submit the EU
constitution, which seeks commonality among the union members, in some
issues of life, to their respective peoples, for approval. And soon, Iraq
would summit her draft constitution, for adoption, by the Iraqis.
The resort to referendum, is gaining popularity around the democratic
world, in the light of the increasing realization that wider and better
public interest would be served, if certain matters are referred to the
people for their decision, instead of (those matters) being allowed to be
tainted with group or sectional interest that most times influence
government decisions. In the view of Prof. B.O. Nwabueze, "referendum is
meant to supplement representative government and to correct some
inadequacies on the part of the representatives themselves".
The idea of "supplementing representative government" apparently
underscores the point that, given the (selfish) nature of man and the
unpredictability of his behaviour, that it would be dangerous to entrust any
representative with such absolute mandate, as to render the participation of
the citizens in the governance of their own affairs, unnecessary. It becomes
obvious, that this requirement of mass participation in a democracy, cannot
be satisfied with a mere involvement of the people, in periodic
elections. For according to Prof. C.B. Nwachukwu, "democracy, properly
understood, denotes a political system in which the eligible people in a
political society, participate actively, not only in determining the kind of
people that govern them, but also in shaping the policy out-put of the
government."
This means that elections, without more, do not furnish sufficient
evidence of popular participation of the electorate in governance, as
democracy demands. This is because, in the view of the learned
Prof. (Nwabueze) "elections are dominated by party interests and spirit, so
that the real mind of the people, does not receive as full an expression, as
might be desired." And if we take "the real mind of the people" to refer to
their wishes and aspirations (unencumbered by any partisan interest), it
follows that such wishes, can only be given full expression, if they are
sought, obtained and fed, into the policy mill of the government.
It becomes clear that what makes the difference between a civilized
democracy and the uncivilized one, is the presence or otherwise (among
others), of the institutional structure that compels the expressed views and
wishes of the people, to provide the enabling compass, in the task of
governance. But even where this institutional framework exists, but is not
developed, as to compel the obedience of the elected representatives to, and
their compliance with these expressed wises and aspirations of the people,
the result is the mutual suspicion and distrust, with which the ruled hold
the rulers. In this absence of any healthy interaction between the two
groups (rulers and the ruled) "the real mind of the people" becomes
difficult to ascertain.
In our own clime, the foregoing situation becomes compounded because,
the emergent elite that replaced the whiteman, quickly embraced the latter's
life-style, which costs, would be difficult to sustain, if what goes on in
government were to be exposed to us, in the name of popular
participation. Consequently, most governmental actions become "a deal" and
which must be protected with the instrument of Official Secrets Act or the
theory of Classified Information, both of which equally hallmarked colonial
administration.
Now, while it is elementary knowledge that sovereignty belongs to the
people, the elected representatives in this part of the world make us
understand that the mandate they have, is absolute. And as such, that it
would amount to "a usurpation of their (the leaders) function" if "we, the
people" should ever ask questions or even seek to have a say, on how we
would wish to be governed, now or in the future.
Democracy Nigeriana!
But even where we have accepted to watch, like unenthusiastic spectators
in a dull match (which many believe, this our democracy has become), the
holders of our mandate, are still suspicious of our mood. Accordingly,
tinted (car) glasses and prison-like walled and gated residential houses,
provide the 'needed' refuge, as the hunger-contorted faces of the masses,
must be avoided. Even the constituency offices (run with public funds) which
should have been serving as venues for popular consultation with the
electorate (as it obtains in civilized climes), are only 'manned' by
salaried ghost workers.
You see, if popular participation of the people, is a democratic
setting, by means of referendum, is still resorted to, in many advanced
democracies, in spite of the relatively higher moral standards of their
rulers, then its adoption in our circumstance, becomes an indispensable
necessity "to supplement" our "representative government and correct the
INADEQUACIES of the representatives themselves." We say this because, if
there is any thing (apart from the prevailing poverty and frustration of the
mass of the Nigerian people etc) that constitutes a threat to both this
nascent democracy and even the survival of the entity called Nigeria, it is
the excesses and inadequacies of those holding our mandate. This situation
is largely due to the fact that our leaders, hardly reckon with the people's
feelings in policy formulations and legislations, as their interests are
totally at variance with ours.
Think of it. How many Nigerians would vote for a reform that has left
the sixth largest producer of oil in the world without any of her refineries
working, thus making us dependent on imported fuel and subjecting the masses
to market forces that operate in the world oil market? How many of us, would
affirm (in a referendum), to have seven cups of garri at N100.00
(i.e. before the latest fuel price hike), just for us to answer a
cassava-exporting country? What do you think the result would be if "the
rest of us" are asked to choose between Option A4 (that our politicians
loathe because of its openness) and any other? Which poverty-stricken
Nigerian, would vote for the retention of executive immunity clause that has
simply provided the needed cover for the mindless looting of the public
treasury? Who would say "No" to a proposal to return the country to true
federalism (being opposed by those politicians who fear the loss of personal
advantages and positions, the prevailing structure affords them)? Who would
not want a constitution that would govern him to be sent for approval by the
people in a referendum, instead of being referred to a group of
Ghana-must-go bag huzzlers? The list is endless.
The point is that the democratic culture of popular participation by the
people should be evolved, encouraged and sustained in our political
system. The importance of this lies in its ability to engender a sense of
involvement in the people in the business of governance. But more
importantly, it would cement the relationship between the rulers and the
ruled, and reduce the suspicion, with which the latter view the activities
of the former. Also, it would render unattractive the rulers' tendency of
relying on the instruments of coercion, rather than on the support of the
people, as their source of strength and power. It is only when this
situation obtains that our leaders would hear the people's testimony that,
indeed, what we are operating presently, is a "government of the people, by
the people and for the people".

Original_Search_Title: Tsonga Project As Index of Agricultural Revolution
Headline: Nigeria: Tsonga Project As Index of Agricultural Revolution
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Recently, President Olusegun Obasanjo was at the new farming site of
the New Nigerian Farmers in Tsonga in Kwara State, where he challenged local
commercial institutions to support agriculture for food sufficiency in the
country. Tunde Sanni reports
The people of Tsonga watched as the chopper marked 5N-BDY that brought
President Olusegun Obasanjo and his host, Governor Bukola Saraki to the
community for the presidential visit to the commercial farming site touched
the ground. If it was not the first time the people would see the president,
it was obviously the first time the first citizen of the country would visit
that remote part of the country to have a glimpse of the New Nigerian
Farming site.
For Obasanjo, the visit was an extension of his passion and belief in
farming, a vocation he took to, moments after he stepped down from public
office in 1976 by establishing Obasanjo Farms Nigeria (OFN) Ltd in Otta,
Ogun State.
The disbelief of the native people stemmed out of their initial
opposition to the project and their violent reaction when the state
government decided to forge ahead with it. They were shocked that the
project was becoming a reality after all with all the trappings of a
presidential backing.
Community leader, Alhaji Alhassan Ndagba Abubakar echoed the minds of
his people when he told Obasanjo on return from the farm sites: "We didn't
know what this project would be at the beginning but today, we can see
clearly. We are grateful to God and the government that brought this project
to us." He confessed that many of them have really been taught new
techniques in modern farming to the extent that "somebody like me has a big
farm where about 300 hundred workers are presently working." He expressed
gratitude to the state governor and the traditional ruler of the area, "who
sensitised the people and convinced some of the cynics about future
prospects of the initiative, one of which, we are all witnessing today."
Another community leader, Alhaji Ndako Ndagi, gave reasons for the
initial opposition of the people to the project. He said it was borne out of
the fear of losing their land, "but we are extremely grateful to the
government now on account of numerous benefits we are getting from the
project, particularly the compensation given to all the affected local
farmers who are now applying modern techniques of farming on their new farm
lands."
Alhaji Shuaib Yamman Abdullahi, a gubernatorial aspirant, who lost the
2003 shadow election on the platform of the ruling Peoples Democratic Party
(PDP) to Saraki, also alluded to the groundswell of skeptism about the
project. "Because people thought it was going to be one of the numerous
white elephant projects of the government, not knowing that it would be a
project of development and prosperity as it is being realistically
executed," he said. He told the president that the project has transformed
the community into a laboratory from where mechanised agriculture practical
lesson could take place.
For the host governor, the invitation to the podium for his speech was
like a hen come to roost as he had had to overcome several hiccups to see
that the project lived to witness the presidential visit. In celebrating the
coming of Obasanjo to the farm site, the governor arrayed banks executives,
apparently to use the presidential might to suppress their stringent loan
conditions.
The leader of the white farmers, Mr Allan Jack shocked the governor when
he was at the site to flag off the planting season for the commercial
farming project that the banks had not been there for them as their tough
conditions had made the farmers to abandon the financial institutions and
sought refuge with the meager resources in their possession.
Saraki could not hide his excitement at the prospects of the project
against the background of the employment generation of the project and the
transfer of skills and technology to the local farmers since it flagged off
some months back. He reminded his august guest, "Mr. President would recall
that about a year ago, I led representatives of the Zimbabwean farmers to
the Aso Rock Villa, that was a time that despite the high conviction we were
willing to make, we were not sure that this project would take off." He
added: "Your Excellency declared during that visit that while Nigeria would
not like to take away from Zimbabwe what is good for Zimbabwe, we would like
to keep in Africa, what is good for Africa", asserting that the statement
bolstered his confidence in the project.
Even while the project was still in its infant stage, the governor had
started to reel out its advantages. "Even at this initial stage of the
project, we are beginning to see how good this project is for Nigeria. It is
barely over 12 months when we signed the MoU and it was less than two months
that I flagged off planting on these farms that you have seen today and we
can already have a picture of what to expect. In concrete terms, these farms
already pay a monthly total wages of N13million to local farm hands and
staff." The governor also mentioned the boost in sales of roadside
electronic shops and sellers of household items.
He highlighted a significant index of the project as its ability to
create large opportunities for learning and transfer of skills to the local
farmers. He explained, "this is why even though we have had to relocate some
of the local farms, we have ensured that these are within the precinct of
the commercial farms in order to make learning and skills transfer almost
automatic." He recalled that one of the important components of the MoU with
the expatriate farmers was to "designate a 15th farm as a training farm
where on weekly basis, our local farmers and extension staff can share
skills and knowledge," believing that the designation would improve the
practices and the productivity of the legion of local farmers.
He affirmed the challenge the project thrusted on the state, which was
the challenge of spearheading national self-sufficiency in food, and other
agricultural produce is beyond the capability of just 15 farmers,
postulating, "it is for this reason that we have also focused on the
development of local agricultural initiatives by small-scale farmers
especially on rice, cashew and cassava and established Youth Farm Centre in
Malete, the idea", which he noted, was to camp some of our willing youths,
numbering at about 100 on the farm. "We also appointed one of the Zimbabwean
farmers as a farm manager. This farm manager imparts skills on the young
men, who concentrate on their farming".
He noted that for him "the greater achievement would not be in seeing
Kwara State spear-heading agricultural development and self-reliance in food
production in Nigeria; rather, it would be in seeing many other states in
the country with potentials for agriculture replicating our modest
achievements, while we constantly seek to surpass ourselves".
Saraki recalled that when he came into office in 2003, the challenges he
met, among others, prompted him into the project. "It was clear therefore,
that if we must achieve development, whether in terms of reducing poverty,
improving human development standards or creating the right environment and
structures for investments, we must begin to break new grounds, explore new
opportunities and create innovative and dynamic strategies".
On the Duku-Lade rice irrigation scheme, Saraki called on the president
to come to his aid on the expansion of irrigation facilities so that the
state could be better positioned to achieve the presidential vision on rice
production". The governor said: "Your Excellency, as I have had the
opportunities to mention to you in the past, our irrigation scheme in
Duku-Lade was built by the government of Northern Region in 1963.This
project had, however, been abandoned since then until our government revived
it."
He added, "it was by rehabilitating the Duku-Lade irrigation scheme that
we were able to achieve the 200 hectares first dry season harvest in
May. The irrigation scheme has an installed capacity to cover 4000
hectares. This is certainly more than a single state. Especially a state
like ours could carry."
The governor recalled that following the presidential initiatives on
increasing rice production, the state early this year was able to
revolutionalise rice production in the state by ensuring "the first ever dry
season rice harvest in the Duku-Lade, area of the state in collaboration
with the Vee-Tee Rice of India. He stressed that the objective was to make
the local rice growers take advantage of the Federal policy on
self-sufficiency in rice production by 2006 and solicited for federal
government assistance in his bid to raise N1 billion to cultivate addition
800 hectares for cassava while 250 hectares is already under cultivation for
rice.
He, however, lamented that "the State's Agriculture Development Project
(ADP) is facing tough challenges in dealing with the avalanche of requests
by enthusiastic farmer groups. It should be noted that rather than give out
cash to farmers, ADP only hands out inputs and services to farmers".
He told the gathering that though the Federal Government is crucial for
most of the things the state planned to achieve "we have also leveraged
funding schemes and opportunities provided by the banks, especially the CBN
which through its Trust Fund Model has made it possible to mobilise funds
and resources for some of our initiatives on rice, cassava and cashew." He
said a through this fund model the state government has sourced N210m for
this planting season through the assistance of the CBN.
"By next year" he said, "we intend to raise N2billion and we already
have N422m out of the N1b we have to contribute to this sum."
Obasanjo, in his response, sympathised with the farmers on the stress
they were going through in getting loans from commercial institutions and
expressed the determination of his government to come to their aid.
Obasanjo, who led a handful of serving and former ministers and members
of the National Assembly to the community, regretted that Nigerian banks
were not positioned to appreciate the financial enormity of large-scale
farming. He said he had mobilised the Nigeria banking sector to the site,
including the CBN Governor, so as to be encouraged into partnering with
better lending rate. The president urged Nigerians to go into real farming,
saying that the era of psychedelic farming was over.
Ministers on the entourage of the President for the one-day visit
included Alhaji Adamu Bello, Agriculture; Mr Fidelis Tapgun, Industries;
Babalola Borishade, Aviation; Bashir Awotorebo, State for Water; Tayo Alao,
State for Education and former Minister of State for Health, Hajia Aminat
Ndalolo.
Obasanjo, while inspecting the large expanse of farm sites, condemned
the current interest rates. He said there was no way any one involved in
such a large scale project could access funds at about 20 per cent being
currently charged by the banks and called for a review of interest rates
being charged by banks on commercial agriculture projects in the
country. The president said that a financial backed large-scale farming was
one of the means to bail the nation out of underdevelopment and hunger.
His words: "I gave the governor every support for this venture but you
have to exercise patience because there is still something in Nigeria that
all of us have not learnt how to do sufficiently and that is how to provide
adequate financial backing by the banks for agriculture.
"It is important you exercise patience and talk with the banks because
there is no way we can develop agriculture without money and there is no way
we can develop it at the interest rate of 20 percent. It is ridiculous. If
it is above single digit the farmer will have to think again.
"I am appealing to bankers to appreciate how a rural area can be
activated; can be made to develop through agriculture; in essence, we will
have to work out, with the support of the Central Bank of Nigeria, how we
will make sure that various farmers are given loans at the rate of interests
they can afford and at the rate of interest we know it will make it
worthwhile for them to satisfy their commitment".
He, however, assured the agitated white farmers, who set the tone for a
presidential soft loan for farmers that the Federal Government and the
Central Bank would meet with the country's commercial banks to educate them
on the potentials and profitability in commercial farming, using the
successful Tsonga projects as a good case.
One other area the meeting would also look into, according to the
President, is the need to increase the level of funding available for such
projects under the Agricultural Guarantee Scheme, which the Managing
Director of First Bank Plc. Mr. Jacob Ajekigbe told him, was not capable of
supporting projects like the ones in Kwara.
Ajekigbe was among top bankers invited to tour the vast farmland with
the President as part of efforts by the state government to convince them of
the need for support. Currently, some of the projects are being suported by
Intercontinental Bank Plc. Other bank executives in attendance included the
Managing Director of Trade Bank, Prince Tunji Adeniyi and Mr. Tony Elumelu
of UBA Plc.
Obasanjo, particularly, commended the white farmers for their absolute
dedication and commitment to the project, saying that "for you people to
have left your country and live alone in this bush is more than a enough
demonstration of commitment and seriousness which farming requires." He
pleaded with them to bear with the peculiarity of Nigerian banks by their
initial unwillingness to invest in farming, and assured that very soon the
situation would change.
Obasanjo observed that most of the farmers in the villages were now too
old as any farmer who is above 65 years old could no longer properly bend
his waist. "So we have to create successor farmers and inculcate modern ways
of doing things, these new farmers should take over and do it the way new
generation farmers are doing it and ensure that it is sustained so that
there can be corresponding result," he said.
While speaking on the need for the commercial farmers to start thinking
of storage and processing facilities for their huge farm produce, Obasanjo
disclosed that the Federal Government would in the next one or two months
make grain silos with a total storage capacity for 25,000 tonnes of grains
available all over the country.
He challenged the Zimbabwean farmers to also look into the areas of
massive exportation of their products and not concentrate on the consumption
in Nigeria alone. The President also told them that the concept of the
project is Nigeria-based and urged them not to deviate from it in the
interest of Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Bukedea Women Vow On Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Bukedea Women Vow On Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE women of Bukedea have vowed to convince their husbands to vote for President Yoweri Museveni in 2006.
The chairperson of Bukedea Women Strugglers Association (BUWOSA), Margaret Ilaborot, said this during the recent distribution of 500 sacks of cassava stems donated to them by President Yoweri Museveni.
About 990 women in the 30 parishes of Bukedea county in Kumi, subscribe to the association. Each parish got 15 bags of stems.
Area MP Peter Esele said the donation was realised after he appealed to the president to rescue the people of Bukedea from poverty. He said women were in the forefront in the fight against poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Participate in Cassava Projects, Women Told
Headline: Zimbabwe: Participate in Cassava Projects, Women Told
Body_Text:
Harare — GOVERNMENT has urged women to actively participate in cassava production
projects as a means to economically empower themselves.
The Minister of Women Affairs, Gender and Community Development, Cde
Oppah Muchinguri, said as part of Government's commitment to women
emancipation, the ministry would "resuscitate defunct agro-projects" that
women had embarked on countrywide as a way of increasing self-sustenance of
the family structure.
In a speech read on her behalf by Deputy Minister Cde Abigail Damasane
at the Jekesa Pfungwa/Vulingqondo (JPV) 5th biennial conference in Harare
last weekend, Cde Muchinguri called on women to apply for Reserve Bank of
Zimbabwe facility funds to resuscitate defunct projects in their areas.
"We are starting from the ward level. Go and approach a gender officer
at the DA's (District Administrator) offices in your area for the cassava
seeds and try it.
"Most of the needy community members comprise women and youths, most of
whom still do not have access to resources like finance and land while
attitudes still prevail to the extent that the disadvantaged community
members are not actively involved in the management of resources in these
communities," she said.
The minister also urged women to support strategies aimed at raising
standards of living at family level. She applauded the JPV group for coming
up with training projects benefiting Zimbabwean women.
These include training in business management and team building,
sustainable agriculture, food and nutrition.
"I am pleased that you are doing awareness campaigns through gender
workshops, HIV and Aids discussions, wills and inheritance laws workshops
and home-based care programmes which have benefit the community," the
minister added.
"I note with pleasure that JPV programmes are community-driven, planned,
implemented and monitored by both men and women to ensure long-term
improvement of food security and sustainability."
The conference theme: "Combating HIV and Aids and Gender Imbalances
Through Economic Empowerment" suited the ministry's poverty alleviation
strategies.
These strategies include dairy and piggery projects, adult literacy,
village banks and women's clubs.
"Let us not be intimidated by the economic recession that we are going
through, we need to rise up to the challenge that is before us," said Cde
Muchinguri.
JPV chairperson Mrs Sibusisiwe Chitekuteku said her organisation was
aimed at empowering women through training extension and awareness on
various issues. It boasts of 10 000 members in 36 districts. Zimbabwe has 76
districts.
"Our programmes are aimed at completely changing the whole woman for the
country's benefit," said Mrs Chitekuteku.
Some of their programmes include sustainable agriculture and food
security training, gender and development and small-scale enterprise
development among women.
"We are involved in the soap and bread making projects around the
country," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Bank Consoloidation Will Strengthen Economy - Minister
Headline: Nigeria: Bank Consoloidation Will Strengthen Economy - Minister
Body_Text:
The Minister of State for Finance, Mrs. Nenadi Usman, has said that the on-going consolidation programme in the banking industry will not only strengthen the capital base of the banks but also strengthen the financial base of the entire nation's economy.
The minister spoke at the opening ceremony of the 7th National Conference on Finance and Economic Development in Kano, where she said the consolidation programme will not only strengthen the banking industry but also have a multiplier effect on the nation's economy at large.
Also, she said the on-going privatization programme will create more jobs in the country and further boost economic activities.
According to the text of her speech, which was made available to Business Trust, she explained that other government policies like the cassava initiative are aimed at boosting the country's exports, pointing out that Nigeria is currently the highest exporter of cassava in the world.
She noted that with the debt relief granted the nation by Paris Club and the economic reform programme, the entire nation will soon be transformed and the country will be in a position to face global economic challenges.

Original_Search_Title: Umuahia South Awards N8m Road Contract
Headline: Nigeria: Umuahia South Awards N8m Road Contract
Body_Text:
Abia — Umuahia South local government area of Abia has awarded N8 million contract for the tarring of Ogbodi-Ukwu 1.3 km road, the council chairman, Chief Ndukwe Adindu, has said. Adindu told reporters at Ubakala that he planned to tar three roads before leaving office.
Other roads proposed for tarring include Nsukwe road in Ubakala and Amakama/Umuajata Olokoro road, both World Bank-assisted.
Reviewing his achievement in the last 14 months, he said the council was constructing an ultra-modern legislative chamber and offices, valued at N24 million. "This will be completed before September 10, this year," he said.
Adindu said his administration's blue-print to the council was the provision of bore holes and electricity to each of the 13 political wards.
He said most of the water projects were powered by public power supply because the projects were participatory in nature, with each community expected to procure a 2.5 KV generator to power its borehole.
The chairman said that during the period under review, he set up a demonstration farm in the council with 20,000 pineapple suckers, 40,000 yam seedlings and 2.5 million cassava stems. Adindu said the administration had already completed a poultry building.
To boost food production, fertilisers were procured at N1,800 and sold to farmers at a subsidised rate of N1,500, he said.
On health, he said that five primary health centres were built, while old ones were rehabilitated, adding the council was expecting a container load of health equipment, donated to it by an NGO in Canada.
He said that council recruited 150 persons, in addition to 70 teachers to reduce unemployment in the area and that some of those recruited were deployed to Works unit to boost the administration's direct labour programme.
Other programmes, aimed at alleviating poverty in the area, include distribution of 100 motorcycles, 100 wheel-barrows and 250 wrappers to indigent persons and widows.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Plans N3bn Cassava Processing Plant
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Plans N3bn Cassava Processing Plant
Body_Text:
Lokoja — Governor Ibrahim Idris of Kogi State is to establish a multi-purpose cassava processing plant at the cost of N3 billion.
The governor, who disclosed this to newsmen in Lokoja, said he's determined to sustain the capacity of the state to be the second largest producer of cassava in the country.
He pointed out that his administration has also placed order for the procurement of eight varieties of cassava species suitable for the production of ethanol, snow with flour, chips pellets and starch.
The governor said his administration has so far released a total of N26 million to boost cassava production in the state.
He added that his government has put in place formidable strategies to address the problems hanging the development of agriculture in the state.
The governor added that Kogi State Agricultural Trust Fund was been designed to eradicate poverty among farmers by encouraging them to grow exportable agricultural produce and bring derivative fund to the state.
Idris noted that the state has established three rice processing centres in the state in view of the federal plan to ban the importation of rice as from January next year.
He added that efforts would be intensified to boost the production of rice in the state.
On the distribution of tractors, the governor said 227 tractors with complete accessories and 1,250 tricycles have been distributed to farmers.
He, however, commended the efforts of the state Agricultural Trust Fund for making the state a model in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Brazil Honours President Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Brazil Honours President Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Abuja — Brazil rolled out the red carpet and threw in a colourful military
parade, complete with 21-gun salute, to usher in President Olusegun Obasanjo
who began a three-day reciprocal state visit there on 7 August.
Host President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, who paid a two-day state visit
to Nigeria from 11 to 12 April, was at hand to welcome President
Obasanjo.
Both leaders signed a series of bilateral agreements, which according to
the Senior Special Adviser on Media Affairs, Mrs Remi Oyo bordered on trade
and investments, technical cooperation, cultural revival and regular
political consultations.
The Presidential Spokeswoman said Brazil has signified interest in
solidifying the cultural ties between it and Nigeria in the exchange of
teachers to teach African history, cassava and tropical fruits.
At a reception dinner later, President Obasanjo thanked his host for the
reception and expressed optimism that the visit would widen Nigeria-Brazil
relations.
"We are happy to note that we are on the march towards re-igniting the
golden years of Nigeria-Brazil relations of the 1980s. The value of our
bilateral trade then peaked at over $2 billion and our joint cooperation
profile covered virtually every facets of human activity.
"The successes achieved so far especially in our collaboration on the
production of anti-retroviral drugs in Nigeria and the joint agricultural
research projects in cassava and tropical fruits production are reassuring
and sure to expand the frontiers of our cooperation.
According to President Obasanjo "Brazil under President Lula has been a
true friend of Africa and a development partner. Let me therefore with all
sense of candour salute the life-long personal commitment of President Lula
to fighting the course of people weighed down by poverty, hunger, disease,
racial discrimination, injustice and other forms of social and political
inequities and inequalities.
"In the same vein our renewed diplomatic efforts to revive the south
Atlantic Zone of peace and cooperation and our commitment to convene the
inaugural summit of leaders of Africa, the Caribbean and South America must
not wane.

Original_Search_Title: Katakwi Needs 1,700 Litres of Pesticide to Kill Grasshoppers
Headline: Uganda: Katakwi Needs 1,700 Litres of Pesticide to Kill Grasshoppers
Body_Text:
Kampala — A TOTAL of 1,700 litres of pesticide (cypermethrin chemical) worth sh17m
is needed to control the strange grasshoppers in Katakwi district. The
insects invaded the district in June last year. The grasshoppers identified
as sahelian grasshoppers (haeriglyphus tataricas) have destroyed about 1,000
acres of sorghum, maize and millet.
"In May 2005, 1,100 litres of pesticide were dispatched to the district
and good results were registered, but there is a shortfall of 1,700 litres
