Tooro, the late Sir George Rukiidi offered land to host the fleeing Rwandese
in his kingdom, Rwakaikara was the chief of the then Rwamwanja
sub-county. He helped not only in identifying land but also in getting the
Rwandese to settle.
Giving an account now, Rwakaikara insists the land has never been an
official settlement but was instead "lent" by the King of Tooro at the
request of his counterpart King Kigenyi of Rwanda as a temporary settlement
for members of the royal family who by tradition could not mix in the same
camps with commoners.
The understanding then was that after some time, the Ugandan government
would find alternative accommodation for the refugees in Kyaka or elsewhere
to settle more permanently. The permanent camps were created in Kyaka,
forming the present day Kyaka I and Kyaka II.
But due to the large numbers of refugees, those who settled in Rwamwanja
never moved until the Tutsi-led Rwanda Patriotic Front (RPF) war of
1990-1994 that saw many return home, freeing the land.
Who is at fault?
Mr Augustine Kayonga, the chairman of Kabarole district, which formerly
also included present day Kamwenge and Kyenjojo districts is angry that the
government is taking advantage of the King's hospitality to grab the
land.
Kayonga is one of four people who have engaged the government in a court
battle on behalf of the 1,000 peasant families faced with eviction.
"For us, the important thing to bring out is the plight of the people,
the government has no legal claim on this land. The land was lent to the
Rwandese princes by the King of Tooro. When they left, the Kingdom
encouraged its people to re-occupy the land they had previously occupied,"
Kayonga told the Daily Monitor in an interview in Fort Portal on July
19.
Other interested parties the Daily Monitor spoke to, claim that the
government is taking advantage of the long period the refugees were hosted
on this land to lay claim on it.
There are rumours that "big people" are involved in the wrangle, playing
a "behind the scenes role" to steal the land. "Otherwise how does one
explain the use of armed government soldiers to survey the land? If the
government was genuine in its actions, why don't they even have an LC
official to guide them on where the settlement land is, why can't they use
local people as porters to cut the boundaries? This means there is something
sinister," said a woman whose maize garden was cut through by the
surveyors.
Mzee Rwakaikara echoes the same words. "We are angry that the government
has betrayed us by sending the army to grab our land. The army is supposed
to protect Ugandans and their property [but] where have these soldiers come
from?"
A visit to the area confirmed the army's involvement.
UPDF soldiers clad in camouflage combat fatigues with AK 47 assault
riffles slung on one shoulder and machetes or axes in the other hand were
working around at the bushes and crops to clear the boundary.
"We think there are interests more than refugees," says Kayonga, adding
that, "the claim that the government might be seeking to free the land for
investors does not hold because the current occupants are also engaged in
serious economic activity."
Some settlers have large ranches on the land while others have
commercial-size maize and cassava gardens.
Mr Ivan Kunihira, is the LC I chairman for Nkoma cell. Like his people,
he is also faced with eviction from land his ancestors have occupied for
many generations.
"My father's grave is here, his father's grave is there next to him and
his grandfather lies next so I don't understand how the land of my
forefathers can now become government land," Kunihira said.
Land belonging to the churches and other public institutions like
schools is also not being spared.
Facing eviction is Rwamwanja Church of Uganda parish, which occupies a
30-acre piece of land. It was built in 1916 and was given a land title in
1930. According to the parish priest, Rev. Yehu Kabyakyaka, the matter of
the church land is being forwarded to the Bishop of Rwenzori diocese to
pursue in court. In the same category is another church on a 20-acre plot at
Mutukura, a health centre at Nkoma and Rwamwanja Senior Secondary
School.
Intervention
Tooro Prime Minister, Mr Stephen Rwakijuma Nyabongo in separate letters
to the Prime Minister, Prof Apollo Nsibambi and the Commissioner for Lands,
says the kingdom is shocked at the government's move to evict people from
the land it does not own.
In one of the letters dated January 7, 2005, to Prof Apollo Nsibambi,
Nyabongo wrote, "Tooro Kingdom administration was recently shocked to learn
that the Minister of Disaster Preparedness and Refugees had issued out an
operational order to evict and survey the [Rwamwanja area].
"In the history of the Kingdom and records, it is not known that
Rwamwanja was ever gazetted as a refugee settlement."
Nyabongo challenged the government to clarify "as to when this land of
Rwamwanja became government land." He also petitioned President Museveni
over the matter saying it had political, economic and cultural dimensions
that required attention.
Earlier, on January 5, the Vice President, Prof. Gilbert Bukenya wrote
to the Prime Minister raising complaints made to him by the Rwamwanja
residents and Tooro kingdom officials over the land survey and Minister
Ali's operational order.Bukenya ordered an immediate halt of the surveying,
which had created a lot of tension among the many families and asked the
Prime Minister to convene an urgent stakeholders' meeting.
Nsibambi, wrote to the various stakeholders on January 13, calling a
meeting on January 20. It was attended by among others, the National
Political Commissar, Dr. Crispus Kiyonga, Minister Adolf Mwesige (then in
the office of the Vice President), the Minister of State for Lands, Mr
Baguma Isoke and the Minister of State for disaster Preparedness Ms
Christine Amongin Aporu.
After the meeting, Nsibambi constituted an inter-ministerial committee
to investigate the matter, but before the committee could report, a new team
of surveyors with UPDF protection returned to Rwamwanja to continue
work. Nsibambi also tasked Mwesige to give a legal opinion on the survey and
threat of eviction.
Mwesige wrote his opinion saying the government has no claim on the
Rwamwanja land. "The only gazetted refugee settlements in Tooro kingdom are
Kyaka 1 and Kyaka II (Kyenjojo district) and Kahunge (Kamwenge
district). The Rwamwanja area is not a refugee settlement," Mwesige wrote.He
argued that it was "not conceivable" for the people of Tooro to cede the
Rwamwanja area because of the many important cultural and historical sites
there in.
The surveying officials - the chief surveyor, who the Daily Monitor
learnt is called Charles Bafaki and his colleague only identified as Musoke
declined to make any comments. The UPDF officials led by commandant Alphonse
Nteziyali and Lt. Adonia Kamukama did not comment.
But the Principal staff surveyor Fort Portal, Mr A.O. Itorot in a letter
dated January11 says the government does not own the Rwamwanja land, and
reported that there had been a previous attempt by the Ministry of Local
government to gazette the land in 1989 but the scheme had been abandoned mid
way.

Original_Search_Title: The Lost Boys of Sudan
Headline: Sudan: The Lost Boys of Sudan
Body_Text:
The Lost Boys of Sudan An American Story of the Refugee Experience
By Mark Bixler
From genocide in Africa to a second chance in America
In 2000 the United States began accepting 3,800 refugees from one of
Africa's longest civil wars. They were just some of the thousands of young
men, known as "Lost Boys," who had been orphaned or otherwise separated from
their families in the chaos of a brutal conflict that has ravaged Sudan
since 1983. The Lost Boys of Sudan focuses on four of these
refugees. Theirs, however, is a typical story, one that repeated itself
wherever the Lost Boys could be found across America. It is a story of the
countless challenges of "making it" in a strange new place after years on
the run in Sudan or in refugee camps in Kenya and Ethiopia.
Jacob Magot, Peter Anyang, Daniel Khoch, and Marko Ayii were among 150
or so Lost Boys who were resettled in Atlanta. Like most of their fellow
refugees, they had never before turned on a light switch, used a kitchen
appliance, or ridden in a car or subway train - much less held a job or
balanced a checkbook. We relive their early excitement and disorientation,
their growing despondency over fruitless job searches, adjustments they
faced upon finally entering the workforce, their experiences of post-9/11
xenophobia, and their undying dreams of acquiring an education.
As we immerse ourselves in the Lost Boys' daily lives, we also get to
know the social services professionals and volunteers, celebrities,
community leaders, and others who guided them - with occasional detours -
toward self-sufficiency. Along the way author Mark Bixler looks closely at
the ins and outs of U.S. refugee policy, the politics of international aid,
the history of Sudan, and the radical Islamist underpinnings of its
government. America is home to more foreign-born residents than ever before;
the Lost Boys have repaid that gift in full through their example of
unflagging resolve, hope, and faith.
Mark Bixler, a staff writer at the Atlanta Journal-Constitution, has
also been a reporter for the Winston-Salem Journal.
Learn more about the author at www.lostboysbook.com.
March 2005 288 pp.  6 x 9 in.  14 photos 1 map
ISBN 0-8203-2499-X cloth $24.95
"Mark Bixler shows what the refugee experience is like for tribal,
traditional, and traumatized people as they crash into modern America. While
there are quite a few books on the Sudanese in America, this is the one that
connects personal stories to history, foreign policy, and public
policy. It's erudite and readable, a rare combination."  - Mary Pipher,
author of The Middle of Everywhere: Helping Refugees Enter the American
Community
"The journey of the 'Lost Boys of Sudan' is both heartbreaking and
inspiring. It speaks to the strength of the human spirit to survive and grow
under even the most abject circumstances. Their plight eloquently shows us
the terrible consequences for children of war, and their personal triumphs
over adversity symbolize a great hope for Africa and the global community."
- President Jimmy Carter
"Mark Bixler's fascinating narrative follows four young men coming of
age as they navigate from a past that saw the slaughter of their families,
the destruction of their communities, their flight to years of temporary
asylum, their childhood denuded of adult assistance and supervision, in at
best a fourth-world environment, to, suddenly, the most complex and
competitive society on earth. Bixler also plumbs the strategic limits of
American society; the rescue and resettlement of individual refugees such as
these is tied to the principled oversoul of America. These young men will
succeed here; as they do, we succeed too."  - Roger Winter, former director
of the Office of Refugee Resettlement and executive director of the
U.S. Committee for Refugees, 1981-2001
Table of Contents
Preface Chapter One. Landing
Chapter Two. Bread in the Dishwasher
Chapter Three. The Spoiling of the World
Chapter Four. A Bitter Wind
Chapter Five. Selective Compassion
Chapter Six. The Level of Responsible People
Chapter Seven. Are Y'all Resettling Any of These Guys?
Chapter Eight. Body Language in the Workplace
Chapter Nine. September 11, 2001
Chapter Ten. Chasing the Wind
Chapter Eleven. Can You Name Your Sisters?
Chapter Twelve. This Is Your Future
Chapter Thirteen. Driving
Chapter Fourteen. Don't Get Obsessed
Chapter Fifteen. Peace?
Chapter Sixteen. Gentlemen of the Future
Epilogue. November 2004
Notes
Bibliography
Acknowledgments
Index
An excerpt
Chapter One: Landing
The city of Atlanta appeared through the airplane window like a distant
island in a sea of green, a cluster of buildings surrounded by trees. A road
unfurled like a whip in the world below, teeming with squat moving specks of
indeterminate color, sunlight gleaming off metal, cars and trucks and vans
going who knew where in the middle of the day. In the center of the city
rose a stand of office towers that seemed to climb higher as United Airlines
flight 1905 descended, its landing gear down, seats returned to upright
positions. Passengers peering through thick, rounded glass windows could
distinguish parking lots and green interstate signs on a brilliant summer
afternoon. Among those looking out the windows in coach were four young men
seated in a row near the wing. They were tall and slender and dark as
night. Each wore white canvas tennis shoes with thin red stripes and an
oversized gray sweatshirt that said "USRP" across the chest, gifts from
people in Nairobi hired by the United States Refugee Program to outfit the
young men for a trip into modern America. They sat still in their seats at
the end of a three-day journey, on the brink of a new life somewhere in the
landscape unfolding before them. They took turns leaning forward or sitting
back so that one or another could try to divine something of the new world
rushing up toward them, their eyes darting right and then left, intent,
focused on a landscape that yielded few secrets from the air.
Those four young men called each other "colleague" or "brother" but were
not colleagues or brothers in the strictest sense of the words. They had
been separated from parents in the swirling chaos of civil war in Sudan
nearly fourteen years earlier and had marched through the violent desolation
of East Africa with thousands of boys in similar straits. They had come of
age in the wilderness and in refugee camps and had grown accustomed to
eating one meal a day of corn or sorghum or some other donated grain. They
would tell you about month-long spans on a diet of unripe mangoes or
cassavas and whatever water might have collected in a hollow on the
ground. At times they had eaten soft mud and drunk their own urine to
survive, but others had succumbed to hunger. The men flying into the United
States on that brilliant afternoon in July, a day so full of possibilities,
could not banish the memory of friends who had starved to death, the image
of skin pulling taut over ribs, the sound of rattling wisps of breath. And
so they had not known quite how to react in the last three days when smiling
European and American men and women in tidy slacks and skirts strolled down
airplane aisles wheeling stainless-steel carts to deliver more food than any
of them could fairly have expected to see in months not so long ago. Trays
arrived bearing exotic green leaves and unfamiliar meat. There was bread,
which they recognized, beside small foil-wrapped squares of a cold, hard,
yellow substance, which they did not recognize. And there were three-pronged
plastic instruments everyone else used to eat. So much was strange to
them. Even the lemon juice, they would say. The lemon juice tasted different
than the lemon juice they'd been given in the refugee camp in Kenya, their
home for the last nine years.
As a voice crackled throughout the airplane to announce a final approach
to Atlanta, one of the four, Jacob Ngor Magot, clutched a white plastic bag
and a small black knapsack that held all that he owned - immigration
documents, a book outlining what refugees could expect in the United States,
and a change of clothes. He and the three men sitting nearby would live
together in Atlanta, to be joined in a few weeks by two others from the
refugee camp. Nearest to Jacob sat Peter Ayuen Anyang, the oldest and
darkest and tallest of the four, a man in his early twenties with a high,
sloping forehead and an overbite that defined his grin. He had planned to
bring a book of Christian songs in his native Dinka language, but someone
told him that U.S. authorities did not allow new arrivals to bring books, so
he left the hymnal and arrived instead with letters from others in the
refugee camp to friends who were sprinkled around the United States, in
Boston, Dallas, San Diego, Seattle, and Sioux Falls. In the refugee camp,
Peter had lived near Daniel Khon Khoch and Marko Aguer Ayii, two other young
Sudanese men who were on flight 1905 that day and who also would be his
roommates in the United States. Daniel and Marko had been together most days
for the last fourteen years. They had eaten together, played soccer and gone
to church together, watched boys succumb to dehydration and disease and
attacks from lions and hyenas. Daniel was like the protective older brother
to his shy younger friend. He wore a purple plastic rosary around his neck
as he had every day for the last nine years, since an Italian priest named
Father Joseph had given it to him in 1994. Daniel had sung in church choirs
in the refugee camp and dreamed of becoming a priest. Marko, who could not
speak English as well, talked about maybe becoming a pilot or soldier or
doctor.

Original_Search_Title: Presidential Monitoring Team Okays NDDC Projects
Headline: Nigeria: Presidential Monitoring Team Okays NDDC Projects
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — Presidential Monitoring Committee set up to keep an eye on Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) yesterday said the interventionist agency has no phantom projects.
Chairman of the Presidential Monitoring Committee, Ambassador Lawrence Ekpebu, made the assertion in Port Harcourt while addressing members of NDDC Board and top management during a visit to the commission's corporate headquarters, at the end of their monitoring exercise.
Ekpebu, who was accompanied by the nine other members of the Committee, said "we have not found any ghost projects. We have found all the projects reflected in the NDDC records."
Established under NDDC Act, the committee is authorised to monitor the management of the commission's funds and the implementation of its projects.
Describing NDDC as a crucial instrument for peace and development in the Niger Delta, the committee commended the commission for its efficient use of the limited resources available to it.
"With limited resources NDDC is engaged in virtually every aspect of development in the Niger Delta," he noted.
However, the committee has decried NDDC's involvement in projects that ought to be executed by state and local governments. "We feel this is not correct. We feel NDDC should wind up what it is doing in some areas and let the state and local governments handle them," he said.
The committee suggested that NDDC should limit itself to the construction of major roads and bridges, dredging of waterways, reclamation of flood-free land for agriculture, industries and habitation.
According to him, it is the responsibility of the Federal Government to work hand-in-hand with NDDC in the execution of these kinds of major projects.
He said once this kind of demarcation is made, NDDC would be more effective. The committee urged NDDC to get more involved in the development of high-level manpower especially for the oil and gas industry through the award of "automatic scholarships to Niger Delta indigenes so as to give them higher levels of competence to enable them compete for higher level jobs in the petroleum industry."
The committee pledged that in carrying out its watchdog function, it will not witch-hunt anyone. "We are products of NDDC Act and we intend to work for peace and development in the Niger Delta. Through our collaboration the work of the NDDC will be enhanced."
Earlier, NDDC Chairman, Ambassador Sam Edem, explained the commission's new focus is youth development and empowerment. He said NDDC plans to generate at least 200,000 jobs for youths across the Niger Delta region through various programmes, such as massive cultivation of cassava.
Edem urged the committee to make a case for increased funding of NDDC and the utilisation by NDDC member states of their 13 per cent derivation funds in oil producing areas.
Noting that NDDC has become so omnibus that every problem in its member states is referred to it, Edem said the commission is willing to develop the Niger Delta, but the resources are not sufficient. "There is need for the state governments to commit 13 per cent of the 13 per cent derivation funds to the affected areas in the states," he said.
NDDC Chairman promised that the commission would partner with the committee to ensure that "the goals of the Niger Delta Regional Development Master Plan are attained."

Original_Search_Title: The Debt Talk: Debt Relief As Tragedy
Headline: Nigeria: The Debt Talk: Debt Relief As Tragedy
Body_Text:
Ofeimun, yesterday, alleged that Nigeria's funds siphoned abroad are bigger than the the debt owed. Today, he says Obasanjo's pursuit of the Abacha loot assumed the colour of mere vendetta
Mike Obadan, Director General of the National Centre for Economic Management and Administration in Ibadan who provides a neat itemization of the many debt-management strategies that could be adopted -- including debt cancellation, sound macro-economic and sound external debt management policy, authentication of external debt position, tying new loans to viable projects, making regular debt portfolio review and avoiding traditional re-scheduling agreements in view of their inadequacies, privatization and moratorium on external borrowing, debt conversion and debt buy-back.
These are stock agreements across the many contributions all of which appear however to be ritually stuck on the pros and cons of debt-relief as debt-forgiveness. Except for Jeffery Sachs of Havard University who does special pleading for debt cancellation, in my view not very convincingly, almost all the contributions are taken by the necessity to reach for debt forgiveness.
On their own, Mathew Martin, Director of Debt Relief International and Ann Pettifor Coordinator of Jubilee 2000 Plus, concentrate on arguing a case for debt relief within a human rights oriented regime to enable Nigeria salvage her citizens while meeting international debt obligations. A harder position is taken by Callisto Madavo, Vice President of the Africa Region of the World Bank who makes it quite clear, as the others do in different ways, except for Jeffry Sachs, that given Nigeria's resources the debt burden is not too heavy, hence full debt cancellation is not on for Nigeria as it would cripple or even close the multilateral financial system. Nigeria's debt pack, being too much owed (83.16%) to bilateral Paris Club is presented as capable of disruptive possibilities if it is allowed to become a model beyond the poorest of the poor.
An intriguing part of this near-consensual position is that there is also a proper acknowledgment by all the contributors that so much of Nigeria's loans made a return journey back to Western banking institutions as loot and sheer capital flight and are resting secure in private accounts offshore. Beginning with Charles Soludo who argues that the return of 25% of the 100 billion dollars private Nigerian assets abroad will obviate the need to seek further external funding, the case is well touted across the board. It is quite well made in the painstaking analysis done by Professor S. Ibi Ajayi of the University of Ibadan on External Debt, Capital Flight and Growth in Nigeria.
From his analysis, some hope can be garnered for making a useable distinction between mere capital flight and looted funds. Such that: it ought to be worth the trying for any government genuinely interested in crafting a durable debt management strategy, to demand the funds. Ibi Ajayi, using a schema and model that he has developed from the best traditions in the field, applies it to information provided by international monetary institutions like the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund. His tabulations show that the greatest volume of capital flight took place in 1980-82, 1987, 1988, 1990, 1992, 1995, and 1996. The years of the least flight are 1984 and 1993-1994.
In spite of this evident possibility of tracing what happened in the flight of capital in terms of determining which was loot or normal income stashed abroad and kept from the harm's way of a crashing economy, the surprise is that so little is made by all the contributors to the need for returning the loot as a pre-condition for a proper Nigerian response to the creditors. So much ink is wasted on the question of debt-forgiveness and to a lesser extent debt cancellation in an ad nauseum progression. Not that they do not all make the right noises about the bloated private accounts abroad that have exacerbated (and somehow can explain?) Nigeria's debt crisis. None pays much mind to it as a substantive strategy on its own terms in spite of the facts available.
The seemingly more categoric position on returning looted funds is taken by Paul Collier Director of the World Bank, who worries that Nigeria has little to show for the 280 billion dollars earned as income over the same period of the critical borrowing. However, he considers that much more important for Nigeria is to worry about how to keep the Nigerian currency devalued to size, to avoid capital flight which he believes is the bane of over-valued currencies. He asserts that "Over the past 30 years an overvalued exchange rate has been the most consistent disaster in Nigerian economic policy. It has destroyed export industries and seduced wealth abroad". Of course, it is not easy for anyone who knows the specificities of the Nigerian economy to agree that this is really the problem. But the themeqis not peculiar to Paul Collier.
It is actually the hobbyhorse of all the contributions which tend to proffer solutions that in the end project the usual IMF and World Bank conditionalities as the cure for the debt overhang. In line with it, Collier makes quite a meal of the case of Uganda which, presumably by swallowing the IMF medicine made a leap in growth that Nigeria could emulate. Uganda, on whose performance Soludo offers cautionary notes, is of course, being presented in the same manner that Ivory Coast and Kenya were once positioned as star successes in capitalist economic development until they proved to be in the same trap as poor socialist Tanzania and Mozambique. It was all part of the politics of World Bank theory-making and freefor-the-poor-medicine which has mined many third world economies and which in the case of Uganda had to be disobeyed by Museveni to get any enduring results.
As against the World Bankers, common sense suggests that anyone genuinely interested in dealing with the debt trap ought not to see Nigeria in the context of other countries without taking cognizance of specificities that make all the difference. Given the rumours and the proof already provided that much of the monies had found their way back to the West, a debt management strategy would be expected to take off by, first of all, pursuing what can still be salvaged from the loot.
When Obasanjo came to power in 1999, he appeared to be interested in doing this. The pursuit of the Abacha loot to European finance houses looked like such a strategy. More and more by the day, it has come to assume the colour of mere vendetta. Until Western creditors began to threaten to discountenance Nigeria's bid for debt relief unless there was proof of real anti-corruption campaign, President Obasanjo did not appear to wake up to the need to confront the issue of the debts in private accounts abroad.
Nigeria's problems squarely. It is not just in what he and his team say the begging bowl strategy has achieved but what it has destroyed and continues to threaten. The truth is that Nigeria has been presented to the rest of the world as a very unsenous country, one that will carry an elephant on her head and still go scratching for rodents. To escape this image and to wipe it out from the memory of international players, will take a doing. But not even the so called friends of Nigeria are willing to tell this to President Obasanjo.
Because Nigeria, under Obasanjo, has preferred the will o'the wisp of debt forgiveness, the smart alecs of the international market-place (amongst whom many Nigerians are to be counted) have been lining up to collect their fees for one jousting for scheduling, rescheduling, defaults, and buy backs and what have you. Whatever Mrs Ngozi Okonjo Iweala may be saying about this, she cannot annul the reality that fees are paid and added to the account for every deal that Nigeria makes or only half makes on the trail of debt forgiveness and debt management. Thanks to Okonjo-Iweala's and Mansure Muhtar's proficiency in institution- building, Nigeria may now be able to keep a proper account, and our negotiators may be more transparent in their pursuits. But this merely spells a greater proficiency in knowing how bad or well things are without offering an exit from the mayhem.
What is beguiling about the debt forgiveness binge is that in order to have it, Nigeria's debt managers had to unnecessarily put the noose of IMF conditionalities as holy writs and albatrosses around their own necks. There was an exit from the debt trap which did not require that route. It called for a format of economic management that is more proactively towards production rather than waiting for the Godot of a trickle down from a liberalization that abhors genuine planning.
Under the banner of privatization, which assumes that a government that has no business in business can be relied upon to know how to privatize, they have pursued a loss-making anti-productivity charade which uses the incongruous case of the privatization of the communication sector as a ground for the privatization of all other enterprises. Let's even forget that Nigeria has lost about 200 billion Naira in pursuit of privatization.
Because the liberalizers turn out to be more interested in privatization than in supporting gen the private enterprise (which are not the same thing) they have been unable to press policies that can make Nigerians who have money to keep it on-shore. They have been unable to design policies that can credibly woo Nigerians with money abroad to bring the money back home. It makes it laughter-inducing to hear a lot of talk about attracting foreign investors to an economy which Nigerians with money will not bring their money into unless it is for the take-over of monopolies and monopsonies. Or to talk about attracting foreign investments to an economy which Nigerians are avoiding in order to establish factories abroad for the purpose of producing for the Nigerian market.
Truth to tell, you wouldn't need to attract foreign investment if Nigerians feel that their money was safe within the country. There would be enough indigenous money to power investments if only Nigerians would feel that the managers of the economy know what they are doing. No serious Nigerian with real money believes that the people in government know what they are doing.
They go along with government shenanigans for personal competitive advantage over others, in order not to lose out in the quicksand of Nigerian power-play. As for the Nigerians without real money, they are so convinced that the economic managers are swindlers that they would rather cut corners than wait for the proverbial trickle down that the World bankers and IMF undertakers in our midst expect as follow-ups to the misery of their bitter medicines.
So how is confidence to be built all around? The whole issue centres on the simple matter of managing the Naira like a proper currency. Who is going to bring hard earned money to an economy where the currency, arrantly-devalued, is doing mambo and shakey-shakey? Since the managers believe less in Nigerians than in creditors who are laughing at us behind their handkerchiefs, they have been displaying such unguarded disrespect for the Naira. Serious foreign investors take a cue from them.
Unless such an investor is assured of a quick kill based more on government deals than serious market orientation, they demand guarantees that no Nigerian would have needed to ask of a Nigerian government that is genuinely concerned about productivity. The foreign investor who can see how the ideology of reforms is deforming everything simply needs to look at the disrespect for the national currency and he and she will bid some time. Take the case of the President's enterprising choice of a Minister of Finance and sundry lower level interveners in Nigeria's economic decision-making who are on dollar salaries paid by creditor countries. There have been many mealie-mouth attempts to make light of this business of appointing someone who will not agree to earn the Naira but who must be saddled with the task of managing the Naira.
Unless it is explained by the policy of appointing indigenes to do the job of proconsuls, we are being asked to suspend disbelief as to why it should happen at all. Arguably, the Naira is obviously regressing to the level of toilet-paper and therefore cannot meet the international commitments of high-flying Nigerians who must be kept in accustomed comfort. The pity is that rather than raise the Naira to the level of the currency that the international jet-set with globalized commitments would be proud to earn, Mrs Okonjo-Iweala has spent all her time as finance Minister brewing up one scheme after another for devaluing the national currency.
In order to make it seem as if there is something different from Ibrahim Babangida's fiasco of structural adjustment programmes, the economic team with which the Finance Minister must help President Obasanjo, has crafted a homegrown but servile programme of mendicancy which an otherwise erudite Charles Soludo on his way to the Central Bank has marketed as National Economic Empowment and Development Strategy (NEEDS). After what I have read of Soludo's writings I would have said he was now in a position to see how, without offending the Bretton Woods institutions, he could out-bid the inequality in the international system to which he adverted so much attention in The Debt Trap In Nigeria. But the game is not really in his hands. All the fine phrasing in the policy documents adds up to a few ball points:
devaluation of the Naira, liberalization, privatization and inordinate search for debt forgiveness. Or, to put it differently, because of the mis-begotten search for debt forgiveness and the need to humour creditors rather than embark on genuine developmental strategy, they opted for policies that will make sure the NAIRA will never become respectable. So that the creditors will be happy that Nigeria is where they want her to be: incapable of ever standing up to their wiles.
And, in order to make sure that there are no serious organized groups to counter the drift, the whole programme is cushioned by a full steam constitutional effort to decapitate the labour movement, (after smashing the liver of the political party system) and reducing the Universities, in the name of reforms, to mere handmaidens of a poorly-funded parastatal. (And, creating a situation that authorizes even people still in government to promote their own private Universities as rafts for the normalization of private as public purpose).
On the surface, apostles of NEEDS market it as a strategy that does not believe in borrowing more money. But what does that mean in a country where the death of the national currency has already spelt out the death of industries, educational institutions, transportation, health facilities. When champions of debt forgiveness talk about passing a law to forbid unconscionable borrowing in future and at the same time talk about not completely ruling out borrowing, it is partly because they know that the way the NEEDS works, unless the upturn in oil revenue remains on the side of excess, will send us back to the creditors in no time. What does it mean, in any case, to say that you wont borrow more money in an economy where the lynching of the national currency has ensured that what Nigerians used to earn in a week is now earned in six weeks.
It means that we all work six times more to earn a day's wage in relation to externalities. Since there are only twenty four hours in a day and no new inventions, apart from cassava, has been found by the Presidency for raising productivity, we all know that the struggle for oil money is the beginning of survival. This is why all the talk about non-oil exports is actually a way of reminding us that those who killed the marketing boards are about to lure Nigerians into false confidence about a future of prosperity based on agricultural produce. They are lying to themselves and to us. If the lying works, it would mean that few Nigerians would be able to afford Nigerian-produced foods.
It will worsen the current situation in which the sweat that could have bought a brand new car in the past now buys a miserable tokunbo and money for medical equipment that could have gone round five hospitals can only now produce inadequately for one hospital. The situation, in relation to the knowledge industry, is such that no serious Professors abroad want to teach in Nigeria and Nigerian Professors look over-privileged when they are allowed nondescript placements abroad. Even in the supposedly non-militarized Fourth Republic, as Professor Niyi Osundare told us recently in his valedictory, the universe in the Nigerian University has been under erasure. It all boils down to what has happened to the Naira: which is why Paul Collier would expect to earn so much more money than a Nigerian Professor who has higher credentials than he has and must acquire so much of his professional ware from the same international market.
For that matter, one paradox, un-noticed by the festivating crowds around NEEDS is that the only reason the strategy appears to look good these days is that there is a forced recapitalization of the banks, and recently there has been a state-pampered attempt to establish a mega-company that is supposed to stand up to the multi-nationals around us. Bright ideas in wrong times. The good reasoning, even if not the open intention, is that with the Naira permanently in trouble, if you do not recapitalize you would soon be in more trouble than the market can take. By the same token, while the NAIRA remains the beleaguered currency that it has been since Obasanjo discovered the need for jumbo loans, the brouhaha about establishing a mega-company merely spells mega troubles.
With the Naira in trouble, recapitalization is really like running hard in order to stand still. To expect a mega-company without monopoly status in its chosen trenches to compete with companies operating from hard currency bases, will turn out to be more a matter of prestige, regime-maintenance strategies and therefore politics rather than a matter of economics and productivity. For this reason, from the standpoint of the freedom that Nigeria expects to be able to exercise as a result of being freed from depth servicing after debt forgiveness, it is important to stress it, and do so clearly, that no strategy that fails to take the value of the Naira into consideration will pass muster as an exit from Paris Club debts.
Actually, this is the point to turn to the particular mode of debt forgiveness that President Olusegun Obasanjo has pursued and netted as he has told the National Assembly. In the President's words," ... what we have now been given assurance to expect by the Paris club is that Nigeria will clear its arrears of $6 billion of the $30 billion owed The package in final terms that we are to expect would yield debt relief of about 60 percent on our current Paris Club debt. We shall pay off the 40 percent balance through a buy back operation. The total write off is about $18 billion which compares very favourably with the recent $40 billion write off of debts for the 18 highly indebted and poor countries of the world by the developed nations".
Interestingly, although the President says that the debt forgiveness is REAL, he has not said that it is in the bag. He uses terms like "what we have now been given assurance to expect" and the "final terms that we are to expect". These are not about the REAL, but expectations. So, no quibbling about it: the President has spoken in the very language in which the NEEDS document promises prosperity and poverty alleviation. The expectations can be proved to be quite wishful. It is REAL only if the Policy Support Instrument (PSI) is actually accepted.
President Obasanjo has said that the PSI which he would present to the creditors is the very NEEDS document that is the basis of the current reforms already going on. This is saying that an agreement to put the Naira down for the hacking is part of the means by which Nigeria is seeking debt forgiveness. It does not say so in so many words. But the recent announcement of a qqming increase in the price of fuel is enough as an eyeopener. I do not hesitate to say, in this connection, that the NEEDS document looks a very good instrument for enthroning political impatience with critics and selling a new and testy political class schooled in the practice of describing the creation of poverty as reforms.
But faced with impetuous price increases, such as we are told to expect, few Nigerians can remain absolute fools. It becomes obvious to all concerned that an underdeveloped economy based on NEEDS will always put the poor and the rich under undue pressure. If anything was needed to weaken a poor country and make her dependent on creditors, nothing could be better designed. While the President and his economic team are talking about the freedom they will now have to carry out programmes in accordance with Nigeria's national aspirations, they forget to say how this will happen with devaluation of the Naira. The fact that the President is talking about using a package that includes devaluation to convince the creditors about Nigeria~ s deservingness of debt forgiveness ought immediately to put every body's back up. What is in the document for the creditors that can also be said to be for Nigerians?
This is one question that must be answered in plain terms, with facts not sentiments, as the Minister of Finance, likes to put it. Truth is, what is for the creditors in the NEEDS document is against any productivity in Nigeria that can grant respite from external dicta. And that is the gospel truth. And that is what makes it so attractive as a bait for debt forgiveness. The creditors know this and that there is nothing in the document that can truly help Nigeria to pay what Nigeria owes. At any rate, they have decided to make assurance doubly sure by insisting that Nigeria must pay 12 billion dollars in two installments in order to qualify for debt forgiveness of 18 billion dollars.
Even the least knowledgeable Nigerian can see that this is targeted at the oil windfall that has netted Nigeria about $24 billion dollars in foreign reserves. Suppose Nigeria did not have such an amount? And, by the way, many Nigerians should be wondering where Nigeria would be finding that kind of money if the rest of the world had listened to President Obasanjo O'S consistent arguments against a rise in the price of oil at a time some OPEC countries were pushing for it. This is only part of the whimsicality that has governed Nigeria's search for debt forgiveness.
Besides, the fact that President Obasanjo has already agreed to paying so much money without seeking the say so of the National Assembly and the state governments which own vast proportions of that money is a tell-tale of how much incivility is being built into the process. Which country in the world but Nigeria has ever allowed herself and her money to be so parted? Which country with Nigeria's potential would fail to find what to do with 12 billion dollars in order to be able to produce the means for liquidating the 30 billion dollars that is dragging Nigeria to her knees!
Of course, I concede that an economic establishment already too sold on the moribund theories of devaluation, liberalization and privatization as the way of saving a third world country, cannot be relied upon to know how to spend 12 billion in a way that brings prosperity. After all, Babangida got twelve billions and used it to worship at the shrines of frippery. Which is to say, that those who wish to advise Obasanjo to do the right thing must know that his team has been so trained to doing the wrong thing that if they try to do the right thing, they may botch it. The National Assembly should therefore be well advised to let the President know that the money he wishes to give away in his gamble -- yes it is a gamble - does not belong to the Federal Government alone but to all the states and local governments as well. No Nigerian wants to spend that kind of money in pursuit of a lie.
It may be just as well to remind the President of his own appreciation of the fact that Nigeria is not in the same boat as those countries that got debt forgiveness without conditionalities. From postwar Germany, post-Hussein Iraq, Poland, Yugoslavia, Kyrgyzstan, Pakistan and Afghanistan to others in Eastern Europe -- these were countries in a crisis of survival. Many Nigerians remember the comic relief of Obasanjo threatening, obviously in frustration, that he was prepared to resign if the fall of a President was all it required for a country to get debt-forgiveness.
It was good to hear him admit before the National Assembly that those luckier countries that got debt forgiveness have more geo-cultural, political, economic military, strategic significance for the creditor countries than Nigeria has. What he does not add is that the very reason that Nigeria is not of such strategic significance is the reason that we should not rely on the goodwill of the creditor nations. They have no reason to do Nigeria a favour. For them it is warfare-trade by other means. This makes it rather embarrassing that the President of Nigeria should be expressing so much faith in either Tony Blair's Government or his Commission.
The hard fact is that Britain has her own problems and Tony Blair needs to serve Britain's national interests. As Mark Curtis notes in his book WEB OF DECEIT (Vintage 2003): "Two decades of Conservative rule left Britain with the worst poverty record in the developed world, according to the OECD.
Poverty affected 20 percent of the population between 1991 and 1996. This was a worse record than even the US -- where 14 percent were affected -- and compares with 10 percent in Germany and 7 percent in Sweden. A UN report found that a fifth of adults in Britain were functionally illiterate and that 13.5 percent were permanently living in poverty, a worse record than all other developed countries, except the US and Ireland". (P.223).
Speaking objectively, this is to say that Tony Blair has a patriotic duty to raise Britain out of the morass by helping countries like Nigeria to continue to buy from Britain. Obasanjo and his economic team ought therefore to know what to buy and what not to buy or take from Britain, in order for Nigeria's national interests to be well served. Whereas Tony Blair must remember that his political party, the Labour Party was once blamed for taking Britain to the IMF like a poor third world country, he would certainly not want to be blamed for not knowing how to use third world countries, like Nigeria to escape that fate during his tenure. Or after.
Blair does not have to be as virulent as Margaret Thatcher whose miserable monetarism set the stage for the impoverishment of Britain while savaging her third world trading partners. Obasanjo ought to know this, He ought also to know that, whether as a former military or civilian dictator, he will always be blamed for allowing so called developed countries to use Nigeria to bail themselves out of bad patches rather than angle for terms of mutual prosperity. If truth be told, it makes sense for Britain to forgive countries like Burkina Faso, save them for another day, while ensuring that a country like Nigeria supplies what will be lost in the process. At any rate, why does Obasanjo expect British workers to subsidize the folly, if not profligacy, of his country without demanding a pound of flesh in return?
So to say, I think that Britain and the other creditor countries would be acting irrationally to give debt-forgiveness to a profligate and cowardly elite like Nigeria's which would rather beg for crumbs thai juild the Naira into a hard currency and make a bold lurch for her hard earned money stashed away in foreign vaults. In my view the rest of us ought to find ways to make the Nigerian government turn a new leaf and face up to the reality that it is not in Nigeria's interest to throw 12 billion dollars away in pursuit of a vacuous debt forgiveness which, no matter how it is looked at, promises tragedy.
If we are so keen on meeting our international obligations lets meet it squarely. Demand the return of Nigeria's looted funds whose exit into offshore havens all the creditors have connived at. And insist on saving the Naira by putting it where our oil wealth, not non-oil expectations, have put us in world affairs. If our insistence on saving the Naira requires that we pay 20 % of our national income for debt serving purposes, why not. Simply consider the saving graces of a proper management of the Naira which, in a shot gun manner, can relieve the country of more than 80% of liabilities arising from following the moribund undertaker economics imposed by the creditors.

Original_Search_Title: Unemployment, Economy And National Growth
Headline: Nigeria: Unemployment, Economy And National Growth
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE deplorable state of unemployment in the country today, together with
the hopeless situation of our collapsing economy set in serious doubts over
the fate of this nation, despite all the hue about reforms and growth within
the nearest future of this civil regimes. The sad aspect of this is that
every move, initiative, or innovation complicates the already existing
condition, and slaughter finally the ebbing hopes through the glaring
indications and empirical evidences of increased difficulties, consequent
upon the solutions that were meant to proffer.
The only regular excuses for incompetence in this matter are that we are
still very young and can not expect to reap dividends of democracy within
this short period of five years and that the military bastardized the
economy in the wasted years they were in power, which we ought to have used
to take this nation to a greater heights.
But what is on ground with regard to the economy and condition of life
shows the military eras to be better and to be preferred now by most people
to this obstinate and incorrigible misgovernment of today, which dragged the
people to almost despair.
For instance, prices of commodities in the market were still affordable
prior to 1999, and unemployment had not assumed such a rapid increase in the
number of able-bodied and healthy youths and graduates without jobs. Even
the few that have jobs today are insensitively underpaid, and find it
difficult to meet up with their basic needs let alone assist a dependant, or
even see to qualitative education for the children.
One often finds it difficult to fathom the pride and purpose of a
government that sits to find that its first and main objective, which is to
see to economic comfort of the people, is failing. What sense would such a
government and its prattling make to the people with empty stomach? And how
does the government expect to find dedication and commitment in hungry
people, who are entrusted with governmental public services? The truth is
that the present leadership, I suspect does not yet know the hazards and
implications of running a society with collapsing economy.
Or, could it be that they rarely know the danger involved in economic
quagmire, and have to embark on the reforms this time? But ironically, the
reform seems to be making and would make matters worst, in creating more
hardship, unemployment and hunger in the land than were obtained
before.
Nigerians were not lamenting this much until the inception of this
reform. Without adequate encouragement of local productions, and
establishment of alternative industries that would prevent adverse effects
from being noticed, or rather occurring, most of the imported products (both
consumable and non-consumable) received bans. Who does not know that an
economy improves and foreign exchange earning increases with less
importation of foreign goods and, much exportation of local products as we
are constantly made to understand by the present government. The complaint
is that there are no provisions for the substitutes of banned products, and
the few available ones fall into economic law of increased prices at
scarcity. This is why commodities in the markets had skyrocketed beyond the
reach of the common man.
Even the banking sector has not helped matters with the radical and
inflexible method of reshaping its market this time. The method of
consolidation of the banking sector via N25 billion re-capitalization, which
was initiated middle of last year, is commendable. But the disturbing issue
is that CBN governor Prof. Charles Soludo, and his boss Mr. President,
failed to read the situation on ground. They did not approach this programme
gradually so as not to create more problems that were meant to solve. The is
evidence that they failed to note the eventual hazard and possibility of
having more people thrown into unemployment market if this consolidation
should result to merger of banks (and with the deadline. There are no job
opportunities in the society, and those who will bid farewell to banks have
nowhere to hang on, but only left to join the unemployed to loiter.
What about the cassava-flour policy, which the government boasts of
making great achievement from? On page 14 of Punch, August 5, 2005, was
reported that, "Some of the benefit of cassava was listed by minister of
Commerce, Alhaji Idris Waziri, the saving of about N28 billion which was
expended annually on importation of flour in bakery sector as well as
another N1 billion from imported sweeteners for the pharmaceutical and
alcohol and beverage industries which could be extracted from cassava."
Truly, this is great achievement, but what use is an achievement that
does reflect neither on the economy nor on the people? Every sensible parent
who has three cups of garri as food and source of their sustenance would not
sell two and half cups of it and use the earnings to decorate their house
and afterwards, start battling with starvation and almost go begging for the
same food they let out. The 10 per cent increase of cassava to the flour for
the purpose of generating higher revenue through exportations and the
exportations of raw garri and its chips has resulted to continuous increase
in the prices of this commodities. "For instance, one metric tonne of
cassava chip now goes for N20,000 as against N14,000 it went for between
January and March, 2005... an average price for a 100 kilogramme bag of
white garri varies from N4,000 to N4,200, while the yellow oiled-coloured
goes for between N5,200, N5,400 or N5,600" the prices which half of them
obtained the same commodity, few months ago. Nothing is wrong in exportation
of our cassava or use of it to augment flour, but it is wrong to embark on
such venture without having surplus, and sufficiently make provision for
food for the people at cheap and affordable prices.
At every corner, the people are complaining and expressing worries over
this condition. And as if the hardship is not enough, another threat came
from the leadership, to retrench 75,000 federal workers, the plans that are
still being incubated. Is this how the government that said they meant well
for the people show the love by stripping them of their means of livelihood?
Which jobs will the people undertake if this evil is successfully hatched?
Are they not going to file in amongst the unemployed or may be, the
government is preparing to take them to special place to work? Anyway, let's
wait and see events as they unfold. But it would have been better if job
opportunities were available where they can move in when retrenched.
That millions of the unemployed need jobs depends on the economy, and
there is no way we can talk of employment if the economy has not been worked
on to give opportunities for that. That is why there is every need to shout
if that is how the government will hear the cries of the people, and
prudently handle the affairs of this nation, or give way for better minds to
do the job.
My concern is that this very condition of unemployment is seriously
affecting the people, and turning the society into a jungle. The unemployed
will certainly not fold their hands to allow hunger kill them, and in order
to survive go about flaunting social and moral codes, and damn every
consequences of such wrongdoings.
On the pages of our newspapers are frequent reports of armed robbery
attacks and menace of the fraudsters, and the police expect to be commended
for doing great jobs when such news are indications of deterioration in
society, infested with jobless citizens. No wonder there is this undue
penchant for overseas emigration amongst the people in recent times.
On a serious note, most of these people who were nabbed by the security
agents, would ordinarily not involve in the crime that has now restricted
their freedom, if they had had an alternative in gainful employment to
combat the challenges that face them as humans. For the youths, some of
these challenges are that, they need to be independent, become a little bit
comfortable to be able to marry, and rear offsprings. Those who are married
have a duty to train their children and feed the family, and nobody will
understand them if there is no food on the table. How will these challenges
be met in this joblessness and idleness we face today in Nigeria? Sequel to
this, women on their part outgrow their marriageable age, and either go into
unsound relationships or take to prostitution.
I will say that in a society where economy is murdered, the people
scramble for the remnants of its carcass into the grave where they end up
and bury their fate. We can dream of national growth if we do not boost the
economy to the comfort harboured by masses. The government failed to
understand that the rancour and acrimony harboured by some section of this
country is rooted in economic discomfiture. Had there been booming economy,
where citizens had reasonable comfort to enjoy, the heat and threats from
these aggrieved members of the society would have been amicably
resolved.
Therefore, the government should make haste and wake up to its task of
seeing to this economic revamping, in manners that would not create more
problems to the people. Also, let something be done to this teeming number
of the unemployed, to give them sense of belonging, and restore their faith
in this nation. To neglect this is to wake up from the dream of national
growth, and journey to national disintegration through national destruction
as its natural consequence.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Shouldn't Subsidise Petroleum Products - Kupolokun
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Shouldn't Subsidise Petroleum Products - Kupolokun
Body_Text:
Lagos — In the last 12 months, crude oil prices have been going in one direction. The upward trend. But while other oil producing companies are smiling to the banks, the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), which is in charge of managing the nation's oil wealth, says it is about going under. The Group Managing Director of the corporation, Engr. Funsho Kupolokun at a session with THISDAY Board of Editors in Abuja, blames this downturn in its fortune to the burden of subsidizing domestic prices of petroleum products which the corporation now bears. What to do? End subsidy on fuel consumption once and for all. He also enumerated strategies the NNPC is employing to stay afloat. Excerpts:
How much does it cost to build a medium size refinery, and how much are you losing importing petrol?
A medium size refinery should be anywhere between $1.5 to $1.8 billion. However, when you have a refinery you do TAM every two years. Until President Obasanjo came in, no Turn Around Maintenance was done on these refineries. The resultant effect of it is that we are talking of a refinery that has become more or less discredited. So it was only since 1999 that we started putting in money, so on the average if we are going to maintain each of the refineries, with the petrochemical plants, the pipelines, on the average between $200 million to $250 million for each of them over a period of three years. But with it we will also have the opportunity of modernizing the refineries because you continually modernize.
In terms of how much we are losing, today we are talking of N22 a litre, a few days ago it was N25 a litre. And that is what translates to the N600 million a day given the volume we are doing.
The argument on why fuel prices should increase has not really changed, this is the same argument that you made when we began this journey, that if you don't increase prices you would not be able to pay salaries. I'm surprised that you are still making the same argument. Secondly, recently we had session with the Vice President and he said left to him he would have scrapped the NNPC, how do you justify the continued existence of the NNPC in the light of this situation?
This argument has never gone away. Today, the truth of the matter is that facts are facts. You can't slant it in any form or shape. If I say I am owing marketers one billion dollars and you don't belief me but Financial Times of London published it. What else to say and I was in Brazil and I was getting frantic calls that, that was what is in Financial Times of London. So, everybody on the streets of Abuja knows the financial position of the NNPC. As to what you said the VP said, I was away somewhere yesterday so I didn't read the papers. But even when I would have read it, there is no way on earth you would expect me to comment, that would be totally unacceptable. However when I do read it, if there are lapses we need to correct, we will continue to make efforts to improve on what we are doing and you could see all that we are doing in different areas. We will continue to improve on any area we were being told that we are not doing well enough.
If it were solely economics, this issue of deregulation would have been simple to solve. But as it is, this thing is not just economics. If politics has to come into this matter, it is clear that this appropriate pricing may never be appropriate, because there is no time you re going to increase prices that labour will not talk and government will listen. What can really be done?
If we want to start doing things on basis of political consideration and not commercial reality of it, what is going to happen is that you re saying to me that we should not really build it as aggressively as possible and I don't expect you to say that because I know that it is necessary for us to do it. But to be able to do it efficiently and as fast as possible, we have to put some commercial reality check into it. If not, if we say lets do it, lets remember politics is involved, anything that politics is involved you know it moves like snail. And we here in NNPC we don't want to move like a snail for something that Mr. President had said do, and he gave us this time frame that it must be done. So we have to do it within the time frame, so it has to be done and we have tried as much s possible to make all our mega stations commercially viable.
But each time there is an argument for deregulation or increase in prices, the government says it will invest the proceeds in education, in infrastructure. After getting the increase, the promises they made are not delivered. How then do you think the citizen should support another increase in prices? Secondly what has happened to your gas to liquid initiative with Chevron, will it have to feed the Niger Delta floating stations?
Promises made in the past, where is it and how does it fit? But from what I have shown, it is obvious that a lot of money is going into this industry. Re-investment. If we are talking of cost reflective pricing, unless pricing is appropriate and cost reflective, you are still not out of the woods. But even with the marginal increments we have gotten in the past, you could see that a lot of investments have gone in. So, what we need is to strive to manage the whole process to complete liberalisation. The only difficult thing we are facing here is that uncharacteristically the industry is producing prices going only in one direction which is an unusual performance. Usually over a 10-year period, there is what you call mean reversion. Price always tends to revert to the mean. Today we are at a different level of mean price but over 10 years, it should revert to the mean. Normally prices should still go down, I don't see oil prices going to $100 per barrel. If the industry is what we know, that has been tested in the last 30 years. It will always come down. The important thing that we need to do is that when prices are also on the way down we should reflect that very rapidly. And I will be the first to say let us reflect the downward side.
You talk of the GTL, the GTL is not going to feed on this, but there is mini-refinery coming up in Escravos. That mini refinery is part of the whole package and came out of the directive of Mr. President. That is already on now. Once we get that it will supply products to the floating stations. So it is whole package of intervention.
It does seem that a way of breaking out of this vicious circle is to actually attack the supply end of this argument. You said earlier that global refiners' interest in the privatisation of our refineries is limited, why is this so? Secondly, why is it taking so long for the private refineries to take off?
The question has stated my argument very strongly. Why is it that global refiners have shown limited interest? Why is that those who got refining license two years ago, it is only now that they are showing interest? It is simple. If prices are not cost reflective I will not put my money and invest. We have said it, we are talking of $1.5 billion investment. But now that they see that government is making effort at deregulating the industry, they are coming back, they are now beginning to show interest. But if what we've done so far, we've not done it, we have not given the right signal we can't be here talking for 10 years they will not show interest. The interest is now coming because they see that we are doing, because they believe this government is serious about opening up the downstream.
You also ask why is it that we do not have a regulation to refine a certain percentage of our crude volume in Nigeria. We have sent to the industry a draft regulation. They have come back with all sorts of opposing views. We are meeting with them to hear them and work on their views where appropriate. But we are doing what we have to do. Within two weeks you will hear from us. So we align with you because you believe that is what should be done and we are doing it so we are aligned.
There is bidding conference on Friday (today). To what extent is there a condition that for anyone to get any of the lucrative blocks he must also refine in Nigeria, because it is better to deal with it at licensing stage than when people are already operational?
I don't know if you know what letters I have gotten, because the question you ask is in virtually all the letters I got back. It is like this, okay, if you guys want it put it as a condition precedent in your licensing round. But between you and me, that cannot be in the best interest of the country. We are doing some 2.6 to 2.7 million barrels per day capacity, infact by the time Bonga comes up we will be talking of productive capacity of 3.0 million bpd. If we excuse all that and we say we are not touching that, it is the man who is coming to get a block, may be a deep offshore block who is not going to produce until years time that we are making regulation for. In 10 years time, you and me won't even be in this boardroom to start with. If we are still on this planet Earth. Then, you can imagine that, that will be a joke. But what is the fact that is on ground? The fact is there is nothing new under the sun. In those days, the different companies contributed to domestic refining. Until somehow, I didn't know how it happened, in the late 70s, that bit became only NNPC that is obligated to put crude into the domestic refineries. All you could be arguing is to say, for the barrel 'am taking in here, I want a cost reflective price for what I'm putting in here. That is another issue.
A lot of people feel the NNPC is manacled, that it does not take independent decisions. Again, there is the recommendation by the Mantu Committee which the government has not touched and here you are talking of another price increase, what can you say to this?
You talk of interference in NNPC. The only thing I told you the President directed me to do is the floating mega station. If a president says I want a floating mega station in Soku or Join Krama, is there anything wrong in that, would you call that interference? I think it is proper and in order. But really talking about interference, that could be the case in the past but since 1999 and I have been around since 1999, government interference is no longer there. And that is why we were able to do what we are doing.
You talked about Mantu Committee, I guess you may want Senator Mantu to comment on this.
If you are to recommend to the PPPRA on the ideal price of a litre of fuel, how much would you say it is? What is the update on your relationship with AP?
What price do you recommend? I have said it loud and clear that my business is to discuss my business. How is NNPC fairing in the face of existing pump prices. Somebody else has a responsibility for pricing and that is PPPRA. So I can tell you what I am losing but I cannot opine on what PPPRA will be comfortable with. But we've made our position clear. We are not shy to say what we have to say but it is up to PPPRA to take the decision that is within its own statutory responsibility
As for AP, we are discussing with AP to resolve the indebtedness issue. I can see that people are talking about it. I don't see the big deal. If I owe you, you will ask me to pay. There is no way I will continue to owe and you would ask me to pay. And when we found what our cash flow is like, we then said everybody owing NNPC even N1.00 we should chase him and collect it. And everybody else in this country, outside this country, we chased them and we collected up to the last penny. Check, there is no marketer in Nigeria today that is owing us. So what is therefore unique in the case of AP. AP is owing us, pay because we are in bad shape. That is as simple as the story is.
Why would the government sell crude for domestic consumption at international market rate, whereas a sizeable volume from the country's production could still be exported at market price to earn good money?
Let me say one thing, nobody is against subsidy. It is done everywhere in the world. But everywhere else they don't subsidise petroleum products. They subsidise items that would affect the masses more than the ones that affect the elites. I don't want to get into this and the poor people debate. But when you subsidise petroleum product consumption, it is the likes of you, the likes of Kupolokun that benefit more because the poor man's consumption on annual basis, the total volume he consumes in a year is very minimal compared with the consumption of the GMD of NNPC because it is the GMD of NNPC who will go from here to there, and therefore for every litre you are subsidising when you take the total volume , you are subsidising me more. And I do not think that is fair. It is not fair, it is not equitable and it is unjust. And that is what is not done anywhere else in the world. However, you could take what comes from this to subsidise the right sectors, agriculture, education, health services. For instance if any of THISDAY editors require medical attention, the Chairman can fly him abroad if it becomes necessary. However, that poor man in the village has to make do with the medical centre in the village. Therefore we should put the money into the medical centre in the village and hospitals in Nigeria. So this debate of whether we should subsidise petroleum products, we've trashed it over and over and I don't think we should go back to it.
You say you are in deficit of N238 billion. Can you also manage your other resources more efficiently? For instance, could it be a bit more efficient if you sold your interest in the joint ventures, as it is being called for, or if you have them fund themselves or if you don't have to pay cash calls. Is there a more efficient way to run our joint ventures and save money to net off your deficits?
You've raised so many issues. One is the issue of value for money, whether there is transparency or not. Whether NNPC likes it or not, the country today is strong on accountability, transparency and for this government of President Obasanjo it is like a doctrine. And I told you that with all the initiatives in place, it is going to be difficult for somebody to fool around. Having said this, there are a number of petroleum arrangements. One is concession arrangement and in this you are saying we should sell down our interest to free us from funding problem and that transparency will be assured, because nobody will be cash-calling. To start with, 85 percent tax rate is what the taxation level is. And on land we are talking of tax rate of 20 percent, in shallow offshore 18 and half percent and over 100 metres you are talking of 16.75 percent. When you put all these together, you find out that government's share of the economic rent is anywhere between 78 to 82 percent. So, if we say we are participating the way we are participating all that we are struggling about is about 15 to 20 percent, it makes it so easy to canvass that we should divest because all we are talking about is having a share of the remaining 15 to 18 percent. But the truth of the matter is this. If when we are there and we call for the budget and we meet with them, we look at the contract and we say we are not too sure if they are not playing games, by the time we go out of it completely, even the so called tax and royalty that we say we are entitled to we are not going to be sure it will be there in the quantum it should be. Because when you are not there, when you are not part of it, then anything happens.
Now talking about funding, the least onerous funding is equity funding. That is known. And we agreed with you. However, government has a lot of competing priorities -roads, agriculture, education, and these are not easily bankable projects. So money should go into these non-bankable sectors and therefore we could through alternative funding seek loans, seek alternative funding schemes to fund oil projects that are always typically in their own right bankable and a number of these alternative funding, there are PSC variants, where somebody puts all his money, when oil is won, he recovers his funds first and the balance is split in an agreed ratio. So I believe alternative funding should continue.
Can't you explore a situation where you sold your shares in those Joint Ventures to the NPDC, so you can have joint operations, joint exploration and all that. And NPDC on its own can even manage some of the fields as participants and the oil majors could just sit back?
What we do with the joint venture? I was in Benin three weeks ago and we signed the first of such agreements where we are in an international joint venture arrangement with a joint operating concept with Chevron and NNPC for the first time will be the operator and we will all fund but NNPC will operate the field. The second of such is coming very, very soon with Shell. It is just like we are aligned, we think alike between the boards of NNPC and THISDAY there is an alignment.
Another question which can address the issue of transparency is that, why can't we sell our crude in Naira, where all the multinationals open their LCs into Nigerian banks and then the dollars they pay to the Nigerian banks can be bought back by the NNPC?
On that, there are two aspects to it. One is what it does to the bank and two paying abroad whether we are really able to trace it. Whether we are really able to trace it, that I can assure you that it goes into an account in New York and that is known to everybody and there is no way anybody can fool around with it and we've never had problem with that.
We are talking about the joint venture partners' section of it. The money of the multinationals themselves, their own crude.
The Petroleum Act will not allow that. The Petroleum Act as at today says when you win, you can freely dispose off your crude.
Lets deal with the concept and not the Act.
Talking of what impact it will do for the banking sector, I wouldn't want to comment on that. I think Charles Soludo will do better on that.
What are your medium and long term plans regarding oil prospecting in the Chad Basin area?
Let me tell you this. We drilled a number of wells in the past. We tested these wells and we could not talk of any significant discovery. In more recent time we hired some consulting firms to look at the results that came out of these wells. But since 25 years technology has improved and the sort of advice we are getting from the consultants they are excited. It shows that with today's technology that the sort of results we got 25 years ago may not be what we are getting today. And therefore what we should be talking of now is how to go and test, explore in those places. But the way to do it is to put them on the licensing round and call for tender. And people who win, because the technology is now available, they can test these fields. Until you drill you will not know exactly what is there but I can tell you that we are very enthused to the sort of things we are getting from our consultants.
Recently the country got debt relief and suddenly there is this renewed bid for fuel price increase. In the past the push came from NNPC and PPRA but this time the Presidency is involved, Budget Office, Finance Ministry. Is fuel price hike part of the conditions for the debt relief? Is it part of the PSI?
To the best of my knowledge, it is not a condition and as far as I know we have others who appreciate our positions. They are sympathetic. They know the right thing and they are saying what they believe in. But I'm presenting what the position is in NNPC it has nothing to do with debt relief or any conditionality for debt relief. I stick to what I know.
Where are you now in your bid to invest in other countries?
As I am talking now, we have 12 of our people in Equitorial Guinea and we are also talking with other countries in the region and in another six months we will be putting signatures to paper. We are going to invest in exploration in a number of countries in the region.
Deregulation might be desirable but the politics of doing it is a key consideration. How can we break this jinx, if the government has the political will, how can it do it so that we don't go in this repetitive circle?
Subsidy and correct pricing. Today it could be X tomorrow it could be Y and the next day it could be Z all the way down. What we said sometime last year was that if we get to some level lets go for a price modulation and a modulation works like this. When there is surplus to gain at the pump you put that money in a basket.
