When there is a deficit like this you pool from the basket so that the man at the pump will not see significant shocks, though there will be movements it will be rather smooth.
Your pension is in deficit in billions of naira and I think the President has given you the go-ahead to sell one or two of your assets to make up for the deficit. What is the position on this?
Very correct. Some deficit, we are working on it. Again it comes to what we are saying. If a man retires and because all these are happening, we are not able to put the proper amount to fund the pension, which is the real crux of the matter. When a man who has retired and thinks the only thing he has is his pension and he is waiting to enjoy it till God will say it is time, and you now tell him that NNPC is not able to do this because NNPC is not particularly solvent, the man will not be too happy. I was a pensioner myself, I left in 1993 and if I was not doing anything else and I depended on my pension and you now start telling me stories that because you are not recovering correct price from the pump therefore you are not able to put enough money in the pension and therefore I could not get my pension at the right level and on time definitely, I cannot be happy. That is why NNPC should not be crushed.
Recently local industries cried out against scarcity and hike in price of power fuel oil. What is the situation?
I don't think there is scarcity in low pour fuel oil. The only thing is that there was a mismatch between the export and domestic prices, but that has been corrected.
Where are you in the pursuit of the Local Content Policy?
A lot is happening in the area of local content. The joint qualification system, JQS, is going to be launched any moment from now. We will go to one centre point, you have every information, every data you need about a contract, about a particular contractor short-listing. Then we talk about the Petroleum Market Place, we are doing the pilot one any moment from now. And by January, we will commence a Petroleum Market Place.
The petroleum market place is a reverse auction. If you do auction, you say what's your price, you give it to the highest price.. But in the case of petroleum market place, everybody will be there on screen and you put it on screen and we are all watching and nobody can fool around with the other party. And then you keep putting your prices and reducing it. That is why we say it is reversed, until you reach a point of resistance. So the man who has the least price for that contract gets it. It does two things, you bring the price of the contract to bear pole and indeed from the survey, we will be able to save a billion dollars per annum with the petroleum market place. But it also will bring to an end the issue about transparency about contracts. The number of bidders will be those pre-qualified from the JQS. So the bidders are those who know what they are doing.
In addition, talking about local content, we have said all engineering work must be done in Nigeria by 2006. But it is not just enough to make pronouncements. We are following it up with action. We conducted a study, we found that to do engineering works in Nigeria, for the volume, the quantum of work because I told you the industry is undergoing, there is an explosion in terms of industry activities, we will require some 2,500 practicing design engineers. And the number we have from the study is 600. so there is a shortage, there is a gap. We need to move rapidly to train Nigerian engineers to meet the gap. We also said all FPSO topsides must be assembled in Nigeria. We are working with the oil companies. When the day comes you have to do it.. If you are ready you do your own project, if you are not you shut down.. so because of that everybody is working with us.
And when we talk of investing in the domestic end, all the oil companies are obligated to do one independent power project. We must get 3,000 mega watts of electricity from the oil industry, and this is also going on very well. Afam is well underway, Kwale/Okpai phase one is done,. The Chevron in Ijede is well underway, Obite of Elf is well underway. The Mobil one should be incorporated in the Mobil LNG, is also well underway. And all these projects will be commissioned by God's Grace before May 29, 2007.
The Federal Government plans seven power plants for the Niger Delta, but the available gas cannot meet the demand for these plants. What are you doing to ensure that these plants have enough?
The Niger Delta power plants, there are seven of them and they are being pursued aggressively. We are working with the Power Holding Company. The Minister of Power and Steel is pushing the project. For us what we need to supply is the gas. However, the gas reserves today is 187 trillion cubic feet (tcf). But 187 tcf of the gas which is plenty. But the point is, what is the volume that is immediately available for delivery before 2007. That is the question. And how do you develop this gas volume in an optimised manner. Another question. And then some of these gas are coming as gas cap, which may not be immediately available until after sometime. But even for the ones that are immediately available you need at least two years to develop the gas plants. So, if you begin to do it on specific bases which was what we did in the past, it cannot be optimal. So we are now beginning to think of clusters, we are now beginning to think of nodal architecture in development of this gas. However, as to whether we will be able to deliver, we've done a study and it's obvious that we will be able to deliver for all these projects before 2005, because we have a number of things.
If you have no money how do you do it?
The money is available, I told you this is being driven by the Minister of Power and Steel and the money is available for the downstream part. For the upstream part, we will put it as part of our joint venture budget which will be going to government to get the money.
What initiatives do you have in the area of renewable energy.
We are going to look at alternative energy sources. We are looking at Ethanol, to start it and we are going into solar. For ethanol, I was in Brazil, we came back. We are going to really push ethanol usage for PMS. What we will do and which is done in most countries, is to inject 10 percent of ethanol and the remaining 90 percent will be gasoline and works and it has been tested everywhere.
What do you gain by that? First, in terms of cost we will gain a lot, it is much more cost effective. But two, in terms of the environment, all the environment issues will be taken care of. The first co-operation agreement we signed Monday last week with Petrobras and COIMEX and we expect to have the first mixture, to supply a PMS/Ethanol mixture by January 1. The programme is on-going.
It is not importing ethanol to supply the domestic market. We are also going into the distilleries. We also want to distil and produce ethanol in Nigeria. And to do that you use two things, cassava and sugarcane. And we are also going to get the private sector interested in the production of cassava and in production of sugarcane. And these will be mass-employers.
It will generate employment massively. That is the biggest thing for me apart from the environmental issues. And what is more, the industry has remained an enclave unto itself like an ivory tower in the past. But we are now beginning through local content, through ethanol and RED to link the oil industry to other sectors, and when you have a marriage of agriculture with oil industry the only result you can get is substantial growth in the economy.

Original_Search_Title: Focus On Greedy Politicians And Government Minister Vanishes Into Thin Air
Headline: Sierra Leone: Focus On Greedy Politicians And Government Minister Vanishes Into Thin Air
Body_Text:
Freetown — On Sunday 21st August 2005, I have just
finished eating a mountainous plateful of cooked rice with cassava
leaves and resting when my cell phone rang.
The melodious female voice was pleading with me to join her at the
beach for the Sunday relaxation. Of all the women in the world, I
least expected a call from his lady. She has always proved to be
tough (Miss hard to get). Honestly speaking, I have chased her for
over twelve months without success. By then I was desperately
searching for someone like her to fill in a gap. All my carefully
planned gospels, which I use to preach to win the heart of tough
ladies did not help either "I am deeply engaged" she told me one day.
The man she was referring to according to her is married with
children so I refuse to give in Celtel Top Ups is my witness. "I
don't mix matters up so please understand" was her final statement
that stopped me from calling. Since then I have distanced myself from
her until on that Sunday when she finally break the long silence.
She described the exact location where to meet her at the beach. I
clicked my memory to the past and pretended not to be interested in
her invitation so I created some bureaucratic protocols to add more
value to my prestige. But I later succumbed.
The next minute I was looking at my reflection in the dressing
mirror. Dressed like any 'Jay Cee' on a short vacation, I descended
from Kabassa Lodge where I now stay, hits the street of Juba Hill,
down to Lumley roundabout and boarded a taxi to "Bintas" beach bar.
Away I went with only Le 5,000 in my pocket excluding taxi fare. That
particular day I was dead broke like a Church rat.
At the beach bar I was the first to spot her. Her eyes balls were
moving clockwise and anti clockwise like a hungry lion in search of
it prey. An eye contact with her physical make up, I became
intoxicated with admiration She was dressed in her casuals like a
real African beauty Queen destined to live on planet earth forever.
The spirit of "kamu kamu" invaded my brain cells. To confess, I
regretted the unnecessary bureaucratic protocols I created over our
telephone discussion.
When she finally spotted me, her embraced did not go without
expressing true and honest love for me "The storm is over. I am all
yours from today hence fort" She said. This was followed by a
marathon kiss. That kiss can best be described as palatable. "Is this
a dream? " I whispered to myself. I touched my back pocket for the Le
5,000. It was there Her warm body temperature over shadowed the cool
breeze at the beach. This sent me into heaven on earth.
She pointed to a reserved chair meant for me. As I was about to take
my seat and contemplating on what to do next with just Le 5,000 in my
pocket, I noticed one astonishing thing that distracted my attention
from her. I saw a Government minister (name withheld sitting with
four "Kolonkos" sorry with four ladies.
The negative effect of bleaching was written all over the ladies.
Their black and red fingers and feet and half naked attire coupled
with cigarette smoking was enough for one to conclude that they were
"kolonkos".
I gazed at them in amazement.
The table of the minister was full to the brim with all kind of
expensive drinks: Heineken, Becks, Guinness stout etc. Plates of
pepper chicken and other "yum-yum" added more decoration to their
table.
Trust those "kolonkos" for their unlimited appetite for delicious
foods at the expense of their victims.
Those who were fortunate or unfortunate to sit close to them got only
the aroma of the chicken as their own share. I was no exception.
The unchecked spending of the minister became a concern to me. He was
extravagant, a perfect example of a corrupt government official in a
lucrative government ministry.
Oh money! Drinks and other food items were pouring like August rain
on the table of the minister.
Whether it was a financial coup by the four ladies to exploit the
minister, I cannot tell but any friend of theirs who passed by their
table, they will draw the attention of the minister to their friends.
The minister in turn will instruct, "Go to the bar and take drinks of
your choices. Just tell the waitress to add it to my bill". Eight
extra ladies benefited from that scholarship. The total bill finally
came and was handed over to the minister by the waitress. The figure
I over heard triggered my heart to best faster.
I jumped on my feet and stood up to do God alone knows what. But the
amount involved was equivalent to pay for two years rent for the
three bedrooms flat I am currently occupying at Juba Hill.
A pat on my shoulder from my queen saved the minister from further
microscopic gaze. She might have made several attempts to talk to me
without my response.
When she finally succeeded in getting my attention, I noticed that
she had ordered a drink for me - 'Don simon wine', my favourite. The
rest was conversation centered on love. I took a long sip of my wine.
As I put down my glass cup on the table I noticed that the minister
has vanished in tin air with the four Kolonkos.
In the heart of greedy politicians, there is promiscuity, there is
murder, there are abominable lies, and there is deception,
corruption, suspicions, greed, arrogance, unforgiveness, intolerance,
egotism, ungodliness and blaspheming. Greedy politicians are destiny
quenchers, satanic aide-de-camp {ADC}, financial swallowers, bondage
distributors, progress arresters, poverty activators, problem
expanders and planters of evil trees.
These categories of politicians can manipulate anything under the sun
for their own advantage even if it involves killing. They are the
political Pharaohs, Goliaths, Herods and Jezebels of our time. If you
like call them political bastards or idiots. Their motto is 'Self
before country'.
A lot of frustration, failure, lack of achievement and purposeless
living, which we come across today, can be traced to these Jezebels
and pharaohs. While accomplishing their devilish plans and in the
process you are identified, as an obstacle a short cut to the grave
becomes a must or you are silenced behind bars (Good morning Paul
Kamara).
These idiots relied so much on their connections and or party
affiliation that a new vocabulary has emerged to describe them "the
untouchables." They are power drunk, insensitive to people's feeling,
money & property conscious and corrupt to the highest degree.
But these Pharaohs, Goliaths, Herods, and Jezebels shall be buried
alive, in Jesus name. Join me to say- Amen.
Take for example the attitude of the (Dis) Honorable Member of
Parliament Dr. (?) Fatmata Hassan, ordering the beating of the acting
Editor of 'For Di People' Newspaper Hary Yansaneh by her children
(only DNA can tell whether infact those children are the true blood
of the late admirable Komeh) on May 10th resulting to his death on
July 27th at the cupid hospital all because of property. Fatmata
Hassan is a true Jezebel of our time.
But the death of Harry Yansaneh has added more ego to the profession.
Harry is gone but his death has allays the fear of journalists to
live in fear of personal security. Our identity now speaks aloud.
Let the political Pharaohs and Jezebels know that Newspaper editors
are the schoolmasters of the common people. The likes of Mohamed
Samoura, I. B Kargbo, Philip Neivelle, Christian Kelly, Jonathan
Leigh, Olu Gordon, Frank kpossowa, Moses Kargbo and Musa Yamba etc
are to be feared more than 2000 RPGS or mortar bombs fired together.
These editors have vowed to avenge the death of the late Harry
Yansaneh (except Democrat editor) and expose the corrupt political
elements in our society.
Bravo "korthors"!
May the soul of Harry Yassaneh rest in perfect peace

Original_Search_Title: Operation Declare Your Assets or Forfeit Your Surplus
Headline: Nigeria: Operation Declare Your Assets or Forfeit Your Surplus
Body_Text:
Please let us stop chasing the ghost, cut the chase and go after the individual surplus. Every Nigerian knows where the money is. We know there is even more money outside the country; foreign governments have been sensitized to the source and they are now embarrassed as receiver of stolen properties. The problem is those conspicuous spenders who are spending the money in our face, living it in our face and no one can query them. Actually, Obasanjo had to negotiate with one to keep some stolen millions to avoid lengthened litigation, only to renege on the terms of his release.
Once upon a time, a Nigerian would take ten percent kickback from the Federal Government contract, but never from the local area where everybody knows everybody. Time has changed. Conspicuous spenders have taken over. They are encouraged by praise singers who eulogize them. There are two ways to dislodge their ill gotten surplus - 1. By revolution or 2. By due process.
There has to be due process. It has to be done with human face so that it does not become a class or an ethnic thing. I do not think Jerry Rawlings is better than those he killed in Ghana in the name of revolution.
Many of us are copy cats. If it is done in American and Europe, fine. If that is the only way to catch the fat cats, I will tag on. Well, the best way to catch a drug dealer, a spy, a terrorist and a rogue in these countries is to demand accountability for any conspicuous spending. Can you imagine a Nigerian oppressor without conspicuous consumption?
Conspicuous spending is a major part of our moral bankruptcy. It sends a powerful message to the have-nots to make money by any means possible. The armed robber, Oyenusi's confession did not teach us any lesson. They always return to the crime scene.
The oppressor, for fear of dying, has to spend his money sooner than later. Watch out for his cars, his houses, political donations and funding of privileged organizations. We have a tax system in Nigeria but we have deliberately not used it to catch the oppressors. We are on the right path though. If anyone needs government service, they ask for three years income tax receipts. Great! Let us move a step further. If you under-declare, explain how you are going to finance your proposals.
Some Nigerians inflate their earnings when trying to bring relatives and friends to Europe or America. Some swear to Affidavits about their earnings and properties. They have to present these to convince the Embassies of their capabilities to care for those sponsored. Little did some of them realize that Uncle Sam was taking note. At the end of the year, the tax man knocks based on the earning declared!
It is one thing to live like a pauper in Europe or America trying to help a relative, it is quite another to live like a king in Nigeria spraying dollars and pounds claiming to be a pauper, on record. We know the salary of head of Governments, head of the Arm Forces, head of corporations. If we multiply it by two, taking "fringe benefits" into consideration, the oppressor would still have a case to answer. The allowance the politicians award themselves these days is nothing short of legalized looting, while the masses groan.
The story these days is that some individuals who are not well connected are being turned into scapegoats, to make anti corruption bodies look good or bad, only to be discharged by our court of law. They must be chasing ghosts to come empty handed. If cash is seized, properties seized, cars are seized and video camera shows conspicuous spending in Europe and America, who will discard those as evidence? These are stronger evidences than the reports of committees. It is used in the countries we mimic and copy to death.
American CBS 60 Minutes showed how easy it is for a foreigner on a visit to get Nigerian passport, how children of African dictators spend money overseas. Our Press has lived up to their reputation in Africa, as the most vigorous against all odds.
I have been investigated by a manager in the Central Bank myself. Before my family joined me in Nigeria in 1981, I had to send them some percentage out of my modest salary to help pay mortgage and my student loan. The Niara was mighty then. I never had Federal or State scholarship just like many of us. I had to work and go to school. Nevertheless, he decided to investigate me because I did not "cooperate" and the result of the investigation solidified my application. What about those who "cooperated" and were left uninvestigated, while wasting time on me? I now realize how lucky I was, he could have gotten vicious.
Poor Nigerians have been asked to show proof of any money anywhere and anti corruption bodies would go after it. Haba! Asking the mouse to catch fat cats! I think it should be the other way round. Go after their cash and belongings and let them account for it. That is why these countries have laws against money laundering.
There are so many projects the loot could be invested in, other than being squandered. If it is spent to open a business, we can say at least gainful employment is created. No matter what we say about Abiola, he created jobs, to his own credit. That money could have been used to create first class hospitals and solid returns could have been made. Americans and Europeans are going to first class hospitals in Asia for elective surgery that are too expensive at home while Nigerians travel out for headache.
Private schools investment is not so risky to make them so expensive. One wonders what they teach and how any Nigerian could afford them. They all have foreign sounding names, and Nigerians can pay in any currency. Well if the market is there, share the loot! However, public schools have been reduced to daycare centers. Where is the balance?
We now have men of "timber and caliber" who have not stolen all their life. But wait! All they did was deposit hijacked salaries, in special banks while families go desperate and hungry. As soon as another allocation is released, they replace the one in the bank while the new allocation collects interest. They dismantle the fabric of our society by denying workers the regular flow of sustenance.
This is new in Nigeria. Nobody used to mess with salary. This other side of the coin is the destruction of families by non-payment of wages. Husband or wife without salary goes begging or goes into private practice, "pp". The wife tries to cover for the husband, asking for neighbors' help - "borrow borrow". This is the beginning of the end of family as we know it in our society. When a woman goes begging, her honor is compromised. She loses respect for the husband. When that happens, her home becomes unstable. The next victims are the children.
We are now destroying our communities because morals have sunk to the lowest level, discipline and order have succumbed. These chain reactions culminated into criminal activities and prostitution in our country and beyond. It is manifested on our street, when allocation of salary and logistics do not reach the target and police collect twenty naira as "family support" and maintenance of vehicles. It is misery and suffering, a vicious circle in the land of untapped resources because of this curse-oil discovery.
Nigerians can learn from the situation of our brothers and sisters in Diaspora, if a case study is needed. Once the man is shamed out of the house, he is castrated. The family can not hold. We are falling apart faster than we realize. The only difference is that Africa is still a village where every man is a father figure. Every child has somebody to call a father. Even that is changing.
In a depressed economy where money is not circulating, prices should fall, right? Wrong, not in Nigeria!
If a country gets the opportunity to export its surplus product, like cassava that can grow all over Nigeria, one would think that as more cassava is produced, more jobs will be created and the jobless would have money in their pockets, right? Wrong, not in Nigeria.
It seems the Government is happy that gari is now expensive and the farmers would be richer because the high cost will spur production. Is rice more expensive in Asia because it is exported? What do we have in Nigeria to eat within the grasp of the poor man? I do not understand why we turn every blessing into a curse.
Now I am having second thoughts about exporting cassava until proper planning is in place - not until kingdom come! People can not get gari to eat. Rice is even cheaper if the poor man could afford either. How can we make provision for export without corresponding increase in production? Cooperative farming by giving grants and revolving loan to agriculture graduates and farmers should have preceded export. On one of his foreign trips after the 1999 election, it was Obasanjo who asked foreign Governments if "na democracy we go shop?" We all clapped for him.
Hunger changes man into unpredictable animal. The days you asked children to eat well before drinking is gone. A woman had to confess that she had to put more pepper in food so that her children could drink more water as they eat. Nigerians skip meals certain part of the day and label it: 0-1-1 or 0-1-0 or 0-0-1.
It was Umaru Dikko who said he had not seen a Nigerian picking from the dust bins as American poor pick from the trash cans. That was a prediction then, a reality now. What is the Agriculture Minister trying to tell us about gari? He needs to declare his asset and forfeit his surplus. He will then realize that the price of gari is not funny. What is wrong? We all know what is wrong, jo! How can we correct it is the question.
Starting from today (as if I am Obasanjo), anyone who wants to hold public office, even as a dog catcher (majamaja) must forfeit his/her surplus assets. It must be repeated at the end of every term or change of position or every tax year. A relative who got transferred assets or "gift" must forfeit it and be punished for receiving stolen property.
I have noticed that it is the rich who apply for Government programs (house, land etc.) in Nigeria. If lands that were taken from families for the purpose of establishing Government Reservation Areas (common good?) are not needed anymore, it should revert to the owners. Even properties of students returning from abroad which had got stuck at the Ports were also sold to the rich. Could it be because these policies are made and tailored for filthy rich instead of dwindling middle class and the poor?
So, any application to any level of Government, even for a parking permit, land, house, vehicle license must show, not only tax clearance (not cooked) as it is now done but must be willing to forfeit any surplus twice above his or her salary declared.
This is not new; Murtala Mohamed declared his assets and forfeited the surplus. That did not stop Professor Ohanbamu of the University of Benin from taking him to court.
One of our Governors, while living in the US, had to forfeit about half a million dollars in his account to the US Government because his "lawyers advised" him that it might be more expensive to fight it. Well, let us mimic and copy that. If you can not account for it, it belongs to the Government.
Ribadu claimed that there are 20 Governors laundering money into foreign accounts, apart from the one that was caught red handed. There are local government chairmen who have switched the use of cocoa bags to carrying naira. If we block them at home and block them outside Nigeria, there will be less incentive to steal. What good is money a Nigerian cannot spend?
Politicians award themselves allowances for trips inside and outside Nigeria indiscriminately as if they govern in Europe or America. Where they got their standard or guidelines from baffles me. That is the petrol that flames the anger of the people. I do not have to be from the South-south areas where environmental pollution has made fishing and farming impossible to be mad.
We must commend the Judge who granted strict bail condition to Tafa Balogun, but we must take a few steps further. All the sureties must declare their assets or forfeit the surplus. That will instill discipline among Nigerians that if you are going to stick out your neck, you must be clean.

Original_Search_Title: In Spite of Cholera, Katanga Residents Defy Order to Suspend Sale of Food
Headline: Uganda: In Spite of Cholera, Katanga Residents Defy Order to Suspend Sale of Food
Body_Text:
Nairobi —  None of the over 1,000 families in Katanga has a toilet; some draw
water from a well next to a burst sewer, writes Frank Sserwaniko
KIMWANYI ZONE IN KATANGA is a triangular low-lying area measuring about
500 square metres. It is the dwelling place for some 7,000 people, some of
them foreign students at Makerere University.
Although it is located right in the city, adjacent to three major
national establishments - the Public Service Ministry headquarters to the
south, Mulago Hospital to the east and Makerere University to the north,
Katanga has a serious public health problem and is reputed to be one of the
filthiest parts of Kampala.
Cholera hit the area two months ago, killing six people, while three
others were hospitalised. However, these are among the rare publicised
cases. Many residents conceal the presence of cholera and other sanitary
diseases for fear of prompting a long-standing eviction plan of some 105
families.
The eviction is meant to pave way for the repair of a burst sewerage
pipe. While city by-laws require that property developers leave at least
four metres between their houses and the sewer lines, this has been
ignored.
An attempt to evict the residents last year failed after the central
government intervened, accusing the opposition-led Kampala City Council
(KCC) of harassing its supporters.
Following the cholera outbreak in June, the city council and local
leaders intensified their public health campaign, urging residents to
observe basic hygiene.
However, families continue to draw water from a well adjacent to the
burst sewerage pipe. Matters are not helped when children as young as five
are sent to collect water without the supervision of adults.
This writer witnessed children ignoring the main pipe and fetching water
from the temporary reservoir where the water from the well freely mixes with
the sewage. Some of them were happy to quench their thirst with this
contaminated water as they played with it.
Said the area council chairman Hassan Wasswa, "We were supposed to close
that well, but residents have objected. They wanted us to provide an
alternative clean water pipe in the same location, which we could not do
immediately."
He said the National Water and Sewerage Corporation had extended a clean
water pipe into another location in the area, but some residents had shunned
it because of the longer distance they had to walk to reach it.
Katanga residents have also defied a KCC order to suspend the sale of
food and drinks in the area. The order was announced in mid-June by the city
director of health services Mesak Mubiru to curb further spread of
cholera.
"It is an issue of survival. Most people here get their meals from food
kiosks. They cannot afford meals in more decent restaurants," says Charles
Wasswa, a chapati maker. "While the City Council stand was to suspend the
sale of food items altogether, local leaders discussed with the people and
agreed to improve sanitation."
He said he had improved the cleanliness at his stall, adding that his
sales had not been affected by the cholera threats.
None of the over 1,000 families in Katanga has a toilet, local leaders
say. Residents use three public toilets where adults have to pay Ush100 (5
US cents) to access them. Children don't pay. Many residents find the US 5
cents prohibitive. The same amount can buy a piece of yam or cassava for
supper.
This has forced some of slum dwellers to use polythene bags to dispose
of waste, which is later thrown into the sewage channels.

Original_Search_Title: Let Resource Control Be
Headline: Nigeria: Let Resource Control Be
Body_Text:
Lagos — Among notable issues I had looked forward to be sorted out at the National Political Reform Conference were, resource control, the tenure of elected executive office holders, disbandment of states as federating units, and a return to regional government as was the case in the beginning. We are all aware that it is over ten years since this agitation for a conference that should be independent in nature without the interference of the government began.
I was stunned when President Olusegun Obasanjo, at the eleventh hour, gave his nod to the convocation of the conference but immediately caught cold on hearing that government would nominate whoever would go to the conference. It was at this juncture that those of us with unbiased mind, began to understand that at the end of the day, the whole farce was going to be an 'owambe' gathering, 'Come Chop' and for this reason, I believe, great minds like Prof. Wole Soyinka and Pa Anthony Enahoro decided to stay away, because they knew that nothing good would come from out of Nazareth.
Today, they have been vindicated. Any wonder why Dr. Paul Unongo, a former minister and a vocal critic of Obasanjo government and an Obasanjo nominee to the confab in an interview granted to Sunday Champion on February this year, concluded that the confab could make or mar Nigeria and warned that unless the Aburi issue of 1967 was re-examined, exhumed and given a befitting response no progress be made.
The hue and cry, coming from the former Eastern and Midwestern regions that their people be part of decision making on how the resources from their zones should be distributed, is a pointer to the fact that, there should be a rethink and the time is ripe. We should go back to what we had in the beginning of this journey as enshrined in the 1960 and 1963 Independence and Republican Constitutions which provided for fiscal federalism that gave rise to the principle of derivation which the Nigerian tripod feet stood.
A premonition of what the confab would look like became obvious when it was announced that those grey areas of Nigeria's life should remain 'no go areas' confirming the prophecy of our elder comrades like Pa Enahoro. In a situation where there was a predetermined consensus, was there any need for the gathering, other than loot sharing?. The South south region which constitutes majority of oil producing states in the country were of the view that it would be better, and helpful, if we went back to the maiden constitution which empowered regions to have control over whatever resources both human and natural in their territory.
In a paper delivered by Dr. Arthur Agwuncha Nwankwo of the Eastern Mandate Union EMU, to his fellow delegates at the confab, he went memory lane to bring to fore the Raisam Formula on resource control which provided for 50 per cent of the region of origin of whatever resources, and 20 per cent to the federal government as its contribution for maintenance of the integrity of the nation while the remaining 30 per cent was to be shared equally among the then three regions of which the Eastern region, which was the region of origin was a beneficiary, bringing its share to a total of 60 per cent.
Between 1967 and 1970, Nigeria and the former Eastern region, fought a civil war caused by inequality and insecurity which claimed about 2 million people of which about 95 per cent were easterners. For the war to be well prosecuted against the Easterners, the derivation formula was abrogated with nothing going to the region of origin of the resources.
For whatever reason, the federal government should be informed that the war is over, or is Nigeria still fighting? May be. With the killing of the Apo Six in Abuja and Peter Ede the Okada guy in Ikeja, Lagos and the continuous hounding of Igbos who went to watch a football match and others who are languishing in different prisons, the war may not have been over.
Comparing the social situation today, with what it was in the 60s, one would agree with me that had we continued with the old practice of resource control and true federalism, our palm produce, cocoa products, ground nut, cotton, and coal to mention a few that sustained us would have been exploited the more. The federating units and new emerging geo-political zones, would also have continued to look inwards, purposely to explore and exploit their immense endowments even in agricultural sector to boost food production that will serve the purpose of internal consumption and for export, at least now that cassava has become a foreign exchange earner and a hot cake in the Asian market.
The South south in their submision at the Confab were of the view that it would be better and helpful if we went back to the maiden constitution which grants regions and territories autonomy over their land and whatever resources therein, so that people can help themselves in developing their areas.
This writer, as someone who with time, has studied the country is aware that the country if properly harnessed should be the most endowed country in the world, particularly in natural resources.
If you will permit me, I will take you a little into what this country can boast of both natural and human resources, possibly zone by zone or state by state. South East -Gold, Salt, Limestone, lased/zinc, oil and gas, clay, iron ore, lignite, glass-sand, phosphate, gypsum, marcasite. South South, Clay, Limestone, Lead/zinc, uranium, Salt, lignite, oil and gas, gypsum, manganese, marble, glass-sand, iron ore, kaolin, gold, dolomite phosphate and bitumen. South West. Kaolin, feldspar, tatium, granite, syenite, glass sand, clay, Bitumen, sandstar, oil and gas, gemstone, gypsum, coal, Bauxite, talc, Tantalite, Tourmaline, Columbite, Marble, sillimanite, Gold, Cassiterite, Aquamarine, dolomite, phosphate. North West Barytes, kaolin, gold, limestone, phospate, gypsum silica-sand, clay, laterite, potash, flakes, granite, gold, salt, sapphire, serpentines, asbestos, Amethyst, kyanite, Graphite, sillimarite, Mica, Aquamarine, Ruby, Rockcrystal, Topaz, Fluorspar, Tourmaline, gemstone, tantalite pyrochlore, cassityerite, copper, glass-sand, lead/zinc, and marble. North East. Kaolin, Bentonite, Gypsum, Magnetite, Barites, Bauxite, Gemstone, Lead/zinc, Diatomite, Soda-ash, Amethyst Uranium, clay, limestone, oil and gas.
North Central: Gold, marble, iron ore, cassilerile, columbite, tantalite, feldspar, mica, bergy, dolmotic, supphire, tourmaline, quartet, Amathyst, Topaz, Zircon, Ilmenite, Galena, Barytes, Limestone, Mica, Cooking coal, Talc, clay, salt, chal write, lead/zinc, coal, Bauxite, Gemstone, Gypsum, oil and gas, Kaolin, Bitumen, emerald, Tin, Granite Bentonite, Pyroclore, wolram, Bismuth Fluoride and molybdenite.
With these abundance natural, resources who says we are not blessed?. The continuous parasitic feasting on oil alone is not healthy and is not a good sign of a people that want to survive. I am of the opinion that this country should be regionalized so that people can go home and start thinking on what to do and how best to develop their areas. This over-concentration and centralization in Abuja and Lagos is not the best for us. It is possible that, had Nigeria not engaged the former Biafran territory in a civil war, may be their technical might would not have been put to test. That resulted in the production of the mysterious Ojukwu Bucket (Ogbunigwe).
The issue of resource control seems to be at the centre of the latest hostility. In the current political brouhaha that has divided the country along North/South lines. Since this country is richly endowed with all sort of natural resources, which Americans and Europeans can not boast of, then where lies the animosity?. Alhaji Umaru Dikko the leader of the Northern delegates to the just unconcluded Confab, told the South south that the North brought up the South south, and that before the discovery of oil, the South South were parasitic on the agricultural produce from the North. He also went further to remind the South South that without them (The North), the Igbos would have reclaimed their houses in Port Harcourt in the abandoned property saga and regretted that South south could forget so easily. Dikko added that, if South south wanted history, they were ready to remind them. He concluded by telling them that in every relationship, there must be a senior partner. Now, with all these issues on the table, I want to ask what happened to agriculture that used to be the mainstay of our economy. Was it outlawed by any decree?. It should have been on this basis that the issue of regionalism be reconsidered, so that people can go back home and begin to resuscitate their economic mainstay. For the avoidance of doubt, those who are afraid of resource control and Nigeria's break up (even though I don't believe in it) should note that resource control encourages competition and elevates capability.
Today Nigeria is 45 years; the old Eastern Nigeria has held the executive head of the country for 6 months, the Western Nigeria, nine years so far, while North had been there for 35 years. A state like Nigeria, where the winner takes all, where there is a senior partner, a state where whether you love us or hate us we cannot be ignored no matter the number of years at the helm of affairs, I think the northern populace should have been the after-sought bride in education at this age, as the world has become a global village through technology.
Umaru Dikkos view should be treated with 'bugaboo' Information abound that the north constitute the majority in education disadvantaged states and issues of this nature should have bothered people like Dr. Umaru Dikko even though he had claimed that the north is well educated just like their southern counterparts. He added that the only difference is that while the southerners went to western school, the north went to Islamic school and can even write better in Arabic. He asked if Germans, French or Chinese speak English upon all their exploits particularly in technology, which I have been looking forward to seeing from our northern brothers. People like Major Abubakar Umar (rtd.) should lecture Umaru Dikko and bring to his knowledge that what the northern youths need is liberation from the shackles of oligarchic grip.
Let this serve as a letter to my fellow northern youths because the earlier they ask their leaders questions about their plight, the better because they stand to loose. "If I am to paraphrase the words of Abubakar Umar, he said that 'the north also has been lacking in development compared with the West, we have not developed any resources in the north. The feudal masters themselves have been masters of the poor. They have enslaved them, and used them for their own material benefits. Up till today, I have not seen any part of the north that has benefited from the feudal class. On education and infrastructural development, he said the north today has the lowest standard of education and infrastructure development. A sound mind, well-educated person is better than an ignorant one that does not understand his environment.
As it is today, Nigeria is a federation where the federal government holds the ace but at this time, it is high time the north take a second look at the word of former American president J. F. Kennedy who said that people should think of what they can do for their country and not what their country can do for them. Please let us stop being lazy.
Today Nigeria cannot produce a toy gun because of fear of the unknown. As I conclude, let us not forget the prediction of experts that oil is an exhaustible natural resource, if the fifteen years period suggested by them is anything to go by.
That is, it is expected that the oil reserve will dry up by 2020. Unfortunately, as a people with shot memory of history, in June this year, America informed us that there was the likelihood of Nigeria disintegrating in 15 years time thereby tallying with the same 2020. Then, what will be our lot and the generations to come?. With all these, I think it is high time we started mending our roofs because nobody knows when the rainy reason will set in.

Original_Search_Title: The Debt Talk: Debt Relief As Tragedy
Headline: Nigeria: The Debt Talk: Debt Relief As Tragedy
Body_Text:
Ofeimun blames our debt burden on Obasanjo. "He it was who took the first jumbo loan and its follow-up between 1977 and 1979 during his First Coming as a military dictator."
It would seem to be a matter of fate that Nigeria's debt problems are catching fire during President Olusegun Obasanjo's current tenure because he was the one who created the problems in the first case. Put bluntly, he bears a direct personal responsibility for Nigeria's accumulated debts. He it was who took the first jumbo loan and its follow-up between 1977 and 1979 during his First Coming as a military dictator. Before then Nigeria's debts hovered between $488.3m in 1970 to $375.6m in 1976. Debt serving ratio was on the down-ward incline from 0.7 in 1975, 0.5 in 1976 and 0.4 in 1977. Though different figures are being touted, there is a clear agreement that Obasanjo increased Nigeria's debt burden in the first instance by about 146%. He did it to tide over a down turn in oil sales which was worsened by market-rejection of his inordinate raising of the price of Nigeria's sweet crude.
It yielded a cash-flow crisis that could have been taken care of in a less easy-go and more future-sensitive manner. Unfortunately, in the era of doing everything by 'immediate effect', the drop in industrial output from 19 percent in 1976 to 3.9 percent in 1977, to give one sectoral effect of the down-turn in oil sales, was met rather cavalierly by looking for a quick fix. Not to forget: this was in the era in which a glut of petro-dollars was threatening to ruin Western banking institutions, and the famed loan sharks were looking for how to dump the excess on hapless Third World countries. Many Third World leaders were blackmailed, bribed, threatened and sometimes toppled just to make sure that they took the bait. Although no Nigerian has written his memoirs to tell compatriots how the logic played out on our shores, the well-known fact is that by the time Obasanjo left office in 1979, Nigeria, as he told the National Assembly recently, was owing US$3.2 billion, more than 300 hundred percent above the norm.
What may well be added is that these loans were different from the usual kinds that Nigeria was used to. The nature of the loans was hinted at by S.B. Falegan in The Guardian of Sunday July 31, 2005, where he noted that he "was privileged to be involved as the Director of Research in the Central Bank of Nigeria in the first and second jumbo loans negotiations under General Obasanjo when he was Military Head of State in the mid 70s". To quote Falegan in extenso: "One thing that was clear to me at the negotiations is that while Nigeria was a necessitous borrower, the lender was an avaricious blood-sucker, always asking for payment on due date, failing which a pound of flesh is taken. An example will suffice. Were Nigeria to default any repayment on the loan (amortization, interest, maturity, etc) she was to forfeit all its assets, foreign or domestic, to the lender. I could not control my anger at such proposal which was meant to mortgage Nigeria totally and one lesson I learnt during the negotiations is never fear to negotiate and never negotiate out of fear."
So much is hidden in these words that we may never know. Nor is it known whether the principals behind the Nigerian negotiators felt the same anger as Falegan. What has become obvious is that the loans that Nigeria took fouled up the ground and, by implication, actually required that Nigeria, meaning the successor regime of Alhaji Shehu Shagari, would have to take more loans in order to stay afloat. Thus, although there was another oil boom for Nigeria thereafter which should have empowered the Shagari government to move away from the death trap spelt by the loans, the NPN government went haywire.
The government raised planned expenditure from 8.258 billion Naira to 23.695 billion Naira in 1980 and then negotiated and took 8 billion dollars more of loans. It is not known how much arm-twisting or duress tipped the government over. What is clear is that, under loud prodding by those who claimed that Nigeria was under-borrowed and that a country should borrow when it is buoyant, the government took the new gush in oil wealth as a warrant for borrow-borrow. Incidentally, the return of oil boom had also empowered Shagari to boast, in sheer diplomatic misjudgment during a state visit to the United States, that he would use the oil weapon to fight for Africa's freedom. At a time the Western industrialized world was groaning under increases in oil prices, Shagari was virtually challenging the loan sharks to a duel.
With such a prospect, the government did not need to have taken any more loans to put a debt-noose around its own neck, just as Obasanjo did not need to have taken the loans that he took in the first case. But then in the usual Nigerian penchant for everyone wanting to be part of the action because accountability even for debts incurred was non-existent in the country, many state governments seized the opportunity to weight the loan bandwagon with their own demands. Political alliances and coalitions were shored up by the carrot of loans guaranteed by the Federal Government which today owes 75% of the debts as against 25% owed by the states - one of which (thanks to Governor Orji Kalu of Abia State) has only just emerged from the law courts in a failed attempt to resist payment of debts owed by the old Imo State.
By the way, let me, quickly, admit that I am only able to say that neither Obasanjo nor Shagari needed to have taken more loans because I am coming from an alternative format of national economic management which is not as outdated as an ideology or practice as the one that powers Federal strategies at the moment. (I write this with apologies to the Minister of Finance, Mrs Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, who has damned some critics as labouring under outdated ideologies). I work according to the assumption that even individuals who spend their personal incomes like drunks ought to spend public funds with circumspection.
I uphold a format of economic management therefore that finds the current Federal debt management strategy to be one mired in exactly the same murk, a bowing to the trumpets of the loan sharks, as in the regime of both Obasanjo as military dictator and the civilian successor he handed over power to. Without solid base-building through serious man-power development, and without a deliberate crafting of a technology-acquisition programme, without genuine fore-planning to match the grip of the liberal ethic of leaving plans to externalities, the country was confronted with the imminent logic of going a-sorrowing because we went Business School, from Corporate boardrooms to the corridors of Nigeria's unaccountable power, careers have been built on being in tune with the IMF dictates and being on the right side of world bank philosophies which prescribe ideas that were developed for thriving industrial economies but are now being deployed to repress economies that are trying to rise above mere subsistence. The pity is that not every Professor of Economics can see the difference between the two. Which is why it is proverbial to say that economics is too important to be left to economists.
For that matter, many of them are just political sheep in lion's clothing who have succumbed to the power-play built into received theories that they spool with unrestrained arrogance. This is another way of saying that in the age of indigenous pro-consuls, we are confronted by the class of phenomena so well known to students of political science as the third dimension of power. It covers situations in which the most astute and proficient exercise of power belongs where people believe that they are acting in their own interest while, in actual fact, they are merely following the dictates of their overlords. For proof, check out all the home-grown programmes from Babangida's SAP to Obasanjo's NEEDS. The approach to debt management buried in them is so much hostage to this third dimension that the unsuspecting may be tempted to believe that the decisions are being taken by the Nigerian state. But who is the Nigerian state in a global conundrum where pro-consuls and sundry consultants from "outer Mongolia" are the only ones allowed to think the way forward?
Let me amend what I have just stated. Anyone who wishes to know the thinking that has been active in debt management in the Fourth Republic cannot do worse than read the book The Debt Trap in Nigeria: Towards A Sustainable Debt Strategy, co-edited by Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, Charles Soludo and Mansur Muhtar. All three editors are now respectively Minister of Finance, Governor of Central Bank, and boss of the Debt Management Office. The book, published by Africa World Press in 2003 came out of an International Conference on Sustainable Debt Strategy held in Abuja in 2001 at a time the prime protagonists were consultants rather than insiders in government. Unlike the other book on the subject, Nigerian External Debt Crisis: Its management, edited by
Adebayo Olukoshi and published by Malthouse Press in 1990, this one is more power-driven than knowledge-motivated.
Happily, the editors of the new book have not said anything inside government that is radically different from what the book offers on the critical question of Nigeria's debt peonage. Of the twelve contributors, five including Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala and Mansur Muhtar, Paul Collier, Graeme Wheeler, Callisto Madavo are certified World Bankers. The first and core chapter by Charles Soludo, a former consultant for World Bank institutions, may be treated as a summative advance of their positions. Soludo's analysis pulls a difference in emphasis however by stressing how the impact of unequal power in the international system, and especially resource transfers from the poor to the rich, act as further weighting of the scales against those who borrow. Soludo's brilliant analysis of the incredible dereliction of hopes that accompanies the borrowing syndrome is followed by much temporizing about the need to avoid the debt trap. His thrust is well complemented by Okonjo-Iweala's concern for the inadequacy of institutional frameworks for debt management.
She provides an exciting format for future management within an inconclusive attempt to apportion responsibility for the debts (who borrowed what?) so far incurred as well as itemize and analyze the patterns of borrowing. Her position in this sense spells out concretely the format for meeting the expectations of Graehme Wheeler, Vice President and Treasurer of The World Bank, who gives tips on Sound practices in Government Debt Management. Such practices are further unpacked by Mike Obadan, Director General of the National Centre for Economic Management and Administration in Ibadan who provides a neat itemization of the many debt-management strategies that could be adopted -- including debt cancellation, sound macro-economic and sound external debt management policy, authentication of external debt position, tying new loans to viable projects, making regular debt portfolio review and avoiding traditional re-scheduling agreements in view of their inadequacies, privatization and moratorium on external borrowing, debt conversion and debt buy-back. These are stock agreements across the many contributions all of which appear however to be ritually stuck on the pros and cons of debt-relief as debt-forgiveness.
Except for Jeffery Sachs of Havard University who does special pleading for debt cancellation, in my view not very convincingly, almost all the contributions are taken by the necessity to reach for debt forgiveness. On their own, Mathew Martin, Director o1.Debt Relief International and Ann Pettifor Coordinator of Jubilee 2000 Plus, concentrate on arguing a case for debt relief within a human rights oriented regime to enable Nigeria salvage her citizens while meeting international debt obligations. A harder position is taken by Callisto Madavo, Vice President of the Africa Region of the World Bank who makes it quite clear, as the others do in different ways, except for Jeffry Sachs, that given Nigeria's resources the debt burden is not too heavy, hence full debt cancellation is not on for Nigeria as it would cripple or even close the multilateral financial system. Nigeria's debt pack, being too much owed (83.16%) to bilateral Paris Club is presented as capable of disruptive possibilities if it is allowed to become a model beyond the poorest of the poor.
An intriguing part of this near-consensual position is that there is also a proper acknowledgment by all the contributors that so much of Nigeria's loans made a return journey back to Western banking institutions as loot and sheer capital flight and are resting secure in private accounts offshore. Beginning with Charles Soludo who argues that the return of 25% of the 100 billion dollars private Nigerian assets abroad will obviate the need to seek further external funding, the case is well touted across the board. It is quite well made in the painstaking analysis done by Professor S. Ibi Ajayi of the University of Ibadan on External Debt, Capital Flight and Growth in Nigeria. From his analysis, some hope can be garnered for making a useable distinction between mere capital flight and looted funds. Such that: it ought to be worth the trying for any government genuinely interested in crafting a durable debt management strategy, to demand the funds. Ibi Ajayi, using a schema and model that he has developed from the best traditions in the field, applies it to information provided by international monetary institutions like the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund.
His tabulations show that the greatest volume of capital flight took place in 1980-82, 1987, 1988, 1990, 1992, 1995, and 1996. The years of the least flight are 1984 and 1993-1994. In spite of this evident possibility of tracing what happened in the flight of capital in terms of determining which was loot or normal income stashed abroad and kept from the harm's way of a crashing economy, the surprise is that so little is made by all the contributors to the need for returning the loot as a pre-condition for a proper Nigerian response to the creditors. So much ink is wasted on the question of debt-forgiveness and to a lesser extent debt cancellation in an ad nauseum progression. Not that they do not all make the right noises about the bloated private accounts abroad that have exacerbated (and somehow can explain?) Nigeria's debt crisis. None pays much mind to it as a substantive strategy on its own terms in spite of the facts available.
The seemingly more categoric position on returning looted funds is taken by Paul Collier Director of the World Bank, who worries that Nigeria has little to show for the 280 billion dollars earned as income over the same period of the critical borrowing. However, he considers that much more important for Nigeria is to worry about how to keep the Nigerian currency devalued to size, to avoid capital flight which he believes is the bane of over-valued currencies. He asserts that "Over the past 30 years an overvalued exchange rate has been the most consistent disaster in Nigerian economic policy. It has destroyed export industries and seduced wealth abroad".
Of course, it is not easy for anyone who knows the specificities of the Nigerian economy to agree that this is really the problem. But the themeqis not peculiar to Paul Collier. It is actually the hobbyhorse of all the contributions which tend to proffer solutions that in the end project the usual IMF and World Bank conditionalities as the cure for the debt overhang. In line with it, Collier makes quite a meal of the case of Uganda which, presumably by swallowing the IMF medicine made a leap in growth that Nigeria could emulate. Uganda, on whose performance Soludo offers cautionary notes, is of course, being presented in the same manner that Ivory Coast and Kenya were once positioned as star successes in capitalist economic development until they proved to be in the same trap as poor socialist Tanzania and Mozambique. It was all part of the politics of World Bank theory-making and freefor-the-poor-medicine which has mined many third world economies and which in the case of Uganda had to be disobeyed by Museveni to get any enduring results.
As against the World Bankers, common sense suggests that anyone genuinely interested in dealing with the debt trap ought not to see Nigeria in the context of other countries without taking cognizance of specificities that make all the difference. Given the rumours and the proof already provided that much of the monies had found their way back to the West, a debt management strategy would be expected to take off by, first of all, pursuing what can still be salvaged from the loot. When Obasanjo came to power in 1999, he appeared to be interested in doing this.
The pursuit of the Abacha loot to European finance houses looked like such a strategy. More and more by the day, it has come to assume the colour of mere vendetta. Until Western creditors began to threaten to discountenance Nigeria's bid for debt relief unless there was proof of real anti-corruption campaign, President Obasanjo did not appear to wake up to the need to confront the issue of the debts in private accounts abroad. When the President, quite unpresidentially, began to talk about having been given the names of those who took monies abroad, it was more than embarrassing because the demand for that list, although risky for a government generally believed to be steeped in loot-sharing, was supposed to be the beginning of wisdom. How come that what was open secret in international banking circles before 1999 was only becoming news for Nigeria's President in 2005? It is as if like the famed Okigbo report, the President was waiting for Nigerians on the streets to supply the names of those who owned the loot abroad.
The more perturbing part and what Nigeria's policy makers in Abuja have not managed to grasp is that Western creditors are not being charitable when they give lists of what Nigerians own abroad. They are genuinely having a good laugh at Nigeria's expense. The question is: how can people who are keeping loot money in their vaults insist that unless you are seen to be fighting corruption they will not give you debt-forgiveness? Why was it not the Nigerian President giving the Western creditors the unflappable condition that unless they disgorged the looted funds in their vaults Nigeria would not pay one kobo more of the debts supposedly owed to the Paris Club?
Or, let us even assume that Nigerian monies abroad are not stolen money, but money that was legitimately earned but haemorrhaged abroad by people who did not trust that their money would be safe in Nigeria. Shouldn't that have immediately suggested to the managers of the Nigerian economy that they need to come up with policies that would make Nigerians keep their money at home rather than send it abroad? Shouldn't that have suggested a case of actually identifying the Nigerians who have their monies abroad, to plead with them to bring the money home? Sofar, instead of wooing Nigerians, our debt managers prefer to kneel before creditors who have no patriotic stake in whether the Nigerian economy crashes or climbs. They have opted for prostration before the gods of the international debt racket, taking it as grand strategy to kneel with begging bowl, demanding to be treated same way as drought-stricken and famine-ravaged countries for debt-forgiveness? To hear Nigeria's President saying that "even if we managed to pay the interest and charges alone, there was no way in which we could ever hope to pay the principal" proves to be such a howler. The statement is actually only correct because the President has not done the right thing and is doggedly pursuing the wrong thing.
To be sure, because Obasanjo did not adopt the bring-back-our-money strategy, he has not been able to see that his pursuit of debt-forgiveness is not only an expensive binge, but that his begging bowl approach is disruptive of genuine policy making and a disquieting defacement of rational problem solution. The wasted years of pursuit, the sleepless nights and 160 days in the air, the mammoth man-hours that the President of the Republic has to devote to the whole exercise and the distraction that these bring to the process of genuine economic planning and governance cannot be over-emphasized. They must be accounted in terms of opportunities that the President has missed to face Nigeria's problems squarely. It is not just in what he and his team say the begging bowl strategy has achieved but what it has destroyed and continues to threaten.
The truth is that Nigeria has been presented to the rest of the world as a very unsenous country, one that will carry an elephant on her head and still go scratching for rodents. To escape this image and to wipe it out from the memory of international players, will take a doing. But not even the so called friends of Nigeria are willing to tell this to President Obasanjo. Because Nigeria, under Obasanjo, has preferred the will o'the wisp of debt forgiveness, the smart alecs of the international market-place (amongst whom many Nigerians are to be counted) have been lining up to collect their fees for one jousting for scheduling, rescheduling, defaults, and buy backs and what have you. Whatever Mrs Ngozi Okonjo Iweala may be saying about this, she cannot annul the reality that fees are paid and added to the account for every deal that Nigeria makes or only half makes on the trail of debt forgiveness and debt management. Thanks to Okonjo-Iweala's and Mansure Muhtar's proficiency in institution- building, Nigeria may now be able to keep a proper account, and our negotiators may be more transparent in their pursuits. But this merely spells a greater proficiency in knowing how bad or well things are without offering an exit from the mayhem.
What is beguiling about the debt forgiveness binge is that in order to have it, Nigeria's debt managers had to unnecessarily put the noose of IMF conditionalities as holy writs and albatrosses around their own necks. There was an exit from the debt trap which did not require that route. It called for a format of economic management that is more proactively towards production rather than waiting for the Godot of a trickle down from a liberalization that abhors genuine planning. Under the banner of privatization, which assumes that a government that has no business in business can be relied upon to know how to privatize, they have pursued a loss-making anti-productivity charade which uses the incongruous case of the privatization of the communication sector as a ground for the privatization of all other enterprises.
Let's even forget that Nigeria has lost about 200 billion Naira in pursuit of privatization. Because the liberalizers turn out to be more interested in privatization than in supporting gen the private enterprise (which are not the same thing) they have been unable to press policies that can make Nigerians who have money to keep it on-shore. They have been unable to design policies that can credibly woo Nigerians with money abroad to bring the money back home. It makes it laughter-inducing to hear a lot of talk about attracting foreign investors to an economy which Nigerians with money will not bring their money into unless it is for the take-over of monopolies and monopsonies. Or to talk about attracting foreign investments to an economy which Nigerians are avoiding in order to establish factories abroad for the purpose of producing for the Nigerian market.
Truth to tell, you wouldn't need to attract foreign investment if Nigerians feel that their money was safe within the country. There would be enough indigenous money to power investments if only Nigerians would feel that the managers of the economy know what they are doing. No serious Nigerian with real money believes that the people in government know what they are doing. They go along with government shenanigans for personal competitive advantage over others, in order not to lose out in the quicksand of Nigerian power-play. As for the Nigerians without real money, they are so convinced that the economic managers are swindlers that they would rather cut corners than wait for the proverbial trickle down that the World bankers and IMF undertakers in our midst expect as follow-ups to the misery of their bitter medicines.
So how is confidence to be built all around? The whole issue centres on the simple matter of managing the Naira like a proper currency. Who is going to bring hard earned money to an economy where the currency, arrantly-devalued, is doing mambo and shakey-shakey? Since the managers believe less in Nigerians than in creditors who are laughing at us behind their handkerchiefs, they have been displaying such unguarded disrespect for the Naira. Serious foreign investors take a cue from them. Unless such an investor is assured of a quick kill based more on government deals than serious market orientation, they demand guarantees that no Nigerian would have needed to ask of a Nigerian government that is genuinely concerned about productivity. The foreign investor who can see how the ideology of reforms is deforming everything simply needs to look at the disrespect for the national currency and he and she will bid some time. Take the case of the President's enterprising choice of a Minister of Finance and sundry lower level interveners in Nigeria's economic decision-making who are on dollar salaries paid by creditor countries.
There have been many mealie-mouth attempts to make light of this business of appointing someone who will not agree to earn the Naira but who must be saddled with the task of managing the Naira. Unless it is explained by the policy of appointing indigenes to do the job of proconsuls, we are being asked to suspend disbelief as to why it should happen at all. Arguably, the Naira is obviously regressing to the level of toilet-paper and therefore cannot meet the international commitments of high-flying Nigerians who must be kept in accustomed comfort. The pity is that rather than raise the Naira to the level of the currency that the international jet-set with globalized commitments would be proud to earn, Mrs Okonjo-Iweala has spent all her time as finance Minister brewing up one scheme after another for devaluing the national currency.
In order to make it seem as if there is something different from Ibrahim Babangida's fiasco of structural adjustment programmes, the economic team with which the Finance Minister must help President Obasanjo, has crafted a homegrown but servile programme of mendicancy which an otherwise erudite Charles Soludo on his way to the Central Bank has marketed as National Economic Empowment and Development Strategy (NEEDS). After what I have read of Soludo's writings I would have said he was now in a position to see how, without offending the Bretton Woods institutions, he could out-bid the inequality in the international system to which he adverted so much attention in The Debt Trap In Nigeria. But the game is not really in his hands.
All the fine phrasing in the policy documents adds up to a few ball points: devaluation of the Naira, liberalization, privatization and inordinate search for debt forgiveness. Or, to put it differently, because of the mis-begotten search for debt forgiveness and the need to humour creditors rather than embark on genuine developmental strategy, they opted for policies that will make sure the NAIRA will never become respectable. So that the creditors will be happy that Nigeria is where they want her to be: incapable of ever standing up to their wiles. And, in order to make sure that there are no serious organized groups to counter the drift, the whole programme is cushioned by a full steam constitutional effort to decapitate the labour movement, (after smashing the liver of the political party system) and reducing the Universities, in the name of reforms, to mere handmaidens of a poorly-funded parastatal. (And, creating a situation that authorizes even people still in government to promote their own private Universities as rafts for the normalization of private as public purpose).
On the surface, apostles of NEEDS market it as a strategy that does not believe in borrowing more money. But what does that mean in a country where the death of the national currency has already spelt out the death of industries, educational institutions, transportation, health facilities. When champions of debt forgiveness talk about passing a law to forbid unconscionable borrowing in future and at the same time talk about not completely ruling out borrowing, it is partly because they know that the way the NEEDS works, unless the upturn in oil revenue remains on the side of excess, will send us back to the creditors in no time. What does it mean, in any case, to say that you wont borrow more money in an economy where the lynching of the national currency has ensured that what Nigerians used to earn in a week is now earned in six weeks. It means that we all work six times more to earn a day's wage in relation to externalities. Since there are only twenty four hours in a day and no new inventions, apart from cassava, has been found by the Presidency for raising productivity, we all know that the struggle for oil money is the beginning of survival. This is why all the talk about non-oil exports is actually a way of reminding us that those who killed the marketing boards are about to lure Nigerians into false confidence about a future of prosperity based on agricultural produce. They are lying to themselves and to us.
If the lying works, it would mean that few Nigerians would be able to afford Nigerian-produced foods. It will worsen the current situation in which the sweat that could have bought a brand new car in the past now buys a miserable tokunbo and money for medical equipment that could have gone round five hospitals can only now produce inadequately for one hospital. The situation, in relation to the knowledge industry, is such that no serious Professors abroad want to teach in Nigeria and Nigerian Professors look over-privileged when they are allowed nondescript placements abroad. Even in the supposedly non-militarized Fourth Republic, as Professor Niyi Osundare told us recently in his valedictory, the universe in the Nigerian University has been under erasure. It all boils down to what has happened to the Naira: which is why Paul Collier would expect to earn so much more money than a Nigerian Professor who has higher credentials than he has and must acquire so much of his professional ware from the same international market.
For that matter, one paradox, un-noticed by the festivating crowds around NEEDS is that the only reason the strategy appears to look good these days is that there is a forced recapitalization of the banks, and recently there has been a state-pampered attempt to establish a mega-company that is supposed to stand up to the multi-nationals around us. Bright ideas in wrong times. The good reasoning, even if not the open intention, is that with the Naira permanently in trouble, if you do not recapitalize you would soon be in more trouble than the market can take. By the same token, while the NAIRA remains the beleaguered currency that it has been since Obasanjo discovered the need for jumbo loans, the brouhaha about establishing a mega-company merely spells mega troubles. With the Naira in trouble, recapitalization is really like running hard in order to stand still. To expect a mega-company without monopoly status in its chosen trenches to compete with companies operating from hard currency bases, will turn out to be more a matter of prestige, regime-maintenance strategies and therefore politics rather than a matter of economics and productivity. For this reason, from the standpoint of the freedom that Nigeria expects to be able to exercise as a result of being freed from depth servicing after debt forgiveness, it is important to stress it, and do so clearly, that no strategy that fails to take the value of the Naira into consideration will pass muster as an exit from Paris Club debts.
Actually, this is the point to turn to the particular mode of debt forgiveness that President Olusegun Obasanjo has pursued and netted as he has told the National Assembly. In the President's words," ... what we have now been given assurance to expect by the Paris club is that Nigeria will clear its arrears of $6 billion of the $30 billion owed The package in final terms that we are to expect would yield debt relief of about 60 percent on our current Paris Club debt. We shall pay off the 40 percent balance through a buy back operation. The total write off is about $18 billion which compares very favourably with the recent $40 billion write off of debts for the 18 highly indebted and poor countries of the world by the developed nations". Interestingly, although the President says that the debt forgiveness is REAL, he has not said that it is in the bag. He uses terms like "what we have now been given assurance to expect" and the "final terms that we are to expect". These are not about the REAL, but expectations. So, no quibbling about it: the President has spoken in the very language in which the NEEDS document promises prosperity and poverty alleviation. The expectations can be proved to be quite wishful. It is REAL only if the Policy Support Instrument (PSI) is actually accepted.
President Obasanjo has said that the PSI which he would present to the creditors is the very NEEDS document that is the basis of the current reforms already going on. This is saying that an agreement to put the Naira down for the hacking is part of the means by which Nigeria is seeking debt forgiveness. It does not say so in so many words. But the recent announcement of a qqming increase in the price of fuel is enough as an eyeopener. I do not hesitate to say, in this connection, that the NEEDS document looks a very good instrument for enthroning political impatience with critics and selling a new and testy political class schooled in the practice of describing the creation of poverty as reforms. But faced with impetuous price increases, such as we are told to expect, few Nigerians can remain absolute fools. It becomes obvious to all concerned that an underdeveloped economy based on NEEDS will always put the poor and the rich under undue pressure. If anything was needed to weaken a poor country and make her dependent on creditors, nothing could be better designed. While the President and his economic team are talking about the freedom they will now have to carry out programmes in accordance with Nigeria's national aspirations, they forget to say how this will happen with devaluation of the Naira.
The fact that the President is talking about using a package that includes devaluation to convince the creditors about Nigeria~ s deservingness of debt forgiveness ought immediately to put every body's back up. What is in the document for the creditors that can also be said to be for Nigerians? This is one question that must be answered in plain terms, with facts not sentiments, as the Minister of Finance, likes to put it. Truth is, what is for the creditors in the NEEDS document is against any productivity in Nigeria that can grant respite from external dicta. And that is the gospel truth. And that is what makes it so attractive as a bait for debt forgiveness.
The creditors know this and that there is nothing in the document that can truly help Nigeria to pay what Nigeria owes.
