The implication here, it goes without saying, is that rather than cementing conjugal relationships in times of crises, the brief "conjugal visits" actually serve to sever relationships by, quite predictably, forcing the non-inmate spouse, or partner, to seek more emotionally fulfilling intimacy elsewhere.
HUMANITY
The next most logical question becomes: But why should anybody bother whether an inmate's relationship with a partner on the outside is strained or cemented by such brief "conjugal visits"? And the logical answer appears to be that in many a civilized society, the mere fact of one's incarceration, for whatever number of reasons, fundamentally does not detract from one's immutable humanity as well as the need to treat the societally sanctioned as such, a bona fide human being with the right to the visceral expression of one's humanity.
Another dimension of the story and one that is clearly muted, but may yet be its most significant dimension, is the fact that both the student who conducted the study, Ms. Ina van Havere, and the Belgian Justice Minister, Laurette Onkelinx, are women. And in a heavily patriarchal global community, it cannot be gainsaid that women often bring an added and, perhaps, a more humane dimension to the manner and tenor in which proscribed elements of society are perceived. And this is not to be all together unexpected; for having hitherto been unjustifiably proscribed from many avenues of societal endeavors, women, generally and relatively speaking, tend to be more sensitive to questions of injustice than men. Which is why on the issue of romance, Ms. Ina van Havere cast matters in such inimitably deft and delicate terms: "The time constraints [imposed by the niggardly brief conjugal visits] mean [that] couples are forced into bed almost immediately [upon a spouse's visit to a prison inmate, thus] leaving [the couple] no time to talk [and pet]."
And, of course, who said Ghana and Ghanaians could not take a cue from this more humane and civilized treatment of our prison inmates? Even the most criminal elements among us?
*Kwame Okoampa-Ahoofe, Jr., Ph.D., teaches English and Journalism at Nassau Community College of the State University of New York, Garden City. He is the author of ten volumes of poetry and prose, including Sounds of Sirens: Essays in African Politics and Culture.

Original_Search_Title: Oil Workers Strike, Threaten Refinery Fires to Halt Selloffs
Headline: Nigeria: Oil Workers Threaten Fire, to Shut Refineries
Body_Text:
Lagos — The leadership of the National Union of Petroleum and Natural Gas Workers (NUPENG) and its Petroleum and Natural Gas Senior Staff Association of Nigeria (PENGASSAN) counterpart, have directed the nation's oil workers to shut down the four refineries and all petro-chemical plants 14 days from now, should government refuse to halt alleged desperate moves to sell off Eleme Petro-Chemical Company Limited and Port Harcourt Refinery next month.
The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) has, in the meantime, concluded plans to commence the use of ethanol fuel from the beginning of next year, in an effort to slash imports of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS), while protecting the environment from the hazards of hydrocarbon emission.
Workers of the Eleme Petro-Chemical Company yesterday began an indefinite hunger strike to press home their demands.
President of PENGASSAN, Comrade Uche Okoro, at a joint press conference by NUPENG and PENGASSAN in Lagos yesterday, said the leadership of the two unions under the auspices of NUPENGASSAN, a fusion of NUPENG and PENGASSAN, would meet today with Dr Edmund Daukoru, Minister of State, Petroleum Resources and Dr Funso Kupolokun, Group Managing Director of the NNPC to intimate them of their decision.
He alleged that Eleme Petro-Chemical Company which worth over $2.4 billion was slated to be sold at a meagre $200 million, an amount he said was less than what was required for the Turn Around Maintenance (TAM) that would be due next year.
The oil workers also advised government not to contemplate another hike in price of petroleum products, warning that Nigerians could revolt because the economic reality was very provocative.
"Already, Indians who have no knowledge of petro-chemical plants have been allowed into the premises and have been asking funny questions. In fact, there have been two visitations by people who are not credible and have no knowledge of petro-chemical plants.
"We have discovered that there is desperation to sell the plants by September. We do not know where the desperation is coming from and that is why they are compromising due process. If what is happening is allowed to play itself out, what will happen will be worse than what is happening between NNPC and African Petroleum (AP). The plant is being planned to be sold at a mere $200 million.
"This is even less than what is required to do the TAM due in 2006. An independent assessor we commissioned to assess Eleme Petro-Chemical plant has told us that it is worth over $2.4 billion.
"So, the national secretariats of the two unions have since Saturday at the NEC meeting held in Abia State, directed all our members to shut down all the plants across the country after 14 days, starting from Saturday if government does not halt this desperate move to sell the refineries without due process," he said.
At an earlier press briefing by NUPENGASSAN, addressed by the leadership of both unions, Comrade Okoro said the oil workers had lost confidence in the Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE) because it continued to undermine due process.
President of NUPENG, Comrade Peter Akpatason, said BPE had jettisoned all the agreements reached by stakeholders especially the partnering option in the privatisation of the refineries where government will still retain 49 per cent equity shares in the refineries and petro-chemical plants.
He said: "As it stands today, the battle line appears to have been drawn between the workers and BPE. All our members are very restive at the moment because of what is happening. In this regard, the Joint NEC-in-Session hereby directs the NNPC in-house unions to take appropriate steps to compel BPE to comply with the agreement."
NNPC employs ethanol to crash prices
The NNPC has also served notice that it expects to make savings in excess of $245 million (about N32.8 billion) by next year when the programme takes off effectively.
Dr. Kupolokun, Group Managing Director of the corporation, announced this at the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) by the NNPC, COIMEX and Petrobras of Brazil in Abuja yesterday.
The NNPC boss said petroleum imports reception facility at Mosimi depot as well as the refineries would be revamped before December 31, this year to enable them take delivery and mix ethanol imports with PMS.
On the amount of savings to be made on this approach, he said the real gain was on the technical side, adding: "We won't go into it if we are not going to make money."
The corporation, he said, would be saving Nigeria scarce foreign exchange and that during the seeding programme, $105 million would be saved, while another $140 million would be saved from PMS imports next year when the programme takes off.
He said part of the implementation strategy was to carry out a feasibility study to enable the establishment of the ethanol industry in Nigeria along with seeding the Nigerian market with fuel ethanol in the first phase."This phase will be followed by development and commercialisation of ethanol fuel in Nigeria which will involve cultivation of large scale plantations of sugarcane and cassava and construction and operation of distilleries for the ethanol production of fuel ethanol.
He explained that the objective of the Nigerian ethanol programme was to firmly establish a thriving industry utilising agricultural products like sugarcane, cassava, etc, as a means of triggering significant employment generation, while improving the quality of automotive gasoline in Nigeria.
"Ethanol is emerging as an important motor fuel in view of the Kyoto Protocol Agreement. As a high-octane, water free oxygenate, fuel ethanol is one of the tools available for fighting air pollution from vehicles.
"It is also a green house gas emission friendly fuel which reduces global warming, and ensures complete elimination of the use of octane enhancing tetra ethyl lead which is a toxic pollutant."
As the end of the ceremony, Dr. Kupolokun told Vanguard that the corporation could no longer bear the heavy weight of petroleum products subsidy, and that only cost reflective pricing could spare the downstream sector imminent supply disruption and chaos.
Also speaking at the ceremony, Mr. Samir Anwad, Managing Director of Petrobras said it took Brasil 10 years to develop ethanol fuel. "Today in Brasil, cars run on ethanol fuel, gasoline, or a mixture of both. We are very proud to share with Nigeria all the good values of ethanol and I expect to be back here in a year to discuss the gains Nigeria has made in the use of ethanol fuel," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Oil Workers Oppose Planned Fuel Price Hike
Headline: Nigeria: Oil Workers Oppose Planned Fuel Price Hike
Body_Text:
Abuja/Lagos — Oil workers yesterday expressed their opposition to plans by government to increase fuel price and said in doing so, the Federal Government would be abdicating its social and economic responsibility to the citizenry.
The position of the oil workers was made known by the Joint National Executive Council (NEC) of the National Union of Petroleum and Natural Gas Workers (NUPENG) and the Petroleum and Natural Gas Senior Staff Association of Nigeria (PENGASSAN), and it coincided with a disclosure by the Federal Government that the domestic pump prices of fuel was being subsidized at a daily rate of N300 million.
In a statement released to newsmen yesterday, PENGASSAN and NUPENG condemned the use of import-parity price to determine domestic fuel prices, while the local refineries failed to perform at optimal levels.
"The Joint NEC-in-Session frowns against the import parity and persistent increase in the price of petroleum products in the country...The Joint NEC-in-Session rejects any proposed fuel price hike and that refineries must be made to continually function optimally," the unions stated.
The oilworkers' unions further demanded for the release and immediate implementation of the report of the Senator Ibrahim Mantu-led Palliative Committee.
With their position on the issue PENGASSAN and NUPENG joined the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), which last week equally voiced its opposition to fuel price hike.
Moves to raise fuel prices surfaced recently after the NNPC, citing high cost of crude in the international market, said it might soon be unable to finance the supply and distribution of petroleum products if the pump prices are not adjusted to allow for cost recovery. The Corporation said it has lost $900 million so far supplying fuel at N44.50 per litre.
The Petroleum Products Pricing Regulatory Agency (PPPRA), which backed the NNPC in its bid to recover cost, said that going by the current market fundamentals where the landing cost of petrol last Friday stood at N70.00 per litre, the open market price of the fuel should be N76.50 per litre.
Director-General, Budget Office of the Federation, Mr. Olabode Agusto said yesterday that the NNPC was subsidising the pump price of gasoline by over N300 million per day, which, according to him, has impaired the Corporation's ability to pay the federal government as at when due in respect of crude oil sales.
Speaking yesterday in Abuja at an interactive session with the Public Sector on the 2006-2008 Federal Government Medium-Term Expenditure Framework, Agusto said the Federal Government would have to part with an estimated N50 billion of its revenue to offset its own share of the subsidy.
He said the projected revenues for 2005 were down by another N160 billion because the actual crude oil production fell short of the projected production level.
He said that "the budget was predicated on oil production of 2.71 million barrels per day (bpd) whereas actual production for the first quarter was 2.39 mbpd. The government in the 2005 budget had pegged the price of crude oil at $30 per barrel."
He said given the NNPC estimate that the average production for the year is likely to be about 2.4 million bpd, the loss in production would stand at 12 per cent.
Noting that the projected independent revenue has also reduced by N40 billion, the director-general stated that on the whole, the total revenue loss totaled about N250 billion from N1.47 trillion to N1.22 trillion.
He gave the share of the Federal Government in the federation account at 48 per cent and the remaining 52 per cent going to states. Agusto also said N1.5trillion has been appropriated for spending as N450 billion - salaries; N100 billion -pension; N100 billion overheads; N200 billion - domestic debt service and; N100 billion - foreign currency debts service.
According to him, "N950 billion from N1.5trillion has already gone."
Agusto recalled that for the 2004 budget, revenue was N35 billion higher than expected and that oil production volume was targeted at 2.2 million bpd while actual was 2.5 million bpd. He however said this was dampened by a significant shortfall in independent revenue.
On the expenditure side, he said actual spending was N2 billion (5per cent) lower than budgeted because only 80 per cent of overhead was released. For recurrent expenditure, he pointed out that actual spending was N31 billion (6 per cent) higher than planned because "we had to clear some arrears of salaries and pensions).
He added that domestic service was N8 billion (9 percent) higher than planned because of "slightly higher level of debt." He also said foreign debt service was N19 billion (5 per cent higher than planned because "the Federal Government bore the debt service payments of some states and ended up paying 84 per cent of debt instead of 75 per cent."
For capital expenditure, he said spending was N60 billion lower than planned because "budget was approved in April and implementation of the capital budget started then."
Meanwhile, the NNPC said it expects to make savings in excess of $245 million (about N32.6 billion) by next year when it begins the use of ethanol fuel.
The Group Managing Director of the NNPC, Engineer Funsho Kupolokun, made the disclosure at the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) between the NNPC, COIMEX and Petrobras of Brazil, in Abuja yesterday.
Kupolokun disclosed that petroleum imports reception facility at Mosimi depot as well as the refineries will be revamped before December 31, this year to enable them take delivery and mix ethanol imports with PMS.
The Corporation, he said, would be saving Nigeria scarce foreign exchange and that during the seeding programme $105 million will be saved, while another $140m will be saved from PMS imports next year when the programme takes off.
He said part of the implementation strategy is to carry out a feasibility study to enable the establishment of the ethanol industry in Nigeria along with seeding the Nigerian market with fuel ethanol in the first phase.
"This phase will be followed by development and commercialization of ethanol fuel in Nigeria which will involve cultivation of large scale plantations of sugarcane and cassava and construction and operation of distilleries for the ethanol production of fuel ethanol," he said.
He said the objective of the Nigerian ethanol programme is to firmly establish a thriving industry utilizing agricultural products like sugarcane, cassava, etc, as a means of triggering significant employment generation, while improving the quality of automotive gasoline in Nigeria.
"Ethanol is emerging as an important motor fuel in view of the Kyoto Protocol Agreement. As a high-octane, water-free oxygenate, fuel ethanol is one of the tools available for fighting air pollution from vehicles.
"It is also a green house gas emission friendly fuel which reduces global warming, and ensures complete elimination of the use of octane enhancing tetra ethyl lead which is a toxic pollutant," he said.
He noted that beside providing improved income for farmers, the use of ethanol fuel would generate additional revenues for government, create new jobs, while the residue largesse can be used for power generation within distilleries with the potential to export surplus power to the national grid.
He said that the decision to partner COIMEX and Petrobras of Brazil is informed by their expertise in the area, and that they would assist in the area of technology and know-how acquisition, marketing and logistics of the fuel ethanol programme.
Petrobras Managing Director, Mr. Samir Awad, expressed the readiness of the Brazilian company to assist in transferring the ethanol fuel technology to Nigeria and to make it a viable and sustainable project that would contribute to the goal of improving the economic condition of the country.
"We are very proud to share with NNPC and Nigeria, all the good points of ethanol, both the solutions that were found in the Brazilian case which can be transferred to the Nigerian case that would help the country in achieving the goals of improving the economic conditions in the country", he said.
Awad described the collaborative initiative as the first major business venture between Brazil and Nigeria adding that it is a noteworthy development in the area of South-South co-operation.

Original_Search_Title: Workers, Govt Set for Showdown Over Fuel Price Hike
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Price Hike: Workers, FG Set for Showdown
Body_Text:
Lagos — OIL workers vowed yesterday to deploy all labour instruments in their arsenal to fight the proposed hike in the price of petrol by the Petroleum Products Pricing Regulatory Agency (PPPRA).
But the Group Managing Director of the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), Mr. Funso Kupolokun, said an increase in the price of the product has become inevitable to avert supply disruptions.
However, making their stand on the planned hike public at a press conference in Lagos, the oil workers under the aegis of National Union of Petroleum and Natural Gas workers (NUPENG) and Petroleum and Natural Gas Senior Staff Association of Nigeria (PENGASSAN) condemned the move.
PENGASSAN president, Comrade Uche Okoro, who spoke on behalf of the two unions, said the prevalent proverty in the country does not warrant any hike in the price of petrol.
Rather than jerk up the price he said the Federal Government should channel part of the nation's earnings from the international oil price windsfall to subsidize the cost of importing the product.
His pronouncement on the issue was the outcome of the National Executive Councils (NEC) meeting (NEC) of both unions held in Aba, Abia State, at the weekend.
They kicked against what they described as the import parity and persistent increase in the price of petroleum products in the country, saying it had apparently led to the abdication of social and economic responsibility of government to the citizenry.
Comrade Okoro said in Lagos that the situation in the country now does not warrant any increase in price of petroleum products.
Reading a communique issued at the end of the NUPENG/PENGASSAN joint NEC meeting, Comrade Okoro argued that it was the duty of PPPRA to fix price of petroleum products and not the NNPC.
He said in the present situation he believed that the NNPC would listen to the PPRA's decision and cancel the proposed increase.
They demanded the release of the Senator Ibrahim Mantu-led palliative committee report and its immediate implementation.
Other resolutions passed by the session included accusation that the Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE) has undermined due process slated for privatisation as earlier agreed in the stakeholders meeting.
Okoro said the session observed lack of transparency in the whole privatisation process and demanded that the BPE organise itself and come out with a more transparent procedure that would indicate who the investors are and the percentage of the shares that should go to the investors.
Meanwhile, Federal Government yesterday initiated bilateral cooperation with Brazil on production of renewable energy complement to petrol for internal utilisation.
NNPC signed two separate memoranda of understanding (MoU) with Petroleo Braziliero SA (Petrobras) and COIMEX, both of Brazil, for production of fuel ethanol for automotive and sundry applications.
The development come even as NNPC raised alarm over an imminent chaos in the nation's downstream petroleum market should the PPRA fail to effect price correction to guarantee sustainable supply.
The Nigerian Fuel Ethanol Programme which is part of the nation's broader economic reform agenda, NNPC said, would establish an industry that would use sugar cane and cassava to produce clean burning fuel and also generate electricity.
Ethanol, a high octane, water-free oxygenate, is emerging as an important fuel tool against air pollution, green house gas emission, global warning and toxic pollutants from vehicles.
According to Mr Kupolokun, the programme holds immense economic benefits for Nigeria in terms of revenue, job creation and increased income for sugarcane and cassava farmers.
Also, sugarcane residue (bargasse), he said, will be used for power generation within distilleries and export to the national grid.
"The ethanol programme is also a strategy of diversification of Nigeria's resources and has the potential for significant savings during the seeding programme," Mr Kupolokun said.
He added that the fuel ethanol project was a manifestation of Federal Government's drive for employment generation, capacity building, empowerment and cleaner environment.
The project, he pointed out, would entail new technological cooperation between the two countries in agriculture and other new technologies in biomass fuel production.
The implementation strategy, he explained would involve feasibility study for establishment of ethanol industry in Nigeria and seeding the Nigerian market with ethanol fuel in the first phase.
The next phase, Mr Kupolokun disclosed, will be development and commercialisation of ethanol fuel in Nigeria, involving large sugarcane and cassava farms and building of distrilleries for fuel ethanol production.
Explaining that the renewarble energy option was part of government's efforts to tackle the pricing crisis in the downstream market, Mr Kupolokun, however, said fuel price hike in the interim was inevitable to avoid supply disruptions.
He said NNPC has already recorded over N238 billion deficit in the domestic market and owes importers another one billion dollars or N134 billion duce to non-profitable operations to keep the downstream market running since October 2003.
He said the cooperation was doing everything to bring down prices in the long run, adding that the fuel ethanol programme was designed to put downward pressure on prices and also strike a profitable balance.
Long term measures that will bring down price, he listed, include reconfiguration of downstream facilities for ethanol blending in early 2006, floating of few private refineries, building of a new minature refinery at Escravos and requirement of oil producers to refine locally.
He said the objective of the new programme was to make Nigeria Africa's refining hub.

Original_Search_Title: For Abia People, It's Another Green Revolution
Headline: Nigeria: For Abia People, It's Another Green Revolution
Body_Text:
Lagos — Encouraged by government, people of Abia State are going back to the farms with the hope that the state can become the food basket of Nigeria.
It's on record that ever since Orji Uzor Kalu came to power as the governor of Abia State in May 1999 he had remained in the eye of the storm. But interestingly, he has always come out of the log-jam, even though some times harassed, he was never battered. And in spite of those obstacles, Kalu has succeeded in establishing himself as one of the moving spirits of the struggle for a just society based on justice, equity and fairness to all irrespective of ethnicity, tribe or religion.
It is on record that he has contributed immensely to the development of the private sector of the Nigerian economy. And ever since he became the governor of Abia State, he has persisted in using his wealth of experience and connections to chart a new course for the state.
At the kick-off of his campaign for the gubernatorial seat in December 1998 at Recreation Club Aba, Kalu had, contrary to wide expectations that he would mount the podium to attack his opponents, reeled out his programmes of action for the people if he was elected to the hot-seat.
His campaign promises then was that he would embark on a massive rehabilitation of infra-structural facilities in the state, especially roads, which he said would encourage investment opportunities as well as enhance communication amongst the people of the various areas.
Kalu had also promised to embark on an industrial revolution in the state, and to evolve welfare packages for the people, as well as run a government of transparency and accountability.
Today, six years after, there is a visible presence of democracy dividends in the state, such as good roads, electricity, water, rehabilitated schools, improved health facilities, increased agricultural production, quality housing programme, improved condition of service and regular payment of workers salaries, in adding to prompt payment of pension and gratuity of retired workers. Kalu has fulfilled his promise of rehabilitating a substantial number of roads in the state.
As a result of decay in the health sector, Kalu on assumption of office in 1999 commenced a free medical scheme which goes about the rural communities to treat patients. So far, over two million people have benefited from this programme. Shortly after inauguration for a second term in office in 2003, Kalu awarded contracts for the rehabilitation of all the General Hospitals in the state.
So far, work at the hospitals at Isuikwuato, Bende, Isiala Ngwa North and Ukwa West have been completed, while the multi-million naira Abia State University Teaching Hospital in Aba has already taken off in full force.
Already, up-to-date and state of the art medical equipment have been purchased by the state government to be installed in the General Hospitals. Based on its pragmatic performances in the health sector, Abia State has been recently selected as a beneficiary by the World Bank in its Health System Development Grant. By this gesture, Abia State stands to receive well over N200 million from the world body to enable it boost its health-care delivery programmes in the state.
Above all these, Kalu has shown an astute commitment to the cause of the national unity, which in fact, has earned him the admiration of many Nigerians across ethnic barriers and partisan politics. Any keen observer of politics would discern that Kalu has truly demonstrated his beliefs that the enormous challenges of development of the Nigerian-nation calls for creativity and resourcefulness in generating a change in the well-being of the people by evolving people-oriented initiatives.
The governor, who had never hidden his love for the masses, said the people had ever since given him their unflinching support which has been a boost to his efforts.
He disclosed that his administration has on inception of office in 1999 inherited a prostrate economy characterized by declining capacity utilization in the real sector, poor performance of major infra-structural facilities, as well as an unsustainable liquidity position arising from unemployment and inflation. He also disclosed that his administration had then met an empty treasury, as well as debt of N3 billion.
According to him, "we would have done more except for the poor allocation from the Federation Account. As I am talking to you, the state government spends about N780 million monthly on wage bills; and yet what we get from the Federation Account can barely meet these obligations, considering the huge debt profile of government.
"Our debt (incurred by past administration in the then Imo State) is being deducted monthly from our share of the Federal Allocation. And this is responsible for the present financial difficulties we are facing", he noted, pointing out that "despite these visible obstacles, we have succeeded in delivering quality democracy dividends to our people."
He reiterated his challenge to the nation's leadership to define their humanity through love, service and selflessness. Little wonder then that the Governor has been recognized for this radical posture of governance, both locally and internationally.
In attestation to this, President Olusegun Obasanjo had during his official visit to Abia State in 1999 (just within a few months of Kalu's inception of office), declared Kalu as the "Action Governor of Nigeria".
The President, who after seeing the spate of development programmes already completed by Kalu's administration, especially on road construction, was stunned by the governor's achievement and enjoined other state governors to learn from the Abia example.
In pursuance of the state government policy of rural transformation, the government has supplied 600 transformers and sank well over 800 boreholes in the rural communities of the state. To promote commerce and industry for which the people are noted, government has initiated moves to encourage the growth of cottage, small and medium scale industries in the state.
Kalu's administration has since launched its Small and Medium Enterprises Development Fund (SMEFUND). In the initiative, the state government will contribute one per cent of her annual budget to SME development, and also incorporate the private sector operatives in the development of the sector.
Also, Kalu has instituted one of the most enduring agricultural policies anywhere in the country. His annual distribution of the latest internationally modified seedlings of various crops to farmers speak volumes of his determination to make the state compare favourably in world agricultural ratings.
The state government has cultivated over 700 hectares of cassava farm in addition to a standard fishery hatchery being constructed at the Ogwe Chicken Farms, as well as 10,000 disease resistant banana and pineapple seedlings being distributed to farmers at affordable prices. The state government has also cultivated some 505 hectares of rubber and six million palms. Cocoa and cashew seedlings are currently being distributed to farmers in the state for this year's planting season, amongst others.
All these are in realisation of the need to restore agriculture as the mainstay of the nation's economy. Perhaps, one of the most viable and enduring policies ever embarked upon by the Kalu's Administration is the introduction of tuition-free education in both the primary and secondary schools in the state for the past four years as well as the work-to-learn programmes for the working adults. Added to these, Kalu had equally introduced a free transport service scheme for students and pupils in the state.
Just last year, the World Bank President, Mr. James Wolfehenson led a team of officials of the institution to the state to inspect the viability of made in Aba products for their possible sponsorship by the World Bank in the world market. This, according to Wolfehenson, was because of some encouraging reports received by the bank on the economic viability of the state, especially in the areas of small and medium scale enterprises; and the people's unflinching determination to take their destiny into their own hands.
To achieve this, Kalu had on assumption of office as governor of the state, adopted the policy of limiting government's direct participation in economic activities in favour of private sector involvement. He believes that government's basic responsibility is to provide the enabling enablement for economic growth and social stability, since according to him, "government has no business being in business".
Moreover, a consortium of American investors who visited the state capital, Umuahia recently announced their choice of the state for citing a N14.5 billion ultra-modern Teaching Hospital. Also, the international brewing giant, Guinness Nigeria Plc recently opened its N4 billion plant in Aba, the commercial nerve centre of the state.
So far, available records show that other multinationals have indicated their interest to invest in the state because of the existing enabling environment created for economic growth by government.
As a major way of sustaining his promises of transparency in governance, Kalu has since he assumed office published the state government's audited accounts in the nation's newspapers and magazines on quarterly basis.
And only recently, the Code of Conduct Bureau had gone for Kalu's jugular over what it described as the governor's inability to take their advice on the closure of a foreign bank account which Kalu had declared to the office in 1999 and 2003 respectively as part of his assets.
But Kalu himself noted that it was not in the spirit of the constitution for him to close an account he had operated twelve years before he became a governor. He therefore described the attempt by the Code of Conduct to default and arraign him as "a ruse and legal gamble orchestrated by the powers-that -be to frustrate my presidential ambition in 2007".
Then, one begins to ask if this is another Randolph Hearst case being mirrored down on Kalu? Perhaps, it is only Kalu himself that can give the answer because he knows where the shoe pinches. And definitely, his insight will go a long way to create further understanding of the many riddles of those seeming controversies that have so often trailed his foot - steps and activities over the years.

Original_Search_Title: FG to Import 150 Tractors
Headline: Nigeria: FG to Import 150 Tractors
Body_Text:
Abuja — Federal Government has concluded plans to import about 150 intermediate technology tractors known as power tillers for distribution to cooperative farmer groups in the country, just as it has embarked on the establishment of farm gate processing centres nationwide.
Minister of State for Agriculture and Rural Development, Otunba Bamidele Dada, said this while receiving officials of the Genertec International Corporation of China who are in Nigeria to have a first-hand knowledge of cassava production and processing techniques in the country.
According to the minister, government has taken those steps to reduce the drudgery associated with traditional farming methods, which are always characterised by low productivity and to increase the value-addition of products before they leave the farm environments.
Dada said as a result of the increasing demand for cassava products from Nigeria, the government is now focused on post-harvest processing standards to ensure that whatever that is exported from the Country are of first class.
He told the visiting Chinese delegation that to boost the production capacities of cassava farmers, about 96,000 bundles of high-yielding disease-resistant breader-stalk of cassava, developed in conjunction with International Institution of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), have recently been produced by Roots and Tubers Expansion Programme, Ijebu-Ife and the Roots and Tubers Research Institute, Umudike. Dada disclosed that National Centre for Agricultural Mechanisation at Ilorin has trained over 50 fabricators to produce various processing equipment as part of efforts to reduce post-harvest losses.
He Commended Nigeria/China cooperation especially in agriculture, the result of which is the presence of 500 Chinese agricultural experts working in Nigeria under the South- South Cooperation of the National Special Programme for Food Security.

Original_Search_Title: Cities Deal With Street Children
Headline: Rwanda's Street Children
Body_Text:
Gisenyi — The authorities in Rwanda are encouraging local communities to look
after an estimated 7,000 to 8,000 children who live on the street.
"We never had such a problem in the past," said Anne Gahongayire,
secretary-general at the ministry of gender and family promotion in
Kigali.
"But because of the realities in the family after the war and the
genocide, we have lots of children who have taken to the streets because
their families have... become so fragile.
"You don't have anyone to blame right now."
The street children are in part the legacy of the 1994 genocide in which
some 800,000 Tutsis and moderate Hutus were massacred. But young people are
often made homeless by simple economic pressures, and the tradition in areas
such as Gisenyi and Ruhengeri where men can take several wives, leading to
domestic conflict.
Rwandan politicians continue to develop new ways of helping them, but
the message is clear -local communities, which have the closest ties with
the children, must take on greater responsibility for their well-being.
The principle is similar to the way in which "Gacaca" or community-based
courts, rather than the state judicial system, are being employed to try
those accused of taking part in the genocide, Gahongayire says. "We have to
equip the community," she said. "They know better. They can be trusted
better."
But the non-government organisation Point d'Écoute, which has worked
with street kids in Gisenyi for the past seven years - providing
counselling, food and blankets - says many in the area are shunning their
children.
Aloys Kaberuka, coordinator of Point d'Écoute, says Gisenye has 65 to 70
street kids aged 12 to 17, the majority of whom have parents who are simply
unable to care for them.
"It's very complex," said Kaberuka. "I have some cases of children
unable to find a resolution. It is possible they'll turn to crime to support
themselves because they're in a bad situation."
In an effort to earn money, children do odd jobs such as carrying loads
or washing cars.
Claudine Muhamawenimana, a petite, unsmiling 18-year-old with short
black hair, started out as one of these street children, but now rents a
room in a mud-brick house.
Wearing a grey Adidas T-shirt, she carries her one-month-old baby Isame
in a makeshift sack on her back in traditional African style. The child is
sick.
Asked to tell how she came to be here, Claudine says, "It's a long
story."
After her father died and her mother remarried, her stepfather refused
to accept her.
Working the streets as a prostitute to make ends meet, she met a
20-year-old homeless man who bought her food, clothing and soap. But under
the circumstances, she was still forced to continue selling her body. When
she became pregnant, the man deserted her.
Point d'Écoute has been instrumental in helping her. But there is only
so much financial aid it can provide.
"I have no means to pay the rent, to feed the baby," she said, sitting
on a neighbour's veranda. She wishes she had enough money to start a
business selling cassava, bread and bananas.
The street children are by no means the only headache facing the
government.  It is currently trying to reunite as many as possible of 3,600
children now living in 25 orphanages across the country with their
families.
And there are many children, particularly girls, who have been forced to
assume control of households after parents and other adults lost their lives
to Hutu killers during the genocide, or subsequently to AIDS. UNICEF
estimates that Rwanda has some 100,000 families where the de facto adult is
aged 10 to 18.
The United Nations agency has responded to difficulties in relying on
social workers by offering these young heads of households a mentoring
programme in which community members take them under their wing.
Rwandan politicians are working with international donors to establish a
fund of nearly 11 million US dollars for organisations working with
children. That money should be available by September.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Declares N2trn Income
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Declares N2trn Income
Body_Text:
Lagos — SHELL Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) has declared a net income of about $14.6 billion (about N2 trillion) from an output of 365 million barrels of crude in the 2004 business year.
It also declared payment of $3.8 billion (about N509.2 billion) in taxes and royalties to the Federal Government during the period.
Shell, with over 15 billion barrels of crude oil and condensate reserves in Nigeria, produces nearly half of the country's estimated 2.4 million barrels per day for export.
The company operates the nation's biggest joint venture (JV) and production sharing agreements in the upstream oil and gas sector which sustains the economy.
In Shell's 2004 People and Environment Annual Report, presented to the media by Corporate External Affairs Director, Rev. Precious Omuku, weekend, the company's production chart indicated average daily production of one million barrels for the year.
The crude export graph also presented in the report, indicated an average price of $40 per barrel ($40/bbl) for the year.
Based on a 2000 Memorandum of Understanding (MoU), the company's petroleum profit tax to the Federal Government rose to 83 per cent from $1.2 billion (N160.8 billion) in 2003 to $2.2 billion (N294.8 billion) in 2004.
Royalty to the country also rose 50 per cent from $608 million (N81.5 billion) in 2003 to $904 million (N121.14 billion) in 2004.
The company declared statutory fund contribution to the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) at $69 million (N9.3 billion) from the JV account, out of which $21 million (N2.82 billion) came from Shell.
Besides taxes and royalties, Shell stated that it also made significant payments to state and federal governments as employees' income tax and to the Education Tax Fund (ETF).
On local content policy compliance, Rev. Omuku said 95 per cent of the company's 5000 direct employees were Nigerians, besides 7000 contract staff and 20,000 contractors' staff.
He added that Shell awarded $727 million (N97.42 billion) worth of contracts to local companies, 20 per cent of which came from the Niger Delta.
Describing 2004 as good in the company's performance and operational records Rev. Omuku said trends in community incidents substantially went down in the year resulting in significant reduction in deterred production.
This, he attributed to the company's new sustainable community development model which engages stakeholders' collaboration in tackling specific needs of people.
He said the company, through the model, invested over $50 million (N6.7 billion) in cassava production and processing, roll-back malaria, HIV/AIDS abatement and mitigation, and rural telecommunication.
He, however, listed the challenges to include crude theft, spills resulting from vandalisation, and community resistance to personnel access to facilities.

Original_Search_Title: How She Won the Gold Medal
Headline: Uganda: How She Won the Gold Medal
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT is a beautiful village full of desolate people. Welcome to Okazara
village, found in Arua District's Vurra County. It is Dorcas Inzikuru's home
village.
This is where the 23-year-old world 3,000m steeplechase champion started
training for the race she then probably didn't know existed.
To become a world champion steeplechaser, you need to be extraordinarily
tough and determined, with exceptional force of will as well as finely
developed skills. You would need more or less the same qualities to survive
in Vurra.
The terrain consists of undulating hills; the locals have to do a
considerable amount of hill climbing. Survival is hard; it is all about
wrestling a living from a very reluctant land. It is Arua's food basket,
though the title has not brought prosperity to the area.
It is these undulating hills of Vurra, covered with a thick carpet of
greenery, and the poverty-stricken population, which provided the perfect
training ground for the young woman who has won Uganda a gold medal at a
World Athletics meet, after a 33-year quiet period.
For a village that has produced a world champion, Okazara looks rather
deprived, to put it politely. Most of the villagers are peasant farmers who
survive by growing cassava and bananas.
Although it lies just 15km south-west of the rapidly growing Arua town,
Okazara remains one of those villages where an iron-roofed house is
considered a rare phenomenon.
That is why Mzee Jackson Luluwa's home is rapidly becoming a local
tourist attraction. His house is not only iron-roofed, it even has glass
panes. The retired cleric did not build this "mansion" from his earnings
from the church. It was a gift from his fleet-footed daughter Inzikuru,
using her earnings on the track, plus a donation of sh1.5m from sports
philanthropist Micheal Ezra, who had noted her potential. medal in the
5,000m race at the World Junior Athletics Championships held in Chile, South
America.
She was welcomed back home as a heroine, and was voted Sports
Personality of the Year by the Uganda Sports Press Association (USPA).
The achievement turned Dorcas into a globetrotter, as she soon started
receiving invitations to compete in different countries, giving her exposure
and vital experience, the lack of which has been the undoing of many
promising Ugandan athletes.
As her well-thumbed passport can testify, the girl from Vurra, whose
dream was to represent her district at national level, has been to all the
five continents.
In a past interview with Sunday Vision, Inzikuru attributed all these
achievements to two things: hard work and prayer.
She always begins her day at 5:00am with a prayer, a childhood habit
cultivated by her pastor father back in Vurra.
She then goes out for her first training session of the day: an hour's
run covering between 12 and 15km. According to her, it is better to run in
the morning before the sun and the rest of the world wakes up to distract
you.
She normally trains twice a day, unless there is a competition round the
corner where she needs to rest and give both her mind and body time to
prepare for the race ahead.
In between training and competition, Dorcas finds time to play her
favourite Christian music and interact with friends, not necessarily fellow
athletes.
A simple down-to-earth girl, Inzikuru belongs to that rare breed of
athletes who won't allow competition fever to get to their nerves, or
victory go to their heads. Becoming a world champion, with $60,000 in her
purse is unlikely to change the character of Inzikuru, the girl from
Vurra.
It was perhaps for that reason that Inzikuru, together with soccer star
Jackson Mayanja, were picked to act as ambassador for Right to Play, an
international charitable organisation that uses sports role models to get to
disadvantaged children.
To get to where she is today, Inzikuru has had to pay a hefty price. Her
studies had to be put on hold for a year, to enable her focus more on
training for the 2004 Athens Olympics.
The decision triggered a barrage of protests from Inzikuru's family, who
felt education was the only way through which their daughter could make it
in life.
At one point, Inzikuru's poverty-stricken family questioned the logic of
their daughter globetrotting, when her agemates were attending school. Not
even the argument that travelling was part of the learning process would
convince them.
Nor were they impressed by the fact that while her colleagues back at
school were struggling to locate the various countries on the world map,
Inzikuru was actually visiting them.
The family's biggest quarrel was that none of the "big" money their
daughter was supposed to be earning on the running track abroad was
trickling down the slopes of Vurra to improve their lives.
The general feeling was that the money from their daughter's sweat was
ending up in the pockets of a clique of Inzikuru's handlers who have kept
her on a short leash, since she went international.
Since the moment a local FM radio broke the news of Inzikuru's victory,
Arua district has been alight with excitement. Their daughter has done them
proud. But there is another cause for the excitement: the $60,000 prize
money Inzikuru received.
It seems to generate more excitement among some people than the
medal.
Already, some sharp-minded businessmen are cobbling together business
proposals for Inzikuru's consideration.
With 2006 just round the corner, local politicians are already jostling
for space on Inzikuru's back, where they can ride during campaign time.
Eligible bachelors are dusting their coats, just in case the golden girl
decides to quit the track after hitting the jackpot.
Not that the clergymen are keeping out; the church where Inzikuru's
family once took shelter, might need a new roof.
As usual, the people of Vurra are hoping that a little of it will
trickle down the slopes into their lives.
After all Inzikuru is their daughter. That is not all. It was on these
very slopes that she acquired the extraordinary toughness, determination,
will power as well as some of the skills that have turned her into a world
champion. Before this village marvel came into existence, Mzee Luluwa's
family of nine had to do with a grass-thatched hut like most of their
neighbours. Some three years ago, the hut collapsed, almost burying the
entire family.
For quite some time, the family had to take shelter at the local church
where the father was working as a lay preacher. That is where a Sunday
Vision team found them when it visited some three years ago.
A middle-aged lady, with a baby strapped to her back, and bunch of
freshly uprooted beans in her hands, warmly received the team. She was
introduced as Inzikuru's mother.
Through an interpreter, she immediately started lamenting about the
family's plight. The family hut had collapsed, and a replacement was yet to
be erected.
With a stay-at-home mother, the family had no extra income to supplement
the father's miserly earnings as a lay preacher. As the oldest of the eight
children, it was really up to Inzikuru to save her family from
destitution.
Studying hard to get a good job to support the family would have been
the most sensible thing to do. But raising the money to keep her in school
was another struggle.
Unknown to Inzikuru, by bringing all these trials and tribulations into
her life, the gods were preparing her for a career on the track where, to
become a champion, you need to be extraordinarily tough and determined.
While her mind was being fortified by the numerous problems threatening
to pull her family under, Inzikuru's body was in turn being hardened by the
hilly landscape where her family lived.
The hills Inzikuru had to climb daily from her home, located at the foot
of Okazara hill, to run the various errands that her parents always sent her
on, put her in such great physical shape that the first time she had to run
on a flat surface, a running track, it was like running downhill.
After outrunning everyone in the local meets in Arua, Inzikuru was
selected to represent her district at the 1995 National Post-Primary
Athletics Championships, where she came third in the 5,000m race.
Although she had not won, Inzikuru returned home with a light
heart.
She had achieved her life dream, which had been to represent her
district at a national level, just like her father did in the past as a
sprinter, before swapping his running kit for a cassock.
According to former Olympian John Goville, Inzikuru must have received
the running genes from the father. "Father and daughter have the same body
structure. They both look athletic," said the athlete turned policeman.
So while Inzikuru's idea was to equal her father's feat, other people
had different ideas; they were not going to leave such a promising talent go
to rot. One of those people was Sam Egama, a literature teacher at
St. Joseph's College Ombachi, who encouraged Inzikuru to think beyond
local.
That is when it occurred to the young girl that her sporting talent
could become a first-class ticket to the better life that had so far eluded
her and her family. And she was right.
For starters, it got her a place at the Mvara SS, one of the best in the
region. Soon she was a regular at Uganda Amateur Athletics Federation (UAAF)
organised meets.
Five years since she first stepped on the running track, and after
several regional and continental wins, Inzikuru won a gold medal in the
5,000m race at the World Junior Athletics Championships held in Chile, South
America.

Personality of the Year by the Uganda Sports Press Association (USPA).

Original_Search_Title: Sustaining Devt Vision in the Eastern Heartland
Headline: Nigeria: Sustaining Devt Vision in the Eastern Heartland
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Imo State Government unveiled what it calls a tripod vision on which development effort would be anchored.
Miss Perpetua Okonkwo is a 100 level student of agricultural technology at the Michael Okpara College of Agriculture, Umuagwo, Imo State. Obviously less than 20 years old, she is attending to pigs, preparing their food. The job is tasking and time consuming, but Perpetua does not show it. Instead, he smiles to you and tells THISDAY, "it is good for my development." In other circumstances, a girl her age would balk at any suggestion that she should get herself engaged in such menial, some will say, dirty job.
Miss Adanma Nwegbe is a fourth year student of agricultural economics at the Imo State University, Owerri (IMSU). Her chores include to feed turkey, make vegetables like pawpaw leaves, water leaves etc. in the free range turkey area, where the turkeys lay eggs.
What is her impression of her work? "Good even though it is too much". That is her response.
Perpetua and Adanma are among the students currently on internship at the Songhai Redemption Farms at Nekede, Owerri, Imo State. There are other young men and women in the farm. While some like Perpetua and Adanma are on industrial attachment, many others are employed in the farm.
Songhai Farms is a project of the Imo State Government, which has revolutionalised farming in the state.
Songhai Farm was developed from the Agricultural Development Corporation (ADC) that had become moribund. The Songhai concept is that of self-reliance, a concept developed by a catholic priest Rev. Father Nzeamujo, based in Porto Novo, Benin Republic.
The project manager of the centre, Mr. Cletus Okonkwo, an architect, says at the time Governor Achike Udenwa decided to set up the farm using the Songhai philosophy of self-reliance, the place was almost moribund. "The place has come back to life and the people are now re-oriented as they are being trained to make use of their hands," he tells THISDAY.
He continues: "The governor in his search for how to achieve his three cardinal principles (agriculture, industrialisation and re-orientation) sent 20 people to Rev. Fr. Nzeamujo for training. He now established the Songhai Centre for to achieve to achieve self-reliance, re-orientation and food production. Young graduates and school leavers come and are trained in different aspects of food production,"
Okonkwo says that the centre has several departments which include fisheries, poultry, snailry, piggery, grass cutter, turkey, cassava, etc. In addition there is a feed mill to produce food for the animals.
THISDAY is taken round the complex. It is a very large centre. The pineapple orchard has 250,000 suckers. The snails co-habit with plantation in the plantain plantation.
The piggery is one area that arrests attention. Unlike what happens in other places where pigs are raised in very dirty environment, the pigs at the Songhai Centre are even washed by the attendants.
The project manager says it is only in the centre that day-old turkeys are hatched in Nigeria and it is done at the price of N10 per one for the public. The turkey section has both incubator and hatchery. Okonkwo says it takes 28 days to hatch a turkey and 21 days to hatch a chicken.
Okonkwo says the centre was started in 2003 and that so far about 200 people have been trained since inception. Some of those trained have been absorbed to open Songhai centres in the local government areas of the state.
On November 29, 2004, President Olusegun Obasanjo was at the Songhai Centre, Nekede as part of his presidential tour of the Imo State. On the visitors' register, he wrote his impression of the centre. "Good effort. May I commend Rev. Fr. Nzeamujo and the governor (Udenwa) for the resuscitation of this prime property and the infrastructure. Please make it a farming school to produce practical, practising and inspired young and new generation farmers. Please let initiative, self-reliance be their watch word."
The agricultural revolution quietly taking place at the Songhai Centre, Nekede has its philosophical foundation in the Redemption '98 agenda unveiled by then candidate Achike Udenwa, who was seeking the mandate of the people of Imo State to be their governor in the 1999 gubernatorial election. This is the position of Dr. Vin Udokwu, Chief of Staff in the Government House, Owerri.
According to him, the governor's concern then was to redeem Imo State from several years of neglect and misrule. Udokwu says the idea is that there are principles that will guide the administration in the event of victory at the polls. "When we won, the principles included rehabilitation of infrastructure, ordered social values. We have been religiously following that mission statement. The only addition is that by the second term, there was an addition, agriculture, industrialisation and re-orientation. If the citizenry is properly oriented, every other thing will follow. If you go round the state you will discover that all we set out to do we have done at least 80 to 90 per cent," the chief of staff says.
Udokwu says the emphasis of the state is on small scale industries especially considering the Imo people who live outside Imo State. He says not too long ago, the state government commissioned the first diagnostic industry in Nigeria that is manufacturing HIV testing kits in the state. According to him, the owner of the company lived in the United States and decided to come home to establish the company after hearing Udenwa's clarion call.
"We are building industrial clusters for artisans to help build small scale industries. We now tell people that eve after education, you can go for self employment. Through our poverty alleviation programme, we are training and empowering hairdressers, vulcanizers, artisans, fashion designers, etc.," Udokwu says further.
In recent times the buzzword of the government's economic empowerment programme is poverty alleviation. In fact it has become the plank on which governments both at federal and state levels have anchored their economic development programme.
In Imo State, the poverty alleviation programme is one that has received so much attention. Dr. Joe Okoroji is the Special Adviser to the Governor on Poverty Alleviation. Speaking with THISDAY in his office he says the poverty alleviation programme in Imo State is the only agency of government that tackles poverty alleviation from two major angles, namely skill acquisition and training of new farmers.
"What we do is that we go round the 27 local government areas, select people who are unemployed but who could be employed and we train them on different skills. We have about 22 skills ranging from wielding, fashion designing, video, carpentry, block molding, vulcanizing, etc. We train them, we buy them the equipment for which they will have to pay us back.
"Let's take vulcanizing for example, we train you for three months, after that we don't expect you to be a professional in three months. We want to train as many people as possible at the shortest possible time to stand on their feet, make mistakes and learn from there. We keep them away from crime, to be able to take care of their family. The equipment costs about N35,000, we don't make any money from them. They pay us how much it costs us to buy the equipment over time to enable them have a break, two months moratorium before they now start paying us N2,000 a month. During this time we will be monitoring them to know how things are going with them, if they need more money.
"We have a bank that we use. Our system is complicated in the sense that if we visit you and discover that you need more money you could ask the bank for a loan to the tune of N100,000. There is no collateral here. The poverty alleviation programme will suck in the interest while the beneficiary pays back the principal sum. That's the major thrust of our poverty alleviation.
"We encourage producers, Imo State happens to be the only state in the federation where basket for transporting tomatoes, onions and all those perishable foodstuff are produced. What we do is to visit them, form them into self-help groups that will become co-operative for them to increase production," Okoroji tells THISDAY.
The current thrust of the state poverty alleviation programme, he says, took off in 2003 though the department was established in 2000 by Udenwa.
What is the guarantee that beneficiaries pay back to ensure continuity? Okoroji says that has been taken care of. According to him, there are poverty alleviation co-ordinators in every local government area and each co-ordinator is to counter-sign the application form of each beneficiary vouching for the beneficiary's ability to pay back. Each co-ordinator has a motorcycle to enable him reach every nook and cranny of the local government area to collect. They will be paying for the motorcycle at the rate of N5,000 a month. In addition, each poverty alleviation co-ordinator gets N1,000 a month to fuel his motorcycle.
Okoroji also says his department has entered into arrangement with mobile telephone giant, Globacom to create one thousand jobs within one month by way of call centres. He says each beneficiary will get a line, handset and N4,000 worth of recharge cards as take-off grant. "These will get a lot of people off the streets", he declares.
On the number of beneficiaries so far, Okoroji says since the programme started in late 2003, the department has trained about 10,000 people on skill acquisition and over 15,000 farmers in 1,020 self-help groups.
He says there are people that are trained by the agency without being empowered in terms of equipment. He cites as examples those trained in computer and physiotherapy. He says physiotherapists are much sought after now especially with people suffering stroke. Because of paucity of funds, he says, it is not possible to buy equipment for beneficiaries of these programmes to get equipment from the agency. The agency's computer centre in Owerri has about 20 computers available for all those willing to be trained but they have to live in or around Owerri to be able to attend training, he says.
For those trained in video coverage, Okoroji says the agency cannot buy them video cameras but can afford to buy them ordinary professional cameras.
For farmers, Okoroji says the agency helps to ensure that the self-help groups are registered with the State Ministry of Commerce as co-operatives. That way they will be entitled to loan from the Nigerian Agricultural and Rural Development Bank.
According to Okoroji, there are funds for agriculture set up by the Federal Government which farmers in Imo State are not aware of. His agency, he says, tries to ensure that they get the loans. For every N50,000 a farmers' co-operative puts in the bank, they will be entitled to N250,000.
He says the idea is that Imo State becomes the bread basket of Nigeria and to redress the present situation in which only tired, old men and women engage in farming.
The administration has built an ultra-modern Imo State University Teaching Hospital (IMSUTH) in Orlu. The university has 33 departments and is now fully equipped to render all services expected of a teaching hospital. Already there are medical students in their third year MB BS programme. The complex was commissioned by Obasanjo in November 2004.
Dr. Chidi Emma Marume is the Imo State Commissioner for health. He tells THISDAY that the teaching hospital is an attempt by the state government to provide three things in one, namely a tertiary institution to provide health services at the highest level and to provide training facilities for medical students already enrolled into Imo State University. The other purpose, he says, is to provide facilities for research into different aspects of medicine especially in maternal medicine, pediatric medicine and renal medicine.
"Because of what we have in mind, we looked for affiliation with Howard University and Medical School. We also got affiliated to Drew University, Los Angeles and the University of Illinois, all in the United States.
"We have first class facilities. In the department of renal medicine, there are eight dialysis machines only waiting to be put to use. In this country, no other centre has this.
"In pediatric medicine department we have all the facilities for expert management of children. The emergency department is one of the best in the country in terms equipment and manpower. We have nine ambulances and the hospital as a whole has five theatres.
"We also have a warehouse filled with spare parts. We do not have to buy spare parts for the next two years. We imported cleaning equipment with the necessary chemicals that will last two years. We imported a complete laundry unit from India and we had to bring people from India to install it. It is fully computerised from medical records to accounts.
"The foreign hospitals we are affiliated to we signed memoranda of understanding for students and staff exchange programmes in telemedicine", Marume says.
Hon. Noel Agwuocha Chukwukadibia, one time Speaker of the Imo State House of Assembly, is the Commissioner for Public Utilities. Sitting in his office in the imposing State Secretariat, he tells THISDAY that the success of Udenwa's tripod vision depends on his ministry. "Udenwa commissioned me to ensure that the tripod vision does not fail, thus making mine the most important ministry. Part of my charge was to husband the tripod vision because if we don't give you energy we can't talk about industrialisation; if we don't irrigate your field you can't talk about sustainable food production," he says.
According to Agwuocha, in 2004, the state government gave out 46 transformers to communities and 31 this year. So far, between 1999 and 2005 we have given cables over 300 transformers to communities where there was no electricity. The idea is to encourage cottage industries. He says the intention of Udenwa is that by 2007 every community in Imo State must have electricity.
He informs that the state government has signed a joint venture agreement with Anooco/Sarr of Boston in the United States for 140 megawatt Independent Power Project (IPP) using gas turbine. He says the Federal Government has okayed the project.
He says the project is worth over one billion dollars and will be sited in Izombe. There are six other industries that are part of the project, he says. Agwuocha says the plan is that Udenwa will commission it before leaving office in 2007.
The state secretariat was commissioned by Obasanjo in 2004. Agwuocha says his ministry did external electrification of the new secretariat by direct labour.
Also, the street lighting of the Central Bank behind the secretariat was done by his ministry by direct labour, he says. The contractor had given a bill of N228 million but it cost his ministry not more than N168 million, he says.
Sir Bright Nwelue, the Chief Press Secretary to the Governor, has brought a lot of finesse and panache to the office. Since taking over as the governor's chief image maker, he has made a difference. He tells THISDAY that Udenwa believes so much in his aides. "A lot of people outside government keep appreciating what he is doing. He is simple even in his mode of dressing. He has given identity to the Igbo man. He is an accountant and he believes that politics of bitterness is not necessary. He believes that if there is no peace in Imo State there can be no development. Nigeria believes that Imo State is the most peaceful State in the country. It has helped in his vision. He does not have time for somebody without creativity. He is a loyal party man and he expects his aides to be loyal. He has even suprised his critics.
"Some people misconstrue his peaceful disposition for weakness but sometimes they get to know that he is in charge. After the Otokoto saga we thought it would be difficult to make Imo attractive to foreign investors but today that is not the case. The number of hotels in Owerri is amazing. People want to come and spend their money in Owerri because it is peaceful."

Original_Search_Title: Unravelling the Riddles in Kalu
Headline: Nigeria: Unravelling the Riddles in Kalu
Body_Text:
Lagos — Governor Orji Uzor Kalu of Abia State has always been in the eye of the storm. He has shown courage in his public statements and criticisms of government policies, and he equally has been absorbing barrage of attacks each time he stirs controversy. In this report, Emeka Osondu examines the personality of Kalu and his six years stewardship in Abia State
There once lived an American business magnet named Randolph Hearst. Hearst was known to be a blunt and brash fellow, who used to call a spade a spade. Perhaps, Hearst was being influenced by the enormous wealth he had created from his various business ventures or maybe, he was just being his usual self.
Randolph Hearst was into banking, oil exploration, communication, and publishing, amongst others. And to say the least, he made a huge success out of them all. Yet, at the height of his success, he also made a forage into the American national politics.
At his party convention in one of the Eastern states of the United States in 1954, Hearst was said to have stood up and addressed the delegates. He told the delegates that most people within the American leadership circles had said he was a coarse person; and that he did not possess the kind of learning that most American leaders do.
Hearst further claimed that those people had so often accused him of pronouncing the word "bird" as b-i-r-d instead of b-u-r-d. So, he ended up rhetorically challenging them that; "If bird is b-u-r-d, then what in hell is board?"
Therefore, people who knew Hearst had often seen him as an enigmatic and mystifying person, who often did what he did on instinct.
And because of his perceived shrewdness, his actions had so often generated controversies in the society. Yet, he was not alone in this attitude. There are other models like Randolph Hearst. Take for instance the case of Nigeria's own Orji Uzor Kalu, who even at the prime age of 45 years, has had many controversies and riddles to his credit.
Infact, it's on record that ever since Kalu came to power as the Executive Governor of Abia State in May 1999 that he had remained in the eye of the storm. But interestingly, he had always come out of the log-jam, even though sometimes harassed, but had never been battered.
And inspite of those obstacles, Kalu has succeeded in establishing himself as one of the moving spirits of the struggle for a just society based on justice, equity and fairness to all irrespective of ethnicity, tribe or religion.
It is on record that Kalu has contributed immensely to the development of the private sector of the Nigerian economy. And ever since he became the governor of Abia State, he has persisted in using his wealth of experience and connections to chart a new course for the state that was held hostage since its creation in 1991 by inept leadership.
At the kick-off of his campaign for the gubernatorial seat in December 1998 at Recreation Club Aba, Kalu had reeled out his programmes of action for the people if he was elected to the hot-seat.
His campaign promises then was that he would embark on a massive rehabilitation of infrastructural facilities in the state, especially roads, which he said would encourage investment opportunities as well as enhance communication amongst the people of the various areas.
Kalu had also promised to embark on an industrial revolution in the state, and to evolve welfarist packages for the people, as well as to run a government of transparency and accountability.
Today, six years after, there is a visible presence of democracy dividends in the state, such as good roads, electricity, water, rehabilitated schools, improved health facilities, increased agricultural production, quality housing programme, improved condition of service and regular payment of workers' salaries, in adding to prompt payment of pension and gratuity of retired workers. Governor Kalu has fulfilled his promise of rehabilitating a substantial number of roads in the state.
As a result of decay in the health sector, overnor Kalu on assumption of office in 1999, commenced a free medical scheme which goes about the rural communities to treat patients. So far, over 2 million people have benefited from this programme. Shortly after inauguration for a second term in office in 2003, Kalu awarded contracts for the rehabilitation of all the General Hospitals in the state. So far, work at the hospitals at Isuikwuato, Bende, Isiala Ngwa North and Ukwu West have been completed, while the multi-million naira Abia State University Teaching Hospital in Aba has already taken off in full force.
Already, an up-to-date and state of the art medical equipment have been purchased by the state government to be installed in the General Hospitals. Based on its pragmatic performances in the health sector, Abia State has been recently selected as a beneficiary by the World Bank in its Health System Development Grant. By this gesture, Abia State stands to receive well over N200 million from the world body to enable it boost its health-care delivery programmes in the state.
Above all these, Kalu has shown an astute commitment to the cause of the national unity, which infact, has earned him the admiration of many Nigerians across ethnic barriers and partisan politics. Any keen observer of politics would discern that Kalu has truly demonstrated his beliefs that the enormous challenges of development of the Nigerian nation calls for creativity and resourcefulness in generating a change in the well-being of the people by evolving people-oriented initiatives.
Despite the fact that the state-owned Enyimba International Football Club had won the last two editions of the CAF Championship trophy, Kalu has continued to lend his unflinching support to the club, so as to enable it defend the title and retain the cup, as well as to make another significant outing in the on-going GLOBACOM League in the country.
Kalu has rebuilt and upgraded the Aba Township Stadium to an international standard, and work is at top-gear in the rehabilitation of the Umuahia Township Stadium. The state government has floated a new division two club OUK United, which is currently, battling for the top chart position in the year's league.
It could be recalled that Enyimba International Football Club had won the GLOBACOM League for three consecutive years since Kalu became the Abia chief executive. On the other hand, the state handball team, the Valliant, have been vibrant, and are the current Commonwealth champions.
As a major way of consolidating on the encouraging outing of the state contingents at the last national sports festival, the state government has inaugurated a total of 27 sub-committees to further brain-storm on how to improve the various sports. He was recently crowned the Pillar of Sports in Nigeria by the sports writing body, SWAN.
He disclosed that his administration has on inception of office in 1999 inherited a prostrate economy characterized by declining capacity utilization in the real sector, poor performance of major infrastructural facilities, as well as an unsustainable liquidity position arising from unemployment and inflation. He also disclosed that his administration had then met an empty treasury, as well as debt of N3 billion.
According to him, "we would have done more except for the poor allocation from the Federation Account. As I am talking to you, the state government spends about N780 million monthly on wage bills; and yet what we get from the Federation Account can barely meet these obligations, considering the huge debt profile of government.
"Our debt (incurred by past administrations in the then Imo State) is being deducted monthly from our share of the Federal Allocation. And this is responsible for the present financial difficulties we are facing", he stressed, pointing out that "despite these visible obstacles, we have succeeded in delivering quality democracy dividends to our people."
He reiterated his challenge to the nation's leadership to define their humanity through love, service and selflessness.
In attestation to this, President Olusegun Obasanjo had during his official visit to Abia State in 1999 (just within a few months of Kalu's inception of office), declared Kalu as the "Action Governor of Nigeria". The President, who after seeing the spate of development programmes already completed by Kalu's administration, especially on road construction, was stunned by the governor's achievements and enjoined other state governors to learn from the Abia example.
In pursuance of the state government policy of rural transformation, the government has supplied 600 transformers and sank over 800 boreholes in the rural communities of the state. To promote commerce and industry for which the people are noted, government has initiated moves to encourage the growth of cottage, small and medium scale industries in the state.
Kalu's administration has since launched its Small and Medium Enterprises Development Fund (SMEFUND). In the initiative, the state government will contribute one percent of her annual budget to SME development, and also incorporate the private sector operatives in the development of the sector.
Also, governor Kalu has instituted one of the most enduring agricultural policies anywhere in the country. His annual distribution of the latest internationally modified seedlings of various crops to farmers speak volumes of his determination to make the state compare favourably in world agricultural ratings.
The state government has cultivated over 700 hectares of cassava farm in addition to a standard fishery hatchery being constructed at the Ogwe Chicken farms, as well as 10,000 disease resistant banana and pineapple seedlings being distributed to farmers at affordable prices. The state government has also cultivated some 505 hectares of rubber and 6 million palm. Cocoa and Cashew seedlings are currently being distributed to farmers in the state for this year's planting season, amongst others. All these are in realization of the need to restore agriculture as the mainstay of the nation's economy.
Perhaps, one of the most viable and enduring policies ever embarked upon by the Kalu's administration is the introduction of tuition-free education in both the primary and secondary schools in the state for the past four years as well as the work-to-learn progammes for the working adults. Added to these, Kalu had equally introduced a free transport service scheme for students and pupils in the state.
Just last year, the World Bank President, Mr. James Wolfehenson had led a team of officials of the institution to the state to inspect the viability of made-in-Aba products for their possible sponsorship by the World Bank in the world market.
