This, according to Wolfhenson, was because of some encouraging reports received by the World Bank on the economic viability of the state, especially in the areas of small and medium scale enterprises; and the people's unflinching determination to take their destiny into their own hands.
To achieve this, Kalu had on assumption of office as governor of the state, adopted the policy of limiting government's direct participation in economic activities in favour of private sector involvement. He believes that government's basic responsibility is to provide the enabling enrolment for economic growth and social stability, since according to him, "government has no business being in business".
Moreover, a consortium of American investors who visited the state capital, Umuahia recently had announced their choice of the state for citing a N14.5 billion ultra-modern Teaching Hospital. Also, the international brewing giant, Guiness Nigeria Plc. recently opened its N4 billion plant in Aba, the commercial nerve centre of the state.
So far, available records show that other multinationals have indicated their interest to invest in the state because of the existing enabling environment created for economic growth by government.
As a major way of sustaining his promises of transparency in governance, Kalu has since he assumed office published the state government's audited accounts in the nation's newspapers and magazines on quarterly basis. But his opponents have continually accused him of political high-handedness, inaccessibility and financial improprietary in running the state administration.
And only recently, the Code of Conduct Bureau had gone for Kalu's jugular over what it described as the governor's inability to take their advise on the closure of a foreign bank account which Kalu had declared to the office in 1999 and 2003 respectively as part of his assets.
But Kalu himself noted that it was not in the spirit of the constitution for him to close an account he had operated 12 years before he became a governor. He therefore described the attempt by the Code of Conduct to arraign him as "a ruse and legal gamble orchestrated by the powers-that -be to frustrate my presidential ambition in 2007."

Original_Search_Title: The Storm Over Obasanjo's Third Term
Headline: Nigeria: The Storm Over Obasanjo's Third Term
Body_Text:
The third term controversy appears not to be abating.
Everyday, at least a major newspaper in the country carries one story or the other on the issue.
It would seem that both the presidency and its critics appear to be saying the same thing in different ways.
The Presidency says OBJ has no intention to run a third term. The critics say he should convince the world that he has no such intention by stopping all his aides, friends and supporters who clamour for a third term. Mr. President has refused to do that and the critics are saying it's the hand of Esau the but voice of Jacob.
Now we have a third dimension: Could anybody stop the President if he wants to?
Without repeating all the arguments against a third term for Mr President, the issue remains incontrovertible that nobody born of man and woman is indispensable. So our great OBJ cannot be a special breed of human being. The impression those who keep up the campaign for a third term appear to be creating is that the man who occupies the Presidency is so indispensable that the country will collapse, if we don't keep him there for a little while longer.
This country has had such gruelling experiences with its leadership that it is no longer news that our leaders could go anytime. But from our experience also, we know that they don't take the country along with them.
Sir Ahmadu Bello left, the nation did not collapse. Chief Obafemi Awolowo died, Nigeria continued. Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe departed. We mourned the exit of all of them, but that did not keep Nigeria in a state of permanent mourning.
After all our crises, we have since 1999 bumped our way through dictatorship to constitutional democracy and a cardinal aspect of our fledgling democracy which is fundamental to the constitutionality of governance is that the president shall be allowed to run for two terms, if he gets the mandate of the people and shall gracefully leave office at the expiration of his second term.
Nowhere in the constitution is it suggested or implied that the president can under any circumstances whatsoever be permitted to exceed its constitutional mandate. So the issue of a third term ab initio remains a constitutional nullity.
To evade the issue, proponents of a third term want President Olusegun Obasanjo's continuation in of office in order to secure the progress he has made so far with reforms. That is a very tenuous argument indeed.
The issue of OBJ's reforms are not generally acceptable, although nobody can deny that they have affected Nigeria and Nigerians substantially. If anything, they have caused so much pain that whatever beneficial effects derivable from these reforms are unlikely to become generally applicable in the next decade given our experience thus far, 6 years into Obasanjo's administration.
At best, we have heard too many negative progress over the reforms.
Take cassava, for instance, it is our staple food in Nigeria. Fortunately, we found an international market for cassava and the government plunged into it.
Today, the Chinese who are scrambling for Nigerian cassava are using it both industrially and to improve some of their staple food back home (a kind of macarroni). But, in Nigeria, the poor man's food has become too expensive. At the end of the day, it is only the rich farmer who can cultivate thousands of hectares of cassava plantations and the smart middle man who will buy up about 60% of the small scale cassava farmer's product who will be smiling to the bank. Yes, we have made progress with that reform but at this time, we made the lives of our people more miserable. Where then shall we place Jeremy Bentham's dictum that the society progresses only if it can provide for the greatest good of the greatest number.
There are more such negative results which the country is trying to cope with. All that we have seen thus far do not appear to have the moral strength to make anyone seek a third term for OBJ. On the contrary, the president would have made his mark on Nigeria by starting it all.
Take the issue of corruption. No matter that some people may regard the fight on corruption by OBJ as half-hearted, the credit remains that he's the first Nigerian leader to move against corruption in high places.
In 6 years, this is as far as Obasanjo can go. To want to push him to extend his tenure unconstitutional to do more may be pushing the man beyond his limit. But above all, this issue of third term appears to have critical negative prospect for constitutional governance in Nigeria.
Already, the president has become too controversial in his perceived unconstitutionality on many issues which appears also to erode the rule of law and these are not precedents which lovers of democracy will love to continue.
The odds are too much against the idea of a third term for Mr. President, but some people do not appear to recognise this and so the debates continues.

Original_Search_Title: Butare Exports Cassava Flour to France
Headline: Rwanda: Butare Exports Cassava Flour to France
Body_Text:
Butare — Eleven (11) tonnes of cassava flour from Butare province were recently exported to Racines company in Montpellier, France.
According to Jean Claude Bizimana, the director of UNR Outreach Center, working with Pearl project, "Racines company has ordered for more cassava flour from Rwanda and Pearl has been directed to collect, process and pack more cassava flour that will be sold to France in September this year.
"They have ordered for more cassava flour and have also requested us to increase the quantity of tones sent to them. They have appreciated the quality and we also hope to put more emphasis on the increase of cultivation and production of cassava though the growth of the crop has strongly been affected by the mosaic disease," says Bizimana.
The flour would fetch â,B,(B14,300 (Frw9.3m). Pearl buys cassava flour from local farmers especially those who belong to the 14 Associations that are sponsored by the project for enhancing agricultural developments in Rwanda through linkages. Most of these associations are situated in Gikonko and Butare .
A Kg of fresh cassava is bought at between Frw20 to Frw40 from local cassava growers and is sold at â,B,(B1.3/Kg in France.
The cassava is dried and processed by ISAR, the Agriculture Research Institute with help of COVEPAR (valorization and exportation of Rwanda's agriculture products) that operates under Pearl project.
The cassava processing mill is being constructed in Gikonko district and is expected to be completed in September this year.
Cassava ranks fourth in the world in terms of contribution to world carbohydrate provision (it follows sorghum, rice and corn). In Rwanda, only sweet potatoes provide more carbohydrates per capita than cassava. Cassava can be eaten unprocessed, or as a low-fat, high-carbohydrate flour.
Pearl's research findings in the US and Rwanda have identified cassava as an excellent crop for export promotion, as it is extremely producer-friendly. Cassava is resistant to drought, flood, and disease, and can be planted at any time. Moreover, there are established markets for cassava products in Africa, Europe, and the US.

Original_Search_Title: The Caretaker of Hot Springs
Headline: Rwanda: The Caretaker of Hot Springs
Body_Text:
Kigali — With its palm beaches, nice hotels and lakes, Gisenyi has many tourist attractions among them the famous hot springs. Like his clients, Martin Bashakirahe, 65, believes the hot springs cure fatigue and other sorts of ailments. Bashakirahe is keen on his job as a caretaker of the hot springs.
Bashakirahe works with Virunga wildlife clubs of Rwanda. For two years, the hot springs have been the source of his income. Bashakirahe is in charge of security at the hot springs. He receives forty to eighty visitors per day.
Located near BRALIRWA, the hot springs attract many a visitor to Gisenyi. "My existence is attributed to these hot springs," says Bashakirahe. "I cannot go hungry with all these people that frequent this place." His clients include both local and foreign tourists, students and many others.
People neighbouring the hot springs and Bashakirahe himself believe that the hot water in the springs cure fatigue and ailments like flue , bruises and many others.
"I strongly believe in the healing power of this water but many people do not know its importance," says one of the people neighbouring the springs. "Some people bring food stuffs like sweet potatoes, cassava; bananas, eggs, which they drop into the hot water and in a few minutes it is ready for eating.
Bashakirahe at least earns Frw 500 and 1000 a day. He says the wildlife club does not pay him and he only depends on what some visitors to the springs give him. He is also occupied with cleaning the place such that visitors do not find it dirty as it is his source of income, regardless of whatever little he earns.
However, as caretaker of the hot springs, Bashakirahe faces some challenges in his work among them is the presence neighbouring children especially street children who bring a lot of food stuffs to cook or boil and leave then the place very dirty. They do not mind its being a tourist site.
The problem of not being paid by the Virunga wildlife clubs also worries him, language barrier is another problem. He cannot freely communicate with foreign tourists. He does not like it when people come to visit the hot springs and walk away without giving him any coin.
Jealousy and envy from his neighbours in Nyamyumba district is another challenge. Wearing a wildlife identity card has created many enemies than friends, Bashakirahe's neighbours envy him because the card gives him the right to collect and get money from different people that visit the hot springs. He has managed to make few friends who help him chase away people who disguise themselves as caretakers of the hot springs and demand money from visitors.
On a good day, if he gets more money he shares it with his friends. Residents wish and propose that the place should be developed to become an active tourists' destination.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria to Earn $12b From Cassava Export - Waziri
Headline: Nigeria to Earn $12b From Cassava Export - Waziri
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — Minister of Commerce, Ambassador Idris Waziri has disclosed that Nigeria is capable of earning over $12 billion in the exportation of cassava chips and other agro allied products if the agricultural sector is given unreserved attention.
Waziri, who led a Chinese delegation on an investment visit to the Ogun State Governor, Otunba Gbenga Daniel in Abeokuta, said the Obasanjo administration believed that cassava is one of the wonder crops that would make the nation's economy developed in no distant future.
The minister further urged Nigerians to change their attitude by going back to farming for a better and prosperous society.
Waziri, who added that the assessment of the crop outside the country revealed its marketability, disclosed further that over 5,000 metric tonnes of cassava chips from Nigeria were being utilized in China.
"Over 500,000 metric tonnes of palm oil and 3,500 metric tonnes of seismic seeds are also being processed in China based on our findings during our visit to China," the minister said.
He advised that rather than investing solely in the oil sector, diversification of the economy through the promotion of the agricultural sector would attract foreign exchange earnings, create wealth and alleviate poverty among the people.
He described Ogun State as a role model in the promotion of cassava cultivation, pointing out that the Chinese firms were interested in cassava chips and other agricultural products.
While urging Nigerians to embrace the various policies of government, especially in the agricultural sector, Waziri said those policies were to alleviate the suffering of farmers.
In his response, Governor Daniel, who commended the investors from China for their interest, pointed out that the current cassava revolution in the state was in realization of the economic potentials of the crop.
"We are committed to cassava revolution, apart from what the state is doing, I have directed my commissioners, other political functionaries and the people generally to cultivate cassava and let us have it in abundance," Daniel said.
The governor affirmed that one of the dreams of President Obasanjo was to diversify the economy from a monoproduct economy; stressing that promotion of agriculture would stem rural-urban migration and create wealth for the nation.

Original_Search_Title: Malawi Facing Serious Food Crisis
Headline: Malawi Facing Serious Food Crisis
Publisher: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (Rome)
Body_Text:
Rome — Malawi is facing its worst food crisis in more than a decade, the result of a combination of factors, including drought, floods, consecutive poor harvests, endemic poverty and the effects of the HIV/AIDS pandemic, FAO said today.
More than 4.2 million people, or over 34 percent of the population, are unable to meet their food needs. Production of maize, Malawi's most important staple crop, is estimated at nearly 1.3million tonnes this year, the lowest in a decade and around 26percent less than last year's relatively poor harvest.
"Early and above average rains had raised hopes for a good crop, but the rains failed during the critical period from late January to end of February when the maize crop was pollinating and forming cobs," said Tesfai Ghermazien, FAO emergency coordinator in Malawi. "The dry spell also coincided with cassava and sweet potato planting in some areas."
In addition, exceptionally heavy rains in December and early January caused flooding and crop losses, especially in the southern and central part of the country.
"The impacts of the failed harvest won't be felt fully until the lean season sets in between October and April," said Ghermazien. "We need urgent assistance from the donor community to prevent a further escalation of the crisis and to avert widespread hunger and malnutrition, especially among children under the age of five."
Agriculture-driven economy
Agriculture, which is mainly rain fed, is the most important sector of the economy in Malawi, accounting for about 39percent of gross domestic product and employing around 85percent of the workforce. It contributes to more than 90percent of the country's foreign exchange earnings.
Natural disasters
In recent years, there has been an increase in the frequency and severity of natural disasters such as droughts and floods, with serious repercussions on crop production. This year's drought is expected to result in a national cereal gap of between 300000 to 500000 tonnes.
"The challenge is to provide immediate relief supplies to the affected populations and to design long-term recovery strategies to avert similar situations in the future," said Ghermazien. "The promotion of drought-tolerant crops and crop diversification, for example, will help mitigate the impacts of droughts."
HIV/AIDS a major problem
The HIV/AIDS pandemic continues to be a major social and economic problem for the country, with around 15percent of the population estimated to be HIV infected.
The impact on the agricultural sector has been significant, with loss of labour due to death, illness or the diversion of labour to care for the sick sharply affecting production and leaving a large part of the population without adequate food supplies.
Range of interventions needed
"Most of the areas affected by drought or flooding this year were already facing critical food shortages, and many families lost both their crops in the field and their food stores," said Ghermazien. "These households will need food aid, but also agricultural inputs, such as seeds and fertilizers for the next planting season, starting in October."
Assistance is also needed to help vulnerable households broaden their economic base. FAO is promoting crop diversification to reduce reliance on maize, small livestock production, small-scale irrigation and income-generating activities.
Interventions such as the promotion of home gardens and nutrition education for HIV/AIDS-affected households and malnourished children are needed to help improve the health and nutritional status of these most vulnerable groups.
Other proposed activities include the promotion of drought-tolerant crops, such as cassava and sweet potatoes, afforestation in flood-prone areas to improve soil structure, and establishment of fruit tree nurseries and primary school orchards to improve child nutrition.

Original_Search_Title: Udenwa And the New Imo Agenda
Headline: Nigeria: Udenwa And the New Imo Agenda
Body_Text:
The first two years of Governor Achike Udenwa's second term administration has become a revolution as not only has he done much in developing Imo State, he has also made every son and daughter of the state to have a sense of belonging. This is indeed true as all those who left People Democratic Party (PDP) to other parties in the state now have reasons to come back. Some others have decamped from their political parties and joined PDP.
A year ago, the governor promised that Imo State is bound to have a changed landscape given the kind of development measures they had put in place. People did not know what he meant by that statement, but now seeing his achievements, there is no doubt that his second term mission is well understood. Governor Udenwa has vigorously pursued the various initiatives put in place to revitalise and transform the economy of the state and has consolidated the gains he had made in this area, while striving to break new grounds.
In Agriculture, which is one of the significant aspect of the Tripod agenda, the ratification of the state Agricultural Policy and subsequent inauguration of the State Agricultural Council set the stage for the full implementation of the government's set policy. This is aimed at stimulating massive food production and creating a better deal for farmers. The government has made massive input into the production of three important cash crops, namely rice, cassava and oil palm.
Last year farm credits to the tune of N100 million was given to farmers, while more than 35,000 bags of fertilizer were sold at subsidised price. Twenty seven tractors were procured and distributed to the 27 local governments in Imo state. Four rice mills with de-stoners and ten oil mills were established at various locations in the state.
It is also known that the government had earlier leased out Concorde Hotel to a foreign firm which now pay N45 million per annum to Imo State Government. As a result of the measures put in place to stimulate industrialisation, many foreign investors have been visiting Imo State from China, Israel, Poland, Germany, the United States and other places too numerous to mention.Many indigenous organizations like banks and other financial institutions have also started establishing in Imo State. The major drawback the government has is power, that is electricity which is being tackled. .
As it is not only programme of the Tripod Vision the government is pursuing, road network in Imo State also has witnessed a quiet revolution. It will not be long before the difficulty experienced in road travel in Imo state will be a thing of the past.
The State Secretariat Complex which is the best in the country so far was also commissioned by President Obasanjo and the civil servants have moved in without hitch. Facilities at the Sam Mbakwe Airport Owerri have been updated and Imo State University Teaching Hospital has been commissioned while housing estates at Nekede and Umuguma were also either completed or acquired. The Local Government Councils have been persuaded to construct four kilometres of roads each. This will further improve the state's road network
In public utilities, most of the water and electricity projects which was embarked on by the administration have been completed. Transformers, feeder pillars, high tension and low tension wires worth one hundred and fifty-six million, six hundred and ninety-six thousand, five hundred Naira were donated to various communities last year alone. Generators worth N48.6 million were distributed to power various water schemes, while bulk purchase of water treatment chemicals worth N10.6 million were made.
More than 1,896 civil servants have benefitted from government sponsored computer programmes. Five hundred computers were acquired and distributed to ministries while the computerisation of personnel records is almost completed.
The government has continued to pay salaries and allowances of workers and soon all arrears owed to workers in the state will be wiped out. Harmonization of pensions has also been concluded. Recently the state executive of the Nigerian Labour Congress paid a courtesy call on the Governor to thank him for paying their salaries on time and urged him to lift the embargo on promotion.
In the education sector, the state government has continued to rehabilitate and build new classrooms. More teachers have been employed in the core science subjects and teaching aids have been provided.
It will be incomplete if mention is not made on how Governor Udenwa is strengthening the People Democratic Party in Imo State by wooing back those who left the party for other political parties. Recently, at the Dan Anyiam Stadium, Owerri, a reception was held for many big wigs who returned to PDP and those who decamped from other parties to join PDP. Some of those who were received were the President of Manufacturers Association of Nigeria ( MAN ), Chief Charles Ugwu, who left PDP in 2003 for the All Nigeria Peoples Party. Another returnee is Chief Humphrey Anumudu who also left the party in 2003. Also at the welcome party was the former Special Assistant to President Olusegun Obasanjo on Ecology, Chief Martin Agbaso.
Other were Chief Ziggy Azike, a delegate to the just concluded National Political Reform Conference, Dr Ezekiel Izuogu was not present at the ceremony but he sent a representative. Chief Emeka Nwajiuba who also left the party in 2003 to the National Democratic Party was also there.
Other returnees are : Chief Joe Ekechi, a prominent advertiser based in Lagos, Mrs Chiaka Ugworji, Chief Ugoha Ibekwe, Chief Chika Okafor, Chief Elvis Akukwe, Chief Charles Onyeagbako, a former Local Government Chairman, Chief Martin Ogbede-Nwachukwu, Chief Chime Nzeribe, also a former local government chairman and Chief Celestine Uwakwe.
During the reception, Governor Udenwa said that the event marked another phase of the healing process for the party in the state, ahead of the challenges of 2007. He said, with the return of the old members in addition to the decampees , opposition in the state had collapsed. He, however, pleaded for the accommodation of opposition irrespective of the party. He felt that the fresh challenge facing the party was how to manage its success. This shows that Governor Udenwa, who is the leader of the party in the state is also doing very well politically.
Governor Orji Kalu of Abia State who recently visited and toured Imo State stated that those who criticise Governor Udenwa by saying that he is not doing much in Imo State are liars and called on them to visit the state and see things by themselves. Imo State is also blessed with a hardworking First Lady, Nneoma-Nwereugwu Theresa Agbonma Udenwa, who has been through her foundation, Heartland Childcare Foundation given loans and equipment to the less privileged in the society and the rural women. She has been mobilising all the women in the state and making them to be useful to themselves and the society in general.
According to her, she is giving the foundation a good footing so that it will continue when she leaves office as the First Lady of Imo State. The foundation which is being run with donations from individuals and corporate organisations have acquired a permanent site for its office.
In conjunction with the Ministry of Women Affairs and Social Development, the Heartland Foundation has made many women entrepreneurs.
Ministry of Women Affairs and Social Development In the Ministry of Women Affairs and Social Development much has been accomplished in the last two years. According to Mrs Gertrude Ego Oduka, the Hon. Commissioner of the Ministry, the ministry is not for women alone as the name sounds , but is for every living human, that is women, children, disabled, the less privileged, the lunatics or destitute among others. That is it is the ministry for the family. They recently celebrated the family day in Imo State.
She said that her ministry mobilised Imo State women in co-operation with the First Lady Mrs Agbonma Udenwa, who she said has been very helpful. After mobilising the women, they discovered that they had great potentials which could be harnessed for their benefit and those of the society at large by setting up business for them. "We are not talking of the elite group, but the major grassroot women group, the rural women," Mrs Oduka said.
We started by grouping them into organisations and corporate groups. We discovered that we could not empower them individually, so we then brought out one equipment and installed it for them and trained the manpower that will handle it," she added.
This not withstanding, before they give any organisation or cooperative any equipment, they must have studied the area to ensure that it is conducive for the type of business the women will be into. Some of the equipment they gave the women are: garri processing machine, palm kernel cracking machine, palm oil processing machine, cassava pillet machine, tomato grinding machine etc. Some of these machines were distributed last year during the second Imo Women Day.
In terms of political position, the Hon. Commissioner for Women Affairs and Social Development said that, in Imo State women got a fair share in Governor Udenwa's administration; that is why his administration is discribed as being women friendly. She said that there are three women local government chairperson in the state and more than 67 elected councillors and many appointed supervisors.
Some of the projects being handled by the Ministry of Women Affairs and Social Development since the second term of this administration are as follows: Destitute Home (Admin block renovation) at Umunibe Ngor Okpala which cost the Ministry N1,073,386.15 and is already completed, FSP park construction of Lock-up shops and Mbari Street Owerri Children's Parliamentary Building phase I and 2 at Orlu which is being executed at a cost of N9,697,500.75.
Other contracts awarded by the ministry include renovation of Hostel block at destitute home, Umuneke Ngor at a cost of N810,339, drilling of water borehole and construction of overhead water tank at destitute home Umuneke Ngor at N4,929,507, garri processing house at destitute home Umuneke Ngor at a cost of N598,285.80 and renovation of kitchen and dining block at Umuneke Ngor at a cost of N964,132.
The projects also include state Remand Home fencing as wing A, B and C at Logana Ngor Okpala at a cost f N2,824,637.50, Fencing of wing D.E. and F at the same place at cost of N2,874,036.90, renovation of staff quarters and hotel at a cost of N1,200,000. Construction of additional Halls (two in number) at Mberi Street, Owerri at a cost of N20,291,404.75 and Boys Approved School construction and fencing at Arondizuogu, Ideato North Local Government at a cost of N8.0m. Others are renovation of workshop/kitchen block at Arondizuogu Ideato, LGA at a cost of N1.9m. Hostel Block 1 reconstruction of culvert at Arondizuogu at a cost of N413,529.8.

Original_Search_Title: Kwara Appoints Youth Farm Manager
Headline: Nigeria: Kwara Appoints Youth Farm Manager
Body_Text:
Lagos — Few months after the successful take-off of the commercial farming initiative in Kwara State, the state government has appointed one of the pioneered commercial farmers under the initiative, Colin Spain, as Farm Manager for the Youth Integrated Farm Settlement at Molete.
Besides, government has also pledged to provide the farm with modern facilities to enhance commercial farming in the state.
The governor's Special Assistant on Youth Empowerment, Barrister Muideen Jimba, disclosed this in Ilorin during an interaction with the new manager and participants at the farm settlement.
Jimba disclosed that the over 1,000 hectares of farm land and the abandoned facilities at the farm site would be rehabilitated and put to use to empower the youth farmers.
He said the farm, which has the capacity to accommodate about 200 youths, would start with 100 farmers in the pilot scheme, and advised them to remain disciplined and dedicated to ensure the success of the project.
According to him, the Molete Youth Farm Settlement, which last year planted maize and cassava, would be developed to encourage more unemployed youths to embrace commercial farming rather than looking for white collar jobs that are not readily available.
Jimba, who said vandalised NEPA cables in the area would be replaced to restore its electricity supply, disclosed that arrangement is almost completed to introduce Global System of Mobile Communication (GSM) to the area for easy access to the outside world.
He advised other unemployed youths in the state to embrace farming, and pleaded with the participants to give maximum cooperation and support to the new farm manager.
Addressing the participants, Spain, who said he has 32 years experience in commercial farming, assured that he would assist the youths to make a living from farming.
The farm manager said the participants would be trained in all aspects of farming and advised them to give him maximum cooperation and take attendance seriously.
Spain expressed appreciation to the state government for the opportunity granted him and reminded the participants to consider any assistance from the state government as a soft loan which has to be repaid.
The official planting of late maize for this cropping season would commence on the farm Thursday under the supervision of the manager. Other crops expected to be planted later include rice, soyabean, tomato and mango.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Warns On Cassava Virus
Headline: Uganda: Govt Warns On Cassava Virus
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Ministry of Agriculture has warned of an outbreak of a new cassava disease in Uganda.
The commissioner for crop production, Kamayombi Bulegeya, identified it as Cassava Brown Streak Virus (CBSV).
"CBSV causes rotting at advanced stages," Bulegeya said on Friday.
He said the disease is characterised by patches of yellow mixed with the normal green colour on the leaves.
"The yellow patches are more prominent on mature leaves," he said.
Bulegeya said the stem of an affected crop develops dark brown streaks with dead spots or leaf scars.
"These streaks are most prominent on the upper green portions of the stem," he said.
Bulegeya said the disease also distorted the shape and storage roots.
"It may cause cracks and discolouration of the storage roots and rotting of the tubers in advanced stages of infection," he said. Citing ministry reports, Bulegeya said the disease had broken out in Mbale, Mukono and Soroti districts.
"In Arapai and Tubur in Soroti, authorities have imposed a quarantine on the movement of planting materials," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Commerce Manager Tasks Research Institutes on Improvement of Cassava Production
Headline: Nigeria: Commerce Manager Tasks Research Institutes on Improvement of Cassava Production
Body_Text:
Lagos — Commerce Minister, Ambassador Idris Waziri, Saturday, in Abeokuta spoke of the desirability of the various research institutes in the country to ensure that they go into real researches in how to produce more cassava per hectare than the current quantity that Nigeria gets per hectare.
Amb. Waziri, at the head of a three-man Chinese Trade visit to Nigeria, who visited Abeokuta as part of their visit also stressed that it was very important that Nigeria quadrupled its current production of cassava to meet demand.
He said the target should be 150 million metric tonnes per annum, adding: "If we are really to satisfy both internal and external demand for cassava and cassava products, then it is imperative that a synergy be created between the research institutes and grassroots farmers to ensure that research findings are made available to local the farmers." The minister advised that "Nigeria has to go into mechanisation of cassava production, carry many people as possible to go into cassava production and apply the best agronomy practices in cassava production to ensure that we have a lot of cassava produced."
Earlier at an interactive session with with various cassava farmers'cooperative societies in Abeokuta, the Ogun State capital, Amb. Waziri said all hands must be on deck for Nigeria to achieve its targeted output of 150 million tonnes of cassava per annum as against its current quantum of between 34 and 36 million tonnes, advising the farmers to work hard to improve on the production capacity of the country. He told the farmers that there was huge market for their products in China, saying also that "currently, Nigeria's production of between 34 - 36 millions metric tonnes of cassava per annum, goes for consumption. But right from last year till date when cassava has not been commercialised, we have been able to sensitise the farmers to invest in agriculture production, processing and marketing."
The minister added that his "committee has been able to sensitise and commercialise cassava and as result of this, there is now a high demand on cassava. There is very high demand on cassava for human consumption, animal feeds and industrial application.
And on a courtesy visit to the Ogun State Governor, Chief Gbenga Daniel, the minister speaking on the various agricultural commodity initiatives of President Olusegun Obasanjo, said one of the initiatives of the President is the Presidential Initiative on Cassava. He explained that the President believed that cassava is one of those wonder crops that can change the economy of the country. He noted that when he was appointed last year, as the Chairman of the Presidential Committee on Cassava Export Promotion, he stated his committee went into action immediately and engaged on tour outside Nigeria to see how to market cassava throughout the world.
He explained that his delegation to China last year was able to procure export orders for 500,000 metric tonnes of cassava chips, 500,000 metric tonnes of refined palm-oil and 3,500 metric tonnes of sesame seeds from China for Nigeria, adding that China with a population of about 1.5 billion people was a huge market for Nigeria in agricultural commodities.
Also addressing the farmers, leader of the leader of the Chinese delegation, Mr. Luo Ming, said they were in the in the country to assess its capacity and readiness to supply cassava chips and pellets to the Chinese, telling the farmers that they represented Genertec International Corporation of China. Ming added that they were attracted to Nigeria by President Olusegun Obasanjo's call on the Chinese to come and invest in Nigeria.
On the minister's delegation were Mrs. Modupe Sasore, Special Assistant to the President on Export Promotion and CEO of NEPC; George Gang, General Director, China Investment and Trade Promotion Centre in Nigeria; Chen Yao, Project Manager, Genertec International Corporations, Beijing; China and Luo Ming, General Manager, Genertec International Corporation, Beijing, China.
Others were Badeji Abikoye, Trade Commissioner, Nigeria Trade Office, Shanghai, China and Mrs. Ronke Daniel, Chairman/Managing Director, Ladmoke Company Limited, the first company that exported the first shipment of 50 metric tonnes of cassava chips to China early this year.
Genertec International Corporation, Beijing, China were the people that took the trial order of the 50 metric tonnes of cassava from Ladmok Company Limited. The Trade delegation went round various farmers' cassava processing complex in Abeokuta and Ilaro and terminated at the Obasanjo Farm, Ota. The visit that will move to Kwara and Nasarawa is expected to last for 10 days.

Original_Search_Title: Economy: Provision of Tractors: Imperative for Cassava And Rice Initiatives
Headline: Nigeria: Economy: Provision of Tractors: Imperative for Cassava And Rice Initiatives
Body_Text:
The International Institute for Tropical Agriculture recently strongly recommended the use of a hand-pushed power tiller for small and medium scale farmers to reduce cost of cassava production in Nigeria. This as reported in "This Day" of March 29, 2005 was the outcome of a one -- day meeting between Directors of the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Scientists at the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture (IITA) Onne station in Rivers State.
This is a cheering news and worthy of commendation in view of governments' efforts at eradication of poverty through food sufficiency. Worthy of particular mention is the Federal Government initiative on cassava production.
At the end of the meeting, the Head of Unit, Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development Port Harcourt, was quoted as having said; "We were at IITA to know the best mechanization methods to recommend for small and medium scale cassava production in Nigeria". The participants at the meeting according to the report discussed ways of making cassava production less cumbersome and more lucrative for Nigerian farmers. The officer in charge of IITA -- Onne station according to the report said; "now that the Nigerian government is providing the political will to support large scale cassava production in the country, it is pertinent to examine the best cost-effective mechanization options that will enable the farmers optimize production without Destroying their land and yet deriving the best possible yields from their crops.
Cassava production is labour intensive. No single farmer can profitably plant one hectare of cassava using the back breaking manual labour".
The meeting, assertions of the participants and recommendation could not have come at better time than now when it has become obvious that the way out of the mess in which the agricultural sector has been plunged is "agricultural renaissance" through mechanized agriculture. This means a renewed interest and a refocused attention to not just agriculture but mechanized agriculture which machinery and implements must be readily available and at the same time affordable.
The importance of agriculture and food sufficiency to many countries of the world including Nigeria cannot be over emphasized. It provides food for a nation's populace and raw materials for her industries amongst others.
Nigeria during the independence days was an agricultural giant, but the sector gradually came to be visited with criminal neglect and abandonment resulting in hunger, crime and other woes in our country today. There is no doubt that hunger, starvation and wretched standard of living are the major motivation for the reign of violence, terror and crime that has bedeviled this country in recent times.
It is worthy of note that no country that is unable to feed its populace can be said to be developed irrespective of its achievements in other areas. There can be no peace, no law and order when people are hungry; when the streets are "adorned" with the pitiable and sorry sight of wretched and haggard looking creatures who are easily prone to violence and crime because as the saying goes, a hungry man is indisputably an angry man.
Research informs us that the bane of agriculture in Nigeria today is the hoe and cutlass method of production. It is regrettable, that at this time when the agricultural sector has been revolutionized by scientific and technological inventions, our people still "march on" with human sweat aided by the hoe and cutlass in addition to other obsolete and antiquated methods of agricultural production. This has left us in the dark and progressively retarded the growth of the sector.
It is this "hoe and matchet agriculture" that has forced our youngsters to abandon agriculture in search of the non-existent and elusive white collar jobs in the cities. And faced with the non-existence of such jobs, they become miscreants and desperadoes constituting societal nuisance.
It is against this backdrop that the recommendation of the Onne meeting becomes germane. The provision of Power tractors which are now being proudly manufactured locally by Betcy Industries for small and medium scale farmers will go a long way in reducing the cost of producing not only cassava but other crops thereby ensuring food sufficiency. The power tractor is the ideal equipment for small and medium scale farmers because it is not expensive coupled with its portability. It is reliably gathered that each goes for less than N1m.
To ensure the accelerated and unimpeded availability and delivery of the aforesaid equipment to the doorsteps of these farmers, it is hereby recommended that governments at all levels, enter into partnerships with this local industry which manufactures/assembles these brand of tractors and other implements/machinery for mechanized agriculture. This will serve as a boost and incentive to our local industries and encourage them in their contributions to national development inspite of the hostile environment in which they operate. It will also make available, affordable machinery to the farmers in view of the fact that majority of them are subsistence farmers who may not be able to afford the cost unaided nor go for them unless they are brought nearer to their doorsteps.
It is hoped that efforts at various levels to move agriculture forward will be sustained through the provision of workable machinery and implements to small and medium scale farmers who form the hub of agriculture in this county. The local industry which manufactures/assembles these equipment must be encouraged too. It is only by so doing that we can bring the lost glory of agriculture in this country. And the time to do this is now.

Original_Search_Title: Fruit Seedlings Distributed to Farmers
Headline: Ethiopia: Fruit Seedlings Distributed to Farmers
Body_Text:
Dessie — Some 12.8 million fruit seedlings have been distributed to farmers in 16
woredas of South Wollo Zone of the Amhara State, the zonal agriculture and
rural development branch office disclosed.
Natural resources team leader with the office, Abraham Tsegaye told WIC
that the seedlings nurtured in nurseries owned by the government,
non-governmental organizations and farmers' associations were distributed to
farmers living in areas suitable for horticultural development.
Professional assistances have also been rendered to farmers engaged in
the transplantation of the seedlings, he said.
According to Abraham, as the farmers were not market-oriented they had
difficulty in becoming food self-sufficient. The schemes would however help
them to improve their livelihood.
Among the seedlings distributed are oranges, avocadoes, grapes as well
as pieces of cassavas and sweet potatoes, he said

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria, China Trade Hits $2 billion
Headline: Nigeria, China Trade Hits $2 billion
Body_Text:
Abuja — Trade transaction between Nigeria and the People's Republic of China in
2004 was over $2 billion.
A breakdown of the trade figure showed that total Chinese imports from
Nigeria was $1.42 billion while Nigerian exports to the Asian country was
$458 million. The figure for the Nigerian export, however, was for the
non-oil sector only.
Prince Badeji Abikoye, Nigeria's trade commissioner in Shanghai, China,
revealed this yesterday when a Chinese trade delegation visited Commerce
Minister, Ambassador Idris Waziri, in his office in Abuja.
Abikoye said that trade between the two countries traditionally involved
trade surplus in favour of China, but for the first time, Nigeria recorded a
favourable trade with the Asian country in 2004.
According to him, trade prospect between the two countries remained high
for 2005, especially with growing requests from China in the solid mineral
sector and agricultural sector in Nigeria particularly cassava products and
cocoa beans.
Ambassador Waziri, who received officials of Genertec International,
said that the improved term of trade for Nigeria came as a result of the
export drive of his ministry. Nigeria could capture the large Chinese
market, he added, but that "the problem is our capacity."
The minister, who is the chairman of the Presidential Committee on
Cassava Initiative, said that cassava products will earn more for Nigeria
than oil when Nigerians learned to take full advantage of the massive
potentials of the growing sector. Mr. Elvis Emechetta, the president of the
Nigeria-Chinese Chamber of Commerce, represented the private sector at the
forum.
The leader of the Chinese delegation, Mr. Luo Ming, suggested that
Nigerian exports should be of the right standard. He noted that cocoa beans
from Ghana were of the right standard, adding the export of cocoa beans is
being dominated by European businessmen and that the Chinese would like to
trade directly with Nigerians.
Last week, Waziri took the Chinese businessmen on a tour of cocoa farms
and facilities in the south west. The Chinese are also into large-scale
farming and the procurement of machinery. Genertec the biggest exporter of
cocoa beans in China being privatised, was on a two-week exploratory visit
to Nigeria. Apart from cassava, the company interested in other agricultural
produce such as cocoa beans, soya beans and sesame seeds.

Original_Search_Title: In Ebonyi, Land Tears Communities Apart
Headline: Nigeria: In Ebonyi, Land Tears Communities Apart
Body_Text:
Lagos —  The people of Ikwo South Local Government of Ebonyi State are steeped in
a communal land tussle because the property from their forbearers seems to
have been shared to some individuals without due process. Agha Ibiam who
visited the warring communities writes
For a visitor in Ikwo South Local Government Area of Ebonyi State, one
can take away or seize whatever belongings of any citizen of the
communities, but no attempt should be made to either forcefully or
unlawfully take away their land. Arable land is the only source of the
economic and social wellbeing and survival. It is therefore no wonder that
this God-given wealth and one of the treasured factors of production has
caused so much communal clashes within the communities in recent times.
Recently, the people of Izzi in Ebonyi State and Yala community in Cross
River State were locked in a clash over land matters. Lives and properties
worth millions of naira were lost in that incident. But the most agonising
aspect was a clash between communities of the same descent over a piece of
land between Ndiagu and Ndufu Amagu communities alleged to have been
allocated to individuals of Ndiagu Amagu by the Chairman of Ikwo South Local
Government Council, Mrs. Chinyere Ochere without due process.
The genesis of a recent problem in Ikwo started when the chairman of the
council went to press to announce that all land seized for political reasons
in Ikwo South Local Government Council be released to their owners. "It has
come to my notice that during the 2003 electoral campaigns and elections in
Ikwo South, especially in Ndiagu Amagu autonomous community, portions of
land belonging to some people were seized from them on political
reasons".
She continued: "All such portions of land are hereby released to their
original rightful owners. For the avoidance of doubts, the following
portions of land wrongfully seized from the owners are hereby released to
them. Namely, the Agbo Ndiagu Amagu, World Bank land-Ndiagu Amagu, Azu-farm
land-Ndiagu Amagu.
"In the interest of peace, all forms of development in the form of
illegal buildings or structures by any trespassers are hereby stopped. All
crops such as rice, cassava, yam, planted on the affected portions of land
must be harvested by the farmers who planted them. In the interest of peace,
the original and rightful owners of the land must not harvest the crops as
this would be viewed as stealing.
"All traditional rulers in Ikwo South Local Government Area are hereby
directed to ensure that all their subjects comply strictly without fail to
those orders made in the interest of peace", Ochere announced on a local
radio in Ebonyi State.
However, the questions the people of Ikwo, even the traditional leaders
she authorised to maintain compliance asked were: who sent her, does she
know the origin of the land, who did she consult, what's her motive and for
whose interest or was she just playing to the gallery?
Which ever way the decisions of the chairman is looked at, the
communities and other observers believe that there is political undertone to
what she did. They claimed that it was an attempt to destabilise the sleepy
community against 2007 election. Though Ochere was not in the office when
THISDAY visited, it was gathered that the woman appears to be unconcerned
about the suffering masses, some have been internally displaced as a result
of the clashes there over the land which pitched a group of young men called
the Odighi-eshi boys (Area Boys) and one of the community's she had wished
the land to.
It was gathered that Ochere has openly told those who may care to listen
that there is no crises in Ikwo South, rather some politicians are raising
false alarm that people of Ndiagu Amagu want to make trouble with them. But
the Secretary to the Local Government, Mrs. Grace Nwogwu told THISDAY that
"there is problem in the local government, but I don't have details about
it".
However, from THISDAY's observation in Oronga community, there were some
internally displaced people loitering about. There was also the case of
Chief Nwafogu Ochere who is in search of his children. It was gathered that
the man has about 42 children with about eight wives. There was also a case
of a helpless woman with triplets who has resigned her predicament to
fate.
In a letter to Governor Sam Egwu of Ebonyi State by the first state vice
chairman of Council of Traditional Rulers in Ebonyi State, HRH Eze Dominic
Aloh and signed by 12 traditional leaders of the community, which was a
response to the statement credited to the Ikwo South council chairman stated
that, "We wish to place on record that we the people of Amagu (Ndufu/Ndiagu)
autonomous communities in Ikwo South are not aware of any land or piece of
land seized by person or persons in any part of Amagu for any reasons.
"It is however on record that in the olden days when the people of Ikwo
clan were expanding southwards towards the Cross River directions of Awkum,
Oshopong, Igbo and other Mbembe speaking communities, who later went to
Cross River in the old Obubra and Afikpo Divisions, the land which the
original inhabitants had abandoned. Amagu people have been using the said
land, communally and at certain times, allowed the seven villages of Ndiagu
Amagu community to be caretakers of the said land called "Egi-Igbo".
"It will be recalled that when Amagu people realised in 1994 that the
said land had become a source of incessant unrest to the Ndiagu Amagu
villages, particularly Ndieke village, the people of Amagu decided to take
back their said land and establish the 21 village of Amagu (Ndufu/Ndiagu)
autonomous community as it then was, to be known and called "Ohaukwu or
Ndiechi".
The traditional leaders also reminded the governor that, "the caretaker
villages of Ndiagu Amagu begged Amagu people to allow them the use of the
said land for two more years, that till 1996. This request was granted by
Amagu people and for which the caretaker villages showed their
appreciation. It will also be recalled that at the relevant time, the
National Chairman of Amagu Development Authority and leader of Amagu people
when the said land was taken possession of in 1994 is the husband of the
executive chairman, Mr. E. N. Ochere, who signed all the relevant documents
of the take over of the land by Amagu people.
"It would also be recalled that sometime in 2002 when the Amagu
(Ndufu/Ndiagu) Development Authority entered the land to allot homesteads to
the representatives of the respective 23 villages from eight Amagu Ndufu
villages and 15 Amagu Ndiagu autonomous communities filed a suit in the High
Court claiming ownership of the said land. The action was instituted against
the two traditional rulers of Amagu (Ndufu/Ndiagu) autonomous communities
and eight other leaders of Amagu," the traditional rulers emphasised.
The traditional leaders, prayed the Governor to order a high powered
investigations into the provocative incitement and false publications,
announcements and directives of the chairman of Ikwo South. Copies of that
letter were also circulated to some very important personalities of Ebonyi
State, including the police and made available to THISDAY.
When THISDAY called at Ebonyi Government House, the Chief Press
Secretary (CPS) to the Governor, Mr. Onyekachi Eni also acknowledged that
the problem in Ikwo South was basically a communal dispute that has lingered
for sometime. He told THISDAY that there are other ancillary issues that
come to compound the misunderstanding overtime.
The position of government according to him was that by the nature of
Governor, he is always disposed to ensuring that there is peace in the state
and also ensuring that the matter is settled amicably which must be based on
truth, facts and not on interest groups. The CPS said it was in
consideration of that and in pursuit of truth, justice and peace that the
State Government set up a panel of inquiry into the matter, which is headed
by former Acting Chief Judge of Ebonyi State, Justice Paul Obi Elechi.
"They have conducted public sitting about the area. The report has not
been submitted to the government and I do hope that when it is submitted,
government will study the recommendation and find a way of implementing it
to ensure that there is peace in the area. The Commissioner of Police is
also aware of the security situation in the place He has been sending
policemen occasionally to the place", he said.
Observers have however accused the police of taking sides with the
chairman of the council. They said in many instances police have been
seriously attacked, matcheted and even their riffles seized by the
Odighi-eshi boys working for the council boss without arrest. Again, when
THISDAY called at the office of the Police Public Relation Officer,
Mr. Chijioke Nwokoma, it took a lot from him to acknowledge that actually
there is problem in Ikwo South.
It was when he was asked if some riffles were seized from some policemen
that went to keep surveillance that he unwittingly accepted. "Yes, some
policemen were attacked while on routine patrol at Ikwo. We usually have a
unit of mobile policemen on standby for every situation, especially in that
area where there is always land dispute. So our Area Command was moved to
quell the situation. Arms seized from them have been recovered", he
explained.
It was also gathered that since the problem started, no arrest of any
sort has been made. Both the Odighi-eshi boys, who it was told have taken
laws into their hands, go about unmolested because of the cover they receive
from Mrs. Ochere.
At Oronga, Nze Njoku Alogbu who spoke on behalf of the traditional ruler
of the area said the displaced people--who milled around--are from Ndieke,
Enyeghara-Igwe, Okworike, Umuege and Umuomara and were chased way by the
people of Ndiagu Amagu, especially by the Odighi-eshi groups due to the land
tussle at Eghibo.
"On May 27, when we were returning from Abakaliki, after testifying to
the panel, the opponents of peace laid ambush against some people, beat them
up, and seized their bicycles and motorcycles. When the case was reported to
the Divisional Police Officer, he sent some two policemen. At Ndieke, the
people called Odighi-eshi boys, got hold of them and beat them
thoroughly.
"We waited to see when those boys will be arrested, but nothing happened
till date. Later, some mobile policemen were sent to investigate the
assault. The policemen reported to Mrs. Ochere, telling her that they were
sent to arrest the boys. She sent her special assistant to inform the boys
that some policemen were coming to arrest them and that they should be
prepared and make sure that none of them be arrested.
"Unfortunately, when the mobile policemen reached there, the laid
ambush, surrounded them, beat them up and seized their riffles. Again
nothing happened. The Odighi-eshi boys have now become law on their
own. They have chased people away from their homes and destroyed property,
including farm produce.
The internally displaced people now live from hand to mouth. If they
don't borrow, they won't eat", Nze Njoku said.
He urged the state government to arrest the boys who are perpetrating
the evil and are shielded by Mrs. Ochere. He believed that if the boys are
arrested, peace would return in the area. The seven villages in Ndiagu Amagu
that was asked to take custody of the said land due to its closeness to the
property, include, Ndieke, Ubeghe-Omege, Okwa-Eyum, Enyaghara-Igwe,
Okworike, Umuomara and Ndinkashi.
Though lives have not been lost in the said land dispute in Ikwo South,
observers said that there is need for the state government to restore peace
and call the chairman of the council, whom they believe has been fanning the
ambers of crisis to order.

Original_Search_Title: IITA Moves to Improve Cassava Production in Ughelli
Headline: Nigeria: IITA Moves to Improve Cassava Production in Ughelli
Body_Text:
Lagos — Farmers and investors in Ughelli, Delta State, were the target of
International Institute of Tropical Agriculture's (IITA's) capacity building
effort in cassava production and processing.
Prof. Malachy Akoroda, an agronomist with the IITA Integrated Cassava
Project (ICP), stated this while addressing 56 members of the Olomu Better
Life Farmers Multipurpose Cooperative Society (FMPC) in Ughelli at a
three-day training programme on cassava root production and rapid
multiplication of stems.
He said that cassava production is a value creation business that must
taken advantage of by Nigerians.
"Cassava production business is not like any other distributive trade
where you collect finished products and sell on credit to retailers. Rather,
it is a business that requires careful planning, timeliness, calculations
and budgeting. The success of cassava business depends largely on the
availability of fresh cassava roots, stems and leaves. Hence, the need for
aggressive training programs embarked upon by the International Institute of
Tropical Agriculture (IITA) on rapid multiplication of cassava to ensure the
availability of quality planting materials needed by many growers," he said
Akoroda said there are some basic steps the cooperative society must
take to embark on large scale cassava production. These include selection of
a large piece of land for farming, effective land preparation without
destroying the top soil, sourcing of improved cassava planting materials,
sourcing of farm inputs: fertilizers and agrochemicals. "These inputs must
be purchased well in advance before the beginning of farm operations", said
Professor Akoroda. In addition, members must have good knowledge of weed
control, farm management techniques, and post harvest processing, storage,
marketing and export. He said farm financial records must be properly kept
to control unnecessary expenses and ensure good delivery of work on the
farm.
On farm management techniques for instance, Professor Akoroda explained
that supervision is the "watch word". He said in a situation where the farm
workers are not properly supervised the farm business will become bankrupt,
adding that there must be labor control, planning of daily work schedule in
the farm, security of the farm, records and book keeping and general human,
material and financial management. He said for Nigeria to reap the benefits
of large scale cassava production, there must be the right infrastructure
including private tractor hire services, good farm mechanization knowledge,
rural road networks and private cassava processing centers run strictly as
business enterprises.
Akoroda congratulated the Olomu FMPC for their vision, industry and
perseverance in their drive to embark on large scale cassava production for
export. The Cooperative Society which was established in 1990, is an
umbrella body for 15 other smaller cooperative associations at the Ughelli
Local Government Area. The association currently maintains eleven hectares
cassava farm, seven fish large ponds and 15 oil palm processing
enterprises.
Also speaking on the occasion, Mr. Vincent Nwafor, Rivers State Station
Manager for the Cassava Enterprise Development Project (CEDP), explained
that good land preparation is essential for sustainable cassava farming. He
remarked that ridging is not required in most of Delta State, because of the
high rainfall in the area, adding that plough and harrow are ideal to avoid
erosion. He added that adequate dose of NPK fertilizer should be applied to
maintain soil fertility.
Godspower Osuwa, Senior Research Supervisor explained herbicide weed
control procedures, and rapid multiplication by direct planting of
pre-sprouted 3-4 node stakes at 1 by 0.5 m spacing on un-ridged
land. Mr. Azubuike Onoje demonstrated the 24 steps for better handling of
cassava stems for achieving high cassava yields.
The Chairperson of the Olomu FMPC, Chief (Mrs) Esther Whegbere said
members of the cooperative society have their individual farms. Mrs Whegbere
who is also the Chairperson of the Delta State Rice Farmers Association of
Nigeria (RIFAN) remarked that the Cooperative body was interested in cassava
export, adding that the training was also aimed at empowering members on
cassava production to generate income and improve their living
conditions.
There was a practical demonstration of some farm tools including the
power tiller, motorized cassava cutting machine and secateur, while a truck
load of IITA improved cassava stems were given to the people for
multiplication against next year.
Earlier at the opening ceremony, the Chairman of Ugheli North Local
Government Hon. Ediri O. Idisi remarked that food is most essential to
man. He said both the rich and the poor need food for survival. He commended
IITA for coming up with improved food crop production technologies for the
benefit of Nigerian cassava farmers. The Chairman urged the Olomu FMPC
members to take the training seriously to improve their productivity.
The high point of the training program was a field visit to some of the
cooperative and individual farms. Members defied the rains to get involved
in land marking, measurement, cassava stem cutting, and planting. At the
farm they were able to note series of "small errors" in field work often
made by farmers that prevent maximum cassava stem and root yields. They were
able to note for instance, that those cassava cuttings that do not germinate
after planting, add to the farmer's loss and should be checked and replaced
within the first four weeks. The cooperative members were also able to
recognize that a stem of 25 cm long, containing 5-7 nodes is the ideal
length of cassava stem for planting when root production is targeted.
In the 32 hectare cassava farm of Chief Raphael Emecho, members were
overwhelmed when they saw a near ideal commercial farm. Established in 2000,
the farm has four different blocks. It is an integrated farm, containing two
fish ponds, and a gari processing center. A Quantity Surveyor by profession,
Emecho said he started farming on a small scale in 1990. The crops are
planted in straight rows, with good access roads running round the farm. He
keeps a good record of all expenses on the enterprise. He currently exports
his gari to the UK, adding that he is planning to increase the farm to one
hundred hectares in the near future.
Emecho remarked that he has 10 permanent workers and many more casual
laborers on the farm, but that he virtually lives on the farm to prevent
frequent pilfering of farm products which could result in serious financial
loss to him. According to participants, the overall assessment of the
training was excellent, because it was practical, participatory and
demonstrative.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava 'Ll Give Nigeria $12bn Annually - Minister
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava 'Ll Give Nigeria $12bn Annually - Minister
Body_Text:
Acting — COMMERCE Minister, Amb. Idris Waziri, has disclosed that Nigeria could realise $12 billion foreign exchange annually from the exportation of garri and other Cassava derivatives.
He disclosed this just as he canvassed for a return to agriculture by Nigerians, saying the sector holds the ace to the nation's stability as well as rapid socio-economic development.
In an interview with newsmen at the weekend in Lagos, Amb. Waziri, who is also chairman of the Presidential Initiative on Cassava, pointed out that rather than rely on crude oil as the major source of foreign exchange as is currently the case, a quick return to agriculture would change that trend.
Calling for an urgent change in our present attitude towards agriculture, he attributed the nation's socio-economic and political instability on desperation by leaders to share from crude oil revenue.
Said he: "Agriculture is the only hope for this country whether we like it or not because from agriculture you can get a lot of products from agro-allied industries. In cassava alone, we can get over 1000 derivatives and each of them can be an industry.
"So, if the cassava initiative is properly implemented the way we want it, Nigeria can earn over $12 billion per annum.
"How much do we earn from oil? And this is causing problems for us in this country including political instability, social instability, economic instability and corruption.
"Everybody wants to be in office because of oil money and we are all sitting on goldmine...So I am encouraging you, my brothers and sisters, that the time has come for us to change our attitude and orientation and go back to land because that is where money is."
Ambassador Waziri, who led a three-man Chinese delegation on a tour of some cassava farms in Ogun State, said apart from having the potential of becoming the nation's number one foreign exchange earner, agriculture would create employment and stem rural-urban drift.
Earlier, at an interactive session with cassava growers at Abeokuta, the Ogun State capital, the minister said all hands must be on deck for the country to achieve its targeted output of 150 metric tonnes of cassava per annum as against the current quantum of between 34 and 36 million tonnes.
According to him, attaining the output would enable Nigeria meet its local requirements and for export.
Also addressing the farmers, leader of the Chinese delegation, Mr. Luo Ming, said they were in the country to assess its capacity and readiness to supply cassava products to the Chinese market.
He said they are representatives of Genetic International Corporation of China, adding that they were attracted to Nigeria by President Olusegun Obasanjo's call on the Chinese to come and invest in the country.
Earlier, Chairman of Ogun State chapter of Cassava Growers Association of Nigeria, Chief Muritala Abimbola Igbalaiye, called on the federal and state governments to assist his members with soft loans and equipment.
The delegation also met with President Obasanjo in his Ota farm and Governor Gbenga Daniel of Ogun State.

Original_Search_Title: Starving the Nation
Headline: Nigeria: Starving the Nation
Body_Text:
MPs to look into rising costs of foodstuffs' 'Garri no more the poor
man's food' 'Local production of rice in steady decline', are some of the
recent headlines which tell us that food, a basic necessity in the human
life, has become quite a struggle for the majority of Nigerians. This is
extremely sad, and it emphasizes how successive governments have failed to
help the average Nigerian to have a better life.
Our country is blessed with good soil across the nation which should
produce the various staple foods.
Not so long ago, there was an abundant production of regional
foodstuffs. From the South to the Middle Belt, we got as major foodstuffs,
plantains, palm oil, cassava, yams, rice, a variety of beans, bush meat,
various types of snails and fish and lots of vegetables and seasonal
fruits. The poor man's staple food then from these areas were mainly
plantain, maize and cassava in its varied forms. From the core North, we got
rice, black-eyed beans, millet, guinea corn, yams, sweet potatoes, groundnut
oil, guinea fowls, and beef. The poor man's food in this area then were
sweet potatoes, cassava, millet and guinea corn meals, sugar cane and
groundnut. All over the country, protein in the form of beans and meat of
various types, was available. Those who couldn't afford to buy beef from the
market, could go to the bush to lay traps for small animals, collect snails,
etc. to cook soup. There was no malnutrition of any form in the
country. People may not always have what their hearts desired to eat, but
there was no starvation.
If you had no food, you could go into the forest and gather plantain,
cocoyam, cassava and palm products which were growing wild, with no specific
owners. Same thing with fruits and vegetables. Snails and small animals were
there free for your soup. A hungry family then was a lazy family. Most
families, even the town-dwellers, were linked to one farmland or the other,
and thus, they had steady supply of some foodstuffs. Other food items that
you didn't produce, you got cheaply. When civil servants retired in those
days, many of them retired to their home towns or villages to farm.
Gradually, over the years, our leaders have tightened the noose which
killed the incentive to farm meaningfully. Take the case of fertilizers and
poultry and animal feeds. The government sensitized the farmers to the need
of using these chemicals and feeds to boost the growth and production of
farm crops, animals, poultry, fish, etc., but people complain they played
politics with them, making them available only to card- carrying political
party members, some of whom went to sell at high prices, what was meant to
be given out free, or at reduced costs. The result, coupled with the
perennial shortage of farmlands, due to pollution, etc, was that farmers and
rearers who had no easy access to them, lost interest in farming, and there
began a steady migration of young people to the cities to look for jobs and
business opportunities. With the decline of small time farmers, came
escalating prices of local foodstuffs as there wasn't enough produced to
feed the community. Villagers took their products to the cities and large
towns where they would get good prices for them.
I was surprised when a friend who spent last Christmas in her village in
the East, told me that foodstuffs are cheaper in Lagos than there. She had
bought rice, vegetable oil and provisions from Lagos to take home. She had
even fried meat, chicken and fish to take along.
What's happening to local production in the villages? It's
decreasing. People are tired of farming tired farmlands with no help from
the government. To get their votes, politicians would promise to help the
farmers with facilities which would improve their yields. To lend credence
to their promise, they would hand out bags of rice and beans to community
leaders to distribute to their subjects. When they get to the seat of power,
they promptly forget the help they had promised! Until the next elections!
Who be suffer-head? Tilling the hard ground loses its attraction, and so
also does sitting out all day and night to catch fish. Young people are
impatient! They want to see quick results of their labour and with minimum
stress! So, it's 'goodbye' to the village and its hardships and 'hello' to
the city with all its dangerous temptations. Do our rulers care?
The 'ogas' who retired and went into farming, engage in mechanized
farming, using tractors and other time-saving and high-yield machines, which
made the exercise less tedious and yielded more harvest.
One would have thought that this would help boost food production and
bring down the prices of foodstuffs, but no such luck. What they produce is
for export - to earn them foreign currencies which would enable them live an
opulent life! Some of them cultivate whatever the government (which normally
comprises these people's close friends and relations) decide should be
exported - cassava, palm produce, etc., and these become priced out of the
reach of the man on the street. The government makes no provision for home
consumption.
It doesn't help the small scale farmers and encourage them to feed their
communities, so that we wouldn't miss our staple foods that the large scale
farmers are exporting. We're left to wade through the mud that the
authorities have created by their insensitivity to our pains. It would be
interesting to see what the lawmakers can do to bring down prices. Has it
occurred to them that the first meaningful step they should take, if they
are serious, is to get the government to remove or reduce drastically, the
tax on raw materials for essential commodities, even if it's for a period,
to start with? When production is cheap, food prices would crash.
Fertilizer should be highly subsidized and made available to all
categories of farmers, without any favour. To be able to do this
effectively, all farmers should be registered at local government level, so
that they can be recognized by the government and can be given their
allocation. There should be outlets for sales to backyard farmers. Farmers
should form groups and have leaders which would liaise with government and
professionals to discuss whatever problems they may be having.
Next, all the local government areas should be empowered from the
central government to buy vehicles which would be rented out at affordable
prices to the farmers, so that they can easily convey their products to the
nearby markets or selling points. Farmers, middle-men, transporters, market
men and women, and government officials should come together regularly, to
fix the prices of our major foodstuffs.
Government should un-ban essential commodities which are not produced in
the country. They should interact seriously with businessmen and women, and
market men and women, and the youths, so that before any essential commodity
is banned, it's being produced at affordable prices in the country.
Our leaders should not look out only for themselves, but for the
populace as a whole. That's what they are in leadership positions for.

Original_Search_Title: The Return of 'Neglected' Crops
Headline: Africa: Beating World Hunger: The Return of 'Neglected' Crops
Body_Text:
For many centuries, farmers in southern India's Kolli Hills grew nearly 30 varieties of millet. But during the past three decades, the cereal fields were replanted with cassava and sago palms, as more and more farms agreed to supply starch for local producers of processed food.
Today, millet is not grown there, and local communities instead eat government-distributed rice, which although sold at a discount price is less nutritious.
Far away in the Andes of South America, traditionally cultivated grains such as quinoa (Chenopodium quinoa) and amaranth (Amaranthus caudatus) are a natural source of protein and iron. Like native potato varieties however, they are seen as 'poor people's food' and are being replaced by noodles and rice.
Similarly, sub-Saharan Africa is endowed with almost 1,000 types of leafy vegetable and fruit rich in micronutrients. But again, these are not considered fashionable to eat -- unlike exotic, imported cabbages -- so are disappearing from the African landscape.
Nor have any of these crops featured much in modern agricultural research, even though -- given their nutritional value -- they could contribute to food security and poverty alleviation.
Shrinking food basket
Since the early 20th century, people have relied increasingly on a select few plants for food, with about half of the world's calorie intake coming from just three crops -- rice, wheat and maize.
According to the International Plant Genetic Resources Institute (IPGRI) based in Rome, Italy, at least 7,000 plant species could be cultivated for food, but only 150 crops are grown commercially.
"The world increasingly relies on a shrinking food basket of a few crops to fulfil the dietary needs of its people," agrees M. S. Swaminathan, a leading Indian crop expert and chair of the M. S. Swaminathan Research Foundation (MSSRF), which focuses on sustainable agriculture and rural development.
Traditionally, crop research has paid little attention to species that are important to a community or region, but not in international markets.
"Underused species important for agricultural biodiversity have not been the subject of international research and development," warns Rodney Cooke, director of the technical advisory commission at the Rome-based International Fund for Agricultural Development.
Neglected crops and hidden hunger
To address this, IPGRI launched a project in 2001 to incorporate these 'neglected' crops into ongoing research activities throughout the developing world.
