The current focus is on millets in India and Nepal; medicinal plants in Egypt and Yemen; grains in Bolivia, Ecuador and Peru; and leafy vegetables in Africa.
In April 2005, IPGRI, the Global Facilitation Unit for Underutilized Species and MSSRF held a meeting on agricultural biodiversity in Chennai, India.
Participants agreed that increasing research on neglected crops could improve nutrition among poor people and help achieve the UN Millennium Development Goal of halving the number of people suffering from hunger by 2015 (see Neglected crops 'crucial' to beating hunger).
They warned that interpreting the goal as meaning that each person gets more food ignores the fact that malnutrition is also about people not getting enough micronutrients, vitamins and minerals -- a problem described as "hidden hunger".
IPGRI estimates that 150 million children -- 27 per cent of the world's child population -- are underweight, with malnutrition contributing to at least half of the 10.4 million child deaths each year.
Vitamin A deficiency, which can cause blindness, afflicts 120 million children a year, of whom 250,000 to 500,000 go blind. Some two billion people are anaemic because of iron deficiency.
In the past, these deficiencies were tackled through food fortification programmes, but these cannot be replicated in remote rural areas, points out IPGRI director-general Emile Frisson. "Dietary diversity is the way forward," he says.
Back to the fields
Together with local partners in the South, IPGRI is returning several neglected crops to the fields.
One partner, Bolivia's Foundation for the Promotion and Research of Andean Products (PROINPA) is working with farmers to improve crops, food security and sustainability. Various types of local potatoes, roots, grains, cereals, legumes, vegetables and fruits are researched, grown, processed into food products and promoted.
In India, MSSRF is collaborating with IPGRI and the International Fund for Agricultural Development to re-establish the traditional role of millets in the farming system and local diet.
Like the Kolli Hill dwellers, poor communities in Ballia village in Orissa, eastern India, grew a variety of millets 30 years ago. This all changed when an irrigation project allowed intensive rice cultivation with two or three crops in a year. As rice demanded more labour, nobody could be spared to grow the millets.
During drought or when food resources are scarce, people in Kolli or Ballia who cannot afford to buy food through the public distribution system survive on rice stored for sowing during the next crop cycle. This can in turn lead to problems buying seed for the next season.
No grain, no gain
MSSRF is helping these communities build their own village 'grain banks' to store both rice and millet. Local community groups manage an initial reserve of the grains for members to borrow from during times of need. The 'loans' must be returned as grain, along with grain 'interest'.
Farmers in Kolli say the grain banks are helping to conserve traditional millet varieties that were in danger of being lost.
Meanwhile, Kenya is witnessing renewed interest in local vegetables.
"Ironically, as Africa grapples with nutrition problems, it is endowed with a high diversity of underused fruits and vegetables that are rich in micronutrients," says Ruth Oniang'o, founder of the Rural Outreach Program, a Kenyan non-profit organisation that promotes African leafy vegetables to improve the nutritional status and livelihoods of vulnerable groups, especially women and children. .
Since 2001, IPGRI -- with support from the International Development Research Centre in Canada -- has launched major public awareness campaigns, trained farmers to grow leafy vegetables in clean conditions and worked with a marketing expert in Kenya on how to attract new customers.
A local NGO, Family Concerns, distributes the farmers' produce to Kenya's largest supermarket chain.
Oniang'o says there is an urgent need to increase research on nutrition and crop genetics, and to improve seed storage facilities, and processing and marketing of African leafy vegetables.
From fields to supermarkets
It is not enough to encourage local farmers to grow their traditional crops. Successful marketing is just as important for creating sustainable livelihoods.
Take yacon (Smallanthus sonchifolius), a succulent root from Peru that was once eaten by farmers to quench their thirst. Twenty years ago, yacon was not included in Peru's crop statistics and was absent from its supermarket shelves.
In the 1980s Japanese scientists found the roots were high in a low-calorie sugar called oligofructose, which could be used in an energising drink, while the leaves contained a compound that lowered blood sugar and could be useful for diabetics. As news of the findings spread in the late 1990s, demand increased. Today, yacon is available in Peruvian supermarkets.
Elsewhere others are working hard to create demand for farmers' products.
Gerardo Jorge Blajos of PROINPA says that in Bolivia school breakfasts now use up to 120 tonnes of quinoa flour every year and about 30,000 nursing mothers get three kilograms of quinoa every month from government-funded programmes.
Overcoming the obstacles
But despite these initiatives, small farmers growing crops outside the mainstream find it difficult to enter international markets.
Yacon may be enjoying a renaissance in Peru, but it cannot be sold in Europe. The European Union's Novel Foods Regulation states that foods not present in the EU before 1997 must be proved to be free of allergenic, toxic and other hazards before they can be sold. Yacon farmers simply do not have the resources to supply exhaustive data.
In fact, the only exotic plant product that has been allowed into Europe since that legislation came in is the juice of the noni fruit (Morinda citrifolia), which is marketed by a large US company that was able to supply extensive food safety evidence.
"Pioneering small companies in developing countries are losing out," says Michael Hermann from IPGRI's regional office for the Americas, in Colombia.
He adds that while those promoting exotic foods must increasingly accommodate legitimate food safety concerns and generate data needed for their acceptance in target markets, the high burden of proof has discouraged investment in supply chains and in market development.
Making neglected crops a sustainable option
Participants at the Chennai meeting called for action to conserve agricultural diversity and to make better use of it to improve food security, nutrition and incomes for the rural poor in developing countries.
As well as more research on traditional crops, they recommended that policies to preserve them be integrated into national development plans, and food and nutrition security programmes, especially those providing food aid and school meals.
Getting neglected crops back on the menu is important, they said, but so is ensuring that the poor get a fair share of any commercial benefits that arise from exploiting these genetic resources.
Some of the ongoing efforts have shown the way forward. The Kolli Hill millet farmers have taken an interest in conserving their local varieties, bringing more area under millet cultivation and improving yields by using traditional breeding methods.
Incomes have increased, in part because the farmers grow millet without using chemicals. A local company is working with the farmers to export their millet as organically grown food, for which there is considerable demand in the West.
Inspired by this initiative a local government agency in the Kolli Hill area is trying to promote such similar activities elsewhere in the area.

Original_Search_Title: Ambassador Calls for Action On West African Economic Integration
Headline: West Africa: Ambassador Calls for Action On West African Economic Integration
Body_Text:
Abuja — THE Minister of Commerce, Amb. Idris Waziri has called for
non-politicisation of trade liberalisation and economic integration among
countries of the West African sub-region and urged member nations to ensure
concrete actions on all agreements and protocols to that effect.
Speaking at the Regional Trade Forum organised by the ECOBANK Group and
the Export Group of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) in Abuja,
yesterday, he noted with regret that economic integration and trade
liberalisation had suffered much setback in the sub-region due to undue
politics among its members.
"We are wallowing in abject poverty, diseases and hunger for which there
is no need for anyone of them. We have had enough talking. We need actions
now. Our various governments have signed series of agreements and protocols
on trade liberalisation and economic integration but we have seen little of
this due to unnecessary politicising of the issues.
"In Africa, we emphasise too much on politics instead of economy and how
we can better the lives of our people. We are faced with very weak
infrastructure. That should engage our minds. We should address that, we
should address development agenda so we can trade internationally.
"As long as we emphasise too much on politics, we can't have economic
integration and free trade which have become the hallmark of other regions
of the world and we should remember that no region will wait for us. We have
to be honest, hardworking and patriotic." he said.
Amb. Waziri warned that with Africa accounting for a mere 3 per cent of
the world trade, the dream for sustainable economic growth would ever remain
a mirage without extra efforts at promoting intra sub-regional trade.
Earlier, the MD of ECOBANK Nigeria, Mrs. Funke Osibodu, urged federal
government to open its borders to Ghanaian products, particularly, textiles,
cassava starch, cutlasses and plastics, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Tourism in Nigeria: Beyond the Claptrap
Headline: Nigeria: Tourism in Nigeria: Beyond the Claptrap
Body_Text:
Tourism is a word that has fascinated successive governments in
Nigeria.
Each of the regimes seems to recognize the potential of the tourism
industry to boost the economy and corporate image of the country in a
variety of ways. Unfortunately, Nigeria's dream of boosting her tourism
potentials has never left the drawing board - that is, if it ever got on the
drawing board in the first place. The exploitation of the economic and other
potentials of tourism in Nigeria is thus merely mouthed by policy-makers but
never implemented. To this extent the current leadership of the culture
sector in Nigeria is no exception. Its concept of the meaning and potentials
of culture is as skewed as its approach to the development of culture and
tourism in Nigeria.
For every country, there are different means of boosting tourism. In
Nigeria, beyond the various interesting sites in different states of the
federation, rich art and cultural traditions remain two major areas which
harbour the potential of boosting the Nigerian economy if well
managed. Certainly this reality should not be news to anybody if we had a
sense of history and were able to recount and appreciate the gains of Festac
'77, in spite of its alleged wastefulness.
As a collage of cultures and ethnicities, Nigeria needs to exploit her
variety of cultural manifestations to her own economic advantage. Each of
the major ethnic groups in the country has one festival or the other that
could become a money-spinner when positively harnessed. There are, for
instance, New Yam Festivals, Fishing Festivals, Masking Festivals, Wrestling
and many others which could have become major events in countries where
people are more realistic, less materialistic, and more responsive to their
common challenges.
In Nigeria, rather than become rallying points in the pursuit of unity
and economic well being, such festivals are wont to become markers of
cultural differences. Some years back in Enugu and Anambra states, for
instance, there used to be New Yam and Mmanwu (masking) Festivals.
The failure of the festivals to attain expected social and economic
goals by becoming major tourist events is not only a function of the ethnic
rivalry that is bound to be the vogue in a multi-ethnic state like Nigeria;
it is also a testimony to the inability of the country's successive
leaderships to comprehensively harness the resources and potentials inherent
in her variegated culture and art traditions. When government talks about
boosting tourism without any serious plan to critically uplift the art and
culture of the Nigerian peoples, it leaves a cold towel on the
shoulder.
Certainly, one cannot help but wonder what actually is on the ground in
terms of infrastructure and systematized events that can attract local and
international tourists.
Besides these core factors, stability in all its ramifications must also
be considered as a prominent denominator in the sustenance of a vibrant and
viable tourism industry. In that requirement, Nigeria scores much less than
a pass mark. In spite of its young but rocky democracy and the bugs amount
of window-dressing that is the stock-in-trade of its ever flamboyant leaden
and phislistinic politicians, Nigeria remains largely unstable politically
and economically. It lacks the primary base upon which the tourism industry
thrives.
Tourists, I am afraid, would not be excited by roadblocks that remind
one of police states, unreliable transport system, horrible roads, daily
power failure, intimidating refuse heaps, strangulating communication
system, tattered cityscapes, among other inadequacies. Since, as a young
nation, the potentials to outgrow this ugly situation are there for Nigeria,
it would have been worthwhile for its leaders and peoples to begin to dust
aspects of her arts and culture for tourist exploitation in the (near)
future. Beyond the so-called traditional arts which include the festivals
mentioned above, Nigeria's "contemporary" art and artists are world class
and can be the subject of major events that can bring regular visitors to
the country.
The Nigerian art exhibition circuit, for one, has remained largely
localized for decades, despite the significant role some of the country's
notable artists have played and continue to play in the international art
arena. It is very shameful for Nigeria - the self-acclaimed Giant of Africa
- that the only two major Biennales (biennial international art exhibition)
that happen on the continent have been in Dakar and Johannesburg. Although
The Pan-African Circle of Artists (PACA) with its secretariat in Nigeria has
also inaugurated a Biennale (known as Afrika Heritage) since 1995, more
needs to be done not only to boost PACA's effort, but also to ensure that
Nigeria take her rightful place in art internationale. But before then,
Nigeria must borrow the Zimbabwe example in instituting major national art
exhibitions and festivals which will form a springboard for possible
international ventures since, as it is said, charity begins at home. In this
regard, such institutions as the National Museum, National Gallery of Art
and the National Council of Arts and Culture should become more responsive
to the challenges of the environment.
But in making tourism work at this stage of Nigeria's development, a
very serious look should be taken at the enabling factors to see whether
they have been most favourably disposed to boosting the industry. Chief
among these, perhaps, are the security issue in Nigeria and the inability of
the security agencies to tackle the problem with a convincing sense of
commitment. In the day, Nigeria is a police state; there are roadblocks
everywhere; gun-totting policemen are having a field day collecting various
sums from motorists. In the night, Nigeria (except Abuja and a few other
cities, perhaps) is one huge hell hole. There are no streetlights;
everywhere is pitch dark and NEPA is ever ready to worsen the
situation. Night life is nil and recreation is non-existent. People live a
badly regimented life. This kind of scenario does not attract foreigners or
tourists. In 2002, the Pan-African Circle of Artists organized its fourth
biennale in Lagos. Mxolisi Sapeta, a South African artist was one of the
foreign participants.
As part of his itinerary, he travelled from Lagos to Enugu in the
company of Ayo Adewunmi and me to give lectures at Institute of Management
and Technology, Enugu, and University of Nigeria, Nsukka. We were travelling
in a bus belonging to Ifesinachi Industries Ltd. The bus had barely left the
station when the police escort approached Mr. Sapeta and demanded to know
his identity. Adewunmi and I intervened, explaining that he was an artist
and lecturer from South Africa and a guest of the Pan-African Circle of
Artists. The policeman insisted that Sapeta must produce his passport, that
he was "different", referring to his tangled hair (commonly known as dread
locks in these parts). When I asked him if he had not seen people with such
hair in Nigeria, the policeman got irritated and abused us throughout the
journey, embarrassing Mr. Mxolisi the more. Another recent example can also
be drawn from the fifth PACA biennale in 2004 when a policeman in Enugu
stopped a bus conveying the foreign participants to dinner. He insisted on
collecting N20.00 from the driver or he would discharge the passengers.
When I told him to save the image of Nigeria, that the passengers were
all foreigners, he was not perturbed. Whereupon I asked the visiting artists
to disembark from the bus so that we would walk down to the eatery which was
just a few yards away. The power-drunk policeman now gave up and asked the
bus to continue, still pressing, but now more politely, that we should "find
him something". We didn't. But these are not germs of a promising tourism
culture. For tourism to succeed, the totality of the enabling culture must
be positivizing enough. Tourism is not an enclosed, isolated enterprise that
would function outside society. If society is cancerous as in Nigeria, every
aspect, including tourism, would suffer.
This is where Nigeria's Minister of Culture seems to miss the point when
he talks on television about tourism. His maxim of "one community, one
(tourist) product" does not show an appreciation of the evolutionary nature
of the tourist industry. Tourism cannot be commoditized; it is a socializing
phenomenon and cannot be perceived or conceived like goods in the open
market. In January 2004, the Pan-African Circle of Artists embarked on a
study tour of some West African countries including Nigeria, Benin, Togo,
Burkina Faso, Mali and Ghana. Wherever we went outside Nigeria, the major
cities were so different from Nigeria's.
There was potable water, electricity supply was not too epileptic,
street lights were on in the night and people did not have to retire early
like chickens; armed robbery was not a way of life, although it was
prevalent; above all, policemen showed some restraint in their actions,
although there were a few exceptions. In Ghana where we spent the longest
time, we did not know the colour of the police uniform until we left. In
addition to all these, there were interesting sites to visit; arts and
crafts villages, beautiful open air restaurants, slave castles, art centres,
sculpture parks and so on. In Nigeria, some of these things are not
developed enough to interest visitors on a sustainable basis. Some of the
parks have been partitioned and sold to opulent and thoughtless
politicians.
In Enugu, the zoo has been overtaken and shared by human mammals,
including politicians and other money-bags. All over Nigeria, public
monuments, especially sculptures have been abused and broken. I have seen
public sculptures covered with electioneering posters and buntings. Yet when
our leaders travel out, they enjoy visiting tourist centres abroad, taking
photographs beside such public sculptures. But at home, the same things hold
little or no significance.
The little that remains of our cultural heritage which can help boost
tourism is also endangered. In this era of post modern pentecostalism,
culture is misconstrued in its entirety as evil. Things which provide fun,
entertainment and economics, in other places have been branded in
Nigeria. In Nigeria today, masking is disappearing because it has been
declared to be evil. Even title-taking, such as the ozo title of the Igbo is
anathema. But are these peculiar to Nigeria? Is masquerade not extant in
other parts of the world, including Europe and America? Have the masking
festivals in such places not been transformed into major carnivals?. If some
of our churches frown at title-taking, for instance is the head of the
Anglican Church, the Queen of England, not a traditional ruler? It is a pity
that our own part of the world has been overtaken by extremis - the belief
in the end of history and the mass attempt to aid that process.
The "fire of the holy ghost", an otherwise useful instrument in the
pursuit of salvation, has overstepped its bounds. It has been transformed
into a nihilist tool.
The fire is burning the cultural heritage of Africa to its very
foundations and one is left to wonder whether it is not a rechristened old
child, that is, colonization in a new, magnificent and more attractive
garb. Whatever it is, it threatens to uproot the African cosmology and
extract its soul. When cultures are rendered sterile in their encounter with
new ideas and visions, they have very little to offer and cannot sustain a
tourism industry. The point I am making here is that tourism is an
all-encompassing phenomenon and that those in charge in Nigeria should look
beyond their nose and adopt a more practical and holistic approach to its
development in Nigeria. Tourism does not thrive on legislation or on open
harangues on television and in newspapers. It is enduring tradition. If it
must evolve in Nigeria, a in Nigeria, a conducive atmosphere needs to be
created and the task should transcend the Ministry of Culture and
Tourism.
The need to diversify Nigeria's source of income has been the subject of
much debate over the years. The over-due imperative to review, restructure
and reposition Nigeria's mono-causal ecoaomy is one that deserves urgent
consideration. Beyond petroleum, agriculture, and the new-fangled song about
cassava, tourism is the coming thing. The role to be played by artists and
all shades of cultural actors in such a triumph of tourism is not in
doubt. Nigeria and Nigerians certainly need this breakthrough in tourism if
they are to be better appreciated internationally. Those in charge should
not go on talking and talking while the tourism dream waits in vain. For
philippics does not solve practical problems nor can it make the corpse that
is Nigeria's tourism industry walk.
C. Krydz Jkwuemesi, painter and theorist, teaches art at the University
of Nigeria, Nsukka. He is also the International Secretary of The
Pan-African Circle of Artists.

Original_Search_Title: Chief Mwansakombe Bemoans Poor Roads
Headline: Zambia: Chief Mwansakombe Bemoans Poor Roads
Body_Text:
Lusaka — GOVERNMENT will face an uphill battle to convince people in next year's elections if it does not work on feeder roads, chief Mwansakombe has said.
In an interview on Saturday, chief Mwansakombe said people in his area were only complaining about the poor state of the roads.
He said the work that has been done so far on the Samfya-Lubwe road and the current work being done on Lubwe-Kasaba road would soon come to waste with the onset of rains.
He said it was the first time the Samfya-Lubwe-Kasaba road was being worked on in 30 years.
"We have been independent for 41 years but the roads are not good.... This will cost those leaders in government during elections. When they come for campaigns they will inevitably touch on the road they are just looking at now," he said.
Chief Mwansakombe said unless government dealt with the roads now, its leaders would have an uphill battle to convince the people it was committed to work.
"It started in Samfya where people voted for an independent candidate. People here don't usually complain but just look at their leaders, but when time for elections comes, they will talk," he said.
He also said there was very little development to talk about in his area.
He said he personally was involved in initiating developmental projects in his chiefdom.
"I am now touring my chiefdom and I am encouraging parents to send their children to top schools. It's not mistreating them but it is meant to impart in them the goodness of sending their children to school to eradicate ignorance and poverty," he said.
Chief Mwansakombe who said he was also checking on the cleanliness of the villages said he also encouraged his subjects to start growing cassava as an alternative crop to defeat hunger.

Original_Search_Title: Chinese Farmers Arrive Kwara for Consultations
Headline: Nigeria: Chinese Farmers Arrive Kwara for Consultations
Body_Text:
Lagos — A Chinese delegation would arrive Ilorin, Kwara State capital, Tuesday
next week on a three-day fact-finding mission on importation of cassava
chips and allied products from Nigeria.
The visit by the delegation from Genertec International Corporation of
China, was part of a twelve-day visit by the delegation.
The visit by the Chinese company to discuss importation of cassava chips
was coming after China received her first shipment of a trial order of the
commodities.
Discussions have already reached an advanced stage between the Kwara
State government and the Chinese company on importationof agro based
products from the state.
The delegation while around would hold consultations with officials of
the Ministry of Agriculture, Agricultural Development Project, members of
the Cassava Growers Associations as well as exporters and suppliers of
cassava chips.
Besides, the team would also visit notable cassava farms in the state
and farm site of Kwara State Commercial Agricultural project being pioneered
by ex-Zimbabwean farmers in Shonga before returning to Abuja.
The visit of the delegation was facilitated by Kwara indigenes in China,
who have been in the vanguard of mobilising Chinese prospective investors to
the state.
The visit would afford the state an opportunity to showcase the various
efforts of the Saraki-led administration towards economic emancipation of
the state and encourage other investors from China to develop interest in
the area.

Original_Search_Title: A Province That Needs Extra Attention
Headline: Rwanda: A Province That Needs Extra Attention
Body_Text:
Kigali — The history of Kibuye province has been characterized by exclusion and isolation by the bad regimes that ruled this country through divisive ethnic and regional politics.
Kibuye Province was severely shattered by the 1994 Genocide and war. The consequences can be seen today in terms of depopulation, low business, and low levels of general development. Nuwamanya Sulah visited the province to find out the business life there.
Below are the findings.
Location
Kibuye province is situated in the south-west of the country. It is bordered by Gisenyi Province in the north, Gitarama in the east, Gikongoro in the southeast, Cyangugu province in the south and Lake Kivu in the west.
Trade
The province's strategic position can boost its economic development through inter-trade with other Provinces and external traders with the neighboring DRCongo According to Grégoire Dusabemungu, the official in charge of planning, economic development and environment. "Kibuye's position gives it great opportunities of trading if we can be helped by the Government and other businessmen. We have L.Kivu and magnificent islands that are good for fishing and tourism," he said.
Energy
According to Grégoire, among the major economic potentials is the untapped methane gas that has been discovered from L.Kivu and that can be an alternative energy source to hydro power.
Tourism
Kibuye is a tourist destination. It has a scenic view of undulating hills with a multitude of meandering rivers between them. River falls on the mountains slopes can be a potential for hydro electricity, as noted by Grégoire and the vice-mayor in charge of planning and economic development of Kibuye town council Jean-Marie Vianney Nkurunziza.
Kibuye Province has great potential in the tourism sector but this has not been fully exploited. Among the many areas that need attention are beaches and islands. Amahoro, an island, is said to have a shape of 'Napoleon's hat' .Other attractions include King Rwabugiri's retreat palaces, Urutare rwa Ndaba, and King Ruganzu's bed. The province has natural forests like Nyungwe and Mukura forests that are a home to various species of animals.
Agriculture
Kibuye province is endowed with fertile soils on slopes of hills and valleys. Crops such as beans, sorghum, Soya, sweet potatoes, bananas, tomatos, coffee, tea, pyrethrum, pepper, and onions are grown, but mostly on small scale. Commercial farming is minimal and the produce is supplied at the local markets to meet their (farmers') basic needs.
Livestock
Animals reared include cattle, pigs, and goats. They are reared on small scale, and their products such as milk, beef/pork/mutton, hides and skins are sold in local markets to meet family basic needs like food, shelter, education and health.
Small scale enterprises
"The number of private businessmen is still very low. Cooperatives and associations are not functioning well," said the provincial public relations officer Landrade Umuraza.
There is however some progress with Abagi, a credit and saving association that is mainly comprised of women who sell agricultural seeds and pesticides to farmers in different parts of the province. Another promising association is Budaha specialized in horticulture and making of juice called Zamuka from the fruits grown.
Lumbering
There is tree felling in Nyungwe and Mukura forests. There are various carpentry workshops in the town center and few others spread in other districts. Good service workshop a carpentry workshop visited by our reporter in Kibuye trading centre make most of the domestic furniture, but on a small scale. Asked whether he has an idea about producing for export the proprietor replied, "I lack the capital and skills to produce products that can compete favorably on international market."
Hotels and lodges
There are few hotels and restaurants in the province to accommodate tourists and other visitors to the province. Some of the hotels include Centre Bethanie, Golf Eden Rock and St James Motel.
The Kibuye Guest Gouse was closed, now awaiting re-privatization after Pascal Munyampirwa, the proprietor, allegedly failed to comply with the terms and conditions of the contract of sale.
Business
Kibuye which is the major town in the province is very small and poorly organized .The shops are entirely retail and the few that exist have few items. According to Josephine Muhoza, a shop attendant, the business is slow because people are very poor. "The business here is not profitable," Muhoza lamented, traders stock from Kigali and neighboring DRCongo and this makes commodities to be sold expensively because of the transport charges of long distances.
Transport and telecommunication
Roads: The main road from Kigali to Kibuye is tarmac and it takes few hours to Kibuye town. The Kibuye-Cyagugu road is bad and according to Nkuruziza, the state of the road has affected trade between Kibuye and Cyagugu. The other roads joining the districts are murram and bad too.
Water transport is another means of transport people of Kibuye use to connect to Gisenyi province through L.Kivu using Bralirwa S.A motor boats that bring its products to Kibuye Town. There are other private boats that transport people to different islands.
The province has ONTRACOM, a government transport body, ATRACO express, OKAPI express and other private taxis that connect to Rwanda's capital-Kigali and other provinces.
Telecommunication
Kibuye can easily communicate with other places in the country through MTN, Rwandatel (TERRACOM).
Banking and other financial services
Kibuye province has branches for some local commercial banks such as, the Intercontinental African Bank (BACAR), Bank of commerce, development and industry (BCDI) and Banque Populaire. There are other micro- finances in the province that are coming up that give credit to the residents.
Manufacturing
The province has only one tea factory in Sovu. Tea growing areas in Sovu include Tabire and Rushenyi. There are five coffee washing stations that include one in Gisuzu, Kibuye town and three stations in Rushenyi.
Prospect: A banana flour processing plant could be developed in the area according to city authorities. "The banana flour processing plant has not yet got a developer to make it operational. We hope, the plant would change the economic status and life of Kibuye people and Rwanda in general", Dusabemungu said.
Production for export
The major cash crops of Kibuye province are coffee and tea that are grown on slopes of high hills. Other crops that are grown for export to other provinces in the country are pepper, passion fruits and bananas. "We are glad that Kibuye has contributed to tea and coffee export in Rwanda. Though the production capacity is still very low due to poor farming methods and natural calamities" noted Dusabemungu.
Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) in Kibuye
There are a number of NGOs working in the province in various areas such as capacity building and financing poverty reduction programmes. These include the following;
- Peace and Decentralization (PED), has helped in road maintenance, rehabilitation of schools, construction of administrative offices, offering financial assistance to local associations, organizing seminars and developing provincial and town development plans of actions for every year.
- PREPAF, offering assistance in poverty reduction programmes, construction of bridges and markets and sensitizing people on income generation.
- Care International, which has helped in the fight against AIDS by offering both material and financial support to various AIDS associations and people.
- JUMELAGE, which has also offered assistance to many Schools, hospitals and local associations for improving people's welfare.
- CARITAS, it is also an NGO that has played a pivotal role in the province's fight against soil erosion and assisted in organizing and sensitizing the local population on income generation.
Social services
Education: Kibuye is one of the earliest beneficiaries of formal education in Rwanda. Birambo, Kirinda, Rubengera and Murunda where missionaries first settled have produced the cream of Kibuye's educated young men and women for decades.
There are a few government secondary and primary schools. Only one branch of a tertiary institute--Kigali Institute of Education (KIE) -- and a station of forestry for the National University of Rwanda (faculty of agriculture).The few schools are very scattered in different districts and their accessibility is difficult due to bad roads and long distances.
Health: The Church is a leading institution in providing healthcare and has been running hospitals and health centers in places like Mugonero, Kirinda and Murunda. There are only four hospitals in the whole province, with one in Kibuye town that has only five doctors. Kibuye town has one health center with only nurses accredited to it and only one dispensary. Health centers were severely damaged during the genocide, which continues to affect their performance even today.
Economic challenges
Kibuye province faces enormous economic problems that need to be solved urgently. Among the challenges that the province is facing are; illiteracy which is at the level of 45% according to the provincial officials, poor transport system, lack of enough developmental support for its residents, lack of information communication facilitieslike (ICT)-internet in the province, lack of markets for their agricultural products and poor methods of farming. Agriculture is also hit by soil erosion rivers.
Strategies to overcome the economic challenges
In order to address fundamental issues affecting the Province, all districts designed five year strategic plans which outline various activities that will be carried out to solve problems affecting the people.
Kibuye provincial authorities have also designed their own strategic plan for the next 5 years which aims at facilitating the plans of individual districts. The priority areas as stipulated in this plan are;
- Education
- Good Governance
- Agriculture
- Environmental Conservation
- Health
- Gender and women empowerment
- Infrastructure
General analysis
The provincial authorities have zeroed in on tourism, exploitation of Lake Kivu beaches, coffee and tea production, hotels, lodges and restaurants, improving water and road transport and commercial livestock farming as areas that need investing hugely.
Despite efforts of improving the general security situation in the province, poverty, illiteracy, low levels of development and investments, poor infrastructure and a lax in business affect the business and thus need urgent government attention. The portion of uncultivated land is still big. The supply of rudimentary tools and modern agricultural input and machinery are scarce. Rains are irregular and at times they are torrential and cause enormous destruction.
"Mosaic disease in cassava has proved disastrous," Alphonse Rutikaga a farmer in Rushenyi district said.
Markets are generally poorly stocked and deficits in beans and cereals become acute in the high altitude zones of Budaha- Ndiza and Buberuka.

Original_Search_Title: Nepad Agriculture Programme: a Poverty Fighter, Says Asika
Headline: Nigeria: Nepad Agriculture Programme: a Poverty Fighter, Says Asika
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Last week in Abuja, the Senior Special Assistant to the President and
CEO, New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) in Nigeria, Chief
(Mrs.) Chinyere Asika, told how Nigerians are taking ownership of the
Comprehensive African Agriculture Programme (CAAP), a programme of
NEPAD.
In a related development, Mrs. Dupe Adelaja, Director General of the
Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency (SMEDAN), who wants SME
operators to add value to agricultural resources, reveals how SMEDAN is
partnering with the United Nations Forum for Arts and Culture (UNFAC) and
the National Directorate of Employment (NDE) to involve SME operators in
cultural, environmental and technological programmes. Meanwhile, the
Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Corporation (NAIC), led by Mr. Kwatri
K. Yusuf, is repositioning to cultivate the confidence of both agricultural
and conventional insurance business.
What is the Comprehensive African Agriculture
Programme (CAAP) about?
It is an action plan developed by the New Partnership for
Africa's Development (NEPAD) for a Comprehensive African Agriculture
Programme (CAAP), which is being implemented at the country level by all 53
African countries that are members of NEPAD. CAAP is to be integrated into
the action plans of African countries and domiciled in their ministries of
agriculture. For instance, if you come to Nigeria, the ministry of
agriculture has been foremost in implementing the NEPAD agriculture
programme. One of such programme is the national programme for food
security. Another one is the NEPAD Cassava Initiative. And the one that is
to take place now (August) is the NEPAD Fish for All.
NEPAD country offices in African countries monitor these programmes
while UN agencies like the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) provide
the technical assistance. Usually, when these programmes are being developed
and domesticated at the country level, the particular UN agency that has
been mandated to look after that programme, country ministries, the NEPAD
country office work together.
Have these programmes taken off or are they yet to take off?
They have taken off. President Obasanjo has informed severally that
Nigeria is not only the world's largest producer of cassava, she has started
exporting cassava, for the first time in history. Sensitisation has been
very high and production has gone high and we expect it to continue to
increase, as the many uses of cassava becomes known to people, both locally
and abroad. There is a high demand for cassava now.
Nigeria is also becoming a supplier of food, maintaining food security
especially in those African countries that are at risk. Like what is
happening now in Niger. President Obasanjo has done so much and most people
don't seem to know what he has done. The fact that we have enough strategic
food reserve to be able to send food to a stricken country like Niger is
something that we have to celebrate as a country. The president has many
initiatives in the ministry of agriculture. They all fall within CAAP. I
think that Nigerians are beginning to take ownership of the programmes and
this is exactly what NEPAD wanted to achieve.
What do you mean by ownership?
The ownership means that Nigerians have come to be interested in finding
out more about cassava. Cassava has always been around with us. We've always
eaten out of cassava, but Nigerians are becoming interested in the
industrial uses of cassava. They have become much more informed about
cassava and have bought into it. I mean, in some part of this country, it is
a taboo for men to farm cassava; but even in those parts now men are eager
to cultivate cassava. To be able supply the needs of the world.
What is the next step in the programme?
The president has told us that a product like cassava cannot leave this
country unless we have added value to it. So everybody is looking at
production, processing for local use and export. Cassava is an area that is
going to explode. That is what I mean by ownership. Nigerians have bought
into it. We've always had it, but we used to ignore it, we used to leave it
for rural women. Cassava is no longer a poor man's crop. The president
thinks that in the near future we can earn more money from cassava than from
crude oil.
Do you have any general comments?
The most important thing is that we are thinking of those things that
tackle extreme poverty. When NEPAD produced its document on the cassava
initiative it identified cassava as poverty fighter, which is in line with
the UN Millennium Development Goals, which is also in line with the National
Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS) document. This
particular crop can enrich so many people, reduce hunger, increase rural
earnings and livelihood and therefore reduce poverty in Africa.
SMEDAN, UNFAC and NDE Partner for SME Devt
The Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency of Nigeria (SMEDAN)
and the United Nations Forum for Arts and Culture (UNFAC) have also agreed
to promote cultural, environmental and technological programmes in the
country, reports Etim Imisim.
The agreement was reached when UNFAC delegate, led by Prof. Catherine
Acholonu, paid a courtesy call on SMEDAN's director general, Mrs. Modupe
Adelaja in Abuja recently.
One of the core issues at the meeting was how to stimulate growth in
rural economy and create employment among rural dwellers throughout the
country. In this regard, according to a release from SMEDAN's chief press
officer, Mr. Levi Anyikwa, Adelaja and Acholonu considered poverty
alleviation an urgent matter, since components of the programme such as
tree-planting exercises would not succeed if the people remained poor.
Alongside the efforts to boost SMEs was the need to promote the use of
alternative source of power.
"They reasoned that it would be unrealistic to think of boosting SME
activities without power supply as SME operators can do very little given
the prevailing power situation in the country", the release said. UNFAC and
SMEDAN settled on "the use of solar energy by SMEs since it is not only
renewable but also cheaper and environmentally friendly," the release
added. "SMEs cannot afford to rely on the national grid if they must achieve
the goals of income generation, wealth creation and poverty alleviation."
According to Adelaja, one of SMEDAN's responsibilities is to empower
people by promoting SME activities, using the available resources. She said
also that SMEDAN's capacity building training for UNFAC graduate will
complement their skill acquisition needs of the programme. The purpose was
to make them successful entrepreneurs, she Meanwhile, in a related
development, SMEDAN and the National Directorate of Employment (NDE) have
also agreed to collaborate to further develop the SME sector and create
jobs, Anyikwa said in a separate release.
Adelajo who met an NDE team in Abuja spoke of the need for a quick
intervention in SME activities to address the worsening poverty in the
country. She said NDE could help in this, if it updated its data on
employment and labour in the country. This, she said, would form the basis
for effective planning and the execution of employment-generating
initiatives.
Responding, the leader of NDE's delegation, Mr. Nnamdi Udo, assured that
the directorate is working towards updating its employment records. Udo, who
is the director of the Small Scale Enterprises Unit of NDE, added that the
directorate has improved the lives of Nigerians through its of vocational
and entrepreneurial training programmes over the years. Various categories
of Nigerians from primary school certificate holders, secondary school
leavers to university graduates have benefited, the release said.
Yusuf Tasks NAIC on Service Delivery
The Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Corporation (NAIC) would realise its
potential for growth if it could cultivate the confidence of the agriculture
and general insurance business, reports Etim Imisim.
Mr. Kwatri K. Yusuf said this would be possible if the staff of the
corporation braced up to the competitive nature of industry.
The managing director of NAIC spoke in Abuja recently during an in-house
workshop organised for NAIC managers who were drawn from the corporation's
zonal and state offices nationwide. The corporation is trying to reposition
itself in the agriculture and general insurance business. The training was
meant to refocus and reorient staff towards effective service delivery.
A release from Mr. Butrous Pembi, NAIC corporate affairs manager, cited
Yusuf saying that the corporation is not in the recurrent budget of the
federal government, and that it needed to "adopt aggressive and creative
marketing strategies for its survival."
Yusuf said that NAIC has competent professionals in its fold to
underwrite any general insurance outside its core area. Agricultural
business had been the exclusive preserve of the corporation over the
years. But Yusuf urged the staff to be prepared to be competitive in its new
drive for conventional insurance. The training was also intended to enhance
the capacity of staff to generate both the subsidised and conventional
insurance.
"While we hold very firmly our number one position in the agricultural
insurance sector, we should improve our rating in conventional insurance,"
Yusuf said.
"Gone are the days of a sit and wait insurer," he added. "You must be up
and doing, making contacts to get new businesses and at the same time
holding existing clients dearly to ensure that they are not snatched by
competitors."
The corporation has established standard procedures for processing
claims, he said. "Signed discharge vouchers are settled within 24 hours," he
said, "and claims are settled within a maximum of one week."
Yusuf recalled that the founding manager, Mr. Ishiyaku Jafiya, made NAIC
what it today. It is his duty as the current leader to leave the corporation
a better place "for those that are coming after us."
NAIC has a humble beginning. It started "from only two crops and two
livestock items on a subsidised basis." Yusuf recalled when times were hard
and the corporation owed salaries. But because of the commitment of the
staff, the corporation survived.
He however warned the staff against rate-cutting and other shady
deals. "The issue of fraud will not be condoned," he said.
"I assure you there will be no safe haven for fraudsters," he
added.

Original_Search_Title: Success Digest Organises 16th Expo
Headline: Nigeria: Success Digest Organises 16th Expo
Body_Text:
Lagos — Success Digest holds her 16th Business Opp-ortunity Expo at SADC multipurpose centre situated at 28 Esuola Street Off Ago Palace Way, Okota Lagos State. Between August 9-13th, 2005.The Business Expo, the second this year is aimed at raising and nurturing entrepreneurs and making available proven skills that will enable those already in business and those preparing to start one to excel.
The Expo, which will bring together experts in different entrepreneurial field, will kick off with the Sunny Obazu-Ojeagbase absolutely free Customer appreciation seminar, Tianshi wealth, Alternative medicine and internet opportunities.This will be followed by cassava Summit with Boma Anga, Ayo Olubori and Matna food, Nigeria's highest Producer of Cassava starch.Other features include internet Boot Camp for beginners, Real Estate Investment, Export of damage Phones, Fish pond construction and free expo show and exhibition for the public.
The five day Expo Training programme, which will be practical and intensive, will include setting up of cottage industries, small and medium scale enterprises in the agricultural, the chemical and Allied, information and telecommunication technology and the educational sectors.
In the agro-sector, the expo will cover courses like mass fish production and technology, production of cakes, special cookies, Yoghurt and juice, modern poultry and turkey farming, snail farming technology, stock feed production,grass-cutter farming, dry split Ginger, Hibiscus and pepper processing for export, etc.

Original_Search_Title: UNAAB Students Empowered for the Future
Headline: Nigeria: UNAAB Students Empowered for the Future
Body_Text:
Lagos — Students of the University of Agriculture, Abeokuta, (UNAAB) have been practically empowered to take bold steps towards being self employed or becoming employers of labour in future.
Speaking to the students during an interactive session facilitated by the university, the branch manager of the Federal Government owned Nigerian Agricultural Cooperative and Rural Development Bank Ltd (NACRDB), Abeokuta, Mrs. Joy Ehirin said the students could obtain loans to the tune of N250,000 and above with very little collateral, with which they could begin their own business. This she emphasised, was aimed at making the students self-sustaining when they leave the university.
Earlier, while welcoming the branch manager to the university, the Vice Chancellor, Prof. Israel Adu, thanked her for honouring the university's invitation. He said the interaction was one of the ways the university prepares its students for life after graduation.
He explained that the feedback from UNAAB graduates had encouraged the university to allocate resources for this aspect of students training.
Meanwhile, an appeal has gone to the Federal Government to pay more attention to specialized universities like the University of Agriculture, Abeokuta, which is blessed with ample human and material resources to move the nation forward.
The appeal was made by Mr. Ezekiel Leosho, a UNAAB contact farmer from Ogijan-one of the university's extension villages, who came to give moral support at the maiden maize roasting ceremony of the university of Agriculture, Abeokuta.
The highlight of the ceremony was a farm tour round the Agriculture Media Resources and Extension Centre (AMREC) demonstration farms.
The maize roasting ceremony, a fall out from the existing annual yam roasting ceremony, was in fulfillment of the promise made by Prof. Adu, last year.
The idea of the ceremony, the VC said, was to provide an avenue for all staff to know in practical terms what could be done on the field, and to encourage individuals departments and colleges to practice what UNAAB teaches its students. The VC also expressed satisfaction and pride in the university farms.
The outgoing Director of AMREC, Dr. Yaya Adeoti, thereafter led all on the farm tourist to where hectares of yam, pepper, cassava, maize, garden-egg, plantain, cocoa, pawpaw and cowpeas were cropped as well as a fish pond.
He told members that the objective of the farm is to demonstrate enterprise combination on a unit of land for maximum return to the farmer.
The incoming director of AMREC, Prof. Akin Omotayo, who was expected to take over the reins of office from Dr. Adeoti with effect from August 1, said part of his vision for the future, included improving the relationship with ADP's in the states amongst others.
Members of the university community were treated to farm fresh roasted corn with different varieties of garden eggs, topped with UNAAB bottled table water and palm-wine at the occasion.

Original_Search_Title: Ondo Commissions Cassava Factory
Headline: Nigeria: Ondo Commissions Cassava Factory
Body_Text:
Akure — The factory has an installed capacity to produce over 12 tonnes of
cassava flour per day. Minister of Commerce, Ambassador Idris Waziri, who
heads the Presidential Initiative on Cassava Revolution, speaking on the
occasion, commended the Ondo State Government for becoming the first state
in the drive towards creating employment and generating cheap foreign funds
from cassava.
Said he: "With the establishment of this gigantic factory in a record
time of five months, Ondo State has not only made history as the first state
to embark on massive production of cassava, its record would be hard to
challenge by other states as the cassava factory that is being commissioned
today (yesterday) is very huge and has the capacity of producing not only
cassava flour, chips and pellets but it is capable of producing pounded yam
flour, poultry feeds, ethanol and starch for industrial use.
"This is a huge leap in the right direction and I congratulate His
Excellency, Dr. Olusegun Agagu and the entire people of the Sunshine State
for this feat."
Governor Agagu said as part of government resolve to create job and
encourage private participation in the siting of industries in the state, it
went into partnership with the proprietor of the hitherto moribund Oke-Ayo
Farms Limited in Ondo town to form the Sunshine Food Processing
Factory. Under the arrangement, government owns 49 per cent while the owner
gets 51 per cent of the company shares.
"A sum of N40 million was subsequently released for the rehabilitation
of the once moribund factory and today, less than five months after the
Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) was signed, we are commissioning the
company," Agagu said.
The factory, which he noted, had already generated over 250 jobs was
carried out by government under its Accelerated Poverty Alleviation
Programme (APAA). The governor said government had signed MoUs with two
other companies to establish two more cassava processing factories in the
state.

Original_Search_Title: Businessmen Told to Add Value to Cassava
Headline: Nigeria: Businessmen Told to Add Value to Cassava
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerian businessmen and women have been urged to add value to the the cassava industry by taking advantage of the current awareness about cassava to invest in large scale cassava production and processing to meet local demand for garri, fufu, and other products for export.
Dr. Lateef Sanni, Food Technologist/Post Harvest Specialist of the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) made the call at a two-day workshop titled "Adding value to cassava, a new agricultural revolution" held at the Airport Hotel, Ikeja, Lagos. The workshop which was attended mainly by representatives of food processing companies, and large scale farmers was organized by IITA in collaboration with the office of the Special Assistant to the President on Food Security, and the Centre for Sustainabl Agricultural Development (CSAD), a Lagos based non-governmental organization ( NGO).
Dr. Sanni emphasized that IITA has continued to train individuals and corporate bodies on the production of high quality cassava flour, chips, animal feed composition, and various food products to commercialize cassava production. He urged the participants to evolve the culture of packaging their products according to international regulatory standards to attract not only local but foreign markets. He expressed delight that some of the products are currently being exported to Europe and the United States of America (USA).
Speaking on management of large-scale cassava farms, Mr. Paul Ilona IITA's International Trials Manager said one important ingredient for success in cassava business especially as a large scale farmer is volume.
He emphasized that "it is only by rapid expansion of available cassava farms in the country that the current high demand for the commodity for industrial products could be met". Mr. Ilona said farmers who have less than one hectare cassava farm cannot expect the same profits on investment with those who have five hectares or more. He said it is only through mass production of cassava that the current high cost of gari can be forced down.
He spoke of common pitfalls in cassava production, and urged new comers in the business to "start small and gradually scale up their farms with good management practices", that will put value in their business with time. He advised the new comers to hasten slowly so as not to rush out when they encounter problems.
Explaining the many business opportunities in cassava farming, Mr. Ilona listed sourcing of planting materials, chemical weed control techniques, tractor hiring, transportation, and processing, as some of the opportunities where individuals and cooperative farmers can easily make a fortune. He gave example of a group of farmers in Oyo State who have made more than N15 million profit by supplying cassava stems this planting season. He added that the high demand for improved cassava stems for planting will certainly continue for several years to come, meaning that those who go into the business of rapid multiplication of cassava stems will always continue to smile to the bank.
The workshop was declared open by Mrs. M.O. Adetunji, Special Assistant to the President on Food Security.
She said the Presidential initiative on cassava was introduced to create market opportunities for cassava within and outside Nigeria. Mrs. Adetunji said go were the days when there used to be a glut of cassava production with ridiculous prices being offered farmers. She called on all stakeholders in the cassava business to ensure the sustainability of the current awareness on cassava production for human food, animal feeds and industrial utilization.
She expressed delight that the Federal government has provided the enabling environment for cassava stakeholders to find solution to the challenge of credit facilities for farmers and invest in large scale mechanized farming. She added that the government has opened up foreign markets outlets for investors to export cassava products. Mrs. Adetunji emphasized that "agricultural development is synonymous to rural development, hence State and Local
Governments should provide basic infrastructure such as good rural road networks, transportation and tractor hiring" for the benefit of the rural farmers.
She also advised farmers to form cooperatives to attract loan to increase their productivity.
About 100 participants attended the workshop. Mr. Agbor Ndoma, Executive Director of the Center for Sustainable Agricultural Development (CSAD), organizers of the workshop, spoke on its objectives which include creating public awareness on the various uses of cassava

Original_Search_Title: The Alternative to Resource Control
Headline: Nigeria: The Alternative to Resource Control
Body_Text:
Lagos — Oke Epia in this report, writes that the 4th annual lecture of the Senator Abraham Adesanya Foundation which held last week in Lagos with a focus on the potential of agriculture as an alternative to oil in the generation of national income, re-emphasizes the quest to go back to the soil.
Quite unlike the case with many past leaders and distinguished persons in Nigeria, Senator Abraham Adesanya is being celebrated while he is alive. Regarded as one of those who struggled at the vanguard to ensure the birth of the current democratic government, Adesanya's life and contributions to the country's democracy has always inspired similar struggles for justice, fairness and equity as it relates to correcting the imbalances in the federal set-up currently being practised.
It is therefore not surprising that the struggle for a fair share of revenue allocation derived from the states which make up the country has often found a connection in him whether in active activism or geroncratic advocacy. Though he merely sat all through the duration of the occasion, the octogenarian was definitely in good company when the issue of resource control and true federalism took the centre stage at the 4th annual lecture of Senator Abraham Adesanya Foundation, which held at the Golden Gate Restaurant in Lagos last week.
Though there is no doubt that the agitation by delegates from the South-south geo-political zone in the just concluded National Political Reforms Conference (NPRC) for better compensation for the exploitation of oil resources in the region gave a huge preponderance of the lingering issue of resource control in the country, it has been a long standing controversy nonetheless. But the consensus at the Adesanya Foundation lecture was that if every region of the country would look inwards and embark on a concerted exploitation of resources within its area especially agriculture, the quarrel over resource sharing in Nigeria's federal experiment could be successfully surpressed. This was the dominant message put across at the lecture in honour of the Afenifere leader.
Coming at a time when an attempt to restructure the polity along very significant lines ended in a controversial deadlock, laying the options that could help to assuage hurt feelings brought about by the resource control debate before the table, may be a good idea indeed. That was perhaps, the goal organizers of the lecture had in mind when they chose to address the quest for agriculture as an alternate income earner for the economy as the topic of discourse. And by inviting non other than Akwa Ibom State Governor, Obong Victor Attah, arguably the staunch advocate for resource control of the South-south geo-political zone, to deliver the keynote address, the point could not have been nailed home any stronger.
The tone of the day's lecture was set by an address delivered by Alhaji Muniru Muse, Chairman of Apapa local government who is also the chairman of the foundation. Muse noted succinctly that the abandonment of Nigeria's vast agricultural resources for the black gold was one of the reasons for the current agitation for resource control by some sections of the country. According to him, "in recent years, there havehas been agitation from different quarters over the state of our nation's economy. We are aware of the present agitation for regionalism and resource control. The arguments have varied as the proponents and oppositions have varied.
"It is quite a fact that prior to the discovery of oil in commercial quantity in Nigeria, the regions had traded in agricultural commodities. The West was known for her cocoa, the East for palm produce, the North for cotton, cow hide, groundnuts and the South-south for rubber produce. Today, I do not need agricultural economists to tell us the present state of agriculture in Nigeria. This year's lecture dwells on the need for the greenery option. Until there is total food security in Nigeria, we would not stop our call."
Delivering his paper titled: "Taming our angst: the greenery option," Governor Attah made it clear from the onset that "at a time when Nigeria has been held down by arguments over what percentage of derivation revenue accruing from petroleum oil should be paid to the oil-bearing states, this discourse will attempt to bring into sharp focus a sector which if revived, will reduce the anxiety of many Nigerians."
Espousing a clear understanding of Nigeria's resource endowments across geo-political lines, Attah took the audience through an adequate dosage of the history of national resource exploitation since the post-independence era of the 1960s. This included the efforts of the various regional administrations to establish comparative advantage in crop and husbandry production, how much each contributed to the federal purse at the centre, and how such produce were sold and stored to ensure benefits for the farmers, etc. Examples of failures in the agricultural enterprise were also given including the adoption of the Israeli farm settlement schemes by the regional governments of the First Republic which according to him, suffered set-back due to its politicization. "The schemes failed woefully because each of them was introduced in a hurry as a political expediency without proper planning, staffing, adequate funding and careful selection of settlers," he said, adding: "The political parties in power used the schemes for compensating political thugs and supporters, touts and other jobless citizens for their roles during the parties' electioneering campaigns."
The Akwa Ibom governor also highlighted government efforts after the collapse of the First Republic citing the establishment, constraints some of them faced. Mentioned in this category included the National Seed Multiplication Scheme during the Second National Development Plan, the National Accelerated Food Production Programme (NAFPP) of the mid-1970s, Operation Feed the Nation (OFN) of the military administration of General Olusegun Obasanjo, the introduction of River Basin Development Authorities and several other agricultural development programmes.
From the past, Attah moved on to highlight current efforts of the Federal Government since 1999 including the presidential initiative on cassava, presidential initiative on fisheries and aqua culture, and the presidential initiative on livestock production, among others, noting that "these programmes area at various stages of implementation and progress."
Another significant part of the lecture was the introduction of the Israeli experience in agriculture for the purpose of comparison and inspiration for the Nigerian situation. In doing so, Attah noted that Israel's success in agriculture "to date lies in the determination and ingenuity of farmers and scientists who have dedicated themselves to developing a flourishing agriculture in a country which is more than half desert, thus proving vividly that the real value of the land is a function of how it is utilized."
Having laid a background for the lecture, the speaker proceeded to the main theme which dwelt on how a concerted return to the soil by various governments in the country can help assuage perceived feelings of hurt and misgivings arising from the agitation for resource control. And to succinctly capture the point, the governor said the practice of true federalism in the country would provide the needed framework for the different governmental units to see the development of their territories as their individual responsibilities instead of a near wholesale dependence on federal allocations derived largely from the sale of crude oil.
He said: "What went wrong is that we turned the concept of federalism on its head, we forsook the autonomy that the regions had, we abandoned resource control which had stimulated competition and self-reliance among the regions, and instead we made ourselves vassals of the Federal Government which itself came to depend on one thing: oil. That spelt our total undoing. There is no doubt in my mind that if we had continued with the practice of resource control and true federalism, the federating units would also have continued to look inwards with a view to exploring and exploiting their immense endowments in the agricultural sector to boost food production for internal consumption and export.
He continued: "We need bold policy initiatives to revive our greenery option, the sort of initiatives that won us acclaim in the eyes of the world and brought about debt relief. Oil unfortunately is not a volume employer yet its destructive potentials area of a tsunamic proportion. The few who are fortunate to become involved in this sector become super rich. Our sole reliance on this one commodity has not helped our employment strategy particularly with local content as low as it is. So it creates a visible dichotomy that has aggravated the angst of the unemployed youths not only in the Niger Delta but throughout the country.
"In making laws for oil exploration and exploitation, we must ensure that we do not pollute our fishing waters and destroy our land, all of which would limit our potentials for the greenery option."
Attah's advocacy for Nigeria's return to true federalism and resource control found a ready congruence with the views expressed by Mr. Femi Pedro, Deputy Governor of Lagos State who represented his boss, Governor Bola Tinubu, at the occasion. While praising Pa Adesanya for his contributions to the country's development especially, Pedro did not fail to use the opportunity to draw attention to the seized local government funds meant for Lagos state. He stated that while true federalism would have tolerated the seizure, the patience of the people of the state was running thin. The banker-turned-politician did not fail to use the forum provided to show just how much he had picked up on the field of politicking as he subtly took on the seeming campaign moves of chairman of the event, Chief Tunde Fanimokun, who is aspiring to become governor of Lagos in 2007. During his address, the latter had taken liberty to make statements considered by many to imply some political image-laundering for himself.
The occasion was graced among others, by representative of Vice President Atiku Abubakar, Chief Titi Ajanaku, acting leader of Afenifere, Pa Reuben Fasoranti, Senator Femi Okurounmu, Chief Adebayo Adefarati, Senator Olabiyi Durojaiye, Group Captain John Ebiye, Navy Captain Joseph Adewusi and Chief Edet Amana. Others included Chief Ayo Adebanjo, Alhaji Aliko Dangote, Mrs. Funke Talabi who represented wife of Lagos State governor, Chief (Mrs.) Remi Tinubu and Chief Gani Adams, leader of Odua Peoples Congress (OPC).
Underpinning the air of celebration and joyful mood of the event, dances and cultural display by a group from Akwa Ibom State provided entertainment and fun for the gathering just as donations were also made by guests to further the cause of the foundation.

Original_Search_Title: Limbe Council Begins Demolishing Houses
Headline: Cameroon: Limbe Council Begins Demolishing Houses
Body_Text:
Authorities of the Limbe Urban Council in collaboration with the DO of Limbe, have started demolishing houses and structures within the municipality built on waterways. These structures have been obstructing the flow of rain water, which in turn, cause floods during heavy rains, posing threats to human lives.
The demolition started in Motowo quarters on Thursday, July 28. The Government Delegate to the Limbe Urban Council, Samuel Ebiama Lifanda, the DO of Limbe, Mbiwan Nchaffu, the Police Commissioner, are supervising the exercise.
The house of one Pius Ojong, a semi-permanent structure, was the first to be demolished. Located beside a culvert, a greater part of the house was on the bed of a stream flowing by. Downstream, at end of the house, was a makeshift embankment of polythene bags filled with stones.
Asked why he failed to heed calls to evacuate his house, Ojong said he was not asked to move but to make an embankment.
But the Delegate said it was not true.
Lifanda said the Council's evaluation technical team had been to Ojong's home several times and he was well informed of the fact that his house had been constructed against the regulation of building houses beside streams.
He was reminded that government safety regulations,required that his house be 15 metres away from the stream.
The demolition team then moved to Mawoh quarters where the house of a councillor to the Limbe Council, George Chenyi, was also destroyed. The said house too, was located beside a stream.
The downstream side of the house had already been partly destroyed by floods a few weeks back.
The team ended the demolition in Cassava Farms along the banks of the Ngen-ngele stream, where a pigsty was brought down.
Demolition To Save Lives
The Delegate had earlier on Wednesday, July 27, said the demolition exercise was aimed at pre-empting any further loss of lives and property in the municipality in the event of floods.
He was speaking in the conference room of the Limbe Urban Council in Bota to those whose houses and structures have been earmarked for demolition.
"Many of the houses built on waterways obstruct the free passage of run-offs and this is one of the main causes of floods."
The Delegate said this could no longer be tolerated. He told the people that the aim was not to put everyone's house down.
"We are aiming at those houses built on waterways and those which are in danger of being swept away in the event of a flood," he explained.
As to who will be affected, the Delegate said the Council had visited quarters that were recently affected by floods following weeks of persistent torrential rains. During these visits, he said houses were identified and marked out.
Plea For Time
Some of those to be affected by the demolition pleaded for one more week to look for alternative sites. But the Delegate said it was necessary to start off rather than wait for danger. The people had already been sensitised and with the persisting rains the worst could be in the offing.
2001 Floods, Landslide
On June 26 and 27 in 2001, a flood and landslide disaster hit Limbe Town claiming 24 lives, sweeping away quite a number of houses and property.
The extent of the damage that was valued in millions took quite some time and money for the government, the council and the population to recover from the immediate effects.
Speaking to The Post, the Delegate said the demolition exercise would continue in the days ahead. He called on those whose houses have already been marked to carry out the destruction, so as to save some of their materials.
The DO for Limbe, Mbiwan Nchaffu said, "if this city has to maintain its status, then people have to respect town planning regulations."
He reiterated the call for those whose houses have not yet been destroyed to take the cue and start doing it themselves.
Mbiwan said those who are decrying the demolitions today will certainly come to see reason for it in the near future.
Roadside Garages
The Delegate, in a meeting after the exercise said all roadside garages shall have to be moved. He said this decision was in line with the on-going exercise to decongest the highways and streets in order to keep the town clean and convenient for commuters.
He added that he had already given all the owners a week's deadline, which has expired.
As an alternative, the Delegate said the council has decided to lease out land to all those whose garages are located along the road from the town's entrance.
Lifanda said the council will stop all those who will not heed the call to move.
The DO said special attention should be given to Bonadikombo, Mile Four, which is the entrance to the city. He added that as an alternative, an avenue of ornamental flowers could be developed along the Mile Four stretch of road down to the town.
Opportunity For Youths
The Delegate announced that the Council has decided to lease out land to any person or youth interested in growing and selling flowers. He noted that the flower market is very lucrative, especially in Douala and many people are making a huge fortunes from growing and selling flowers.
It was for this reason that he said the Council, instead of employing youths as casual workers, has decided to offer them the opportunity.
He encouraged the youths not to miss this opportunity, as the leasing cost will be affordable.

Original_Search_Title: PC Allays Hunger Fears, Says Crop Harvest Better
Headline: Kenya: PC Allays Hunger Fears, Says Crop Harvest Better
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Coast Provincial Commissioner Cyrus Maina has moved to allay fears that people might die out of starvation. Maina said crop performance was good in most parts of the region particularly the lower Coastal belt but said crop failure was evident in areas away from the belt.
He named areas that had been badly affected by drought as Kinango in Kwale, parts of Lamu, Malindi, Tana River and Kilifi districts.
He said while last year close to 600,000 people faced starvation and needed urgent relief supplies, this year, the estimate figures for those in need of relief aid was lower.
He attributed this to good rains and use of the right seeds. He said a third of the region had sufficient food that could last for several months.
Most primary schools in Kinango and Samburu divisions have introduced school feeding programme to retain the pupils in class.
In Tana River District where there crop failure was witnessed for the second year running, several pastrolist communities have started moving to higher grounds looking for pasture.
There is 60 per cent food deficient at every household level in Kilifi District, area DC Ukur Yatani said.
A joint survey by the Office of the President, the World Food Programme, Ministries of Education and Agriculture, showed that the most affected areas were Bamba, Vitengeni, Chonyi and Kaloleni.
"Data is still being compiled to find how many people require relief assistance in the district," Yatani said.
In Bahari constituency, a third of the 250,000 people need urgent relief food aid.
Joe Khamisi, the area MP said though rains were good in the area, some parts bordering Chonyi and Ganze required urgent food aid.
In Mombasa District, Changamwe constituency, which borders Kaloleni and Kinango divisions require relief food to feed over 30,000 people.
Area MP Ramadham Kajembe said crop failure in the outlying areas of the constituency meant urgent measures was needed.
Nyanza Province traditionally a food deficit area, will have an unprecedented bumper harvest this year due to good rains.
Speaking to The Standard yesterday, Nyanza Provincial Director of Agriculture D K Njeru said the province has hit its annual food target.
"Nyanza has traditionally been a food deficit area especially in districts such as Nyando, Suba, Kuria, Homa Bay and Bondo, which have over the years relied on relief food," Njeru said.
He said the yield this year has been good across the board for staple crops like maize and beans including traditional food crops like finger millet, sorghum, cassava, potatoes and groundnuts in most areas and bananas in Kisii.
Meanwhile the country's breadbasket region of the North-Rift is expected to register a bumper harvest this season.
Apart from Turkana and pockets of West Pokot Districts, the rest of the vast region is expected to have surplus food harvests.
The National Cereals and Produce Board (NCPB) Public Relations Manager, Mr Kipserem Maritim, told The Standard that a survey showed an increase in maize production in the region.
Most of the maize is produced in Trans Nzoia, Uasin-Gishu, West-Pokot, Keiyo, Nandi South and North-Nandi districts.
He said maize harvest is projected to increase by 3 million bags from last year's 12 million.
Maritim allayed fears of possible food shortage saying there would be a bounty maize harvest.
He said the targeted maize acreage this year was 600,000 hectares but only 350,000 hectares had been covered.
