The department, he said, also target another 70,000 hectares of maize during the short rains later in the year. He attributed the projected increase in maize production to good prices, ready markets and farmers getting paid in time.
He said the average harvest of wheat in the province was 3 million bags and that it was expected to go up by 1 million bags.
The acreage for beans this year was expected to hit 280,000 hectares although only 155,000 hectares had been covered, he said.

Original_Search_Title: Kanyezi Aids Committee: a Role Model From Dedza for Malawi
Headline: Malawi: Kanyezi Aids Committee: a Role Model From Dedza for Malawi
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Dedza district has an HIV and AIDS committee that is engaged in the
fight against the pandemic using unbelievably meagre resources.
This is against a propensity for a lot of HIV and AIDS organisations to
be established purely to satisfy their founders own individual needs.
Most of the resources meant for the fight against the pandemic have
been used to purchase top-of-the-range vehicles rather than being used
specifically in this fight.
It is a breath of fresh air therefore, that there are still other
people out there who have dedicated their lives to assisting their
communities in this same HIV/AIDS fight for no other reason than to
overcome this greatest of challenges faced by countries in the sub
Saharan region.
The pandemic has taken a heavy toll in many of our villages in Malawi,
making many helpless as they are either infected or affected.
It is often too shocking to see many of the people one fraternizes with
being reduced to skeletons and cabbages before gradually succumbing to
the inevitable deaths due to HIV and AIDS related diseases.
A lot of initiatives as a means of intervention to fight the pandemic
have seen us creating institutions that could come up with 'a reasoning
strategy' to counter the Human Immunodeficiency Virus.
Worst still the virus has proved to be elusively calculative that the
world scientists have racked their brains in order to find a cure
against it, with little success in sight.
To mitigate the impact of the pandemic, the Malawi government
established in its twenty-eight districts what are known as District
AIDS Coordinating Committees (DACC). These are further split into
Community AIDS Coordinating Committees (CAC) that work in co-ordination
with Village AIDS Committees (VAC).
In Dedza district there is one Kanyezi Community AIDS Coordinating
Committee, which was established in 1998. This group is usually known
as Kanyezi CAC.
This CAC that is composed of people whose level of literacy is not so
high has achieved a lot in its fight against the pandemic and, by their
successes are often more knowledgeable on the pandemic than many better
resourced organisations.
The CAC has come up with a working operational structure that uses a
relay reaction comprising of a Home Based Care (HBC) Committee, an
Orphan Care Committee, a Youth Empowerment Committee and a Women's
Resource Mobilisation Committee that is created for Income Generating
Activities and Preventive Counsellors.
Felesta Zige is one of the Kanyezi CAC's trained HBC providers of which
221 are females out of a total 321.
She says sometimes when the patients do not have shelter the Youth
Empowerment Committee helps in mobilising the people in the area to
build a shelter for them.
Zige says as HBC providers their job also entails that they advise
those affected by HIV and AIDS related diseases on the importance of
producing a will when they still have some sanity.
She says others get angry when advised about this as they think the
insinuation is that they are about to die.
Zige says they teach them that a will is not for the educated, neither
is it for the rich nor those living in urban centres, because usually
when they tell people about writing out and leaving a will their reply
is always; 'that is for the rich who have vehicles.'
"During their illness we also register the number of children they have
and upon their deaths we consider the children as orphans. We hand them
over to the Orphans Committee, which also starts looking after them
thereafter.
Azele Kachale, the CAC's Orphan Care Committee Chair says his Committee
also works closely with the VACs which link up with parents and
traditional leaders in the villages to identify any orphans that need
taking care of.
Currently, the CAC looks after 6,400 orphans in 310 villages.
Phillipina Tsinkha is Chairlady for the CAC's Women Resource
Mobilisation Group that is a composition of women volunteers who donate
an agreed amount of money and time regularly. They have identified a
treasurer to keep the money.
Tsinkha says the women are allowed to borrow from the savings and pay
back with a small interest.
The treasurer releases the money after agreed and clear unanimous
instruction from the group through what is known as the Rotating
Savings Credit Association (ROSCA).
"We look at these interests as a profit which we then given out to
orphanages as part of our charity work," she says.
Perhaps the biggest project the CAC has embarked on is the
establishment of Community Based Child Care (CBCC) in the 310 villages,
which it looks after. The CBCC are also a major beneficiary of the
proceeds, which are generated by the women's groups.
Each CBCC has identified gardens, which all the committee members
jointly cultivate to produce food for orphans and the patients under
the care of the HBC committees.
Kachale says the gardens are therefore another source of funds since
they sell the surplus and the proceeds goes to activities like
assisting orphans or sensitising the youth about dangers of risky
behaviour, and care of those suffering as the result of the HIV and
AIDS.
"We also undertake the diversification of crops as we plant cassava and
sweet potatoes alongside the maize cultivation," he says.
Tsinkha disclosed that so far, 37 CBCC have each built feeding houses,
which they are using as catering points where food is prepared and
given to orphans and vulnerable children who come to there to eat a
regular meal.

"We do not only depend on the rains but during the dry season we even
cultivate in the dambos as this helps so much to mitigate any of the
food problems that we face like the just ended growing season where we
experienced drought," she said.
She said the preventive committees in all the VACs are responsible for
children of between 6-15 years whom they constantly teach about the
dangers of HIV and AIDS.
She said they believe what the CAC has been teaching the youth is
bearing fruits.
Often in the more rural areas all that is needed for some success to be
enjoyed in the fight against the pandemic is a little resource base and
a massive heart for volun-teer work. The work in Dedza should lead the
way as to how much can be accomplished with little.

Original_Search_Title: Napep Trains 5,073 Unemployed Youths in Jigawa
Headline: Nigeria: Napep Trains 5,073 Unemployed Youths in Jigawa
Body_Text:
Dutse — NO fewer than 5,073 unemployed youths were trained in various skills by the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP) under capacity acquisition programme from 2001 to date in Jigawa State.
The state coordinator of the programme Alhaji Abdulhamid Sadeeq disclosed this at the launching of resettlement of farmer's empowerment / capacity acquisition programme (CAP) held in Dutse, said the youth were trained in the state using the existing 563 training centres out of which 650 received their training in various skills under the NAPEP / NDE CAP programme.
Alhaji Abdulhamid Sadeeq explained that the state secretariat had registered and attached 87 unemployed graduates to 34 organizations for 9 months under the managed attachment programme and the sum of N10, 000 was paid to them as monthly allowance.
According to the coordinator, the state has received an allocation of 40 KEKE-NAPEP tricycle, seven were sold outright to individuals at the cost of N180,000 each and 33 were given out on loan for commercial purpose at the cost of N200,000, adding that most of them were in neighboring Kano State to generate revenue to the beneficiaries.
He maintained that the programme was designed to assist both the youth and old people across the state to become self reliant in the society, the state secretariat will soon conduct a micro credit facilities scheme in the state aimed at assisting various associations and groups.
Alhaji Abdulhamid advised people in the state who have interest to participate in the micro credit and farmers empowerment scheme to form or join association such as Rice, Cassava, Wheat farmers Association of Nigeria and also the associations should register with the state NAPEP for easy access to members.
Vanguard reports that NAPEP was established by the present administration to ensure well coordinated and monitored poverty eradication efforts nationwide. The programme focuses on the coordination of the activities of 17 federal parastatals as they function throughout the federation.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Stops Importation of GM Foods
Headline: Ghana Stops Importation of GM Foods
Body_Text:
Ghana has taken a strong stance against the importation and cultivation
of Genetically Modified (GM) foods in Ghana.
The Food and Agriculture minister, Mr. Ernest Debrah said the country
would reject, without hesitation, the importation of any Genetically
Modified (GM) foods, crops and materials into the country although it might
solve the famine problems being experienced, especially in the Northern
part.
This implies that the government of Ghana has resolved to oppose
anything to do with GM foods. Mr. Debrah said this last Friday in
Accra.
GM technology in agriculture first appeared in the mid 1990s in the
United States of America (USA), which is still the world's largest grower of
GM crops.
A decade later, while member states of the European Union (EU) proceed
cautiously on allowing commercial plantings of GM crops, increasing numbers
of developing countries are joining the US in allowing the commercial
planting of GM crops.
In 2004, 81 million hectares of land were under legal cultivation of GM
crops in 17 countries. This is around 1.6 % of the total agriculture land in
the world and the area is growing at a rate of 20 % every year. The year
also saw 8 million farmers legally growing GM crops, up from 7 million in
2003.
The actual number of farms growing GM crops and the amount of land given
over to GM crops are both likely to be much higher than the official figure,
as illegal planting is widespread, particularly in Argentina, Brazil, India
and Mexico as well as some portions of Africa.
The majority of the crops grown on commercial scale has been developed
by private companies and either crop would be used in animal feed or GM
cotton.
So far, private companies have shown little interest in developing GM
crops unless they have the potential to be bought and sold on a mass
scale. Because of this, only four varieties of GM crops, soyabean, maize,
cotton, and canola occupy 99% of commercial plantings, and are worth more
than $40 million each year. Majority of the crops are modified to resist
viruses and insects as well as tolerate chemical weed-killers.
By contrast, scientists and governments in developing countries are more
interested in research and commercialization of GM food crops for human
consumption and help ensure food security. Varieties of wheat, rice, sweet
potatoes, millet, sorghum, cassava and many other fruits and vegetables are
being developed in laboratories and test plots across the developing
world. The traits being tried out are largely insect and virus
resistant.
In developing countries, public institutions such as the Ghana Atomic
Commission in Ghana, fund much of the researches into GM crops.

Original_Search_Title: FEWS NET Warms of Possible Food Crisis
Headline: Mozambique: Food Aid Requirements for Mozambique
Body_Text:
London — The Famine Early Warning Systems Network,
FEWS NET, warned on Wednesday that southern and central
Mozambique are facing a possible food security crisis. 
 According to the Vulnerability Assessment Group and the Crop
and Food Supply Assessment Mission, 430,0000 people need
immediate and continuous food aid until next March, with an
additional 160,000 people who may need help later this year.
FEWS NET believes that decision makers should pay more
attention to these food shortages, and prepare contingency
planning measures to address the situation. 
 However, reports from the World Food Programme (WFP)
indicate that the Mozambican government and its partners have
prepared for problems, but have not been given the resources to
implement in full the measures they propose.
The food shortages are being blamed on severe rainfall
deficits across much of the country, resulting in a poor maize
harvest and significant reductions in the yield of other crops.
According to FEWS NET, food shortages are worst in the
southern region, where grain crops have been badly hit by the
lack of rain. It found that there are no significant food
deficits in the northern region, traditionally a food surplus
area.
The worst hit provinces are Gaza and Inhambane. FEWS NET
points out that markets in these provinces are limited, making
people more reliant on the harvest. It also warns that in these
provinces, cropping is limited to only one season, which makes
farmers more vulnerable to failed rains.
The report warns that there is currently a lack of food aid
for all those hit by the drought, stating that "at present,
available food aid resources only cover the needs of about 37 per
cent of those identified as requiring immediate assistance". 
 It continues that resources must be mobilised urgently now
to help those hit by the drought and to prevent the need for 
more expensive interventions like supplementary or therapeutic
feeding later.
The FEWS NET report points out that the Mozambican
government has already planned and begun to execute plans for
assisting in agriculture and water provision. The problem is that
donors have not met the needs. 
 According to a WFP report, 110,000 people received food aid
in June. However the WFP warned that while food distribution has
increased, breaks in the food pipeline are hampering efforts to 
step up assistance.
The WFP is working to meet the regional food crisis, in
which over ten million people need food aid in southern Africa.
Other countries that need help are Lesotho, Malawi, Swaziland,
Zambia and particularly Zimbabwe, where drought has come on top
of the destruction of commercial agriculture, thanks to a chaotic
land reform programme.
The WFP points out that much of Mozambique has experienced
reasonably good grain production in comparison to other countries
in the region, with a harvest of 1.92 million tonnes of grain -
just three per cent lower than a year ago. 
 However, it warns that the production disparity between the
fertile north and the dry south has increased significantly.
Three provinces in the north increased production by 12 per cent,
while the harvest in the south has fallen by 43 per cent.
As a result of this mixed harvest, and despite an increase
in cassava production, WFP estimates that 70,000 tonnes of grain
to feed 580,000 people will still be required this year.

Original_Search_Title: We're Set On the Best for Nigeria (ii)
Headline: Nigeria: We're Set On the Best for Nigeria (ii)
Body_Text:
Lagos — We also drew attention to support provided by the international community for other states with lesser than impressive reform programmes and political and security commitments like Nigeria. Our Team made sure that we explained in clear terms that our oil wealth not withstanding, on a per capita basis, it comes to less than 50 cents per Nigerian per day which makes us a poor country indeed.
Finally, we showed in very clear terms that monies saved from debt relief would at the federal level go to pro-people and pro-community development areas such as education, health care delivery, agriculture and food security and infrastructure such as roads, electricity, water supply and transportation. With our improved credit worthiness and image, there will be more private investment in Nigeria to provide employment for Nigerians, create wealth and reduce poverty. This would reduce crime, corruption, social decay and community dislocation. We gave commitment on fighting corruption and instituting a regime of transparency, accountability, openness and probity.
In the course of this campaign, we attended scores of meetings, workshops and conferences making a case for Africa and Nigeria. In the past two years, the Minister of Finance took every opportunity to campaign for debt relief with her colleagues, the 19 members of the Paris Club to whom we owe most of our debts and to other stakeholders. We equally took advantage of opportunities with the G8, the World Bank, IMF, and other bi-and multilateral organizations to make the case for debt relief for our country. I personally took this campaign to all possible fora and never minced words in making a good case for debt relief at the conferences of the World Food Programme (WFP), FAO, International Labour Organization (ILO), South Summit, Africa-Asia Summit, World Economic Forum, as well as at meetings with leaders of the United Kingdom, United States, France, Japan, India, China, Indonesia, Sweden, Norway, Finland, and The Netherlands, all in our effort to get a better deal on the debt issue.
I must confess that at some point it looked like we were fighting a lost battle. I know how many times my Honourable Minister of Finance wept for Nigeria. I recall the visit of a senior IMF official where we were told point blank that debt relief for Nigeria was more or less a pipe dream. In December 2000, Nigeria signed the Agreed Minute with the Paris Club thus paving the way for the signing of Bilateral Debt Rescheduling Agreement with each creditor country. With 19 members in the Paris Club you can imagine how strenuous this exercise was. It was completed in December 2004. At some point we were offered only 30% which we considered to be rather too small and too inadequate.
We had done our research and found that as far back as 1954, the London Conference had reduced Germanyís debt stock by 50%. In more recent times, Iraq was offered close to 80% after the fall of Saddam Hussein, Poland's debt was cut in half and all interest written off; Yugoslavia was given a 66% debt reduction without conditionality; Russia was given a 50% debt reduction without conditionality; Kyrgyzstan got 50% reduction, and Pakistan, Afghanistan, and others in Eastern Europe received various percentages of debt relief without conditionality. Of course we knew that our case was different, our location and role in the global divisions of labour and power was different; our continent or sub-region was different, and our issues and place in the perceptions and calculations of strategic global players were also different.
We were confident that with hard work, consistency, and effective policy at home, we would change the minds of our creditors and obtain debt relief. Even when the House of Representatives came up with a Resolution calling on Government to stop servicing the debts and to declare a unilateral default, we were cautious. This was because while we appreciated the support and mood of the House of Representatives, we also knew that although the Resolution might have served some purpose, the international community was unimpressed and told us so. The international community also drew our attention to the fact that such a line of action would have been detrimental to the cause we had been pursuing. They reminded us of similar action by Argentina and the consequences. Hence, we stuck to appeals, meetings, dialogue, and demonstration of what we were achieving with our reform programme. This strategy began to yield results slowly. In fact, a delegation of the National Assembly that visited some key countries found that opinion had started to shift in favour of consideration of debt relief for Nigeria. We appreciate the efforts of the delegation.
At a point in time, we were offered 70% off our debt servicing rather than the debt stock and we rejected this because it would not significantly affect the debt level. But we also knew that with our reform programme that assisted us in obtaining IDA-only status from the World Bank as well as the completion of the Debt Sustainability Analysis (DSA) of Nigeria, the door would be open for us. The DSA, completed with the support of the IMF and World Bank, was to show in clear terms how Nigeria could never get out of the debt trap unless substantial debt relief was granted. In addition to this, the study showed that the Millennium Development Goals, designed to cut poverty in half by 2015 could never be achieved in Africaís most populous nation without debt relief.
Our home-grown reform programme, encapsulated in the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (Needs), was monitored on a quarterly basis by the IMF, at the invitation of the government, in order to provide the global community with objective assessments and reports on how well we were doing and our positive ratings must have helped with the positive disposition of our creditors. Our commitment to good governance as well as our anti-corruption war showed positive results to the world and to our creditors in particular.
Negotiation with the G8 was the factor that finally opened the way. This was not an easy negotiation as the rules of the Paris Club operated in a way that required CONSENSUS among the 19 member-nations before debt relief could be granted. Given the traditional position of the Club that Nigeria was not a candidate for debt relief by any yardstick, we knew this was a gargantuan challenge that had to be overcome. That we are an oil producing country did not help matters especially in the context of rising oil prices and substantial improvements in our foreign reserve. But we were not deterred because we were convinced that a lot of policies and programmes were dependent on our obtaining debt relief. We also know that the debt issue is a political issue rather than an economic one.
The negotiations went on for months and, at times, at odd periods of the day and night including hundreds of phone calls and direct meetings. The understanding and support of our largest creditor, the United Kingdom, for our campaign for debt relief, in particular the strong and determined support of Prime Minister Tony Blair and the Chancellor of the Exchequer, Gordon Brown went a long way to enrolling the support of other creditors. In my recent trip to Europe, one of the Ministers we met did not hide her hostility to the idea of debt relief for Nigeria, based unfortunately, on old perceptions and stereotypes. But we had our arguments and at the end of the day, we succeeded in convincing the G8 that Nigeria deserved debt relief.
But the meeting of G8 Finance Ministers in early June of 2005 represented the breakthrough when in acknowledgement of the fundamental changes taking place in Nigeria, in support of our political and social reforms, in recognition of the courage with which we have fought corruption and economic indiscipline, and our commitment to fiscal prudence, they agreed to support a sustainable debt treatment for Nigeria within the framework of the Paris Club. The formal announcement of the decision to grant debt relief to Nigeria was made at the Paris Club meeting of June 29, 2005.
As I stated in my address to the nation on June 30, 2005, what we have now been given assurance to expect by the Paris Club is that Nigeria will clear its arrears of $6 billion of the $30 billion owed, following which there will be a stock reduction on Naples Terms while we will have to buy back the remainder. This will represent, for the first time, a total exit, if you like, total freedom from Paris Club debt.
The package in final terms that we are to expect would yield debt relief of about 60 percent on our current Paris Club debt. We shall pay off the 40 percent balance through a buy back operation. The total write off is about $18 billion which compares very favourably with the recent $40 billion write off of debts for the 18 highly indebted and poor countries of the world by the developed nations. This, debt relief offered to us, I am pleased and proud to say, is the direct product of our relentless and persistent endeavour over the past six years. We are taking advantage of a window of opportunity which the Paris Club of nations offered us.
Let me state, once more, for those who say we did not get any relief or that the terms were ambiguous, the debt relief for Nigeria is REAL! In fact, such amount of write off by the Paris Club in terms of procedure and sequence is unprecedented for a country that is not in chaos or that is not being rewarded for offering strategic military support. And even then, the IMF would insist on its own supervised programme to be put in place, consisting of the usual stabilisation and structural adjustment programmes with implementation certified by the Fund before any debt relief deal could be contracted. This was waived in favour of a Policy Support Instrument (PSI) as the NEEDS programme was considered good enough to manage our efforts at repositioning our political economy for sustainable growth.
What are the benefits of this debt relief? Let me reiterate the points as I made them before. First, it represents a direct saving on debt service repayment, interest, surcharges and other fees; second, it improves our credit worthiness in the global community and builds credible financial confidence for our transactions; third, it means that we will stop all debt rescheduling arrangements with the attendant costs once we clear our exit payments to be paid in two stages; fourth, the well over US$1 billion currently spent on debt servicing obligations annually will now be available for investment in pro-people and pro-development issues especially health, education, water, roads, and food security; fifth, investors and investments will come to Nigeria knowing we are no more classified as a ìbad and doubtful debtî country; sixth, new investments will create jobs and new wealth and this would translate into improved living standards; and seventh, foreign export credit guarantee agencies that had withdrawn insurance covers for goods and services because of the debt burden can now be expected to confidently review their positions thus eliminating the 100% cash cover currently required for imports into Nigeria. But more importantly, there is nothing like freedom- freedom from debt and the image that the debt relief and exit from Paris Club debt give to Nigeria.
To get out of the debt trap and lay a new foundation for growth, development and democracy, we must join hands and resolve to break with the bad past; identify new voices and new leaders; reject business as usual; vote for new values of accountability, transparency, fair competition, social justice, and the upliftment of the living standards of Nigerians. We must revamp, reconstruct and reposition our institutions and put in place the sort of leadership that can consolidate the gains of reform, sustain confidence in our economy from within and without, redirect attitudes away from the decadent past, and condition our people for hard work, efficiency, effectiveness, good governance, social justice, and collectively driven prosperity.
The economic reform agenda that is designed to reposition Nigeria for all round prosperity has now unleashed a new sense of mission and a patriotic sense of nation among our people. But these new energies can only be deployed for purposeful development if, and only if, we resolve to work together, eschew opportunism, discourage political grandstanding, protect our current gains, and plan for a solid future. It is to the credit of the Economic Management Team, the cabinet and all members of Government as well as all Nigerians that the world has accepted our reform agenda as a good model to be replicated elsewhere and this has played a major role in our obtaining debt relief.
Let me point out here that the recent 100 percent relief granted to 18 low income and heavily indebted countries in the developing world which included 14 African nations is in large measure a precipitate of our campaign efforts. As a leading African nation, with the Chairmanship of the African Union (AU) as part of our continental responsibilities, we never shied away from pushing the case for Africa. And we shall continue to do so. We have already made the case that while thankful for the relief, these countries will still require substantial assistance to get out of the abyss of poverty, underdevelopment, marginalisation, and crisis. We have also made a case for other highly indebted countries that were excluded from the current package.
Distinguished and Honourable Members of the National Assembly, it has been a tough and rough struggle but we thank Almighty God that today we can look back and feel proud that we did not get discouraged, diverted, intimidated, or forced to lose sight of our goals. Our commitment to Nigeria and Nigerians was always strong and on the four areas of resource flow identified by this Administration at the beginning- trade, investments, ODA and debt relief, we have virtually settled the issue of debt relief. We continue to do well on attracting investments and the record shows that so far, we are on an upward scale compared to the past. But we want to do much more. On the trade we are doing much better than when we came in and things are looking much brighter. Only recently, we exported cassava products to China and this is just one example of new opportunities for trade that we are opening up. I can assure you that this Government is fully committed to consolidating the gains of reform and empowering Nigerians to attain the highest points of their productive and creative abilities.
I can only say to those that doubted that we would ever get debt relief or those that felt that we were merely junketing around the world doing nothing; history and events have vindicated us. We are challenged and strengthened in our conviction by their position. But we urge them all to abandon their cynical and backward looking attitudes and begin to see the Nigerian cup as half full rather than half-empty. They should become part of the vanguard of hope for all. This is the only way to encourage our people to resist the agents of reaction, conservatism, negativism, backwardness, opportunism, violence, corruption, and socio-political rascality. Let us all join hands to build a new, secure, peaceful, productive, and democratic society.
Distinguished and Honourable Members of the National Assembly, how do we guarantee or ensure the future? This is the question that all Nigerians and even friends of Nigeria are asking today. We must protect our citizens from the scavengers and kleptocrats of the past and from their off springs and associates who continue to detest due diligence, accountability, responsibility, fair competition, transparency, due process, and social justice. We must learn from the past because those that fail to do so often repeat the mistakes of their forebears. We must collectively decide to ensure and assure the future of our children, the stability of our country, the solidity of our economy, and the progress of our society. We must work hard to contain those tendencies, individuals, and interest groups that wish to mortgage our future, undermine our foundations, contaminate our souls, squander our present, and compromise our happiness as a people.
The Ministry of Finance is setting up a Virtual Poverty Fund to monitor and track Millennium Development Goals (MDG) expenditures related to the budget and in 2006 this mechanism will be fully used to track budgetary expenditures arising from the debt treatment. A Bill on Public Sector Borrowing has been completed and will be sent to you shortly for consideration.
For the National Assembly, I urge you not to relent in your efforts at ensuring stability and peace in the nation. One way of doing this is to ensure quick passage of Bills that are before you or that are coming to you for consideration and enactment into law. The Fiscal Responsibility Bill, the Procurement Bill, Tax Reform Bill and other Bills associated with the reform agenda need to be in place to ensure that we move rapidly away from the past. We are reviewing the balance of the debts with a view to finding ways of settling them based on special repayment terms. We are also working on a Bill that would regulate the process of obtaining foreign loans by all levels of government. This will include pegging the interest on such loans to ensure concession.
We must all join hands in the fight against political rascality, fiscal indiscipline, arrogance of power, and bad leadership. Our war against corruption must be deepened and widened until we sanitise the entire society. We must publicly and privately never condone any form of corruption anywhere or try to accommodate or rationalize failures, weaknesses, disloyalty and ineffective leadership. This is one way to prevent a return to the past. Let me assure you all that the struggle to rid Nigeria of erratic power supply and the fight against corruption and the evil trio deadly diseases of HIV/Aids, malaria and tuberculosis will continue to be waged relentlessly.
Distinguished and Honourable Members, this is our story, our struggle, our successes, our hopes and our best wishes for Nigeria today, tomorrow and forever. After six years of solid hard work, sometimes with encouraging and sometimes with discouraging signs from within and without, God has granted us success in a near miraculous way. It is success for Nigeria and Nigerians who want to claim to be part of the success story. Of course we know that failure is an orphan. Let us all savour the sweet taste of the success but knowing fully well that the determination and singleness of purpose that has brought success will continue to be required to take us through the reform agenda. In this, I will not be found wanting. I hope that you too will not be found wanting.
May God continue to bless Nigeria.
Thank you.
Being the second and concluding part of the speech President Olusegun Obasanjo Tuesday presented to the National Assembly on the Report of the National Political Reform Conference and recent debt relief granted Nigeria

Original_Search_Title: Ebonyi to Site Projects in Border Communities
Headline: Nigeria: Ebonyi to Site Projects in Border Communities
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — To put an end to the incessant inter-communal clashes between it and its neighbours, Ebonyi State government has decided to establish infrastructural facilities between boundary communities that have history of land struggle.
Therefore, Governor Sam Egwu has located a building for skill acquisition centre for unemployed youths between Ebyia Local Government council in Ebonyi and Okokwu Local Government council in Benue State.
The chairman of Ebyia Local Government council, Barrister Augustine Nwankwagu, told newsmen yesterday that his council spent N18 million to equip the complex, which provides teaching and learning spaces for different types of trade, including metal fabrication, woodworks, auto-mechanics, information technology, catering and fashion designing.
He pointed out that the decision of the governor in providing the facility is informed by the need to engage the youths in noble ventures and keep them out of evil machinations.
The chairman said the youths "would be given soft loans to ply the trade they have learnt, to empower themselves economically and become useful members of the society."
He added that in a bid to encourage the people of the two communities to live in harmony, Ebonyi State government has also constructed more than 20-kilometer road that runs through Iziogo and Onuenyim to Okpokwu Local Government area of Benue State.
" This road would facilitate movement of farm produce and persons of the two communities. Those coming to the popular Nwofe market to buy yam and cassava would also find the road useful", he stated.
He disclosed that his council does not collect stall fees from traders in the Nwofe market saying that the peace and harmony being enjoyed by the neighbouring communities of the two states makes up for what could have accrued to the council by way of internally generated revenue.
The council boss added that the Amachara Comprehensive Health Centre which has bed space for 20 patients, a dispensary and staff quarters for resident medical personnel was equally located in such a way as to cater for the health needs of people on the interstate boundaries.
Nwankwagu commended the Traditional Ruler of Nwofe, Eze Emmanuel Nwojiji and his cabinet for their contribution in ensuring peace and progress in the area.

Original_Search_Title: A New Political Agenda Coming, Says Opadokun
Headline: Nigeria: A New Political Agenda Coming, Says Opadokun
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Three years after his exit from the pan Yoruba socio-political
organisation in which he had served for many years as the chief
scribe,Mr. Ayo Opadokun has been maintaining taciturn political
posture. Opadokun,a lawyer and human rights crusader, however, broke his
silence in this interview with Philip Ogunmade, where he explains his life
experience since then,his major commitment now to float a political party
along with his associates, his regrets following the alleged failure of
political figures,the aftermath of the confab and other sundry national
issues.
Three years ago, you quit Afenifere,the socio-political organisation
that you served for 15 years.What has life been to you since then?
Life has been fine.Life has been meaningful to me.It has given me the
opportunity of sitting down to review my little contributions towards the
socio-political activities of the time and of my nationality.I will say that
I'm thankful to God for what He used me to do in the lives of our people, as
a nation,within the geographical entity called Nigeria.I believe with all
humility that God had used me to make life better for the ordinary man.
I've been privileged in the last 3 years or so to be able to sit down
and reflect on my past contributions.I believe that I've received God's
guidance in a way that has been unimaginable.When I reflect on different
epoch on my service as the General Secretary of Afenifere and how God has
enabled one to contribute in the derivation and sustenance of credibility
for that organisation when I was its chief scribe,I will continue to thank
God.
So, I 've been in everyway thankful to God.I'm a little bit devoted now
towards the formulation of a much more credible alternative agenda to the
current shenanigans that Nigeria can witness in the political
landscape.
What in clear terms is the alternative agenda you talk about and how are
you going about it?
I believe that most Nigerians will agree with me that while some of us
were being killed,some loosing their blood,some loosing their sweat,some
others loosing their earthly possessions,others loosing almost everything
that was dear to them and some of us were being sent to different jail
houses,in our very credible campaign for the resurrection of democracy in
Nigeria,we believe that whatever government would be put in office after
that our struggle should be able to make life noticeably better than what
the situation had been;that we would be able to put in office much more
responsible government that would enhance the quality of lives of our
people.But unfortunately,that hope and aspiration have been subtantially
shattered by the state of affairs of the country today.There are matters
that are not treated by the current rulers,though they have all the
opportunities in life to change the terrible state of afairs for the
better.But I'm not impressed about their performances.Come to think of
it,the shortage of foodstuffs in Nigeria market today is
embarrasing.Salaries are poor across the length and breadth of the
country.Employment is dwindling. Although I'm not uinmpressed about the few
additional jobs that have been created through GSM and the recent greater
devotion to agriculture particularly that of cassava production.But these
are too small compared to the level of unemployment we now have in the
land.That's why Nigeria's rated as one of the poorest countries of the world
despite the abundance of richness of resources.
I'm totally uinmpressed by the quality of education being
provided.Again,another terrible situation has to do with the matter of power
supply.If only the situations were terribly bad in 1999,the sixth -year
sojourn of the current ruler is more than enough to radically change the
regime of power supply in Nigeria.It is a shame that we are yet to produce
more than 3,000 megawatts out of which it will be possible to transmit up to
2,500 mega watts of that power to the end user. And when there's no
power,there's very little that we can do.That leads to another one that
makes me so sad that this government has failed abysmally to rescurcitate
the only credible means of mass movement of people in transportation like
urban transport. There's criminal misuse of public funds.In the modern
world,the means of mass movement is the railway system.I don't know the
reason this government has not been able to create an efficient railway
system to work in Nigeria.So these have been my concerns.
You were talking about the contributions you made to the lives of your
people. Do you mean your people back in Kwara State?
No. I mean the people of Yoruba nation.
Are you talking about the contributions you made in the past or about
what you are trying to do at present?
I don't need to blow my trumpet.People have spoken more than enough
about that, as to their own understanding of my own contributions to their
existence in the country today.But then,now what I'm really doing in the
last few months...I 've been approached by much more responsible honest
men.I 've been involved in how we can really herald a much more
responsible,much more responsive, much more credible alternative agenda and
platform to the political shenanigans that's going on in the country
today.
Are you floating any organisation to that effect?
Nothing is ruled out.
What is your main agenda and how's it going to be rolled out?
You wait and see. When we are ready, we 'll let you know.
You were central to the activities of Nadeco during the pro-democratic
struggle. But one wonders why you are not involved now in the activities of
Pronaco .Why?
I feel it's strategic for me to be on the side lane watching events
which are unfolding.
In other words, you prefer to be complacent?
I'm still watching on the side lane. When I'm ready, you 'll know where my
jurisdiction is.
Shortly after you quit Afenifere,your people organised a civic reception
in your honour believing that you would have more time for them now.You also
said that you were leaving Afenifere to serve your people.But till today,you
have remained in Lagos,how then do you fulfil that dream?
If it's striking a balance,in Nigeria,what obtains with most political
leaders hitherto is to live in a certain place and develop strategic linkage
with their home towns or home villages.And they are available for situations
that demand their attention.What has happened since then has been that I'm
in constant touch with Offa.When there's a reason for me to go,I report
there regularly. And in the leadership of Offa Descendants' Union led by
Asiwaju of Offa, Chief Emmanuel Adesoye,he has always carried me along in
whatever happens there.When there's an issue,I go and remain there for as
long as it takes that issue to be adressed.You remember my political
hero,Chief Obafemi Awolowo.He was living in Lagos until the time he
permanently returned to Ikenne.Moreso,Senator Abraham Adesanya is in Lagos
and he's very relevant in Ijebu-Igbo.The late Ahmadu Bello was living in
Kaduna and he was on ground in Sokoto.
What is your comment about Afenifere?
No comment.
And why?
I have said that quite frankly,I will not allow myself to be used to
create a platform of confusion in Afenifere bearing in mind that God had
used me to generate and contribute to build credible profile for that
orgainsation when I was there.So,I won't have any reason to run any
commentary about it.
But do you still maintain individual relationship with some of them?
Yes.
Is such a relationship healthy?
Since I was born a Christian,the Bible in the book of Mark says if I
don't forgive, my heavenly Father 'll not forgive me.So,I have no grudge
against anybody.
What is your major commitment now?
I believe the current government has failed substantially to deliver
credible,adequate incentives to people.People like me who are privileged,
will partner with others to be able to make impact in the next
election.
That means you are planning to participate actively during the next
election?
I don't want to make that kind of categorical and very tall,profound
statement.But I want to say that I believe that if God permits,I will
partner with other persons to promote credible alternative agenda for the
Nigerian political landscape.
You are likely to seek an elective position?
You don't do a thing like that.Serious minded people like me won't get
involved in that kind of unnecessary agitation for contest.The primary
objective of an ideal political leader is to be part of a credible platform
that can convincinly sell its programme ideas in its manifestoes in such a
manner that can compel and win power so that those things contained in their
manifestoes 'll be manifested in concrete government policy for
actualisation.
The idea of wanting to come to politics because you want to be
governor,you want to be this and that is not what we consider as primary
purpose of politics.How many Nigerians will know that the current Prime
Minister of Britain,Tony Blair had been in parliament since 1980.He had
understood the terrain before going to hold the position of the Prime
Minister of his land.But here,certain elements want to come from the
bush.They want to be president,they want to be governors,they want to be
anything.That's is one of our problem.So,for people like me,that's not the
most relevant thing.What matters to me is constructing a credible political
platform that will succeed in articulating the most profound public policy
agenda that Nigerians can identify with and capable of organising itself to
win in an election.Whatever happens after then,will be as a result of what
the party's hierachical alternative think of you. So that shouldn't be your
primary objective,but how the system can achieve its objective.
Different groups like The Patriots and Pronaco had emerged in this
dispensation with little or no impact at all.How then do you think this new
platform you want to create will make any difference?
You know strategies are not disclosed on roof tops.What I can assure you
about this is that these people I'm associating with are not only
credible,they are sufficiently strong enough in their constituencies.Some of
them are in office today.Some are not, because the situation has been that
the manner by which the last transition took place,a number of credible
Nigerians did not take part and most of them were Abacha's political
confederates and they have remained in power.So,it has not created ideals to
make the situation better than it was.As a matter of fact,majority of them
were the major promoters of Abacha to succeed himself.Many of them are
governors and are in big positions.So,you can't expect that things 'll be
better.
Now let me refer to Pronaco and The Patriots which came with progressive
ideas and have not been able to achieve anything.I agree with you.One must
appreciate what created those organisations.One,those who constitute those
organisations are not political leaders.They have good intentions but they
don't really belong to effective political platforms that can appeal to an
ordinary man on the street as to where to go politically or who they should
vote for.Some of those organisations prefer to be neutral -that they are not
into partisan politics.The thing is,I'm not really into that kind of
arrangement.Power is the ultimate. Otherwise,you 'll remain in perpetual
deformity why these shadow elements will continue to govern and once they
succeed in manipulating their way into the public office,you can't deny them
of the right of saying they are speaking for the people.You who are
criticising them will be regarded as an interloper,that because you 've lost
election,you are angry.So, if serious minded people therefore, have their
way as to pack themselves into a strong formidable,cohesive,responsible and
very resilient political organisation that can challenge them in their
talks,that can make it impossible for them to make a repeat of the election
rigging arrangement,that is the kind of of thing I'm associating myself
with, so that politically,constitutionally,legally, we can bring on board a
much more credible governance into the Nigerian nation.
When will the platform emerge?
Let's wait and see.Consultations are still going on.
Are you the anchor?
No.You 'll be told like I said.When we are ready,the media is the most
important channel of communicating with the public.
So,highly placed,credible people are involved in this arrangement to
salvage our country.The situations we are witnessing is not cheering.I give
kudos to Obasanjo for the success he has achieved in getting debt relief for
Nigeria.I don't want to talk about people who collected the debts,who were
dubious because there's no tangible evidence about what they used those
debts for.Those of them who are not even in office now are greatly rich at
the expense of the Nigerian nation.
How do you see the just concluded Confab?
My view will be as follows: Between the regime of failure and
success,there are problems that have to be noticed.The conference provided a
room for proper articulation of some fundamental matters of our
land;provided room for those things that mattered to be presented to a wider
audience of the Nigerian people.Say for example,the South-South people were
able to provide more than enough evidence as to why they deserve more than
the current 13 per cent of the derivative principle of distribution.
Without inhibition,if there's any element of understanding of their
plights that petroleum has been wasting their assets,their land that has
been degraded and devastated and which cannot be rejuvenated;that cannot
even be restored when petroleum is no more available in that place,they need
enough money under a very concrete agenda,to put certain things in place
that will make men and women,young and old in that environment to begin to
say that this is what we have in bargain,that while we lost this,we were
able to make up for it.
Two,the other people who had suffered from one neglect or the other,we
were able to represent them in holistic manner before the generality of
people.It also provided some measures of opportunities for Nigerians to
identify with issues of importance as evidenced by the activities of zonal
leadership on critical issues of national restructuring like the issues of
resource control,rotation of presidency,issues of devolution of power .But
I'm totally devastated and disappointed by the leadership that emanated from
Yoruba land.But for Dr.. Tunji Otegbeye,Senator Femi Okurounmu,of all the
lots, all others would occur to me to have a marching order to go against
all the major pillars which the Yoruba nation has always campaigned for
rigorously,be on regionalism which provided Nigerian nation with the
opportunity of faster development in every facet.The people from Yoruba land
said their marching order was against regionalism.But South -South which had
wanted regionalism had to abandon their course because they wanted to
support their campaign for resource control.
The Middle Belt wanted regionalism.But the Yoruba elements that had been
foistered on Yoruba people were against regionalism.They had been exposed
for what they are.It also exposed some other elements from other zones of
the country.These are people who have failed to appreciate a fundamental
change in the political equation of Nigeria.And in that conference,there's
what I will call hidden agenda for which the conference was called.The fact
is that there, in that conference,there were strong allegations.Documents
were smuggled in -the so-called six-year single term thing in a curious
manner.The conference's secretariat denied it.And the Presidency kept on
saying it was so-called part of the conference agenda which the people
didn't see.So,these were the things that one could gather in that confab.I
suspect that what the promoters of this agenda wanted to achieve,they didn't
achieve it through that conference.I won't say this conference has failed
totally because it has laid some foundations.But I will say that it has
substantially failed.I have no doubt that there must be another
conference.
Given all these lopsidedness that characterised the conference,doesn't
it therefore mean that dialogue is not the solution to Nigerian problems?
Even if we were to be involved in internal warfare,at the end of it
all,we still must come to the roundtable.Therefore,
talking,conference,dialogue is still the ultimate.There is no alternative.I
dare say that the so-called National Political Reform Conference differed
completely from the national conference we agitated for.When with the leader
of the Nigerian Bar Association,Mr. Alao Aka Bashorun we came up with the
idea of national conference in 1989,we wanted to hold the meeting at the
National Theatre before President Babangida's security men prevented the
meeting from holding. The idea was for us to be able to push Nigeria forward
on these national questionsof equity and justice,political and economic
matters, build national infrastructure to address elements of
government,that have been devastated over the years.So,it's not that
dialogue has failed.It is because the objective rationale of political
conference was in total opposite direction to what national dialogue should
be.So,let's continue to be resolute about talking.

Original_Search_Title: Grassroots Disaster-Prevention in Western Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Grassroots Disaster-Prevention in Western Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A 1999 study commissioned by the United Nations Secretariat for the
International Decade for Natural Disaster Reduction (IDNDR) described
sub-Saharan Africa's vulnerability to natural hazards in the following
terms:
"The situation in most countries on the African continent continues to
deteriorate at an alarming rate, and the objectives of the United Nations
General Assembly resolution establishing the IDNDR are not being met.
"Disasters are not increasing because of the increase in the frequency
of hazards, but because of the increasing vulnerability to hazards."
This assessment can be applied to Kenya, particularly its western
region.
Flood season
Twice a year, as the rainy seasons draw near, Kenyans across the country
gaze expectantly at the sky in the hope of generous showers that will
fertilise the land most of them subsist on.
But in Kenya's western provinces, the rains are dreaded.
Every year, the region that stretches from the eastern banks of Lake
Victoria to the 2,000 m-high escarpment of the Nandi Hills is
flooded. Properties and livelihoods are drowned, and deadly waterborne
diseases such as malaria, bilharzia, cholera, dysentery and typhoid are
spread.
To cope with this recurrent natural disaster, the Kenyan Red Cross
Society (KRC) has launched a province-wide campaign to prevent damage to
people and property.
Despite a debilitating lack of means, crushing poverty, and one of the
highest incidences of HIV/AIDS in the country, some farmers have
successfully resisted the rising waters of the long rains, which drench the
region each spring.
Man-made erosion, nature's disaster
In the province of Nyanza, regular flooding began in 1985. The
ever-growing population has put unprecedented strain on the
environment. Tree have been felled to make firewood, houses and utensils,
and overgrazing by herds has accelerated soil erosion, clearing the way for
gushing streams that form in the plains during each rainy season.
Man's ecological footprint - increased soil erosion - has turned against
him by making farmlands more prone to flooding, in a region where floods
were once exceptional.
Every year in April rain pours down the Nandi Hills into the plains of
Nyanza, running through the rising rivers that feed Lake Victoria, the
world's second largest freshwater lake. The luxuriant swamps gradually
become clogged, and the rivers overflow to flood the fields. At the same
time, the lake expands to engulf arable lands.
In 2004, a single week of rain was enough to cause floods that wreaked
havoc on 10,000 residents, according to Philimon Majwa, coordinator of the
KRC branch in Kisumu, the provincial capital.
Entire fields of cassava [a staple food crop] were turned to swamps,
cattle drowned, huts were washed away, and the few concrete buildings of the
region were inundated.
Those who lose their meagre belongings every spring are too poor to
afford insurance schemes to cover their losses.
Isaac Adede, a geography teacher and Red Cross volunteer in Awasi, a
village located in the Nyando district flood plains, has tried to convince
victims of the floods to relocate to less vulnerable grounds. "But they
won't leave - after each flood they come back. They say they can't leave
their land, where ancestors and family members are laid to rest," he told
IRIN.
Talking about the weather
The KRC has been training local communities to prevent and respond to
floods for two years. "Until our leaders realise we must act at the
community level, we'll face the same problems every year,' Majwa
explained.
Red Cross volunteers have been teaching villagers that cutting down
trees increases the risk of flooding. According to Majwa, the poor level of
education in the region explains the lack of awareness of the risks that
erosion presents.
In addition, the KRC has also been training residents to be efficient
first-line responders to disaster by teaching them first aid, as well as
hygiene practices. "The main advantage of the Red Cross is our structure:
our network of volunteers goes right down to the community level," said
Majwa.
Raising local residents' awareness is crucial, as prevention is the only
possible mitigation for natural disasters of this magnitude.
Fighting torrents with shovels
To keep their settlements from being flooded, communities have no choice
but to prepare for the waters that inevitably come each year.
Pointing at what looked like a series of black veins on a hydrological
map of the Nyando plains, Adede explained the preventive strategy.
Soil in the region is mostly clay, preventing absorption by the
subterranean water tables. But proper drainage of the excess precipitation
into the lake must be ensured. This is done by draining existing waterways
and enlarging riverbeds, so that water runs all the way to the lake.
A process of de-silting must be repeated after each flood, as rushing
water shifts the soil and builds up banks that tamper with the flow. Despite
visible efforts, the government has insufficient machinery and means to
carry out sufficient de-silting.
Buildings and plots of lands must be protected from the rising
tide. Residents dig trenches and build dams around their plots, some
extending for hundreds of metres. When the flooding starts, most residents
take up shelter on fortified school compounds, some of which are accessed by
elevated footbridges made of rough wood planks.
Such operations require either heavy machinery, or intensive
labour. Both are hard to come by in the region. The local government is
cash-strapped, while residents are poor and struggle to cope with
disease.
How disease and poverty help hazards
According to a recent report by the Society for International
Development on Inequality in Kenya, the province of Nyanza is the region
hardest-hit by HIV/AIDS.
"Even though AIDS is a disaster in itself, it is also a contributor to
[the impact of] natural disasters," Majwa told IRIN. The virus wipes out the
workforce in its prime, leaving elders and youngsters to carry out the
demanding physical tasks of digging ditches and building dams.
Poverty is also a factor. The material means to take preventive measures
are often lacking.
As a result, food-for-work schemes have been carried out by local
authorities, providing meals to residents who spend a day labouring.
But this is rarely enough. "Last year, the government provided food on
three occasions, for about a day's work each time," said Adede.
Despite the enormity of the task, some preventive operations do
succeed.
Saving Badalangi
Badalangi, a region in Western province, north of Nyanza, borders the
upper part of Lake Victoria. It has flooded consistently every spring since
1969, due to man-made erosion and deforestation.
Kenya's government started building dykes in 1981, according to Ali
Husseyn, who volunteers at the local branch of the Red Cross. But they broke
repeatedly, unable to prevent millions of cubic metres of water gushing in
from local tributaries to Lake Victoria and flooding fields.
In 2003, the floods were so severe that they caused 21,000 residents to
be displaced and sheltered in temporary camps, Husseyn recounted.
Settlements in the region consist of tiny huts, made of mud stacked on
fragile wooden frames and covered by a thatched roof. The floods wash them
away instantly.
However, the following year the government dispatched army troops and
the National Youth Service, a community-support government organisation, to
help locals erect higher, more robust dykes.
The scheme was a success. In the spring of 2004, the inevitable floods
did not reach the fields.
However, the dykes must be constantly monitored and maintained.
Elias Nyongesa, a farmer from the village of Igingo, has participated in
the year-long effort to uphold the flood-resistant obstacles. "When the
floods come, if the dykes break, we have no alternative but to go to the IDP
camp," he said.
Villagers therefore reinforce dykes and ditches under the direction of
village committees. Trees are planted along the edges to hold the soil in
place. The fast-growing eucalyptus tree, which reaches a satisfactory height
in five years, is a favourite. Papyrus is sown between the dykes and the
threatening rivers, as it absorbs a high volume of water and breaks the
velocity of the streams' overflow.
But even if the villagers were willing to relocate to higher ground, out
of the reach of the floods, the government rarely provides plots to
re-accommodate entire villages.
And so, as the rains come, season after season, the wretched urban slums
of nearby Kisumu seemincreasingly attractive to those who have nothing left
to lose.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Transcorp Shops for N6bn
Headline: Nigeria: Transcorp Shops for N6bn
Body_Text:
The Transnational Corporation of Nigeria (Transcorp) Plc is currently
shopping for N6billion to enable it kick-start its operations in the oil and
agricultural sectors of the Nigerian economy.
According to its plans, of the N6billion required N5 billion is expected
to be ploughed into the planned 150,000 barrels per day (bpd) refinery, N50
million for investment in Cassava and oil processing while the rema-inder
N500 million will go into infrastructural requirements and professional
fees.
The projects plan, accor-ding to the Nigerian Stock Exchange Consult
Ltd, will make Transcorp profitable on its first year of operations.
As the report stated, the corporation is expected to rake in N3.2
billion as profit before tax, pay a dividend of N1.5 billion and pull in
gross revenue amounting to N13.9 billion.
In its second and third years of operations, the projection was that it
will achieve gross revenue of N14.9 billion, N16.7 billion, profit before
tax of N4.8 billion and N5.4 billion and pay dividends amounting to N2.5
billion and N3.3 billion respectively.
The corporation's direc-tors are projecting the above because Transcorp
plans to raise its refinery capacity from 150,000 to 400,000 bpd as the
years go bye, saying that the module refinery model will enable it
distribute products across the country.
According to them, "Transcorp will in the next five years dominate the
market of Africa, and penetrating markets of United States and Europe. This
initiative will in a short while transform Nigeria into a world class
economy and make it investment destination of the world.
Speaking at the launch of Transcorp last Thursday, Eze Festus Odimegwu,
MD Nigerian Breweries Plc said that the establishment of the corporation
portends that wealth creation is not an end on itself, but a way of
bettering the life of the people.

Original_Search_Title: Sweet Potato Seedlings, Cassava Pieces Under Transplantation
Headline: Ethiopia: Sweet Potato Seedlings, Cassava Pieces Under Transplantation
Body_Text:
Bahir Dar — The Amhara Agriculture and Rural Development Bureau said over 158 million seedlings of sweet potato and pieces of cassava are under transplantation in the state.
Public Relations Officer with the bureau, Shiferaw Tesfaye told WIC Sunday that 154 million seedlings of sweet potato and over 4.5 million cassava pieces have been planted in moisture stress woredas in the state.
He said 111,300,000 of the root crops were already transplanted on 1,160 hectares of land, adding that 67,328 family heads, including 14,768 women have participated in the cultivation process.
The disease resistant root crops would help in ensuring food security in food insecure areas, he added.
Shiferaw has also called on farmers to devote 300 square meters of land for the cultivation of sweet potato and cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Top IMF Official Visits Malaria Research Centre
Headline: Mozambique: Top IMF Official Visits Malaria Research Centre
Body_Text:
Manhica — The deputy director of the International Monetary Fund (IMF), Takatoshi
Kato, recognised in Maputo on Monday that endemic diseases, such as
tuberculosis, malaria and HIV/AIDS, have serious negative consequences on
African economies in general, and on Mozambique in particular.
Kato was speaking during his visit to the Manhica Health Research
Centre, about 80 kilometres north of Maputo, which is undertaking pioneering
work in the international efforts to find an effective vaccine against
malaria.
Although AIDS may have a higher media profile, malaria remains the
largest single cause of morbidity and death in Africa, particularly among
children under the age of five.
Kato said he was very impressed with the work undertaken at the Manhica
centre. The IMF, he added, would continue to encourage governments to
develop initiatives of this sort "since such diseases have a very negative
impact on economies".  The research at Manhica was "an added value" for
Mozambique and for Africa, he said. Finding a vaccine for malaria could
result in enormous savings "because a great deal of money currently spent on
treating sick people could be used in other development activities".
In Manhica, Kato, accompanied by Agriculture Minister Tomas Mandlate,
and Maputo Provincial Governor Telmina Pereira, also met with members of the
Eduardo Mondlane Cooperative that produces banana, cassava, maize, sugar
cane, and vegetables, and is also experimenting in fish farming.
During this visit he offered a cheque for 5,000 US dollars to the
members of this cooperative as a gesture of support.
Currently the 92 members of this cooperative are working an area of
about 65 hectares, and the provincial government regards its work as an
example of how rural people can wage the struggle against absolute
poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Human Rights Hampering Fight Against Prostitution - Lumbama
Headline: Zambia: Human Rights Hampering Fight Against Prostitution - Lumbama
Body_Text:
Lusaka — NORTH-Western Province Permanent Secretary Denny Lumbama has said human rights had made the fight against prostitution very difficult.
He was commenting on the high levels of prostitution in Solwezi.
He said it was difficult for the government or the police to fight the vice because of human rights.
Lumbama said the only way forward was to come up with a national policy that addressed this issue.
"Human rights have made the fight against prostitution very hard. Prostitution is a problem. We need to find a solution at a national level," he said.
Lumbama also explained that HIV/AIDS was being fought vigorously in the province under the help of Zambia National Response to AIDS (ZANARA).
He also praised Kansanshi and Lumwana mines, for putting in place the HIV/AIDS component at their work places.
And provincial minister Bert Mushala said that the province needed to start growing maize on a national scale.
Commenting on the hunger situation in the province, Mushala said there was hunger in the Zambezi and Chavuma districts.
He said 15.5 metric tonnes of maize had been sent to Zambezi district to avert the hunger crisis.
He said generally, the province had enough food in stock but flooding caused hunger in the two districts.
"Zambezi and Chavuma districts need relief food," he said.
He said the province needed to venture in large-scale production of cassava, sorghum, maize and sweet potatoes.
Mushala said the market was available for the crops because FRA was buying all the food crops in all districts in the province.
Meanwhile, Mushala said that trainee nurses have been bonded by government.
He said they would not go anywhere but would be sent to rural government clinics for two years after their graduation.
Mushala said this was because government paid for their training.

Original_Search_Title: 19,000 Nigerians to Benefit From US Job Opportunity
Headline: Nigeria: 19,000 Nigerians to Benefit From US Job Opportunity
Body_Text:
More than 19,000 youths are to benefit from the USAID funded Job and Business Development Services Initiative (Jobs Nigeria) across the country.
Already about 100 youths of Kano origin have benefited from the first phase of the program which is aimed at improving the quality of lives of low income and disadvantaged individuals in developing countries through the provision of sustainable human resource development services.
The program which is of the Opportunities Industrialisation Center International (OICI), founded in the early 1960s by the late Reverend (Dr.) Leon Howard Sullivan, an African-American, offers services in educational training, technical training, employment generation, conflict-resolution, reconstruction and peace building, civil society development, agriculture and food service. Others are health, nutrition and HIV/AIDS as well as entrepreneurship and business development especially in the areas of micro-enterprise, micro-finance and sustainable services among others.
Speaking at the official launching and commissioning of the OICI jobs Nigeria initiative in Calabar, Cross River state, recently, the OICI Country Representative in Nigeria, Mrs. Jacqueline Hardware Glin, stated that OICI operates through assistance with partners.
According to her, a number of partners have identified with it to ensure the presence and success of the program in their areas across the country, adding that the Cross River state government, being one of them, has provided accommodation and other facilities for the prog-ram's takeoff in the state.
She disclosed that more youths would benefit from the initiative which is funded by the United States Agency for International Development (USAID), stating that the issue of youth unemployment which of great concern to the United States govern-ment would be drastically reduced at the end of the year if necessary support and assistance is given to the initiative.
Governor Donald Duke expressed delight at the initiative of the OICI as well as the USAID for funding the program, saying that it will help improve the quality life of low income and generally disadvantaged persons in these countries.
Duke mentioned that the gesture by OICI will further support the government's intervention programmes aimed at poverty reduction.
"Our state's intervention programmes have been mainly in agriculture while indi-viduals and communities are encouraged to become self reliant through the growing of their own crops such as pine-apple, oil palm, cassava, cas-hew nuts, cocoa and castor seeds, among others," the governor added.
He also expressed the hope that beneficiaries of the OICI jobs Nigeria programme would team up with other potential entrepreneurs in the state to take advantage of the investment opportunities in agriculture and tourism which government has provided to fully participate in the economic growth of the state and the nation in general.
In attendance were the officials of the United States government especially the Ambassador of the United States in Nigeria, Mr. John Campbell, top government functionaries in Cross River and other partners of the OICI across the country.

Original_Search_Title: Transcorp: an Economic Elixir
Headline: Nigeria: Transcorp: an Economic Elixir
Body_Text:
Lagos — HISTORY was made in Abuja last Thursday, when President Olusegun
Obasanjo formally launched, Transnational corporation Plc (Transcorp),
Nigeria's first mega private sector-owned corporation.
The colourful ceremony took place at the Banquet Hall of the
Presidential Villa, and attracted eminent personalities from all walks of
life including top government functionaries, captains of industry,
stakeholders and the international community.
Transcorp was initiated by President Obasanjo as part of his economic
reform agenda. It was incorporated in November 2004 with the objective of
creating a globally competitive industrial conglomerate which will supply
world class products and services to local and international markets. It is
also expected to complete favourably with similar giant multinational
corporations in bidding for huge contracts within and outside Africa. It has
an initial capital of N6 billion.
Transcorp's focus areas are; Oil and Gas, Agriculture and Agro Allied,
Technology, Power and International Trade, all located in a free Trade
Zone/Industrial Park to take advantage of Federal Government's incentives
for export as well as to have access to port facilities.
The body is chaired by the Director-General, Nigerian Stock Exchange,
Dr. Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke and has top industrialists, bankers and oil magnets,
including; the President, Dangote Industries Limited, Alhaji Aliko Dangote,
Managing Director of Zenith Bank Plc, Mr. Jim Ovia, Chief Executive, Zenon
Oil and Gas Limited, Mr. Femi Otedola, Managing Director, Nigerian Breweries
Plc, Chief Festus Odimegwu, Managing Director/Chief Eecutive of Standard
Trust Bank Plc, Mr. Tony Elumelu, Managing Director of First Bank of Nigeria
Plc, Mr. Jacob Moyo Ajekigbe, Chief Execuitve officer of Orange Drugs, Chief
Tony Ezenna and Chairman of International Procurement Services Limited,
Alhaji Waziri Kabim Mohammed, among other board members.
Various speakers at the occasion stressed the need to restructure the
nation's economy to increase its efficiency to enhance the level of
industrial production and to re-orient production towards products and
processes in which the country has comparative advantage.
President Obasanjo said in a key note address at the occasion that
government decided to throw its weight behind Transcorp because of the
private sector's leading role in rebuilding Nigeria.
While formally launching the body President Obasanjo said government had
agreed to grant special concessions to Transcorp. They include: license to
build a 400,000 barrels per day refinery (to be built in phases); license to
build an Independent power plant, access to the Federal Government Cassava
report for the construction of a Cassava processing export facility, and
designated land mass for the construction of Free Port facility, among
others.
He said "we are mindful of the fact that large scale projects, which can
stimulate large-scale economic activity, will require huge capital
investment. And we also know that as much as foreign investment is desirable
because it comes with skills, technology and experience, domestic capital is
preferable because it stays commited to the investment since the investors
it represents live at home, work at home and will most probably retire at
home."
Speaking further, President Obasanjo enumerated eight benefits that
accrued from the recent $18 billion debt relief granted Nigeria by the Paris
Club of Creditors.
Listing them (benefits) the president said the debt relief presented a
direct saving on debt service repayment, interest and surcharges and other
fees adding that it "improves our credit worthiness in the global community
and builds credible financial confidence for our transactions". Besides, he
said, "it means that we will stop all debt rescheduling arrangements with
the attendant costs once we clear our exit payments to be paid in two
stages; the well over US$1 billion currently spent on debt servicing
obligations, will now be available for investment in pro-people and
pro-development issues especially in health, education, water, roads and
food security."
Continuing, president Obasanjo said "investors and investment would come
to Nigeria knowing we are no more classified as a "bad and doubtful debt"
country. New investments will create jobs and new wealth and this would
translate into improved living standards," among others.
Welcoming guests earlier, Mrs. Okereke Onyiuke said Transcorp would
serve as a vehicle to exploit emerging opportunities in the global market
place such as supplying cassava feeds to the livestock producers of Western
Europe, or supplying the United States markets not just crude oil but also
the refined petroleum products that have higher margins.
She said, "Our operating procedure has been fashioned as a conglomerate
with effective subsidiaries managing our business ventures in these
different focus areas. That will mean that technical experts in each of
these areas can be focussed on their areas of expertise while the overall
board, will provide direction, strategic planning, as well as access to
international finance and global markets.
"The Nigerian public will have a unique opportunity to participate in
the activiites of Transcorp, early next year, when we hope to make it a
public quoted company listed on the Nigeria Stock Exchange. This means that
our people both here as well as in the Diaspora, can invest their savings in
Transcorp and make superior reforms."
Speaking also on the occasion, Alhaji Dangote noted that Transcorp was
Nigeria's answer to the Choebols of Korea (Daewoo, LG, Samsung, KIA, among
others) which used scale economies to pull their country out of economic and
industrial backwardness
He said Transcorp was established in response to the need to establish a
"mega corporation," "created" in Nigeria, built by Nigerians and owned by
all Nigerians, which will respond to market opportunities which require
heavy capital investment not just at home but in the sub region and around
the world. According to him, such an entity would be the vehicle to
reposition Nigeria in Africa and the world as a nation ready to compete in
global markets.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Price Drops As Traders Prepare to Stock
Headline: Uganda: Maize Price Drops As Traders Prepare to Stock
Body_Text:
