Kampala — MAIZE grain has been unstable with prices dropping from Shs240 to Shs180
per kg in Kisenyi market.
The cheapest grain this week was recorded from Busoga and Kiboga at
Shs400 per kg while Masaka and Arua had the highest wholesale price of
Shs450 and Shs400 respectively.
The price of Cassava chips again went up due to scarcity increasing
prices to Shs420 per kg from Shs400 per kg in Kisenyi market last week.
Kampala
Kampala markets had good supply last week for most of the
commodities. Beans [Nambale) supply in Kisenyi and Owino markets was offered
at Shs450 per kg, Kahuura at Shs500 per kg and Yellow beans at Shs600 per kg
off lorry price. Matooke prices dropped in all markets to Shs175 per kg. A
bunch sold at Shs4,000.
This is a due to the good supply and low demand in the market. Irish
potatoes have increased in price because the Singo harvest season is coming
to a close. Singo Irish sold at Shs30,000 a sack of approximately 120-130kgs
at Owino market and Shs33,000 in Nakawa while Kabale Irish and Kisoro sold
Shs34,000 and Shs35,000 in Owino market respectively.
Tororo
There was an increase in the supply of commodities such as millet,
sorghum, rice, Soya, groundnuts and Irish potatoes. This is as a result of
harvesting season in and outside Tororo thus high supply. Meanwhile cassava,
beans, sweet potatoes and fish remained highly priced following their
scarcity.
Lira
Farmers have started planting beans for the second season while at the
same time preparing for simsim planting. Most commodities are gradually
coming to the market especially the newly harvested crops but with high
moisture contents. The general price level is falling due to good supply and
a lot of maize has found its way to Mbale in exchange for plenty of
beans. Sorghum is being ferried to Kampala where it's has a better pay.
Kasese
Groundnuts harvest is going in Kilembe, Rukoki, Maliba and
Kyabararungira sub counties. Prices have fallen due to increased
supply.
Mbale
Last week maize was offered Shs20 lower than the previous week at Shs240
per kg due to the harvest season. Other commodities that registered an
increased supply include maize flour and sorghum grain.
Arua
Rain was received in some parts of the district accompanied by strong
winds causing a lot of destruction to food crops like beans, cassava and
maize particular in Aroi Sub-county. Beans and Sorghum flour prices rose by
Shs100 and Shs50 respectively to Shs550 and Shs600 at wholesale.

Original_Search_Title: Citizens Groan As Prices of Foodstuffs Hit the Sky
Headline: Nigerians Groan As Prices of Foodstuffs Hit the Sky
Body_Text:
Lagos — A visit to any of the markets nationwide for foodstuffs could leave an
average Nigerian with high blood pressure. Indeed, a Lagos housewife, Mrs
Cecilia Igwe, told Vanguard last Tuesday that she always has headaches each
time she goes to the market because of the rising prices of foodstuff. After
such trips, Mrs Igwe's worry is always how to convince her husband that
house-keeping is not enough.
According to her: "My husband gives me a fixed monthly food allowance
for the entire family irrespective of the spiralling increases in prices of
foodstuff which seem to occur every week. I frequently ask him to supplement
what he gave at the beginning of the month as it is hardly ever enough."
She said that sometimes her husband obliges her by giving her additional
money, but sometimes he does not.
"In such cases I have to supplement the money myself from my meagre
income," she said.
Mrs Igwe's frustration was re-echoed recently by a top female politician
and cabinet commissioner when she said at a public event organised by
women's rights activists that housewives in the state do really bear the
brunt of galloping prices of foodstuffs as their husbands still give them
fixed allowances for providing food in the house notwithstanding the
frequent hikes in prices of foodstuffs.
"But as women we have to handle the situation by ensuring that we
supplement whatever our husbands give us; at least to ensure that their
children are fed," she said.
However, it is not only women in the state who are worried. Already, the
spiralling costs of foodstuffs have put regular and balanced meals beyond
the reach of many Nigerians. Although this situation has manifested
regularly over the years, the situation this year may prove to be more
serious than what has been witnessed in the recent past.
A foodstuffs seller at Oshodi railway line market who identified himself
as Okwudiri Stephen lamented that he and many of his colleagues had
abandoned selling certain foodstuffs because they had been priced out of the
reach of the common people. He cited the example of ogbono, a common
ingredient used in preparing soup by many Nigerians from the southern part
of the country.
He said: "Last year we used to buy a bag of ogbono for N12,000, but now
it is selling for between N45,000 and N50,000 at Abakaliki, Nsukka, Ondo and
other places where we used to buy it."
As a result of the high cost of ogbono, he said many of the foodstuff
sellers don't sell it anymore.
Stephen further said a bag of melon -- another soup ingredient enjoyed
by many Nigerians -- which they used to buy for N9,000 last year in Keffi,
Nasarawa now sells for N13,000.
"In addition, we now pay N450 to convey a bag of it to Lagos. But the
cost of transportation last year was only N250," he said.
He said as a result of the hike in price, a small milk tin measure of
melon now sells for N30, up from N15 which was the price last year.
Further probes by Vanguard revealed that the price of garri, one of
Nigeria's most popular staple food has also gone up astronomicaly. A garri
seller at Alamutu, Mushin market who identified himself as Young Akitoye
said: "We used to sell a bag of white or red Bendel garri for N1,800 or
N2,000 last year, but now it is N3,300."
According to him, "the staple was sold for between N2,200 and N2,500
just last December. But suddenly in January, it climbed to N2,800, N3,000
and now N3,300."
Akitoye lamented that the price of garri has continued to climb steadily
since 1999, adding that a bag of the commodity was sold for N500 only six
years ago.
Besides, the price of smoked fish from the northern part of the country
has almost doubled in the last one year. A fish seller who identified
himself as Abubakar Bello said that he used to buy a bag of fish for N6,000
in the Lake Chad area of Borno State but now, the same bag of fish sells for
N11,000.
He continued: "To convey a carton of smoked fish from Lake Chad area to
Lagos used to cost only N100, but now it is N500, and my bus fare is
N3,500."
The price of imported rice, another widely consumed staple food in
Nigeria, has also risen beyond the reach of most Nigerians. At Agege market,
a 50-kilogramme bag of the popular Gold Cap Rice which sold for N5,600 last
May has risen to N6,000. Other foreign brands like Royal Crown and
Vietnamese Rice now sell for N5,800 and N5,500 respectively. Indeed, the
hike in prices of foodstuffs has been confirmed by several bodies across the
country.
The Federal Office of Statistics (FOS) in its monthly Consumer Price
Index dated April 5, 2005 confirmed the upward hike in the prices of
foodstuff. According to the report: "The composite Food Index rose by 3.4
per cent in February, 2005." The report also revealed that the level of the
composite food price index was higher than the corresponding level a year
ago by 18.7 per cent.
However, observers of the agricultural sector have argued that prices of
staple food tend to go up during the dry season. But this view does not
tally with the FOS' report which showed that the hike in food prices in the
last one year surpassed what obtained in the corresponding period last
year. This is a disturbing signal to Nigerians.
Nevertheless, some middlemen have blamed the hike in prices on the
increase in transportation fares following the last hike in the prices of
petroleum products. In addition to this, there are also rumours that the
prices of some food products like garri have gone up as result of a recent
policy adopted by the Federal Government which stipulates that at least 10
per cent of the content of our local bread should be sourced from the
crop. It is also believed in some quarters that the recent widely publicised
agreement reached between the Federal Government and China for annual
exchange of 500,000 metric tonnes of cassava chips for agricultural
equipment may have also partially caused the sudden scarcity of the product
and the subsequent hike in the price of garri.

Original_Search_Title: Transcorp: Emergence of the New Mega Corporation
Headline: Nigeria: Transcorp: Emergence of the New Mega Corporation
Body_Text:
Lagos — Only those who were fortunate to witness the formal launching and
unveiling of the plaque of Transnational Corporation of Nigeria Plc
(Transcorp) that took place last Thursday at the Presidential Villa, Aso
Rock, will appreciate the message of the new transformation by the President
Olusegun Obasanjo administration.
"It is now my distinct honour and pleasure to formally launch the
Transnational Corporation of Nigeria Plc to the glory of God, the benefit of
all Nigerians, and the progress of Nigeria. May God continue to bless
Nigeria," the President declared.
Most of the people at the launching venue felt that the above statement
by the President has demonstrated strongly his vision and mission for this
country.
The President had earlier noted that "today represents another positive
milestone in our collective journey towards the repositioning of Nigeria for
peace, security, growth, development and democracy. If you look around you,
and if you are honest with yourself, you will agree that this is a new
Nigeria. Even the expectations, confidence, and determination on your faces,
tell me that we are all prepared to join forces to ensure that we are all
prepared to join forces to ensure that we move away from the past and
embrace reform to the benefit of our people and country. This is why, I must
thank you all for honouring the invitation of Nigeria's private sector today
to witness the formal launch of Nigeria's own global company, Transnational
Corporation of Nigeria Plc".
In her welcome address during the occasion, the Chairman of Transcorp,
and the Director General of the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE), Dr. (Mrs.)
Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke expressed the feelings of most of the people present
when she said: "The Obasanjo administration has shown clear determination
that they are committed to a total redevelopment of Nigeria's economic
environment and the private sector is clearly in the lead with this new
direction."
She noted that "never before in the history of Nigeria have we witnessed
so many changes and reforms being pursued at the same time."
Rhetorically, she gave an insight into the Obasanjo administration's
achievements thus: "It all started with the National Economic Empowerment
and Development Strategy (NEEDS), then we saw fiscal reform, then came
budget regulation and due process, we then witnessed the telecommunication
revolution, after which we saw a new discipline in monetary controls and
government spending, then came the all impressive pension reform which is
expected to mobilise over N600 billion for investment in Nigeria."
On the Transcorp, Okereke-Onyiuke told the President that "you have done
it again; in your time Nigeria has moved from mega projects to mega banks
and now to mega corporations. The Transnational Corporation of Nigeria Plc
is your brainchild, your vision which has now become our mission."
She told the President that the team he brought together has worked very
hard to bring the corporation to reality, while they also believed that they
have designed a winning strategy that will not only put Transcorp in a
position to supply goods and services to markets around the world, but also
to provide for Nigeria its very own blue chip company where the research,
innovation, processes and decisions are undertaken by Nigerians here in
Nigeria.
Okereke-Onyiuke assured the President that Transcorp would raise the bar
in quality and process delivery and change the attitude of the world as it
regards made-in-Nigeria products.
She further assured Obasanjo that "the team at Transcorp is made up of
well meaning Nigerians who share your passion and dedication to rebuilding
Nigeria."
The 32 core members of the initial committee working to bring the
corporation to life include Alhaji Aliko Dangote (President, Dangote Group),
Mr. Eze Festus Odimegwu (Chief Executive Officer of Nigerian Breweries Plc),
Mr. Jim Ovia (Managing Director/Chief Executive of Zenith Bank Plc),
Mr. Jacob Moyo Ajekigbe (Managing Director/Chief Executive Officer of First
Bank of Nigeria Plc), Otunba Funso Lawal (Chief Executive Officer of the
Charterhouse Group, a Lagos-based consulting and financial advisory services
group), Mr. Femi Otedola (President and Chief Executive Officer of Zenon
Petroleum & Gas Limited), Mr. Tony Elumelu (Managing Director/Chief
Exe-cutive Officer of Standard Trust Bank Plc), Mr. Tony Ezenna (Chief
Executive Officer of Orange Drugs), Alhaji Waziri Mohammed (Non - Executive
Director, Oando Plc), Mr. Adegboyega Olulade (an investment banker,
stockbroker and financial consultant), and Mr. Nicholas Okoye who is
Transcorp's technical secretary and business strategy adviser. He is also
technical adviser to the Diretor General/Chief Executive Officer of the
Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE).
Speaking also at the occasion, Eze Festus B.O. Odimegwu, Managing
Director/Chief Executive Officer, Nigerian Breweries Plc commended
Obasanjo's vision to raise a new Nigeria which has been demonstrated by the
establishment of Transcorp.
Reinforcing his desire to build a new Nigeria, Obasanjo declared that
the Federal Government has agreed to grant a number of concessions to the
newly launched Transcorp to demonstrate government support for the mega
company.
Obasanjo announced that his administration will grant the company
licence to build a 400,000 barrels a day refinery (to be built in
phases).
He added that the government has also agreed to grant licence to build
an independent power plant; access the Federal Government cassava report for
the construction of a cassava processing export facility; designated land
mass for the construction of Free Port facility; continued support to help
open up-markets on the African continent; and to make Transnational
Corporation of Nigeria Plc a partner in the nation's current policy on
private-public partnership, creating additional opportunities to develop
large scale projects in oil and gas, power and Information and
Communications Technology (ICT).
The President said that although the corporation will not solve all our
economic problems and put Nigeria squarely on the path to economic
prosperity, it is a very good start and if well operated or managed, it is
bound to serve as a strong anchor for our economic progress.
He said the government is throwing its weight behind Transcorp because
it is a homegrown initiative made up of local business people who have
commitment, tenacity, courage, vision, patriotism, exposure and the
resources to see the initiative through.
"Our recent reforms in the pension sector are expected to stimulate the
mobilisation of domestic capital on an unprecedented scale, and now with the
US$18 billion debt relief granted to Nigeria by the Paris Club of Nations,
Nigerian firms that will dare to compete globally will be doing so from a
very strong backdrop," Obasanjo said.
He went ahead to list the benefits of the debt relief which included a
direct saving on debt service repayment, interest, surcharges and other
fees.
The President added that the debt relief will improve Nigeria's credit
worthiness in the global community and builds credible financial confidence
for the nation's transactions.
Also, the country will benefit from the stoppage of all debt
rescheduling arrangements with the attendant costs once, the country clears
its exit payments to be paid in two stages.
He explained that the well over US$1 billion currently spent on debt
servicing obligations will now be available for investment in pro-people and
pro-development issues especially health, education, water, roads, and food
security among others.
In a detailed comment on what Transcorp stands for, Mr. Nicholas Okoye,
Technical Secretary/Business Strategy Adviser, Transnational Corporation of
Nigeria Plc, said the Corporation was incorporated on November 17,
2004.
According to him, Transcorp is the initiative of the President Olusegun
Obasanjo's administration aimed at giving Nigeria the competitive edge to
complete globally.
"This is a bold step to provide for the nation, one mega firm that can
penetrate markets around the world as well as dominate the local markets in
products and services that were previously serviced by foreign firms or to
service markets that have been completely neglected," said Okoye.
He said that Transcorp is Nigeria's answer to the Cheabols of Korea
(Deawoo, Samsung) which used scale economies to pull their country out from
the abyss of the developing nations' vicious circle.
Okoye said that Transcorp will do the same for Nigeria, while providing
its investors with a good return on their investment.
He noted that "the business model is designed to meet five core revenue
centres with a sixth one, which leverages off competitive advantages
inherent in the others. This include Oil and Gas, Agriculture and
Agro-allied Business, Information Technology, Power, International Trade and
Free Trade Zone/Industrial Park."
On Transnational Oil and Gas, Okoye said the Corporation will invest in
both the upstream and downstream sectors of the Oil and Gas industry, while
a concession from the Federal Government for a license to build a new
refinery is already in progress.
This license, according to Okoye, will permit the Corporation to build a
scalable facility using the latest technology in petroleum refining.
He added that the facility will not only meet the local demands for
refined products but also it will export refined products to the rest of the
world.
It is also his view that the Corporation will also participate in
upstream activities, especially in the emerging deep off shore exploration
and production business.
Transcorp is expected to bid for blocks as well as provide world-class
production facilities for holders of the other blocks in need of the most
modern technology in farming out crude oil.
The Corporation has plans to acquire Pipe Line infrastructure of the
Petroleum and Pipe Lines Marketing Company (PPMC), and expand on that
network to develop Africa's largest network of Pipe Lines for crude, refined
products and petro-chemicals.
Okoye said that Transcorp is presently accessing the value and integrity
of the nation's refineries to decide whether an acquisition in the on-going
privatisation exercise would be necessary.
Besides, Transcorp agriculture strategy is being built on the Obasanjo
administration's policy on agriculture, the Corporation's technology is
expected to further advance Nigeria to the promise-land.
THISDAY checks showed that heavy investment in software development,
outsourcing, biotechnology, call centres, and hardware assembly will form
the beginning of Transcorp's drive to develop a business in technology.
"Africa's technology requirements exceeded $40 billion in 2004. And
almost 99.8 per cent of these funds were spent on foreign vendors and
foreign technology companies. Transnational technology will be set up in the
transnational industrial park (export free zone). This centre will roll out
solutions for industrial applications in inventory controls, shipping,
production and supply chain management. It will also serve as a commercial
research and development facility for Corporate Nigeria and Africa," said
Okoye.
For the Transnational power, information made available to THISDAY shows
that the Federal Government has agreed to grant a license to build an
independent power plant to supply residential and commercial residents.
It is also expected that Transnational Corporation will export to the
governments of Ghana, Equatorial Guinea, Senegal, San tome, Gambia,
Bukinafaso and so on.
Okoye recalled that Africa is spending over $150 billion yearly on
importing consumer products.
"Transcorp will redirect the main production facilities in China to meet
our transfer prices and supply these markets. When this is successful, we
will then have a value chain of trade and investment built on the back of
consumers of Africa. This will expand Transcorp's ability to supply to
larger markets of Europe and the United States and achieve greater
scale. This process will be managed by a careful strategic plan of acquiring
the production rights for Africa from major manufacturers in China, Taiwan,
Singapore, Japan and Korea.
Okoye further disclosed that Transnational Industrial Park/Export Free
Zone will be established near the coast line to enhance the nation's ability
to do international business as well as attract the right partners going
forward.
According to him, all the business units that have production facilities
such as refining, raw material processing, agro-allied business as well as
information technology centre will be sited in the industrial park.
He added that "this unit will use most of the funds for working capital
to buy goods in large volumes from Asia and to supply them to the West
African sub regional markets.
It would be recalled that Transnational Corporation recently signed an
Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with Zenon Petroleum and Gas Limited.

Original_Search_Title: Point of Order: the Debt We Owe
Headline: Nigeria: Point of Order: the Debt We Owe
Body_Text:
MR. President has been telling those of us who have blamed him for
globe-trotting for one year of his first four years in office that what he
had been gunning for has come our way. 

From a country nobody wanted to see
or listen to, we are so loved now, and thanks to Mr. President's singular
effort, that those we owe are even ready to forgive us our financial
trespasses. I join many who are happy at our President's achievement, and
would like the congratulations to also rub off on Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, one
of the most ideologically focused finance ministers we have had. If there is
anyone who can lay more claim to the knowledge of the intricacies of the
workings of financial institutions that call the shots in the West, and on
earth today, let that braggart step forward and display his
credentials.
But if no one is saying something that the cassava grower can
understand, I would like to be better briefed on what it is that we stand to
gain by coughing out at this material time a whopping $6 billion to start
with, then another $6 billion so that we would not have to pay a billion
dollars a year for the next 12 years and accumulate an additional $10
billion in debt. I am getting confused myself. But this is what I thought I
saw our darling Ngozi telling us on television the other day to explain why
we should be happy that our debts are being forgiven on terms by the Paris
Club. But in my own way of looking at what we call the future, I wonder
whether someone out there is not taking us for a ride, and celebrating that
we fell for their ploy. I must explain my ignorance in these matters and
confess that my contribution is from the perspective of the one who says
only today matters and wants tomorrow to take care of itself.
Let me put the cards on the table in four scenarios. Scenario One is the
Present Loss Scenario. Scenario Two is the Future Gain Scenario. Scenario
Three is the Bad Debt Scenario. And Scenario Four is the Reparation
Scenario. Let us accept a timeslot from which we analyze the situation. Let
us think of 15 years from now. But let us first look at what we have been
into over the years. This thing we have been into will form part one of this
presentation. Next week, we should look at the four scenarios in some little
more detail.
A little more than a quarter of a century ago, we found ourselves
borrowing to meet whatever commitments led us into this path of difficult
return. With the re-emergence of civil rule in 1979 and the difficulty that
came our way with maintaining structures which up to now we have been
recklessly pushing to expand and retain, we increased our indebtedness to
well-organized international bodies. Some of the things we borrowed money to
provide were consumables, including rice from the paddy fields of
Asia. Umaru Dikko is the key personality I remember very well because he was
in charge of ensuring that Nigerians received their allocations of imported
food. We have vast lands that can grow rice, and it is there on the records
that Uncle Ben rice had its origin in the Niger Delta. But we would not grow
rice, or any other food. The oil money was there and we were credited with
boasting once upon a time that our problem was not money but what to do with
it! Gowon who was said to have made the statement was too much of a
gentleman to have condescended to the level of scheming to manipulate the
public treasury. Many others designed ways of taking away our money even
when we no longer had enough to meet our urgent commitments.
So with the fall in the price of oil and consequent reduced earnings, we
found it difficult to reduce consumption. But before the next line of credit
would be granted, we had to be subjected to demeaning conditions. The
different creditors would insist that we acquire a testimonial of good
behaviour from the International Monetary Fund. IMF had to be sure we were
better managers of our economies. And they decided what better management
was. They even had offices in ministries that handled money. Their demands
included our withdrawing subsidies of any description, reducing the work
force, distancing governments from businesses through privatization; and
devaluing the national currency. For some 20 years now, we have been laid on
the financial slabs of western interests. What we borrowed has been repaid
over and over, but the arrangements were such that the more we paid, the
more they told us we owed! If you refuse to service the debts, your
factories would be grounded because the parts to run them come from their
production lines.
As at now, our factories are not running because the warehouses make it
more as rented quarters for churches. So instead of factories bursting with
goods being produced by our teeming unemployed, the familiar sight is an
army of ushers and worshippers asking God to give them a daily bread they
have no opportunity to work for. The infrastructures are underdeveloped. The
educational institutions are not worth the name. The products of such
institutions are intellectual knit wits, and the certificates they come away
with are not reflective of any serious tutelage.
As at this time when we are being forgiven our debts on terms we should
now be able to guess, the situation has not improved. A new name for
dismissing people from work has changed from downsizing to rightsizing. Even
when monetization of entitlements has become national policy, we are owing
those who are affected. Nationally, local contractors are owed, and
pensioners are not paid as and when due. In fact, some years ago, in 2002,
we were owing them more than one trillion naira. The programmes we have, if
any at all, to cater for the aged, the youths, women and other deprived
groups hardly leave the drawing boards. Our policemen are poorly paid and
the reaction time to emergencies is zero. At the time of writing this, a
cargo plane crash-landed at Murtala Muhammad Airport and it took a full 24
hours to get planes flying again. We do not have facilities to tackle urgent
challenges.
Last week, I listed some of the many priority areas the President
promised to start addressing within the first six months of his taking
office on May 29, 1999.
These are the areas - the crisis in the Oil Producing Areas; Food
Supply, Food Security and Agriculture; Law and Order with particular
reference to Armed Robbery and to Cultism in our educational institutions;
Exploration and Production of Petroleum; Education; Macro-economic Policies
- particularly Exchange Rate Management, etc; Supply and Distribution of
Petroleum Products; The Debt Issue; Corruption, Drugs, organized fraud
called 419 activities, and crimes leading to loss of lives, properties and
investment; Infrastructure - Water Supply, Energy, Telecommunication, Ports,
Airways, national Shipping, Nigerian Railways etc; Resuscitation of the
Manufacturing Industries; Job creation, and creation of conducive
environment for investment; Poverty Alleviation; Housing - both Civilian
Housing Programmes, and Barrack Refurbishment and New Constructions for the
Armed Forces and the Police. ECOMOG; Health Services; Political and
Constitutional Dialogue; and Women and Youth Empowerment.

Original_Search_Title: Attah: a Governor's Rising Profile
Headline: Nigeria: Attah: a Governor's Rising Profile
Body_Text:
Lagos — Events in recent may have indicated that the profile of Governor Victor Obong Attah of Akwa Ibom State is increasingly on the rise as the governor may have taken his brief of office beyond mere governance, writes Olawale Olaleye
Deep and sound, Governor Victor Obong Attah of Akwa Ibom State has since his assumption of office in 1999 exhibited that there are far more that a politician can do in office than merely overseeing the affairs of the state or whichever position. And subtly, he has been able to distinguish himself in far more noble areas to underscore this position. Representing a variety of interests, Attah is in certain quarters known as the father of resource control, while some call him a true federalist or democrats. Yet, in other places, especially in the academia, he is most referred to as an intellectual or better still, a distinguished lecturer.
His struggle for resource control cannot be over-emphasised in the politics of the South-south region of the country. Perhaps, sensitisation was low on resource control until Attah took the fight head long. But today, everyone truly born of that region now sees the need to be in charge of their God-given natural resources. Their agitation has been hinged largely on the level of underdevelopment that has over the years ravaged the region. And now, they have realised it is about time they took their future into their very hands. They have though not won the struggle outrightly, they still have not failed in the real sense of the struggle. A concise analysis of the stalemated National Political Reform Conference (NPRC) can justify that viewpoint.
A couple of months ago, precisely in May, 2005, Attah was in Lagos to give a lecture. The Centre for Free Speech, a non-government organisation had organised a lecture on Freedom of Expression in Africa and Attah was invited to dissect the prospect and challenges of such. His grasp display of understanding in such issue was what usually cut his admirers unaware. And as if planned, the following month, June 2005, Attah was again invited to Lagos State however this time at the Lagos State University (LASU).
The initiative was of the MBA (Master in Business Administration). It was their 10th anniversary ceremony and Attah was billed to speak on "The Practice of Full Tenets of True Federalism as prerequisites for lasting democracy in Nigeria. That occasion afforded many to appreciate Attah's understanding of the concept of true federalism and its tenets therein. He did not betray the intrigues of politics. He was deservedly hard on the topic and did justice to it.
Come July 25, 2005 Attah will be in Lagos State for the third time. Following the sequence of invitation since May, he is this time, invited by the Senator Abraham Adesanya Foundation to the fourth in the series of lecture at the Chartered Institute of Bankers, Victoria Island, Lagos. Attah, a prospective awardee is however billed to deliver a lecture titled "Taming Our Angst: The Greenery Option". According to the invitation letter addressed to the governor, the implication of the choice topic is going back to agriculture as a core option in the nation's drive for industrial growth.
Naturally, while Attah has the leverage, according to the letter to alter the topic if he so chooses, it however appears a more interesting topic to him because despite oil, the governor has over time, engaged in massive agricultural development in the state. Those who are his fans have said a lot about Attah's love for agricultural development in the state. Small wonder, the governor was quick to encouraging the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC)'s cassava project. Chairman of the commission, an Akwa-Ibom State born Nigerian, Ambassador Sam Edem was said to have chosen to kick-off the programme in the state because of the governor's disposition towards agriculture. Today, so much has been said on the impact of the agricultural initiative, apart from being adjudged the best in the federation. For instance, it is believed that the move which is a target of the youth and women has been able to create the middle class, a social status that is said to be missing in the annals of the system in the state a long time ago.
However, back to the brass tacks of core politics, some political pundits have begun to ascribe Attah's moves to subtle political plans. Though, none has been able to establish what Attah's interest could be and in what sphere of the political turf, especially that the 2007 election year is fast on the track, it is however believed his moves are strategic positioning to be able to secure the number two tickets by the time the chips are completely down. This is though, against the calculation of his people who are strongly canvassing for the presidency.
Attah, it is believed may have continued to undertake such steps in other to prepare himself for whatever contingency come 2007 even though he has refused to give clues as to where and what he may eventually end up with.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo: Nothing Better Than Being Debt-Free
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo: Nothing Better Than Being Debt-Free
Body_Text:
Abuja And Uche Obike in Lagos — President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday called on the business community to fully appreciate the feat achieved last month when the Paris Club of creditors agreed to write off 60 per cent of Nigeria's debt. He said "there is nothing like freedom from debt and the image that the debt relief and exit from the Paris Club debt give to Nigeria."
Obasanjo who was speaking at the launch of the Transnational Corporation of Nigeria, a mega company floated by some top Nigerian private sector operators, called on the private sector "to be part of a new Nigerian movement to keep our country debt-free."
He also rescinded the decision of the Federal Government to stop issuance of licenses for building of oil refineries by granting a concession to the new corporation.
He said the benefits of the debt relief granted Nigeria by the Paris Club range from direct saving on debt service repayment, interest, surcharges and other fees to improving Nigeria's credit worthiness in the global community.
He said other benefits from the debt relief are that investors and investments will come to Nigeria, new investments will create jobs and new wealth and "foreign export credit guarantee agencies that had withdrawn insurance covers for goods and services because of the debt burden can now be expected to confidently review their positions thus eliminating the 100 per cent cash cover currently required for imports into Nigeria".
The President said that the debt relief will improve Nigeria's credit worthiness in the global community and build credible financial confidence for the nation's transactions.
Also, the country will benefit from the stoppage of all debt rescheduling arrangements with the attendant costs once it clears its exit payments to be paid in two stages.
He explained that the well over $1 billion currently spent on debt servicing obligations will now be available for investment in pro-people and pro-development issues especially health, education, water, roads, and food security among others.
While emphasising that the Federal Government "is throwing its weight behind Transnational Corporation", Obasanjo said the present era of globalisation is a time "when nations and business people must think boldly and expand their horizons beyond the local market. It is only such bold and creative efforts that can increase turnover, create job opportunities thus reducing social tensions, improve incomes and living standards, and put nations on the path to sustainable growth and development".
Obasanjo also described board members of Transnational Corporation among whom are Director General Nigeria Stock Exchange, Dr. Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke Chief Executive Officer of Nigerian Breweries, Festus Odimegwu; President Dangote Group, Alhaji Aliko Dangote; Managing Director of Zenith Bank, Jim Ovia; Managing Director of First Bank of Nigeria Plc, Jacob Moyo Ajekigbe; Chief Executice Officer of Charterhouse, Otunba Funso Lawal; President of Zenon Petroleum, Femi Otedola; Managing Director of Standard Trust Bank, Tony Elumelu; Chief Executive of Orange Drugs, Tony Ezenna; Chairman, Nigerian Railway Corporation, Waziri Mohammed and an investment banker and stockbroker, Adegboyega Olulade as "vocal business people who have commitment, tenacity, courage, vision, patriotism, exposure and the resources to see the initiative through".
He added that "nations the world over, today, are pulling their best brains and resources together to better position their peoples to compete in a more sophisticated global economy and Nigeria will not be different. It is no longer enough to dominate your local environment. Firms from traditionally large economies have expanded into regions and markets all over the world and have put their nations on a path to sustainable economic prosperity."
The President said that although the corporation will not solve all our economic problems and put Nigeria squarely on the path to economic prosperity, it is a very good start and if well operated or managed, it is bound to serve as a strong anchor for our economic progress.
He said the government is throwing its weight behind Transcorp because it is a homegrown initiative.
"Our recent reforms in the pension sector are expected to stimulate the mobilisation of domestic capital on an unprecedented scale, and now with the $18 billion debt relief granted to Nigeria by the Paris Club of nations, Nigerian firms that will dare to compete globally will be doing so from a very strong backdrop," Obasanjo said.
Obasanjo said the world was now passing through an era when nations and business people must think boldly and expand their horizons beyond the local market.
"It is only such bold and creative efforts that can increase turnover, create job opportunities and reduce social tensions, improve incomes and living standards and put the nation on the path of sustainable growth and development," he said.
Obasanjo also directed Secretary to the Government of the Federation, Mr. Ufot Ekaete, to ensure the public private partnership becomes a reality and invite the private sector operators to the next retreat of top government functionaries ministers and permanent secretaries.
The president also pledged to encourage Nigerians to subscribe to the Transcorp shares as soon as they were floated at the stock exchange.
Okereke-Onyiuke, in her welcome address, said "the Nigerian public will have a very unique opportunity to participate. Early next year, we hope to make Transnational Corporation of Nigeria Plc a publicly quoted company listed on the Nigerian Stock Exchange. This will mean that our people both here as well as in the Diaspora can invest their savings in Transcorp and make superior returns. In the process, they will be part of the new Nigeria".
She, however, urged "officials of State Governments and other arms of government to understand our direction and to partner with us to build a stronger, more vibrant Nigeria. We ask Nigerians to join us and we ask the world to watch out for us, because Nigeria is coming". He added that the corporation would not die even after the end of the Obasanjo administration, just as it would not accept government money.
She commended the Obasanjo administration for showing clear determination that it is committed to total redevelopment of Nigeria's economic environment.
"Never before in the history of Nigeria have we witnessed so many changes and reforms being pursued at the same time," she said.
Okereke-Onyiuke recalled that "the government started with the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS), then we saw fiscal reform, then came budget regulation and due process, we then witnessed the telecommunication revolution, after which we saw a new discipline in monetary controls and government spending, then came the all impressive pension reform which is expected to mobilise over N600 billion for investment in Nigeria."
She added that the operating procedure of Transcorp has been fashioned as a conglomerate with effective subsidiaries managing their business ventures in these different focus areas.
Dangote, in a remark at the occasion said "Transcorp is determined to extract value out of the Federal Government's initiative on cassava. We intend to do this by investing in a modern large scale cassava processing plant which will allow us to supply the $17 billion animal feeds markets in Europe as well as supply the N150 billion baking industry at home with flour supplements. We will enhance the cocoa export markets and introduce cutting edge processing for chocolate concentrates, which will guarantee us larger markets in the global community".
He said, "our technology business is expected to be second to none. We will focus on providing infrastructure to the various emerging industries that depend heavily on technology especially the telecommunications industry. This means that we do not intend to be a service provider at this time but will invest in technologies that form the backbone of that industry. In addition, we will create for the first time in Nigeria, a technology village where young people will study the latest technology applications and design solutions for homegrown industrial applications".
"Our investment in an independent Power Plant is a clear strategy to have a sustainable energy source for further expansion of our businesses. Our Power Plant will supply our businesses as well as other businesses that are located near our industrial park/free port zone," he said.
The formal launch during which Obasanjo unveiled the plague of the corporation was also attended by Governors Ahmed Muazu (Bauchi), Sam Egwu (Ebonyi), Gbenga Daniel (Ogun), Abba Ibrahim (Yobe) and Donald Duke (Cross River).
Transcorp is expected to put in $150 million towards the building of a refinery that is expected to put out 400,000 barrels of oil per day.
Transcorp was incorporated on November 17, 2004 to, according to its Chairman, Mrs. Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke, "provide for Nigeria its very own blue chip company where the research, innovation,processes and decisions are taken by Nigerians here in Nigeria". The corporation recently signed a Memorandum of Understanding with Zenon Petroleum and Gas Limited.
Other concessions granted the corporation are a license to build an Independent Power Plant, access to the Federal Government cassava report for the construction of a cassava processing export facility, designated land mass for the construction of free port facility, continued support to help open up markets on the African continent and to make the corporation a partner in Nigeria's current policy on Private-Public Partnership.

Original_Search_Title: President Elated, As First Indigenous Multinational Takes Off
Headline: Nigeria: President Elated, As First Indigenous Multinational Takes Off
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has formally flagged off Transnational
Corporation of Nigeria, a business conglomerate floated by leading Nigerian
private sector operators, signalling a new era of deeper indigenous
initiative in the country's economic development.
At a well-attended ceremony held at the Banquet Hall, State House Abuja
on 21 July, a clearly elated President Obasanjo explained why Government was
throwing its weight behind Transnational Corporation.
Because it is a home grown initiative, the President contended that in
this era of globalisation it is only such bold and creative initiatives that
can increase turnover, create job opportunities thus reducing social
tensions, improve incomes and living standards, and put nation on the path
to sustainable growth and development.
He described the board members of Transnational Corporation as "a vocal
business people who have commitment, tenacity, courage, vision, patriotism,
exposure and the resources to see the initiative through" adding in today's
world, nations are pulling their best brains and resources together to
better position their peoples to compete in a more sophisticated global
economy and Nigeria will not be different.
(Click to view photographs of the President inaugurating Transnational
Corporation at the Banquet hall, State House, Abuja)
He granted some concessions to the new corporation, which include,
- License for building of oil refineries, obviously rescinding the
earlier decision of the Federal Government to stop issuance of
licenses.
- License to build an Independent Power Plant,
- Access to the Federal Government cassava report for the construction
of a cassava processing export facility,
- Designated land mass for the construction of free port facility,
- Continued support to help open up markets on the African continent and
to make the corporation a partner in Nigeria's current policy on
Private-Public Partnership.
Chairperson of the company and Director-General of Nigerian Stock
Exchange, Dr. Mrs Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke in her welcome address, said Nigerians
at home and in the Diaspora, would have a unique opportunity to invest in
the company, next year when the its shares will be listed on the Nigerian
Stock Exchange.
She commended the Obasanjo administration for showing clear commitment
to total redevelopment of Nigeria's economic environment adding that never
before in the history of Nigeria have so many changes and reforms been
pursued simultaneously.
The board members of Transnational Corporation include, Stock Exchange,
Okereke-Onyiuke, Chief Executive Officer of Nigerian Breweries PLC, Mr
Festus Odimegwu, President Dangote Group, Aliko Dangote, Managing Director
of Zenith Bank, Jim Ovia, Managing Director of First Bank of Nigeria Plc,
Jacob Moyo Ajekigbe, Chief Executive Officer of Charterhouse, Otunba Funso
Lawal and the President of Zenon Petroleum, Femi Otedola.
Other are the Managing Director of Standard Trust Bank, Tony Elumelu,
Chief Executive of Orange Drugs, Tony Ezenna, Chairman, Nigerian Railway
Corporation, Waziri Mohammed and an investment banker and stockbroker,
Adegboyega Olulade.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Outlines Measures to Boost Cassava Production
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Outlines Measures to Boost Cassava Production
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Government on 21 July outlined a number of measures to
integrate cassava production with the industrial sector.
With an annual output of 33 million metric tonnes, Nigeria is rated the
world's largest primary producer and consumer of cassava.
Special Assistant to the President on Food Security, Mrs. Oluwatoyin
Adetunji stated this at a two-day international seminar on "Adding Values To
Cassava: A New Agricultural Revolution" at the Lagos Airport Hotel,
Ikeja.
Mrs. Adetunji listed some of the measures to include:
- Establishment of special units with a mandate to look into the
creation of market opportunities for cassava, since Nigeria has great
potentials in the production of the commodity
- Setting up of the Presidential Initiative on Cassava and the
Committees on Increased Cassava Production/Processing and Export to fashion
out ways of increasing the country's productive capability, value addition,
and sourcing for both domestic and foreign markets
- Directing bakers and other confectionery makers to include 10 per cent
cassava flour in the production of bread and other allied goods
- Increased assistance through the provision of micro-processing
centres, tractors for land preparation and drying equipment to reduce the
cost borne by farmers and processors of agro-allied products
- Providing tractors to states and local councils at 50 per cent subsidy
since 2003 to increase cassava production, and
- Increased funding of the Nigerian Agricultural Credit and Rural
Development Bank (NACRDB) to meet the present challenge in the sector.
She further stated that government has funded research projects that
would produce five high yielding cassava varieties adaptable to all the
ecological zones in the country.
The workshop was organised by the Centre for Sustainable Agricultural
Development (CASD), Lagos in collaboration with the International Institute
of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), Ibadan, Oyo State.

Original_Search_Title: Ekounou Roundabout: Traffic Hold-up Scares Drivers
Headline: Cameroon: Ekounou Roundabout: Traffic Hold-up Scares Drivers
Body_Text:
Its takes about 30 minute to cross from TEXACO filling station to Chambre Frois.
Have you stopped a taxi for Ekounou these days? Many taxi drivers do not want to ply the neighbourhood for fear of traffic jam which has intensified at the Ekounou main roundabout. It takes over 30 minute to drive from TEXACO main petrol station to "Chambre Frois," a distance of about 500 metres.
Several reasons have been attributed to the traffic hold-ups. The roundabout is surrounded by markets from all its four entrances. At peak hours, sellers and buyers completely block the main lanes with bunches of plantains, bags of cassava, vegetable and other foodstuff. Make-shift counters and huts have also gradually narrowed the main lanes, causing pedestrians to dispute space with vehicles.
The haphazard location of off-licences and warehouses is another major problem. The go-slow reaches its peak when cargo and brewery trucks begin delivering their goods from one customer to another. Often, brewery trucks block the road with crates of beer. "Cargo boys" who cruise from one part of the market to another, fearlessly transporting luggage across the roads to various taxi points for onward transmission to other parts of the town also obstruct the free-flow of traffic.
An illusionary policeman, popularly known as Samedi, is a stumble bloc. In the absences of traffic police officials, he owns the road. Samedi who is said to be mentally deranged, rushes from one lane to the other directing vehicles to opposite directions. One will hardly distinguish Samedi from a police officer as he dresses in the official traffic police helmet over kaki green and white gloves. Sometimes he stands on the way of vehicles, demanding CFA 500 as bribe.
Ekounou roundabout is one of the spots regularly used by gamblers and magicians. From all four entrances, eager crowds submerge the road, nodding and clapping to the songs and drums of magicians who display snakes and alligators, given deaf ears to cars hooting from all directions. Gamblers display their art by the roadside, attracting huge crowds which tussle with drivers for space.
The police is however at work. They run from angle to angle, pushing the crowd and gathering foodstuff on the motor-way. The traffic officials told CT that the width of the main lanes leading to the roundabout must be enlarged in order to reduce regular traffic hold-ups and accidents.

Original_Search_Title: FG Licenses Transcorp to Build $250m Refinery
Headline: Nigeria: FG Licenses Transcorp to Build $250m Refinery
Body_Text:
Abuja — A private sector-driven mega company, Transnational Corporation of Nigeria Plc (TransCorp), with investment interests in five core areas, was formally unveiled yesterday with the Federal Government granting it immediate approval to build a $250 million (about N33.25 billion) refinery in Lekki Free Port Zone, Lagos.
The company has already signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with Zenon Petroleum, an indigenous downstream marketing company, which is providing the initial take-off capital of $100 million. TransCorp is expected to provide the $150 million balance.
The facility is projected to refine 400,000 barrels per day (bpd) of crude oil on full capacity, but 150,000 bpd is to be covered under the first phase.
President Olusegun Obasanjo, while launching the company at the State House Banquet Hall, Abuja, also announced other concessions which include a licence to build an Independent Power Plant (IPP) and access to government's cassava report for the construction of a cassava processing facility.
Others are designated land mass for the construction of free port facility, continued support to help open up African markets, as well as making the company a partner in the current policy on private - public partnership (PPP).
To this end, he directed the Secretary to Government of the Federation (SGF), Chief Ufot Ekaette to work out the modality for a retreat with members of the Organised Private Sector (OPS) on how to foster an enduring partnership.
TransCorp, according to the promoters, will be investing in oil and gas, agriculture, power, Information Communication Technology (ICT), international trade and free trade zone/industrial park with most of these businesses to be domiciled at the Lekki Free Port.
President Obasanjo described the new mega corporation as "a very good start", hoping that it could serve as a strong anchor for economic growth if well managed.
"It is a home-grown initiative made up of local business people who have commitment, tenacity, courage, vision, patriotism, exposure and the resources to see the initiative through," he noted.
He urged Nigerians at home and abroad to buy-in into the company even as he stressed that the recent 60 per cent ($18 billion) debt relief, granted the country by the Paris Club, was expanding the scope of investment viability of the country.
The president further revealed that a member of board of TransCorp, Mr. Adegboyega Oluade has already spearheaded mobilisation of Nigerians in South Africa who are set to bring $400 million to invest at home.
In her welcome address, chairman of the interim board of directors, TransCorp, Dr. (Mrs) Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke who is also the Director General of the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE) said that the company was a wholly-owned private entity without any government funding even though the idea of a mega corporation was that of President Obasanjo.
She further stated that it was similar to what private sector operators did in Asian countries like Malaysia, Singapore and South Korea to turn around their economies, even as she assured that the company would not compete with companies of members, but complement.
Dr (Mrs) Okereke-Onyiuke also assureds that the company would not accept investments from state governments, but it would welcome private individuals.
With N6 billion expected to be raised at the Stock Exchange in the first round of funding, the capital base is expected to be jerked up to N67 billion through Initial Public Offering (IPO).
A member of the board and leading industrialist, Alhaji Aliko Dangote, who also spoke, pointed out that the MoU on the refinery was signed last month in a bid to increase local refining of crude as well as strengthen the African market.
He said large volume investments in cassava cultivation, cocoa processing, construction of a technology village and power, among others, would soon follow.
The formal launch was attended by Governors Sam Egwu (Ebonyi), Gbenga Daniel (Ogun), Donald Duke (Cross River) and Abba Ibrahim (Yobe). The SGF, Chief Ekaette, members of the Federal Executive Council (FEC) and captains of industry also graced the occasion.

Original_Search_Title: FG Grants Transcorp License to Build Refinery
Headline: Nigeria: FG Grants Transcorp License to Build Refinery
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday announced that the Federal
Government has granted concessions to the new mega firm , Transnational
Corporation of Nigeria (Transcorp) Plc to build a refinery and an
Independent Power Plant (IPP) in its effort to encourage the private sector
in building a virile and fruitful nation.
President Obasanjo also said that the $18 billion debt relief granted to
Nigeria by Paris Club of Nations will help Nigerian firms compete globally
with their foreign counterpart.
Speaking during the formal launching of Transcorp in Abuja yesterday,
President Obasanjo said " the Federal Government has agreed to grant
Transcorp license to build a 400,000-barrels a day refinery to be built in
phases, License to build an Independent Power Plant".
Other concessions granted to the firm include : Access to the Federal
Government Cassava report for the construction of a Cassava processing
export facility, Designated land mass for the construction Free Port
facility, continued support to help open up markets on African
Continent.
President Obasanjo urged all Nigerians to invest in the company, saying
" we are not saying that this Corporation will solve all our economic
problems and put Nigeria to economic prosperity, but it is a very good start
and if well operated or managed , it is bound to serve as a strong anchor
for our economic progress. The firm's ability to create new jobs here in
Nigeria will be an almost immediate benefit. And these will be the real
jobs, which require higher skills and better pay".
Commenting on the benefits of the $18 billion debt relief granted to
Nigeria, he said "the relief represents a direct saving on debt service
repayment, interest, surcharges and other fees; second, it will improve our
credit worthiness in the global community and build credible financial
confidence for our transactions, third, it means that we will stop all debt
rescheduling arrangements with attendant costs once we clear our exit
payments to be paid in two stages; fourth, the well over US $ 1 billion
currently spent on debt servicing obligations will now be available for
investment in pro-people and pro-development issues especially health,
education, water, roads and food security; fifth investors and investments
will come to Nigeria knowing we are no more classified as a "bad and
doubtful debt country".
The Chairman of Transcorp, Dr (Mrs.) Ndi Okereke Onyiuke assured all
Nigerians that the company will not die after the administration of
President Obasanjo, saying: "The company will be a public quoted company,
meaning that it is not a government outfit but a company owned by private
individuals."
According to her, "the Obasanjo Administration has shown clear
determination that they are committed to a total redevelopment of Nigeria's
economic environment and the private sector is clearly in the lead with this
new directions".

Original_Search_Title: Jak's Men Sail Through in E/Region, Yilo Krobo, Fanteakwa Await Turn
Headline: Ghana: Jak's Men Sail Through in E/Region, Yilo Krobo, Fanteakwa Await Turn
Body_Text:
As compared to some other regions where district assembly elections have been conducted twice, none of the district assembly elections so far conducted in the Eastern Region has been drawn or conducted on a run-off.
Out of the 17 districts, 15 of them have their District Chief Executives (DCEs) nominated by the president, remaining two districts- Yilo Krobo and Fanteakwa waiting.
So far, 10 out of the 15 nominees have been confirmed by their district assemblies, with the Kwahu West (a newly created district) East Akyem and Atiwa, also a newly created district, recording 100 percent votes.
Asuogyaman, Manya Krobo, West Akyem , Akuapem North and Kwahu South district assemblies are the five so far that are yet to hold their confirmation election of the president's nominees for the DCE position from next week.
The Eastern Region, until the creation of two new districts- Atiwa and Kwahu West has 15 districts.
President J. A. Kufuor, out of the figure, decided to retain five to continue their work as DCEs for another four years while he nominated two for the new districts. They all have been confirmed without troubles.
The five districts where the president retained their DCEs are Akuapem North, Suhum/Kraboa/Coaltar, Kwaebibrem, East Akyem and New Juaben districts and municipal assemblies.
Currently, the expectation of residents in the Fanteakwa and Yilo Krobo is very high as they keenly listen to news every morning, afternoon and evening to hear the names of those nominated.
In Yilo Krobo, majority of the residents' expectation is that, the president will retain the incumbent DCE, Mr. Christian Kofi Tetteh to continue his development of the district. Chiefs, opinion leaders, supporters of the New Patriotic Party (NPP) and even some political opponents have expressed satisfaction about the DCE's administration of the area.
Of particular commendation is the establishment of the 108 acres of mango farm, interspersed with cassava, which can fetch the assembly not less than ¢2 billion every season when it starts fruiting, and which the assembly can use to construct development projects without relying solely on government's common fund.
For that of Fanteakwa, most of the residents are expecting that the president would nominate someone from the Mumuado zone, specifically, Lawyer Baron Yaw Amoafo whiles the supporters of the incumbent DCE, Mr. Ebenezer Ofoe Caesar also believe he would be retained.
Not surprising, all the nominees have been confirmed by their district assembly members because the Eastern Region minister, Mr. Yaw Barimah had been asking them to put party politics behind them and elect or confirm the nominees so that, together as one people, they could develop their areas.

Original_Search_Title: Popular Sunyani Chop-Bar Under VAT Probe
Headline: Ghana: Popular Sunyani Chop-Bar Under VAT Probe
Body_Text:
Sunyani — The deputy Minister of Finance and Economic Planning, Mr. Agyemang Manu, has directed the Brong Ahafo Regional VAT Service to monitor the daily sales of Good Mark Foods, a popular chop bar at Sunyani, for at least one or two months, to establish whether she actually qualifies to register for the Value Added Tax (VAT).
The deputy minister explained to the Proprietor of the bar, Madam Josephine Brobbey, that the VAT law is not new in the system, and there was a lot of education being carried out.
According to Mr. Agyemang Manu, the government had not intended to kill businesses in the country, but rather create an enabling environment for her citizens to invest.
He debunked the suggestion of Madam Brobbey that the VAT service wanted to collapse her business, stressing, "VAT is not meant to collapse businesses."
The deputy minister who was in Sunyani to persuade the Proprietor of the bar to reach a compromise with the VAT Service, urged her to register with the service to enable them monitor her sales effectively.
About a couple of months ago, the VAT service had engaged in a legal battle with Good Mark Foods on her failure to register and pay VAT.
This legal battle took a different turn when her colleagues in the industry threatened to embark on a nationwide demonstration if the VAT Service did not withdraw the case from the court.
Subsequently, the VAT had to withdraw the case, while negotiations with Madam Brobbey to get her into the VAT net continue.
The acting Regional VAT Officer for Brong Ahafo, Samuel Sackey, explaining who qualifies to register and collect VAT, disclosed that ACT 546, section 5 that talks of threshold, does not apply to service providers.
According to him, the threshold applies to only those in the retailing business. Mr. Sackey said there were lots that a business entity could benefit for registering with VAT and therefore urged Madam Brobbey to take that advantage.
As to whether Good Mark Foods would be exempted or included in VAT, the Brong Ahafo Office is yet to take directives from the Commissioner of VAT. On her part, Madam Brobbey told the minister that her bar faced a lot of problems, such as payment to her cassava suppliers and other items that went into making the business, and that sometimes, rains destroyed her business, because the place was an open one.

Original_Search_Title: Rebuilding Liberia's Tourism Industry for Economic Growth
Headline: Liberia: Rebuilding Liberia's Tourism Industry for Economic Growth
Body_Text:
The modern tourism industry owes its genesis to a 1260-1271 voyage
across China, the Far East, and other parts of the world by legendary
explorer Marco Polo.
It is said that no other man-Christian, Saracen, Mongol, or
pagan-explored much of the world during his time than Marco Polo, son of
respected Italian merchant and seafarer, Nicolo Polo. History tells us that
in A.D. 1260, the 17th year old Marco Polo, his father, Nicolo, and uncle,
Maffeo made a daring journey over high seas from Italy to China that
eventually led to the creation of trade routes between Venice and China to
much world wide acclaim. The journey by Marco Polo et al not only created a
new avenue for cross-continental and leisure travels, but also created the
impetus that laid the foundation for the modern tourism industry. Marco Polo
and party visited the great ancient city of Constantinople and proceeded to
the kingdom of the Great Kublai Khan.
Marco Polo recorded accounts of their four-year exploration in his
widely circulated book, Description of the World, which opened the eyes of
Europeans to commercial opportunities and tourism travels in faraway
lands. Polo's accounts of Chinese paper money, books on philosophy,
politics, religion, and mechanical devices, as well as precious metals,
stones, ivory, jade, porcelain, silk, coal, and costly merchandise, pearls
and spices-some of which he brought back with him-brought him fame and
increased outside interest in eastern commercial markets (Burton,
1988). Hence, from 1450 to 1750, the "Age of Exploration" or travel tourism
began to thrive and Europeans moved about the world in ways never before
experienced, which changed for good the way humans travel about the
world.
Today, tourism is a multi-million business and key foreign exchange
earners in many nations of the world. The nonprofit American tourism
organization, Tourism Works for America, indicates in a recent report that
tourism is the third largest retail industry in the United States, next to
automotive dealers and food stores, creating up to 19 million jobs,
including more than half of a million executive positions. The Madrid-based
World Tourism Organization (WTO) also states that 10 per cent of the world's
gross domestic product (GDP) derives from international tourism, with net
intake of about $443 billion in 1997. The WTO estimates that by 2020 tourism
travel worldwide would climb to 1.6 billion tourists for a net revenue
intake of about $2 trillion.
On the African continent, Ghana, Kenya, and Uganda stand out as
tourism-driven economies. According to traveldocs.com, in 2003, tourism
became Ghana's largest foreign earner, about $600 million, while the BBC
reports that tourism is a cardinal economic booster in Kenya, generating
half a million jobs, which account for 12% of the Kenyan economy. In Uganda,
tourism is the second highest foreign exchange earner next to coffee,
generating $276 million from 6,000 visitors between 1993 and 1996. Tourism
is being developed and promoted elsewhere in Africa and other parts of the
world, and we in Liberia need to exploit our tourism potential for the
development and promotion of a fledging tourism industry in the 4th
republic.
Our Tourism Potential
In spite of television images and news reports portraying Liberia as a
country ravaged by starvation and the residual effects of a 14-year civil
war, much of the roof of Liberia is fertile and well watered, with
awe-inspiring scenery dominated by highlands and savannah
grasslands. Liberia has tourist attractions such as shallow lagoons,
islands, lakes, mangrove swamps, capes, rivers, colonial-styled wide porch
homes and 350 miles white sandy beaches. Liberia's unique history as the
first black African republic and a haven for freed black American slaves in
the 19th century can be great tourist attractions. In addition, palace of
culture and ethnic Liberian kingdoms such as Bassa Kingdom, Belle Kingdom,
Gola Kingdom, Klao (Kru) Kingdom, Grebo Kingdom, Krahn (Wee) Kingdom, Mende
Kingdom, Sapo Kingdom, Kpelle Kingdom, Kissi Kingdom, Pleebo Kingdom, Gbii
Kingdom, Via Kingdom, Dei Kingdom, Gio (Dan) Kingdom, Loma Kingdom, Mandingo
Kingdom, and the Maih (Mano) Kingdom can be valuable tourist attractions
once developed to host annual cultural history tours, rituals displays, and
beauty contests, farming celebration, Women's Day just to name a few
activities.
We can even advance our argument further by counting our tourism
blessing in the likes of the soft tone language of the Rivercess Bassa, the
mellow singing of the Krahn (Wee) to the toes tapping "che-che-che" of the
Gola salsa. The mosaic of people, especially our women, have distinct and
exotic beauty not found anywhere in the world. For example, Liberian women
are blessed with "ring neck" among the Vai, Loma, Kpelle, and Gola peoples,
and the "coco-la waist" among the Belle and Loma peoples. In the height
division, Mandingo and Gio women represent our nation very well with their
tall and slender sexy physique. As to complexion or skin color, Liberian
women can showcase a variety of skin tone from very dark to very light, with
particularly the Kissis (dark skin) and Grebos (light-skin or chocolate
color). Other cultural attractions include the Bassa Ghna-ghna, the Gola
Gbe-tu, the Dan giraffe-like Spirit dancers, the Mende ngafa, and the Dan
female acrobatic dancers or "snake girls." We can also showcase the Liberian
Rum or cane juice and palm wine as tourist attractions, in the same way the
Jamaican Rum has been a key tourist attraction since 1655
(http://www.appletonrumus.com/recipes.asp).
We have has more spices and recipes in Liberia with better aroma to
bring her cane juice and rum to world attention. We can make more money from
our paradisiacal natural resources. We in Liberia can showcase our mosaic of
places, traditions, art forms, celebrations and experiences as key tourist
attractions that highlight our diversity. Liberians are one of the
friendliest, sweetest, and good nature people in the world, which qualities
are the best ingredients for tourist attraction, as tourists will pay to see
these uncommon qualities. Generally, people don't visit a country for its
airports, hotels, or stadiums but for its cultural traditions such as the
unique Liberian handshake, cultural dance, customs, mores, traditional
rituals, and other outdoor activities. Liberia's tropical beaches and
waterfalls are unique tourist attractions as they provide a paradise for
angling, fishing, surfing, swimming, and potential for golfing. Liberia's
rivers, bays, and tributaries are also great tourist attractions because of
the opportunities they provide for sea kayaking, kayak sailing, snorkeling,
scuba diving, scuba-breathe diving, salt water sport-fishing and windsurfing
in the tropics.
The majestic St. John River in Grand Bassa County, Cestos (or Nuon)
River in Rivercess County, Yar River in Nimba County, St. Paul River and
Cavalla (Youbou) River in Maryland County, and the Mano River that connects
Guinea, Sierra Leone and Liberia are among a few of Liberia's magnificent
rivers for environmental tourism. Our nation should design a fly-in safari
so that tourist will experience the rugged beauty of Liberia's landscape
from the air and see the highlights of one of the most watered nations in
Africa. Even now the dead Sohnii-whein or Sohn Creek that divides Monrovia
into halves can be resurrected for "River walk," just like the San Antonio
Riverwalk in the United States, which generates millions of dollars in
tourism revenue to the people of the State of Texas each year.
In the island arena, the Providence Island in Montserrado County, Dead
Island in Maryland County, Tregbe Island in Grand Kru (Kloa) County, the
Massatin Island near Lake Piso in Grand Cape Mount County are few of
Liberia's scenic islands for tourism development and promotion. It is said
that the Massatin Island got its name from an old lady named Massa, who
lived alone on the island during 15th century, which makes the Massatin
Island an added tourist attraction for its unique history.
Environmental Tourist Attractions
Apart from the rivers and islands, Liberia enjoys the earth's finest
climate for environmental tourism. The natural and breathe taking beauty of
herds of prairie animals such as elephants, viviparous toad, cross river
gorilla, water buffalo, lions, zebra duiker, leopards, Diana monkey,
Iguanas, white mangabey, chimpanzees, Nile hippopotamus-derogatorily called
pygmy hippopotamus-must be developed for wildlife-watching tour or
safari. Liberia possesses some of the world's rarest flora and fauna and 30%
of the country's landmass is covered with forest in reserves and World
Heritage Sites. Liberia is legendary for its hippotamus (not pygmy
hippotamus), which is found nowhere else on earth. One should, however, note
that while "pygmy hippopotamus" is mainly confined to Liberia, neighboring
nations such as Sierra Leone, Guinea and the Ivory Coast have small herbs of
them. In fact, in 1927, Mr. Harvey Firestone of the Firestone Rubber
Plantations Company in Liberia gave as a gift to U.S. President Calvin
Coolidge one of Liberia's male hippos, which he named Billy. Billy became
parent to many of the hippos found in zoos across the United States
today.
Liberia is an untapped tropical for varied birdlife or birder's
paradise. Our nation's luxuriant mountain offer some of the most challenging
trekking in West Africa and virgin forest can attract any nature enthusiast
and birders. Some of the world's most unusual birds are found in Liberia. We
can showcase a variety of birds, from our parrots (considered to be one of
the smartest in the world) to the "dancing birds," the gymnobucco calvus,
gymnobucco peli, pogoniulus scolopaceus, pogoniulus white-breasted guinea
fowl atroflavus, pogoniulus subsulphureus, buccanodon duchaillui, lybius
vieilloti, and the "lazy" dodo bird. Birders will flock to Liberia
year-round to spot colorful resident and migratory birds if Liberia can
develop a Birding Trail or designate several forest sites for bird
viewing. We can also develop a national Bird Sanctuary for research
purposes, national wildlife refuges and other birding hotspots should be
easily accessible for tourist visits and bird watch.
Liberia is also known for iron ore mining, but there are other ways to
use our mountains besides mining. The mining for iron ore at such mountains
as Bong Range, Putu Range, Nimba, and Bomi, have not generated the desired
economic benefits to the local people in each mountain community or
county. Therefore, we need to protect the other mountains such as Mt. Bee in
Margibi County, Mt. Wologisi in Lofa County and Mt. Gedeh in Grand Gedeh
County from mining exploitations. These mountains can be reserved for
mountaineering, trekking or excursion activities. For example, Mt. Gibi's
Oracle, with its parallel tunnels and a" rock kitchen" upheld by two rock
pillars, which extend against the walls of the mountain and resemble the
Hanging Garden of Babylon, has such rarest sacred wonders such as white bats
and Zhor birds that can be great tourist destinations and revenue generating
outlets. Similarly, Mt. Zeesiah in Margibi County with its rare rock
containing human footprints can bring in thousands of tourist dollars as
well.
Apart from rivers, islands, mountains, Liberia also needs to promote its
food, music, and dance troupes as tourist attractions. For example, the
Kendeja and Besoa Cultural Centers near Monrovia can be boosted by
establishing similar cultural centers in each of the 15 political
subdivisions or counties of Liberia. Each county shouldn't only have several
cultural troupes and centers to entertain tourists about indigenous culture,
customs, and traditions unique to each county, but there should also be a
month-long annual event or "Carnival" to display our nation's traditional
folk dance and music events, workshops, traditional samba shows and other
activities. Moreover, since food has always been a bonding element in social
situations such as birth, death, dating, wedding, work, play and so forth,
delicious Liberian dishes and foods such as Jollof rice, plantain, bitter
ball, baked eddoes, cassava, Malaguatta pepper, mushroom, coffee, kola,
cocoa, mango, okra, palm nuts, papaya, and dumboy can be great tourist
attractions as well. Using the Liberian mortar and pestle to pound farm
rice, and prepare dumboy and other grains is a great Liberian tradition
handed down through the generations, which any tourist would be interested
in knowing. The Liberian "Country Chop," consisting of meats, fish, and
greens fried in palm oil, fish cooked in coconut cream, along with fried
plantains, and Beef Intestine Soup for Dumboy are a wealth of dishes to
attract tourists to Liberia.
In essence, Liberia has enormous tourism potential to generate revenue
for economic development in the country if only we could tap into the
resources we have. Liberia enjoys good dry and sunny weather, coupled with a
gentle breeze unique in West Africa. Hence, Liberia has potential for the
following tourist attractions and recreation activities:
fee fishing
skeet shooting
canoe livery
biking
bird watching
hiking
hang gliding
hot air balloon rides
What Other Nations Have Done
Tourism as we know it today did not appear on the world page until the
19th century, as an out-shoot of leisure travel, which has formed the larger
part of the tourist industry. Naturally, as the vanguard of the Industrial
Revolution, Great Britain set the pace for leisure travel by offering
leisure time to factory owners, traders, and other pillars of the Industrial
Revolution. France followed suit with launch of the French Riviera, one of
the glamorous holiday resorts in the world. The French Riviera, winter sport
in Sweden, competitive boxing in Great Britain, and professional tennis at
Wimbledon became renowned tourist attractions for some time prior to
improved transportation and communications infrastructure that allowed large
movement of people across international boundaries. It is said that in 1841
Thomas Cook of Great Britain organized the first known tour in human history
by transporting a group teetotalers to Lounghborough from Leicester. Great
Britain later developed the Grand Tour, which featured 18th century British
artists and their European contemporaries in the likes of Claude Lorrain are
consumers. Florence, Naples, and Rome soon emulated the British example to
attract "tourists" up the social ladder such as William Blake and Lord
Byron.
In the United States, the desert-ridden Las Vegas was changed into an
oasis for gambling and tourism, while preserving its natural beauty as the
central of attraction. In 1855, Las Vegas was still a small town populated
by the Mormons. In 1905, the town was formally established by auctioning out
land. Then in the 1930s, with the construction of Hoover Dam, Las Vegas was
transformed into a major tourist attraction for legal gambling and such
landmarks as the Red Rock Canyon, Lake Mead, the Grand Canyon, the Mirage,
