and the Stratosphere Tower Hotel. New hotel/casinos such as MGM Grand Hotel
and Casino, Desert Inn, the Sands, Hacienda, and Tropicana attracted more
tourists to a city built right in the middle of the American desert. In
other words, the Americans turn the 2.17 miles by 4.18 miles wide Grand
Canyon in Las Vegas into a money making machine, as the Grand Canyon is
"among the most popular camping and hiking destinations and all around
must-see in the Southern Nevada area"
(http://www.lvlg.com/lasvegas/attracts/parks.htm).
I mentioned earlier about the tourism industries in Ghana, Kenya and
Uganda. For example, the Ghanaians first set about the formal development of
tourism in their country by evaluating the country's tourism resources in
1970, just 13 years after independence in 1957 (Obuarn Committee, 1972). The
objective of the Ghana Tourism Program which the UN Secretary Kofi Annan
served as Director of Tourism in Ghana from 1974 to 1976 was to catalogue
and classify the potential tourism resources for a five- year development
plan covering the period 1972-1976. As a result of this study, the
government issued a White Paper on Tourism, which identified investment
areas for foreign participation, including various concessions and
incentives for investors. Between 1972 and 1978, other studies on various
aspects of Ghana's tourism industry were conducted, but due to local
financial and technical constraints, in Ghana, the studies had to be funded
and carried out largely by foreign agencies and personnel. But this did not,
however, diminish the growth of the Ghana tourism industry as a key foreign
exchange earner today.
I believe if the rest of the world can turn their deserts and natural
attractions into principal economic and employment powerhouses, Liberia can
learn from these nations and develop a robust tourism industry in Liberia
that will ensure a better standard of living for all Liberians. With natural
and agricultural resources such as cash crops, rivers, lakes, diamond, gold,
iron ores, timber, palm oil, sand, mountains, rare birds, and even fine men
and women, Liberia should be nothing less than a treasure trove in West
Africa. Sadly, all these natural resources do not mean a grain of sand to
the socio-economic, political, and cultural development of our nation and
people when our political leadership continues to fail us by their poor
leadership and management styles.
The Way Forward
If the treasures I have named in this paper--- from the enchanting view
and snowy peaks of Mt. Nimba to the sobering calm of Lake Piso; from the
hardwood forests, mangrove swamps, and savannah plains of central of
Liberia, to alluvial diamond deposits in western Liberia; from meandering
rivers and miles and miles of powdery white sandy beaches and panorama of
wildlife criss-crossing the country, then let someone tell me what other
wealth we need in Liberia. I think Liberia has enormous wealth by which we
could develop our country and enjoy a decent standard of living but for poor
national leadership and management. For instance, after 157 years of our
national existence as a nation and people, we still do not have modern rail
systems, air transport systems, bus systems, and adequate medical
facilities, ferry rides or easy access to our rivers, lakes, and
islands. Our country is grossly underdeveloped while we sit on "diamond and
gold" and still beg for food and water to survive.
I know we cannot change our past but we have every opportunity for
rebuilding our shattered economy by developing our tourism potential to our
advantage. We should use tourism to showcase the best things about
Liberia--its colorful culture and rich history, lush, heritage sites,
historical shrines, green forests and fine, white sand beaches and abundant
marine life. We can dub our new tourism program "Liberian Bests" in order to
set high expectations for our post-war tourism program. I believe it is our
moral and ethical duty to change Liberia's image from a beggar nation with
or trusteeship threat handing over it, to a haven of tourist attractions and
economic boom, without handling our country over to foreign agents. I would
be glad if many Liberians devoted time and brainpower to rebuilding Liberia
than to sit back as the UN, EU, ECOWAS and our "mother country", the United
States, endeavor to place Africa's oldest independent republic under
trusteeship. I think if Liberia were placed under trusteeship of any kind,
it would not only be a great insult to the intelligence of all Liberians,
but also a clear test of the lack patriotism and nationalism in
Liberia.
Does my opposition to trusteeship mean I condone the current plight of
the Liberian people? "No," because if so, I wouldn't be discussing how
tourism can be used to improve our lives. In fact, I see many fellow
Liberians who are so sick and tired of our national woes and other ills
society to want to entertain the recent UN, EU, ECOWAS theory (economic plan
or trusteeship for Liberia). The whole idea of a trusteeship over the land
beneath which my ancestral fossils and the umbilical cords of our parentage
and us are buried makes skin creeps, for the least. Liberia is a land that
has given us so much of nurturing, a land enchanting beauty and fascinating
roles in world peace and stability is about to relinquish its independence,
its culture and way for life for the unpredictable mantle of foreign
trustees. And I am talking here about a country that is a founding member of
the League of Nation, now the United Nations, a country that produced the
first female president of the United Nation Security Council in person of
Angie Brooke Randolph, a nation that cast the decisive vote for creation of
the state of Israel, and a country that is founding member of the
Organization of African Unity (now African Union), the Economic Community of
West African States (ECOWAS), the African Development Bank (ADB), the Mano
River Union (MRU), and the Non-Aligned Movement. Besides, Liberia didn't
only play a key and critical role in the fight against fascism that almost
obliterated its existence at the hands of Nazi Germany, but it also led the
touch of the independent movement of Africa and fought, defeated and sent
apartheid to the dustbin of human history.
I feel sick because Liberia's history is so rich for us to fall so flat
on our faces under trusteeship. Instead of tourists coming to Liberia to
read about its indelible role in human civilization, Liberians are waking up
to yet another stranger chapter. This is a fearful legacy to be left to
future generations of Liberians. I therefore submit that although we now
live in another Liberia, a Liberia that can no longer hold onto the glories
of the "Sweet Land of Liberty", are all Liberians corrupt to the point that
trusteeship is the best option to our unity and development on the eve of
national elections? Do we really need to mortgage the future of Liberia to a
trusteeship instead of the vision of a new administration after the October
elections? We must have the political will to rebuild Liberia for the better
by choosing the kind of national leaders that will hold our country
together. We must show our moral empathy or courage as lawyers, doctors,
journalists, farmers, students, market women and men to rebuild our
country. We must endeavor to build a new malaria-freed 4th Liberian Republic
endowed with capital cities with technological innovations in communications
and transportation to facilitate the free movement of goods, services,
people, and information across Liberia in the faster and cheapest ways. We
need an integrated transportation system and new national capital city that
include:
1 Planned airports
2 Sport Stadiums-around which Liberia should design and develop (West
Africa Major Soccer WAMC) which will be first of its kind on the Continent
3 Recycling plants
4 Public and private buildings
5 Colleges and Universities
6 Four four-lane superhighways (such as a new Liberia Highway and
specific lanes for trucks only)
7 Recreation Parks
8 Bus stations
9 Buildings with postal zip codes for efficiency mail delivery and zonal
location
10 Rail systems
We also need an Integrated Ferry System connecting ports of Harper in
Maryland County, Greenville in Sinoe County, and Buchanan in Grand Bassa
County to one another and to rivers and lakes adjacent to these ports for
the free movement of people in these communities. We should develop our
tourism industry to include not only traditional canoe safaris but also
other non-vehicular mode of transportation. I believe Ferry is by far
cheaper than regular transportation, so I am proposing that contacts should
be established between the Liberian government and Marine system companies
in the United States or Europe to build a custom ferry system at strategic
crossing points in Liberia such as the port areas. Passenger tolls and other
taxes could be imposed and collected to pay whatever marine company selected
for the project. In addition to tourism, the lower decks of the ferry could
be used to transport foods and other items like the Staten Island Ferry in
New York does. Since 1905, the Staten Island Ferry has carried over 19
million passengers annually on a 5.2-mile run between the St. George
Terminal in Staten Island and the Whitehall Terminal in lower Manhattan in
the United States.
Of course, in order for the tourism plans proposed to be successfully
implemented, a number challenges will have to be met. First, we will need to
expedite sites visits, improve the planning process, streamline our current
tourism program and human resource capacity development to implement an
ambitious national tourism development plan in a coordinated manner, with a
multiplicity of public and private sector entities at the national, regional
and local levels. The second challenge is to establish a separate Ministry
of Tourism detached from the Ministry of Information, Culture and Tourism
for efficient management and coordination of the revamped Liberian tourism
Program. The third challenge lies in financing the plans with respect to
general infrastructure, tourism facilities, attractions, marketing and
delivery of quality tourism services. Hence, I would suggest that the four
funding sources below be considered:
1 government funding
2 establishment of a Tourism Development Fund
3 international donor assistance and
4 private sector investment.
I don't think we in Liberia need to sit around and cry about the kind of
infrastructure developments and standards of living we should have been
enjoying instead of begging for handouts from others. But we are not the
only nation playing catch up in terms of our development. The great nations
of Europe had to count on the generosity of the Americans through the
Marshall Plan to rebuild their societies. We can do the same. We can start
with developing a tourism program that rivals Ghana and the Caribbean
countries with respect to natural beauty, adventure sports and hiking,
excursion tours and safaris up our mountains, traditional villages, and open
fields.
Equally important for our tourism program is a "one stop" Foreign
Investment Assistance Center (FIAC). For example, if anyone wants to visit
or do business in Liberia, that person could go to a FIAC and process all
necessary travel and business documents from there. Fees to establish
business in Liberia must be written down and Liberian lawyers should be
available at each FIAC to help these investors with their business
documentations. In other words, FIAC offices will do all the phone calls so
investors will not have to go from one office to another, as is the case at
present. Each FLAC should be staffed by representatives of the various
Liberian ministries and agencies concerned with tourism and business travels
and investment opportunities in Liberia. Each FLAC is also likely to reduce
unnecessary bureaucratic, corruption and expectation to be tipped for
service.
I think those bringing investment or visiting our country MUST not be
discouraged through any form of bitter experiences. With the FLAC, the
visitors or businessmen or women do not have to deal with greedy bureaucrats
demanding kickbacks for their services. I do not see the need for the
issuance of visa to visitors and investors going to Liberia because Liberia
is still emerging from civil war, and I don't think it is a wise thing to do
now. And this is a fundamental question we need to consider as we explore
the idea of developing a tourism industry and our investment sector in
post-conflict Liberia. We need to look at the larger picture as opposed to
delaying those who want to do business in our country with a low capital
investment for a mere $50 or $100 in kickbacks.
Finally, we ought to muster the political will and moral fortitude to
succeed as a nation and people and stop our petty jealousy by fighting to
sabotage ideas and projects that do originate with us. The future economic
growth of Liberia depends not only on teamwork but also on the collective
vision of all the sons and daughters of Liberia. In this context,
improvement in teamwork, communication, and coordination at all levels of
Liberian society, including public and private entities and higher
institutions of learning in Liberia will be the key to the future success of
any tourism program in post-conflict Liberia. I fervently hope and pray that
the God of our Ancestors will help us to work hard to restore and uphold the
national honor, liberty, and glory associated with our national
sovereignty. It is in honor of our national sovereignty that I beseech you,
my fellow Liberians, to weigh this whole matter of trusteeship of our nation
in the sanctum of your heart. I believe if we can search our individual
hearts and find that self-governance, no matter how problematic is better
than trusteeship, then we will join hands together as one people and speak
forcefully against trusteeship. The fate of Liberia now trembles at
potential loss of our self-determination, sovereignty, independence if we
permit outsiders to take advantage of our divisions and impose trusteeship
on our country and ourselves. With a deep-seated love for ourselves and our
country, we can rebuild Liberia to become a tourist paradise. I believe
hardship or the threat death can never destroy the ideas and visions of a
great people, so we should never surrender our national sovereignty to
anyone because of our present hardship from the 14-year civil war. We can
rebuild Liberia, and we can start today by revamping our tourism
program.
Syrulwa Somah, Ph.D., is an Associate Tenured Professor of Environmental
and Occupational Safety and Health at NC A&T State University in
Greensboro, North Carolina. He is the author of several books, including,
The Historical Resettlement of Liberia and It Environmental Impact,
Christianity, Colonization and State of African Spirituality, and Nyanyan
Gohn-Manan: History, Migration & Government of the Bassa (a book about
traditional Bassa leadership and cultural norms published in
2003). Dr. Somah is also the Executive Director of the Liberian History,
Education & Development, Inc. (LIHEDE), a nonprofit organization based
in Greensboro, North Carolina. He can be reached at: somah@ncat.edu or
infor@lihede.org

Original_Search_Title: Rising Food Cost
Headline: Nigeria: Rising Food Cost
Body_Text:
Lagos — VIRTUALLY every administration in Nigeria had laid emphasis on agriculture given that food ranks top of the three basic needs of human beings. Since the 70's when President Olusegun Obasanjo introduced Operation Feed the Nation (OFN) during his first coming as military leader, every successive regime had made a public show of according priority to agriculture.
From Alhaji Shehu Shagari's Green Revolution to Obasanjo's second coming and establishment of Special Programme on Food Security (NSPFS), agriculture has been pivotal in government projects. The essence is to have a well-fed, healthy, and productive citizenry since as the saying goes, health is wealth.
But the spiralling cost of food items in recent times is detracting from the essence of this emphasis on agriculture.
It is ironic that with the on-going implementation of a food security programme which has gulped 45 million dollars, food prices are soaring rather than declining. Even the National Economic Intelligence Committee (NEIC) blew the whistle in its first quarter report for 2005 where it lamented the soaring prices of food items, insisting, evidently, that the situation portends danger for the ordinary Nigerian, and indeed, the entire populace.
Such staple food items as rice, garri, beans, bread, tomatoes, among others, have all had their prices virtually doubled in the last four months. A measure of garri, a food item made out of cassava, which sold at N130 in a few months ago now sells for between N230 and N270, just as a bag of rice, which sold at a little over three thousand naira, now sells for six thousand naira and above. Other items are affected in various degrees. The point must be made that at no time in the last decade did food items cost this much.
This is against the background of President Olusegun Obasanjo's recent public comment on the situation wherein he described the soaring prices as "normal" and "seasonal." Admittedly, food items adjust slightly upwards during such planting seasons as this but at no time has the effect been this alarmingly debilitating. This year's situation is, therefore, out of the ordinary which is why the government should apply extraordinary solutions to the problem.
The president says grains have already been released from government strategic reserves. He says care is being taken in the process to avoid flooding the market. But there is hardly any evidence that those grains released have impacted on the prices of food items. Cassava, a major crop from which garri is made, is largely being exported on account of this regime's accelerated cassava production programme which has resulted in increased output of the crop.
As foreign exchange is being earned from this crop, it should not be done at the expense of Nigerian people, who literally watch the crop leave their shores while they go hungry, as it were. While on a visit to Nigeria, the Executive Director of the United Nations World Food Programme, Mr James Morris, called for concerted efforts to eliminate hunger stressing that the objective is first amongst the eight Millennium Development Goals.
This regime obviously understands the foregoing prompting it to spend about N13.3 billion to procure 245,000 metric tonnes of fertiliser in 2004 and reduce duty on that vital agricultural input to just five per cent. The result has been a steady growth in that sector at the impressive rate of seven per cent per annum.
However, these successes pale into insignificance in the face of spiralling food prices. We call for the release of more grains from the strategic reserves to cushion the current hard situation. And this should pose no problem, especially since indications of a bumper harvest have been received for this year. Every encouragement, ranging from combating the menace of middlemen in the distribution of fertiliser, to buying back excess grains, should be given to farmers in order to expand gains made in the agricultural sector. In the mean time, government must take urgent steps to redress the rising cost of food items.

Original_Search_Title: Talk About Crop Diversification, Irrigation Always - PAM Director
Headline: Zambia: Talk About Crop Diversification, Irrigation Always - PAM Director
Body_Text:
Mapatizya — PROGRAMME Against Malnutrition (PAM) director Dr Drinah Nyirenda has
called for an end to the habit of people only talking about irrigation and
crop diversification during drought years.
Speaking at Luyaba Basic School in Mapatizya constituency of Kalomo
district on Friday when she addressed over nine graduate farmers in
cassava-growing, Dr Nyirenda said the country's authorities and stakeholders
had a tendency to only talk seriously about crop diversification and
irrigation when there was drought.
She said the government and people must always talk about crop
diversification and irrigation, both in good and bad years.
She said the country was supposed to reduce hunger by half, by the year
2015.
Dr Nyirenda urged the people of Kalomo in particular and Southern
Province in general to promote cassava growing if they were to survive the
severe hunger that often haunts the province.
Dr Nyirenda said PAM would help promote the production, marketing and
processing of cassava together with the diversification of other crops, such
as sorghum, millet and beans, in the province
"You need these foods on your tables at once, not alternating at every
meal. You have been regarding cassava not as food but a snack, now you have
to change your mindset," said Dr Nyirenda.
She was also happy that most household heads were vigorously
participating in the cassava-growing project in the area, a trend she said
would ensure sustainability.
And in a vote of thanks, one of the graduate farmers, Lackson Bulawayo,
confessed that the people in Luyaba regarded cassava as a wild tree until
PAM introduced the cassava crop as food.

Original_Search_Title: Transcorp Corporation Sets Agenda, to Float IPO
Headline: Nigeria: Transcorp Corporation Sets Agenda, to Float IPO
Body_Text:
Transcorp Corporation of Nigeria (Transcorp) is set to boost the economy
as the Federal Government has given its backing for the commencement of the
entity in an attempt to give Nigeria the competitive edge to compete
globally in virtually all sectors of the economy.
The Corporation has also set a target to float an Initial Public
Offering (IPO) that would provide opportunity for Nigerians to commit
resources and be part owners of the outfit.
This is a bold step to provide the nation a mega firm that can penetrate
markets around the world as well as dominate the local market in products
and services that were previously serviced by foreign firms or service
markets that have been completely neglected
The Director-General of the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE), Dr (Mrs) Ndi
Okereke- Onyiuke who is the chairman of Transcorp had disclosed that
Transcorp is Nigeria's answer to the Cheabols of Korea (Deawoo,Samsung)
which used scale economies to pull their country out of the abyss of the
developing nation's vicious circle.
According to her, "Transnational Corporation will do the same for
Nigeria while providing its investors with a good return on their
investment. The business model is designed to meet five core revenue centers
with a sixth one, which leverages off competitive advantages inherent in the
others."
The revenue centers include: Oil and Gas, Agriculture and Agro-Allied
Business, Information Technology, Power, International Trade, and Free Trade
Zone/Industrial park.
She stated that Tansnational Corporation will invest in both the
upstream and downstream sectors of the Oil and Gas industry. " A concession
from the Federal Govermnent for a Licence to build a new refinery is already
in the works. This licence will permit Transnational to build a scalable
facility using the latest technology in petroleum refining. This facility
will not only meet the local demands for refined products but also it will
export refined products to the rest of the world. Transnational will also
participate in upstream activities, especially in the emerging deep
off-shore exploration and production business," she noted.
She further stressed that Transnational will bid for blocks as well as
provide world-class production facilities for holders of other blocks in
need of the most modern technology in farming out crude oil. Transnational
also intends to acquire Pipe- line infrastructure of the Petroleum and
Pipelines Marketing Company (PPMC), and expand on that network to develop
Africa's largest network of Pipelines for crude, refined products and
petrochemicals.
In the area of agriculture, Onyiuke said: "The Agriculture strategy of
the organisation will be built on the Obasanjo administration's policy on
Agriculture - cassava processing - to meet the overwhelming demand for
cassava flour based on the Federal Government policy to include 10 per cent
cassava flour in the making of bread, and cassava exports to the growing
demand for alternative sources of feed for the millions of livestock in
Europe. Cassava markets in Europe alone are estimated at over $17 billion a
year whereas local markets for cassava flour are worth over N150 billion."
Commenting on the technology she said: "Heavy investment in software
development, outsourcing, biotechnology, call centers, and hardware
assembly, will form the beginning of the firm's drive to develop a business
in technology. Africa's technology requirements exceeded $40 billion in 2004
and also commercial research and development facility for corporate Nigeria
and Africa.
According to Onyiuke: "The Federal Government has already confirmed to
the Interim Board of Transnational Corporation that a licence to build an
independent power plant to supply residential and commercial residents will
be granted in the shortest possible time. The strategy is to build a plant
that will serve the Transnational Industrial Park and beyond. In this way,
the various businesses located at the park will have uninterrupted power
supply.
The Interim Board of Transcorp include: Mr.Festus Odimegwu, Chief A.B.C
Orjiako, Mr. Jacob Ajekigbe, Mr. Tony Elumelu, Mr. Jim Ovia and a host of
other personalities.
Speaking also, Secretary of Transcorp, Mr. Nicholas Okoye stated that
Transcorp will use most of its funds for working capital to buy goods in
large volumes from Asia and supply them to the West African sub-regional
market. Investments that require less capital such as cassava processing and
software development will also take off with this first round of funding. An
Initial Public Offering
(IPO) on the floor of the NSE will provide substantial capital for the
implementation of other areas of the business model," he added.

Original_Search_Title: Japanese NGO Rehabilitates Irrigation Ditch, Road
Headline: Angola: Japanese NGO Rehabilitates Irrigation Ditch, Road
Body_Text:
Benguela — Lobito/The Japanese Non-Governmental Organisation (GICA), is
rehabilitating 25 kilometres of the main irrigation ditch of Canjala
commune, Lobito district, in central Benguela province.
According to Canjala administrator, Luís Gonzaga, the ditch will
irrigate over l000 hectares of land to benefit nearly 500 families of that
commune, located some 90 kilometres north-east of Lobito port city.
With the rehabilitation of the main irrigation ditch, Canjala peasants
might have their farming activity relaunched.
GICA will also rehabilitate nearly 45 kilometres of the secondary road
that links the headquarters of the commune with the Kambanga village that
has more than 25 farms.
The administration who would not disclose the amount spent in both
operations, said that they will boost production and flow of field products
to the cities of Lobito and Luanda.
Main crops in Canjala are maize, peanuts, beans, palm oil, cassava and
various types of vegetables.

Original_Search_Title: ADF Promotes Quality of Agricultural Production in Ghana
Headline: Ghana: ADF Promotes Quality of Agricultural Production in Ghana
Body_Text:
The Board of Directors of the African Development Fund (ADF), the
concessional window of the African Development Bank (ADB) Group, has
approved a loan of 17 million Units of Account* (US$ 24.7 million) to
finance the Export Marketing and Quality Awareness Project in Ghana.The
project will contribute towards the goal of increasing the export earnings
of non- traditional agricultural products. The objective of the project is
to increase incomes of horticulture crop farmers and exporters and of
cassava producers.
The project components comprise the following: ? Production and
Productivity Enhancement ? Export Marketing Promotion and Infrastructure
Improvement ? Capacity Building ? Project Coordination and Management
The project will be implemented in the following four Regions of Ghana:
Central, Eastern, Greater Accra and the southern-most part of the Volta, due
to its proximity to the seaport and airport.
Most of the horticultural products for fresh export are produced in
these regions. The project area is home to 8.2 million people equivalent to
45% of the country's total population, of whom 4 million live in rural
areas. About 13,502 rural households will directly benefit from the
project. It is envisaged that a total of 12,000 people employed at various
levels of the horticultural export industry will be trained out of which
4,700 will be women.
The major beneficiaries of the project are the members of the existing
producers/exporters associations for horticultural export crops.
Although the project's main focus group is the participating farmers and
exporters, the project would benefit all horticultural growers and exporters
through the upgrading of market information and intelligence capacity,
improvement in infrastructure facilities like feeder roads, storage houses,
refrigerated vehicles, opening windows for marketing in regional and
European markets, strengthening of quality control and certification
facilities and services.
It would also provide the environment where private investors would be
encouraged to undertake further investments. For the cassava industry, the
project would raise the quantity and quality of the produce even for that
which is sold on the local market, thereby encouraging other farmers to
invest in similar processing facilities and hence create a market for the
small-scale growers.
For the farmers, this would expand the reliable market for their
produce. In addition, the HIV/AIDS awareness campaigns and malaria
prevention activities are expected to contribute to improved health of the
population.
Increased production will create more employment in the rural and urban
areas and lead to increased incomes for smallholder men and women producers
and exporters. All of these improvements will contribute to improved welfare
and reduced poverty.
The Bank Group's operations in Ghana started in 1973. To date, the Group
has committed a total of US$ 710 million on 51 operations in the
country. 1UA = US $ 1.456613 as at 01/07/05

Original_Search_Title: Iita, Cassava Machine Fabricators Discuss Local Production
Headline: Nigeria: Iita, Cassava Machine Fabricators Discuss Local Production
Body_Text:
Lagos — A one-day interactive session of Nigerian machine fabricators organized by the Integrated Cassava Project (ICP) of the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (Iita) has been held at Ibadan.
The interactive session, which drew participants from small and medium scale industries across Nigeria aimed at encouraging fabricators to develop made-in-Nigeria processing machines for the cassava industry.
According to Dr Lateef Sanni Iita Food Technologist/Post Harvest Specialist, the objectives of the interactive session was to review existing machines and make a comparative study of others brought from foreign countries. He said the session among other things was aimed at discussing the efficiency and capacity of the locally manufactured machines with a view to suggesting modifications, improving and upgrading them, and drawing an action plan for a mass production of the certified machines in the country. The meeting was a follow-up to a similar one held earlier in the year. The current meeting focused on the locally-made cassava peeling machines.
In a presentation, Dr Marco Patino, the ICP Agro-Enterprise Specialist explained the functions of the various cassava peeling machines manufactured in foreign countries, notably China, Brazil, and Thailand. He said some had a capacity of 10-50 rotations per minute (rpm) with rotators, while others rotate at 10-20 rpm. Dr Patino highlighted the significance of the machines in the processing of cassava for starch, flour and gari, but added however, that none of them is 100 percent efficient.
Series of presentations and demonstrations were also made by participants from the Project Development Agency (Proda); the Federal University of Technology, Akure (Futa); the Ibadan-based African Regional Center for Engineering Design and Management (Arcadem)'; and the "A" and "H" Engineering Farm, Iwo. The participants discussed among other things the concept, functions, capacity and limitations of their respective peeler machines. Other participants who attended the meeting were Addis Engineering, Niji Lukas, Odubela Engineering, Adebash, and Tropical Engineering.
Earlier in his welcome address, the Program Manager of Cassava Enterprise Development Projects (CEDP), Dr Gbassey Tarawalli, called on fabricators to share their experiences with Iita to enable them improve and upgrade their existing machines product. Dr. Tarawali who spoke on behalf of Dr. Alfred Dixon, ITA Cassava Breeder, said the fabricators were expected to make a significant contribution to the budding Nigerian cassava industry.
Some equipment brought by fabricators were tested at the Iita farm unit with a view to suggesting some modifications for further improvement. They include mechanized cassava peelers, planters, harvesters, dryers and pressers. Another session was scheduled for the 4th of August when all fabricators will be expected to present their machines for further testing and evaluation.
In his closing remark, Dr Sanni spoke of the challenges ahead, stressing that improved Nigerian-made peeler machines would supply equipment to the much desired domestic and foreign markets. He urged the local fabricators to register as members of the Cassava Equipment Fabrication Association (Cefan), so that their interest would be articulated, while Iita will accord them the technical support.

Original_Search_Title: BATN Foundation Explores Agriculture, It for Poverty Alleviation
Headline: Nigeria: BATN Foundation Explores Agriculture, It for Poverty Alleviation
Body_Text:
Lagos — The British American Tobacco Nigeria Foundation has moved to employ
Information Technology and agriculture as major tools in the pursuit of
their vision to help in the nation's battle to reduce poverty and empower
the people.
The Chairman of BATN Foundation, Chief Olu Akinkugbe, who spoke in Lagos
at the unveiling of the development plan of the foundation for the year 2005
said they are driven with intense passion to do every thing within their
power to reduce poverty in the country and bring development to the Nigerian
people throughout the entire 6 geopolitical zones.
Speaking on some some steps taken by the foundation to bring empowerment
to thepeople he said the BATN Foundation's Agricultural Development Project
which is one of their major tools has the objective of assissting farmers
improve their social and economic well being.
According to him the objective which is being achieved in partnership
with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) aims at
improving the quality and yield of food crops with focus on maize, cassava
and watermelon.
In the project the the farmers did not only produce crop for their
consumption but also had marketable surplus, therefore serving as a source
of income for the beneficiaries through direct link to markets.
The agricultural initiative called the Ago-Are Agricultural Development
Program was planned to be an intervention in the areas of providing improved
agricultural information dissemination and training, input linkages, and
facilitation of market linkages.
"At its inception the programme targeted a total of 100 rural farmers
consisting of 50 female farmers and 50 male farmers who were enlisted in the
program"
The male farmers were mobilized to cultivate improved varieties of
cassava, and maize, while the female farmers cultivated watermelon, a crop
that was introduced to the community for the first time"
"These farmers were provided with all the inputs they required cultivate
the mandated crops ranging from seeds, to fertilizers and persticides among
other numerous farm inputs.
The foundation also assisted the farmers to plough and propare 2
hectares of land each or their produce.
All the farmers had to do was just to plant and look after the crop from
the planting to harvesting."
Akinkugbe explained that the vision of the foundation was inspired by
the first and seventh statements of the Millenium Development Goals MDGs,
which call for the eradication of extreme poverty and hunger as well as
pursuit of sustainable environmental management.
Also speaking during the event, the ExecutiveDirector of the Foundation
Mr Foluso Koku explained that all the intended objectives of the programme
were surpassed.
Analysing the different enterprises of the programme, he stated the
following:
For the cassava and maize production the farmers were provided with all
the input and with technical support from IITA we recorded a very successful
season.
In the case of water melon BAT Nigeria Foundation introduced the
production of watermelon to farmers in Ago-Are for the very first time with
50 female farmers. The intention was to assist the farmers in the production
of a mid-season crop,which will serve as a source of extra income to the
farmers. Each farmer was assisted with the cultivation of one acre of
farmland and was also provided with improved watermelon seeds and herbicides
to guide against insect infestation.
At the end of the production process, all the farmers were linked to the
market for the sale of their produce.
With benefit of training and marketing experience enjoyed from the first
crop of watermelon, some of the farmers successfully and without support,
cultivated watermelon in the next planting season.
In the area of Information Technology the foundation selected 12
unemployed graduates trainned them on IT skills and then opened and equipped
the internet café with 25 computers and a VSAT facility that provides
internet connectivity.
In this initiative, the 12 beneficiaries were employed in the
establishment.
The internet café also doubled as a business centre where customers also
have the opportunity to make telephone calls through the Internet.
The centre aldo provides additional services such as digital
photography, typesetting, photocopying, scanning, sales of GSM recharge
cards, binding and laminating.
Furthermore, BATN Foundation installed a brand new soundproof 27 KVA
generator for both sections of the enterprize, in order to ensure 24 hours
uninterrupted power supply.
The 12 beneficiaries that went through 18 months training are currently
earning income from proceeds of theCenter. They are also joint shareholders
of the business venture.
BATNF executed the project in 3 stages. The first stage was a 7-month
computer and business management training of the 12 beneficiaries that
passed the interview stage.
The second stage focused on establishment of an internet café and
business center for the beneficiaries to operate.
In conclusion the third and final stage of the project called the Kaduna
Community Internet Café and Business Center was donated to the Vision Trust
Foundation ( VTF) to manage in partnership with the 12 beneficiaries.
Objective of BATNF Poverty Reduction Project is to contribute towards
poverty reduction and to minimize the high level of unemployment within
benefiting communities in Nigeria.
This project focuses on the empowerment of young adults to be
self-employed.
Funded by BATN Foundation the Kaduna Poverty Reduction Project was
executed in administrative collaboration with Vision Trust Foundation, which
is an NGO based in Kaduna.

Original_Search_Title: 'Our Intention is to Improve On Our Eating Habit'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Our Intention is to Improve On Our Eating Habit'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Dr. (Mrs.) Rosemary Essien, the Special Assistant to Governor Attah
on Food Sufficiency, tells Mary Ekah the effort of the administration to
make Akwa Ibom State the flagship of the agricultural sector in the
country.
You are the Special Assistant to Governor Attah on Food Sufficiency,
a seeming oversight function. To what extent have your contributions
repositioned the agric-sector in the state?
Well, as a special assistant on Food Sufficiency, we strive to represent
His Excellency, Arch.(Obong) Victor Attah in our area of
specialization. These include but not limited to supervision and monitoring
of agric projects across the state and to see particularly how the masses
(the prime target of government) can benefit. It might interest you to know
that even the presidency also has a Special Assistant on Food
Sufficiency. Hence, we have also striven to grab our share of the Federal
Government's allocation in that regard for the overall benefit of Akwa Ibom
State. Apart from that, being that His Excellency has special interest in
food production, we monitor and ensure that there is not just food in
quantity for the people, but in quality throughout the state.
At the moment, we are trying to organize a special programme on food
security. Although it is the federal government's brainchild, the essence is
to replicate that with a view to covering more senatorial districts of the
State. We are also taking a more-than-cursory look at small agric-based
ventures which the majority of the people usually overlook. These
encompasses snail, mushroom and fish farming. With the Snail Farmers'
Association already on ground, we are poised to start a pilot snail farm in
the state - this arises from the workshop we also held for them (snail
farmers) earlier. Similarly, edible mushroom farming is seen as a
gap-bridging measure, in view of the ubiquitous and well known poultry and
piggery farming. That aspect has equally received the blessing of the state
government, which informs why arrangement has been concluded towards
inviting an expert who would educate intending farmers on the proper method
to adopt and so on, in view also of mushroom's richness in protein
content. The beauty of this is that, it hopes to create lots of
jobs. Government has been able to procure tones of fertilizer, distributed
to farmers at heavily subsidized rates. The intention of government here, we
can see, as being a deliberate move to ensure increased production in
various aspects of farming.
What aspect of the agric sector reforms would you say has recorded
the highest success rate so far?
There are lots of cassava programmes even at the Federal level but we
are proud in the agric-sector because Akwa Ibom State is rated number
one. In other words, we are the only state in Nigeria to have fully paid our
share of the counterpart funding of cassava projects. As earlier stipulated,
we have tree crop rehabilitation program, cocoa, oil palm and rubber. And to
shore up the throughput, an investor is being expected in the state. What
makes the expected investor unique is his intended innovative fishery -
"caged fishery", a departure from the conventional pond method. Plans are
already completed to start integrated agric-project(s) comprises like
chicken hatchery, food mill, chicken layer farm, chicken boiler farm,
chicken processing plant, pig farming and pig processing plant.
It was widely carried in the dailies that the IITA was building a
resource centre somewhere in Akwa Ibom State. What is your position on the
project and what contributions would you envisage from the centre towards
food sufficiency and sustenance?
I would like to correct an impression that IITA was constructing a
Resource Center in Akwa Ibom State was not unconditional. What this means is
that the state had paid #76.2 million as counterpart funding for cassava
mosaic disease(CMD). Hence, with their expertise on cassava, they came to
build the Resource Center at Nung Udoe, Ibesikpo Asutan local government
area. The thrust, however, is for processing of cassava into chips, pellets,
flour and so, which is number one priority towards having exportable
agricultural products.
What purpose is government's integrated farmers' scheme in the state
meant to serve with respect to job creation?
It is fundamentally a youth empowerment program aimed at assisting the
youth who may not be opportuned to secure white collar jobs. They are
trained in areas of their interest, e.g. piggery, poultry, crop farming and
so forth, thereafter, they are empowered. It is interesting to note that His
Excellency even defied the last public holiday to visit these centers to
enable him see how the beneficiaries were faring. Added to these is the aim
of tackling youth restiveness in the state. This, as can be seen,
demonstrates His Excellency's unequaled commitment in the
agric-sector. Meanwhile, to ensure higher participation level, the
empowerment is organized in batches. So far, two batches have been provided
with revolving loans of #300,000.00 only, with Union Bank PLC as the
facilitator. The third batch will follow soon. That is one way I feel, His
Excellency is helping the youth to ensure self-reliance. But compliance
level is maintained by a monitoring and evaluation team set up by the state
government. Their key responsibility is to keep a tap on participants'
stages of development, while the extension officers assist them in tackling
development problems.
It is no longer news that when government makes provision for
micro-credit facilities, most or all of the beneficiaries, rather than repay
in order for others to also benefit, usually claim it is their share of the
"national cake". For such trend not to rear its ugly head again, what
measure(s) is the government evolving to forestall a reoccurrence?
At this point, I should like to give a distinction between one
terminology and the other. Whereas the integrated scheme has individuals,
especially school leavers as beneficiaries, in the micro-credit facility,
government asks people to form co-operatives, while the entire process is
facilitated through the bank to ensure repayment.
In what ways does government hope to give cassava and other
agric-produce in the state a global outlook, given that supply may
definitively outweigh local demand?
Our slogan is "whatever your profession, farm!". That's why we encourage
everybody to go into cassava farming, including you, the reporters. We also
encourage improved variety with a view to increasing yield. The essence is
to multiply so that others can benefit. The increased production, hopefully,
will be attained because even NDDC is looking forward to acquiring 3000
hectares for cassava production, vis a vis the fact that the state
government does not leave any stone unturned in encouraging people who have
50 hectares and above of farm lands. With this, the surplus will be
processed and exported. You know, cassava flour is used for bread
production, cake, biscuits and for pharmaceutical products.
The success of large-scale agric production lies in the provision of
world class, technology-driven storage facilities. What is government's
preparedness in the provision of storage facilities for the anticipated
deluge of farm produce?
We have storage facilities such as grain silos at USE OFFOT, which has
five bins of 200 tons each with a combined capacity of 1000 metric
tones. Take a look at this ..., (She pointed at a photograph where she
inspected a storage facility). There is a technology-driven warehouse at Idu
Uruan, with a storage capacity put at 400 metric tones. Similarly, there is
yet another 1000 metric tones silos at Mbiabet Ikpe. We are still working on
these and others.
What would you like to be remembered for with the spate of agric
reforms going on in the state?
If our people (Akwa Ibomites) have enough food to eat and be
self-sufficient, we are not lacking though but my intention is to see how we
can improve on our eating habit. I mean, eating quality or health-boosting
food as opposed to quantity.
Who is Dr.(Mrs.) Rosemary Essien?
I am basically a simple person from a humble background, born to Chief
and Mrs. Moses Akpan Ukpong of Ikot Ukpong, Okon, Eket local government Area
of Akwa Ibom State. I am interestingly, the last in a family of six children
- four males and two females. Aside from that, I would say also that I am a
village girl. In other words, in spite of the fact that I grew up in Lagos,
I actually returned to the village at the outbreak of the civil war. In the
process, I attended my primary school at Okon and then and St. Francis
secondary school, Okon.
However, I had to further my education and on that score, I gained
admission into the University of Calabar, where I studied Zoology for my
first degree. Thereafter, I did my primary assignment (youth service) at Oyo
State. And in a bid to consolidate academically, I proceeded for my Masters
degree at the University of Ibadan in 1982.
After my masters programme, I had a stint as a lecturer in the then
college of education, Uyo. I equally lectured when the school was upgraded
to the status of a university (University of Cross River State as it then
was).
In 1985, I got married to Engr. Anietie Essien and consequently joined
him in Lagos. In 1994, I obtained my Ph.D. from the University of
Ibadan. And like I said earlier, am married with children.
Have you ever been in public service before now or were you in the
private sector?
Like I said, I first served as a lecturer at the then College of
Education, Uyo. And even when it was upgraded to university level, I
continued to work there. Interestingly, however, perhaps owing to my area of
research in my Ph.D. programme, i.e., "Cassava Mealy Bugs and its Damage
Assessment". But curiously, I never was keen about jobs outside Lagos, due
ostensibly, to my family. Consequent upon that, I joined my husband in his
business. You know, he is a computer engineer and as such operates under
license as Rosan Ventures, Ikeja, Lagos. He also runs a full lithographic
printing press, where I served as the Executive Director. Along the line, we
veered into Rosan Foods, located at Oduyemi Street, Anifowoshe, Ikeja. These
were basically where I worked before my husband, in his capacity as a
staunch PDP member, introduced me to politics.
How do you allot your time between job and home to produce the
desired result?
There is time for everything, so the bible says. A time to work and a
time for the family. However, when my job springs its usual impromptu
surprises, I would have no option than to oblige. Also, the necessary
synergy is attained given that my husband is such a supportive type, in
spite of the distance between us. But above all, Rosemary is a
Christian.

Original_Search_Title: '$12bn Nigeria Solid On Ground'
Headline: Nigeria: '$12bn Nigeria Solid On Ground'
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Some one year ago, Charles Soludo, a professor of Economics was
appointed Central Bank Governor. Before his appointment he was Chief
Economic Adviser to the President and a major player in the drafting of the
NEEDS (National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy) document, the
philosophy behind the Obasanjo administration's economic programme.
Shortly after he took over at the CBN, Soludo gave the banks December
2005 deadline to have N25 billion minimum capitalisation, a policy that
sparked off a major revolution in the banking industry. He has applied the
same passion and courage in making the CBN perform its primary duty - banker
to the government. For more than two hours last Wednesday in his office in
Abuja, Soludo played host to a team of THISDAY Board of Editors to explain
what he's been doing in the last one year and his plans for the
future. Excerpts:
It's been a privilege to be at the Central Bank from where the economy
of the country is directed. We are here to pay you a visit on the first
anniversary of your very eventful tenure. How has the last one year been and
what do we see in the future?
It's been barely one year and a month since we moved over to this place
and like you said at the beginning, one started running from the very first
day because, first, Nigeria cannot afford to wait and second, the magnitude
of the changes that needed to happen for Nigeria to begin a road to the
future is so great that there was really little time to waste. Again, we
were trying to reinvent the wheel in terms of diagnosis, analysis and so on
and so forth. So we hit the ground right from the beginning and one was
driven by, in my own way of looking at it, clear vision as to what we think
the Central Bank ought to be in this 21st century. A Central Bank that
pursues the role of Nigeria in Africa, the role of Nigeria in the emerging
new world order and the kind of financial system that Nigeria should
have. And so the overriding thing about this vision is that it is Nigeria's
place to lead the black race. It is Nigeria's place to lead Africa, and the
Central Bank must play a pivotal role in not only chauffeuring and managing
the macro-economy, but also in helping to move forward and orchestrate a
financial system that will make Nigeria Africa's financial centre.
The central bank by law is mandated to perform four key functions. The
first is the issuance of legal tender currency and coins in Nigeria. The
second function is maintenance and management of Nigeria's external reserve
in order to preserve the international value of the legal tender
currency. And the third is to maintain a sound monetary policy and a stable
financial system and finally the fourth one, to act as banker and financial
adviser to government. Within each of them, they could also be highly loaded
in terms of what it means, especially the third one. Particularly, if you
put it in compartment, the functions of the central bank are basically one,
operational, that is, the area of currency management, reserve management
and banking relationship. The second is about monitoring, exchange rate and
macro-economic management for the purposes of maintaining price stability
and the third component is that, it is a regulatory function and that is
where the whole issue of having a sound financial system comes in. And
finally, advisory function, that is, the financial adviser to the
government. And we decided to now move in those four areas.
Let me take it from the first one, the issuance of legal tender currency
and management of currency. We quickly recognised that as we speak and over
the years, the Central Bank has not been meeting barely 60 per cent of the
demand for new currency, in terms of supply. Our index, what we should be
supplying should be about 3.5 billion to 4 billion new notes a year. The
central bank was under-performing with regards to the first four core
functions. And something needed to happen. Then why was it not supplying as
much as was needed. A number of reasons.
First, the currency structure is a little bit problematic. The currency
notes, if you look at that note which we are working on it's restructuring,
N5 is as large as N200, N500, it costs almost the same to print any of
it. It cost about N10 to print a N5 note, which tells you obviously
something was fundamentally wrong with the cost structure. Related to the
cost was that we realised that the major reason was that the Nigerian
Security Printing and Minting Company (NSPMC) was not working and the
government was then importing currency. We asked what was happening to the
mint, we were told the plan then was to concession it in a way to a foreign
company and we thought that was a national disgrace. We decided that this
was time we needed to take it over, restructure it and get the mint to work,
because if the mint works, at least the cost structure, we know that part of
those costs, even the cost of importation is highly over-bloated. So the
issue of the mint working was, for us, integral to the success and
performance of the number one function of the central bank and we therefore
made a case that we needed to take it over. We are the key stakeholder of
the mint itself and the mint failing to work jeopardises our own ability to
work and therefore we needed to take over the mint, restructure and prove a
point. And then we made a case and finally the president approved that we
should take over the mint and by February 28 this year, we finally signed
the transfer purchase agreement with the BPE and by March 9, we started. We
set up a new board and the same day we decided to hire an international
firm, Accenture to help us with the recruitment of the management of the
mint. Our mandate is to restructure it between 3 to 5 years and to sell down
our own share, and like I said we want to do it much more quickly. From a
management team of 13, a Managing Director, 4 Executive Directors and 9
General Managers, we have now pruned them down to 5. One MD, 4 GMs and no
EDs, and none of the old people. So we have a brand new team there,
recruited through an international competitive recruitment exercise. We
didn't care who and where they come from.
Following that in the same area is this currency restructuring. Payment
system reforms are also going on. We had a subcommittee of the bankers
committee working on that. We've now inaugurated the national payment system
committee, which I chair. We have met twice now and we are moving
forward. For the electronic payment system to ensure speed, transparency,
and also effectiveness of the overall and security of the payment system,
that is on-going.
On the second key function which is maintenance and management of
foreign reserve. As you are aware, we now have the largest bulk of reserve
in sub-Saharan Africa, it is about $24 billion and the highest we've ever
had in our entire history and that also has a challenge in terms of its
effective management. Consequently, we are doing quite a couple of things in
that regard, we've joined the World Bank reserve management advisory
programme and they are managing part of our reserve now. But we are now
working hard to develop in-house capacity. I don't see what they do that we
cannot find in Nigeria within the central bank and the rest of the world to
be able to do and even do much better. That is the medium-term agenda, to
establish our own infrastructure. Currently, we are in the process of
appointing a global consortium, also appointing some fund managers to help
in managing some of it. Hopefully also, we can get the amendment of our
legislation done because the CBN Act now imposes some restraints in terms of
what we can put the money into and what we can't. It must now be put into
only sovereign instrument, but we want to seek for a little bit of
flexibility in doing that.
So we are moving up our reserve and exchange rate has remained fairly
stable because that is the reason why we are mandated to maintain reserve,
to preserve the international value of the currency. It remains fairly
stable. Last year they actually appreciated by 3.1 percent against the US
Dollars, the first time it has ever done so since 1986 in nominal term. And
even early part of this year, actually by May/June, it appreciated in almost
all markets, against all major currencies. Is it the Euro, even the Pound
Sterling. And we started something different and new. We are now an exchange
rate target bank of plus or minus 3 per cent. This hasn't been done before,
where central bank takes this credibility and say come what may, we are
prepared to defend the Naira, not to depreciate by more than 3 per cent and
not to appreciate by more than 3 per cent and so this has boiled up the
confidence of the investors, especially portfolio investors because you now
know that at most, if the currency depreciates, you lose 3 per cent.
The third key point which is the heart of central bank, where it becomes
a policy institution and a regulatory institution, monetary stability and
that's where the conduct of our monetary policy falls in and I will talk
about that in a second. For the second component is sound banking or
financial system. As you are aware, July 6 last year, depending on whom you
talked to, for the majority of Nigerians, it is like a revolution day for
some bankers. It was like their black Tuesday. But I am not quite sure any
bank can still call it black Tuesday. I think majority have now turned
around to us to recognise that was the revolution day. That was the day we
unveiled the 13 point reform agenda of the banking system, as a major
response not only to the inadequacies and failings of the system itself, but
also an agenda for building a completely new banking system that is
appropriate for the 21st century and consistent with our vision that Nigeria
should be the financial centre of Africa and to build banking system that is
strong and reliable and that is financed by the number of businesses. We've
also embarked on very strict enforcement of strict adherence to corporate
governance in the sector, we had an incentive system. You must have read
about the forbearance that was given to the banks, where we cancelled 80 per
cent of the debts owed to us. Limitations of any government ownership in the
banking system to no more than 10 per cent by 2007. We are also embarking on
micro-finance community banking policy reform, we are working on this and I
think in due course we will come up with some major reforms of these two
areas; the community banking and micro-finance policy for the
country. Mortgage finance reform is also ongoing and the whole thing about
deepening the money market. We are going to be issuing bonds very soon and
the central bank has also included 3 year-old bond and the definition of
liquidity, liquid assets for banks to make them attractive for banks to hold
and so on
What have we achieved so far in the banking sector reform? Quite
phenomenal I must say. By the time we announced this policy, only 2 banks
had N25 billion as total shareholders fund. Today, there are about 21
groupings of banks involving about 64 banks, which are in the process of
merging. Some banks are going it alone and our expectation is that
altogether between 25 to 30 banks will emerge. That is an expectation which
is likely to emerge. There might be more or so, especially as some of them
are still expecting to get in money from abroad. The interest rate regime
have crashed and this is happening for the first time since the
liberalisation of the banking system in the 80s. In fact, the inter-bank
rate went far below 1 per cent in the last 3-4 weeks. The mop up has now
raised it to a little bit over 1 per cent now, but before then, it was far
below 1 per cent and fund managers were scratching their heads. Little
wonder therefore that lending to the private sector is also rising
phenomenally on an annualised basis.
A new CBN is emerging. Plans toward risk-based supervision are actually
ongoing. Funding of large scale projects are beginning to happen. Several
foreign branches are growing in West Africa and so on and this is also
beginning to happen.
For me, the most important thing that has also happened is really like
the policy itself, which is the inducement of a new vision. An era of big
thinking. It is like the policy itself has unleashed a new era of big
thinking in the banking system amongst the banks themselves. Now everybody
wants to be the biggest.
On active monetary policy, which in many cases is at the heart of
central banking anywhere in the world, we tried to signal that central bank
is a policy institution, by creating a new economic policy department, the
new department will take off end of this month or even before the end of
this month. They've advertised for people with Masters degree and Ph.D in
Economics. The idea is to have a crack team, which should have the best
concentration of economists of any institution in Nigeria, the best and the
brightest will be here in this economic policy department because central
bank everywhere is an agent of continuity and stability.
We are also moving towards quarterly review of the minimum rediscount
rate and quarterly review of monetary policy generally including interest
rate regime. We are also committed to zero ways and means as a deliberate
policy, but in the extreme case, to grant government up to 5 per cent of the
previous year's actual revenue. That is the limit and that is why they are
not getting anything more. The law actually allows up to 12.5 per cent, but
we've given them up to 5 per cent. We are committed to restructuring
monetary policy committee, we are moving towards inflation targeting. But
that is an arena over the medium term going up on monetary targeting,
inflation targeting. We now have a single treasury account, we are preparing
to become bankers for major parastatals as a part of our liquidity
management policy as it were.
What has happened in the last one year was actually a year of setting
the pillar, trying to put the key pillars for this new edifice that we want
to build and I think that is something. I think, with every sense of
modesty, we believe that we will move forward significantly in trying to put
those key pillars as it were.
Let's start from where you stopped. Paul Volcker was one of the central bank governors who was known for targeting and bringing down inflation from
13% -14% double digit, to single digit. That achievement was followed by
Alan Greenspan. Within those two regimes, they've kept inflation in most of
the west, well within single digit and below. And the impact of that on the
economic growth, on poverty, on many things has been profound. What is your
plan for inflation?
Inflation is the prime function of central banks anywhere in the
world. Keeping stable prices is a major challenge of central bank all over
the world and the charge in our third function for monetary stability is at
the heart of that. I didn't mention some of the results in that regard that
for the first time in 2004, in almost two decades, the CBN's first target
growth of money supply has actually got it below that. We set a target of 15
per cent growth rate for broad money (M2) last year and we actually ended
with 14 per cent and that was what helped in bringing inflation from about
23.8 per cent end-December 2003 to 10 per cent end-December 2004. It
contributed significantly. However, there are a few caveat that I want to
add in terms of the challenges that we face as Central Bank of Nigeria. The
first is the composition of inflation in Nigeria. In Nigeria, 62 per cent of
what constitutes inflation is actually accounted for by food price. So if
you have bumper harvest, you have a dampening effect on food prices. If you
have famine or people don't farm and you have shortage of supply relative to
existing demand, prices go up. In other words, the significant chunk of the
price formation happen to be largely outside of what the central bank
controls and this is very important in terms of the price formation process,
that much of it is really driven by food prices as it were. And in some
cases, if you also have structural farmers, trade policy for instance, if
all of a sudden, you ban the importation of key essentials and the prices
shoot up, there is very little monetary authorities can do in that
regard. You can be bringing down, controlling money growth and so on until
you then squeeze the economy out.
The second key challenge is that money supply in Nigeria, for example
this year, is largely driven by what happens in terms of the monetisation of
our foreign exchange earnings. We have a peculiar fiscal federalism where
the constitution mandates that you pay in all the proceeds into a federation
account and you share among the federating units and each of them have got
statutory powers to spend, irrespective of the implications on monetary
policy. Therefore, what you have is like, in a case where from last year,
we've moved the benchmark oil price from $25 to $30 that is already 20 per
cent increase in terms of the level of injection of new money into the
system on the fiscal side. Added to that, you are also sharing excess crude,
which is the savings from last year. You are also monetising it. Here is the
dollar component, surrender it to the central bank and we give you the Naira
equivalent. We keep the dollar component as a safeguard to preserve the
value of the naira that we have issued so that in case people come back, and
they want to import, they have the dollar. So you keep the dollar that is
the foreign reserve. Now, what you have is that the rate of this injection
coming from the fiscal side, that literally entails creating money, is a
very unique feature of our type of economy. In most other countries, the
major source of revenue of spending by government is really coming from
taxation and so on, so it doesnít involve injection of new money into the
system and so in that kind of circumstances, central bank is really severely
constrained. Now, with these two points I have made, first is from the
composition of inflation, second in terms of the source of the growth of
money itself, we are then expected to now curb up the excesses. And so what
are we doing?
First, we now have as in many countries of the world definitions of
inflation into two components; what we call the core inflation, which is the
non-food inflation and of course what you may call the headline inflation or
the overall inflation. We are watching these two closely and see what
progress we are making, even the non-food components, several aspects of it
are also determined by structural factor, not just monetary. Like I talked
about trade policy for example, you wake up and you ban the importation of
rice, which is a national staple, it is bound to raise price. If you
liberalise the downstream petroleum into the transport cost and then other
cost rise up, okay what do monetary authorities do in this case? The point
that I have made by this explanation is for you to see clearly that the
larger component of our inflation is not definitely monetary policy. But
there is still the other arm which we still have to do something, which is
like controlling money in order to control the aggregate demand. This is
where money comes in, controlling money, so that even if you have shortage
of supply of food at least you make sure you control the level of demand
chasing the few available goods so that in the end the prices will not
soar. This is because it makes a difference whether everybody has got one
million in his pocket chasing a few baskets of garri or everybody has got
one thousand chasing the same basket of garri. It will make a difference in
terms of the price of that garri. So what we do is to control the aggregate,
and how? What are the instruments available, we are now working in terms of
deepening the money market and getting a variety of instruments. Currently,
the treasury bills which we are trying to divest, and that is why the
issuance of bonds, which will help to succour some of the liquidity. Second,
beyond the open market operations on daily trading in the OMO, we are now
deploying a set of blunt instruments. It is like we are having an unusual
dance, beating of the drum and we have to dance a little differently. The
money market is still shallow, largely underdeveloped. There are the fiscal
structure, its such that sterilisation is difficult because the law requires
you to monetise and share. Last year, we were able to talk to the states and
also the federal government and they listened to us not to share everything
that they earn and that helped. We recognise as well that part of the
liquidity surge is that because government is so dominant, very heavy, its
deposit in the banking system is a sort of easy money and therefore, today
it is over N200 billion. In fact, as at our last meeting, NNPC alone was
having over N105 billion in the commercial banks and this money just lies
there and what do they do with it, they have to earn returns on them, they
don't just look at it. They come look for treasury bills, they don't find,
they use it and come to the forex to speculate and you have this huge demand
at the foreign exchange market. We woke up one morning and we say okay, we
will pull back these amount of money. We pulled about fifty-something
billion of NNPC money in the commercial bank and you see the demand in the
forex market dropped dramatically over night. That is a blunt instrument and
that is why we say we are now setting up the machinery to become the banker
to about 5 to 7 key parastatals of government, so that we suck up, there is
no new injections coming from them into the banks, that is one major area of
liquidity control.
Why don't you benchmark personal earnings against the dollars so
whatever rate you deal with have an impact. So how do you deal with the
continuing poverty of the people and at the same time the government is
getting richer. All the government need to do is to take the dollar exchange
at any rate it chooses and it has Naira. That was for many years, the source
of finance for many governments. How do you use the exchange rate to both
impact inflation and living standard?
I think you raised very deep questions that require very long response,
but I will try to abridge it. The first is to say that the current regime we
have, I said it a moment ago, strives towards stability and that is why we
have announced for the first time in our history an exchange rate band and
that is the greatest assurance other than a fixed exchange rate regime that
is the closest you have in terms of guaranteeing stability of the exchange
rate and that is where we are today. So the first fear that you have is
already allayed by the current regime that we have and that is the regime of
exchange rate stability. But that, you can only sustain and let me get down
to the fundamentals in terms of what you raised. I wish we could go back to
the old days. Maybe, by then, the total stock of M2 in the economy was
probably N5 million, and then relate it to the earnings of your foreign
exchange. This thing is a simple arithmetic, how much forex do you have? It
is like exchange of commodity. How many yams do you have versus how many
tubers of cassava do you have and you want the two of them to exchange. If
you've got 10 tubers of yam and 10 tubers of cassava and you want them to
exchange, you can exchange one for one, but if you've got only 10 tubers of
yams and you have on the other hand 300 cassavas, that is a problem. As at
the time you were talking about, the stock of money supply in the economy
was probably N5million and you could see why you could actually maintain
that level of exchange rate as at that time by fiat. But then overtime, you
realize what happen, once the supply, what you have in terms of naira
outstripped what you had on the other side, then obviously if you get it by
force, then black market will emerge, because the demand for it becomes
fierce. And what you have now is a situation where you have four hundred and
something billion naira in circulation and what do you earn in terms of
forex? There is another way, which is government deficit, before government
financed this deficit mainly by resort to central bank finance as another
way of creating money, but that is the collective decision of the entire
country. Through these various means of creating money and injecting, you've
created a pool of hundreds of billion of naira out there and there is
nowhere with your 10 billion dollar earning matching against N400 billion,
N500 billion. I am not talking about the total stock, I am talking about
just currency outside the banking system. Now you have over a trillion, in
terms of the total money in the economy and that is all deposit plus the
ones outside, I mean the broad M2. You can't still have that
equilibrium. Once you liberalise this and you have the forces of demand and
supply, it will obviously give you a higher rate, that is one big thing. The
second, I would say for the future, it is something that the central bank
exists to do that is to maintain price and exchange rate stability, if every
segment plays its own role, our job here is to keep the naira stable, but it
has to be an exchange rate that responds to the fundamentals. When you talk
about benchmark, it depends on the deep issue, which you need quite a bit of
analysis to come down, because it is not as easy as the way you paint. You
might ask whose income, salaries of people paid for by government, the
private sector, the informal sector, whose income and that is an economy
where many believe that the informal sector accounts for as high as 40 per
cent of the total output in the economy. That's also an economy where the
total GDP, income, when you decompose it in terms of the sources of income,
the bit coming from government, is minute, it's a smaller proportion of the
total income. So then the question is, are we saying the incomes of civil
servants who are paid for by government gets dollarised? And that will raise
another issue. Again who has the dollar? You can't benchmark it for
everyone. The second thing which is the other aspect that might make it a
little bit absurd, is the size of what you call the tradeable versus the
non-tradeable. What is the proportion of your total economic activity that
is traded?
Suppose, we change our constitution and say 100% resource control. Where will government spending come? Government money will now come as you have
said it should come. Internally, by taxation and by other means
internally. We've built over the years lazy governments, that have also
taken people's resources to impoverish the people. If oil income were to be
controlled by the people of the Niger Delta, where will the government's
resources come from ? Where will its earnings come from? Today, oil price is
$60 in the market, it makes absolutely no meaning to the average Nigerian,
whether oil price is $10 or $60, the people it matters to are the ones in
government because they have more money to spend as they choose and as you
know most of our government at all levels are not accountable. How can you
make people benefit if there is no money in the oil market?
The whole issue of resource control is a big issue, a big topic, and I
hope the National Political Reform Conference has solved this one, but the
point is that how do people benefit? Let's put it this way, I guess I would
also avoid hyperbole on the extreme of it because it is important to put the
dynamics right. Currently, you have heard, of course you've seen the
performance of the economy in 2004. Somebody will say ah! This oil price was
generous, but that the first time we had an oil price boom and non-oil
sector out-paced the growth rate of the oil sector. It comes in many ways;
government spending is just one way, one aspect of it. The other aspect of
it is that this government spending in various sectors also grew
phenomenally, at least at the federal level, and I am sure many state
governments also grew. Leave out the issue about corruption, you talked
about corruption and that some governments have not been efficient. We are
struggling to do so at the federal level. But the impact fits in, in various
ways, not even in terms of your exchange rate, which is your passion. We
tried to control money supply largely last year, but we must also give
credit to the fact that there was a build up of reserve, we must also
acknowledge is partly due to the high oil prices. And that has implication
for the stability of the exchange rate. If you are running out of reserve,
that is why we are in a position where we are able to respond, if the demand
for forex is $180 million, we supply it, that helps to keep exchange rate
stable and with the stability and appreciation of the exchange rate the
inflation number came down dramatically. If you take the build up of
reserves alone what the confidence meant, sovereign risk rating and what
that has meant in terms of even lines of credit to the commercial banks. But
the issue of level of exchange rate is a different question because I am
