sure you know the exchange rate of the Italian Lira before they moved to
Euro was over 2000 to a dollar, you know that the Japanese Yen is a hundred
and something and it has remained a hundred and something over the years to
a dollar. What they worry mostly and what most policy makers worry
everywhere is about stability and that is very critical. So for our own
case, I will leave the other part of it, we will take it to the
conference.
Some banks who were at the market for their IPOs, had been probed by the CBN some shares were not approved during verification. The NSE
director-general had a problem with that by referring to the President's
statement that, no matter where the money comes from, so far it is coming
into the economy, it is okay. What is your take on that?
Secondly you said by the time the recapitalisation exercise is over, wewill likely have between 25 to 30 banks. Last year we had so many groups
that came up and some have pulled out of their groups. Don't you think you
have been unduly optimistic?
Let me start with the first one on the verification of capital. First is
that I want to believe Dr. Okereke-Onyuike who told us that the press
misreported her and I want to believe her because she was here the very next
day after that newspaper report. She was here at the National Consultative
Council on Consolidation and she said, in fact, she even called the
President to say she didn't say such a thing. Of course she couldn't have
said that. Put it the other way, I am not sure anybody, and I donít think
it is a reasonable thing to let anybody bring anything into the banking
sector particularly. Because this is also a country that has anti-money
laundering act and it is still the law of the land. NDLEA law is still
active and this has not been repealed. Particularly for the banking sector
that you are trying to clean up, for a payment system that should be safe,
transparent and so on especially that your banking system is your window to
the rest of the world. That is the vehicle through which we interact with
the rest of the world in business and if you start up on the footing by
saying, that particular bank is owned by drug baron, the other bank, the
major shareholders are 419ners and that becomes the image of the banking
system that you give to the rest of the world, you are dead. I am sure even
for the country, that is not the kind of banking system you would want to
emerge. You don't want drug barons, fraudsters that would be deadly for the
safety of depositors fund so to speak. Banking industry, unlike many other
industries, such as the manufacturing company or whatever, that is
different, the day it stops producing, it is it's capital that will go
down. The bank is different, the day it stops, it goes down with innocent
people's money and to that extent the central bank has a responsibility to
the generality of Nigerians to be sure that their money is safe. That is why
we even have to do some kind of security checks on people to be directors of
banks. Because you must be able to certify that these are fit and proper
persons to run a bank, otherwise you wake up one morning, they wreck the
place and depositors money would go. And this is not only just because we
have the laws in our books, but because we must also ensure that the
integrity of the entire financial system is not just jeopardized by having
some fraudsters, one or two of them owning banks. But we've also been very
conscious of the fact that many people may not have the best of
documentations in the world, they may not have all the numerous
documentations we have required and therefore we set more team and try to
verify as quickly as we can and at least the people must meet what we
consider the minimum standard and so far the good news is that our rejection
rate is below 10 per cent.
The second one is about the optimism. I think we are fairly optimistic
and I think the optimism is well grounded. We are running very fast and in
fact many didn't even expect that people are going to receive approval in
principle about this stage. The fact that you already have 2 groups that
have crossed this stage even much earlier tells you how timely the process
is in terms of the time-table and so these are the early ones that have
finished. But in terms of the time-table, it is on course and you know the
process of doing due diligence is not easy.

Original_Search_Title: Qingsong Warns Farmers Against Misusing Money
Headline: Zambia: Qingsong Warns Farmers Against Misusing Money
Body_Text:
Kalomo and Sinazongwe — FOOD and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) country representative Dong Qingsong has warned that he would embarrass farmer groups which are in the habit of misusing donor money.
Speaking in Kalomo and Sinazongwe districts of Southern Province over the weekend when he toured the districts to inspect Telefood projects sponsored by his office aimed at alleviating hunger among the local farmers, Qingsong said he would ensure that donor assistance and money were properly accounted for.
Qingsong said his role was to beg from other donor countries and then render that help to the farmer groups' projects aimed at alleviating poverty in the country.
He said it was because of the food shortages that Southern Province was experiencing that FAO was forced to partner with government and non-governmental organisations (NGOs) in trying to mitigate the food crisis in the province.
He said about US$76,000 was given to each of the two NGOs namely Africare and Programme Against Malnutrition (PAM) promoting cassava growing in the areas.
Qingsong said the British Embassy in Lusaka had agreed to give FAO more money to help the Zambian people.
"That's why it is my responsibility to go out and beg for money to help Zambia. That's why I say, you should be careful in the way you use the donor money and assistance. I will embarrass those farmer groups who don't properly use such assistance," he said.
He gave an example of a farmer group in Chongwe district that ate all the chickens that they were given except the building that they could not eat and vowed never to give such an organisation any other assistance.
Qingsong said people of Southern Province should not only regard white maize as the only food.
He said the colour of the food should not be an issue but survival and urged people to regard cassava as food also.
"Please, we have to be practical. Once we consider that whatever is there and is edible, should be eaten, then, there will be no hunger in the province even at household level," he said. "So let us work together. Me, I want to work with you if you are serious."
And Nankupasya village secretary Constantino Malambo said farmers would be happy to diversify into other crops such as cassava in view of the erratic rainfall the province was experiencing.
Malambo noted that cassava was a drought resistant crop and could survive in areas such as the valleys.
He asked for more cassava cuttings to be distributed to all villagers in Mapatizya.

Original_Search_Title: No Power Tussle in NDDC - Edem
Headline: Nigeria: No Power Tussle in NDDC - Edem
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — CHAIRMAN, Board of Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC), Amb. Sam Edem, has denied any power tussle between it and management of the commission.
Amb. Edem said this at a press briefing in Port Harcourt, Rivers State.
Edem who was reacting to an allegation that a tussle between him and Managing Director of the commission, Mr. Emmanuel Aguariavwodo led to his suspension, said he has nothing to do with it.
"I repeat, there is no power tussle at the commission. The Act that created the commission is very clear as to who does what and when, so there is no power tussle," he insisted.
According to Amb. Edem, creator of NDDC, President Olusegun Obasanjo, has his ways and means of finding out what was happening at the NDDC and also has powers to take the decisions affecting the commission.
He debunked speculations that Aguariavwodo was suspended from office on the strength of a petition written by the NDDC chairman.
"Now quote me, I repeat, quote me. I don't know how to write petitions. I have never written one in all my career in foreign service and I don't intend to write one. I don't know how to write petitions," he said.
Amb. Edem explained that as chairman of NDDC, it was his duty to carry all staff along, including the former managing director.
He disclosed that he had never awarded any contract since coming into office, adding that his major desire was to create tools for youths of Niger Delta region.
He stated that he was not aware of any contract to build schools. According to him, when these contracts were to be awarded the board was expected to follow due process and adhere to guiding principles in the award of contracts.
Edem, who lamented the rate of unemployment in Niger Delta said the major challenge facing him was how to create jobs.
Awarding contracts, Edem said, is not on his list of priority, rather his focus was how to affect youths positively by making them gainfully employed.
One of the ways of creating jobs for youths, Edem disclosed, was through revamping agriculture under the commission's quick impact scheme.
He said cassava could bring in much earning as oil and employment would be created through cultivation, bush clearing, processing, packaging and marketing.
This, NDDC boss disclosed, that the Chinese had asked for a million tons of cassava from Nigeria, lamenting that only 8 tons could be produced.
The chairman announced that as a take-off, NDDC is targeting 3000 hectares in each state, adding that by middle of next year, cassava products from the scheme would be available in the market.
He also disclosed that companies from the USA, Korea and South Africa will partner with the commission in processing and marketing cassava products.

Original_Search_Title: GM Cassava Uses Viral Gene to Fight Disease
Headline: Africa: GM Cassava Uses Viral Gene to Fight Disease
Body_Text:
Cassava leaves showing signs of mosaic disease
Researchers have used genes from a virus that periodically devastates
cassava crops in Africa to create cassava plants that can resist the
virus. The finding could save African farmers large economic losses.

Original_Search_Title: Putting It Point Blank to FCT Residents...
Headline: Nigeria: Putting It Point Blank to FCT Residents...
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Minister of the Federal Capital Territory, Mallam Nasir el-Rufai, last week took time off to explain to residents of the city issues that have been subject of controversy with his administration. As usual, removal of illegal structures was among the list for the occasion.
Called FCT Town Hall Meeting, the occasion was an interactive meeting with residents and stakeholders in the project called FCT which provided sterling opportunity for all to bare their minds and in return get an explanation from the man who has been roasted more than he is toasted in the discharge of his duty. And detailed explanation they got.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger Begins to Bite in Namitete
Headline: Malawi: Hunger Begins to Bite in Namitete
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Residents of Namitete, on the outskirts of Lilongwe have started
feeling the consequential effects of a shortage of the staple crop,
maize with most going for days without a proper meal, it has been
learnt.
This situation has forced many of the school-going children to
completely abandon their classes at school to find food for survival
purposes.
"Most of the children in the area are no longer interested in going to
school. They cannot learn anything on an empty stomach and all they do
now is to look for food all day long," said one resident Joseph
Mtimawafa.

Original_Search_Title: Mwanawasa's Achievements
Headline: Zambia: Mwanawasa's Achievements
Body_Text:
IF you asked anyone about the formula for success, it would be quite impossible to tabulate, but ask about the formula for failure, and the writing is on the wall for all to see - it is to want to please everyone in all you do.
This observation, first made by one great philosopher of many centuries ago, seems to be the only prescription that has worked for President Levy Mwanawasa and his Government to achieve whatever can be counted to his credit.

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Supply China 30,000 Barrels of Crude Oil Per Day
Headline: Nigeria to Supply China 30,000 B/D of Crude Oil....Corporation to Make$800m
Body_Text:
Abuja — PETROCHINA International and the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) have signed an oil supply pact that will result in the sale and export of 30,000 barrels of crude per day (p/d) to China to help power the fast expanding economy.
It was gathered that the contract is renewable after one year and is expected to fetch the Corporation about US$800 million, given the prevailing international market prices of crude oil.

Original_Search_Title: Famine Threat As Elephants Invade Farms
Headline: Kenya: Famine Threat As Elephants Invade Farms
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Famine will persist in Taita Taveta District unless urgent steps are
taken to stop the destruction of crops by elephants, a councillor has
said.
Addressing a meeting at Sagalla Location at the weekend, Mr James Mboga
said many farms, especially in the lower parts of the district, had been
invaded by the beasts from Tsavo National Park.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria-China Sign $800m Crude Oil Sale Agreement
Headline: Nigeria-China Sign $800m Crude Oil Sale Agreement
Body_Text:
Abuja — Nigeria and China over the weekend sealed a Long Term Crude Oil Purchase deal amounting to $800 million (about N105.6 billion) per year.
The agreement, which is subject to yearly renewal, will see the Chinese government buying Nigeria's crude oil to the tune of 30,000 barrels daily for a period of five years.

Original_Search_Title: Ogun to Fight Robbery With GRRS
Headline: Nigeria: Ogun to Fight Robbery With GRRS
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — Ogun State Government is to launch an anti-robbery outfit to be known as Gateway Rapid Response Squad (GRRS) as part of efforts to curb armed robbery especially in the border areas of the state.
Governor Gbenga Daniel made the disclosure while fielding questions from a cross-section of people at Wasimi Okuta in Imeko/Afon Local Government, during his tour of the council area.

Original_Search_Title: High Prices of Food: Obasanjo Should Act Now
Headline: Nigeria: High Prices of Food: Obasanjo Should Act Now
Body_Text:
The unprecedented rise in the prices of essential items like sugar, salt, flour etc is putting a lot of strain on household budgets all over Nigeria. The price rise began with cement which rose from less than one thousand Naira to over 1500 Naira. Equally prices of food items like maize, guinea corn, yam, garri and beans have been on the rise. The killer punch in the skyrocketing of prices has been the sudden jump in prices of essential commodities like sugar, salt and vegetable oil. Nigerians are now paying up to 9,000 for a 50 kilograms of sugar which used to be sold for less than 3,000 just three months ago. The price of salt has also trebled just as flour and vegetable oil are also on the rise.
If things are very tough for people on fixed incomes, imagine how it has been for people whose annual income is next to nothing, yet have to pay in some instance three times more for items like salt and sugar. The jump in price is also coming at a difficult time for people in the rural farming zone when the farming season is just beginning. Prices of food items are usually high at this time when it is far from harvest time and most food reserves are exhausted.

Original_Search_Title: Celebrating Liberia's Heritage: Who is the Kpelle Woman?
Headline: Liberia: Celebrating Liberia's Heritage: Who is the Kpelle Woman?
Body_Text:
As we celebrate Liberia's independence this month, Liberian Observer
Woman's Page is celebrating the things that make us Liberians - our various
ethnicities and cultures, and all the things that make us who we are proud
to be. What is a Kpelle woman? Woman's Page editor Boto Bradford interviews
Dumalo Traub Dennis, a Kpelle-Gbandi from Handii, a small village in lower
Bong County, Liberia. Dennis currently works and lives in Minnesota with her
husband Jeff and two boys, Zanneh and Jeff, Jr.
What is your full name?

Original_Search_Title: Igbinedion And the Transformation of Edo Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Igbinedion And the Transformation of Edo Economy
Body_Text:
Lagos — Up until the late 1990s, anybody standing at the Ring-Road end of Mission or Akpakpava Road in Benin City, capital of Edo State, could have an unobstructed view of the road to its end at New Benin or Ikpoba Slope. Traffic on either road was light in view of the fewness of cars and other vehicles available in the city. The pace was generally unhurried. Keeping appointments in the city was no ordeal, as the individual could arrive on time. Similarly, commercial activities were limited to buying and selling of mainly agricultural produce-yams, plantain, cassava etc - in the five markets in existence. Highbrow shopping was done by the few elite in the few supermarkets in the city. Notable among the stores that operated well before the Structural Adjustment Programme unravelled merchandising were Omo Stores, along Mission Road, Kingsway Stores located at the present site of the Oba Market and later relocated to Akpakpava Road and Leventis Stores on Sakponba Road. These stores dealt mainly on children's wears, canned foods, toiletries, novels, wines and spirits. Car dealerships and electronics stores were also few, with only SCOA and Leventis companies as the outlets for the sale of Peugeot and Mercedes cars respectively. Private car dealers, which number in their hundreds today, were non-existent.
Banking business was operated by about five banks: Union Bank, First Bank, United Bank for Africa, New Nigeria Bank and the defunct African Continental Bank, which provided some facilities for the relatively few businesses that operated in or out of the state at the time. The industrial development of Edo State, or Bendel as it used to be before the state creation exercise of 1991, was limited to the state-owned Bendel Brewery, which produced Crystal beer and the Bendel Cement Factory, Okpella, producers of Rhino cement; Guinness Nigeria Plc, produced Harp lager and Guinness Stout. Even such private enterprises as health clinics and maternity centres, eateries and hotels were equally few.

Original_Search_Title: Southern Africa Faces Severe Food Shortages: Millions of People Need Urgent Humanitarian Aid
Headline: Southern Africa Faces Severe Food Shortages: Millions of People Need Urgent Humanitarian Aid
Body_Text:
Rome and Johannesburg — More than 10 million people will need humanitarian assistance in six
countries across southern Africa over the coming year following yet another
year of poor agricultural production caused by erratic weather together with
late, and in some cases unaffordable inputs, such as fertilizer and seeds,
two UN agencies and the Southern Africa Development Community (SADC) warned
Thursday.
Reports compiled by the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization
(FAO) and the United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) - following recent
joint Crop and Food Supply Assessment Missions (CFSAMs) in Lesotho, Malawi,
Mozambique, Swaziland, and Zambia - show that countries were not able to
grow enough food to meet domestic needs and that even allowing for
considerable commercial imports, serious food shortages will persist until
the next harvest in May 2006.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Drops
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Drops
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA'S annual inflation rate dropped to 10.7 percent in June compared to 11.7 percent in May.
The fall is largely attributed to the substantial decrease in prices of food items especially staple foods, fruits, vegetables and fish during the month.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria's Fate in Biotechnology
Headline: Nigeria's Fate in Biotechnology
Body_Text:
Lagos — The science of biotechnology is a must for any nation wishing to be self sufficient
It is estimated that 15 years from now, 50 per cent of the global economy will be bioeconomy-based, according to the Minister of Science and Technology, Prof. Turner Isoun.

Original_Search_Title: Ten Million in Need of Food Aid, Agencies Warn
Headline: Southern Africa: Ten Million in Need of Food Aid, Agencies Warn
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — More than 10 million people will need humanitarian assistance in six
countries across Southern Africa in the coming year, two UN agencies and the
Southern Africa Development Community (SADC) have warned.
The agencies issued an urgent appeal for US $266 million in food aid to
assist the region's vulnerable people.

Original_Search_Title: Caution on $60 Per Barrel Oil
Headline: Nigeria: Caution on $60 Per Barrel Oil
Body_Text:
Once again, the international price of crude oil has attained a record
height from which it is expected to recede very little. For Nigeria and
Nigerians, it's both good and bad news as we have been through similar
situation in 1973. That was when the Organisation of Petroleum Exporting
Countries, OPEC, first used its power as a cartel to raise crude oil prices
from about $3 per barrel to $40. At that time, the unprecedented price
dictated, not by the market but by fiat, was followed by negative
predictions about its impact on the global economy on the one hand and by
acclamation in the oil producing countries on the other as their economies
presumably stood to benefit.
In the end, neither the worst scenario nor the most optimistic
prediction for OPEC countries occurred. The world economy was in recession
for several years but momentum was restored as several of the OPEC
countries, as net-importers of finished goods, recycled virtually all the
increased revenue from oil. Nigeria's case was even worse. In addition to
legitimate import, most of the oil windfall returned to the western
countries through pervasive corruption without any savings from the oil wind
fall.

Original_Search_Title: Suspected Witches Find Refuge in Kaleni Hills
Headline: Zambia: Suspected Witches Find Refuge in Kaleni Hills
Body_Text:
ON the periphery of Kaleni hills in Mwinilunga district, North-Western
Province, is a mission hospital and Kaleni High school. Adjacent to the
school in a bush, is a nicely-built six bedroomed house which is secluded
from the rest of the houses in the area.
The house was built for old women driven out of various villages by
their relatives because they were suspected to be witches.

Original_Search_Title: C'ttee to Monitor MDGs Inaugurated
Headline: Nigeria: C'ttee to Monitor MDGs Inaugurated
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo on 29 June in Abuja inaugurated the
Assessment and Monitoring Committee on the Millennium Development Goals
(MDGs).
The President reiterated the guiding philosophy behind the MDGs as one
devoted to 'Spare no efforts to free our fellow men, women and children from
the abject and dehumanising conditions of extreme poverty to which more than
one billion of them are currently subjected'

Original_Search_Title: New Cassava Variety to Fight Food Insecurity
Headline: Kenya: New Cassava Variety to Fight Food Insecurity
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya Agricultural Research Institute (Kari) has developed a cassava variety for flood prone Nyando and Kisumu districts.
A researcher at the Kibos Station, Ruth Olale, said yesterday that the cassava, Fleshy Variety, would reduce food insecurity caused by annual flooding and drought.

Original_Search_Title: Biosafety: a West African Regional Approach
Headline: Nigeria: Biosafety: a West African Regional Approach
Body_Text:
Lagos — Many countries of the West African sub-region seek to elaborate the biosafety systems necessary to access and safely use the products of modern biotechnology. Increased attention and effort to develop biosafety is being catalysed by entry into force of the Cartagena protocol for and testing and adoption of genetically engineered crops in the region.
Transgenic insect-resistant cotton is in its second year of field trials in Burkina Faso, and continued commercial production in South Africa. This development has drawn attention to the environment and economic benefit of the technology, and as a result, West Africa countries including Ghana, Benin, mail, Chad, Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire, Senegal and niger have indicated interest in testing biotech cotton, cowpeas, cassava and/or other crops of regional importance.

Original_Search_Title: Who is the Kpelle Woman?
Headline: Liberia: Who is the Kpelle Woman?
Body_Text:
As we celebrate Liberia's independence this month, Liberian Observer
Woman's Page is celebrating the things that make us Liberians - our various
ethnicities and cultures, and all the things that make us who we are proud
to be. What is a Kpelle woman? Woman's Page editor Boto Bradford interviews
Dumalo Traub Dennis, a Kpelle-Gbandi from Handii, a small village in lower
Bong County, Liberia. Dennis currently works and lives in Minnesota with her
husband Jeff and two boys, Zanneh and Jeff, Jr.
What is your full name?

Original_Search_Title: Becoming an Entrepreneur in a Changing Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Becoming an Entrepreneur in a Changing Economy
Body_Text:
Lagos — In an economy with a revolutionary reform programme that lays emphasis on poverty alleviation and the private sector as the driving force of economic revival, what are the challenges of starting a business? It is an issue that was put in perspective recently in Lagos. Max Amuchie writes
Since 1999 when the present civilian administration after several after several years of military rule, the challenge has been how to revive the economy and put the country back on the path of sustainable development. That challenge has been confronted by the government using several measures. But the most far-reaching of the measures is the reform programme the government has put in place.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Wilt, the Disease Robbing the Country of a Major Staple Food
Headline: Uganda: Banana Wilt, the Disease Robbing the Country of a Major Staple Food
Body_Text:
Uganda is the second largest producer of bananas in the world after
India. Currently she is producing over 8.6 million tonnes per annum which is
30 percent of world production.
However, the sustainability of this agricultural and economic position
could be lost because of the increasing effects of the unprecedented banana
bacterial wilt that hit the country four years back.

Original_Search_Title: The Game is Up for Nebbi Poachers
Headline: Uganda: The Game is Up for Nebbi Poachers
Body_Text:
Kampala — When Leba Oketcha worked with the Uganda Electricity Board in the 1980s as a meter reader, nobody looked at him as a potential poacher.
But, back home in Kitolo village, Atyak parish, Pakwach sub-county in Nebbi district, he had left an entrenched poaching culture and when he retired after eight years of service, he got back to live out what he was taught in his early age-poaching.

Original_Search_Title: Our Investment in Vmobile is a Good Deal - Commissioner
Headline: Nigeria: Our Investment in Vmobile is a Good Deal - Commissioner
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Chief Clement Ofuani is Commissioner for Economic Planning, Delta
State. In this interview with Jaiyeola Andrews, he speaks on the state's
privatization and commercialization programme, SEEDS, the state's divestment
from Vmobile, Nigeria's external debt controversies, crisis between the
presidency and national assembly over 2005 budget, World Bank President's
visit to Nigeria, and other issues of interest. Excerpts:
What do you think the state stands to gain from the recent privatization
and commercialization of its enterprises, and what is the fate of the
staff.

Original_Search_Title: Local Brew Raises Eyebrows
Headline: Rwanda: Local Brew Raises Eyebrows
Body_Text:
Kigali — A locally distilled alcoholic beverage commonly known as 'Baritereta' has raised furore among residents of the Nyabugogo suburb of Kigali city.
According to the Kimisagara Sector Coordinator, Edro Albert, the brew is manufactured from different chemicals that make it highly potent akin to drugs.

Original_Search_Title: France Assists Ghana
Headline: Ghana: France Assists Ghana
Body_Text:
The government of France has presented 1.29billion cedis to the
government of Ghana to assist the nation in the implementation of
Agronomical Research in the field of trees and crops cultivation.
The financial support given to the nation will improve the capacities of
the Council for Scientific Research and farmers to arrive at a solution to
pests and diseases in crops and to ensure food security in the country.

Original_Search_Title: DCE Calls for Outgrowers Scheme to Create Jobs
Headline: Ghana: DCE Calls for Outgrowers Scheme to Create Jobs
Body_Text:
It has been suggested to all district assemblies to go into outgrowers
agricultural business scheme to create jobs for the unemployed youth to
generate revenue for districts so as to reduce pressure on government.
The Yilo Krobo District Chief Executive, Mr. Christian Kofi Tettey, made
this suggestion when he took this reporter round the 108 acres of a mango
farm interspersed with cassava at Somanya.

Original_Search_Title: Debt Relief for Nigeria: a Dividend of Democracy
Headline: Nigeria: Debt Relief for Nigeria: a Dividend of Democracy
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Text of a broadcast by President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday
It is with great joy that I address you today on one of the pillars of
success of this Administration. It is the debt relief already announced. I
address you on it for several reasons.

Original_Search_Title: Bee Keeping, Honey: Is It Malawi's Hidden Treasure?
Headline: Malawi: Bee Keeping, Honey: Is It Malawi's Hidden Treasure?
Body_Text:
- Can it take over from tobacco?
It is an undisputable fact that the anti-tobacco lobby in the western
world is determined as ever to completely phase out, off the face of the
earth, Malawi's main foreign exchange earner - tobacco.

Original_Search_Title: Initiatives to Diversify Crops Bear Fruit
Headline: Eswatini: Swaziland: Initiatives to Diversify Crops Bear Fruit
Body_Text:
Mbabane — A government initiative to encourage smallholder Swazi farmers to diversify their crops as a counter to ongoing drought and rising food aid dependency is beginning to bear fruit.
Two years ago, Amos Tsabedze, a small-scale farmer in the southern Shiselweni Region, made the radical move of replacing maize cultivation with cotton. Now the government is encouraging him to switch to cassava, the starchy, semi-sweet tuber for which he has yet to develop a taste.

Original_Search_Title: The Day of Scientific Renaissance of Africa
Headline: Africa: The Day of Scientific Renaissance of Africa
Body_Text:
The Day of Scientific Renaissance of Africa (DSRA) set aside by the Organisation of Africa Unity (OAU) now the African Union (AU), to remind all African governments and people about the critical role that Science and Technology (ST) play in national development.
At the 46th ordinary session of the OAU, now the AU held in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia in 1987, a resolution was passed for all member-states to celebrate 30th June, every year as the Day of Scientific Renaissance of Africa. The event was in remembrance of the continent's great contribution to the rise and development of modern Science and Technology (ST).

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Inaugurates Millennium Devt Goals C'ttee
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Inaugurates Millennium Devt Goals C'ttee
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday inaugurated the Assessment and Monitoring Committee on the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) in Abuja, saying Nigeria is in the final stages of completing its 2005 report.
The MDGs is an eight-point agenda set by the United Nations (UN) as the basis for development especially in the developing countries of the South by 2015.

Original_Search_Title: Committee to Monitor MDGs Inaugurated
Headline: Nigeria: Committee to Monitor MDGs Inaugurated
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo on 29 June in Abuja inaugurated the Assessment and Monitoring Committee on the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).
The President reiterated the guiding philosophy behind the MDGs as one devoted to 'Spare no efforts to free our fellow men, women and children from the abject and dehumanising conditions of extreme poverty to which more than one billion of them are currently subjected'

Original_Search_Title: Rescue Mission Set for Bugesera
Headline: Rwanda: Rescue Mission Set for Bugesera
Body_Text:
Kigali — The provincial governor Epimaque Nsanzurwanda, has unveiled a new plan to set up food storage facilities in areas most affected by the persisting hunger in Bugesera district.
The governor announced this during the just-concluded agriculture expo at Mulindi city suburb, where he said the storage facilities for dry food will enable farmers to keep enough food during the dry season.

Original_Search_Title: Think Of Others !
Headline: Cameroon: Think Of Others !
Body_Text:
The inauguration, one year ago, of the pipeline terminal in Kribi threw a new focus on this extraordinary sea-side resort. Many visitors to the area at that time (and they were many and rich !) discovered the beautiful sea-fronts and outlying dense forest, which with a little effort, could be transformed into a tropical version of the French Côte d'Azur or of the American Hollywood.
Before the oil business came to throw Kribi into the limelight, the city was a placid setting in which farmers and fishermen went about their business unperturbed. The daily chores of local residents turned around coming up to the beach front to sell the day's fish catch to middle-men coming in from Douala or Yaounde. Farmers from the hinterland contented themselves with coming into town to sell their vegetables, plantains, cassava and hunted game.

Original_Search_Title: OPC Men Not Thugs - Coordinator
Headline: Nigeria: OPC Men Not Thugs - Coordinator
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Kwara State Coordinator of Oodua People's Congress (OPC), Comrade Bayo Ifabiyi, has decried the negative attributes of the members of the congress as thugs and miscreants, saying the group stands for the development and progress of its host communities.
Ifabiyi, who conducted newsmen round an eight-hectare of farmland of maize and cassava plantation around Asunnara and Yowere villages in Ilorin West and Asa Local Government areas of the state respectively, said members of the group meant well in any area they found themselves but the group has always been misrepresented.

Original_Search_Title: Churcher Urges Ecowas Ministers
Headline: West Africa: Churcher Urges Ecowas Ministers
Body_Text:
The ECOWAS Ministerial Conference on Biotechnology, has ended in Bamako, Mali, with a reminder to African countries that the decision to accept modern biotechnology and its potential role in enhancing food security on the continent, should be determined by weighing potential or perceived risks against potential benefits.
Ghana's Minister of Environment and Science, Christine Churcher, who made the reminder, noted that although the facts available suggest that the potential benefits of modern biotechnology are guided by good science, socio-economic and political considerations would determine its adoption or rejection.

Original_Search_Title: Famine Feared As Hailstones Hit Bafut
Headline: Cameroon: Famine Feared As Hailstones Hit Bafut
Body_Text:
More than 1000 families in some neighbourhoods in Bafut Subdivision, Mezam Division, are now living under the threats of famine following a severe storm and devastating hailstones that hit that village recently. The storm and hailstones lasted for more than two hours, The Post learnt.
It stripped trees of all leaves and fruits in neighbourhoods such as Njiniweh-Agyati, Swie and Nsoh, which are the hardest hit.

Original_Search_Title: NSE Lists 6bn Units of Akwa Ibom Bond
Headline: Nigeria: NSE Lists 6bn Units of Akwa Ibom Bond
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE) has at the weekend listed a total of six billion units of the First Akwa State Floating Rate Redeemable Bond 2005/2007 on its daily official list.
The listing brought to seven the number of outstanding under securities quoted in the State Government Bond subsector.

Original_Search_Title: Gates Foundation Funds Health Research Projects
Headline: Mozambique: Gates Foundation Funds Health Research Projects
Body_Text:
London — The Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation has announced that it is to spend
436 million US dollars on 43 groundbreaking research projects to discover
new ways to fight disease in the world's poorest countries.
The ultimate goal of the research is to develop health tools that are
not only effective, but also inexpensive to produce, easy to distribute, and
simple to use in developing countries.

Original_Search_Title: Government to Draft Law On Statistics
Headline: Kenya: Government to Draft Law On Statistics
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Government is to draft a law for regulating the way various departments compile Kenya's national statistics.
The draft bill will reflect ongoing reforms of the System of National Accounts spearheaded by the Ministry of Planning and National Development.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger Strikes Border Residents
Headline: Liberia: Hunger Strikes Border Residents
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Returning Liberian refugees say they need seed rice and some agricultural implements to plough the land and supplement their living. "I've been here for three months now, but cannot afford adequate meal for myself and my family," says James Zoedua, who returned from the refugee camp at Zimmi Bagboy, Sierra Leone, at Bo Waterside.
The gradual return of peace to Liberia has sparked the spontaneous homecoming of thousands of refugees and displaced people. But their return has bumped them into the problems of acute food shortages and chronic poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Up Against a Loaded Dice
Headline: Malawi: Up Against a Loaded Dice
Body_Text:
Can we not see our actions show we are politically inept?
I am a little confused with our parliament these days. I understand,
reporters saw in the august house parliamentarians, especially from
the opposition UDF and MCP ganging up to even suggest they can make a
legal judgement which agrees or disagrees with a speech the State
President has presented.

Original_Search_Title: Killer Meal: Health Workers Storm Markets
Headline: Nigeria: Killer Meal: Health Workers Storm Markets
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Worried by the rate at which amala meals, a local staple food prepared from either yam or cassava flakes have been claiming lives in Kwara State, government is to dispatch its health workers to some major markets to take samples of the products.
Commissioner for Health, Dr Bola Olaosebikan made the disclosure to newsmen in Ilorin andhinted that government would send part of the samples to NAFDAC and other relevant agencies for analysis.

Original_Search_Title: Genetically Modified Foods Not Good for Nigerians
Headline: Nigeria: Genetically Modified Foods Not Good for Nigerians
Body_Text:
Lagos — UK-based Nigeria economist and Labour expert and CEO, Linkpoint Employment Solutions Worldwide, Mr Herbert Ijeoma Odika in this interview proffers solutions to some of Nigeria's pressing economic problems. He also spoke on Nigeria's efforts to repartriate looted funds, debt relief and the current export of cassava products to Western nations.
It is a pleasure to comment on events concerning my country. Government needs people like us who have been outside the country and are exposed to other advanced economies to advise it on areas like agriculture which was predominant before the oil boom of the 70s. Before the oil boom Nigeria was generating foreign exchange from crops like cocoa, farming was a major part of productivity of this country. The oil boom saw agriculture decline over the years leaving every citizen to depend on annual budget based on oil sales and depending on day to day expenditure based on the parallel market. The federal government can mobilize and empower the local governments. It is easy to blame the federal government for everything. It cannot be watchdog for everything that happens in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Kaduna Govt Empowers Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Kaduna Govt Empowers Farmers
Body_Text:
Kaduna — Kaduna state government has distributed farming implements worth about
N12million to two Fadama community associations in Kubau local government
area of the state, as part of matching grants for the implementation of the
Fadama II project.
Making the presentations at a ceremony at Anchau, governor Ahmed
Mohammed Makarfi also handed out cheques worth a total of six million naira
to nine other Fadama community associations in the area, for the purchase of
productive assets, as approved by the World Bank.

Original_Search_Title: Abidjan Launches Food Fortifying Project
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Abidjan Launches Food Fortifying Project
Body_Text:
Rivonia — Côte d'Ivoire launches national food fortification project to combat vitamin and mineral deficiency, of at least 14 million people in Abidjan.
The project, announced by Côte d'Ivoire Minister of State, Minister of Health and Population Dr Albert Toikeusse Mabri, is supported by a US$3 million grant from the Global Alliance for Improved Nutrition (GAIN), contributions by the private sector and the Côte d'Ivoire's government. It will be implemented by Helen Keller International, Côte d'Ivoire.

Original_Search_Title: NSCIA Scribe Backs S-South On 25 Percent Derivation
Headline: Nigeria: NSCIA Scribe Backs S-South On 25 Percent Derivation
Body_Text:
Asaba — SECRETARY-GENERAL of the Nigerian Supreme Council For Islamic Affairs, Dr. Lateef Adegbite, yesterday, in Asaba backed calls by the South-South delegates to the national Political Reforms Conference for an increase in derivation from 13 percent to 25 per cent.
He spoke to reporters at the official opening of the 35th annual conference of the Nigerian Society of International Law, which kicked off in Asaba, yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: Letter From Lagos
Headline: Nigeria: Letter From Lagos
Body_Text:
Freetown — Who cares about the problem of Nigeria's
ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) or the potential successor to
Uncle Shege? Certainly not me! Frankly, I've almost spent the entire
part of my per diem buying Nigerian dailies since my arrival in
Lagos. And it looks like I'm returning home without been able to buy
jewelries for my fiancé as gifts from Lagos.
Interestingly, most of the stories I've read from these newspapers
are either centered on the debate over who the next Nigerian
president would be or the political intricacies surrounding the
current leadership problem within Obasanjo's PDP. To the Nigerians,
the ongoing crisis in the oil-rich Niger Delta Region and rampant
reports of routine and fearful armed robberies are no strange news.
These stories form part of the daily customary activities of life in
the country. And many of them are not even worried over such issues.

Original_Search_Title: Adegbite Opposes Absolute Resource Control
Headline: Nigeria: Adegbite Opposes Absolute Resource Control
Body_Text:
Asaba — Secretary General of Supreme Council of Islam in Nigeria, Alh. Lateef Adegbite has kicked against the agitation for absolute resource control, but rather called for the return to the derivation principle inherited at independence.
He described as "unfortunate" the impasse at the reform conference over the issue of derivation principle, which led to the staging of a walk-out at the conference by South-south delegates.

Original_Search_Title: Africa Must Embrace Biotechnology
Headline: Ghana: Africa Must Embrace Biotechnology
Body_Text:
A number of journalists from Anglophone West African countries including Ghana, Nigeria, Sierra Leone and The Gambia, have had frank discussions with African scientists on biotechnology and related issues.
This was at a two-day workshop in Accra from June 16-17, at the end of which, they agreed that there is the urgent need for African scientists to employ biotechnology, to help address food security and health matters on the continent.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Diversification, Solution to Malawi's Agricultural Woes
Headline: Malawi: Crop Diversification, Solution to Malawi's Agricultural Woes
Body_Text:
A Spatial Development Initiatives (SDI's) report on the Tara
Development Corridor by United States Agency for International
Development (USAID) has identified numerous crops in the country that
have export potential and act as an alternative to tobacco, tea and
sugar.
The identified crops are cassava, cut flowers, pigeon peas,
groundnuts, cotton, Irish potatoes; birds eye chillies, macadamia
nuts, paprika, ginger, turmeric and annatto.

Original_Search_Title: Gulu Farmers to Get Seeds
Headline: Uganda: Gulu Farmers to Get Seeds
Body_Text:
Kampala — GULU district agricultural department has procured seeds to be
distributed to farmers in 11 sub-counties under the seeds multiplication
project.
The district agricultural officer, Remmy Ochaka, on Monday said the
seeds, which were procured through the district tender board, included
8,235kg of K1 32 beans and 8,800 bags of cassava cuttings.

Original_Search_Title: Elephants Wreak Havoc in Rutshuru
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: DRC: Elephants Wreak Havoc in Rutshuru
Body_Text:
Goma — Under the scorching afternoon sun in Rutshuru Territory in the
Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), an excited crowd of several hundred
people, armed with machetes and knives, mills around the carcasses of two
elephants stretched out on the ground.
No sooner had the soldiers shot dead the mammals than local residents
gathered round, each impatiently waiting to get a piece of meat.

Original_Search_Title: Political Economy of Sustainable Democracy in Nigeria (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Political Economy of Sustainable Democracy in Nigeria (2)
Body_Text:
Lagos — The value system got distorted; government grew unsustainably as the major source of productive employment and the citizens expected government as the 'giver' to provide everything FREE for them. It did not matter if we didn't pay our taxes. Since government revenues came mostly from 'Federation Account' sharing, the generality of citizens did not see the direct link between their taxes and government spending, and there was also little pressure to demand for accountability. The umbilical cord that ties the government and private sector in most economies also got broken: Governments everywhere in the world are generally under pressure to provide an enabling environment for businesses to thrive because businesses in turn provide employment and generate revenue to government. Because of oil, Governments in Nigeria did not need the private sector for revenue, and because of Government's expansive nature, it depended very little on the private sector for job creation. Beyond rhetoric therefore, there was indeed little incentive to urgently provide an enabling environment for private sector to thrive.
Let me admit that the above characterization was not peculiar to the Nigerian state. Indeed, most post-colonial African states mimicked variants of the above features. Adebayo Olukoshi ("The Elusive Prince of Denmark: Structural Adjustment and the Crisis of Governance in Africa", 1996: 8) graphically summarizes as follows: the post-colonial African state, by its very nature and, therefore, by definition, is at the heart of the economic governance crisis pervading the continent. This state, stripped of the most basic checks and balances of the (late) colonial period, has failed woefully in its developmental mission on account of various inter-related factors: its 'excessive' and 'counterproductive' intervention in domestic economic processes to the detriment of market forces and the private sector; its over-bureaucratization and bloated size; the domination of its apparatuses by clientelist networks and an 'urban coalition' that orients it against the rural (productive) sector and 'rational' macro-economic policies; its submission to 'rampant/macro populism' as it panders to a vociferous 'urban coalition'; its monopolization of the main economic levers in society with the resultant proliferation of rent-generating/ -seeking niches/ activities; and its over-centralization of development which has discouraged local (private) initiative.

Original_Search_Title: UNHCR Programmes Take Aid to Western Province
Headline: Zambia: UNHCR Programmes Taking Development to Refugess' Host Families in Western Province
Body_Text:
ONLY recently Brenda (not real name) 18, was happily married and looking forward to a happy future.
But something that completely changed her life happened; militia men invaded the village in which she lived, shot all the men including her husband before they set all the houses in the village on fire.

Original_Search_Title: Bishop Chisha Calls for Investment in Rural Areas
Headline: Zambia: Bishop Chisha Calls for Investment in Rural Areas
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THERE can be no economic development without investment in rural areas, Luapula Province Catholic Bishop Aaron Chisha has said.
In an interview, Bishop Chisha said it was disappointing that the government had continued to make promises that it would develop rural areas yet nothing was being done.

Original_Search_Title: Travelogue: Letter From Lagos
Headline: Nigeria: Travelogue: Letter From Lagos
Body_Text:
Freetown — Who cares about the problem of Nigeria's ruling Peoples Democratic Party
(PDP) or the potential successor to Uncle Shege?
Certainly not me! Frankly, I've almost spent the entire part of my per
diem buying Nigerian dailies since my arrival in Lagos. And it looks like
I'm returning home without been able to buy jewelries for my fiancé as gifts
from Lagos. Interestingly, most of the stories I've read from these
newspapers are either centered on the debate over who the next Nigerian
president would be or the political intricacies surrounding the current
leadership problem within Obasanjo's PDP. To the Nigerians, the ongoing
crisis in the oil-rich Niger Delta Region and rampant reports of routine and
fearful armed robberies are no strange news. These stories form part of the
daily customary activities of life in the country. And many of them are not
even worried over such issues.

Original_Search_Title: That CBN's Debt Write-Off
Headline: Nigeria: That CBN's Debt Write-Off
Body_Text:
Lagos — The sing-song since the inception of the current political dispensation
that seems more capitalist than the worst type of capitalism, in terms of
human degradation, is the emphasis on market forces and impliedly the
natural selection theory.
People are continuously harassed of being unproductive hence talks of
rightsizing or retrenchment as if Nigeria belongs to only a few. And as
established, the World Bank puts it that 99% of Nigerians share 20% of the
resources of the country while 1% appropriate 80% of it. What a shame!

Original_Search_Title: 'NDDC Cassava Project'll Create 400 Jobs'
Headline: Nigeria: 'NDDC Cassava Project'll Create 400 Jobs'
Body_Text:
Lagos — No less than 400 jobs will be created by the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) cassava production programme at Nsie/Ikono, Offi, Okobo, in Akwa Ibom State.
Ambassador Sam Edem, Chairman of NDDC board, who stated this at the inauguration ceremony weekend, said the presence of NDDC would be immediately felt by Okobo youths.

Original_Search_Title: Masebo Advises Supermarkets to Work Closely With Rural Farmers
Headline: Zambia: Masebo Advises Supermarkets to Work Closely With Rural Farmers
Body_Text:
Lusaka — PROMINENT supermarkets should work hand in hand with rural farmers to market their dry traditional vegetables, local government and housing minister Sylvia Masebo has said.
And Masebo has warned Chongwe commercial farmers to desist from damming and blocking water to small-scale farmers.

Original_Search_Title: VP Advises On Income
Headline: Uganda: VP Advises On Income
Body_Text:
Kampala — Through diversified production, it is possible for a household to earn sh20m per year, the Vice-President, Prof. Gilbert Bukenya, has said.
Bukenya was meeting over 200 members of Busiro North development Foundation (BNDF) at his Kakiri home in Wakiso district.

Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Completes 814 Projects
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) has executed 814 projects since December 2000.
These projects were part of the 1300 projects inherited from the defunct Oil Minerals and Producing Areas Development Commission (OMPADEC). They include:

Original_Search_Title: Kenyan Scientists Reveal New Pest-Resistant GMO Maize
Headline: Kenya: New 'Wonder' Maize Coming
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyan scientists have announced what they describe as a major
breakthrough in the search for pest-resistant maize variety.
The Kenya Agricultural Research Institute (Kari) is to release into the
market the generically modified maize, which is resistant to the stem-borer
- the cause of huge losses to farmers.

Original_Search_Title: NDDC Executes 814 Projects
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Executes 814 Projects
Body_Text:
About 814 projects have been carried out by the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC), since December 2000.
The projects which form part of the 1,300 inherited ones from the defunct OMPADEC include road maintenance and constr-uction, building of classr-oom, hospitals, jetties, provision of electricity and water etc.

Original_Search_Title: Cabinda : Over 200 Exhibitors Attend Farming, Cattle Fair
Headline: Angola: Cabinda : Over 200 Exhibitors Attend Farming, Cattle Fair
Body_Text:
Cabinda — A total of 220 exhibitors are attending the fourth edition of the farming and cattle breeding fair in Angola's northern Cabinda province, that opened Wednesday at the Alto das Rolas market, here.
Thirty tons of farming and cattle products are on display at the fair, with highlight to banana, cassava, potatoes, peanuts, beans, vegetables and lives-stock.

Original_Search_Title: Broken Bridge Annoys Cité Berge Inhabitants
Headline: Cameroon: Broken Bridge Annoys Cité Berge Inhabitants
Body_Text:
The bridge linking this residential area to Bobongo and Petit Parisien has collapsed.
The bridge linking Cité Berge to other residential areas at the village neighborhood has given way leaving the population in the area desperate. The bridge collapsed on Friday night after a heavy down pour that caused floods. The metallic culvert put in place by inhabitants of the area 15 years ago has served as main access road between residents of Cité Berge, Bobongo and Petit Parisien. Due to its constant use and degradation, the local authorities in the area drew the attention of the Douala Urban Council to it. But the council despite promises before, during and after election campaigns failed to remedy the situation. Last Friday it collapsed. The 120m diameter culvert could not withstand the flood current of water from Bilongo passing through "Lax Loud" to Cité Berge. There was stampede among the inhabitants who only discovered the damage in the early hours of the morning.

Original_Search_Title: Impoverished Families Trade Their Children
Headline: West Africa: Impoverished Families Trade Their Children
Body_Text:
Cotonou — Thousands of children are taken each year from the poorest regions of
West Africa and trafficked abroad for profit. Often, their own families are
behind the trade.
It was just after his father died that 10-year-old Dieudonne was put to
work farming in his hometown of Zakpota, Benin. But after barely a week, his
uncle put him in a car and took him to work at a quarry over the border in
Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Exhibit Better Value in Jinja
Headline: Uganda: Farmers Exhibit Better Value in Jinja
Body_Text:
Kampala — A human size bunch of matooke, a 5kg potato or a 7kg pineapple are no longer wonders.
Farmers at the annual Farmers Show have been exhibiting this for so many years.

Original_Search_Title: Bees: a Free Resource
Headline: Uganda: Bees: a Free Resource
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA is endowed with over 20,000 unexploited bee species. The bees pollinate 90% of the vegetation. Bees pollinate flowers as they collect pollen and nectar. In the process, they conserve the plant.
'Traditional bee-keeping (apiculture) has been practiced since time immemorial for the production of honey, wax and propolis, but it has not been documented," says Ramsey Owot, president, Uganda Honey Bee-Keepers Association (UHA).

Original_Search_Title: Cashew Farmers Get ¢3.2bn Boost
Headline: Ghana: Cashew Farmers Get ¢3.2bn Boost
Body_Text:
Berekum —  ... Thanks to ADB
As part of their aim and objective to make the economy grow in the
golden age of business, some financial institutions in the country have
taken it upon themselves to target some farmer groups under their
micro-finance scheme.

Original_Search_Title: Kyenjojo Abducters Were Thugs Not Rebels, Says Bantariza
Headline: Uganda: Kyenjojo Abducters Were Thugs Not Rebels, Says Bantariza
Body_Text:
Fort Portal — The gunmen who abducted people in Kyenjojo district on Sunday were thugs
and not rebels, the UPDF spokesman, Lt Col. Shaban Bantariza, has said. "We
have established that they were thugs who abducted the locals as punishment
for not having money on them," Bantariza said yesterday.
Security sources in Kyenjojo said all the people who were abducted
earlier were released towards daybreak. They had been abducted by two gunmen
wielding a sub-machine gun, a panga and axes.

Original_Search_Title: Peaceful Run-Up to Presidential Election After Shaky Start to the Campaign
Headline: Guinea Bissau: Guinea-Bissau: Peaceful Run-Up to Presidential Election After Shaky Start to the Campaign
Body_Text:
Bissau — Three main candidates are vying for power in Sunday's presidential
election which aims to return Guinea-Bissau to constitutional rule and
stability following a civil war and six years of political turmoil.
Diplomats and local political analysts are expecting a three-cornered
fight between former presidents Joao Bernardo "Nino" Vieira and Kumba Yala
and Malam Bacai Sanha, the official candidate of the ruling African Party
for the Independence of Guinea-Bissau and Cape Verde (PAIGC).

Original_Search_Title: Govt Acts to Avert Hunger in Chama
Headline: Zambia: Govt Acts to Avert Hunger in Chama
Body_Text:
Kitwe — THE Disaster Management and Mitigation Unit in the Office of the
Vice-President has dispatched 691 metric tonnes of white maize to Chama to
avert an impending hunger crisis in both Chama North and South.
In a telephone interview on Sunday, Chama district commissioner Peter
Nyirenda said the government was seriously addressing the reported hunger in
Chama North and that the maize would be distributed to seriously affected
areas within the district.

Original_Search_Title: Textile Firms Get the Nod On Minimum Wages
Headline: Kenya: Textile Firms Get the Nod On Minimum Wages
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Textile manufactures in the Export Processing Zones saw two of their demands met by Finance minister David Mwiraria.
They had wanted the Government to cut the cost of doing business by reducing the minimum wage, which forms the largest component in apparel production industry.

Original_Search_Title: IITA, NABDA Demystify Biotechnology
Headline: Nigeria: IITA, NABDA Demystify Biotechnology
Body_Text:
Lagos — As part of efforts to enlighten people on the positive application of biotechnology tools for agricultural development in Nigeria a one-day awareness workshop on "Biotechnology today and tomorrow" was recently organized for top civil servants of Bauchi State. It was sponsored by the National Biotechnology Development Agency (NABDA), and the Total Development International Foundation (TODEV) a non-governmental organization (NGO), with support from the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA).
In his opening speech, Dr. Wale Adekunle, Information and Communication Support Coordinator who spoke on behalf of IITA DG Haratmann said the workshop was organized to demystify biotechnology and enlighten participants on its positive attributes in the service of mankind. It was also aimed at assisting the participants to know more about biotechnology. "Top civil servants among the participants will become well equipped to take informed decisions on matters affecting biotechnology henceforth", said Dr. Adekunle.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Mosaic Hits Kasese
Headline: Uganda: Cassava Mosaic Hits Kasese
Body_Text:
Kampala — A suspected African Cassava Mosaic Virus has attacked eastern Bukonzo in Kasese district, destroying vast cassava gardens in the area.
A district agricultural officer, Charles Muhindo, confirmed the outbreak of the disease, describing it as a big threat to the fight against hunger.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Gives Cassava to Families
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Gives Cassava to Families
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni recently gave high-yield cassava and potato stem cuttings to over 1,000 families in Iganga district as part of his poverty alleviation initiative.
Salim Saleh Akulembera, an aide-de-camp to the presidential adviser on poverty alleviation, Joan Kakwenzire, handed over the consignment to the beneficiaries at Zirabamuzaale Primary School.

Original_Search_Title: Angolan Producers Eye Namibian Market
Headline: Namibia: Angolan Producers Eye Namibian Market
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The Namibian Chamber of Commerce and Industry (NCCI) says that it
received information from the Namibian embassy in Angola that the
administrator of Lubango is looking for a market in Namibia for Angolan
producers of fruit and vegetables.
The announcement appears to have confirmed the report by the Southern
African Development Community (SADC)'s, Food Agriculture and Natural
Resources (FANR) update for April 2005, which painted a promising picture
for the future of Angola's agricultural potential for the year.

Original_Search_Title: NPP Footsoldiers Grumble
Headline: Ghana: NPP Footsoldiers Grumble
Body_Text:
Koforidua — The Chronicle can report that most of the footsoldiers who went all out and fought for the victory of the New Patriotic Party (NPP) in the 2000 and 2004 presidential and Parliamentary elections in the Eastern Region, are grumbling seriously.
According to them, they have been left to their fate, without being resourced as they were promised by the party bosses before the elections.

Original_Search_Title: Group Clamours for Aba State
Headline: Nigeria: Group Clamours for Aba State
Body_Text:
Lagos — A group, Nzuko Isiala-Ngwa Socio-political Group has commended delegates
to the National Political Reform Conference (NPRC), convened by the Federal
Government, maintaining that its aim is to articulate a new socio-political
order which will enhance and sustain the continued co-existence of the
various ethno-religious groups.
In a statement jointly signed by some members of the group, namely,
Barr. Sam Erugo, Mr. Uzoma Ogbanna, Mr. Alozie Jimonu, Dr. Oluchi Oguma and
Arch. Onuoha, it noted that NPRC will provide an opportunity for various
groups to come together to dialogue in order to find solution to the decades
of subjugation, marginalisation and outright ill-treatment of some groups in
the country.

Original_Search_Title: Construction of Akosombo Dam Has Brought Hardships - MP
Headline: Ghana: Construction of Akosombo Dam Has Brought Hardships - MP
Body_Text:
The Member of Parliament for Shai Osudoku constituency, David Tetteh,
has indicated that the construction of the Akosombo and Kpong hydro-electric
dams have brought great economic and social hardship to the people along the
lower Volta basin.
He noted that prior to the construction of these two national assets,
life in Osudoku and the other areas was meaningful due to the economic
activities of the people.

Original_Search_Title: Starch Production : A New Source of Income
Headline: Cameroon: Starch Production : A New Source of Income
Body_Text:
After a three-day meet the people tour of the Minister of Agriculture
and Rural Development Tchatat Clobert in the Littoral Province, hear comes
another historic meeting between sellers and buyers of starch made from
cassava.
The doors of the meeting which is currently going on in the economic
capital were opened yesterday by the Secretary General of the Littoral
Province Mr. Ngalim Irénè. Mr. Irénè who was representing the Governor
revealed that, the lack of market information, low prices, quality and
quantity are the main hindrance for the development of a veritable starch
industry in Cameroon. He said, over 138 307 hectares of land is being
cultivated by 605 492 Cameroonians for the production of cassava. While the
annual average purchase of root crops and tubers in our homes is estimated
at CFA 37 billion frs. In order to step up production and sales, he
encouraged organisers and participants to put forward constrictive
ideas.

Original_Search_Title: Drought, HIV/Aids Weak Economy Undermine Food Security
Headline: Malawi: Drought, HIV/Aids Weak Economy Undermine Food Security
Body_Text:
Blantyre — The impacts of drought, HIV/AIDS and a weak economy have combined to
undermine already vulnerable households in Malawi's rural areas.
James Morris, the UN Secretary-General's Special Envoy for Humanitarian
Needs in Southern Africa, highlighted this 'triple threat' on a recent visit
to the region that included a stop in Malawi, where he called for a renewed
international response to the crisis.

Original_Search_Title: Researchers Too Are Heroes, Great Heroes
Headline: Uganda: Researchers Too Are Heroes, Great Heroes
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Government dedicated June 9 every year to Uganda's heroes as a
gesture of appreciation and re-commitment to the cause for which those
people excelled for the good of humanity. The bulk of the heroes are former
politicians or at least had strong linkages with politics.
Dr Otim Nape, the director general of National Agricultural Research
Organisation (NARO) says it is time scientists were recognised for the
coveted status.

Original_Search_Title: Promoting Economic Growth and Household Incomes in Financial Year 2005/06
Headline: Uganda: Financial Year 2005/06
Body_Text:
Kampala —  Theme: Promoting Economic Growth and Household Incomes through increased
Savings, Investment, Employment, and Productivity.

Original_Search_Title: Zones Should Rally for Common Interest - Saraki
Headline: Nigeria: Zones Should Rally for Common Interest - Saraki
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Kwara State Governor, Dr. Bukola Saraki recently fielded questions from
journalists on several issues including the 2007 presidency, discipline
within the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), agricultural policy of his
administration and the alliance between the Middle Belt and the South-south
geo-political zones. Lanre Isa-Onilu was there
Ordinarily, Kwara cannot be seen as strategic in terms of resources and
all that, but a lot has been done within a short time. What's the secret?

Body_Text:

Honourable Members of Parliament,

Original_Search_Title: Ending Perennial Hunger Once And for All
Headline: Malawi: Ending Perennial Hunger Once And for All
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Out of eleven Million Malawians, two million will need food aid this
year of their lives which could be lost due to the impending drought
has to be saved.
Not that the drought can be attributed to crop failure this year, but
it is a situation that in the world of accounts is called 'balanced
brought forward' During the first republican rule, there was a time
when Malawi managed to extensively expand in commercial agriculture
which led to a doubling of production of export crops and placed
Malawi among the few countries of the sub-Saharan Africa where food
production kept pace with population growth.

Original_Search_Title: Mu'azu Urges Back to Land
Headline: Nigeria: Mu'azu Urges Back to Land
Body_Text:
Lagos — Governor of Bauchi State, Ahmadu Adamu Mu'azu has enjoined his people to actively participate in farming so as to boost food production stressing that the agricultural sector remained the mainstay of the state's economy.
Governor Mu'azu made the call yesterday at the launching of this year's farming season held at Yana, Shira Local Government Area and reiterated the determination of his administration to continue to give adequate attention to all aspects of agriculture in order to achieve the desired objective.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Tchatat Visits Coastal Farms
Headline: Cameroon: Minister Tchatat Visits Coastal Farms
Body_Text:
The agriculture boss last Saturday rounded up a meet the farmers' tour in the Littoral Province.
The Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development, Clobert Tchatat has called on farmers in the Littoral Province to team-up so that government can easily help them. The Minister was speaking during a three-day meet the farmers tour that saw him make twenty-two stops. During the first stops that began in Pouma and Edea on Thursday, the minister accompanied by the Governor of the Littoral Province Gounoko Haounaye, visited common initiative groups and individuals involved in the transformation of cassava into white and yellow gari, fufu and starch, palm nurseries and plantations for the production of red oil, coffee, poultries, cattle and pig rearing. In most of these farms, the farmers said there are eager to enhance their activities, but were handicapped by the lack of equipment; low prices and at times inability sell their products. One of the farmers cited an example where he had to abandon over 400 bunches of palm nuts because there was no person or transformation company to buy. In responds to these complains put forward by the vibrant Sanaga-Maritime farmers, Minister Tchatat called on them to double their efforts. He said, the nations counts very much on agriculture that is why a road map has been put in place following the prescription of the Head of State. He announced the imminent launching of mechanised programme during which government will ease the acquisition of farm tools by farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Africa Has Potential to Feed Itself - Ezeh
Headline: Southern Africa: Africa Has Potential to Feed Itself - Ezeh
Body_Text:
Lusaka — AFRICA should be innovative in food production to avert food shortages, Nigerian High Commissioner to Zambia Emmanuel Ezeh has said.
In an interview, High Commissioner Ezeh said Africa had the potential to feed itself without assistance from donors.

Original_Search_Title: High Commodities Supply Cuts Down Prices
Headline: Uganda: High Supply Cuts Down Prices
Body_Text:
Kampala — FARMERS should get ready to pocket less as the prices of almost all the
commodities are set to fall, a report from the Uganda markets, said.
The fall in prices is attributed to the high first season crop harvest,
which continues to get to the market from the different production areas in
high quantities.

Original_Search_Title: Biotechnology: Effort to Develop Local Capacity Yielding Results
Headline: Nigeria: Biotechnology: Effort to Develop Local Capacity Yielding Results
Body_Text:
Lagos — As effort to develop regulatory framework for Biotechnology products in African nations increases, the outreach coordinator of the National Agriculture and Biotechnology Project (NABP) Dr. Wale Adekunle has stated that Nigeria's Local Capacity in Biotechnology Development is progressing impressively.
Adekunle who spoke with THISDAY After the Biotechnology awareness workshop titled Biotechnology:Today and Tomorrow' Organised by NABP said that they have been directing effort at building local capacity in the Nation Agricultural Research Institutions.

Original_Search_Title: Bukenya Must Be a Good Doctor, Just See How Many Pigs He's Got!
Headline: Uganda: Bukenya Must Be a Good Doctor, Just See How Many Pigs He's Got!
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Uganda's First Vice President, Prof Gilbert Bukenya, is an unhappy man.
In a revealing interview with The Monitor newspaper, the VP disclosed that some unkind people have been taking photographs of his pigs, paw paws and mangoes.

Original_Search_Title: Government Unveils Drought Statistics
Headline: Mozambique: Government Unveils Drought Statistics
Body_Text:
Maputo — Over a quarter of a million households have been affected by drought in
southern and central Mozambique this year, according to the latest
statistics from the government.
Foreign Minister Alcinda Abreu chaired a meeting with representatives of
the donor community on Monday at which the statistics were presented.

Original_Search_Title: President Periscopes the Next Two Years
Headline: Nigeria: President Periscopes the Next Two Years
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo on 5 June revealed the three most significant expectations he looks ahead to from the numerous reforms put in place by his administration.
Answering questions during a nationwide phone-in television programme "The President Speaks", President Obasanjo said he expects self-sufficiency in rice and cassava production, improvement in the energy sector, attitudinal reorientation, economic revival and improvement in the well-being of Nigerians, increased investment flow especially in agriculture, social services, tourism and solid minerals.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Reviews Six Years of Democratic Rule
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Reviews Six Years of Democratic Rule
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo on 5 June reviewed the democratic experience in the last six years and passed a verdict of above average performance.
At a Media Chat on nationwide television "The President Speaks", President Obasanjo said "so far so good".

Original_Search_Title: Zanzibar: the Place to Be, a Magnet Destination
Headline: Tanzania: Zanzibar: the Place to Be, a Magnet Destination
Body_Text:
Arusha — The sun was starting to set and the Stone Town was closing up and losing its life as I stepped off the ferry after the tiresome of travelling all day. Hidden mosques called people to prayer, and shops were dragging their coloured goods behind wooden shutters.
The Stone Town, in the heart of Zanzibar is the original city and a focus for any visit in the exotic, tropical, sun-blessed and coral fringed archipelago. Arab traders built their architectural masterpieces here as they set up an outpost for trading of spices, ivory and slaves to the east.

Original_Search_Title: Promoting Agricultural Production in the New Liberia
Headline: Liberia: Promoting Agricultural Production in the New Liberia
Body_Text:
Dr. Syrulwa Somah believes that any nation that cannot feed its people
but relies on other nations for basic food supplies has no national pride,
and it is now time for Liberia to restore its national pride through
agricultural production.
Liberia has abundant fertile soil for the growing of bananas, rice,
plantain, bitter ball, cassava, Malaguatta pepper, mushroom, coffee, kola,
cocoa, mango, okra, palm nuts, papaya, pepper, yams, eddoes, sweet potatoes,
greens, and other basic agricultural products consumed in Liberian homes,
cities, towns, and villages everyday. But it is sad that successive
governments in our nation saw the massive importation of rice and other
basic commodities as more than a national effort aimed at increased
agricultural production in Liberia. Previous agricultural production schemes
such as the Green Revolution Program under President Doe and the Green
Revolution Program under President Tolbert's five to ten years food
self-sufficiency plan yielded limited or no tangible results in easing
Liberia's dependency on foreign nations for the supply of rice, the national
staple. Nevertheless, I believe that any nation that cannot feed its people
but relies on other nations for basic food supplies but has no national
pride, and it is now time for Liberia to restore its national pride through
agricultural production. I believe that agricultural production is the
lifeline Liberia and every national leader in the new Liberia would do well
to make agricultural production a national priority.

Original_Search_Title: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: A Story of Biblical Parallels
Headline: Liberia: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: A Story of Biblical Parallels
Body_Text:
Liberian with disability says his greatest desire is to see people with
disabilities given the same opportunities he had. Doctor has written a
position paper to Liberia's legislative body, appealing for rights to jobs
and other possibilities for persons with disabilities.
By the age of 16, Dr. Saqui Malakpai had completely lost his sight. He
dropped out of 10th grade.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo's Vietnam Visit: Wrong Signal on Nigeria's Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo's Vietnam Visit: Wrong Signal on Nigeria's Agriculture
Body_Text:
As a result of the recent visit of President Obasanjo to Vietnam,
several dailies gave the visit a good but disgraceful coverage especially
about the status of our stagnant agricultural growth. Like president Shehu
Shagari before him, the usual impression is that after 50 years of
pre-independence and 40 years post independence, agricultural research for
development is yet to make a notable change in our development effort.
So we start wanting European and Eastern countries of the world to come
to our aid, because "agriculture is the "heart of our lives" (Obasanjo)"
Daily Trust P. 26 of 18th April, 2005. "We want to learn from everywhere in
the world how to improve the quality of agriculture" and "that Nigeria could
learn from Vietnam" especially to learn from their experience in the
production of rice, soya-bean, cassava etc and livestock. Our Minister of
State for Agriculture (who knows hardly anything about agricultural research
and past achievement still waiting to be utilized in Nigerian agricultural
institutions) has to remain behind in Vietnam "to meet the relevant
institutions involved in crops and livestock production in Vietnam".

Original_Search_Title: Southern Africa Must Move On From Staple Maize Crop
Headline: Southern Africa Must Move On From Staple Maize Crop
Body_Text:
Port Louis — Southern African farmers must diversify away from their staple maize crop to more nutritious and drought resistant plants if they are to avoid further food shortages and malnutrition crises, aid workers say.
Maize has been the mainstay of the southern African diet for generations, but a series of crop failures has left millions across the region facing shortages in the last decade as farmers battle drought and the death of workers from the HIV pandemic. "Southern Africa needs to diversify," James Morris, head of the United Nations World Food Programme (WFP), told Reuters at a food aid distribution point in southern Malawi. "But it's not easy. People have been eating maize a long time."

Original_Search_Title: Benue Establishes N306m Mill
Headline: Nigeria: Benue Establishes N306m Mill
Body_Text:
Makurdi — The Benue State government has said it plans to take full advantage of the federal government ban on importation of some agricultural and other processed products, with the establishment of three cassava flour mills valued at N306 million, in the state.
The State Commissioner in the ministry of Commerce and Industries, Barrister Joe. Abaagu, who made this known, also told Thisday, that two Juice factories with a combined total production capacity of 45,560 metric tonnes would also be commissioned very soon.

Original_Search_Title: Makota Disputes 85,000 Metric Tonne Maize Deficit Claim
Headline: Zambia: Makota Disputes 85,000 Metric Tonne Maize Deficit Claim
