Body_Text:
Lusaka — ZAMBIAN Women in Agriculture coordinator Cecilia Makota has disputed the reported 85,000 metric tonne maize deficit.
Commenting on agriculture minister Mundia Sikatana's announcement on Wednesday that the country's maize deficit had narrowed to 85,000 metric tonnes, Makota said reports of widespread hunger cast doubt on the announcement.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Spends N600m On Food Production
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Spends N600m On Food Production
Body_Text:
Owerri — Imo State Government n has spent the sum of N600 million on food production and the development of agriculture in the state.
Commissioner of Agricu-lture, Barrister Soronadi Njoku, said this yeserday while briefing reporters in Owerri .

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Mealy Bug Hits Lango
Headline: Uganda: Cassava Mealy Bug Hits Lango
Body_Text:
Kampala — MEALY bugs have attacked more than 1,000 acres of cassava plants in
Lango subregion in the last one month.
Apac district assistant agricultural officer, Ben Emer, said mealy bugs
(phenacoccus manihotis) invaded the cassava plants in early April.

Original_Search_Title: New Research Questions Link Between Food Crisis And Aids
Headline: Southern Africa: New Research Questions Link Between Food Crisis And Aids
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — The link between HIV/AIDS and hunger in rural communities has received a great deal of attention in Southern Africa, where HIV/AIDS seems to have added a new dimension to the region's four-year-long food crisis.
But a new report has argued that although HIV/AIDS constitutes a humanitarian catastrophe, the impact of the epidemic was not a major cause of the region's food crisis during 2001 and 2004.

Original_Search_Title: Southern Africa Must Move On from Staple Maize Crop
Headline: Southern Africa Must Move On from Staple Maize Crop
Body_Text:
Port Louis — Southern African farmers must diversify away from their staple maize crop to more nutritious and drought resistant plants if they are to avoid further food shortages and malnutrition crises, aid workers say.
Maize has been the mainstay of the southern African diet for generations, but a series of crop failures has left millions across the region facing shortages in the last decade as farmers battle drought and the death of workers from the HIV pandemic. "Southern Africa needs to diversify," James Morris, head of the United Nations World Food Programme (WFP), told Reuters at a food aid distribution point in southern Malawi. "But it's not easy. People have been eating maize a long time."

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo's Vietnam Visit: Wrong Signal on Nigeria's Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo's Vietnam Visit: Wrong Signal on Nigeria's Agriculture
Body_Text:
As a result of the recent visit of President Obasanjo to Vietnam,
several dailies gave the visit a good but disgraceful coverage especially
about the status of our stagnant agricultural growth.
Like president Shehu Shagari before him, the usual impression is that
after 50 years of pre-independence and 40 years post independence,
agricultural research for development is yet to make a notable change in our
development effort. So we start wanting European and Eastern countries of
the world to come to our aid, because "agriculture is the "heart of our
lives" (Obasanjo)" Daily Trust P. 26 of 18th April, 2005. "We want to learn
from everywhere in the world how to improve the quality of agriculture" and
"that Nigeria could learn from Vietnam" especially to learn from their
experience in the production of rice, soya-bean, cassava etc and
livestock. Our Minister of State for Agriculture (who knows hardly anything
about agricultural research and past achievement still waiting to be
utilized in Nigerian agricultural institutions) has to remain behind in
Vietnam "to meet the relevant institutions involved in crops and livestock
production in Vietnam". In 1981 President Shehu Shagari paid a state visit
to England and on top of his agenda was a visit to the Animal Breeding
Centre in Reading to see how Artificial Insemination (AI), a technique to
increase productivity of cattle, worked. There was elaborate
demonstration. I was on Interuniversity fellowship in Reading University
then, and I was ashamed of myself because I had been Director of Animal
Research Institute, Zaria for at least five years at the time and A1
services was so routine that my president only needed to go to Zaria to see
it, learn more, and direct its wide application rather than to see wonders
in UK!! These uterances and actions of our leaders are not only misleading
but a shame to our country as they make us look little after nearly 90 years
of agricultural research for development. They forget or are ignorant that
Nigeria scientists have accumulated enormous research findings in all
aspects of agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: Imo Donated N100m Loan, 35,000 Bags of Fertilizer to Farmers Last Season
Headline: Nigeria: Imo Donated N100m Loan, 35,000 Bags of Fertilizer to Farmers Last Season
Body_Text:
Owerri — IMO State government gave out N100 million as loan to farmers during last year's cropping season, with more than 35,000 bags of fertilizer sold at subsidized price.
Governor Achike Udenwa stated this in a radio and television broadcast to Imo people, in commemoration of the second anniversary of his second tenure in Government House, Owerri."

Original_Search_Title: A Bishop's Panacea to Corruption
Headline: Nigeria: A Bishop's Panacea to Corruption
Body_Text:
Lagos — In the years gone by, when prophets of God spoke, their words were
final. You would ignore them at your own peril. Once they said: "so says the
Lord of Hosts", it was mandatory that their words be respected. King
Pharaoh, that stubborn Egyptian monarch who failed to hearken to God's
warning to let the Israelites go, despite the many visits paid to his palace
by Moses to tell him to free them and let them be, in accordance with God's
injunction, brought a lot of calamities upon his people before ending up in
the Red Sea with his army and chariots. Such was the awe that these prophets
of god were known for then.
There are still such prophets these days, although it is also true that
there are more fakes than genuine ones among them. But this is as old as we
can remember. In those days too, there were fake prophets, some of who also
performed 'miracles'. We remember that some of these people who tried to
compete with Moses (whom the Scripture aptly refers to as (Egyptian
magicians) when they used their evil powers to replicate almost every wonder
that Moses performed. They did that successfully until God proved Himself as
the ultimate God before whom there was no god and after whom none shall
spring up.

Original_Search_Title: I've Done Well - Udenwa
Headline: Nigeria: I've Done Well - Udenwa
Body_Text:
Owerri — As Nigerians mark this year's Democracy Day, Governor Achike Udenwa of lmo State has reviewed the achievements of his administration in the last two years of his second term, and came to the conclusion that he has done well.
ln a State-wide Broadcast today to mark the Democracy Day celebration, Governor Udenwa said though he has done well in the past years he however noted that there were still areas that needed to be improved upon.

Original_Search_Title: FRA Pledges to Buy All Maize, Cassava Countrywide
Headline: Zambia: FRA Pledges to Buy All Maize, Cassava Countrywide
Body_Text:
THE Food Reserve Agency (FRA) will buy all the maize across the country and an additional 2,000 tonnes of cassava, director Fridah Luhila said yesterday.
Ms Luhila said at the Copperbelt Mining Agriculture and Commercial Show in Kitwe yesterday that FRA would spend K89 billion to acquire maize and other agricultural produce this year.

Original_Search_Title: NDDC Targets Youth Empowerment
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Targets Youth Empowerment
Body_Text:
Lagos — YOUTH employment and empowerment would be the focus of the new board of
the Niger Delta Development Commission, NDDC.
Chairman of the board, Ambassador Sam Edem announced this in Port
Harcourt during a forum with youths across the Niger Delta Region.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Innovations Will Begin to Yield Fruits Soon, Says Minister
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture Innovations Will Begin to Yield Fruits Soon, Says Minister
Body_Text:
Umuahia — MINISTER of Agriculture and Rural Development, Mallam Adamu Bello said
on Tuesday that innovations being introduced by the Federal Government in
the area of agriculture would soon begin to yield positive results.
The minister, who spoke through an official of his ministry at the
launching of two books at the Michael Okpara University of Agriculture
(MOUA) Umudike, said however that a lot still needs to be done to achieve
the ultimate goal of attaining food self sufficiency to reduce poverty
level.

Original_Search_Title: Isangano Park Illegal Settlers Given May 31 Ultimatum
Headline: Zambia: Isangano Park Illegal Settlers Given May 31 Ultimatum
Body_Text:
Lusaka — The Zambia Wildlife Authority (ZAWA) in Northern Province has given
illegal settlers in Isangano National Park up to May 31, 2005, to vacate the
area or face force.
And chief Chiwangala of Chilubi mainland said his subjects would not
move away from the area he claims they have occupied since 1804.

Original_Search_Title: Gov Attah Commended
Headline: Nigeria: Gov Attah Commended
Body_Text:
Uyo — Chairman of Akwa Ibom State Ethical and Atitudinal Reorientation
(EARCOM), Mr. Eseme Eyiboh, yesterday commended the state government for
introducing integrity into the process of contract award and supervision in
the state.
Eyiboh said this when a team of journalists, traditional rulers,
professional from whithin and outside the state, went on a tour of
government's projects in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Opposition Out to Blackmail Me - Fayose
Headline: Nigeria: Opposition Out to Blackmail Me - Fayose
Body_Text:
Lagos — Governor Ayodele Fayose was indeed, a dark horse before he ventured into the governorship race in 2003. Not many gave him a chance of grabbing the coveted seat. But he did in a grandstyle. And every other thing is now history. By May 29, he would be two years in office but pressures from opposition are still mounting. Recently, he spoke to a number of journalists on his mid-term scorecard, Collins Edomaruse was there
You are just about two years in office as Executive Governor of Ekiti State. We thought that it is just about time to review your tenure thus far and put it side-by-side with your promises and perhaps look at the next two years in the same office?

Original_Search_Title: We're Not for Political Rascality - Ladoja
Headline: Nigeria: We're Not for Political Rascality - Ladoja
Body_Text:
Lagos — As democracy turns six on May 29, the jostle for 2007 general elections in Oyo State has not however betrayed the frenzy across the country. In this interview with Ademola Adeyemo, Governor Rashidi Ladoja of Oyo State speaks on his two years in office and reacts to claims of non-performance being made by opposition
Critics including the opposition party- AD and even your party-PDP have been bitter with your style of Administration, they are accusing your government of ineptitude and non-performance. What is your reaction?

Original_Search_Title: Face to Face With Former Rebel Sayimbwende
Headline: Zambia: Face to Face With Former Rebel Sayimbwende
Body_Text:
FROM the days of the "Scramble for Africa," the political tide of many countries on the African continent has changed dramatically. Today marks the celebrations of the emancipation of our countries from political bondage.
Most African countries have not just gained political independence but have also embraced democracy - a system of governance where the ordinary people are allowed to have a full say in the affairs and destiny of their land.

Original_Search_Title: Major Challenges Ahead for Bozize After Poll Victory
Headline: Central African Republic: Major Challenges Ahead for Bozize After Poll Victory
Body_Text:
Bangui — After three months and two rounds, the Central African Republic's
presidential and parliamentary elections are over with the final results
announced on Tuesday, but the newly-elected president, Francois Bozize, and
the 105 members of the new National Assembly face the country's same basic
problems as before.
"It's a good thing to win elections but the new government must quickly
renew negations with the IMF [International Monetary Fund] and the World
Bank, and the government must increase its revenue to pay workers who are
starving," Theophile Sony-Colle, a union leader who won a legislative seat
in the capital, Bangui, told IRIN on Wednesday.

Original_Search_Title: NDDC to Check Youth Restiveness - Chairman
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC to Check Youth Restiveness - Chairman
Body_Text:
The chairman of the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC), Amb. San Joe Edem has reiterated the Commission's resolve to stem youth restiveness in the Niger Delta zone.
The NDDC boss maintained this position Saturday in Uyo during the reception organised for him and the state representative, Hon. Pastor Eyo Godwin Moffat by the government and people of Akwa Ibom State.

Original_Search_Title: 'How to Meet High Demand for Cassava'
Headline: Nigeria: 'How to Meet High Demand for Cassava'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Unless farm managers are directly involved in rapid multiplication of
cassava planting materials to meet local demand, the current high cost of
garri and other cassava food products in Nigeria will continue
unabated.
This is because with the new industrial uses of cassava both for local
and export markets, the current level of production of 34 million tonnes per
annum cannot meet local demand.

Original_Search_Title: Bangui : Hard To Make Ends Meet
Headline: Central African Republic: Bangui : Hard To Make Ends Meet
Body_Text:
Before and after the presidential elections of May 8, the daily preoccupation of Bangui's residents remain the same.
The capital of the Central African Republic, Bangui, has an airy look. The streets are well-paved with mango trees on the sidelines, while giant billboards adorn the streets to sensitise all and sundry of the dangers of HIV/AIDS and why its important to keep the city clean. "Bangui la Coquette, Bangui la Fleurie" are some of the messages on billboards.

Original_Search_Title: NEA Empowers 2,200 Women's Groups
Headline: Ghana: NEA Empowers 2,200 Women's Groups
Body_Text:
Accra — About 2,200 women's groups in the Northern and Brong-Ahafo regions have benefited from the Northern Empowerment Association (NEA) programme for women's economic enhancement.
The NEA currently operates in two districts of the Northern region, namely Bole and West Mamprusi, and also Kintampo in the Brong-Ahafo region.

Original_Search_Title: Integrated Shopping Bags & Nylon Extrusion Business
Headline: Nigeria: Integrated Shopping Bags & Nylon Extrusion Business
Body_Text:
Lagos — The economic realities of our time dictate an urgent need for Nigerians to set up small and medium scale industries.
Potential investors must however, consider certain factors sucha s machinery, manpower, raw materials, market and profitability of the proposed project. Considering the above factors, one of the most viable small-scale industries for Nigerian investors is the polythylene sheets extrusions plant. An expatriate who set up this plant in 1986, made a profit of N6million in the first year.

Original_Search_Title: Coping Mechanisms Wear Thin As the Drought Drags On
Headline: Mozambique: Coping Mechanisms Wear Thin As the Drought Drags On
Body_Text:
Maputo — Traditional coping mechanisms are nearing exhaustion in Mozambique as
drought-affected communities struggle with dwindling food production and the
impact of HIV/AIDS on the agricultural labour force.
Preliminary figures estimate that more than 230,000 households in the
southern and central regions of the country will face food shortages this
season.

Original_Search_Title: 'Extreme Poverty'll Be Eliminated By 2010'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Extreme Poverty'll Be Eliminated By 2010'
Body_Text:
Lagos — National Co-ordinator of National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP), Dr. Magnus Kpakpol, has envisaged total elimination of extreme poverty by the year 2010.
Kpakol, who made this remarks yesterday in Lagos during his courtesy visit to THISDAY corporate office, described as extreme the state of abject poverty where a person cannot feed, clothe or shelter himself.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Destroy Bridges in Kasese
Headline: Uganda: Floods Destroy Bridges in Kasese
Body_Text:
Kasese — A number of bridges in the district have been washed away and others
destroyed by heavy floods that hit the district early this week.
The councillor for Kyondo sub-county, Mr John Kambere, said two bridges
of Kurujumba and Buhungu were washed away by floods from River Nyamugasane
on Tuesday.

Original_Search_Title: Sh8bn Worth Maize Lost to Pests
Headline: Kenya: Sh8bn Worth Maize Lost to Pests
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya loses more than eight million bags of maize to storage pests each year, Agriculture minister Kipruto arap Kirwa said yesterday.
Out of the 28 million bags produced each year, 30 per cent, valued at Sh8.4 billion, was thus destroyed, he said.

Original_Search_Title: 'Technology, Science Critical to Industrial Growth'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Technology, Science Critical to Industrial Growth'
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo said yesterday that for the country's
manufacturing sector to experience big lift into the future, it must invest
seriously in science, engineering and technology.
Obasanjo, whose speech was read by his Special Adviser on Manufacturing,
Alhaji Ahmed Abdulkadir, at the opening of a two-day workshop on 'Moving
Research and Development Towards Commercial Produ-ction,' challenged
Nigerian scientists to develop techniques that would enable local
manufacturers compete favourably with companies from technically advanced
countries.

Original_Search_Title: Nebbi Reaps From Village Bank
Headline: Uganda: Nebbi Reaps From Village Bank
Body_Text:
Kampala — OWACHGIU 29, of Nyarauvuru sub-county in Nebbi district, changed his life with a loan from a village bank.
He borrowed sh400,000 and after brain storming with their farmer's group, othogiogicud farmer's group, they resolved to buy two oxen to till their land.

Original_Search_Title: Document Nigeria's Economic Progress
Headline: Nigeria: Document Nigeria's Economic Progress
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Being a text of speech delivered by Eze Festus Odimegwu, MD/CEO,
Nigerian Breweries Plc at the launch of the Olusegun Library in Abeokuta on
Saturday.
This is the first time in Nigeria and probably in Africa that a
Presidential Library is being conceived in honour of a national leader who
has spent his entire life in the service of this nation and indeed
mankind.

Original_Search_Title: Wheat Production
Headline: Nigeria: Wheat Production
Body_Text:
It is time for the federal government to begin a vigorous policy of
mechanized wheat farming in Northern Nigeria.
This is an important programme that the FG may pursue working hand in
hand with relevant state and local governments and their agencies,
recognised self-help or co-operative groups and other non-governmental
organisations. Crucially though, it is necessary to note that the energy and
resources which the federal government has expended in the cassava project,
with the attendant positive results, has shown that if the government
musters the necessary will to act, meaningful strides would be made in wheat
production.

Original_Search_Title: Galamsey Casualties On the Rise
Headline: Ghana: Galamsey Casualties On the Rise
Body_Text:
Accra — AngloGold Ashanti (Obuasi) has restated that the increasing galamsey activities on its concession and other mining areas are worrying, which need national intervention in addressing it.
Mg George Osei-Sasu, the Acting General Manager in-charge of Human Resources, who made the statement called for global action against the illegal mining activities.

Original_Search_Title: 600 Farmers for Cassava Land Allocation to Benefit
Headline: Nigeria: 600 Farmers for Cassava Land Allocation to Benefit
Body_Text:
Benin-City — At least 600 farmers are to benefit from the Edo State special farmland allocation meant to boost cassava production in the state, while N8.2 million will be distributed as micro-credit to farmers by the end of themonth.
In addition to the 3,000 hectares of farmland to be distributed to farmers to cultivate cassava, government has assured that it would assist farmers to obtain low interest loans from the Federal Agricultural Bank, irrespective of their political affiliation.

Original_Search_Title: Bie: Kutato Peasants Get Agricultural Tools
Headline: Angola: Bie: Kutato Peasants Get Agricultural Tools
Body_Text:
Kuito — Peasants of the Kutato commune, municipality of Chinguar (in the central
Bie Province), on Saturday here received some agricultural tools from the
vice governor of this province for social and economic matters, Antonio
Gomes da Conceito.
The offer includes hoes and cutlasses, as well as seeds, fertilisers and
other means used in agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: 2,000 Homeless in Yumbe
Headline: Uganda: 2,000 Homeless in Yumbe
Body_Text:
Kampala — OVER 2,000 people, the majority of them children and women, have camped
at Lombe primary school in Yumbe district after being evicted by the
National Forest Authority (NFA) from forest reserves in Moyo district.
They were evicted from Eria north and south forest reserves in Metu sub-
county on April 25 by NFA officials, who also cut down their cassava
fields.

Original_Search_Title: The Benevolence of Fiwale Hill Mission Rural Health Centre Staff
Headline: Zambia: The Benevolence of Fiwale Hill Mission Rural Health Centre Staff
Body_Text:
IN the winter of 1938, Fiwale Hill Mission Rural Health Centre was founded 32 kilometres south of Ndola.
Sister G.C. Bellin, an Australian national working closely with the South African Baptist Missionary Society (SABMS), is credited with the founding of the centre.

Original_Search_Title: Drought Affects Over 28,000 in Sofala
Headline: Mozambique: Drought Affects Over 28,000 in Sofala
Body_Text:
Beira — Over 28,000 people have been affected by drought this year in the
central Mozambican province of Sofala, according to data provided by
provincial governor Alberto Vaquina to President Armando Guebuza, currently
on a working visit to the province.
Rainfall was only fully satisfactory in two districts, Gorongosa and
Nhamatanda. The districts worst hit are Machanga (which Guebuza is visiting
on Saturday) and Chemba.  The provincial government estimated in March that
over 38,000 hectares of crops had been lost to drought, and these figures
are now being updated.

Original_Search_Title: Galamsey Casualties on Mining Concessions Need Intervention - HR Manager
Headline: Ghana: Galamsey Casualties on Mining Concessions Need Intervention - HR Manager
Body_Text:
ANGLOGOLD ASHANTI, Obuasi, has restated that the increasing galamsey
(illegal small scale mining) activities on its concession and other mining
areas are worrying, and need national intervention.
Mr. George Osei-Sasu, the Acting General Manager in-charge of Human
Resources, who made the statement on Wednesday, therefore called for global
action against the illegal mining activities.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger May Affect This Year's Likumbi Lya Mize - Ndungu
Headline: Zambia: Hunger May Affect This Year's Likumbi Lya Mize - Ndungu
Body_Text:
Lusaka — HUNGER in Zambezi district is likely to affect this year's Likumbi Lya
Mize traditional ceremony, senior chief Ndungu of the Luvale people of
Zambezi has said.
In an interview in Lusaka on Wednesday, chief Ndungu also said both men
and women were sharing a ward at a health centre in his chiefdom.

Original_Search_Title: Govt's Insensitive Talk On Gari
Headline: Nigeria: Govt's Insensitive Talk On Gari
Body_Text:
Lagos — Marie Antoinette hadn't the faintest idea she was inviting trouble when,
shortly before the French Revolution of 1789, she asked the French people to
eat cake if they could not find bread.
To her, there was no difference between the two food items. But the
people did not think that cake was a good substitute for bread. Even if it
was, they found the para-language insulting. Bread was the staple of the
French people. Marie Antoinette was the wife of Louis XVl, the man in-charge
at the time of acute bread scarcity. Louis XVl was not the worst of the
kings that had ruled France prior to the revolution. He just happened to be
the one under whose leadership the people could no longer bear the burden of
the oppression that many of his predecessors had heaped on the French
people.

Original_Search_Title: More Incentives for Cassava Production
Headline: Nigeria: More Incentives for Cassava Production
Body_Text:
Abuja — Determined to reverse the rising cost of garri and other cassava products in the country, as well as boost production, the Federal Executive Council (FEC) on 11 May approved more incentives for cassava farmers.
The Minister of Agriculture, Mallam Adamu Bello explained that the decision followed a presentation by the Presidential Committee on Cassava Production, which reviewed strategies for increased output.

Original_Search_Title: Conned With Corn
Headline: Nigeria: Conned With Corn
Body_Text:
Lagos — This is the time for all Africans to stand up and say no to genetically modified foods.
THE scramble for Africa is getting hotter today than it may have been during the Berlin Conference at which she was partitioned. The partitioning of Africa sowed the seeds of discord and conflict that we are reaping today. Today, certain concepts have been painstakingly constructed and foisted on the continent. And this has been done in order to have Africa so compromised that she would simply just beg to be colonised once more. We are talking about the onslaught by the biotech industry on the innards of this continent.

Original_Search_Title: Comment and Analysis: Conned with Corn
Headline: Africa: Pambazuka news 206: 12 May 2005
Body_Text:
A Weekly Electronic Forum For Social Justice In Africa
NOTE: To view the links that are referenced below, please copy the url
and paste into your browswer.

Original_Search_Title: Youth Ministry Engages 15,000
Headline: Sierra Leone: Youth Ministry Engages 15,000
Body_Text:
Freetown — A memorandum of understanding has been signed between the United
Nations Development Programme (UNDP) and eight implementing partners
for a national agricultural project with four chiefdoms in each of
the twelve districts in the country. This project, according to the
Public Relations Officer at the Ministry of Youth and Sports, Amadu
Bah, is targeting 15,000 youths across the country.
Launching a youth engagement and job creation in the agricultural
sector project in Pujehun district recently, Dr. Dennis Bright
appealed for unity among youths and encouraged them to forget about
the trauma caused by the hard times of the war.

Original_Search_Title: Over 200,000 Family Farms Affected By Drought
Headline: Mozambique: Over 200,000 Family Farms Affected By Drought
Body_Text:
Maputo — At least 237,000 peasant family farms were
affected by drought in the 2004/2005 agricultural campaign in
Mozambique, according to preliminary data presented on Tuesday at
a meeting of the National Directorate of Agriculture.
According to a report in Wednesday's issue of the Maputo
daily "Noticias", consultations are now under way to design a
permanent strategy to minimise the effects of drought that
regularly affects some areas in the central and southern regions
of the country.

Original_Search_Title: 'Cassava Suitable for Youth Economic Empowerment'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Cassava Suitable for Youth Economic Empowerment'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Young people in the country have been urged to take advantage of cassava as a highly economic crop to support the country's effort to reduce monstrous unemployment problem and economically empower its people.
Speaking at a one-day workshop organised by the Junior Chambers International of Nigeria, (JCIN), in Ibadan International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) Trials Manager Mr. Paul Ilona explained that Nigerian youths and unemployed young adults should seek economic empowerment through farming and agribusiness.

Original_Search_Title: Govt's Insensitive Talk On Gari
Headline: Nigeria: Govt's Insensitive Talk On Gari
Body_Text:
Lagos — Marie Antoinette hadn't the faintest idea she was inviting trouble when,
shortly before the French Revolution of 1789, she asked the French people to
eat cake if they could not find bread.
To her, there was no difference between the two food items. But the
people did not think that cake was a good substitute for bread. Even if it
was, they found the para-language insulting. Bread was the staple of the
French people. Marie Antoinette was the wife of Louis XVI, the man in-charge
at the time of acute bread scarcity. Louis XVI was not the worst of the
kings that had ruled France prior to the revolution. He just happened to be
the one under whose leadership the people could no longer bear the burden of
the oppression that many of his predecessors had heaped on the French
people.

Original_Search_Title: Economic And Social Plan Presented to Parliament
Headline: Mozambique: Economic And Social Plan Presented to Parliament
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambican Prime Minister Luisa Diogo on
Monday presented the country's parliament, the Assembly of the
Republic, with the government's Economic and Social Plan for
2005, which envisages a growth rate for the year of between seven
and eight per cent.
The plan, and the accompanying state budget, are being
debated five months late. In principle, the Assembly should vote
on the annual plan and budget in December of the previous year:
but the Assembly did not meet in December 2004, because of the
general elections held that month.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Exports: Nigeria Targets N28bn
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Exports: Nigeria Targets N28bn
Body_Text:
Abuja — Nigeria hopes to earn at least N28 billion from the export of cassava
and cassava related products this year. The revenue is expected from export
of about 34-36 million metric tonnes of the crop which is the country's
current production level, at which level which the highest the world.
Minister of Commerce, Ambassador Idris Waziri who stated this in Abuja
at a special press briefing to flag- off the first shipmment of cassava to
China said the price of cassava had increased from N12,000 per metric tonne
last year to N28,000 this year and that the price was likely to increase in
the near future as more exports were expected to Asian and other African
countries.

Original_Search_Title: Ministerial Scorecards
Headline: Nigeria: Ministerial Scorecards
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Two years into their respective tenures as ministers, questions are
being asked as to what contribution each of the men and women appointed into
the Federal Executive Council (FEC) has made to the President Olusegun
Obasanjo administration. Tunde Rahman, Joseph Ushigiale and Oke Epia x-ray
the performance of these presidential appointees which is critical to the
success of the reform/renewal agenda the President has set for his tenure,
with a view to rating how they have helped in the overall scorecard of the
administration...
Magnus Odion Ugbesia, Solid Minerals

Original_Search_Title: Aid for War-Ravaged Areas Elusive
Headline: Guinea: Population-Guinea: Aid for War-Ravaged Areas Elusive
Body_Text:
Conakry — Madeleine Gomou, a nurse in the village of Konia-Aviation, finds it all
but impossible to do her job.
"We are living in complete deprivation here. There's not even potable
water. We have to take care of patients in spite of our lack of resources
and the means to do tests worthy of the name," she told IPS, pointing to
boxes of medicines that were expected to supply the surrounding population
of 2,500 for just four days. The only food available at the dilapidated
clinic where Gomou works was cassava paste.

Original_Search_Title: Driving Employment the Delta Way
Headline: Nigeria: Driving Employment the Delta Way
Body_Text:
Lagos — The drive to create jobs for its teaming youth has been very dear to the
Delta State Government. Apart from opening up the state through major roads
and building infrastructure, it has put every machinery in motion to create
more jobs in the state, especially in agriculture through a partnership
between the state and private investors. This is part of activities of the
state government to engage the teaming youth to participate in the
development of the state, especially in agriculture and agro-allied
industries.
The Information Commissioner, Mr. Magnus Onyibe, in a statement,
explained that executive council recently approved a proposal for the
establishment of value added industrial processing of cassava and the
rehabilitation of the palm oil estate at Nsukwa.

Original_Search_Title: Nakasongola Farmer Not Affected By Drought
Headline: Uganda: Nakasongola Farmer Not Affected By Drought
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE recent severe drought that hit Nakasongola district leaving
thousands of families starving did not affect Chrysostom Kayise's
family.
Kayise added manure and continuously irrigated his four cassava acres
garden. The drought left about 90% of homesteads without food and many are
still suffering because they did not plan for their families.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Turn to Mangoes, Oranges to Kick Out Poverty
Headline: Uganda: Farmers Turn to Mangoes, Oranges to Kick Out Poverty
Body_Text:
Arua — RESEARCHERS at Abi Agricultural Research Development Centre (ARDC) in
Arua have developed different mango varieties through the use of budding and
grafting technologies.
Grafting and budding are some of the new technologies which the National
Agricultural Advisory Service (NAADS) has come up with to help farmers
increase on their agricultural production.

Original_Search_Title: FRA to Purchase 120,000 Metric Tonnes of Maize
Headline: Zambia: FRA to Purchase 120,000 Metric Tonnes of Maize
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THE Food Reserve Agency (FRA) yesterday announced that it would purchase
120,000 metric tonnes of maize for the 2005/2006 marketing season effective
May 20, 2005.
FRA board chairperson Costain Chilala said at a briefing yesterday that
the purchase exercise would last five months and end on September 30,
2005.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Floor Price Pegged At K36,000
Headline: Zambia: Maize Floor Price Pegged At K36,000
Body_Text:
THE Food Reserve Agency (FRA) has announced K36,000 as the floor price
for a 50 kg bag of maize for the 2005/06 marketing season.
The FRA also says it has recovered K7 billion out of the K200 billion
owed to it by various debtors who include politicians and
co-operatives.

Original_Search_Title: Children 'Spin' Water Out
Headline: Eswatini: Swaziland: Children 'Spin' Water Out
Body_Text:
Mbabane — The children at Lubilini primary school in eastern Swaziland have never
had a real playground. Now they get to spin a merry-go-round in the school's
dirt courtyard, not only for exercise and entertainment, but to pump water
out of the ground.
The water is used to prepare their meals, for sanitation facilities and
to irrigate the school vegetable garden.

Original_Search_Title: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: a Story of Biblical Parallels
Headline: Liberia: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: a Story of Biblical Parallels
Body_Text:
Monrovia — By the age of 16, Dr. Saqui Malakpai had completely lost his sight. He
dropped out of 10th grade.
Ask him what he can cook, and he'll ask you what you want to eat. Palm
butter? Cassava leaf? Pepper soup? Take your pick.

Original_Search_Title: Over Dependency On Oil, Nigeria's Problem - Isoun
Headline: Nigeria: Over Dependency On Oil, Nigeria's Problem - Isoun
Body_Text:
Abuja — MINISTER of Science and Technology, Prof. Turner Isoun yesterday said that the major cause of Nigeria's socio-economic and political problem is over dependence of the nation on a single commodity (petroleum).
Prof. Isoun, who made this statement yesterday at the opening ceremony of a two-day workshop of the stakeholders forum on cassava research and development organised by Raw Materials Research and Development Council (RMRDC) said for the fact that Nigeria has its major source of revenue for the development of the economy, at the expense of other resources has undoubtedly retarded the technological development of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: a Story of Biblical Parallels
Headline: Liberia: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: a Story of Biblical Parallels
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Liberian with disability says his greatest desire is to see people with disabilities given the same opportunities he had. Doctor has written a position paper to Liberia's legislative body, appealing for rights to jobs and other possibilities for persons with disabilities.
By the age of 16, Dr. Saqui Malakpai had completely lost his sight. He dropped out of 10th grade.

Original_Search_Title: Ekiti to Partner With FUTA
Headline: Nigeria: Ekiti to Partner With FUTA
Body_Text:
Akure —  On cassava production, processing
EKITI State government is to collaborate with the Federal University of Technology, Akure (FUTA), in the area of cassava production and processing.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Export Starts With 40 Metric Tonnes
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Export Starts With 40 Metric Tonnes
Body_Text:
Lagos — NIGERIA made history, weekend, with the flagging off of its maiden export of 40 metric tonnes of cassava chips to the People's Republic of China, and on hand to perform the flagging-off ceremony were the Minister of Commerce, Ambassador A. D. Idris Waziri, who was supported by Minister for State for Agriculture and Rural Development, Otunba Bamidele Dada, Lagos Commissioner for Commerce, Mr. Lanre Balogun, who represented Gov. Ahmed Tinubu, and Mrs. Sasore, Special Adviser to the President on Export Programme.
The cassava chips, were being shipped in two 20-foot container consignments, which had passed through the acid test of ensuring the right quality, weight and standard. Commending the company for making history by being the first company to formally commence the export of cassava products to China under the current Presidential Initiative on Cassava, Waziri said: "Your efforts have more than concretised the vision of Mr. President in making cassava an enduring export product that will generate foreign exchange for our country, generate employment opportunities and fight poverty."

Original_Search_Title: Grain Silos Controversy
Headline: Nigeria: Grain Silos Controversy
Body_Text:
C'ttee chairman demanded fertilizer contract - Agric minister
Amid allegations by the chairman, Senate Committee on Agri-culture,
Senator Bode Olowo-poroku over N3.2 billion fraud in the procurement and
disbursement of fertilizer by the Federal Ministry of Agriculture, the
Minister of Agriculture, Malam Adamu Bello, has said the senator is only
making such allegations because last year he deman-ded for, but was not
awarded a fertilizer contract.

Original_Search_Title: Government Employs 500 Agriculture Extension Officers
Headline: Zambia: Government Employs 500 Agriculture Extension Officers
Body_Text:
THE ministry of Finance and National Planning has employed 500 extension
officers countywide to market agriculture products in the country.
Minister of Agriculture and Co-operatives Mundia Sikatana announced this
during a Banana field day at Nthochi farm west of Kalulushi.

Original_Search_Title: ZEC Orders Catholic Dioceses to Speed Up Hunger Survey
Headline: Zambia: ZEC Orders Catholic Dioceses to Speed Up Hunger Survey
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THE Zambia Episcopal Conference (ZEC) has ordered all Catholic dioceses
nationwide to speed up their hunger monitoring survey in readiness for
interventions.
And Nalikwanda UPND member of parliament Simasiku Kalumiana has
disclosed that there is severe hunger in the Barotse Plains.

Original_Search_Title: Kirwa Issues Drought Alert As Rains Fail
Headline: Kenya: Kirwa Issues Drought Alert As Rains Fail
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A famine alert was flashed to donors and Kenya's farmers last night, following insufficient rains.
Agriculture minister Kipruto Kirwa said the March and April rains were insufficient in most parts of the country - patchy and poorly distributed - making 80 per cent of farmers unable to plant. In normal years 60 to 70 per cent of people could have planted.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Can Save N112bn Through Cassava Products - Minister
Headline: Nigeria Can Save N112bn Through Cassava Products - Minister
Body_Text:
Commerce Minister, Ambassador A. D. Idris Waziri has revealed that
Nigeria can save up to $800 million (about N112 billion) through
substituting imports of some products that can be locally made with cassava
in the country.
He said products such as ethanol, starch, glucose syrup, high fructose
syrup, sorbitol, citric acid, among others, can be produced locally from
cassava and this could earn the nation huge income and save import
bills.

Original_Search_Title: Storm Ravages Pallisa
Headline: Uganda: Storm Ravages Pallisa
Body_Text:
Kampala — OVER 800 people were left homeless after a storm destroyed their houses
in Puti-Puti sub-county, Pallisa district last weekend, writes Francis
Okiror.
The storm that killed animals and damaged several acres of crops,
affected 247 homes in which eight permanent, 12 semi-permanent and over 200
grass thatched houses were destroyed.

Original_Search_Title: Corruption: the Evil That Men Do
Headline: Liberia: Corruption: the Evil That Men Do
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Contributing Editor Joe S. Kappia explores the age-old tradition that has crippled the Liberian economy for years.
What is corruption? Who is corrupt? In what form or shape does corruption exist? How do you detect corruption? Is corruption like tribalism or racism? How do you avoid being corrupt? Is corruption a culture or a disease? If it is a culture, how did it develop? If a disease, can it be cured? Or is it like AIDS?

Original_Search_Title: Otema Awany Warns Soldiers Against Stealing Cassava
Headline: Uganda: Otema Awany Warns Soldiers Against Stealing Cassava
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE northern UPDF intelligence coordinator, Col. Charles Otema Awany,
has warned the soldiers against uprooting cassava from the gardens of people
living in camps in Coo Pee and Lukodi in Bungatira sub-county, Gulu
district.
The Bungatira LC3 secretary for women, Margaret Odoki, accused the
soldiers of up-rooting their cassava. Coo Pee camp accommodates 33,368
people, out of whom only 22,000 have access to World Food Programme
rations. Odoki said the camp was hard hit by starvation and shortage of
water.

Original_Search_Title: Breakthrough in Coffee Wilt Disease
Headline: Uganda: Breakthrough in Coffee Wilt Disease
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Coffee Research Institute (CORI) under the National Agricultural
Research Organisation (NARO) has made a breakthrough in the fight against
the coffee wilt diseases.
The disease has been a threat to the coffee industry, Deneth Kyetere,
the director of CRI, has said.

Original_Search_Title: Foodstuffs Prices Soar
Headline: Nigeria: Foodstuffs Prices Soar
Body_Text:
Despite the onset of the rainy season, prices of foodstuffs have continued to rise sharply, a market survey has shown.
Investigations conducted by Daily Trust in some major towns across the country indicates that the escalating prices of food-stuffs have further wor-sened the already deteriorating economic conditions of the people.

Original_Search_Title: 'Why Bakers Shun Cassava Flour'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Why Bakers Shun Cassava Flour'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Master Bakers Association of Nigeria, the umbrella body for bakers in the country, has explained why its members remain skeptical about the Federal Government's directive to them to use cassava flour for bread-making, saying that cassava flour can only be mixed with high quality wheat flour for good results.
The association said that in view of the fact that the quality of imported wheat flour varies depending on the country of origin, time and season, its members cannot afford the risky trial with cassava flour, which might result in an irreversible waste in the event that poor quality wheat flour is mixed with it.

Original_Search_Title: Unemployment? Nigeria Should Look Inwards - NDE DG
Headline: Nigeria: Unemployment? Nigeria Should Look Inwards - NDE DG
Body_Text:
Eng. (Dr.) Samuel Adelodun, the Director-General of the National
Directorate of Employment (NDE,) read mechanical engineering at the Ahmadu
Bello University (ABU,) Zaria from where he graduated in 1970. He was with
the Nigeria Television Authority (NTA) and Federal Radio Corporation of
Nigeria (FRCN) in Kaduna, then known as the Radio Television Kaduna (RTK) as
engineer.
He then worked at the Kaduna Polytechnic and later the Nigeria Defence
Industries Corporation (NDIC). He was first at the Federal Ministry of Works
before his appointment as Director General of the NDE in 2000… In
this encounter with Assistant Features Editor, Nasir Dambatta, the NDE
Director bares his mind on the rampaging unemployment monster and believes
that the way forward is for Nigerians to look inwards for viable employment
alternatives. Excerpts:

Original_Search_Title: China to Import Nigerian Cassava Chips
Headline: Nigeria: China to Import Nigerian Cassava Chips
Body_Text:
Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara state has hinted that the People's
Republic of China has agreed to import one million tones of cassava chips
yearly from Nigeria.
Governor Saraki who accompanied President Olusegun Obasanjo on his state
visit to China, Vietnam, Indonesia and Far East nations, said the Chinese
government had also agreed to set up a GSM accessories assembly plant in
Kwara State.

Original_Search_Title: New Solutions Needed to Lessen HIV/Aids Impact On Farming
Headline: Southern Africa: New Solutions Needed to Lessen HIV/Aids Impact On Farming
Body_Text:
Durban — Two years after Southern Africa's humanitarian crisis exposed the
destructive relationship between hunger and HIV/AIDS, still not enough is
known about the actual impact of the epidemic on rural households.
Now, new research from the recent international conference on 'HIV/AIDS
and Food and Nutrition Security', held earlier this month in Durban, South
Africa, has provided greater insight into how farming communities have been
affected.

Original_Search_Title: Saraki Back From Asia, Assures On Cassava Export
Headline: Nigeria: Saraki Back From Asia, Assures On Cassava Export
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara State, yesterday returned to the state capital after a 10 days trip to Asia, with President Olusegun Obasanjo, and said the state is on the right track in its agricultural policy.
Saraki said there shall be no looking back, on the possible exportation of 1 million tonnes of cassava chips to China yearly, and urged aggressive farming to enable the state meet the target

Original_Search_Title: 'The Zamfara I Met'
Headline: Nigeria: 'The Zamfara I Met'
Body_Text:
Lagos — SHORTLY after the re-enthronement of democracy in 1999, Zamfara State
leaped into limelight as a threat to the nascent democracy. That was as a
result of the insistence of the democratically elected governor of that
state in the person of Alhaji Ahmed Sani (Yerima Bakura), to govern his
people by the Shari'ah Law.
Every person in the country thought he was crazy to wave aside the law
of Nigeria and implement a religious law in his state. Political and
religious sects of the nation branded him. But he tenaciously defended and
implemented the law even till date. But the question remains: why has the
state not been one of the boiling states of the nation, yet an intending
visitor still carries his heart in his hands whenever the state is
mentioned?

Original_Search_Title: Warning of Famine As Rains Delay
Headline: Kenya: Warning of Famine As Rains Delay
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government has warned of famine in Nyanza Province as the long rains
delay.
Residents have therefore been urged to revert to fast-maturing cereals
or face starvation in months.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Export Heats Up Staple Food Market As Prices Gallop
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Export Heats Up Staple Food Market As Prices Gallop
Body_Text:
Lagos — Current hike in prices of staple food especially garri and cassava derivatives poses serious concern to Nigerians, as EBERE NWOJI reports that the situation has been attributed to export of cassava which Nigeria is not yet producing in such a large quantity that could sustain both local and international markets.
BARELY two year after the federal government took the initiative of generating billions of naira revenue from cassava exports, Nigerians are already feeling the negative impact as prices of cassava related food stuff and raw materials have risen astronomically.

Original_Search_Title: Affordable Tractors As Way Forward for Agric
Headline: Nigeria: Affordable Tractors As Way Forward for Agric
Body_Text:
Lagos — The International Institute of Tropical Agriculture recently strongly recommended the use of a hand-pushed power tiller for small medium scale former to reduce cost of cassava production in Nigeria. This, as reported in "THISDAY" of March 29, 2005, was the outcome of a one-day meeting between directors of the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and scientists at the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) at Onne Station in Rivers State.
This is cheering news and worthy of commendation in view of governments' efforts at eradication of poverty through food sufficiency, Worthy of particular mention is the federal government initiative on cassava production.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria's Cassava Trade With Neigbours Booms
Headline: Nigeria's Cassava Trade With Neigbours Booms
Body_Text:
Lagos — International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) has stated that trade in cassava products between Nigeria to Neighbouring African countries such as Niger, Chad and Burkina Faso has reached record levels with garri, cassava chips and flour moving in large volumes from various parts of Nigeria to these African countries
An IITA report, compiled by the Public Affairs Manager of the institute,Taye Babaleye explained that cassava export trade in Dawanau market in Dawakin Tofa Local Government area of Kano State which has now reach very large trade volumes started in response to food deficit seasons in the Sahelian Countries.

Original_Search_Title: Ebonyi C'ttee Flays Chikelu Claims On Rice Quality
Headline: Nigeria: Ebonyi C'ttee Flays Chikelu Claims On Rice Quality
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — THE committee on increased food production set-up by the Ebonyi State government has faulted claims by the Minister of Information and National Orientation, Chief Chukwuemeka Chikelu that rice produced in the Abakaliki Rice mill complex remains inferior to imported rice.
Chairman of the committee, Prof. Frank Idike at a press conference in Abakaliki said Abakaliki Rice was comparable if not better than much of the imported rice. Prof Idike said "the minister was obviously talking about Abakaliki of East central State or old Anambra State or old Enugu State. He said that there are a number of destoning machines which yield products that are 100% stone free.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Calls for Holistic Approach to End Food Insecurity
Headline: Eswatini: Swaziland: WFP Calls for Holistic Approach to End Food Insecurity
Body_Text:
Mbabane — The World Food Programme (WFP) has called for a holistic approach to
tackle food insecurity in Swaziland after four years of drought.
"We need to look at the food shortage situation as a whole, including
all the factors that contribute: not just a lack of rainfall but the
appropriateness of crops; the need to educate farmers on better seeds and
cropping techniques; ways to mitigate AIDS, which is eliminating farmers,"
WFP's country representative in Swaziland, Abdoulaye Balde, told IRIN.

Original_Search_Title: Garri Price Soars
Headline: Nigeria: Garri Price Soars
Body_Text:
Lagos — GARRI, one of the nation's staple foods may soon disappear from the menu of many homes, following the astronomical rise in its price and other food items made from cassava, which is being massively exported.
As the price of garri soars by over 60 per cent, Nigerians have raised alarm over the situation and pleaded with government to urgently address the hike.

Original_Search_Title: Edo Not Answerable to EFCC On Any Matter -- Osadolor
Headline: Nigeria: Edo Not Answerable to EFCC On Any Matter -- Osadolor
Body_Text:
Newly appointed Edo State Commissioner of Information and Orientation, Mr. Kingsley Osadolor, spoke with Sunday Vanguard in Benin City on renewed efforts of the Chief Lucky Igbinedion administration to reposition the state before 2007. Excerpt:
GOVERNOR Igbinedion's plan in the next three years?

Original_Search_Title: Staring At an Uncertain Future
Headline: Kenya: Staring At an Uncertain Future
Body_Text:
Nairobi —  Food prices are rising even as the country faces declining agricultural
production, writes Michael Omondi
As Central Bank of Kenya released last week's official figures on
inflation, it emerged that attempts to keep inflation at a single digit
level are being thwarted by soaring food prices.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Government Faults Contractors
Headline: Nigeria: Delta Government Faults Contractors
Body_Text:
Lagos — It may have been a bad time for Contractors handling the construction of
a three storey administrative building for Delta State High Court of
Justice, following a recent surchaged place on them by the State government
The state government's descison THISDAY learnt was borne out of the
reason that the said contractor (Odazee Nig. Ltd), unilaterally constructed
an additional floor to the two storey building which was not part of the
project plan.

Original_Search_Title: Fayose's Achievements Excite Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Fayose's Achievements Excite Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Lagos — President Olusegun Obasanjo has given the Ekiti State Governor, Ayo
Fayose a pat on the back for his excellent performance in less than two
years in office.
The President, in a letter he sent to the governor following his
just-concluded state visit to Ekiti State, said he was excited by the record
of the administration since it came into being two years ago.

Original_Search_Title: Abakaliki Rice Farmers Tackle Minister
Headline: Nigeria: Abakaliki Rice Farmers Tackle Minister
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Chairman of the Committee on Increased Food Production in Ebonyi State,
Professor Frank Idike, has described as unfortunate a recent statement
credited to the Minister of Information and National Orientation, Chief
Chukwuemeka Chikelu, who was quoted as comparing "Abakaliki Rice" to frosty
marriage relationship.
Prof. Idike who spoke to journalists in Abakaliki stated: "According to
the Minister, no matter the effort put into the processing of Abakaliki
rice, there is always stone in it."

Original_Search_Title: Conned With Corn
Headline: Nigeria: Conned With Corn
Body_Text:
Lagos — The scramble for Africa is getting hotter today than it may have been
during the Berlin Conference at which she was partitioned. The partitioning
of Africa sowed the seeds of discord and conflict that we are reaping
today. Today, certain concepts have been painstakingly constructed and
foisted on the continent. And this has been done in order to have Africa so
compromised that she would simply just beg to be colonised once more. We are
talking about the onslaught by the biotech industry on the innards of this
continent.
The siege is on. Many people imagine that the pressure on Africa to
accept genetically modified grains or other crops as food aid ended with the
widely known case with Zambia in 2002. That emblematic case rightly showed
that every country has the sovereign right determine what type of food to
eat, irrespective of whether it is purchased in the market or is donated as
aid. And it demonstrated to the world that the predicted catastrophe of
Zambians starving never happened. The country thereafter recorded food
surpluses, besides the fact that in the heat of the crisis the shortage was
limited to sections of the country and there were supplies in other regions
that simply needed to be procured for the needy areas.

Original_Search_Title: Bartels Pushes for Indepth Research to Up Crop Yields
Headline: Ghana: Bartels Pushes for Indepth Research to Up Crop Yields
Body_Text:
Mr. Kwamena Bartels, Minister of Private Sector Development and
President's Special Initiative (PSD/PSI), has called for in-depth research
to increase crop yields to satisfy high market demands.
According to him, the current yield of cassava, as raw material for the
Ayensu Starch Factory, was too low and an enhanced variety was needed to
boost the production level of the factory.

Original_Search_Title: As Drought Takes Hold, Door Stays Shut to GMOs
Headline: Zambia: As Drought Takes Hold, Zambia's Door Stays Shut to GMOs
Body_Text:
Hunger is a perennial challenge facing African countries, and Zambia is no exception. But while some nations are prepared to boost supplies by importing food containing genetically modified (GM) organisms, Zambia is sticking to its guns and saying no.
Once an exporter of food, Zambia is in the grip of its third severe drought since 2000. The lack of rain is threatening Zambia's food security -- it needs at least 200,000 tonnes of maize to avert a crisis -- which has led the United States to increase pressure on the country to legalise imports of GM food.

Original_Search_Title: 450 Cassava Growers Undergo Training
Headline: Ghana: 450 Cassava Growers Undergo Training
Body_Text:
Abura Dunkwa — ABOUT 450 cassava growers in the Abura Asebu Kwamankese District in the Central region are undergoing a training programme in accordance with the proposed establishment of a starch factory.
In a speech read on his behalf, Mr. John Akowuah, the acting Executive Director of the Central Regional Development Commission (CEDECOM), said the 10-day training programme would take the farmers through topics like "Group Management and Development Cohesiveness, Entrepreneurship and Leadership Skills.'

Original_Search_Title: Low Maize Stocks in Choma Push Up Mealie-Meal Prices
Headline: Zambia: Low Maize Stocks in Choma Push Up Mealie-Meal Prices
Body_Text:
Lusaka — LOW maize stocks in Choma have pushed up the prices of mealie-meal, the
Agriculture Market Information Centre (AMIC) has observed.
In its bulletin of April 1 to 15, 2005, AMIC noted that Choma, which was
supposed to be a high maize production area, had higher prices of
maize.

Original_Search_Title: Work Together on Food Shortage
Headline: Zambia: Work Together on Food Shortage
Body_Text:
AUTHORITIES already know there will be hunger in some parts of Zambia and are taking measures to ensure no one starves so there is no need for panic.
Some people in villages are exaggerating the figures so that they can receive more stocks from the office of the vice-president.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Prices Rise As Supply Slumps
Headline: Uganda: Maize Prices Rise As Supply Slumps
Body_Text:
Commodities — Last week experienced an increase in maize grain price in Kisenyi Market
from Shs260 to Shs280 off lorry. It is expected to rise further in the near
future. There is a sluggish response to buy among big buyers the likes of
the Uganda Grain Traders Ltd (UGTL), Apony Ltd and others in the grain
industry because of the current high price.
UGTL is buying at Shs310 a kilo of well-sorted maize, 14 percent
moisture content and two percent DD, Apony Ltd offered to buy maize at
Shs275. A considerable amount of grain is still with stockiest/ traders in
the rural areas like Kiboga, Kamwenge and Kigumba who are hoarding it for a
better price.

Original_Search_Title: Akwa Ibom's Quest for Grassroots Devt
Headline: Nigeria: Akwa Ibom's Quest for Grassroots Devt
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Some of the functions of the local governments are to bring
development to the grassroots, but in the past local authorities have
shirked their responsibilities. However, Efem Nkanga writes that a new
revolution is taking place in rural areas in Akwa Ibom State
A new order is reigning in the administration of local governments in
Akwa Ibom State. Local government administration in Akwa Ibom State has come
of age. The state is gradually carving a niche for itself as a state that is
doing excellently well in the area of grass roots governance.

Original_Search_Title: Drought Slows East Agro Reform
Headline: Zambia: Drought Slows East Agro Reform
Body_Text:
Erratic rainfall, floods, wild beasts, human error.
These were some of the factors that members of the national technical committee of the Food Security Pack had to examine when they recently carried out an assessment of the impact of the programme in Eastern Province.

Original_Search_Title: The Death of 44 People Has Hurt Me - Mwata Kazembe
Headline: Zambia: The Death of 44 People Has Hurt Me - Mwata Kazembe
Body_Text:
Kazembe — THE death of 44 people that perished in a road accident has hurt me, Senior chief Mwata Kazembe of the Lunda people of Luapula Province has said.
Commenting on the Kawambwa road traffic accident last week in which 42 pupils and two others died, Mwata Kazembe said he was grieved because he had been robbed of potential subjects who could have contributed to the development of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Bundibugyo Glows From Strife
Headline: Uganda: Bundibugyo Glows From Strife
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE cloud of fear in which Bundibugyo residents lived during the 1997 rebel attacks has cleared. But nine years down the road, the effects of that five-year strife roam in the town and in villages like the vast shredded veil of white clouds that permanently cover the slopes of Bundibugyo's mountains.
I wake up to a cold misty morning. A cold wind sends a shiver.

Original_Search_Title: OAU Develops New Cassava Processing Machine
Headline: Africa: OAU Develops New Cassava Processing Machine
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Obafemi Awolowo University (OAU) Ile-Ife, in Osun says its Faculty
of Agriculture has developed a new cassava processing machine called
"IFESIFTA".
Prof. Roger Makanjuola, vice-chancellor of the university, who disclosed
at a news conference in Ile-Ife on Wednesday, said the new machine would
enable cassava growers process their products at a faster rate.

Original_Search_Title: Recurrent Dry Spells `Cause for Concern'
Headline: Southern Africa: Recurrent Dry Spells `Cause for Concern'
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — The rising temperatures and recurrent dry spells in Southern Africa points to the impact of climate change and are "cause for concern", a senior scientist told IRIN.
Many countries in the region, such as Swaziland and Lesotho, were now entering their fourth year of drought. According to a new Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS NET) report, the 2004/05 season "has been marked by adverse conditions, including erratic rains, intermittent dry spells, and flooding in some areas".

Original_Search_Title: Maputo Governor Visits Namaacha
Headline: Mozambique: Maputo Governor Visits Namaacha
Body_Text:
Namaacha — The governor of the southern Mozambican province of Maputo, Telmina
Pereira, on Thursday expressed her pleasant surprise at the "noteworthy"
economic undertakings in the district of Namaacha, on the border with
Swaziland.
During her visit to the district, Pereira commended the initiative to
introduce alternative crops to face persistent drought and the creation of
small agro-industries by local and foreign business people, that help reduce
the levels of unemployment.

Original_Search_Title: Petauke MP Appeals for Relief Food
Headline: Zambia: Petauke MP Appeals for Relief Food
Body_Text:
PETAUKE Central Member of Parliament Ronald Banda has said the majority
of the people in his constituency will need food assistance because of poor
yields due to bad weather the area experienced.
Mr Banda said in an interview yesterday that due to inadequate rains
people would require to be assisted with relief food to cushion the effect
of looming hunger.

Original_Search_Title: A Clash of Cultures in Wilton Sankawulo's
Headline: Liberia: Book Review: A Clash of Cultures in Wilton Sankawulo's
Body_Text:
Monrovia —  Sundown At Dawn: A Liberian Odyssey
Nvasekie N. Konneh suggests that the latest novel by the former Liberian
head of state and literary icon is remarkably different from his previous
works and comes across as protest despite showcasing a clash of cultures:
African versus European.

Original_Search_Title: Easterners Don't Need Relief Food
Headline: Zambia: Easterners Don't Need Relief Food
Body_Text:
THE National Technical Committee assessing the Food Security Pack in
Eastern Province says the region does not require relief food imports
because dry spells experienced in some districts have not depleted food
stocks.
Speaking in a joint statement on the final leg of a seven-day tour of
the province on Tuesday, the committee comprising experts from Government
ministries, implementing agencies and non-governmental organisations (NGOs)
said some districts had enough food stocks that could be redistributed to
deficit areas.

Original_Search_Title: Ondo Govt Slams Critics
Headline: Nigeria: Ondo Govt Slams Critics
Body_Text:
Akure — ONDO State government yesterday flayed what it called willful and
destructive criticism against the administration of Dr Olusegun Agagu by the
Alliance of Democracy (AD) in the state. Instead, the government said that
the party should seek how to contribute meaningfully and constructively to
the development of the state.
The state's Information Commissioner, Prince Ola Mafo, at a press
conference, said that AD comment on the governor's foreign trips was a
blackmail, adding that it was out of place.

Original_Search_Title: Benue, Nasarawa, Taraba Farmers Meet Over Food Security
Headline: Nigeria: Benue, Nasarawa, Taraba Farmers Meet Over Food Security
Body_Text:
Makurdi, the capital of Benue State, recently played host to 1000
farmers from Benue, Nasarawa and Taraba states who had converged to appraise
their performance in their profession and identify its problems with a view
to solving them over a planned period of time.
The convergence was courtesy of Olam Nigeria Limited, a multi-national
developer and processor of various agricultural produce under the cash crop
category. The event also saw the official re-opening of agro-miller, one of
the country's largest rice processing plant.

Original_Search_Title: 'Knowledge, Most Important for Profitable Export'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Knowledge, Most Important for Profitable Export'
Body_Text:
Lagos —  The Programme Director of Multimix Academy, Mr Madu Obiora, explains
in this interview that the biggest liability to an exporter is ignorance. He
spoke with Crusoe Osagie
What motivated you to venture into export training and consulting?

Original_Search_Title: Govt Moving Maize to Deficit Areas - Sikatana
Headline: Zambia: Govt Moving Maize to Deficit Areas - Sikatana
Body_Text:
Lusaka — GOVERNMENT is constantly moving maize from surplus areas to deficit
areas, agriculture minister Mundia Sikatana has said.
During a rally in Kafue on Saturday, Sikatana urged the people not be
deceived into believing that President Mwanawasa's administration would
abandon them in this period of food deficit which the country is currently
experiencing.

Original_Search_Title: Invest in Food Production, Farmers Urged
Headline: Zambia: Invest in Food Production, Farmers Urged
Body_Text:
THE national technical committee monitoring the food security pack in Eastern Province has urged peasant farmers to invest in their livelihood to safeguard the nation from unfavorable climate.
Speaking during the continued tour of projects in Chadiza, Mambwe, Lundazi and Chama districts, group leader Gregory Mwanza said peasant farmers should seriously treat agriculture as a business rather than engage in it as a by-the-way matter.

Original_Search_Title: Testing a Quasi Democracy
Headline: Sierra Leone: Testing a Quasi Democracy
Body_Text:
In 1997, a military regime, in friendly combat with the Revolutionary
United Front (RUF) rebels of Foday Sankoh took over power, exactly nine
months after the elected government of President Kabbah had begun a new
democratic leadership.
Nine months later, the coup was over turned using Nigerian soldiers and
Canadian money in return for diamond concessions; a fact few Sierra Leoneans
know.

Original_Search_Title: High Fuel Costs Hike Food Prices
Headline: Uganda: High Fuel Costs Hike Food Prices
Body_Text:
Kampala — Food prices have sharply increased in all leading markets causing a scare among market vendors.
Traders and vendors who talked to The Monitor attributed the increasing food prices to the recent rise in the fuel pump prices. The vendors said high transport fares has forced food suppliers to hike lorry prices for food items being delivered to markets.

Original_Search_Title: `Establish National Farms' - LIHEDE Boss Proposes
Headline: Liberia: `Establish National Farms' - LIHEDE Boss Proposes
Body_Text:
The Executive Director of the Liberian History Education and
Development (LIHEDE) based in the United States says his organization
has made several proposals that will improve the lives of Liberians.
Among those proposals include the establishment of national farms.
Dr. Syrulwa Somah suggested the establishment of national farms in
each county for the purpose of producing more food such as rice,
cassava, sweet potatoes, plantain, eddoes and banana.

Original_Search_Title: Rice And Liberia: A Failed Marriage From the Start
Headline: Liberia: Rice And Liberia: A Failed Marriage From the Start
Body_Text:
Monrovia —  The riots of April 14, 1979 will forever be etched in the history of
Liberia as a time when businesses were looted, scores wounded and a few
killed over the increase in the price of rice. Daily Observer
Editor-in-Chief, Rodney D. Sieh explains why the West African nation's
staple food has been the source of many of its troubles and why in
particular, things may never be the same.
Until that fateful day in 1979, Liberia was enjoying the kind of
stability only few could boast of in Africa. But an increase in the price of
rice changed it all, for the worse. It was two days shy of one year later:
April 12 1980. Samuel Doe, a 29-year-old master sergeant led 17
non-commissioned officers in a bloody coup d'etat that led to the overthrow
of President William R. Tolbert. Doe went on to execute Tolbert and most of
his high-ranking government officials, sweeping out any remnants of the old
government and ushering Liberia's first dictator.

Original_Search_Title: Promoting Agricultural Production in the New Liberia
Headline: Liberia: Promoting Agricultural Production in the New Liberia
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Dr. Syrulwa Somah believes that any nation that cannot feed its people
but relies on other nations for basic food supplies has no national pride,
and it is now time for Liberia to restore its national pride through
agricultural production.
Liberia has abundant fertile soil for the growing of bananas, rice,
plantain, bitter ball, cassava, Malaguatta pepper, mushroom, coffee, kola,
cocoa, mango, okra, palm nuts, papaya, pepper, yams, eddoes, sweet potatoes,
greens, and other basic agricultural products consumed in Liberian homes,
cities, towns, and villages everyday. But it is sad that successive
governments in our nation saw the massive importation of rice and other
basic commodities as more than a national effort aimed at increased
agricultural production in Liberia. Previous agricultural production schemes
such as the Green Revolution Program under President Doe and the Green
Revolution Program under President Tolbert's five to ten years food
self-sufficiency plan yielded limited or no tangible results in easing
Liberia's dependency on foreign nations for the supply of rice, the national
staple. Nevertheless, I believe that any nation that cannot feed its people
but relies on other nations for basic food supplies but has no national
pride, and it is now time for Liberia to restore its national pride through
agricultural production. I believe that agricultural production is the
lifeline Liberia and every national leader in the new Liberia would do well
to make agricultural production a national priority.

Original_Search_Title: U.S. to Forge Closer Ties With Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: U.S. to Forge Closer Ties With Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — United States (US) Ambassador to Nigeria, Mr. John Campbell, has said that American government will not only assist both the Federal and state governments towards 2007 elections, but will also have a closer and richer relationship with the Niger Delta region.
He, however, did not disclose the form of assistance US will render to Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Why NPP Failed to Win Manya Krobo Seat
Headline: Ghana: Why NPP Failed to Win Manya Krobo Seat
Body_Text:
Odumase-Krobo —  ... Lazy DCE, corrupt activists cited in post-mortem findings
Nearly four months after the New Patriotic Party (NPP) failed to win any
of the three parliamentary seats in the Krobo area of the eastern region in
the last general elections, the Lower Manya Krobo Constituency branch of the
party, still licking its wounds, is yet to come to terms with how the
National Democratic Congress (NDC)'s Michael Teye Nyaunu was able to floor
them for the third consecutive time, despite the gem of candidates it had
presented every successive election year.

Original_Search_Title: Educator Prescribes Capsules for Liberia
Headline: Liberia: Educator Prescribes Capsules for Liberia
Body_Text:
A Liberian educator based in the United States has advanced several
proposals which, he believes, will significantly help alleviate the chronic
hardships in the country.
In an interview with the Analyst in Monrovia Saturday, Dr. Syrulwa
Somah, executive director of the Liberia History, Education and Development,
Inc. based in Greensboro, North Carolina, United States of America, said
Liberia has abundant natural resources and these can be exploited to yield
self sufficiency in food production and economic prosperity.

Original_Search_Title: HIV/Aids More Serious in Rural Women
Headline: Zambia: HIV/Aids More Serious in Rural Women
Body_Text:
IN one way or the other, the HIV/AIDS pandemic has affected almost everybody in Zambia regardless of social status, age or sex. It is said that you are either infected or affected.
Despite the coming of anti-retroviral drugs (ARVs) and the various HIV/AIDS intervention programmes being carried out by Government and non-governmental organisations (NGOs) people in rural areas are still going through the devastating effects of the pandemic.

Original_Search_Title: SADC Genetic Resources Centre Seeks Funds to Increase Capacity
Headline: Southern Africa: SADC Genetic Resources Centre Seeks Funds to Increase Capacity
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THE Southern African Development Community Plant Genetic Resources
Centre (SPGRC) is sourcing funds to increase the capacity of its
biotechnology laboratory facilities.
SPGRC acting director Charles Nkhoma yesterday said this was aimed at
enabling the existing laboratory to handle more species.

Original_Search_Title: Udenwa Tasks Imo Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Udenwa Tasks Imo Farmers
Body_Text:
Lagos — FARMERS in Imo State have been charged to redouble their efforts this year, to ensure bumper food production for the people of the state and beyond.
The state governor, Chief Achike Udenwa gave the charge, while flagging off the state's 2005 cropping season at Isinweke, the Ihitte-Uboma local government headquarters.

Original_Search_Title: IITA Trains NRCRI Staff On Cassava Utilisation
Headline: Nigeria: IITA Trains NRCRI Staff On Cassava Utilisation
Body_Text:
Lagos — A total of 16 senior members of staff of the National Root Crops Research Insti-tute (NRCRI), Umudike, Abia State, have benefited from a two-day capacity building training workshop on cassava utilisation organised by IITA, at its High Rainfall Station, Onne, near Port Harcourt.
The participants who were drawn from Women In Agriculture (WIA), and the Post Harvest Units of NRCRI, were expected to commence training of rural women and micro-processors in different localities in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Develop Other Assets, Obasanjo Tells Oil States
Headline: Nigeria: Develop Other Assets, Obasanjo Tells Oil States
Body_Text:
Warri — President Olusegun Obasanjo has urged governors of oil rich states of the Niger Delta to take advantage of their abundant petroleum resources now, by using proceeds from oil and gas to develop renewable assets, since oil mineral would certainly get exhausted in the near future,
Rounding off his three-day official visit to Delta State at the weekend, obasanjo expressed delight at the gigantic projects commissioned during the visit, and advised the state governemnt to develop agriculture, manufacturing and other non-oil sectors that could step up the productive capacity of the people and provide gainful employment to the large army of unenmployed citizens of the state.

Original_Search_Title: Preparing Akwa Ibom for Industrial Take-Off
Headline: Nigeria: Preparing Akwa Ibom for Industrial Take-Off
Body_Text:
Rising from its long years of slumber as a civil service state, Akwa Ibom State under Governor Victor Attah presently appears to be in a hurry to tansform itself into an industrial haven through accelerated infrastructural development and urban modernisation. Judith Ufford, Features Editor, who visited parts of the state recently, reports.
THERE is something unique about Ibom Plaza. Alone, it stands out as the first of its kind in the history of city modernisation in Nigeria. Located in the centre of Uyo the capital of Akwa Ibom State, it is a sprawling enclave of sassy shops, water fountains, beautiful bougainvillaea and rose flowers, a viewing centre with wide-screen TV, sparkling walkways and overhead bridges and strategically located park seats. It is like the famous Trafalgar Square in London's central shopping district or somewhere in the centre of flower-crazy Amsterdam. To go straight to the point: Ibom Plaza is a beautiful place. Indeed, like a work of art, it is an architectural delight with the imprint of a genius.

Original_Search_Title: Insecurity Creates Food Shortages in Ituri
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: DRC: Insecurity Creates Food Shortages in Ituri
Body_Text:
Bunia — Once renowned for its food production, Djugu, a territory in Ituri, is rapidly degenerating into a food-deficit zone, according to local agriculture experts and UN agencies.
On the surface, Ituri - a district in northeastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) twice the size of Rwanda - is as green and fertile as ever. The rainy season has just begun and huge rain clouds that build up early in the afternoon are followed by heavy downpours, thunder and lightning.

Original_Search_Title: In the Land of Beer And Piety
Headline: Nigeria: In the Land of Beer And Piety
Body_Text:
Lagos — Raheem Akingbola who just returned from Plateau State writes on the Mwanghavul and Pyem people of Mangu Local Government and their love for both religion and local beer.
