Here, there are two definitions of life: It's an opportunity to serve God and it's also a place created by God as an avenue for its inhabitants to celebrate their living. Everybody goes about his or her business with radiant smiles and cheery looks. The predominant occupation of Mwanghavul and Pyem (pronounced as Fiam) people is farming. The climatic conditions of the place naturally makes them producers of food crops such as; maize, irish potatoes, beans sorghum, cassava, rice, acha, beans, millet, cocoa yam, palm oil, tomatoes, onions, cabbage, carrots etc. For the average man or woman in any of the communities, the daily programme is constant and unchanging; pray, work and enjoy your local beer.

Original_Search_Title: American Biotechnology Expert To Hold Workshops In Tanzania
Headline: Tanzania: American Biotechnology Expert To Hold Workshops In Tanzania
Publisher: United States Department of State (Washington, DC)
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam —  · Minister Keenja to officiate in Dar
A US expert on biotechnology is visiting Tanzania to discuss with
Tanzanian agriculture stakeholders the application of advances in
biotechnology in the agricultural sector.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Benefits From Agric Technical Support
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Benefits From Agric Technical Support
Body_Text:
Bauchi — Bauchi State Government is to benefit from technical support from the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development on cassava production.
It would also benefit from any assistance rendered by the Root and Tuber Expansion Programme, towards improving the state food production.

Original_Search_Title: Building Infrastructure for Empowerment
Headline: Nigeria: Building Infrastructure for Empowerment
Body_Text:
Lagos — Lately, anybody visiting Akwa Ibom State will marvel at the level of construction going on in several parts of the state. Donald Andoor, who was in the state recently, writes that it is becoming a huge construction site
There is something unique about Ibom Plaza. Alone, it stands out as the first of its kind in the history of city modernisation in Nigeria. Located in the centre of Uyo the capital of Akwa Ibom State, it is a sprawling enclave of classy shops, water fountains, beautiful bougainvillaea and rose flowers, a viewing centre with wide-screen TV, sparkling walkways and overhead bridges. It's strategically located park seats makes it look like the famous Trafalgar Square in London's central shopping district or somewhere in Amsterdam.

Original_Search_Title: Rumor of Arms Container Causes Stir
Headline: Liberia: Rumor of Arms Container Causes Stir
Body_Text:
Monrovia — As citizens are yet to overcome the apprehension and fear about the
past war years, every unusual incident tends to cause grave alarm as
was the case of a truck belonging to the Bridgeway Corporation, that
arrived at the Paynesville Red-light early morning at a very high
speed.
The truck bearing license plate number 037 with series number 2638
and carrying a 20-foot container labeled BCC-1-20, driven by one Musa
Kamara created serious suspicion by bye-passing the Mt. Barclay check
point at an excessive speed. The situation caused the UNMIL and
Liberian security personnel as well as residents and marketers to
become stunned with suspicion.

Original_Search_Title: FG Has Provided N14b for Ada Rice Project - Nnamani
Headline: Nigeria: FG Has Provided N14b for Ada Rice Project - Nnamani
Body_Text:
Enugu — GOVERNOR Chimaroke Nnamani of Enugu State says the Federal Government
has provided about N1.4 billion in this year's budget for the rehabilitation
of Ada Rice Project at Adani in Ogboli Local Government Development
Council. About N16 million was spent on the rice project under the National
Food Security Programme last year.
Disclosing this while addressing the people of Ogboli and Adada Local
Government Development Council in continuation of his meet the people tour
and inspection of on-going development projects in parts of the state, the
governor said that the greater chunk of the pilot projects being embarked
upon by his administration in partnership with the donor agencies were being
executed in the various communities in Nsukka area of the state.

Original_Search_Title: DCE Exonerated of Blowing of ¢billions in Ykda
Headline: Ghana: DCE Exonerated of Blowing of ¢billions in Ykda
Body_Text:
- Anti-crusaders urged to smoke peace-pipe
AS PRESIDENT JOHN Agyekum Kufuor is about to name the District Chief
Executives (DCEs) to join the board of Positive Change Chapter Two, one of
the DCEs who is likely to be rejoicing and hoping for re-appointment is,
Hon. Christian Kofi Tetteh of the Yilo Krobo District in the Eastern Region,
following his exoneration from accusations of 'blowing billions of cedis.'

Original_Search_Title: Lamentations of a Serenje Peasant Farmer
Headline: Zambia: Lamentations of a Serenje Peasant Farmer
Body_Text:
- as imminent drought sends shivers
BARELY two years of that drastic drought in Zambia, the country is this
year experiencing yet another similar fate.

Original_Search_Title: A Drought-Prone Country Turns to Cassava
Headline: Zambia: A Drought-Prone Country Turns to Cassava
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Stung by the threat of a poor maize harvest, the Zambian government has
urged farmers to turn to cassava production in the drought-prone southern
and central regions.
According to agriculture experts, cassava is hardier than maize because
it can grow in dry conditions and does not require large doses of
fertiliser.

Original_Search_Title: Rice And Liberia: a Failed Marriage From the Start
Headline: Liberia: Rice And Liberia: a Failed Marriage From the Start
Body_Text:
Monrovia — The riots of April 14, 1979 will forever be etched in the history of Liberia as a time when businesses were looted, scores wounded and a few killed over the increase in the price of rice. Daily Observer Editor-in-Chief, Rodney D. Sieh explains why the West African nation's staple food has been the source of many of its troubles and why in particular, things may never be the same.

It was two days shy of one year later: April 12 1980. Samuel Doe, a 29-year-old master sergeant led 17 non-commissioned officers in a bloody coup d'etat that led to the overthrow of President William R. Tolbert. Doe went on to execute Tolbert and most of his high-ranking government officials, sweeping out any remnants of the old government and ushering Liberia's first dictator.

Original_Search_Title: Traditional Ruler Provides Leadership in Urban Farming
Headline: Nigeria: Traditional Ruler Provides Leadership in Urban Farming
Body_Text:
Lagos — Bennett Oghifo, in this piece, follows a natural ruler's love of urban farming and community development.
For natural rulers, administering the affairs of a kingdom is quite demanding, and this is worsened by an itinerary which spreads from cockcrow to sunset.

Original_Search_Title: Implement Recommended to Cassava Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Implement Recommended to Cassava Farmers
Body_Text:
Lagos — International Institute of Tropical Agriculture has strongly recommended the use of a hand-pushed power tiller for small and medium scale farmers to reduce cost of cassava production in Nigeria.
This was the outcome of a one-day meeting between Directors of the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and scientists at the IITA-Onne Station in Rivers State.

Original_Search_Title: Tanzania to Conduct Field-Trials of GM Cotton
Headline: Tanzania to Conduct Field-Trials of GM Cotton
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tanzania has joined Tunisia, Zimbabwe, Egypt, Burkina Faso and Kenya in conducting confined field trials (CFT) for genetically modified crops that will eventually open its doors to genetically modified organisms (GMOs).
A Cabinet paper on GMO policy has already been prepared and parliament is scheduled to debate and approve the approach towards GMO technologies mid this year, according to Dr Jeremiah Haki, Director of Research at the Ministry of Agricultural and Food Security.

Original_Search_Title: FG Expresses Determination to Diversify Economy
Headline: Nigeria: FG Expresses Determination to Diversify Economy
Body_Text:
Lagos — President Olusegun Obasanjo has said that his administration will not relent in its effort to diversify the economy.
Speaking shortly after receiving his membership card of the Nigeria Cassava Growers Association in his Ota farm today, Obasanjo said that the government was doing a lot to improve agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: Genetically Modified Crops . . . Food Or Poison?
Headline: Nigeria: Genetically Modified Crops . . . Food Or Poison?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Scientists and laymen alike are agreed that bio-technology is a branch of science that is useful to mankind. However, the worry is that genetically modified organisms (GMOs), which are by-product of bio-technology used to enhance agricultural production, has generated so much controversy across the world. Godwin Haruna writes
The conference room of the Treasure Exclusive Hotel, Lagos last week was packed full with delegates from across the world. Any of the delegates that rose to speak on the issue of the moment did so with so much passion either for or against. The issue of genetically modified organisms (GMOs) has become a touchy subject for the rest of the world. This emotional attachment to GMO issues was replicated when Environmental Rights Action/Friends of the Earth (ERA/FoE) teamed up with Third World Network to organise a two-day conference titled: 'GMOs and Africa.'

Original_Search_Title: Promoting Agricultural Production in the New Liberia
Headline: Liberia: Promoting Agricultural Production in the New Liberia
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Dr. Syrulwa Somah believes that any nation that cannot feed its people but relies on other nations for basic food supplies has no national pride, and it is now time for Liberia to restore its national pride through agricultural production.
Liberia has abundant fertile soil for the growing of bananas, rice, plantain, bitter ball, cassava, Malaguatta pepper, mushroom, coffee, kola, cocoa, mango, okra, palm nuts, papaya, pepper, yams, eddoes, sweet potatoes, greens, and other basic agricultural products consumed in Liberian homes, cities, towns, and villages everyday. But it is sad that successive governments in our nation saw the massive importation of rice and other basic commodities as more than a national effort aimed at increased agricultural production in Liberia. Previous agricultural production schemes such as the Green Revolution Program under President Doe and the Green Revolution Program under President Tolbert's five to ten years food self-sufficiency plan yielded limited or no tangible results in easing Liberia's dependency on foreign nations for the supply of rice, the national staple. Nevertheless, I believe that any nation that cannot feed its people but relies on other nations for basic food supplies but has no national pride, and it is now time for Liberia to restore its national pride through agricultural production. I believe that agricultural production is the lifeline Liberia and every national leader in the new Liberia would do well to make agricultural production a national priority.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Aids Wakiso Fight Against Poverty
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Aids Wakiso Fight Against Poverty
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has given Wakiso district sh104m to help fight poverty.
Speaking at celebrations to mark the water week at Gombe sub-county headquarters, district chairman Ian Kyeyune said the money would be used to purchase beans, maize and cassava stems for planting.

Original_Search_Title: Experts Charge Nigerians On Cassava Production
Headline: Nigeria: Experts Charge Nigerians On Cassava Production
Body_Text:
Yola — Researchers and processors have been challenged to be in the forefront
in Nigeria's renewed effort in massive farming and processing of
cassava.
The Special Assistant to the President on Food Security, Mrs. Olawatoyin
Adetunji, threw the challenge recently while addressing stakeholders in food
production during a sensitization and training workshop on production and
packaging of high qualities of cassava flower by local farmers and
processors in country held in Yola, Adamawa state capital.

Original_Search_Title: Tasks Ahead of Edem At the NDDC
Headline: Nigeria: Tasks Ahead of Edem At the NDDC
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Following the Act which set up the Niger Delta Development Commission,
NDDC, in 2000, part 1 section 4 of it states that the office of the chairman
shall be rotated amongst member states in an alphabetical order, a new
Chairman for the Commission, Ambassador Sam Joe Edem, was recently screened
and confirmed by the senate after a rigorous process, reports Efem Nkanga
The new helmsman of the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC),
Ambassador Sam Joe Edem, from Nsit Ubuim Local Government Area of Akwa Ibom
State, is no doubt an accomplished civil servant with an international
exposure as a career diplomat.

Original_Search_Title: Osoba's Claim On Ogun Elections Frivolous - Malaolu
Headline: Nigeria: Osoba's Claim On Ogun Elections Frivolous - Malaolu
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Two years after the 2003 governorship election in State, the outcome
of the polls is still a subject of dispute between the immediate past
Governor, Aremo Olusegun Osoba and the incumbent, Otunba Gbenga Daniel. In
this interview with Tokunbo Adedoja, State Commissioner for Information and
Strategy, Mr. Niran Malaolu bares his mind on the2003 election and several
other issues
From the news room to government ministry, how has it been?

Original_Search_Title: Detective Thriller Called Owo Eje
Headline: Nigeria: Detective Thriller Called Owo Eje
Body_Text:
Lagos — When Remdel Optimum Communications invited journalists to special
premiere of its latest flick, Owo Eje, a screen retelling of Kola Akinlade's
Yoruba novel of the same title, many, thought its another Nollywood pictures
with gory details of how dubious characters make money through ritual
killing. However, Owo Eje which literally means "blood money" proved a crime
block buster and a cherished detective delight.
Forget the straight-jacket plots of the American John Grishams, the
meandering scenarios of the Agatha Christies or the stunt-like tales of the
Ian Flamings, Owo Eje, which emanates from the pen of Yoruba scholar, Kola
Akinlade whose other fictional works include Sangba Fo!, Ta L'ole Ajomogbe?,
Tunde Ati Awon Ore Re, and Yemi Da Bira among others, is an intriguing tale
of blackmail, drama, suspense, tension, 'power show', intrigues and counter
intrigues that trailed investigations into the mysterious murder of a farmer
of another ethnic extraction in a Yoruba community.

Original_Search_Title: Country Facing Yet Another Food Crisis
Headline: Malawi: Country Facing Yet Another Food Crisis
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Yet another food crisis is looming in drought-prone Malawi this year
following a dry spell in the country's south and central regions.
Mzimba district in the northern region is one such area which did not
receive any rainfall from the second week of February until the middle of
March, when the young maize crop had already withered.

Original_Search_Title: Taiwanese Investors Eye Delta State
Headline: Nigeria: Taiwanese Investors Eye Delta State
Body_Text:
Asaba — A team of Taiwanese investors has arrived Asaba, Delta State capital to explore and exploit the huge investment opportunities in the state.
The team led by the chairman Senior Experts Development Association of the Island State Mr. Eugene Chen said their focus while in the state would be in the three key areas of agriculture, mining and textile.

Original_Search_Title: Recurrent Droughts - What Do We Do?
Headline: Zambia: Recurrent Droughts - What Do We Do?
Body_Text:
Lusaka — WEATHER prediction is not an exact science. The weather prediction this
season has let many farmers down, especially the 800,000 smallholder farmers
who fully depend on rain-fed agriculture.
Emerging and commercial farmers have not been spared either. The waste
of seed and fertiliser could have been prevented if this science was
exact. Much of this seed and fertiliser will be paid for by the taxpayer, as
a number of smallholder farmers accessed inputs from the 50 per cent
subsidised Fertiliser Support Programme and the free Programme Against
Malnutrition Food Pack. The continuous song is that Zambia has the potential
to become a giant in agriculture and its economy could be almost completely
agriculture-based. Yet the country's agricultural performance is largely at
the mercy of the weather. No amount of fertiliser or improved seed will give
a good yield in a water-deficient environment. Crop failure also affects
livestock production as livestock feed comes from crops and plants.

Original_Search_Title: Sorghum Farmers Start Reaping Big Dividends
Headline: Uganda: Sorghum Farmers Start Reaping Big Dividends
Body_Text:
Kampala — Life may never be the same again for Charles Erimu, LC3 chairman Atiira
sub-county, Soroti district.
Three years ago, under a scheme promoted by Nile Breweries Limited
(NBL), he started growing sorghum for use in brewering the new Eagle beer
brands.

Original_Search_Title: Forestry Authority Evicts 16,000 Farmers
Headline: Uganda: NFA Evicts 16,000
Body_Text:
Kasese — Over 16,000 people in the district may starve following an eviction order by the National Forestry Authority (NFA) from Mubuku Forest Reserve.
The area district councillor, Mr Saulo Maate, said on Monday that NFA recently issued an eviction order to about 16,000 farmers who had been using the forest since 1988.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Within Japanese Foreign Policy: A Basic Outline
Headline: Ghana Within Japanese Foreign Policy: A Basic Outline
Body_Text:
Relations between Ghana and Japan appear to have taken a dramatic
improvement on the already cordial and fraternal interactions that have
existed since the days of independence.
The reasons for this improvement are myriad, ranging from the personal
style of the immediate past Japanese Ambassador to Ghana, Her Excellency
Mrs. Kazuko Asai right to the commitment of the Koizumi-led government to
promote Japanese foreign policy through increased support and interaction
with developing countries.

Original_Search_Title: Ghanaians Overseas Asked to Increase Contribution to Economic Development
Headline: Ghanaians Overseas Asked to Increase Contribution to Economic Development
Body_Text:
Ghanaians in the Diaspora have been called upon to take advantage of the
favourable political climate at home to contribute their quota towards
socio-economic development.
Mr. Alfred Sallia Fawundu, former UNDP Resident Representative in Guinea
Bissau, Benin and Ghana, said Ghana had set the pace for national
development and Ghanaians abroad should therefore mobilise both human and
material resources to invest in various productive ventures to create jobs
for the large army of unemployed youth.

Original_Search_Title: Bugema Farm Defies Drought
Headline: Uganda: Bugema Farm Defies Drought
Body_Text:
Kampala —  -The university has used irrigation to beat the debilitating effects of
weather
As the country struggles out of one of the worst dry spells in recent
years, Bugema University farm stands unscathed by the drought.

Original_Search_Title: Fresh Pasture Kills Kiboga Cattle
Headline: Uganda: Fresh Pasture Kills Kiboga Cattle
Body_Text:
Kampala — Moses Rwabuzaire, a native of Kyankwanzi village in Kiboga district, was
happy and prosperous with more than 100 in his kraal.
However, Rwabuzaire is now a worried man. During the drought he lost
about three cows daily to starvation. Now that the eastern part of Kiboga
has at least had some rains, the new pastures are causing death to the
cattle. The animals get diarrhoea and simply waste away.

Original_Search_Title: FG to Explore Cassava Potentials
Headline: Nigeria: FG to Explore Cassava Potentials
Body_Text:
Umuahia — THE presidential initiative on cassava production has mapped out a programme that would make cassava a major foreign exchange earner for the country. National coordinator of cassava mosaic disease project, Dr Emeh Okoro said in Umuahia on Monday that his committee has already started a sensitization tour of all cassava producing states of the country with a view to persuading them to pay the necessary counterpart funds that would make it possible for them to be part of the programme.
He said that although 11 states were initially targeted for the programme, more states, including some from the northern part of the country have indicated interest to join the cassava producing states.

Original_Search_Title: What is Happening to Mr. President's Cassava Project?
Headline: Nigeria: What is Happening to Mr. President's Cassava Project?
Body_Text:
Lagos —  The Presidential Committee on Cassava Export Promotion was inaugurated
July 2004 by President Obasanjo. Chaired by Commerce Minister Idris Waziri,
the committee has toured Nigeria's six geopolitical zones twice. First to
sensitise stakeholders on the project itself and next to help form an apex
cassava body. Now a constitution is being drafted for the sector, a star
NEPAD project.
ETIM IMISIM writes that Nigerians have a handle to hold government
accountable for its promise: to make cassava fetch the national economy more
than crude oil is doing.

Original_Search_Title: Drought Leaves Maize Fields Dry
Headline: Zambia: Drought Leaves Maize Fields Dry
Body_Text:
Serenje — DROUGHT has adversely affected most parts of Serenje rural where maize
fields have dried up due to the scorching heat.
Maize stocks from the previous season have also run out as villagers
complain of hunger.

Original_Search_Title: Standards: Minister Canvasses More Responsiveness
Headline: Nigeria: Standards: Minister Canvasses More Responsiveness
Body_Text:
Lagos — National standards bodies in Africa have been charged to be more responsive to the material and human needs of the Africa Organization for Standardization (ARSO), the umbrella body for Standards Institutes in the continent in order to empower it to meet the desires of its stakeholders.
The Minsiter of industry, Ambassador Magaji Muhammed who gave this charge recently in a goodwill messega presented at the opening of the 13th General Assemble of ARSO held in Accra, Ghana, stated that the body should be dynamic and proactive to play an effective role of coordination and facilitation of standardization activities in the continent.

Original_Search_Title: 'FIIRO Set to Meet 2005 Cassava Bread Production Target'
Headline: Nigeria: 'FIIRO Set to Meet 2005 Cassava Bread Production Target'
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Director General of the Federal Institute of Industrial Research Oshodi (FIIRO), Dr Oluwole Olatunji, has stated that the institute is set to meet the 2005 deadline, which the Federal Government has set for the production of cassava/wheat composite flour for bread making in the country.
Olatunji explained that necessary modalities were in place to ensure the full take-off of cassava wheat bread production this year.

Original_Search_Title: A Uniquely Savage War
Headline: Uganda: A Uniquely Savage War
Body_Text:
Nairobi —  David Kaiza goes to northern Uganda where the government has herded 1.6
million people into IDP camps where starvation and sickness rule
AT THE PAJULE THERAPEUTIC Feeding Centre in northern Uganda, 43-year-old
Sylvia Alwoc sits nursing her one-year-old granddaughter and orphan, Winnie
Akano. Winnie is emaciated - her upper arms no larger than her grandmother's
thumb, her skin as wrinkled as that of a person twice her grandmother's
age.

Original_Search_Title: Liberia is At a Brink of Irreversible Environmental/Ecological Impotency
Headline: Liberia is At a Brink of Irreversible Environmental/Ecological Impotency
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Dr. Syrulwa Somah says as Liberia looks forward to the new coming sweeping moon and more hopeful future in 2005 and a 4th republic, the nation must be reminded that the environment is in a grave danger unless an appropriate or effective and efficient integrated system of environmental management is implemented.
Since the dawn of creation, there has always been interaction between human beings and the environment. This was in certain circumstances a one-way action (man effecting the environment) while in other cases it was an interaction (such as our parentage whose interaction with the earth's "fullness" resulted in their working together to gather food for both man and domesticated animals to eat). Those cultures that respect nature (such as the pre-Liberian culture), treating her with understanding, have exacted only a small environmental impact. Those cultures, however, preach domination of nature, imposing their human will upon the environment, have reaped the havoc of calamities that affect humanity's physical, spiritual and mental health, as well as humanity's social well being.

Original_Search_Title: Three Perish After Eating Cassava Leaves
Headline: Tanzania: Three Perish After Eating Cassava Leaves
Body_Text:
Arusha — Three children died last Friday night at Engarenaibor village of Monduli
district after a Spartan meal of cassava leaves with ugali.
According to Arusha Regional Police Commander (RPC), James Kombe, the
incident happened when the three children had supper prepared by their
mother.

Original_Search_Title: FG Steps Up Effort in SME's Industry
Headline: Nigeria: FG Steps Up Effort in SME's Industry
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — The Federal Government has urged leaders of various communities across
the country to make available spaces to be used as Information Centres where
documents and other information needed to set up Micro, Small and Medium
Enterprises (MSMES) can be kept for research purposes by the residents of
such communities.
The Director General of Small and Medium Enterprises Agency of Nigeria
(SMEDAN), Mrs Modupe Adelaja gave the charge while addressing residents of
Ibogun Olaogun in Ifo Local Government of Ogun State.

Original_Search_Title: N150m Approved for Technical Education
Headline: Nigeria: N150m Approved for Technical Education
Body_Text:
Lagos — Technical education in Ondo State got a boost on Tuesday, as the State
Government approved the sum of N150 million for the renovation and
procurement of equipment for its five Technical Colleges.
Also, the state government has signed a Memorandum of Under-standing
(MOU), with a private cassava processing firm which it paid N10 million
equity participation.

Original_Search_Title: Care International Helps Women Build Their Capacities
Headline: Zambia: Care International Helps Women Build Their Capacities
Body_Text:
THE nations of the world could have celebrated this year's International Women's Day in style but it still remains a fact that there is need to do more to promote the welfare of women.
Government as well as other stakeholders need to build the capacity for women to sustain their activities and be able to play a leading role in the development of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Burundi Will Need 40 Per Cent More Emergency Food This Year, UN Agencies Say
Headline: Burundi Will Need 40 Per Cent More Emergency Food This Year, UN Agencies Say
Body_Text:
Burundi, recovering from a long civil conflict, will need 40 per cent
more emergency food this year than last because bad weather has reduced farm
output, doubling the prices of scarce staple foods, three United Nations
agencies and the Government of the central African nation said today.
"The country would face a total food deficit in 2005 of 310,000 metric
tons of food (in cereal equivalent) compared to 259,000 tons last year,
primarily because of unfavourable weather conditions since 2003, affecting
maize and bean yields," according to a joint assessment by the Food and
Agriculture Organization (FAO), the World Food Programme (WFP), the UN
Children's Fund (UNICEF) and the Government of Burundi.

Original_Search_Title: Drought Causes Hunger in Some Maputo Districts
Headline: Mozambique: Drought Causes Hunger in Some Maputo Districts
Body_Text:
Maputo — People are facing hunger in some districts
in Maputo province, particularly in Moamba, Magude, and Manhica,
where most people have lost their first season crops to drought,
reports Wednesday's issue of the daily paper "Noticias".
Maputo provincial governor Telmina Pereira said that these
people are also facing difficulties in acquiring stocks of seeds
for the second planting season, because they have difficulty
conserving seeds acquired in seed fairs for two seasons.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Embrace Animal Traction
Headline: Uganda: Farmers Embrace Animal Traction
Body_Text:
Kampala — FARMERS in the districts of Iganga, Kasese and Katakwi have acquired donkeys and oxen to ease their work and improve their agricultural output.
During a review of farmers' management of intermediary means of transport in Makututu sub-county, Iganga, a team of agriculturalists from FOODNET was impressed at the workload the animals could carry out.

Original_Search_Title: Mzee Nyeko's Mixed Farm Thrives in War-Torn North
Headline: Uganda: Mzee Nyeko's Mixed Farm Thrives in War-Torn North
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE dragging insurgency in northern Uganda has cut a swathe through agriculture.
Medium and large scale farming have been worst hit because vast fertile land in the villages can hardly be accessed.

Original_Search_Title: The Example From Ebebda
Headline: Cameroon: The Example From Ebebda
Body_Text:
Over 40 women groups acquire heavy duty machines and create community farm.
The rural women of Ebebda in the Lekie Division will tell the story better, that tilling the farm with machines is not like working with local tools. It was on March 1,2005 that the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Developments, Tchabat Clobert visited the little village of Djounyat located some two kilometres from Ebebda town to officially launch the national programme on mechanised farming. While there, he witnessed the zeal in the women who have constituted themselves into groups with the aim of producing more and improving the quality of their yield.

Original_Search_Title: Two Million People Face Food Shortage, UN Agency Says
Headline: Burundi: Two Million People Face Food Shortage, UN Agency Says
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two million Burundians would need emergency food aid this year, 40 percent more than in 2004, the UN World Food Programme (WFP) reported on Wednesday.
In a statement, the agency said a crop and food supply assessment report, jointly produced by WFP, the Food Aid Organization (FAO), the UN Children's Fund (UNICEF) and the Burundian government, concluded that the country would face a food deficit of 310,000 mt in 2005, compared to 259,000 mt in 2004, "primarily because of unfavourable weather conditions since 2003, affecting maize and bean yields".

Original_Search_Title: Floods On Chambeshi River Displace Scores
Headline: Zambia: Floods On Chambeshi River Displace Scores
Body_Text:
Lusaka — FLOODS on the Chambeshi river have displaced scores of people, Northern
Province deputy permanent secretary Gabriel Kaunda has said.
In an interview on Monday, Kaunda said the province had received above
normal rainfall, which had led to floods.

Original_Search_Title: 'A Little Wine for the Stomach'
Headline: Nigeria: 'A Little Wine for the Stomach'
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Director of the Centre for Biotechnology and Genetic Engineering, University of Jos (UNIJOS), Prof. Chike Innocent Chuks Ogbonna was the first Nigerian to pick a doctorate in Biotechnology and Industrial Microbiology from the University of Technology, Aston, England in 1980.
One of the national zonal centres for the study of biotechnology in Nigeria and a part of the National Centre for Biotechnology, Abuja, Ogbonna's centre gave a good account of itself during the first Nigerian Universities Research and Development Fair (NURESDEF), organised by the National Universities Commission (NUC) in Abuja, with a number of projects on display. Two of these have been featured on this page. The focus this week is on Ogbonna's project that was exhibited at the fair, Holy Communion and table wines.

Original_Search_Title: Fake Ampicillin Syrup Producers Nab Bed
Headline: Nigeria: Fake Ampicillin Syrup Producers Nab Bed
Body_Text:
Kano — Four persons have been arrested by the police in Kano in connection with the fake production of Ampicillin syrup for children.
Police sources confirmed that the four suspects produced the fake Ampicillin syrup by mixing custard powder with alubo (fermented but dried cassava powder) thereby causing harm to little children.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Sows, Reaps in East
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Sows, Reaps in East
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Museveni is introducing a wealth scheme, he code-named Bonna Bagaggawale, where people will access affordable credit
It is boiling hot. Thick dust hangs in the air. The skies are clear. It is perfect timing. The planting season is yet to come.

Original_Search_Title: Congolese Put Down Their Guitars, Pick Up the Jembe
Headline: Uganda: Congolese Put Down Their Guitars, Pick Up the Jembe
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ugandans used to look down on the Congolese as a lazy nation whose motto
was "more music, less work." Jokes and urban legends described a people so
full of inertia, they even demanded payment from those who tried to assist
them.
Che Guevara's Africa Diary, published a few years ago, talks of
guerrilla recruits who refused to carry their kits, telling the great
revolutionary that they were not lorries - "Mimi apana motoka."

Original_Search_Title: Ogun Empowers 16,000 Jobless Youths
Headline: Nigeria: Ogun Empowers 16,000 Jobless Youths
Body_Text:
AS part of its efforts to reduce the level of unemployment in the state, Ogun State government has empowered over 16,000 unemployed youths of various categories from the state employment generation progrmames, while over 60 per cent benefitted from the micro-credit loan facilities.
Special Adviser to Ogun State Governor on Employment Programme, Mrs. Maria Sokenu, disclosed this at the inauguration of the second phase of the employment generation programme tagged: "Graduate Entrepreneurship Scheme," brainchild of the Ogun State Employment Generation Programme (OGEGEP).

Original_Search_Title: Cassava - Answer to Zambia's Food Security?
Headline: Zambia: Cassava - Answer to Zambia's Food Security?
Body_Text:
AMAZING it was, to have a personal experience of harvesting 46 tonnes of cassava per hectare in one of the cassava trial fields in Lusaka rural.
Drought resistant, with a good root shape, disease free, easily propagated, adapts to poor soil, can be harvested all year round, no pesticides needed and ranging from sweet to bitter, the improved cassava varieties produce between 30 to 40 tonnes per hectare as compared to seven tonnes of maize for the same area.

Original_Search_Title: Pangs of Famine
Headline: Kenya: Pangs of Famine
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Although the government only recently sent out a warning about impending
famine in the country, acute food shortage is already a reality in many
parts of western Kenya. Even worse are the worrying food forecasts for next
year.
In many parts of the region, including traditional food belts, prices of
staple food commodities have shot up three-fold. Last year's drought and
this year's delayed rains have further compromised the food situation in
Western Province, one of the country's major food baskets. Residents now
fear a return of the hunger that ravaged the area in 1984. The hunger was
christened "Inzara yo mugorogoro" (Famine of the two-kilogramme tin).

Original_Search_Title: TPRI Jumps on GMO Bandwagon
Headline: Tanzania: TPRI Jumps on GMO Bandwagon
Body_Text:
Arusha — Tanzania says it cannot afford to be left behind in technologies that increase crop yields, reduce farm costs and increase farm profits, Arusha-based TPRI is pioneering GMO experiments.
With an ever-increasing global population and massive third world hunger, Africa has never been overly excited over genetically modified food. Instead, in many places, genetically modified food is treated as the greatest threat ever to human civilization.

Original_Search_Title: As Delta Government Goes Into Business...
Headline: Nigeria: As Delta Government Goes Into Business...
Body_Text:
Lagos —  At a time the Federal and some state governments are selling off some of
their companies and parastatals in line with the new regime of limited
government involvement in business, the decision of the Delta State
Government to enter into real business in addition to expanding its
investment portfolio may appear curious.
Tunde Rahman, in this report, writes that with its large army of
unemployed, some of whom are engaged in armed banditry in the name of
agitation for control of their resources, the decision by the state
government may create jobs and keep the 'boys' in check. The state's
Commissioner for Economic Planning, Mr. Clement Ofuani, a chartered
accountant and economist, the arrow head of the new initiative, also puts
the issue in perspective saying government's efforts is geared towards
employment generation and wealth creation

Original_Search_Title: 400,000 Refugees in Tanzania Face Malnutrition Due to Donor Shortfall, UN Agency
Headline: Tanzania: 400,000 Refugees in Tanzania Face Malnutrition Due to Donor Shortfall, UN Agency
Body_Text:
Some 400,000 mainly Congolese refugees in northwestern Tanzania will see
their food rations increase by 23 per cent as of Monday, but this is still
well below the standard 2,100 calories per person daily due to insufficient
donations and a food shortage in the region, the United Nations refugee
agency reported today.
"Refugees in our Tanzanian camps are already paying the price for a
dramatic shortfall in almost all foods - cereals, pulses, corn-soya blend,
vegetable oil and salt - due to inadequate funding by donors," UNHCR
spokesman Ron Redmond told a news briefing in Geneva, appealing to the world
community to increase their donations.

Original_Search_Title: Saraki Explains Kwara Govt's Plans for Excess Oil Revenue
Headline: Nigeria: Saraki Explains Kwara Govt's Plans for Excess Oil Revenue
Body_Text:
Kwara — THE Governor of Kwara State, Mr. Bukola Saraki, has said that he would
spend the states' share of the over N300 billion excess oil revenue, on
three priorities areas - water, rural electrification and agriculture.
Fielding journalists" questions after a meeting with the Governor of
Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Prof. Charles Soludo, in Abuja, yesterday,
Mr. Saraki, said he was determined to utilize the oil money on projects that
would directly impact on the lives of the people of his state.

Original_Search_Title: Kwara to Spend Oil Windfall On Water, Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Kwara to Spend Oil Windfall On Water, Agriculture
Body_Text:
Abuja — Kwara State Governor, Bukola Saraki, has disclosed that the oil windfall shared to the state will be applied to three main areas of need in the state namely, rural water scheme, rural electrification and agriculture.
Speaking with newsmen yesterday after paying a condolence visit to the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Professor Charles Soludo, on the loss of the bank's two members of staff with four others while on a mission to Kwara, Saraki said, "our own windfall is going to water, rural water scheme, rural electrification and also agriculture. Those are the two areas we are focusing on. We are paying attention to water to the extent that nobody will have to work more than one kilometre without water.

Original_Search_Title: MPs Commend Naads Programme to Farmers
Headline: Uganda: MPs Commend Naads Programme to Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — Farmers who have accessed farming technologies from the National
Agricultural Advisory Services (Naads) are now capable of handling
commercial farming.
Mr John Odit (Erute South), the Parliamentary Committee Chairman,
recommended that the programme be extended to all districts to equip farmers
with the technology to help them manage commercial farming.

Original_Search_Title: Buying up Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Buying up Nigeria
Body_Text:
On the issue of government and private sector, there can be no angel and
devil scenario.
LAST week Monday the logical process to the privatisation programme took
place in Abuja, when a group of well heeled Nigerian entrepreneurs presented
President Olusegun Obasanjo with their blue print to setup a mega
corporation, with an initial value of 14 billion Naira. According to the
leader of the team Mrs. Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke, which also had Festus Odimegwu,
Aliko Dangote, Jim Ovia and Jacob Moyo Ajekigbe, Otunba Funsho Lawal,
amongst others, the aim of the group is " borne out of President Obasanjo's
original idea of a mega corporation built by Nigerians and owned by all
Nigerians with the sole mission of creating a globally competitive
industrial and business conglomerate which will supply world class products
and services to local and international markets."

Original_Search_Title: Nnenyin Attah: a Passion for Poor Children
Headline: Nigeria: Nnenyin Attah: a Passion for Poor Children
Body_Text:
Lagos — Mrs. Nneyin Allison Attah, the wife of the Akwa Ibom State Governor is a woman known for her uncommon love for little children and women as well as the physically challenged in the state. Her pet project CDT, is the acronym for the Child Development Trust, a non-governmental organization initiated by her to solve the problem of malnutrition among little children in the state.
CDT was established in September of 1999 in response to the impoverished health status of the average Akwa Ibom child. Realising that children are the bedrock for the future development of any society, the CDT's aims and objectives include amongst others, the establishment of a Nutrition Intervention Programme in the first instance, to be followed by a Reading Culture Intervention Scheme. So many families have lost their children to either poverty or malnutrition and have long lost hope that they will ever find a solution to their problems. The CDT programme of Mrs. Attah has tried over the years since 1999 to restore hope in such homes across the state.

Original_Search_Title: Nakasongola in Cassava Galore
Headline: Uganda: Nakasongola in Cassava Galore
Body_Text:
Kampala —  --New varieties mature in less than six months with big tubers
Every household in Nakasongola district has a cassava shamba.

Original_Search_Title: 200 Youths Get Scholarship Awards
Headline: Nigeria: 200 Youths Get Scholarship Awards
Body_Text:
Gombe — NO fewer than 200 youths of Itaka Community in Etung local government area of Cross River State have received scholarship awards from their representative in the State House of Assembly, Hon. Innocent Okey.
Also, women of the community had a garri-processing factory constructed for them by Hon. Okey, thus bringing to an end problems they go through to process cassava for garri.

Original_Search_Title: Food, Agriculture & Peace for Liberia
Headline: Liberia: Food, Agriculture & Peace for Liberia
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Dr. Cyril E. Broderick looks at how hatred and distastes for one another have deterred investment in the land.
I felt quite queasy today in the heart, stomach and soul as I read the first paragraph of today's article (March 4, 2005) in the Internet publication Economist.com, entitled, Rebuilding Failed States: From Chaos, Order What can the world do about state failure? Surprisingly, quite a lot. What churned my stomach suddenly was this line: In August 2003 (see article), The Economist reported from that unhappy city that "famished townsfolk have already eaten their neighbors' dogs and are reduced to scrounging for snails." That report reflected on a desperate and low ebb in the history of Liberia, but after falling that low, Liberia must rise again after these many years of unrest. I started this article several months ago on Liberian agriculture, but I must finish it today, because the need for investment in agriculture is very urgent and critical.

Original_Search_Title: Joe Abbey Speaks On Economy (final)
Headline: Ghana: Joe Abbey Speaks On Economy (final)
Body_Text:
This is the third and final part of Dr. Joe Abbey's thoughts on the
economy from an interview granted The Chronicle on Monday, February 28
2005. The first two parts were published in our March 1st and March 2nd
editions.
The Executive Director of the Centre for Policy Analysis,
Dr. J. L. S. Abbey has stated that for him as an Analyst, what we need to
find out, as a nation is, "how pro-poor are our pro-poor expenditures?" To
him, we must stop the 'business as usual' approach to things for, "poverty
is so personal that unless you talk to people directly, you may take some
things for granted. We must get close to the ground to collect data."

Original_Search_Title: Attracting Foreign Support for Nigerian Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Attracting Foreign Support for Nigerian Agriculture
Body_Text:
Lagos — A RECENT survey by the Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO), an
officiate of the United Nations Organisation, shows that Nigeria has
suffered 80 per cent reduction in food production in the last eight
years.
Indeed, at various markets in Lagos, there were signs of dwindling
quality of most raw food items supply from the hinterland. Among the notable
ones are tubers of yam and cassava, pepper, tomatoes, bags of garri, beans
and even potatoes. Reflecting on this deplorable situation, farm experts
have advised that there is pressing need for Nigeria to ensure and assure
food supplies and adequate food consumption on a sustainable basis. We
require sufficient food supply for national self-esteem, security,
development and socio-political stability.

Original_Search_Title: Ivorians Scared to Return to Volatile 'Wild West'
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Cote D Ivoire-Liberia: Ivorians Scared to Return to Volatile 'Wild West'
Body_Text:
Loguatuo — In this small Liberian village just over the border from Cote d'Ivoire,
one out of every 10 people is a French-speaking Ivorian refugee fleeing the
country's volatile "Wild West."
"Help us please," said Eugenie Lambe, a 24-year-old who last November
fled western Cote d'Ivoire to Liberia along with her grandmother, mother and
eight brothers and sisters, fearing a new flare-up of fighting in the
country's two-and-a-half-year conflict.

Original_Search_Title: Environmental Causes Behind Food Shortages in Northern Kirundo
Headline: Burundi: Environmental Causes Behind Food Shortages in Northern Kirundo
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — Declining rainfall - caused by the destruction of forest, water
catchments and other ecosystems - is one of the major reasons for food
shortages in Burundi's northeastern province of Kirundo and humans are to
blame, local experts said.
A World Food Programme (WFP) official in Burundi, Guillaume Foliot, has
described the country's northeast as a "destitute zone" in which, after
fleeing war, significant numbers of refugees and internally displaced
persons (IDPs) have arrived since 1993, using natural resources by burning
brush and hacking down trees.

Original_Search_Title: FG Warns Farmers On High Price of Cassava
Headline: Nigeria: FG Warns Farmers On High Price of Cassava
Body_Text:
Ilorin — The federal government has warned farmers against any increment in the
price of cassava, following the adoption of a policy by the federal
government to include 10 per cent cassava flour in bread and other
confectionery.
The warning was against the backdrop of protests by feedmillers and
bakers across the country of sudden and sharp increase in the prices of
cassava from the farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Mega Men for Mega Business
Headline: Nigeria: Mega Men for Mega Business
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Transnational Corporation of Nigeria, the mega-corporation formed by a
group of 42 Nigerian investors, enjoys the backing of President Olusegun
Obasanjo and is planned to be Nigeria's biggest indigenous company. Adeyeye
Joseph writes on these investors and the unique relationship that exists
between them and the President.
President Obasanjo likes his things big. The President is the helmsman
of the biggest party in Africa. He wants banking to be the preserve of a few
big banks. And for an economy, he wants one dominated and powered by big
businesses. For a while now, President Obasanjo has been utilising a mixture
of personal charm and presidential clout to realise the last wish. He's been
making efforts to weld the cream of Nigeria's economic elite into a
megabusiness concern that would spearhead the transformation of the Nigerian
business scene. Last week, the President's labour budded. At the State
House, Abuja, the Director-General of the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE),
Dr. Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke, announced the formation of a multi-million dollar
transnational company, The Transnational Corporation of Nigeria. Although,
Dr. Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke tried to stress that this was entirely a private
sector initiative she nevertheless admitted that it was a project sparked
off by a germ of an idea sparked by Mr. President.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Exports Banned to Avert Food Crisis
Headline: Zambia: Maize Exports Banned
Body_Text:
We'll ensure no one starves because of hunger caused by poor rains -
Sikatana
GOVERNMENT has with immediate effect banned the export of maize to other
countries because of the impending drought situation in Zambia.

Original_Search_Title: 'Inland Container Depots for May'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Inland Container Depots for May'
Body_Text:
Abuja — Eight inland container depots planned by Federal Government to ease the movement of goods to and from the hinterlands will take off in about two months time, Nigerian Shippers Council announced yesterday.
The Council's Executive Secretary and Chief Executive Officer (CEO), Capt. Adamu Audu Biu, who disclosed this at a meeting with shippers associations in the North-central zone in Abuja, said the final documents regarding the project would soon be submitted to President Olusegun Obasanjo for approval, to enable the Federal Ministry of Transport and its parastatals sign the final agreements with the preferred operators already on ground.

Original_Search_Title: World Bank Grants N455m to FCT Fadama Project
Headline: Nigeria: World Bank Grants N455m to FCT Fadama Project
Body_Text:
Abuja — Impressed with the performance of the FCT Fadama Development Project, the World Bank is to disburse N455 million to boost the implementation of the project in the territory.
Dr Sidi Jammeh, head of the World Bank delegation, which visited the FCT Fadama Development Project office in Gwagwalada, said the decision to increase funding for the project, was due to the success recorded so far.

Original_Search_Title: 'Involve Large-Scale Farmers in Cassava Production'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Involve Large-Scale Farmers in Cassava Production'
Body_Text:
Minna — The Director of Institute of Agricultural Research and Training (IAR&T) Ibadan, Professor Ebenezer Adebowale has hinged the success of the presidential initiative on cassava production on including medium and large scale farmers in the strategies for the increase of the cash crop.
Specking at the opening ceremony of a two day "Train the trainer workshop on cassava production, under the presidential initiative for subjects matter speculants (SMSs), Minna on Tuesday, the university den also made a case for small scale farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Taxes Make Rural Poor Poorer - Study
Headline: Uganda: Taxes Make Rural Poor Poorer - Study
Body_Text:
Kampala — Taxes, dues and license fees in rural areas are the main cause of
poverty, a report has stated.
The report by the Economic Policy Research Centre (EPRC), based at
Makerere University, says flat rate taxes are regressive and take away most
of the rural people's income.

Original_Search_Title: Mechanised Farming Programme Launched
Headline: Cameroon: Mechanised Farming Programme Launched
Body_Text:
The Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development, Tchatat Clobert, presided at the ceremony yesterday at Ebebda.
All roads in Ebebda Sub-Division of the Lekié Division yesterday, led to the little village of Djounyat, located some two kilometres from Ebebda town. The population, the most of them women of Common Initiative Groups, came out in their numbers to unanimously salute the initiative of the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development to facilitate the launching of a mechanised farming programme in their village for Cameroon. The programme was launched by Agriculture and Rural Development Minister, Tchatat Clobert.

Original_Search_Title: Joe Abbey Speaks On Economy (2)
Headline: Ghana: Joe Abbey Speaks On Economy (2)
Body_Text:
The Executive Director of the Centre for Policy Analysis (CEPA), Dr. Joe Abbey has called on Ghanaians to take the rhetoric out of modernizing agriculture. "Let's get on with it. Let's look at what types of research, what types of innovations we can introduce to make agriculture more competitive."
Agriculture must do it for us! The present method of shifting cultivation would not last, as we would run out of land. We must have water systems that we can manage, so we turn water on when we need it. We've got to get out of the situation where we have a period of drought and another of flooding."

Original_Search_Title: Beyond Natural Resources: Developing
Headline: Nigeria: Beyond Natural Resources: Developing
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Dr. Saraki, Kwara State governor, presented this address recently at
the Harvard Business School in the United States of America. To be
continued.
What we did was to strictly monitor the delivery of the fertilizers as
they arrived and took records of what was kept in each warehouse.

Original_Search_Title: President Grants Approval to Local Investors On IPP
Headline: Nigeria: President Grants Approval to Local Investors On IPP
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo on 28 February received the Strategy Interest Document (SID) of the Transnational Corporation of Nigeria (TCN), a new private sector mega corporation floated by a group of notable Nigerian investors.
The President instantly approved the group's request for a license to operate an independent power plant but asked them to formally bid for any of the four government-owned refineries like other investors.

Original_Search_Title: Where Did the Beauty of the North Go?
Headline: Uganda: Where Did the Beauty of the North Go?
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT'S 2004. He leans back on the deep brown mahogany chair. African paintings adorn the cream walls of his spacious office. They manifest strong bonds to his hosts.
The hot afternoon sun streams through the windows of this L-shaped brick building housing Radio Wa in Lira town. It stands tall amidst trees that dwarf it. The trees form an arch over the Lira - Kampala highway. As sweat gleams on his wrinkled yet youthful face, Fr. John Fraser recalls his first safari in Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians Float N14bn Multinational Firm
Headline: Nigerians Float N14bn Multinational Firm
Body_Text:
Abuja — A MAJOR jerk-up was given to the country's economic development efforts with the disclosure by some notable Nigerian industrial icons that they had jointly contributed N14 billion to float a Transnational Corporation of Nigeria.
The new firm is primed for high net international job understakings and is likened to multinational firms operating in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Centre for Policy Analysis Speaks on Need for Better Statistical Services to Aid Economy
Headline: Ghana: Joe Abbey Speaks On Economy (1)
Body_Text:
... raises eyebrows over figures
The Executive Director of the Centre for Policy Analysis,
Dr. J. L. S. Abbey, has stated that Ghana needs to strengthen the
Statistical Service, to improve on its data collection for planning and
national development purposes.

Original_Search_Title: Dangote, Others Form N14bn Mega Corporation
Headline: Nigeria: Dangote, Others Form N14bn Mega Corporation
Body_Text:
Abuja — Agroup of Nigerian industrialists have pulled resources together to incorporate a mega corporation, Trans-national Corporation of Nigeria, with an initial capital base of N14 billion.
The sponsors of the corporation, who are to pay N100 million as their share contribution include Alhaji Aliko Dangote, the Managing Director of Nigerian Breweries Plc, Festus Odimegwu and chief executive of Zenith Bank Plc, Mr Jim Ovia.

Original_Search_Title: 'Bread Will Become Nigeria's Cheapest Food'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Bread Will Become Nigeria's Cheapest Food'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Bread consumers in Nigeria have no cause to worry about the cost of
their favorite breakfast menu in respect of the recent government policy on
the production of composite bread using cassava and wheat flour.
This is because the Master Bakers Association (MBA), with over 14,000
members in Lagos State alone, has pledged to ensure the success of the
policy on the introduction of composite bread of 10% cassava flour with 90%
wheat flour into the Nigerian menu, without additional cost charged on the
consumers.

Original_Search_Title: Ogun Approves Incentives for Cassava Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Ogun Approves Incentives for Cassava Farmers
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Ogun State Governor Otumba Gbenga Daniel, in a move to improve food security in the State, has released fund for the purchase and installation of Universal Cassava Processing Plant for each of the four zones of the State. The first plant complete with a standby generating set will be installed in the Yewa Zone.
The Ogun State Governor, Otunba Gbenga Daniel, has also approved a subsidy of 50% on fertilizers purchased by cassava farmer in the State. This is part of the measures recently taken by the Governor to give concrete support to the ongoing revival of cassava in line with the vision of President Olusegun Obasanjo. The Governor has also approved a 25% subsidy on all agrochemicals purchased by cassava farmers. Other incentives include the purchase of four brand new tractors and set of implements to be used exclusively by farmers cultivating cassava in Egba, Yewa, Ijebu and Remo divisions of the State. Prince Dele Obadina, the State's Commissioner for Agriculture said to ensure that o nly bonafide cassava growers benefit from the incentives, officials of the Ministry will deal with organised farmers group.
